topics.title: Steps Involved in Making a Casting - Advantages and Applications; Patterns and Pattern Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps Involved in Making a Casting - Advantages and Applications; Patterns and Pattern Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: STEPS IN MAKING A CASTING — ADVANTAGES & APPLICATIONS; PATTERNS AND PATTERN MAKING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition — What is a Casting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *Casting* is a manufacturing process in which a liquid (or molten) material — typically a metal, alloy, or thermoplastic — is poured into a pre-shaped hollow mould cavity that contains the negative geometry of the desired part, allowed to solidify, and then extracted as a solidified part called a *casting*. The process is governed by *solidification mechanics*, *heat transfer*, and *fluid flow physics* and is classified under *primary shaping (bulk deformation / forming)* manufacturing processes.

The mould is usually prepared using a *pattern* (a near-replica of the final part) and a *moulding sand* (or other refractory medium). The molten metal fills the cavity under the influence of *gravity* (static casting) or *centrifugal force / pressure / vacuum* (dynamic casting).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy (First-Time Learner Friendly)**
> Imagine making a *Gulab Jamun* at home. You take a steel mould (or hand-shape) to give the dough its spherical shape, fry it, and then remove the mould — what remains is a solid sphere of delicious sweet. The mould gave shape to a soft material that *hardened* upon cooling.
> Now scale that up to engineering: instead of dough, you use **molten iron at 1300 °C**; instead of a kitchen mould, you use a **sand mould** built around a *pattern* (the master shape). The molten metal solidifies in the cavity, the mould is broken, and out comes a fully formed engine block, pump housing, or turbine casing. That is **casting** in its simplest essence.

### 1.3 Standard Process Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric / Constant | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density of molten cast iron | **~ 6.9 to 7.2 g/cm³** | Mass calculations, pouring rate |
| Latent heat of fusion (Al) | **~ 397 kJ/kg** | Heat extraction during solidification |
| Latent heat of fusion (Fe) | **~ 247 kJ/kg** | Heat extraction during solidification |
| Pouring temperature (cast iron) | **~ 1250 to 1400 °C** | Above liquidus line |
| Shrinkage allowance (cast iron) | **~ 10 mm/m** | Dimensional compensation |
| Draft angle (typical) | **1° to 3°** | Pattern withdrawal ease |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — STEPS IN MAKING A CASTING

A sand casting operation (the most common KTU-examined variant) follows a strict sequence of operations. The KTU 2024 Scheme examiners frequently ask the *flowchart* or *procedural* question, and expect the candidate to know the order and the *purpose* of every step.

> [!NOTE]
> The order is **critical**. Skipping a step (e.g., pouring without a properly dried mould) leads to defects such as blowholes, sand inclusions, and misruns. The KTU evaluator will deduct marks if the order is wrong.

## 2.1 The Nine Canonical Steps

### Step 1 — Pattern and Core Box Preparation
- A *pattern* (master shape) is fabricated to slightly *oversize* the final part dimensions, compensating for shrinkage.
- A *core box* is prepared to produce internal cavities that the sand mould cannot form on its own.

### Step 2 — Sand Preparation and Testing
- *Moulding sand* is a mixture of **silica sand (SiO₂, 85–95 %)**, **binder (clay 5–10 %)**, **moisture (3–6 %)**, and **additives (coal dust, sawdust)**.
- Tested for *permeability*, *green strength*, *dry strength*, *flowability*, and *refractoriness*.

### Step 3 — Mould Making (Cope and Drag)
- The moulding flask has two halves: **cope (top)** and **drag (bottom)**.
- Drag is placed on the moulding board, pattern positioned, sand rammed, struck off, rolled over, cope placed, and the process repeated.
- *Sprue*, *runner*, *gate*, and *riser* are cut or formed to establish the metal flow network.

### Step 4 — Core Placement
- Cores (pre-hardened baked sand) are placed in the core prints of the mould to form *internal features* (e.g., cylinder bore in an engine block).

### Step 5 — Mould Assembly and Clamping
- Cope is placed on drag, weights or clamps applied to resist the *metallostatic pressure* of liquid metal.

### Step 6 — Melting and Pouring
- Metal is melted in a *cupola*, *induction furnace*, or *crucible furnace*.
- Poured continuously until the riser is filled (riser acts as a *feed metal reservoir*).

### Step 7 — Solidification and Cooling
- Heat is extracted through the mould walls.
- Directional solidification is ensured — solidification proceeds from the casting surfaces **toward** the riser, preventing shrinkage voids.

### Step 8 — Shakeout
- The mould is broken (manually or by vibrating the casting on a shakeout grid), and the casting is removed.

### Step 9 — Cleaning, Fettling, and Inspection
- Sand is removed (by shot blasting, tumbling, or hand fettling).
- Gates and risers are cut off (by sawing, torching, or cold cutting).
- *Non-destructive testing (NDT)* like visual inspection, dye penetrant test, or radiography is performed.

## 2.2 Advantages of the Casting Process

| S.No. | Advantage | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Complex geometries** are achievable | Mould cavity can have undercuts, internal cavities (via cores) |
| 2 | **Wide material range** | Ferrous, non-ferrous, polymers, ceramics all castable |
| 3 | **Size flexibility** | From grams (jewellery) to 200+ tonnes (propeller shafts) |
| 4 | **Economical for small batches** | Tooling (pattern) cost is low vs. forging dies or injection moulds |
| 5 | **Near-net shape** | Reduces subsequent machining; high material yield |
| 6 | **Anisotropic properties** | Controlled grain flow possible in directional solidification |

## 2.3 Limitations of Casting (Balanced View)

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU examiner often tests whether you know both sides. List limitations alongside advantages.

- Poor dimensional accuracy (tolerance of ± 0.5 mm typical)
- Surface finish is rough (Ra 12.5 to 25 µm)
- Casting defects (porosity, shrinkage, inclusions) are common
- Labour-intensive process
- Not suitable for very thin sections (chilling effect)

## 2.4 Applications of Castings — Real-World Engineering

| Industry | Typical Cast Components |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine blocks, cylinder heads, pistons, brake drums, manifolds |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blades (investment cast), landing gear housings |
| **Piping** | Valves, elbows, flanges, pump casings |
| **Power** | Wind turbine hubs, hydro-turbine casings |
| **Machine Tools** | Lathe beds, columns, slideways (high damping capacity) |
| **Defence** | Gun barrels, shell casings, armour plates |
| **Art / Architecture** | Sculptures, ornamental grilles, bells |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently asked** in KTU Module 1 numericals. Memorise the symbols and the units.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of molten metal | $\rho = m / V$ | kg/m³ |
| $P$ | Metallostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| $Q$ | Volumetric pouring rate | $Q = V / t$ | m³/s |
| $F$ | Force on mould wall | $F = P \cdot A$ | N |
| $R_s$ | Solidification time (Chvorinov's rule) | $R_s = k \cdot (V / A)^2$ | s |
| $S_a$ | Shrinkage allowance | $S_a = L \cdot (\alpha \cdot \Delta T + \beta)$ | mm |
| $D_a$ | Draft allowance | $D_a = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | mm |
| $M_a$ | Machining allowance | $M_a = $ per-side stock | mm |

**Chvorinov's Rule** is critical — it states:
$$t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2$$

where $t_s$ = solidification time, $B$ = mould constant, $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time)

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 frequently tests this derivation. We derive it from first principles using *Newton's law of cooling* with a *lumped capacitance* assumption.

**Assumption:** The casting is treated as a lumped mass at uniform temperature. Heat is removed by conduction through the mould wall.

**Step 1 — Total heat to be extracted (latent + sensible):**

$$Q = m \cdot L + m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot \left[ L + c_p \cdot (T_{pour} - T_{solidus}) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Heat flux rate through mould interface (Fourier's law):**

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{cast} - T_{mould})$$

where $h$ is the *effective heat transfer coefficient* at the mould-metal interface.

**Step 3 — Equating the total heat to be removed to the integral of the rate:**

$$\int_0^{t_s} h \cdot A \cdot (T_{cast} - T_{mould}) \, dt = \rho \cdot V \cdot \left[ L + c_p \cdot \Delta T \right]$$

**Step 4 — Assuming the average driving temperature is approximately constant (Bi ≪ 1):**

$$h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{avg} \cdot t_s = \rho \cdot V \cdot \left[ L + c_p \cdot \Delta T \right]$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $t_s$:**

$$t_s = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot \left[ L + c_p \cdot \Delta T \right]}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{avg}}$$

**Step 6 — Grouping all the constants (which are material + process specific) into a single constant $B$:**

$$\boxed{t_s = B \cdot \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2}$$

This is **Chvorinov's Rule**, and the key engineering insight is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The solidification time scales with the **square** of the modulus $(V/A)$. To control solidification direction and avoid shrinkage voids, the **riser must always have a higher $V/A$ modulus** than the casting itself. This guarantees the riser remains liquid longest and feeds the casting as it shrinks.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Riser Modulus Calculation (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem:** A casting has a volume $V_c = 0.008 \, \text{m}^3$ and surface area $A_c = 0.6 \, \text{m}^2$. A cylindrical riser of diameter $D = 0.2 \, \text{m}$ and height $H = 0.3 \, \text{m}$ is proposed. Determine whether the riser is adequate. Assume $B$ is the same for casting and riser.

**Step 1 — Casting modulus:**

$$\left( \frac{V}{A} \right)_c = \frac{0.008}{0.6} = 0.0133 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Riser volume:**

$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.2)^2 \cdot 0.3 = 0.00942 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Riser surface area (cylinder lateral + bottom + top):**

$$A_r = \pi D H + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \pi \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.3 + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.2)^2$$

$$A_r = 0.1885 + 0.0628 = 0.2513 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Riser modulus:**

$$\left( \frac{V}{A} \right)_r = \frac{0.00942}{0.2513} = 0.0375 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$(V/A)_r = 0.0375 \, \text{m} \; > \; (V/A)_c = 0.0133 \, \text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the riser modulus is **larger** than the casting modulus, the riser is **adequate** and will solidify **last**. Chvorinov's rule is satisfied: the casting solidifies first, draws feed metal from the riser, and shrinkage is compensated.

## 3.3 Derivation of Metallostatic Pressure

The pressure exerted by molten metal on the mould wall is critical for *flask design* and *mould wall strength* calculation.

**Step 1 — Consider a column of molten metal of height $h$ and cross-section $A$.**

**Step 2 — Weight of the column:**

$$W = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot V \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the base (force per unit area):**

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$

$$\boxed{P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of molten metal (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity ($\approx 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$)
- $h$ = depth of molten metal from free surface (m)

**Practical implication:** For a 1 m deep pour of molten iron ($\rho \approx 7000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$P = 7000 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 68670 \, \text{Pa} \approx 0.687 \, \text{bar}$$

This pressure pushes the sand grains against the flask walls; therefore mould strength (especially green compression strength, typically **0.07 to 0.14 MPa**) must be carefully selected.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Allowances

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pattern allowances are a **high-frequency KTU question topic**. Memorise the *types*, *purpose*, and *formula (where applicable)*.

| Allowance | Symbol | Purpose | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shrinkage / Contraction allowance** | $S_a$ | Compensate for metal contraction on cooling | 10 mm/m (cast iron), 15 mm/m (steel), 20 mm/m (Al) |
| **Draft / Taper allowance** | $D_a$ | Enable easy withdrawal of pattern from sand | 1°–3° on vertical walls |
| **Machining / Finish allowance** | $M_a$ | Provide stock for subsequent machining | 1.5–6 mm depending on size |
| **Distortion / Camber allowance** | $C_a$ | Pre-distort the pattern to counter warping | Judgement-based |
| **Shake / Rapping allowance** | $K_a$ | Compensate for enlargement from rapping pattern | 0.5–2 mm |
| **Core / Core-print allowance** | — | Locating and supporting the core in mould | Determined by core design |

**Shrinkage allowance formula:**

$$S_a = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

where:
- $L$ = original dimension
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion (per °C)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature drop from solidus to room temperature

**Draft allowance formula:**

$$D_a = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

where $h$ = height of vertical surface, $\theta$ = draft angle (typically 1°–3°).

## 3.5 Patterns — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** A *replica* of the final casting (slightly modified by allowances) used to form the *cavity* in the moulding sand. The pattern is *not* the casting itself — it is a *tool* that produces the cavity in which the metal solidifies.

**Pattern = Casting Geometry + All Allowances + Core Prints + Parting Line Features**

## 3.6 Classification of Patterns

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 1 typically asks for a *classification chart* of patterns with one-line descriptions and *sketches* (described textually since we are in text). Memorise the **8 standard pattern types** below.

| S.No. | Pattern Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solid (Single-Piece) Pattern** | One piece, no joints; simplest | Flat, simple castings (handles, simple flanges) |
| 2 | **Split (Two-Piece) Pattern** | Divided at parting plane into two halves | Medium complexity, e.g., pipe elbows, gears |
| 3 | **Multi-Piece Pattern** | Three or more pieces joined by dowels | Complex shapes, deeply recessed features |
| 4 | **Match-Plate Pattern** | Cope and drag halves mounted on opposite sides of a *match plate* | Mass production of small castings (automotive) |
| 5 | **Cope-and-Drag Pattern** | Pattern halves fixed; cope and drag produced separately | Medium-batch production |
| 6 | **Sweep Pattern** | A *sweep* (wooden or metal profile) is rotated about an axis to form the mould | Large symmetrical castings (bell, large pipe) |
| 7 | **Skeleton (False-Core) Pattern** | A wooden *frame* representing the outline; sand rammed inside to form the cavity | Very large castings (lathe bed, machine frame) |
| 8 | **Loose-Piece Pattern** | Pieces attached loosely to main body, withdrawn separately | Patterns with re-entrant features (undercuts) |
| 9 | **Gated Pattern** | Multiple patterns joined to a common runner/gate | Small castings produced in batches |
| 10 | **Follow-Board Pattern** | Pattern mounted on a wooden *follow board* with locating features | Patterns that cannot stand alone (thin, irregular) |
| 11 | **Segment Pattern** | Only one segment of full pattern is made; mould assembled by rotating | Large circular castings (gear blanks, large wheels) |

## 3.7 Pattern Materials

| Material | Properties | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Wood** | Cheap, easy to work, low strength | One-off / small-batch patterns |
| **Metal (Al, Cast Iron, Brass)** | High strength, long life, accurate | Mass production patterns |
| **Plastics / Polymers (Epoxy, Polyester)** | Smooth surface, moderate cost | Medium-volume production |
| **Wax (Investment Casting)** | Lost-wax investment casting | Intricate jewellery, aerospace turbine blades |
| **Plaster of Paris** | Cheap, fragile | Master patterns for short runs |

## 3.8 Comparison — Pattern vs. Casting

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner **loves** the "difference between pattern and casting" question. Know the table cold.

| Aspect | Pattern | Casting |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Wood, metal, plastic | Molten metal (cast) |
| Size | Slightly larger (allowances) | Final required size |
| Surface | Smoother (often polished) | Rougher (sand texture) |
| Purpose | Tool to make mould cavity | Final product |
| Reusability | Reusable (most types) | Single-use end product |
| Corners | Rounded (to avoid sand breakdown) | Sharp (post-machining) |

## 3.9 Core Prints — A Critical Pattern Feature

A *core print* is a **protrusion on the pattern** that creates a seat in the mould sand for positioning and supporting the core.

- *Horizontal core print* → core sits in the parting plane.
- *Vertical core print* → core projects vertically into the mould cavity.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Many students confuse the *core print* with the *core*. The **core print is part of the pattern**; the **core is part of the mould**. The core print creates a *recess* in the mould sand where the (separately-made) core sits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Complete Sand Casting Process Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Pattern and Core Box Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Sand Preparation and Testing]
    B --> C[Step 3: Mould Making in Cope and Drag]
    C --> D[Step 4: Core Placement in Core Prints]
    D --> E[Step 5: Mould Assembly and Clamping]
    E --> F[Step 6: Melting in Furnace and Pouring]
    F --> G[Step 7: Solidification and Cooling per Chvorinov]
    G --> H[Step 8: Shakeout and Casting Removal]
    H --> I[Step 9: Cleaning Fettling and Inspection]
    I --> J{Final Casting Acceptable?}
    J -- No --> K[Rework / Reject]
    J -- Yes --> L[Finished Casting to Stock]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Pattern Allowances Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pattern Allowances] --> S[Shrinkage Allowance]
    P --> D[Draft Taper Allowance]
    P --> M[Machining Allowance]
    P --> C[Distortion Camber Allowance]
    P --> K[Shake Rapping Allowance]
    P --> CP[Core Print Allowance]
    S --> S1[Liquid to Solid Contraction]
    S --> S2[Solid to Room Temp Contraction]
    D --> D1[Internal Walls 1 to 3 deg]
    D --> D2[External Walls 1 to 3 deg]
    M --> M1[Rough Stock for Machining]
    C --> C1[Pre distortion for Stress Relief]
    K --> K1[Rapping Pattern Enlargement Comp]
    CP --> CP1[Locating and Supporting Cores]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Pattern Type Classification (8-Type Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT[Patterns Classification] --> SP[Single Piece Solid]
    PT --> SPP[Split Two Piece]
    PT --> MPT[Multi Piece]
    PT --> CMP[Match Plate]
    PT --> CDP[Cope and Drag]
    PT --> SWP[Sweep]
    PT --> SKP[Skeleton False Core]
    PT --> LPP[Loose Piece]
    PT --> GTP[Gated]
    PT --> FBP[Follow Board]
    PT --> SGP[Segment]
    SP --> SPN[Simple Flat Shapes]
    SPP --> SPPN[Medium Complexity Castings]
    SWP --> SWPN[Large Symmetrical Bell Pipe]
    SKP --> SKPN[Large Machine Beds]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Pattern, Mould, Core, and Casting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Pattern Master Shape] -->|ramming| MLD[Mould Sand Cavity]
    CR[Core Box] -->|baking| CORE[Hardened Sand Core]
    MLD -->|placement| CORE
    CORE -->|assembly| CAS[Casting Cavity]
    MLD -->|pouring| MOLT[Molten Metal]
    MOLT -->|solidification| CAST[Finished Casting]
    CAST -->|shakeout| ROUGH[Raw Casting with Sprue]
    ROUGH -->|fettling| FIN[Final Machined Casting]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block — Directional Solidification Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Riser] -->|feeds metal| M[Mould Cavity]
    M -->|cools first| FAR[Far End of Casting]
    FAR -->|chills solid| C1[Region 1: Solid First]
    M -->|cools second| MID[Mid Region]
    MID -->|solidifies| C2[Region 2: Solid Next]
    M -->|cools last| NEAR[Near Riser Region]
    NEAR -->|last to freeze| C3[Region 3: Solid Last Riser Feeds]
    R -->|remains liquid longest| LIQ[Liquid Feed Metal from Riser]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBAUT404]`
**Define the term "pattern" in casting. List any four pattern allowances.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *pattern* is a **replica of the final casting**, slightly modified by allowances, used to produce the cavity in the moulding sand (1 mark).

Four pattern allowances (½ mark each):
1. **Shrinkage allowance** — compensates for metal contraction on cooling
2. **Draft (taper) allowance** — enables smooth withdrawal of pattern from sand
3. **Machining (finish) allowance** — provides stock for subsequent machining
4. **Shake (rapping) allowance** — compensates for mould cavity enlargement when pattern is rapped for removal

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBAUT404]`
**State Chvorinov's rule and explain its significance in casting design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Chvorinov's rule* states that the **solidification time of a casting is directly proportional to the square of its volume-to-surface-area ratio (modulus)** (2 marks).

$$t_s = B \left( \frac{V}{A} \right)^2$$

**Significance:** The riser must have a *larger* $(V/A)$ modulus than the casting so that the casting solidifies first and the riser remains molten to feed shrinkage cavities (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, PBAUT404, Module 1]`

**(a) Describe in detail the various steps involved in making a sand casting. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pattern and Core Box Preparation:** A pattern (replica of the casting slightly enlarged by allowances) and a core box (for internal cavities) are prepared from wood, metal, or plastic. (1 mark)
2. **Sand Preparation:** Moulding sand (silica + clay + moisture + additives) is mixed and tested for permeability, green strength, and flowability. (1 mark)
3. **Mould Making:** Drag flask placed on moulding board, sand rammed around pattern, struck off, rolled over. Cope placed on drag; pattern positioned in cope half, sand rammed, sprue and riser cut. (1 mark)
4. **Core Placement:** Pre-baked sand cores are placed into core prints of the mould to form internal cavities. (1 mark)
5. **Mould Assembly and Clamping:** Cope placed on drag and clamped to withstand metallostatic pressure. (1 mark)
6. **Melting and Pouring:** Metal is melted in cupola or induction furnace and poured into the sprue until the riser is full. (1 mark)
7. **Solidification, Shakeout, and Cleaning:** Casting is allowed to cool, mould is broken (shakeout), sand is removed, gates and risers are cut off, and the casting is inspected. (1 mark)

**(b) With a neat sketch (describe in words), explain the different types of patterns used in foundry practice. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major types of patterns are:

1. **Solid (Single-Piece) Pattern (1 mark):** A one-piece pattern without joints. Used for simple flat castings; cheapest to make but limited to low-complexity parts.
2. **Split Pattern (1 mark):** Pattern divided at the parting plane into cope half and drag half, joined by dowel pins. Used for medium-complexity castings like pipe fittings.
3. **Match-Plate Pattern (1 mark):** Both pattern halves are mounted on opposite sides of a metal or wooden *match plate*. Enables high productivity; used in mechanised foundries.
4. **Sweep Pattern (1 mark):** A *sweep* (a profile of wood or metal) is rotated about a vertical axis to sweep the mould cavity. Used for large rotationally symmetric castings (large bells, pipes).
5. **Skeleton (False-Core) Pattern (1 mark):** A wooden frame of the outline is built; sand is rammed inside the frame to form the mould surface. Used for very large castings such as lathe beds.
6. **Loose-Piece Pattern (1 mark):** Pieces attached loosely to the main pattern are withdrawn separately to release undercuts. Used for castings with re-entrant features.
7. **Gated Pattern (1 mark):** Several patterns are mounted on a common gate/runner system; multiple castings poured simultaneously. Used for small castings in volume.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, PBAUT404, Module 1 — Internal Choice B]`

**(a) List and explain the various pattern allowances with their significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance (1 mark):** Compensates for the contraction of metal from solidus temperature to room temperature. Pattern is made *larger* by an amount $S_a = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
2. **Draft (Taper) Allowance (1 mark):** A slight taper (1°–3°) is given on all vertical surfaces of the pattern to enable its clean withdrawal from the sand mould without damaging the cavity.
3. **Machining (Finish) Allowance (1 mark):** Extra material is provided on surfaces that require subsequent machining, so that the casting has the correct final size after machining.
4. **Distortion (Camber) Allowance (1 mark):** For shapes prone to warping (e.g., flat thin plates, U-shapes), the pattern is pre-distorted in the *opposite* direction so that the final casting becomes geometrically correct after cooling.
5. **Shake (Rapping) Allowance (1 mark):** When the pattern is rapped to release it from the sand, the mould cavity becomes slightly *enlarged*. The pattern is made slightly *smaller* to compensate.
6. **Draft / Stripping Allowance (1 mark):** Additional taper on deep projections of the pattern (e.g., bosses, ribs) to allow stripping without sand breakage.
7. **Core-Print Allowance (1 mark):** Projections provided on the pattern to create *core seats* (prints) in the mould for locating and supporting the core.

**(b) A casting has volume 0.005 m³ and surface area 0.5 m². A cylindrical riser of diameter 0.18 m and height 0.27 m is proposed. Using Chvorinov's rule, check if the riser is adequate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Casting modulus (1 mark):**
$$\left( \frac{V}{A} \right)_c = \frac{0.005}{0.5} = 0.010 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Riser volume (1 mark):**
$$V_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.18)^2 \cdot 0.27$$
$$V_r = 0.7854 \cdot 0.0324 \cdot 0.27 = 0.00687 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Riser surface area (lateral + top + bottom) (2 marks):**
$$A_r = \pi D H + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$
$$A_r = \pi \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.27 + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.18)^2$$
$$A_r = 0.1527 + 0.0509 = 0.2036 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Riser modulus (1 mark):**
$$\left( \frac{V}{A} \right)_r = \frac{0.00687}{0.2036} = 0.0337 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Chvorinov's solidification time comparison (1 mark):**
$$\frac{t_{s,r}}{t_{s,c}} = \left( \frac{(V/A)_r}{(V/A)_c} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.0337}{0.010} \right)^2 = (3.37)^2 = 11.36$$

The riser solidifies **~11.36 times slower** than the casting.

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
> The riser modulus (0.0337 m) is **greater** than the casting modulus (0.010 m). The riser will solidify *after* the casting and feed liquid metal to compensate shrinkage. **The riser is adequate.** ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Casting Module Questions**
> 1. **Order of steps matter** — Writing "pouring" before "mould assembly" or "sand preparation" is a **direct loss of 1–2 marks**. The evaluator checks the logical sequence.
> 2. **Pattern allowance formulas** — Students often confuse *shrinkage* and *shake* allowance direction. Shrinkage = pattern **larger**; Shake = pattern **smaller** (negative allowance). Mixing this up costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Chvorinov's rule** — Many students write $t_s = B (V/A)$ (linear). The correct relation is **squared**: $t_s = B (V/A)^2$. Losing the square = -2 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 4. **Core print vs. Core** — Examiners test this distinction. Core print is on the **pattern**; core is a **separate sand body** placed in the mould.
> 5. **Riser adequacy check** — A riser is *adequate* only if $(V/A)_{riser} > (V/A)_{casting}$. Writing only the volume ratio is **not sufficient**; you must explicitly compare moduli.
> 6. **Allowance values** — Memorise the typical numerical values (10 mm/m for cast iron shrinkage, 1°–3° for draft). Numerical mistakes cost ½ mark each.

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this twice before the exam.**

- **Casting** = liquid metal poured into mould cavity → solidified → removed.
- **9 Steps**: Pattern Prep → Sand Prep → Mould Making → Core Placement → Mould Assembly → Melting/Pouring → Solidification → Shakeout → Cleaning/Inspection.
- **Advantages**: Complex shapes, wide material range, size flexibility, low tooling cost, near-net shape.
- **Limitations**: Poor accuracy, rough surface, casting defects, labour intensive.
- **Applications**: Engine blocks, valves, machine beds, turbine blades, pipes, sculptures.
- **Pattern = Casting + All Allowances** (Shrinkage + Draft + Machining + Distortion + Shake + Core-Print).
- **Shrinkage** = pattern LARGER (compensates cooling contraction).
- **Draft** = 1°–3° taper for pattern withdrawal.
- **Machining** = extra stock for post-casting machining.
- **Chvorinov's Rule**: $t_s = B(V/A)^2$ — **squared** modulus.
- **Riser design rule**: Riser modulus **>** Casting modulus (always).
- **Pattern types**: Solid, Split, Multi-piece, Match-plate, Cope-Drag, Sweep, Skeleton, Loose-piece, Gated, Follow-board, Segment.
- **Pattern materials**: Wood, Metal, Plastics, Wax, Plaster.
- **Metallostatic pressure**: $P = \rho g h$ — basis of mould wall strength design.
- **Core print** is part of the **pattern**; **core** is part of the **mould** (don't confuse).
- **Chvorinov's rule significance**: Ensures **directional solidification** — riser freezes last and feeds shrinkage.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Steps in Making a Casting & Pattern Making**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech PBAUT404 — Industrial Manufacturing Process*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allowances in Patterns - Types - Materials Used for Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Allowances in Patterns - Types - Materials Used for Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pattern?

In foundry engineering, a **pattern** is a replica of the final casting (with certain modifications and additions) used to produce a cavity in the sand mold. The pattern is not an exact copy of the final product — it is a *slightly modified* copy designed to compensate for the physical and chemical changes that occur during the casting process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A *pattern* is a model of the casting used to create the mold cavity. It is made of wood, metal, plastic, or other materials, and incorporates specific dimensional compensations called **pattern allowances** to account for shrinkage, machining, draft, and other effects.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are baking a cake. The cake batter shrinks a little when it bakes. So, if you want a cake of exactly 10 cm height, you cannot pour batter into a 10 cm tin — you need a slightly *taller* tin so that after shrinkage, the cake reaches 10 cm. Similarly, when molten metal solidifies and cools, it *shrinks*. The pattern is therefore made slightly *larger* than the final casting.

The extra dimensions added to a pattern to compensate for these physical phenomena are called **Pattern Allowances**.

## 1.2 What are Pattern Allowances?

**Pattern Allowances** are the deliberate, controlled deviations in the size, shape, or finish of a pattern from the final casting dimensions. They ensure that after the entire casting process (molding, pouring, solidification, cooling, and machining), the final cast product matches the required engineering drawing dimensions as closely as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Without allowances, every casting would be undersized, rough, stuck inside the mold, or impossible to machine accurately. Allowances are therefore the *silent heroes* of every dimensionally-accurate cast component.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Pattern Allowances

- **Dimensional Compensation:** Counteract volumetric shrinkage of metals during solidification.
- **Mold Release:** Provide draft (taper) so the pattern can be withdrawn without damaging the mold.
- **Machining Reference:** Leave extra material for finish machining operations.
- **Vibration Settling:** Accommodate the ramming effect of sand during mold preparation.
- **Distortion Control:** Pre-empt warpage in long, slender castings.

## 1.4 Standard Classification of Pattern Allowances

| Sl. No | Allowance | Primary Purpose |
|:------:|:----------|:----------------|
| 1 | **Shrinkage / Contraction Allowance** | Compensate for metal shrinkage during solidification |
| 2 | **Draft / Taper Allowance** | Enable easy withdrawal of pattern from sand mold |
| 3 | **Machining / Finish Allowance** | Provide extra material for machining the casting |
| 4 | **Shake / Rapping Allowance** | Compensate for mold wall expansion during pattern rapping |
| 5 | **Distortion / Camber Allowance** | Counteract warping during cooling |
| 6 | **Draw / Drawback Allowance** | Allow core prints and projections to release freely |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Pattern Allowance

### 2.1.1 Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance

When molten metal cools from pouring temperature to room temperature, it undergoes volumetric contraction. Since castings are dimensionally constrained by the mold, this contraction translates into **linear shrinkage**.

- **Liquid Shrinkage:** Volume reduction while metal is still in liquid state (compensated by risers).
- **Solidification Shrinkage:** Volume reduction during phase change (compensated by risers).
- **Solid Shrinkage:** Volume reduction as solid metal cools from freezing point to room temperature (compensated by **pattern shrinkage allowance**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pattern shrinkage allowance is added **only in the solid-shrinkage range**, i.e., from freezing temperature to room temperature.

**Shrinkage Values for Common Cast Metals:**

| Metal | Linear Shrinkage Allowance (%) |
|:------|:------------------------------:|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | 1.0 |
| Cast Iron (White) | 2.0 |
| Steel | 2.0 |
| Aluminum Alloys | 1.25 – 1.5 |
| Copper Alloys (Bronze/Brass) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Magnesium Alloys | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| Malleable Iron | 1.0 |

### 2.1.2 Draft (Taper) Allowance

Draft is the slight taper provided on the **vertical surfaces** of a pattern to facilitate its easy withdrawal from the sand mold without damaging the mold walls.

- **External Draft:** Provided on outer vertical surfaces of pattern (away from parting plane).
- **Internal Draft:** Provided on internal vertical surfaces / cores (toward parting plane).

**Typical Draft Values:**

| Surface Condition | Draft (degrees) |
|:------------------|:---------------:|
| Hand molding, rough surface | 1° – 3° |
| Machine molding, smooth surface | 0.5° – 1° |
| Large patterns, wooden | 1° – 2° |
| Small patterns, metal | 0.5° or less |

### 2.1.3 Machining (Finish) Allowance

Almost all castings require subsequent machining operations (turning, milling, drilling, grinding) on critical surfaces to achieve:
- Required dimensional accuracy
- Better surface finish
- Functional tolerances

Extra material is therefore left on the pattern on surfaces designated for machining.

**Typical Machining Allowances:**

| Casting Type | Allowance (mm) |
|:-------------|:--------------:|
| Small castings (< 300 mm) | 1.5 – 3 |
| Medium castings (300–600 mm) | 3 – 5 |
| Large castings (> 600 mm) | 5 – 8 |
| Rough/Non-critical castings | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Precision castings (investment) | 0.25 – 1 |

### 2.1.4 Shake (Rapping) Allowance

When a pattern is rapped (vibrated/struck) to loosen it from the mold walls before withdrawal, the mold cavity walls **expand slightly**. This causes the casting to be **slightly larger** than the pattern.

To compensate, the pattern is made **slightly smaller** on dimensions perpendicular to the parting plane. This negative allowance is the **shake allowance**.

> [!NOTE]
> Shake allowance is generally **negative** (i.e., the pattern is *reduced* in size on the relevant dimension) and is typically 0.5 mm to 1.5 mm for small/medium patterns.

### 2.1.5 Distortion (Camber) Allowance

Long, slender, or non-symmetrical castings (e.g., flat plates, U-shaped castings, long bars) tend to **warp** during cooling because:
- Different sections cool at different rates
- Internal stresses develop during solidification
- Uneven contraction occurs along thin vs. thick sections

To counteract this, the pattern is given an **opposite curvature** (e.g., convex shape made concave in the pattern). This is the distortion or camber allowance.

### 2.1.6 Draw (Drawback) Allowance

For projections, flanges, or recesses in the pattern that are not parallel to the parting plane, an extra allowance is provided so that the pattern can be withdrawn without the projection getting locked into the sand.

This involves making the projection slightly **tapered** or **reduced in size** on the side that will be drawn against the mold.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Unit |
|:-:|:---------------|:------------|:----:|
| 1 | $L_p = L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)$ | Pattern length from casting length (Shrinkage) | mm |
| 2 | $L_c = \dfrac{L_p}{1 + S/100}$ | Casting length from pattern length (reverse) | mm |
| 3 | $\Delta L = L_c \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}$ | Linear dimensional increase on pattern | mm |
| 4 | $D_{\text{draft}} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | Extra material added per side due to draft taper | mm |
| 5 | $L_p^{\text{total}} = L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right) + 2 \cdot M + 2 \cdot D_{\text{draft}}$ | Combined shrinkage + machining + draft on a length | mm |
| 6 | $L_p^{\text{camber}} = L_c + \Delta_{\text{distortion}}$ | Pattern length with camber allowance | mm |
| 7 | $V_p \approx V_c (1 + 3S/100)$ | Approximate volumetric compensation (for solid shrinkage) | mm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** $S$ is the **linear** shrinkage percentage (not volumetric). The volumetric shrinkage is approximately **three times** the linear shrinkage for isotropic metals. Therefore, $V_{\text{shrink}} \approx 3 \times S\%$ volumetrically.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pattern allowances are the *unsung foundation* of every cast product in industry — from **engine blocks** (automotive), **turbine housings** (aerospace), **pump casings** (fluid handling), to **manhole covers** (civil). The dimensional accuracy of any cast component hinges on the precision of the allowance calculations. In mass production foundries using **match-plate** or **cope-and-drag** patterns, allowances are standardized for each alloy and entered into CAM databases, ensuring repeatability across thousands of castings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shrinkage Allowance on a Linear Dimension

### 3.1.1 Governing Principle

When a metal casting cools from its solidus temperature $T_s$ to ambient temperature $T_a$, it undergoes a linear contraction governed by the coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$.

The total linear shrinkage fraction is:

$$
\varepsilon_s = \alpha \cdot (T_s - T_a)
$$

This is reported as a **percentage**:

$$
S\% = \varepsilon_s \times 100
$$

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Pattern Length Equation

Let:
- $L_c$ = Required casting length (final desired dimension)
- $L_p$ = Pattern length (what we must cut on the pattern)
- $S$ = Linear shrinkage percentage for the chosen metal

**Step 1:** After shrinkage, the casting will be $L_c$. Before shrinkage (i.e., at the moment of solidification), the metal occupied a length $L_p$ on the pattern.

**Step 2:** The contraction is expressed as:

$$
L_c = L_p - \Delta L
$$

where $\Delta L$ is the total contraction.

**Step 3:** Substituting the definition of percentage shrinkage:

$$
\Delta L = L_p \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$
L_c = L_p - L_p \cdot \dfrac{S}{100}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $L_p$:

$$
L_c = L_p \left(1 - \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $L_p$ (the pattern dimension we need):

$$
L_p = \dfrac{L_c}{1 - \dfrac{S}{100}}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the binomial approximation for small $S$ (since $S \ll 100$):

$$
L_p \approx L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** used in industry.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A gray cast iron casting must be 500 mm long. The shrinkage allowance for gray cast iron is $S = 1.0\%$. Calculate the pattern length.

**Given:**
- $L_c = 500$ mm
- $S = 1.0\%$

**Step 1:** Identify the formula:

$$
L_p = L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
L_p = 500 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{1.0}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the bracket term:

$$
L_p = 500 \times (1 + 0.01) = 500 \times 1.01
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:

$$
L_p = 505 \text{ mm}
$$

**Verification:** After shrinkage, the casting will be $505 \times (1 - 0.01) = 505 \times 0.99 = 499.95 \approx 500$ mm. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined Allowance Equation

When **multiple allowances** (shrinkage + machining + draft) act on the **same dimension**, the cumulative pattern dimension is:

$$
L_p^{\text{total}} = L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right) + 2M + 2D_{\text{draft}}
$$

where:
- $M$ = Machining allowance per side (in mm)
- $D_{\text{draft}} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)$ = Draft-induced extra material per side

**Step-by-step expansion of draft contribution:**

$$
D_{\text{draft}} = L_p^{\text{uncorrected}} \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

where $\theta$ is the draft angle in degrees.

### 3.2.1 Worked Example: Combined Allowance

**Problem:** A steel casting of length 800 mm requires shrinkage allowance of 2%, machining allowance of 4 mm per side, and a draft of 1° on vertical surfaces. Find the total pattern length.

**Given:**
- $L_c = 800$ mm
- $S = 2.0\%$
- $M = 4$ mm (per side)
- $\theta = 1°$

**Step 1:** Compute pattern length with shrinkage alone:

$$
L_p^{(1)} = 800 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{100}\right) = 800 \times 1.02 = 816 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute draft contribution per side:

$$
D_{\text{draft}} = 816 \times \tan(1°) = 816 \times 0.01746 \approx 14.25 \text{ mm per side}
$$

**Step 3:** Total draft contribution (both sides):

$$
2 \cdot D_{\text{draft}} = 2 \times 14.25 = 28.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Total machining contribution (both sides):

$$
2 \cdot M = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Final total pattern length:

$$
L_p^{\text{total}} = 816 + 28.5 + 8 = 852.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Interpretation:** The pattern must be 852.5 mm long, 16 mm longer on each end than the desired 800 mm casting.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Allowance Selection Decision Tree

| Casting Condition | Required Allowances | Priority Order |
|:------------------|:--------------------|:--------------:|
| Long flat plate | Distortion + Draft + Machining | Distortion → Draft → Machining |
| Cylindrical pulley | Shrinkage + Machining (bore) | Shrinkage → Machining |
| Thin-walled box | Draft + Machining + Shrinkage | Draft → Machining → Shrinkage |
| Large engine block | All six allowances | Shrinkage → Machining → Draft → Shake → Distortion → Draw |
| Investment casting | Mostly Machining (minimal others) | Machining only |
| Symmetrical short casting | Shrinkage + Draft | Shrinkage → Draft |

> [!NOTE]
> The **order** in which allowances are applied matters in practice. The convention is: **Shrinkage first** (on every dimension), then **machining** on designated surfaces, then **draft** on vertical walls, and finally **distortion/shake** for special cases.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Application of Pattern Allowances

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Drawing of Casting] --> B[Identify Casting Material]
    B --> C{Select Shrinkage Allowance S percent}
    C --> D[Apply Shrinkage to All Dimensions]
    D --> E{Is Surface to be Machined?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Add Machining Allowance M per side]
    E -->|No| G[Skip Machining]
    F --> H{Vertical Surfaces Present?}
    G --> H
    H -->|Yes| I[Add Draft Taper theta degrees]
    H -->|No| J[Skip Draft]
    I --> K{Slender or Unsymmetrical Shape?}
    J --> K
    K -->|Yes| L[Apply Distortion Allowance]
    K -->|No| M[Skip Distortion]
    L --> N[Pattern Dimension Lp final]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Manufacture the Pattern]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Types of Patterns in Foundry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Patterns] --> A1[Single Piece Pattern]
    P --> A2[Split Pattern Two Piece]

    P --> A4[Multi Piece Pattern]
    P --> B1[Loose Piece Pattern]
    P --> B2[Follow Board Pattern]
    P --> B3[Skeleton Pattern]
    P --> B4[Match Plate Pattern]
    P --> B5[Cope and Drag Pattern]
    P --> C1[Sweep Pattern]
    P --> C2[Segmental Pattern]
    P --> C3[Boxed Core Pattern]
    P --> D1[Investment Pattern Wax]
    P --> D2[Shell Pattern]
    P --> D3[Ceramic Shell Pattern]

    A1 --> U1[Used for small simple castings]
    A2 --> U2[Used for medium castings with parting line]
    A4 --> U3[Used for complex castings]
    B4 --> U4[Used for mass production]
    C1 --> U5[Used for large circular castings]
    D1 --> U6[Used for precision investment castings]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pattern Materials Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Pattern Material Selection] --> Q1{Production Volume?}
    Q1 -->|Low lt 50| Q2{Required Life?}
    Q1 -->|Medium 50 to 5000| Q3{Accuracy Needed?}
    Q1 -->|High gt 5000| Q4{Surface Finish?}

    Q2 -->|Short lt 10 cycles| W[Wood Mahogany Teak Pine]
    Q2 -->|Long gt 10 cycles| PL[Plaster of Paris]

    Q3 -->|Moderate| MT[Aluminum Alloy]
    Q3 -->|High| BR[Brass or Bronze]

    Q4 -->|Smooth| ST[Steel or Cast Iron]
    Q4 -->|Very Smooth| PL2[Plastics Epoxy Polyurethane]

    W --> USE1[Single piece small castings]
    PL --> USE2[Prototype patterns limited life]
    MT --> USE3[Medium volume production]
    BR --> USE4[High accuracy medium volume]
    ST --> USE5[Mass production high durability]
    PL2 --> USE6[Complex shapes smooth finish]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Draft Allowance Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Vertical Wall Without Draft
        X1[Pattern Top Edge] --> X2[Vertical Wall]
        X2 --> X3[Pattern Bottom Edge]
    end
    subgraph Vertical Wall With Draft theta
        Y1[Pattern Top Edge Wider] --> Y2[Tapered Wall at angle theta]
        Y2 --> Y3[Pattern Bottom Edge Narrower]
    end
    X2 -.Problem.-> P1[Sand erosion on withdrawal]
    Y2 -.Solution.-> P2[Smooth pattern withdrawal]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Safety Check

- All node IDs use alphanumeric format (e.g., `node1`, `A`, `B1`, `Q1`).
- All labels are clean uppercase text without markdown bolding or HTML tags inside double-quoted strings.
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node IDs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define pattern allowance. List any four types of pattern allowances used in foundry practice.**

**Model Answer:**

A **pattern allowance** is a deliberate dimensional modification made on a pattern to compensate for the physical and processing phenomena that occur during casting, ensuring the final casting matches the required dimensions.

Four types of pattern allowances are:
1. **Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance** – compensates for metal contraction on cooling.
2. **Draft (Taper) Allowance** – provides taper on vertical surfaces for easy withdrawal.
3. **Machining (Finish) Allowance** – leaves extra material for subsequent machining.
4. **Shake (Rapping) Allowance** – compensates for mold wall expansion when the pattern is rapped for withdrawal.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is draft allowance provided on patterns? Mention typical draft values for wooden and metal patterns.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reason for Draft Allowance:** During withdrawal of the pattern from the sand mold, vertical (non-tapered) surfaces tend to drag sand particles and cause damage (erosion or rupture) to the mold cavity walls. Draft allowance (a slight taper) is therefore provided on all vertical surfaces of the pattern to ensure smooth, damage-free withdrawal.

**Typical Draft Values:**
- **Wooden patterns (hand molding):** $1°$ to $3°$
- **Metal patterns (machine molding):** $0.5°$ to $1°$

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions (Module Internal Choice). Students answer **one** full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various types of pattern allowances used in foundry practice. Mention the typical values for shrinkage allowance of cast iron, steel, and aluminum.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical cast iron (gray) pulley of 300 mm diameter and 150 mm length is to be cast. The shrinkage allowance is 1%, machining allowance is 2 mm on diameter and 1.5 mm on length, and draft is 1°. Calculate the final pattern dimensions.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Types of Pattern Allowances:**

1. **Shrinkage (Contraction) Allowance** – Compensates for linear contraction of metal from solidus to room temperature. Pattern is made larger by $S\%$ of the casting dimension.

2. **Draft (Taper) Allowance** – Taper given on vertical surfaces ($0.5°$ to $3°$) to permit clean withdrawal of the pattern.

3. **Machining (Finish) Allowance** – Extra stock (1.5 mm to 8 mm) provided on surfaces to be machined for final dimensions and surface finish.

4. **Shake (Rapping) Allowance** – Negative allowance (0.5 mm to 1.5 mm) to compensate for mold expansion during pattern rapping.

5. **Distortion (Camber) Allowance** – Pre-set curvature opposite to expected warping in slender/non-symmetrical castings.

6. **Draw (Drawback) Allowance** – Taper on projections/recesses parallel to parting plane to prevent mold damage during withdrawal.

**Typical Shrinkage Values:**
- Gray Cast Iron: $1.0\%$
- Steel: $2.0\%$
- Aluminum Alloys: $1.25\%$ to $1.5\%$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given Data:**
- Casting diameter: $D_c = 300$ mm
- Casting length: $L_c = 150$ mm
- Shrinkage: $S = 1\%$
- Machining (diameter): $2$ mm on radius = $2 \times 2 = 4$ mm on diameter
- Machining (length): $1.5$ mm per side
- Draft: $\theta = 1°$

**Step 1: Pattern Diameter Calculation**

Apply shrinkage:

$$
D_p^{(1)} = D_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right) = 300 \times 1.01 = 303 \text{ mm}
$$

Add machining allowance (4 mm on diameter, already applied to pattern):

$$
D_p^{(2)} = 303 + 4 = 307 \text{ mm}
$$

*(Note: For a cylinder, draft is typically applied only on the outer curved surface as a function of length, not on the diameter directly. We consider the diameter as fixed after shrinkage + machining.)*

**Step 2: Pattern Length Calculation**

Apply shrinkage:

$$
L_p^{(1)} = 150 \times 1.01 = 151.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Add machining allowance (both ends, 1.5 mm each):

$$
L_p^{(2)} = 151.5 + (2 \times 1.5) = 151.5 + 3.0 = 154.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Add draft contribution (both ends):

$$
D_{\text{draft}} = L_p^{(2)} \times \tan(1°) = 154.5 \times 0.01746 \approx 2.70 \text{ mm per side}
$$

Total draft contribution (both ends):

$$
2 \times 2.70 = 5.40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Pattern Length:**

$$
L_p^{\text{final}} = 154.5 + 5.40 = 159.9 \text{ mm} \approx 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Pattern Dimensions:**
- **Pattern Diameter:** $307$ mm
- **Pattern Length:** $\approx 160$ mm

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify and explain different types of patterns used in sand casting. State at least one application of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various materials used for making patterns. Compare the properties of wooden, metal, and plastic patterns in a tabular form.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Classification of Patterns:**

**A. Based on Construction (Solid Patterns):**
1. **Single Piece Pattern** – Made as one unit; used for small, simple castings. *Application:* small flanges, simple brackets.
2. **Two-Piece (Split) Pattern** – Split along a parting line; each half in one cope or drag flask. *Application:* pulleys, gears, medium-complex castings.
3. **Three-Piece / Multi-Piece Pattern** – Divided into three or more parts; used for complex castings requiring intermediate parting. *Application:* intricate pipe fittings.

**B. Based on Mounting / Support:**
4. **Loose Piece Pattern** – Has detachable parts that remain in the sand during pattern withdrawal. *Application:* castings with undercuts.
5. **Follow Board Pattern** – Mounted on a wooden board (follow board) to support loose pieces and form one face of the mold. *Application:* large, flat castings.
6. **Skeleton Pattern** – A wooden frame filled with sand, used for very large castings to economize on material. *Application:* large valve bodies, engine beds.

**C. Based on Mass Production Use:**
7. **Match Plate Pattern** – Pattern halves mounted on opposite sides of a metal plate; mounted on molding machine. *Application:* mass production of small to medium castings.
8. **Cope and Drag Pattern** – Cope and drag halves as separate units for simultaneous molding. *Application:* high-volume automotive castings.

**D. Special Patterns:**
9. **Sweep Pattern** – Swept around an axis to generate a mold cavity. *Application:* large circular castings like wheels.
10. **Segmental Pattern** – Forms only a segment of the casting; full mold made by rotating the pattern. *Application:* large gear blanks.
11. **Boxed Core Pattern** – Used to produce cores for internal cavities. *Application:* engine cylinder bores.

**E. Investment Casting Patterns:**
12. **Wax / Investment Patterns** – Made of wax or polystyrene; used once and melted out. *Application:* precision castings, jewelry, turbine blades.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Pattern Materials and Their Properties:**

| Property | Wood | Metal (Al/Brass/Steel) | Plastic (Epoxy/PU) |
|:---------|:----:|:----------------------:|:------------------:|
| **Density** | Low (0.5–0.8 g/cm³) | High (2.7–8.0 g/cm³) | Low (1.1–1.4 g/cm³) |
| **Machinability** | Excellent (easy to shape) | Difficult (requires tools) | Good (castable) |
| **Strength** | Low–Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Durability (cycles)** | 50–200 | 10,000+ | 500–5,000 |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (porous) | Excellent (smooth) | Very Good |
| **Moisture Absorption** | High (warps, swells) | None | Very Low |
| **Cost (initial)** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **Cost per casting** | High (low life) | Low (high life) | Moderate |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | Low | Very High | High |
| **Repairability** | Easy | Difficult | Moderate |
| **Weight** | Light | Heavy | Light |
| **Common Use** | Prototypes, short runs | Mass production | Medium runs, complex shapes |

**Discussion:**
- **Wood** is economical and easily worked but has limited life and dimensional stability. Best for prototypes and small-batch production.
- **Metal** patterns (aluminum alloys for moderate runs, brass/bronze for high accuracy, steel/cast iron for mass production) offer excellent durability, accuracy, and surface finish, but at higher initial cost.
- **Plastics** (epoxy resins, polyurethane) combine the benefits of wood (lightweight, easy to cast into complex shapes) with better durability and surface finish; ideal for medium-volume production of intricate castings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing volumetric and linear shrinkage:** A common error is to apply volumetric shrinkage (≈ 3S%) directly to linear dimensions. Always use **linear** shrinkage for pattern dimensioning.
> - **Forgetting draft on internal surfaces:** Many students mention draft only for external walls. **Internal** surfaces (cored cavities) also require draft (in the opposite direction).
> - **Mixing up shake and shrinkage:** Shake allowance is **negative** (pattern is *reduced*); shrinkage is **positive** (pattern is *enlarged*). Confusing these leads to wrong pattern dimensions.
> - **Omitting units in tabular answers:** KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in tables and final answers.
> - **Calculation errors in combined allowances:** When multiple allowances act on the same dimension, apply them **sequentially** in the correct order, not as simple additive percentages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Pattern** = a *slightly modified* replica of the casting used to make the mold cavity.
- **Pattern allowances** are NOT defects — they are *intentional, calculated* dimensional compensations.
- **Six core allowances:** Shrinkage, Draft, Machining, Shake, Distortion, Draw.
- **Shrinkage is added on every dimension** (typically 1% for gray CI, 2% for steel, 1.25%–1.5% for aluminum).
- **Draft is added only on vertical surfaces** (away from parting plane externally, toward it internally).
- **Machining allowance is added only on surfaces designated for finish machining.**
- **Shake allowance is negative** (pattern is *reduced* by 0.5–1.5 mm on the rapped dimension).
- **Distortion allowance** is an *opposite curvature* given to slender or non-symmetrical castings.
- **Pattern Length Formula (master equation):** $L_p = L_c \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{100}\right) + 2M + 2 L \tan(\theta)$
- **Pattern materials** are chosen based on **production volume, accuracy, surface finish, and cost**:
   - **Wood** → prototypes, low volume
   - **Metal (Al/Brass/Steel)** → high volume, high accuracy
   - **Plastic (Epoxy/PU)** → medium volume, complex shapes
- **Type selection rule:** Match the pattern type to the **complexity of the casting** and the **production volume**.
- **Valuation tip:** Always show the formula, substitution, and final numerical answer with **units** in KTU exam answers.
- **Common mistake:** Adding percentages directly to each other (e.g., $+1\% + 2\% = +3\%$) instead of computing the effective pattern dimension step by step.
- **Industry link:** Modern foundries use **CAD/CAM-integrated pattern design**, where all allowances are pre-programmed for repeatability and quality control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core and Core Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core and Core Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core and Core Making — Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core (Foundry Definition):** A **core** is a preformed, bonded sand insert placed inside a sand mold cavity to produce the internal surfaces, hollow cavities, undercuts, or intricate internal features of a casting that cannot be formed by the mold alone. The core is held in position by **core prints** (projected seats in the mold) and is surrounded by molten metal during pouring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Making:** The manufacturing process by which cores are produced from a mixture of **silica sand + binder + additives**, compacted into the desired shape using a **core box**, and then hardened (by baking, chemical reaction, or self-setting) to develop sufficient strength for handling, assembly, and metal pouring.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of baking a **layer cake with a filling**:
- The **outer cake pan** = the **mold** (defines the outer shape of the casting).
- The **inner filling** (jam, cream) = the **core** (occupies the space where the metal is *not* supposed to be, so that when removed later, an internal hole/cavity is left behind).

The cake batter (molten metal) cannot be poured and then "carved out" from the center easily — so you place a pre-shaped solid insert (core) into the pan, pour the batter around it, and after baking, you remove the insert (core) just as you would scoop out the jam filling. The hole left behind becomes the **internal cavity of the casting**.

## 1.3 Why Cores Are Needed in Casting

| Situation in Casting | Why a Core is Mandatory |
|---|---|
| Internal cylindrical bore (e.g., engine cylinder) | Pattern cannot withdraw sideways |
| Internal passages (water jacket) | Undercuts inaccessible to pattern |
| Complex internal geometry | Single-piece pattern cannot be made |
| Holes through the casting | Molding without core is impossible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Without cores, only **external shapes** of a casting can be produced by sand molds. **All internal cavities require cores** — making core making an indispensable part of foundry practice.

## 1.4 Standard Foundry Metrics for Cores

The following numerical/physical specifications are universally used in foundry practice and **must be memorized** for KTU exams:

- **AFS Grain Fineness Number (GFN):** 50–100 for foundry sands (cores typically use **AFS 70–100** for fine detail).
- **Moisture content in baked cores:** **0%** (cores are essentially *dry*).
- **Tensile strength of baked oil-sand core:** **14–28 kg/cm² (1.4–2.8 MPa)**.
- **Permeability Number:** **80–200** for most core sands.
- **Refractoriness:** Must withstand pouring temperatures of **1400–1550 °C** for steel, **1100–1200 °C** for cast iron.
- **Standard test specimens:** Cylindrical, **50 mm dia × 50 mm length** for compression; **Dog-bone shape** for tensile.

## 1.5 Basic Terminology Used in Core Making

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Core Box** | Wooden or metal tool used to shape the core (analogous to a "mold for the mold") |
| **Core Print** | Projected seat machined on the pattern that creates a cavity in the mold to support the core |
| **Chaplet** | Metallic support placed inside the mold to hold the core against buoyant force of molten metal |
| **Vents** | Small holes/ducts provided in the core to allow escape of gases during pouring |
| **Core Sand** | Special sand mixture formulated for high strength and permeability |
| **Binder** | Bonding agent (oil, resin, sodium silicate) that holds sand grains together |
| **Green Strength** | Strength of a freshly rammed (unbaked) core |
| **Dry Strength** | Strength after baking/curing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Properties Required of a Good Core

A core must simultaneously satisfy **seven conflicting demands** — this is the central engineering challenge of core making:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "The 7 R's of an Ideal Core":**
> 1. **Refractoriness** — resist molten metal temperature
> 2. **Resistance** (Strength) — withstand handling + metallostatic pressure
> 3. **Permeability** — let gases escape
> 4. **Rigidity** + **Collapsibility** (paradoxical!) — strong during pour, but **collapse easily** after solidification to avoid hot tearing
> 5. **Reusability** of sand (in some processes)
> 6. **Residual** strength control
> 7. **Resistance** to moisture pickup (storage stability)

## 2.2 Ingredients of Core Sand — Component-Wise Analysis

| Ingredient | Function | Typical % by weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | Refractory base; high melting point (~1710 °C) | **85–95%** |
| **Binder (Oil / Resin / Sodium silicate)** | Bonds grains together; provides strength | **1–5%** |
| **Cereal / Starch** | Improves green strength and collapsibility | **0.5–2%** |
| **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃)** | Prevents metal penetration; reduces burn-on | **0.5–1.5%** |
| **Dextrin** | Increases dry strength | **0.5–1%** |
| **Moisture (water)** | Activates clay, aids tempering (in green cores) | **3–6%** |
| **Sea Coal / Pitch** | Improves surface finish, reduces veining | **0–2%** |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Core Making Process Flow

1. **Sand Preparation:** Silica sand is dried, sieved, and mulled with binder and additives in a **core sand muller** (3–5 minutes mixing).
2. **Core Blowing/Ramming:** The mixture is deposited into a **core box** and compacted by:
   - Hand ramming (small cores)
   - Core blowing (pneumatic injection)
   - Core shooting (machine)
   - Hot box / shell process
3. **Stripping:** Compacted core is removed from the box.
4. **Hardening/Curing:**
   - **Baking** in an oven at **200–250 °C for 1–3 hours** (oil-bonded)
   - **Chemical hardening** (CO₂ process, cold box, no-bake)
5. **Inspection & Storage:** Cores are checked for cracks, vents are added if missing.
6. **Placement in Mold:** Core is set into the core prints; chaplets placed; vents connected.
7. **Pouring & Shake-out:** Molten metal poured; after solidification, core is broken up and removed.

## 2.4 Classification of Cores

### A) Based on Binder System

| Type | Binder | Curing | Strength Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Green-sand core** | Clay + water | Self-set (wet) | Clay-water bond |
| **Dry-sand core (oil-sand core)** | Linseed oil / Cereal | **Oven baked at 200–250 °C** | Oxidative polymerization of oil |
| **Shell core** | Phenolic resin (Novolac) | **Hot box at 200–300 °C** | Thermoset resin bond |
| **Hot-box core** | Furan / Phenolic resin + catalyst | **Hot box at 180–260 °C** | Thermoset resin |
| **Cold-box core** | Phenolic-isocyanate + amine gas | **Gas cured at room temp** | Polyurethane bond |
| **CO₂ core (Silicate core)** | Sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃) | **CO₂ gas at room temp** | Silica gel formation |
| **No-bake core** | Furan resin + acid catalyst | **Self-set at room temp** | Acid-catalyzed polymerization |

### B) Based on Shape/Position

- **Horizontal core** — laid flat in mold.
- **Vertical core** — hangs/stood vertically (needs chaplets for support).
- **Balanced core** — symmetrical, self-supporting.
- **Stub/cantilever core** — extends from one side only.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are mathematically derived from standard foundry practice. Units are SI unless specified.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$ \text{GFN} = \frac{\sum (m_i \cdot f_i)}{\sum m_i} $$ | **AFS Grain Fineness Number** — weighted average sieve size |
| 2 | $$ P = \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P} $$ | **Permeability number** (volume V of air through height h in time t) |
| 3 | $$ S_c = \frac{F}{A} $$ | **Compressive strength** = Load / Cross-sectional area |
| 4 | $$ S_t = \frac{F}{A_{min}} $$ | **Tensile strength** of core |
| 5 | $$ M\% = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 $$ | **Moisture content** (% by dry weight) |
| 6 | $$ F_{buoy} = \rho_{metal} \cdot g \cdot V_{core} $$ | **Buoyant force on core** in molten metal |
| 7 | $$ \sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T $$ | **Thermal stress** during heating |
| 8 | $$ V_{gas} = \frac{P \cdot A_{vent} \cdot t_{pour}}{R \cdot T} $$ | **Gas volume** evolved during pour |
| 9 | $$ \text{Loss on Ignition (LOI)} = \frac{W_i - W_f}{W_i} \times 100 $$ | % Combustibles in core sand |
| 10 | $$ \text{Compactability} = \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}} \times 100\% $$ | Degree of sand compaction |

**Where:**
- $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$; $f_i$ = sieve size factor
- $V$ = volume of air (cm³); $h$ = sample height (cm); $A$ = area (cm²); $t$ = time (min)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure differential (cm of water)
- $W_w, W_d$ = wet and dry weight of sand
- $\rho_{metal}, \rho_{core}$ = density of molten metal, baked core
- $E, \alpha, \Delta T$ = Young's modulus, thermal expansion coefficient, temperature rise
- $\rho_b, \rho_t$ = bulk and true density of compacted sand

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Foundry Application | Core Type Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine cylinder block** (cast iron) | Oil-sand / Cold-box core | High strength, good collapsibility, intricate water jackets |
| **Pipe fittings** (small castings) | Shell core | Dimensional accuracy, mass production |
| **Steel casting large hub** | CO₂ silicate core | High refractoriness, fast cure, no oven |
| **Hydraulic valve body** | No-bake furan core | Complex internal passages, low volume |
| **Pump impeller** | Hot-box resin core | Fine surface, sharp internal features |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — AFS Grain Fineness Number (GFN)

The AFS Grain Fineness Number is the **weighted mean sieve size** of a sand sample. It is the **single most important parameter** for classifying core sand.

**Step 1 — Sieve Analysis Setup:**
A standard **AFS sieve set** consists of 11 sieves from mesh **No. 6 (3360 μm)** down to **No. 270 (53 μm)**. Each sieve is assigned a "sieve factor" $f_i$ from a standard AFS table.

**Step 2 — Weighing:**
The mass of sand retained on each sieve ($m_i$) is recorded in grams. Total mass = $\sum m_i$.

**Step 3 — Multiplication:**
For each sieve, compute the **product** $m_i \cdot f_i$ using the table values of $f_i$.

**Step 4 — Summation and Division:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GFN} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot f_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **higher GFN** = **finer sand** (better surface finish, lower permeability). A **lower GFN** = **coarser sand** (higher permeability, rougher surface). Core sands typically use **GFN 70–100**.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Suppose 100 g of core sand is sieved with the following results (simplified):

| Sieve No. | Sieve Factor $f_i$ | Mass retained $m_i$ (g) | $m_i \cdot f_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30 | 30 | 5 | 150 |
| 40 | 40 | 15 | 600 |
| 50 | 50 | 30 | 1500 |
| 70 | 70 | 25 | 1750 |
| 100 | 100 | 15 | 1500 |
| 140 | 140 | 7 | 980 |
| 200 | 200 | 2 | 400 |
| Pan | 300 | 1 | 300 |
| **Total** | — | **100** | **7180** |

Applying the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GFN} &= \frac{7180}{100} = 71.8 \approx 72
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **medium-fine core sand** — ideal for medium-size castings.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Permeability Number

**Permeability** is the ability of a sand mass to allow gases to escape. The numerical **permeability number** $P$ is defined so that **higher $P$ = more permeable**.

**Step 1 — Standard Test Setup:**
A core sand specimen of **height $h$ (cm)** and **cross-section $A$ (cm²)** is held in a permeability meter. Air at pressure $\Delta P$ (cm of water) is forced through the sample.

**Step 2 — Volume–Time Measurement:**
A volume $V$ (cm³) of air passes through the sample in time $t$ (minutes).

**Step 3 — Definition of Permeability Number:**
The AFS Permeability Number is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{V \cdot h}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Dimensionless Form:**
With $V$ in cm³, $h$ in cm, $A$ in cm², $t$ in min, $\Delta P$ in cm of water — the units cancel, leaving a **dimensionless** number.

**Numerical Example:**
A core sample has $h = 5.08$ cm, $A = 20.27$ cm². Air volume of 2000 cm³ passes in $t = 4$ min under $\Delta P = 10$ cm water.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2000 \times 5.08}{20.27 \times 4 \times 10} \\
  &= \frac{10160}{810.8} \\
  &= 12.53
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounded off, **$P \approx 13$** — low permeability; this core would need **vents** during pouring.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buoyant Force on a Core (Chaplet Design)

When molten metal surrounds a core, the core experiences an upward **buoyant force** equal to the weight of displaced metal. This force must be supported by chaplets and core prints.

**Step 1 — Definition of Buoyancy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{buoy} &= \rho_{metal} \cdot g \cdot V_{core}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Upward Force:**
The downward support is from the weight of the core itself:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{core} &= \rho_{core} \cdot g \cdot V_{core}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Force to be Supported by Chaplets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{chaplet} &= F_{buoy} - W_{core} \\
            &= g \cdot V_{core} \cdot (\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Chaplets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{F_{chaplet}}{F_{single \, chaplet}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example — A Cylindrical Core in Cast Iron:**
- Core: cylinder, $D = 0.10$ m, $L = 0.20$ m → $V_{core} = \pi (0.05)^2 (0.20) = 1.571 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- Density of baked oil-sand core: $\rho_{core} = 1500$ kg/m³
- Density of molten cast iron: $\rho_{metal} = 7000$ kg/m³
- Each chaplet can support: 100 N

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{chaplet} &= 9.81 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \times (7000 - 1500) \\
            &= 9.81 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \times 5500 \\
            &= 84.79 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{84.79}{100} = 0.85 \rightarrow 1 \text{ chaplet minimum}
\end{aligned}
$$

For safety (factor of 1.5–2), use **2 chaplets** symmetrically placed.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Moisture Content Determination

**Step 1 — Weight Measurement:**
- Initial weight of wet core sand: $W_w$ (with pan)
- Pan weight alone: $W_p$
- After oven drying at 110 °C for 1 hour: $W_d$ (with pan)

**Step 2 — Net Weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net,wet} &= W_w - W_p \\
W_{net,dry} &= W_d - W_p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Moisture % Formula (by dry weight):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M\% &= \frac{W_{net,wet} - W_{net,dry}}{W_{net,dry}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
- $W_w = 102$ g, $W_p = 22$ g, $W_d = 92$ g

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net,wet} &= 102 - 22 = 80 \text{ g} \\
W_{net,dry} &= 92 - 22 = 70 \text{ g} \\
M\% &= \frac{80 - 70}{70} \times 100 = 14.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** For **baked cores**, $M\% \approx 0$ (oven-dried). For **green-sand cores**, $M\% = 3-6\%$. Always report **which basis** the % is on — *wet basis* or *dry basis* — in your exam answer.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Core Sand Property vs Process Selection

| Property | Green-Sand Core | Oil-Sand Core (Baked) | CO₂ Silicate Core | Shell Core | Cold-Box Core |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strength** | Low | **High** | High | Very High | Very High |
| **Permeability** | High | **Medium** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Collapsibility** | **Excellent** | Good | Poor (may cause hot tears) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Surface Finish** | Poor | Good | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| **Cure Time** | None | 1–3 hours (oven) | **Seconds** (CO₂) | 30–60 s (hot) | Seconds (gas) |
| **Storage** | Cannot store | **Stable** | Hygroscopic (absorbs moisture) | Stable | Stable |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| **Best For** | Small, simple | **General purpose** | Large steel castings | Mass production | Mass production |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Core in a Sand Mold (Functional Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the **position of a core inside a two-part sand mold**, showing core prints, chaplets, vents, and the resulting internal cavity in the casting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP_HALF ["TOP HALF OF MOLD - COPE"]
        CopeP["Cope Half - Drag Part"]
        PouringCup["Pouring Cup and Sprue"]
        Riser["Riser"]
    end

    subgraph CORE_REGION ["CORE ASSEMBLY ZONE"]
        direction TB
        CoreBox["Core Box Tool - Shapes the Core"]
        CoreSand["Baked Core - Oil Sand or Resin Bonded"]
        CorePrintA["Upper Core Print - Seating in Cope"]
        CorePrintB["Lower Core Print - Seating in Drag"]
        Chaplet1["Chaplet 1 - Metallic Support"]
        Chaplet2["Chaplet 2 - Metallic Support"]
        VentHole["Vent Hole in Core - Gas Escape"]
    end

    subgraph BOTTOM_HALF ["BOTTOM HALF OF MOLD - DRAG"]
        DragC["Drag Half - Cavity Wall"]
    end

    subgraph FINAL ["FINAL CASTING PRODUCT"]
        CastOuter["Outer Surface of Casting"]
        InnerHole["Internal Hollow Cavity Formed by Core"]
    end

    CoreBox --> CoreSand
    CoreSand --> CorePrintA
    CoreSand --> CorePrintB
    Chaplet1 --> CoreSand
    Chaplet2 --> CoreSand
    VentHole --> CoreSand
    PouringCup --> CopeP
    CopeP --> CorePrintA
    CorePrintB --> DragC
    CoreSand --> InnerHole
    InnerHole --> CastOuter

    style CORE_REGION fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style CoreSand fill:#f5b041,stroke:#7e5109,stroke-width:3px
    style Chaplet1 fill:#aab7b8,stroke:#1b2631
    style Chaplet2 fill:#aab7b8,stroke:#1b2631
    style VentHole fill:#aab7b8,stroke:#1b2631
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Core Making Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Raw Silica Sand - SiO2 99 percent"] --> B["Drying in Sand Dryer 110 C"]
    B --> C["Sieve Classification - AFS Sieve Set"]
    C --> D["Muller Mixing - Sand plus Binder plus Additives"]
    D --> E["Core Box Filling - Hand or Machine"]
    E --> F["Compaction - Ramming or Blowing or Shooting"]
    F --> G["Stripping - Remove Core from Box"]
    G --> H{"Select Curing Process"}
    H --> I["Oven Baking 200 to 250 C for 1 to 3 hr - Oil Sand Core"]
    H --> J["CO2 Gas Curing 30 s - Sodium Silicate Core"]
    H --> K["Hot Box 200 to 300 C - Resin Core"]
    H --> L["Gas Curing Room Temp - Cold Box Core"]

    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Storage in Dry Area"]
    N --> O["Placement in Mold on Core Prints"]
    O --> P["Pouring of Molten Metal"]
    P --> Q["Solidification and Shake Out"]
    Q --> R["Core Removed - Internal Cavity Exposed"]

    style H fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px

```

        Fprint["Fprint - Reaction from Core Prints"]
