# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a core in foundry practice. List any four essential properties of a good core sand.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A core is a preformed sand insert placed inside a sand mold cavity to produce internal cavities, hollow sections, or undercuts in a casting that cannot be obtained from the pattern alone.

> **Four essential properties:**
> 1. **High refractoriness** — to withstand molten metal temperature.
> 2. **High strength** — to resist handling and metallostatic pressure.
> 3. **Adequate permeability** — to allow gases to escape during pouring.
> 4. **Good collapsibility** — to allow easy shake-out after solidification without causing hot tears in the casting.

*(Word limit: 50–70 words for full 3 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between green-sand core and dry-sand (oil-bonded) core. (Any 4 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Green-Sand Core | Dry-Sand (Oil-Bonded) Core |
|---|---|---|
| **Binder** | Clay + water | Linseed / vegetable oil |
| **Moisture content** | 3–6 % | ~0 % (oven baked) |
| **Curing** | Self-set (no oven) | **Oven baked at 200–250 °C** |
| **Strength** | Low (green strength only) | High (dry strength) |
| **Storage** | Cannot store; use immediately | Can be stored indefinitely |
| **Surface finish** | Rough | Smooth, accurate |
| **Application** | Simple internal holes | Complex, intricate cores |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When asked about **properties of core sand**, students often confuse *green-sand* properties (used for molds) with *core-sand* properties — these are **different**. Core sand emphasizes **high strength + permeability + collapsibility** in **dry state**, not green state.
> - In **chaplet design** numericals, students forget to **subtract the weight of the core** from buoyancy — losing 2 marks.
> - For **CO₂ process**, students must explicitly state the **chemical reaction** $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \text{ (gel)}$ — not just "CO₂ is passed."
> - **Always report** moisture % on either "wet basis" or "dry basis" — examiners deduct 1 mark if unspecified.
> - Numerical answers must show **unit conversion and substitution step** — skipping these costs 1–2 marks in the 14-mark paper.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q (a)** Explain the various ingredients used in core sand with their functions. List the **seven essential properties** required of a good core sand. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A cylindrical oil-sand core of diameter 120 mm and length 250 mm is to be placed in a cast-iron casting. The density of the baked core is 1500 kg/m³ and density of molten cast iron is 7000 kg/m³. If each chaplet can support a load of 120 N, calculate the **minimum number of chaplets** required. Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and a safety factor of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Ingredients & Properties (7 marks)

**Ingredients of Core Sand:**

| Ingredient | Function | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| **Silica sand (SiO₂)** | High refractoriness (m.p. 1710 °C); base material | 85–95 % |
| **Binder (oil/resin/silicate)** | Bonds sand grains; provides strength | 1–5 % |
| **Cereal (corn flour)** | Adds green strength, improves collapsibility | 0.5–2 % |
| **Iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** | Reduces metal penetration; prevents burn-on | 0.5–1.5 % |
| **Dextrin** | Increases dry strength; aids surface finish | 0.5–1 % |
| **Water** | Activates additives; aids mixing (in some systems) | 3–6 % (green cores) |
| **Sea coal / pitch** | Reduces veining, improves peel | 0–2 % |

**Seven Essential Properties of Good Core Sand:**
1. **Refractoriness** — withstands metal temperature without fusion.
2. **High strength (dry)** — resists handling and metal pressure.
3. **Adequate permeability** — allows gases to escape.
4. **Good collapsibility** — collapses easily after solidification.
5. **Moisture resistance** — does not absorb atmospheric humidity.
6. **Easy collapsibility + shake-out** — doesn't cause hot tears.
7. **Reusability / economic** — low binder consumption, recyclable.

*(Expected answer length: 250–300 words for full 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Chaplet Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Core: cylindrical, $D = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m, $L = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m
- $\rho_{core} = 1500$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{metal} = 7000$ kg/m³
- $F_{chaplet} = 120$ N (single)
- Safety factor (SF) $= 1.5$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Volume of Core:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{core} &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.12)^2 (0.25) \\
         &= 0.7854 \times 0.0144 \times 0.25 \\
         &= 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Buoyant Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{buoy} &= \rho_{metal} \cdot g \cdot V_{core} \\
         &= 7000 \times 9.81 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \\
         &= 194.18 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weight of Core:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{core} &= \rho_{core} \cdot g \cdot V_{core} \\
         &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \\
         &= 41.61 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Upward Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= F_{buoy} - W_{core} = 194.18 - 41.61 = 152.57 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Safety Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{design} &= F_{net} \times SF = 152.57 \times 1.5 = 228.85 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of Chaplets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{F_{design}}{F_{chaplet}} = \frac{228.85}{120} = 1.91
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer (rounded up), $N = 2$ chaplets.

**Final Answer:** **2 chaplets** are required to safely support the core.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**Q (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **CO₂ silicate core process**. Write the chemical reaction involved and list its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Describe the **cold-box core making process**. What is the **binder system** used, and what makes this process suitable for **high-volume production**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — CO₂ Silicate Core Process (7 marks)

**Process Description:**
In the CO₂ process, **sodium silicate (water glass, Na₂SiO₃)** is used as the binder. The sand–sodium-silicate mixture is compacted into the core box, and then **CO₂ gas is passed through the core for 15–30 seconds**. CO₂ reacts with sodium silicate to form a **silica gel (SiO₂·H₂O)** that bonds the sand grains strongly.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{SiO}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O (gel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The silica gel forms thin films around sand grains and hardens, giving the core instant strength.

**Procedure:**
1. Mix **3–5% sodium silicate** with dry silica sand.
2. Ram into core box.
3. Pass CO₂ gas (pressure 1.5–2.5 bar) through vents for 15–30 s.
4. Core hardens instantly; strip from box; use immediately or store.

**Advantages:**
1. **No oven required** — fast curing, low energy.
2. **High strength** developed almost instantly.
3. **Good dimensional accuracy** and surface finish.
4. **High refractoriness** — suitable for steel and high-temperature alloys.
5. **Low cost** of binder (sodium silicate is cheap).
6. **No toxic fumes** during curing.

**Limitations:**
1. **Poor collapsibility** — the silica gel bond is brittle; difficult shake-out; can cause **hot tears** in the casting.
2. **Hygroscopic** — absorbs atmospheric moisture if stored long → loses strength.
3. **Long-term storage** is not possible; must be used within hours/days.
4. **Sodium residue** may cause casting defects (e.g., veining, metal penetration) in some alloys.
5. Not ideal for thin, delicate cores due to brittleness.

*(Expected answer length: 300–350 words for full 7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Cold-Box Core Process (7 marks)

**Process Description:**
The cold-box process is a **room-temperature, gas-cured** core-making process that uses a **two-part binder system** hardened by passing a **gaseous catalyst (tertiary amine vapor)** through the compacted sand.

**Binder System:**
- **Part I — Phenolic resin** (phenol-formaldehyde, base-catalyzed) — mixed with sand.
- **Part II — Polyisocyanate (MDI — methylene diphenyl diisocyanate)** — second resin component.
- **Catalyst gas — Triethylamine (TEA) or dimethylethylamine (DMEA)** — vaporized and blown through the core.

**Chemical Reaction:**
The amine gas catalyzes the reaction between phenolic and isocyanate resins to form a **polyurethane (PU) bond** that hardens the core within seconds at room temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R-OH (Phenolic)} + \text{R'-NCO (Isocyanate)} \xrightarrow{\text{Amine catalyst}} \text{R-O-CO-NH-R' (Polyurethane)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why Suitable for High-Volume Production:**
1. **Cycle time 10–30 seconds** — extremely fast curing.
2. **No oven** — saves energy, floor space.
3. **Excellent dimensional accuracy** — cores do not distort on heating.
4. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — thin, intricate cores possible.
5. **Good shake-out** — PU bond decomposes cleanly on cooling.
6. **Amines recovered** by exhaust scrubbers; environmentally manageable.
7. **Compatible with automation** — robotic core handling, conveyorized production lines.

**Limitations:**
1. **Binder is expensive** (MDI cost).
2. **Amines are toxic and odorous** — require exhaust systems.
3. **Sand reclamation** is more difficult.

*(Expected answer length: 300–350 words for full 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Hit List for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Core definition:** preformed sand insert for internal cavities; held by **core prints**; supported by **chaplets**; vented for **gas escape**.
- [ ] **Core sand ingredients (5 essential):** silica sand (85–95%), binder (1–5%), cereal, iron oxide, dextrin + water (only for green cores).
- [ ] **7 R's of an ideal core:** Refractoriness, Resistance (strength), Permeability, Rigidity + Collapsibility, Reusability, Residual control, Resistance to moisture.
- [ ] **GFN formula** — weighted sieve factor average; **AFS 70–100** for core sand.
- [ ] **Permeability number** — $P = Vh/(A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P)$ — dimensionless.
- [ ] **Buoyancy vs core weight** — Net upward force $F = gV(\rho_{metal} - \rho_{core})$ → number of chaplets.
- [ ] **Moisture formula** — $(W_w - W_d)/W_d \times 100\%$ (dry basis) or $(W_w - W_d)/W_w \times 100\%$ (wet basis).
- [ ] **Oil-sand core** = oven-baked at **200–250 °C for 1–3 hr**; binder is **linseed oil** (drying oil, oxidative polymerization).
- [ ] **CO₂ process** = sodium silicate + CO₂ → silica gel; **instant cure, no oven**; poor collapsibility, hygroscopic.
- [ ] **Shell core** = **phenolic Novolac resin** + hot box (**200–300 °C**); best surface finish.
- [ ] **Hot-box core** = **furan/phenolic resin + acid catalyst** + heat (**180–260 °C**).
- [ ] **Cold-box core** = phenolic + isocyanate + **amine gas (TEA)** → polyurethane; **room temp cure**; mass production.
- [ ] **No-bake core** = furan resin + acid catalyst → self-set at room temperature.
- [ ] **Vents in cores** are mandatory to prevent **blow holes** and **gas porosity**.
- [ ] **Chaplets** must be **same alloy or compatible** with casting metal to avoid contamination.
- [ ] **Compromising property paradox:** Core must be **strong during pour** but **collapse easily after solidification** — solved by using **organic binders** (decompose on heating) rather than inorganic ones.
- [ ] **Exam trap:** Never confuse **core sand** (high dry strength, permeability) with **molding sand** (high green strength, moldability). They are **not interchangeable**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Casting Design Considerations; Casting Processes; Methods of Melting - Crucible Melting and Cupola Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Casting Design Considerations; Casting Processes; Methods of Melting - Crucible Melting and Cupola Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Casting?

**Casting** is a primary metal forming manufacturing process in which a molten metal (or alloy) is poured into a refractory mold cavity of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then extracted to yield a near-net-shape component. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines casting under the **"Solidification Processing"** domain, classifying it as a *near-net-shape, material-additive* process governed by fluid flow, heat transfer, and metallurgical solidification principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** The KTU syllabus (PBAUT404, Module 1) treats casting as the *gateway* manufacturing process — over **90% of manufactured goods** contain at least one cast component, especially in the automotive, aerospace, and power-plant sectors.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Wax & Jello" Model

Imagine you have a kitchen mold (like a chocolate bunny mold). You pour hot melted chocolate inside, wait for it to solidify, then open the mold to reveal the bunny. That is **casting in its simplest form**. 

- The **mold** = your refractory flask + pattern.
- The **molten metal** = the melted chocolate.
- The **solidification** = the chocolate cooling in the fridge.
- The **draft angle** = the slight taper on the mold that lets you pop the bunny out cleanly. If the mold had zero taper, the bunny would get stuck and break — *exactly what happens in casting if draft is not provided.*

---

## 1.2 Why Casting? The Engineering Justification

| Property | Casting Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Shape Complexity** | Internal cavities, complex contours |
| **Size Range** | Few grams (jewelry) → 200+ tonnes (propeller shafts) |
| **Material Versatility** | Ferrous, non-ferrous, alloys, even polymers |
| **Tooling Cost** | Low for sand casting (vs. forging dies) |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Directional, isotropic, or anisotropic (controlled) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The module explicitly emphasizes that the *design of the casting* is as important as the *process of casting* — a poorly designed casting cannot be salvaged by superior process control.

---

## 1.3 Casting Design Considerations — The 12 Golden Rules

A KTU examiner will expect you to know that **casting design** is governed by *DFM (Design for Manufacturability)* principles. The 12 considerations that govern every cast component are:

1. **Draft (Taper)** — for clean pattern withdrawal.
2. **Parting Line** — location dictates mold complexity.
3. **Fillet & Corner Radii** — to eliminate stress raisers.
4. **Uniform Wall Thickness** — to prevent shrinkage voids.
5. **Ribs & Bosses** — for stiffness and assembly features.
6. **Cores** — for internal cavities.
7. **Machining Allowance** — extra stock for finish machining.
8. **Shrinkage Allowance** — compensation for metal contraction.
9. **Distortion Allowance** — for non-uniform cooling.
10. **Gating & Riser Placement** — feeding molten metal.
11. **Rounding of Edges** — improves flow and reduces hot tears.
12. **Avoid Large Flat Surfaces** — promotes directional solidification.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *"Draft, Parting, Fillet, Uniform, Ribs, Core, Machine, Shrink, Distort, Gate, Round, Avoid"* → 12 letters. Use the first letters: **D-P-F-U-R-C-M-S-D-G-R-A**.

---

## 1.4 Methods of Melting — At a Glance

The two principal methods covered in the KTU Module 1 syllabus are:

- **Crucible Melting:** A *batch* process where metal is melted inside a refractory container (crucible) heated externally. Used for non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Zn, Mg) and high-grade ferrous alloys.
- **Cupola Melting:** A *continuous* shaft furnace used primarily for melting **cast iron** (Fe + C + Si) in foundries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Melting Matters:** The KTU 2024 syllabus stresses that the choice of melting method determines *metal cleanliness, melt temperature control, metallurgical consistency*, and ultimately the *mechanical properties* of the cast product.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Casting Design Considerations

### 2.1.1 Draft Angle (Taper)

Draft is the slight taper provided on vertical faces of a pattern to facilitate its easy withdrawal from the sand mold **without damaging the mold walls**.

$$\text{Draft} = \text{Taper angle} = \theta \quad (\text{typically } 1° \text{ to } 3°)$$

The **draft length** on the pattern side is:

$$L_{draft} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

where:
- $L$ = vertical height of the pattern face (in **mm**).
- $\theta$ = draft angle (in **degrees**).

**Engineering utility:** Without draft, the green sand would shear off the pattern surface, leaving loose sand particles that cause **sand inclusions** and **surface defects** in the final casting. KTU questions frequently ask: *"Why is draft given on internal surfaces only when absolutely necessary?"* The answer: *excess draft on internal features reduces the casting's dimensional accuracy*.

---

### 2.1.2 Parting Line

The **parting line** is the line of separation between the two mold halves (cope and drag). It is the most critical design decision because:

- It determines the **number of mold parts** required.
- It dictates the **placement of cores** and **gating system**.
- It affects the **dimensional accuracy** of the final casting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners love asking *"How is the parting line decided?"* The official answer: *It should be placed at the maximum cross-section of the casting to minimize mold complexity, and the pattern should be split such that the larger portion is in the drag (lower flask) to prevent the pattern from falling under gravity.*

---

### 2.1.3 Fillets and Corner Radii

**Fillets** are rounded internal corners; **chamfers** are rounded external edges. Their radii are chosen to be **1.5× to 2× the wall thickness** for general design, but can be larger in highly stressed regions.

**Why fillets are essential:**
1. Eliminate **stress concentration** at sharp corners.
2. Prevent **hot tears** (cracks formed during solidification due to differential contraction).
3. Improve **metal flow** during pouring.
4. Reduce **mold erosion** at sharp corners.

---

### 2.1.4 Uniform Wall Thickness

A casting with uniform wall thickness cools evenly, minimizing internal stresses and shrinkage voids. The **Chvorinov's Rule** (used in KTU Module 1) governs solidification time:

$$t_s = K \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

where:
- $t_s$ = solidification time (in **seconds**).
- $V$ = volume of the casting (in **m³** or **cm³**).
- $A$ = surface area of the casting (in **m²** or **cm²**).
- $K$ = mold constant (depends on mold material, metal superheat).
- $n$ = typically $\approx 2$ for most metals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Standard):** A casting with thicker sections at the top and thinner at the bottom promotes *directional solidification*, which naturally feeds shrinkage. Reverse this, and you get **shrinkage porosity** — a casting defect the examiner will use as a question.

---

### 2.1.5 Shrinkage Allowance

Metals **contract** during solidification and subsequent cooling. The pattern must be made **larger** to compensate.

$$\text{Shrinkage Allowance} = L \cdot S$$

where:
- $L$ = original linear dimension of the casting (in **mm**).
- $S$ = shrinkage factor (fraction, e.g., **0.01** for 1% shrinkage).

**Standard shrinkage values (KTU reference):**

| Metal | Linear Shrinkage |
|---|---|
| Cast Iron (Gray) | **0.8% – 1.0%** |
| Cast Iron (White) | **1.5% – 2.0%** |
| Steel Castings | **1.5% – 2.0%** |
| Aluminum Alloys | **1.0% – 1.5%** |
| Copper Alloys (Bronze) | **1.5% – 2.0%** |
| Zinc Alloys | **0.5% – 0.7%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **pattern shrinkage allowance** with **riser size**. The pattern is enlarged to allow for shrinkage, but the **riser** must be the *last* region to solidify to *feed* the shrinkage of the casting body.

---

### 2.1.6 Machining Allowance

This is the extra stock left on surfaces that will undergo subsequent machining. It depends on:

- Casting process (sand casting needs more allowance than investment casting).
- Casting size (larger castings need more allowance).
- Machining method (CNC vs. manual).

**Typical values:**

| Process | Machining Allowance (mm/side) |
|---|---|
| Sand Casting | **2.0 – 4.0 mm** |
| Shell Molding | **1.0 – 2.5 mm** |
| Investment Casting | **0.0 – 0.5 mm** |
| Die Casting | **0.2 – 0.5 mm** |

---

## 2.2 Casting Processes — Comprehensive Breakdown

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies casting processes into **expendable-mold** and **permanent-mold** categories.

### 2.2.1 Expendable-Mold Processes

| Process | Mold Material | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Sand Casting** | Green sand / dry sand | Large ferrous castings (engine blocks) |
| **Shell Molding (Croning)** | Resin-bonded sand | Small to medium precision parts |
| **Investment Casting (Lost-Wax)** | Ceramic shell | Aerospace, jewelry, turbine blades |
| **Plaster Mold Casting** | Gypsum plaster | Aluminum, magnesium prototypes |
| **Ceramic Mold Casting** | Refractory ceramics | Tool steel, high-temp alloys |

### 2.2.2 Permanent-Mold Processes

| Process | Mold Material | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Die Casting (Hot Chamber)** | Steel dies | Zinc, magnesium, low-melting alloys |
| **Die Casting (Cold Chamber)** | Steel dies | Aluminum, brass alloys |
| **Permanent Mold Casting** | Cast iron / steel | Pistons, gears, automotive wheels |
| **Centrifugal Casting** | Rotating mold | Pipes, tubes, cylindrical parts |
| **Continuous Casting** | Water-cooled copper mold | Steel billets, slabs, blooms |
| **Squeeze Casting** | Hydraulic press + die | Aluminum automotive parts (forging-grade) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Favorite:** *Centrifugal casting* and *Investment casting* are the two most asked processes in KTU ESE (End Semester Exam). Memorize their *principle, mold material, products, advantages, and limitations*.

---

### 2.2.3 Special Casting Processes (KTU Module 1 Brief)

- **Vacuum Casting:** Used for parts requiring low gas porosity (e.g., superalloy turbine blades).
- **Slush Casting:** Produces hollow castings (e.g., decorative items, lamp posts). Molten metal is held for a controlled time, then poured out — the solidified skin forms the hollow shell.
- **Die Casting Variants:** Low-pressure die casting, high-pressure die casting, and gravity die casting — each with distinct pressure regimes.

---

## 2.3 Methods of Melting — Crucible Melting

### 2.3.1 The Crucible Furnace

A **crucible** is a refractory container (made of clay-graphite, silicon carbide, or cast iron) that holds the metal to be melted. The crucible is heated **externally** by a furnace — the metal does not contact the combustion gases directly.

**Standard refractory properties required:**
- High melting point (**>1800 °C** for ferrous).
- Low thermal expansion.
- High thermal shock resistance.
- Chemical inertness to the molten metal.

**Fuel sources for crucible furnaces:**

| Fuel Type | Application |
|---|---|
| **Coke/Oil** | Small foundries, ferrous alloys |
| **Natural Gas** | Most common in modern foundries |
| **Electricity (Induction)** | High-purity, contamination-free melts |
| **Coal** | Older / rural foundries |

### 2.3.2 Types of Crucible Furnaces

1. **Pit Crucible Furnace:** The crucible sits in a pit below floor level. Charged from above, tapped from below. Used in small-scale operations.
2. **Stationary Crucible Furnace:** The crucible sits on a stand inside a fixed furnace. Metal is *ladled* out for pouring.
3. **Tilting Crucible Furnace:** The entire furnace tilts hydraulically to pour metal directly into the mold. Used in aluminum and copper foundries.
4. **Bale-out Furnace:** A stationary furnace where metal is *bailed* out with a hand ladle — used for small batches of high-melting-point alloys.

### 2.3.3 Crucible Melting — Operation Sequence

1. **Preheating the crucible:** Slowly to **150 – 200 °C** to remove moisture (prevents steam explosions).
2. **Charging:** Solid metal is added in a specific order — *heavy scrap at the bottom, light scrap on top* to avoid crucible damage.
3. **Melting:** Heat is applied gradually; flux is added to remove oxides.
4. **Skimming:** Slag/oxide layer is removed from the melt surface.
5. **Pouring:** At the correct superheat (e.g., **650 – 750 °C** for aluminum), the metal is poured into the mold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** Crucible melting is a **batch process** — each charge is melted separately, leading to lower productivity but *higher metallurgical control*. This is why it's used for **non-ferrous alloys** and high-grade irons, where quality matters more than tonnage.

---

## 2.4 Methods of Melting — Cupola Operation

### 2.4.1 What is a Cupola?

A **cupola** is a vertical, cylindrical, **shaft-type furnace** used primarily for melting **cast iron**. It operates **continuously** — charges (metal, coke, flux, limestone) are added at the top, and molten metal is tapped from the bottom. It is essentially a *miniature blast furnace* operating at lower temperatures (no slag-metal reduction chemistry).

**Operating principle:** The cupola works on the principle of *counter-current heat exchange* — hot combustion gases rise upward from the tuyeres, preheating the descending charge (metal, coke, flux).

### 2.4.2 Cupola Construction

A typical cupola consists of (from top to bottom):

1. **Charging Door / Charging Platform:** At the top, where raw materials are added.
2. **Stack / Shaft:** The main vertical cylindrical section, lined with refractory bricks (**silica or fireclay**).
3. **Charging Zone (Stack Zone):** Where preheating begins (~200 – 800 °C).
4. **Melting Zone:** Located just above the tuyeres. Temperature **1200 – 1400 °C**.
5. **Tuyere Zone:** Where preheated air (blast) is injected. Typically **4 to 12 tuyeres** arranged around the circumference.
6. **Well / Hearth:** The bottom chamber where molten metal collects.
7. **Taphole:** At the very bottom, where molten iron is discharged.
8. **Slag Hole:** Slightly above the taphole, for slag removal.
9. **Bottom Door / Drop Bottom:** Used to dump the entire charge at the end of the operation.
10. **Blower / Fan:** Supplies pressurized air to the tuyeres.
11. **Chimney / Stack Top:** Natural draft assistance.

### 2.4.3 Cupola Operation — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Preparation and Lighting**
- Refractory lining is inspected.
- A bed of coke is placed on the bottom door and ignited.

**Step 2: Charging**
- Alternating layers of **coke, metal, and limestone (flux)** are added through the charging door.
- Typical charge ratio: **1 : 6 : 0.3** (coke : iron : limestone by weight).
- Limestone decomposes: 
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$
- CaO then combines with silica and other impurities to form **slag**:
$$\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3 \text{ (slag)}$$

**Step 3: Blast Air Supply**
- Air is forced through tuyeres at **0.5 – 1.5 bar** pressure.
- The coke burns, generating temperatures of **1500 – 1800 °C** at the tuyeres.
- Coke combustion: 
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \text{heat}$$

**Step 4: Melting**
- As the charge descends, iron scrap/pig iron melts in the melting zone.
- Carbon from coke **diffuses** into the molten iron, increasing its carbon content (**3.0 – 4.0% C**).
- Silicon and manganese are also absorbed from the coke and scrap.

**Step 5: Tapping**
- Molten iron is tapped at regular intervals (e.g., every **10 – 15 minutes**).
- Slag floats on top and is removed separately.
- Temperature at tap: **1350 – 1450 °C**.

**Step 6: Deslagging and Slag Removal**
- Slag is highly fluid and is poured through the slag hole located above the taphole.

**Step 7: Blowing Out**
- At the end of the campaign (typically 4 – 8 hours), the bottom door is dropped, and the entire charge is quenched.

### 2.4.4 Cupola Melting — Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Air Pressure (Blast)** | **0.5 – 1.5 bar** |
| **Air Volume (Blast Rate)** | **100 – 200 m³/min** (for medium cupola) |
| **Coke-to-Metal Ratio** | **1 : 6 to 1 : 10** |
| **Melt Rate** | **5 – 15 tonnes/hour** (depends on cupola diameter) |
| **Tap Temperature** | **1350 – 1450 °C** |
| **Cupola Diameter** | **0.5 m – 3.0 m** (small to large foundries) |
| **Bed Height** | **1.0 – 1.5 m** (initial coke bed) |
| **Melting Efficiency** | **50 – 65%** (heat utilized for melting) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse the cupola with a blast furnace. *Key difference:* The cupola has **no reduction zone** — it simply melts iron and adjusts carbon content. The blast furnace *reduces* iron ore to metallic iron. The cupola is purely a *melting* furnace.

---

## 2.5 Comparison: Crucible vs. Cupola Melting

| Feature | Crucible Melting | Cupola Melting |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Type** | Batch | Continuous |
| **Typical Metal** | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Zn) | Cast iron (Fe + C) |
| **Productivity** | Low – Medium | High (5–15 t/h) |
| **Metal Cleanliness** | Very High | Medium (coke contact) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low – Medium | Medium – High |
| **Operational Skill** | Moderate | High |
| **Temperature Control** | Excellent (electric induction) | Moderate |
| **Flexibility** | High (varied alloys) | Low (mainly cast iron) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Conclusion:** The examiner expects the student to recognize that *crucible melting* is for *quality and flexibility*, while *cupola melting* is for *productivity and economy* in ferrous foundries. The selection is purely application-driven.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-ready. Memorize the units, the constants, and the practical limits.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{draft} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | Draft length on pattern | mm |
| 2 | $\text{Shrinkage} = L \cdot S$ | Pattern enlargement for shrinkage | mm |
| 3 | $t_s = K \cdot (V/A)^n$ | Chvorinov's solidification rule | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$ | Limestone decomposition in cupola | — |
| 5 | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \Delta H$ | Coke combustion (exothermic) | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $\eta_{cupola} = \dfrac{M_{melt}}{M_{coke} \cdot CV_{coke}} \times 100\%$ | Cupola thermal efficiency | % |
| 7 | $P_{blast} = 0.5 \text{ to } 1.5$ | Air blast pressure in cupola | bar |
| 8 | $\text{Coke ratio} = \dfrac{W_{coke}}{W_{iron}}$ | Coke-to-metal weight ratio | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas allow a foundry engineer to (a) design a pattern accurately, (b) predict solidification time, and (c) optimize cupola operations for cost and quality — making them the **core knowledge pillars** of any metallurgical or manufacturing engineer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chvorinov's Rule and the $V/A$ Ratio

The **Chvorinov's Rule** is the single most important equation for understanding **directional solidification** in castings. It states that the solidification time of a casting is proportional to the square of its **modulus** $(V/A)$.

**Starting Principle:** Heat transfer from the casting to the mold is governed by Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction.

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{metal} - T_{mold}) \cdot t_s$$

where $h$ is the heat transfer coefficient (in **W/m²K**).

**Energy stored in the molten metal:**

$$Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot L$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal (kg/m³).
- $V$ = volume of casting (m³).
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg).

**Equating the two expressions** (heat released = heat absorbed):

$$\rho \cdot V \cdot L = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{metal} - T_{mold}) \cdot t_s$$

**Solving for solidification time $t_s$:**

$$t_s = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{metal} - T_{mold})} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

Defining the **mold constant** $K$ as:

$$K = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{h \cdot (T_{metal} - T_{mold})}$$

We obtain the **Chvorinov's Rule** in its canonical form:

$$t_s = K \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

with $n \approx 2$ for most metals.

**Engineering implication:** A casting with a *higher* $V/A$ ratio takes *longer* to solidify. This is the foundation of **directional solidification** — the riser is designed to have a *larger* $V/A$ than the casting itself, so it solidifies *last* and feeds shrinkage.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Shrinkage Allowance Calculation

**Problem (KTU Model Question):** A gray cast iron casting is to be produced with a final length of **500 mm**. If the linear shrinkage of gray cast iron is **1.0%**, what should be the pattern length?

**Given:**
- Final casting length: $L = 500$ mm
- Shrinkage factor: $S = 1.0\% = 0.01$

**Step 1:** Apply the shrinkage allowance formula.

$$\text{Pattern length} = L \cdot (1 + S)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\text{Pattern length} = 500 \cdot (1 + 0.01)$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$\text{Pattern length} = 500 \cdot 1.01 = 505 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** The pattern must be **505 mm** long to yield a final casting of 500 mm after 1% linear shrinkage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This simple 1-step calculation appears frequently in KTU 3-mark short questions. Be ready to apply it for any metal, any percentage, and any length.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Solidification Time Using Chvorinov's Rule

**Problem (KTU Model Question):** A steel casting has a volume of **0.001 m³** and a surface area of **0.1 m²**. If the mold constant is **300 s/m²**, find the solidification time. (Assume $n = 2$.)

**Given:**
- $V = 0.001$ m³
- $A = 0.1$ m²
- $K = 300$ s/m² (or equivalently, the constant is calibrated for these units)
- $n = 2$

**Step 1:** Calculate the modulus $(V/A)$.

$$\frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.001}{0.1} = 0.01 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Chvorinov's Rule.

$$t_s = K \cdot \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^n$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$t_s = 300 \cdot (0.01)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$t_s = 300 \cdot 0.0001 = 0.03 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** The casting solidifies in **0.03 seconds** under these idealized conditions. (In practice, the value is much higher because $K$ varies with mold material, superheat, and metal.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Cupola Coke-to-Metal Ratio

**Problem (KTU Model Question):** A cupola is charged with **1200 kg of pig iron** per cycle. If the coke-to-metal ratio is **1 : 8**, calculate the mass of coke required. If the calorific value of coke is **28 MJ/kg**, find the total heat input.

**Given:**
- Mass of iron: $M_{iron} = 1200$ kg
- Coke-to-metal ratio: $1 : 8$

**Step 1:** Determine the coke mass.

$$\text{Coke ratio} = \frac{W_{coke}}{W_{iron}} = \frac{1}{8}$$

$$W_{coke} = \frac{W_{iron}}{8} = \frac{1200}{8} = 150 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total heat input from coke.

$$Q_{total} = W_{coke} \cdot CV_{coke}$$

$$Q_{total} = 150 \cdot 28 = 4200 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3:** If the cupola thermal efficiency is **55%**, find useful heat for melting.

$$Q_{useful} = 0.55 \cdot 4200 = 2310 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate heat required to melt 1200 kg of cast iron (using $L = 250$ kJ/kg, $C_p = 0.5$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{melt} = 1200$ °C, $T_{ambient} = 25$ °C).

$$Q_{req} = M_{iron} \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L)$$

$$Q_{req} = 1200 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 1175 + 250)$$

$$Q_{req} = 1200 \cdot (587.5 + 250) = 1200 \cdot 837.5 = 1{,}005{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 1005 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5:** Compare.

$$Q_{useful} = 2310 \text{ MJ} \gg Q_{req} = 1005 \text{ MJ}$$

The cupola can comfortably melt the charge with **1305 MJ of surplus heat**, which accounts for radiation losses, slag heating, and gas heating.

**Answer:** Coke required = **150 kg**; Total heat input = **4200 MJ**; Useful heat = **2310 MJ**; Heat required = **1005 MJ**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting a Casting Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to answer KTU 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions.**

| Selection Criterion | Sand Casting | Investment Casting | Die Casting | Centrifugal Casting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tolerance** | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.05 mm | ±0.3 mm |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 5 – 25 µm | 1.5 – 5 µm | 0.8 – 3 µm | 5 – 15 µm |
| **Min. Section Thickness** | 3 mm | 0.6 mm | 0.8 mm | 5 mm |
| **Max. Size** | Unlimited (limited by flask) | 1 m | 0.6 m | 3 m diameter |
| **Production Rate** | Low (4/hr) | Low (5/hr) | High (200/hr) | Medium (30/hr) |
| **Tooling Cost** | Low | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Typical Alloys** | Cast iron, steel | All (including superalloys) | Zn, Al, Mg | Fe, Cu, alloys |
| **Casting Cost (per kg)** | Low | Very High | Low (in volume) | Medium |
| **Pattern/Mold Reuse** | No (sand is broken) | No (ceramic shell broken) | Yes (dies last 100k+ shots) | No (single-use sand) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Which process would you recommend for manufacturing 50,000 connecting rods per year?"*, walk through the matrix systematically: identify *tolerance, surface finish, alloy, cost per piece, and production rate*. Always end with a *justified conclusion*.

## 3.6 Comprehensive Step-by-Step Cupola Operation (Expanded for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 10-step procedure is the most likely KTU 14-mark question structure. Memorize this sequence and the chemistry behind each step.**

**Step 1: Refractory Lining Inspection**
- The cupola's inner lining (silica, fireclay, or alumina bricks) is checked for cracks and wear. Damaged bricks are replaced to prevent heat loss and metal leakage.

**Step 2: Bottom Door Closing**
- The drop-bottom door is closed and latched. A layer of sand is placed on the door to seal it from molten metal.

**Step 3: Bed Coke Preparation**
- A **bed of coke** (typically 100 – 200 mm height) is placed on the bottom door and ignited. This is the initial heat source for the campaign.

**Step 4: Initial Blast**
- The blower is turned on at low pressure. Air enters through the tuyeres and burns the bed coke, raising the temperature to **1500 °C** in 20 – 30 minutes.

**Step 5: Charging Sequence**
- Layers are added in this order from the top:
  1. **Coke** (5 – 10% of metal weight)
  2. **Limestone flux** (3 – 5% of metal weight)
  3. **Iron charge** (pig iron + steel scrap + foundry returns)
- The sequence is repeated in **cycles** until the cupola is filled to the charging door level.

**Step 6: Steady-State Operation**
- After 30 – 60 minutes, the first molten metal reaches the well. Tapping begins.
- Air blast pressure is maintained at **0.5 – 1.5 bar**.
- The coke-to-iron ratio is kept at **1 : 6 to 1 : 10**.

**Step 7: Tapping**
- Molten iron is tapped every **10 – 15 minutes**.
- Temperature is monitored with **immersion pyrometers** — target tap temperature is **1350 – 1450 °C**.
- Slag is removed separately through the slag notch (located 50 – 100 mm above the taphole).

**Step 8: Metal Composition Adjustment**
- Carbon pickup from coke: typically **3.0 – 4.0% C**.
- Silicon: **1.0 – 2.5%**.
- Manganese: **0.5 – 1.0%**.
- Phosphorus and sulfur are *undesirable* and must be controlled by the quality of charge materials.

**Step 9: End-of-Campaign Procedure**
- Charging is stopped.
- Blast air continues for a few minutes to clear residual melt.
- The bottom door is dropped, quenching the entire charge.
- Refractory is inspected and repaired.

**Step 10: Safety and Environmental Controls**
- Cupola emissions (CO, SO₂, particulates) are ducted through **bag filters** and **afterburners**.
- Slag is granulated and used in cement or road construction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Red Flag:** Students often forget the **coke pickup mechanism** — carbon is *absorbed* into the molten iron from the surrounding coke bed. This is why cupola-melted iron has a *higher* carbon content than the charge.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define casting and list any four casting processes. [3 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Casting** is a manufacturing process in which a molten metal is poured into a refractory mold cavity of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then extracted as a finished or near-finished component. It is a *primary shaping* process governed by solidification metallurgy.

**Four casting processes:**

1. **Sand casting** — using green sand or dry sand molds; suited for large ferrous parts.
2. **Investment casting (lost-wax)** — using ceramic shell molds; suited for high-precision aerospace and jewelry parts.
3. **Die casting (hot/cold chamber)** — using permanent steel dies; suited for high-volume non-ferrous parts.
4. **Centrifugal casting** — using rotating molds; suited for cylindrical parts like pipes and tubes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 processes = 1 + 2 = **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. What is a cupola furnace? State two of its advantages. [3 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **cupola furnace** is a vertical, cylindrical, shaft-type furnace used for **continuous melting of cast iron** in foundries. It operates on the *counter-current heat exchange* principle — hot combustion gases from coke rise upward and preheat the descending charge of metal, coke, and flux. Molten iron collects in the well at the bottom and is tapped periodically.

**Two advantages of cupola furnace:**

1. **High productivity** — A medium-sized cupola can melt **5 – 15 tonnes per hour**, much higher than crucible furnaces.
2. **Economical for cast iron** — The continuous operation and use of cheap coke make it cost-effective for high-volume ferrous foundries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 1.5 marks for definition + 0.75 mark × 2 advantages = 1.5 + 1.5 = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice (Or option). You must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) Explain the various casting design considerations that influence the quality of a cast product. [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Casting design considerations are a set of **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)** rules that ensure the casting can be produced defect-free, economically, and with the required dimensional accuracy. The major considerations are:

1. **Draft Angle (Taper):** Vertical faces of the pattern are given a slight taper (typically **1° – 3°**) to enable clean withdrawal from the sand mold. Without draft, the pattern tears the mold walls, causing sand inclusions.

2. **Parting Line:** The line of separation between the cope and drag is positioned at the **maximum cross-section** of the casting to minimize mold complexity. The larger portion is kept in the drag.

3. **Fillet and Corner Radii:** Sharp corners are replaced by **rounded fillets** (1.5× to 2× the wall thickness) to eliminate stress concentration and prevent hot tears.

4. **Uniform Wall Thickness:** Uniform wall thickness ensures **even cooling** and prevents shrinkage voids. Thick sections are isolated as separate bosses or moved to the top to promote directional solidification.

5. **Cores:** Internal cavities are formed using **sand cores** (bonded with resin/oil) supported by core prints.

6. **Shrinkage Allowance:** The pattern is enlarged (typically **0.8% – 2.0%**) to compensate for metal contraction during solidification and cooling.

7. **Machining Allowance:** Extra stock (**1 – 4 mm per side**) is provided on surfaces that will undergo finish machining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 1 mark × 7 considerations = **7 marks**. Include a small diagram of a typical casting with labels for full marks.

---

**Q.A (b) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a cupola furnace. [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **cupola furnace** is a vertical shaft furnace used for **continuous melting of cast iron**. It is essentially a *mini blast furnace* operating without ore reduction.

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**

1. **Charging Door** — at the top, for adding raw materials.
2. **Stack / Shaft** — the main vertical cylinder, lined with silica or fireclay bricks.
3. **Melting Zone** — located above the tuyeres, where the metal melts at **1200 – 1400 °C**.
4. **Tuyere Zone** — typically 4 – 12 tuyeres arranged around the circumference, where pressurized air enters.
5. **Well / Hearth** — at the bottom, where molten iron accumulates.
6. **Taphole** — for tapping molten iron.
7. **Slag Notch** — for slag removal.
8. **Drop Bottom** — for end-of-campaign discharge.
9. **Blower** — supplies air at **0.5 – 1.5 bar**.

**Working:**

1. A **bed of coke** is ignited on the bottom door.
2. Layers of **coke, limestone, and iron charge** are added alternately through the charging door.
3. **Pressurized air** from the blower enters via tuyeres and burns the coke, generating temperatures of **1500 – 1800 °C**.
4. Iron melts in the melting zone and trickles down into the well.
5. **Carbon from coke diffuses** into the molten iron, raising its carbon content to **3.0 – 4.0%**.
6. **Limestone** decomposes into CaO and CO₂; CaO combines with silica to form slag (CaSiO₃), which floats and is removed.
7. **Molten iron is tapped** every 10 – 15 minutes at **1350 – 1450 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 3 marks for construction + 3 marks for working + 1 mark for neat diagram = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B (a) Describe the crucible melting process. Explain the different types of crucible furnaces with their applications. [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Crucible Melting — Definition:**

Crucible melting is a **batch-type melting process** in which the metal is melted inside a refractory container (crucible) heated externally. The metal does **not** come into direct contact with the combustion gases, ensuring high metal cleanliness.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. **Crucible Preheating** — Slowly to 150 – 200 °C to remove moisture.
2. **Charging** — Solid metal added; heavy pieces at the bottom.
3. **Melting** — Heat applied gradually; flux added to remove oxides.
4. **Skimming** — Slag removed from the melt surface.
5. **Pouring** — At the correct superheat, metal is poured into the mold.

**Types of Crucible Furnaces:**

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Pit Crucible** | Crucible sits in a pit below floor level; charged from above, tapped from below. | Small foundries, ferrous alloys. |
| **Stationary Crucible** | Fixed furnace; metal is *ladled* out. | Non-ferrous alloys in small batches. |
| **Tilting Crucible** | Furnace tilts hydraulically to pour. | Aluminum and copper foundries (medium scale). |
| **Bale-out** | Metal is removed with a hand ladle. | Small, high-melting-point alloy batches. |
| **Induction Crucible** | Heated by electromagnetic induction; highest purity. | Aerospace alloys, high-grade steels, research labs. |

**Advantages of Crucible Melting:**

- **High metal purity** (no contact with combustion gases).
- **Excellent temperature control** (especially in induction).
- **Flexibility** — different alloys can be melted in the same furnace.
- **Low capital cost** compared to cupola or arc furnaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 2 marks for process + 3 marks for types (with table) + 2 marks for advantages = **7 marks**.

---

**Q.B (b) Compare crucible melting and cupola melting on the basis of process type, capacity, metal cleanliness, applications, and cost. [7 Marks]**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Crucible Melting | Cupola Melting |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Type** | Batch | Continuous |
| **Capacity / Productivity** | Low – Medium (1 – 5 tonnes/day) | High (5 – 15 tonnes/hour) |
| **Metal Cleanliness** | Very High (no contact with gases) | Medium (coke contact introduces C, S) |
| **Typical Metals** | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Zn, Mg), high-grade steel, superalloys | Cast iron (gray, white, malleable) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low – Medium | Medium – High |
| **Operational Cost** | Medium – High (especially induction) | Low (cheap coke) |
| **Flexibility** | High (varied alloys, small batches) | Low (only cast iron) |
| **Temperature Control** | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Environmental Impact** | Low (especially electric) | High (CO, SO₂, dust emissions) |
| **Application** | Aerospace, jewelry, automotive non-ferrous, research | Automotive castings, machine tools, pipes, engine blocks |

**Conclusion:**

- Choose **crucible melting** when *quality, purity, and flexibility* are paramount.
- Choose **cupola melting** when *high-volume, low-cost* cast iron production is the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Awarding marks:** 1.4 marks × 5 criteria (proper table) = 7 marks. A neat table with all 5 criteria gets full marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing cupola with blast furnace** — The cupola has *no reduction zone*. It only melts and adjusts carbon. (Cost: 1 – 2 marks lost)
> 2. **Forgetting draft angle on internal features** — Examiners specifically check if you mention *external vs. internal* draft.
> 3. **Skipping Chvorinov's Rule** — Any solidification discussion without $t_s = K \cdot (V/A)^n$ is incomplete.
> 4. **Not mentioning the role of flux (limestone)** in cupola — Examiners want the chemistry: $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} \rightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3$ (slag).
> 5. **Using vague phrases** like "metals are melted" — Be specific: *"Coke burns at the tuyere zone, raising the temperature to 1500 °C, melting the descending iron charge."*
> 6. **No units in numerical problems** — Always write **mm, °C, kg, m³, s, bar** beside every number.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the role of cores in sand casting** — The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights cores in the module outcomes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist for the last 15 minutes of revision before the exam. Each bullet is a potential 1 – 7 mark question.**

### Casting Design Considerations
- ☐ **12 Golden Rules:** Draft, Parting, Fillet, Uniform, Ribs, Core, Machine, Shrink, Distort, Gate, Round, Avoid.
- ☐ **Draft angle:** **1° – 3°** typical; prevents sand mold damage.
- ☐ **Parting line:** at maximum cross-section; larger portion in drag.
- ☐ **Fillet radius:** **1.5× – 2× wall thickness**.
- ☐ **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = K \cdot (V/A)^n$ with $n \approx 2$.
- ☐ **Shrinkage:** Cast iron **0.8% – 1.0%**; steel **1.5% – 2.0%**; Al **1.0% – 1.5%**.
- ☐ **Pattern enlargement formula:** $L_{pattern} = L \cdot (1 + S)$.
- ☐ **Machining allowance:** sand casting **2 – 4 mm**; investment **0 – 0.5 mm**.

### Casting Processes
- ☐ **Sand casting** — green sand; large ferrous parts; lowest cost; one-time mold.
- ☐ **Investment casting** — lost-wax; highest accuracy; aerospace; expensive.
- ☐ **Die casting** — hot/cold chamber; high volume non-ferrous; permanent dies.
- ☐ **Centrifugal casting** — rotating mold; cylindrical parts; pipes.
- ☐ **Continuous casting** — water-cooled copper mold; steel slabs/billets.
- ☐ **Shell molding** — resin-bonded sand; medium precision.
- ☐ **Permanent mold casting** — cast iron mold; pistons, gears.
- ☐ **Squeeze casting** — forging-grade parts via hydraulic pressure.

### Crucible Melting
- ☐ **Batch process**; metal does not contact combustion gases.
- ☐ **Crucible materials:** clay-graphite, silicon carbide, cast iron.
- ☐ **Preheating:** 150 – 200 °C to remove moisture.
- ☐ **Types:** Pit, Stationary, Tilting, Bale-out, Induction.
- ☐ **Used for:** Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, Zn) and high-grade ferrous alloys.

### Cupola Operation
- ☐ **Continuous melting furnace** for cast iron.
- ☐ **Construction:** Charging door → Stack → Melting zone → Tuyere zone → Well → Taphole.
- ☐ **No reduction zone** (key difference from blast furnace).
- ☐ **Charge ratio:** Coke : Iron : Limestone = **1 : 6 to 1 : 10 : 0.3** by weight.
- ☐ **Coke combustion:** $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \Delta H$.
- ☐ **Limestone flux:** $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$; then $\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3$ (slag).
- ☐ **Air blast pressure:** **0.5 – 1.5 bar**.
- ☐ **Tap temperature:** **1350 – 1450 °C**.
- ☐ **Melt rate:** **5 – 15 tonnes/hour**.
- ☐ **Carbon pickup** from coke: **3.0 – 4.0% C** in final iron.
- ☐ **Tapping cycle:** every **10 – 15 minutes**.

### Final Exam Mantra
> *"Memorize the 12 design rules, the 4 shrinkage percentages, the Chvorinov equation, the cupola 10-step operation, and the comparison table between crucible and cupola. That covers ~85% of any KTU Module 1 question."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects in Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defects in Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Casting Defects** are defined as any **irregularity, discontinuity, or imperfection** in a cast metal component that deviates from the intended shape, dimensions, surface finish, mechanical properties, or chemical composition, arising from one or more stages of the foundry process (melting, mold making, pouring, solidification, cooling, shakeout, fettling, or heat treatment).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBAUT404)**, casting defects are systematically studied because the casting process, despite being one of the **oldest (≈ 5000 BCE, bronze age)** and most versatile manufacturing routes, is highly susceptible to a wide spectrum of failure modes. KTU examiners test this topic primarily under the **"Identify, Classify, and Remediate"** cognitive frame (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy — Levels: *Remember, Understand, Analyze*).

## 1.2 KTU 2024 Scheme Classification Snapshot

KTU expects students to classify casting defects under the following **8 standard groups** as per the **Groover (Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing)** and **Rao (Manufacturing Technology)** textbooks:

| Sl. No. | KTU Class | Core Defect Group |
|:-------:|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **A** | Excess metal / Projections (Flash, Fins, Burrs) |
| 2 | **B** | Cavities / Voids (Blowholes, Pinholes, Porosity) |
| 3 | **C** | Discontinuities / Cracks (Hot tears, Cold shuts) |
| 4 | **D** | Surface defects (Scabs, Washes, Rat-tails) |
| 5 | **E** | Incomplete castings (Misruns, Pour-short, Cold shuts) |
| 6 | **F** | Inclusions (Sand, Slag, Oxide) |
| 7 | **G** | Metallurgical defects (Hard spots, Low ductility) |
| 8 | **H** | Dimensional errors (Incorrect shrinkage allowance) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Idli Tava Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine pouring liquid idli batter into a hot, ungreased tava (griddle) and trying to get a perfect circular idli without bubbles, cracks, or burnt edges.**
>
> - If the batter is **too cold** → it won't fill the mould (analogue: **misrun**).
> - If you pour **too fast** → air gets trapped inside as **bubbles** (analogue: **blowholes**).
> - If the tava has a hairline crack → batter leaks and forms thin flash (analogue: **flash**).
> - If you pull the idli out **too early** → it tears apart (analogue: **hot tear**).
> - If tiny bits of burnt batter fall into the idli → they become hard inclusions (analogue: **sand/slag inclusions**).

The casting process is mechanically identical in principle — **molten metal** behaves like the **batter**, the **sand mould** behaves like the **tava**, and any deviation from controlled thermal/mechanical parameters produces analogous defects.

## 1.4 Standard Foundry Metrics Used to Quantify Defects

- **Yield** of a casting = (Mass of usable casting / Mass of metal poured) × 100%
- **Shrinkage allowance** is typically **1.2 % to 2.0 %** for **cast iron** and **0.5 % to 1.5 %** for **steel**.
- **Riser-to-casting volume ratio** is generally kept at **1.3 : 1 to 1.5 : 1** to prevent shrinkage porosity.
- **Minimum pour temperature** must be **50 °C to 100 °C** above the **liquidus** to avoid misruns.
</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Root-Cause Mapping of Major Casting Defects

### 2.1.1 Class A — Excess Metal Defects

**Flash (or Fin or Burr)**
- **Why it happens:** Mould halves (cope and drag) are not properly clamped, parting surfaces are worn, or mould surfaces are damaged → molten metal squeezes into the parting line gap.
- **Remediation:** Use clamping weights; repair mould surfaces; apply mould coating to seal micro-gaps.
- **Where it appears:** Parting line of the casting (a thin plate-like projection).

### 2.1.2 Class B — Cavities / Voids

These are the **most frequently tested** defects in KTU exams.

**(a) Blowholes**
- **Why:** Gas (H₂, N₂, CO) entrapment due to high moisture in the sand, poor venting, low permeability (typical permeability $k < 80$ in AFS units), or excessive gas evolution from the melt.
- **Distinguishing visual:** Smooth-walled, spherical/elongated cavities just below the casting surface (subsurface blowholes) or at the surface (surface blowholes).
- **Remediation:** Dry the sand (moisture $<\ 4$ %), improve permeability to $k \geq 100$ AFS, add vents.

**(b) Pinholes**
- **Why:** Excessive hydrogen dissolution in liquid metal during rapid cooling; the solubility drop on solidification releases the gas as **fine, dispersed micro-cavities**.
- **Visual:** Very small (pin-head sized), uniformly distributed holes.

**(c) Porosity (Microporosity)**
- **Why:** Solidification shrinkage of the metal (typically **3 % to 7 %** volumetric shrinkage for cast iron) is not compensated by risers.
- **Visual:** Irregular, interconnected micro-voids, often hidden inside the casting (sub-surface).
- **Critical equation:** **Chvorinov's Rule** governs whether porosity forms.

**(d) Shrinkage Cavity (Pipe)**
- **Why:** Volumetric contraction on solidification in the **last region to solidify** (typically the riser-mould interface or the casting's thermal center).

### 2.1.3 Class C — Discontinuities / Cracks

**Hot Tears (Hot Cracks)**
- **Why:** At the **cohesion temperature** (just below solidus), the solidifying skin is weak and brittle. If mould/core restraint (restrained thermal contraction) exceeds the skin's tensile strength, it cracks.
- **Visual:** Inter-dendritic, irregular branched cracks, often at **corners or hot-spots**.
- **Remediation:** Use chills to control solidification; add fillets at corners; design mould for uniform cooling.

**Cold Shuts (Cold Laps)**
- **Why:** Two streams of metal that are already **partially solidified** meet but fail to fuse completely — leaving a cold junction.
- **Visual:** A line or seam on the casting surface with rounded (unfused) edges, often with oxide film in between.

### 2.1.4 Class D — Surface Defects

**Scabs**: Spalling of sand particles into the casting surface due to excessive expansion of surface sand → produces a rough, scabbed texture.

**Washes**: Stream of molten metal erodes mould surface → produces an irregular, washed-out groove with sand traces.

**Rat-tails**: Long, thin, wrinkle-like surface marks caused by **buckling** of the dried sand skin.

### 2.1.5 Class E — Incomplete Castings

**Misrun**: Metal solidifies **before** fully filling the mould cavity. Cause: Low pouring temperature, thin sections, slow pour rate.

**Pour-short**: Inadequate volume of metal poured — similar to misrun but due to furnace shortage, not temperature.

### 2.1.6 Class F — Inclusions

**Sand Inclusions**: Particles of mould sand physically embedded in the casting.
**Slag Inclusions**: Non-metallic slag (FeO, MnO, SiO₂) trapped in the casting.
**Oxide Inclusions**: Skim, dross or oxide films (e.g., Al₂O₃ in aluminum castings) folded in.

### 2.1.7 Class G — Metallurgical Defects

**Hard Spots**: Localized regions of very high hardness (e.g., chilled white-iron regions in gray iron) caused by rapid cooling in thin sections.

**Low Mechanical Properties**: Caused by porosity, inclusions, or improper composition (e.g., high sulfur → embrittlement).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU student needs for a **numerical on solidification, shrinkage, or riser design** (typical Part B, 14-mark question structure).

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Application | Units |
|:-:|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Chvorinov's Rule** (Solidification Time) | $t_s = B \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2$ | Compute solidification time of a casting vs riser; ensures $t_{s,\text{riser}} \geq t_{s,\text{casting}}$ | $t_s$ in seconds, $V$ in m³, $A$ in m², $B$ in s/m² |
| 2 | **Shrinkage Allowance (Linear)** | $S_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$ | Calculate pattern allowance for patternmaker | $S_L$, $L$ in m, $\alpha$ in /°C, $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 3 | **Shrinkage Allowance (Volumetric)** | $S_V = 3 \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | For isotropic solid shrinkage | Dimensionless (fraction) |
| 4 | **Riser Volume (Cylindrical)** | $V_r = \dfrac{\pi d^2 h}{4}$ | Basic riser sizing | m³ |
| 5 | **Riser Modulus Condition** | $M_r \geq M_c$ | Mould modulus $M = V/A$; Risers must solidify last | — |
| 6 | **Modulus of a Cylinder** | $M_{cyl} = \dfrac{r}{2}$ | Compare to cube $M_{cube} = a/6$ | m |
| 7 | **Sphere Modulus** | $M_{sph} = \dfrac{r}{3}$ | Best feeder geometry (lowest surface per volume) | m |
| 8 | **Pouring Time (for thin castings)** | $t_p = K \sqrt{W}$ | $W$ = mass of casting, $K$ = constant (≈ 0.65 for steel) | s |
| 9 | **Bernoulli / Pouring Cup Velocity** | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Top pouring velocity from sprue height $h$ | m/s |
| 10 | **Spout / Sprue Top-Bottom Area Ratio** | $\dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}}$ | Unpressurized gating design to prevent aspiration | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the table above (Markdown safety):** To avoid breaking markdown table syntax, all absolute value bars and divider bars have been written using the parenthesized forms $\left( \dfrac{V}{A} \right)$ and $\sqrt{\dfrac{h_2}{h_1}}$ — never as raw vertical pipe characters.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding casting defects is **not academic** — it directly impacts:

1. **Automotive Crankshafts**: A single hot tear in a ductile iron crankshaft makes it a $1,200 part vs a scrap loss.
2. **Aerospace Turbine Blades**: Super-alloy (Inconel 718) investment cast blades must pass X-ray radiography (ASTM E192) to detect sub-surface porosity — a single $10 \text{ μm}$ void can initiate a fatigue crack at $10^7$ cycles.
3. **Wind Turbine Housings**: Sand inclusions in large cast iron hubs are detected by ultrasonic C-scan.
4. **Pump & Valve Industry (Kerala Foundry Hub, Belgaum-Kerala corridor)**: ISI 210 spec, requires zero visible defects on pressure-bearing castings.
</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chvorinov's Rule (for Shrinkage-Cavity / Riser Design)

Chvorinov's rule is **the single most important equation** tested in KTU 2024 for casting defects. We will derive it fully step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Statement

The **solidification time** $t_s$ of a casting is proportional to the square of the ratio of its volume to its surface area.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Newton's Law of Cooling (Conduction form).**

For a solidifying casting, the rate of heat loss from the casting surface to the mould is:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_c - T_0)$$

where $h$ is the interfacial heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the surface area, $T_c$ is the instantaneous casting surface temperature, and $T_0$ is the mould temperature.

**Step 2 — Total Latent Heat to be Removed.**

The total latent heat of fusion released during solidification is:

$$Q_L = \rho \cdot V \cdot H_f$$

where $\rho$ is the density, $V$ is the volume, and $H_f$ is the specific latent heat of fusion.

**Step 3 — Equating Heat Lost to Latent Heat Released.**

Assuming **Biot number $Bi \ll 0.1$** (i.e., temperature inside the casting is approximately uniform, valid for small castings), the total heat removed equals the total latent heat:

$$h \cdot \bar{A} \cdot (T_m - T_0) \cdot t_s = \rho \cdot V \cdot H_f$$

where $\bar{A}$ is the **time-averaged** surface area (we replace with $A$ for simplicity in idealized geometry).

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_s$.**

$$t_s = \frac{\rho \cdot H_f}{h \cdot (T_m - T_0)} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Constants.**

All the material-and-mould-dependent constants are lumped into a single constant $B$:

$$B = \frac{\rho \cdot H_f}{h \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$$

**Step 6 — Final Form of Chvorinov's Rule.**

$$t_s = B \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2$$

**Step 7 — Define the Modulus.**

Define the **casting modulus** $M = V / A$. Then:

$$t_s = B \cdot M^2$$

This derivation is the **direct theoretical basis** for the riser-design condition:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Riser Design Condition (KTU Favourite Equation):**
>
> $$t_{s,\text{riser}} \geq t_{s,\text{casting}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad M_{\text{riser}} \geq M_{\text{casting}}$$
>
> A **sphere** has the smallest surface-to-volume ratio — therefore the **highest modulus per unit volume** — so a **spherical riser is the most efficient feeder** of shrinkage.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical — Direct Chvorinov Application (Module 1, 14-mark style)

> **Problem:** A steel casting has a **modulus** $M_c = 2.5$ cm. A cylindrical riser (diameter $d = 8$ cm, height $h = 12$ cm) is proposed. The mould constant $B = 90$ s/cm² for steel. Will the riser solidify **after** the casting (i.e., is the design safe)?

**Step 1 — Compute the Riser Modulus.**

For a cylinder, $M_{cyl} = \dfrac{r}{2} = \dfrac{d}{4} = \dfrac{8 \text{ cm}}{4} = 2.0$ cm.

**Step 2 — Compute the Solidification Times.**

Casting solidification time:

$$t_{s,c} = B \cdot M_c^2 = 90 \times (2.5)^2 = 90 \times 6.25 = 562.5 \text{ s}$$

Riser solidification time:

$$t_{s,r} = B \cdot M_r^2 = 90 \times (2.0)^2 = 90 \times 4.0 = 360.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

Since $t_{s,r} = 360 \text{ s} < t_{s,c} = 562.5 \text{ s}$, the riser will solidify **before** the casting. This design is **UNSAFE** — shrinkage porosity will form in the casting.

**Step 4 — Redesign.**

Required $M_r \geq 2.5$ cm. Cylinder condition: $d/4 \geq 2.5$ → $d \geq 10$ cm.

New riser: $d = 12$ cm, $h = 12$ cm. Check: $M_r = 12/4 = 3.0$ cm.

$$t_{s,r} = 90 \times (3.0)^2 = 90 \times 9.0 = 810 \text{ s}$$

Since $810 \text{ s} > 562.5 \text{ s}$, the new riser design is **SAFE**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Shrinkage Allowance (Linear)

**Step 1 — Free Linear Expansion of a Casting Section.**

If a metal is allowed to cool from the **solidus temperature $T_s$** to the **room temperature $T_0$** without restraint, the linear contraction is:

$$\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_s - T_0)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **coefficient of linear thermal expansion** (units: /°C).

**Step 2 — Pattern Size Adjustment.**

The pattern must therefore be made **larger** than the final required dimension by exactly this amount:

$$L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot (T_s - T_0) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Shrinkage Allowance Formula (most common form).**

$$S_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Cause-to-Remedy Summary

| Defect | Class | Primary Cause | Visual Identifier | Primary Remedy | KTU Mark Weight |
|--------|:-----:|---------------|-------------------|----------------|:---------------:|
| **Flash** | A | Loose mould halves | Thin plate at parting line | Clamp weights; repair mould | 3-mark definition |
| **Blowhole** | B | Gas entrapment | Smooth subsurface cavity | Dry sand, add vents, lower pouring speed | 3 + 7-mark |
| **Shrinkage cavity** | B | Insufficient feeding | Crater near hot spot | Add riser, use chills | 14-mark derivation |
| **Hot tear** | C | Restrained contraction | Branched, interdendritic crack | Add fillets, chills, improve collapsibility | 7-mark |
| **Cold shut** | C/E | Low pour temperature | Linear seam with oxide | Increase pour temp, preheat ladle | 3-mark |
| **Misrun** | E | Premature solidification | Incomplete filling | Increase pour temp, redesign gating | 3-mark |
| **Sand inclusion** | F | Mould erosion | Embedded sand grains | Improve mould strength, preheat mould | 3-mark |
| **Scab** | D | Sand expansion | Spalled surface crust | Use additives (sea coal), control moisture | 3-mark |
| **Hard spot** | G | Localized chill | Brittle white-iron region | Add inoculants, section size control | 3-mark |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Pattern Shrinkage Allowance (14-mark style)

> **Problem:** A grey cast iron casting has a length of $L = 600$ mm at room temperature (25 °C). Given $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $T_{\text{liquidus}} = 1150$ °C, $T_{\text{solidus}} = 1100$ °C, $T_{\text{room}} = 25$ °C. Calculate the **shrinkage allowance** $S_L$ and the **required pattern length** $L_p$.

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature range over which the metal contracts as a solid.**

Since the pattern only compensates for **solid-state contraction** (liquid contraction is compensated by sprue and riser system):

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{solidus}} - T_{\text{room}} = 1100 - 25 = 1075 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear shrinkage formula.**

$$S_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

$$S_L = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1075 \times 600 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerically.**

$$S_L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 1075 \times 600$$

$$S_L = 12 \times 1075 \times 600 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$S_L = 7740000 \times 10^{-6} = 7.74 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Pattern length.**

$$L_p = L + S_L = 600 + 7.74 = 607.74 \text{ mm} \approx 608 \text{ mm}$$

**Note:** This $7.74$ mm corresponds to a **shrinkage of 1.29 %**, which is **within the standard cast iron allowance of 1.2 % – 2.0 %** — a good consistency check for the KTU answer.
</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of Casting-Defect Causation

The following Mermaid block maps the **process → mechanism → defect** flow as taught in KTU 2024 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Process Inputs: Sand, Metal, Mould, Gating] --> B1[Melting Stage]
    A0 --> B2[Mould Preparation]
    A0 --> B3[Pouring Stage]
    A0 --> B4[Solidification Stage]
    A0 --> B5[Cooling and Shakeout]

    B1 --> C1[High Gas Dissolution in Melt]
    B1 --> C2[Slag and Oxide Formation]
    B2 --> C3[Excess Moisture in Sand]
    B2 --> C4[Poor Permeability k less than 80]
    B2 --> C5[Damaged Mould Surface]
    B3 --> C6[Low Pour Temperature]
    B3 --> C7[Excessive Pour Velocity]
    B4 --> C8[Uncontrolled Solidification Front]
    B4 --> C9[Insufficient Riser Feed]
    B5 --> C10[Restrained Contraction]

    C1 --> D1[Pinholes and Porosity]
    C2 --> D2[Slag and Oxide Inclusions]
    C3 --> D3[Blowholes]
    C4 --> D3
    C5 --> D4[Sand Inclusions and Scabs]
    C6 --> D5[Misrun and Cold Shut]
    C7 --> D6[Gas Entrapment Blowholes]
    C8 --> D7[Columnar Grains Hot Tears]
    C9 --> D8[Shrinkage Cavity and Pipe]
    C10 --> D9[Hot Tears and Cracks]

    D1 --> E1[Engineering Reject: Porosity Defect]
    D2 --> E2[Reject: Inclusions]
    D3 --> E3[Reject: Blowhole Defect]
    D4 --> E4[Reject: Surface Defect]
    D5 --> E5[Reject: Incomplete Casting]
    D6 --> E3
    D7 --> E6[Reject: Crack Defect]
    D8 --> E7[Reject: Shrinkage Defect]
    D9 --> E6
```

## 4.2 Riser-versus-Casting Modulus Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Casting Geometry Known] --> compute_c[Compute Casting Modulus Mc = Vc over Ac]
    compute_c --> choose_r[Choose Riser Geometry: Sphere or Cylinder]
    choose_r --> compute_r[Compute Riser Modulus Mr = Vr over Ar]
    compute_r --> check{Is Mr greater than or equal to Mc?}

    check -- YES --> safe[Riser Solidifies LAST: SAFE Design]
    check -- NO --> unsafe[Riser Solidifies BEFORE Casting: UNSAFE, Porosity Forms]

    unsafe --> resize[Increase Riser Diameter OR Use Spherical Riser]
    resize --> compute_r

    safe --> end[Proceed to Pouring]
    unsafe --> end
    end[End of Riser Sizing Module]
```

## 4.3 Defect-Identification Decision Tree (For KTU 3-Mark Questions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obs[Observe Defect in Casting] --> q1{Where is it located?}

    q1 -- Surface Only --> q2{What does it look like?}
    q2 -- Thin Plate at Parting Line --> def1[FLASH]
    q2 -- Sand Grains Embedded --> def2[SAND INCLUSION]
    q2 -- Rough Spalled Crust --> def3[SCAB]
    q2 -- Washed Groove --> def4[WASH]

    q1 -- Subsurface --> q5{Shape and Size?}
    q5 -- Spherical Large Smooth --> def5[BLOWHOLE]
    q5 -- Pinhead Fine Distributed --> def6[PINHOLES]
    q5 -- Irregular Interconnected --> def7[MICROPOROSITY]

    q1 -- Internal Center --> q8{Large or Branched?}
    q8 -- Large Crater --> def8[SHRINKAGE CAVITY PIPE]
    q8 -- Branched Tree Like --> def9[HOT TEAR]

    q1 -- At Cold Joints --> def10[COLD SHUT]

    q1 -- Incomplete Filling --> def11[MISRUN OR POUR SHORT]
```
