# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Definition & Concept)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Define (a) a hot tear and (b) a cold shut in castings. How are they distinguished visually in a fracture test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Hot Tear (Hot Crack):** A hot tear is an irregular, branched, interdendritic crack that forms in a casting at temperatures **just below the solidus**, when the still-thin solidified skin cannot accommodate the **restrained thermal contraction**. It typically appears at **corners, hot spots, or near riser necks** where contraction is hindered by the mould or core.

> **Cold Shut (Cold Lap):** A cold shut is a **seam-like defect on the casting surface** formed when **two streams of partially solidified metal meet but fail to fuse** properly. A thin oxide film is often trapped between them. It results from **low pouring temperature** or a poorly designed gating system.

> **Visual distinction:** A hot tear is a **branched, internal crack with no oxide film** (since it forms in a reducing solidifying environment). A cold shut is a **surface seam with an oxide film** visible at the joint.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
> **What is a "blowhole" in a sand casting? How is it different from a pinhole? Mention two causes of blowholes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Blowhole:** A blowhole is a **smooth-walled, spherical or elongated gas cavity** found just below the surface (subsurface) or on the surface of a casting, formed by the **entrapment of gases** (H₂, N₂, CO, water vapour) that cannot escape the mould before the metal solidifies.

> **Pinhole vs Blowhole:** Pinholes are **very small (pin-head sized), numerous, and uniformly distributed** micro-cavities, typically due to **excess hydrogen** dissolved in the melt. Blowholes are **larger, fewer, and locally clustered** at hot spots.

> **Two causes:** (i) **Excess moisture in the sand** (> 4 %) — at the metal-sand interface, water vapour cannot escape. (ii) **Low permeability of the sand mould** ($k < 80$ AFS) — vents are insufficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Full Question]**
> **(a)** Classify the major **defects in castings** with suitable examples. Discuss the **causes and remedies** of **(i) blowholes** and **(ii) shrinkage cavities** in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical riser of **diameter 12 cm and height 12 cm** is proposed for a steel casting of modulus $M_c = 2.5$ cm. The mould constant $B = 90$ s/cm². Check whether the riser is adequate. If not, suggest a redesign. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Classification (3 marks):** Defects are classified into **8 groups** as per KTU 2024 syllabus (already tabulated in Section 1.2 above). State each group with one example.

**(i) Blowholes — Causes and Remedies (2 marks):**

**Causes:**
- High moisture content in the sand mould ($> 4$ %).
- Low permeability of sand ($k < 80$ AFS units).
- Insufficient venting in mould and cores.
- High pouring speed traps air in the cavity.
- Wet ladle or wet chills.

**Remedies:**
- Dry the sand properly (moisture $\leq 4$ %).
- Add **coal dust or sea coal** to the moulding sand to cushion the expansion.
- Increase permeability by using **coarser sand** or **coke** additives.
- Add **vents** in mould and core.
- Preheat the ladle; pre-dry chills.

**(ii) Shrinkage Cavities — Causes and Remedies (2 marks):**

**Causes:**
- Insufficient metal feed from riser during solidification shrinkage.
- Riser modulus $M_r$ less than casting modulus $M_c$.
- Poor gating design.
- Hot spot in the casting with no chills.

**Remedies:**
- Apply Chvorinov's rule: $M_r \geq M_c$.
- Use **chills** to control direction of solidification (directional solidification toward riser).
- Use **spherical risers** (highest modulus per unit volume).
- Use **sleeve-lined risers** to reduce heat loss.

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute Riser Modulus.**

For a cylinder: $M_r = \dfrac{d}{4} = \dfrac{12 \text{ cm}}{4} = 3.0$ cm.

**Step 2 — Compute Solidification Times.**

Casting:

$$t_{s,c} = B \cdot M_c^2 = 90 \times (2.5)^2 = 562.5 \text{ s}$$

Riser:

$$t_{s,r} = B \cdot M_r^2 = 90 \times (3.0)^2 = 810 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Verification.**

Since $t_{s,r} = 810 \text{ s} > t_{s,c} = 562.5 \text{ s}$, the riser solidifies **after** the casting. **The proposed design is ADEQUATE** (i.e., $M_r = 3.0$ cm $> M_c = 2.5$ cm).

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The riser is safe, with a solidification time **margin** of $\Delta t = 247.5$ s. This is a healthy safety factor (≈ 44 % margin), acceptable in production.

> **Alternative — if the question instead asked to check the previously unsafe design (Section 3.1.3), the answer would proceed to redesign the riser diameter to $d \geq 10$ cm.**

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice, Alternative Path]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **solidification of a pure metal** and a **solid-solution-type alloy**. How does the difference in solidification mode lead to **shrinkage porosity** versus **pipe shrinkage**? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **shrinkage allowance** for a grey cast iron bar of length **800 mm**, given $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, pouring temperature 1250 °C, solidus 1100 °C, and room temperature 25 °C. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Pure Metal Solidification (3 marks):**
- Solidification occurs at a **single temperature** = freezing point.
- The solid-liquid interface is **sharp and planar**.
- The casting shrinks in **two distinct stages** — *liquid contraction* and *solidification contraction* — but no solidification range.
- A central **pipe shrinkage cavity** forms in the last-to-solidify region because solidification proceeds from the mould wall inward as a **uniform concentric shell**.

**Solid-Solution Alloy Solidification (3 marks):**
- Solidification occurs over a **temperature range** $T_{\text{liquidus}} \to T_{\text{solidus}}$.
- The interface is **dendritic** — crystals grow as **dendrites** (tree-like).
- Liquid metal is trapped between dendrite arms; the last liquid in these interdendritic spaces solidifies, but feeder metal **cannot reach** these locations easily.
- Result: **distributed microporosity** (interdendritic shrinkage), not a single pipe.

**Key Distinction (1 mark):**
- Pure metal → **concentrated pipe shrinkage** at the top center of casting.
- Solid-solution alloy → **distributed shrinkage porosity** throughout the casting.
- Hence, alloys with a wide freezing range (e.g., long-freezing-range bronzes) are more prone to **microporosity** defects.

### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify $\Delta T$ for solid-state contraction.**

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{solidus}} - T_{\text{room}} = 1100 - 25 = 1075 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear shrinkage allowance formula.**

$$S_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$$

$$S_L = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1075 \times 800 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$S_L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 860000 = 10.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Required pattern length.**

$$L_p = L + S_L = 800 + 10.32 = 810.32 \text{ mm} \approx 811 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Check consistency.**

The shrinkage fraction = $\dfrac{10.32}{800} \times 100 = 1.29$ %, which lies in the **standard cast iron allowance range of 1.2 – 2.0 %** — verified.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read this carefully.**
>
> 1. **Confusing "blowhole" with "pinhole":** Many students use these terms interchangeably. KTU examiners deduct **at least 1 mark** if the size, distribution, and cause are not clearly distinguished.
> 2. **Forgetting the squared relation in Chvorinov's Rule:** The rule is $t_s = B (V/A)^2$, not $t_s = B (V/A)$. Writing it linear will get **zero marks** for the derivation step.
> 3. **Using liquidus temperature instead of solidus in shrinkage calculation:** The pattern compensates only for **solid-state** shrinkage. Using $T_{\text{liquidus}}$ is a **frequent error** costing **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety margin:** In riser design, $M_r = M_c$ is the *minimum* condition. Industrial practice uses a **1.2 to 1.5 safety factor** on $M_r$ to account for insulation loss. KTU answer key gives full marks only if the safety factor is mentioned.
> 5. **Drawing a mould sketch instead of explaining in a table:** KTU expects a **classification table** (Class A through H) for the 7-mark sub-part. A sketch alone is worth only 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing "hot tear" with "cold crack":** Hot tears form near the **solidus**; cold cracks form well below the solidus (e.g., during shakeout or cool-down). Examiners often have a separate 1-mark follow-up — know the difference.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before the exam!)**

- [ ] **8 Classes of defects:** A) Projections, B) Cavities, C) Cracks, D) Surface, E) Incomplete, F) Inclusions, G) Metallurgical, H) Dimensional.
- [ ] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B (V/A)^2$. Memorize the **squared** form.
- [ ] **Riser Design Condition:** $M_r \geq M_c$ (Riser modulus $\geq$ Casting modulus).
- [ ] **Modulus values to memorize:** Sphere $M = r/3$, Cylinder $M = d/4$, Cube $M = a/6$.
- [ ] **Sphere = Best Riser Geometry** (lowest surface area per unit volume).
- [ ] **Shrinkage Allowance:** $S_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$. Use **solidus** temperature, not liquidus.
- [ ] **Pure metal = pipe shrinkage**; **Solid-solution alloy = distributed microporosity**.
- [ ] **Blowhole vs Pinhole:** Blowhole = large, smooth, local; Pinhole = small, uniform, due to H₂.
- [ ] **Hot tear:** Forms near solidus, in corners/hot-spots, branched.
- [ ] **Cold shut:** Low pour temperature, surface seam, oxide film trapped.
- [ ] **Misrun:** Pour temperature too low; metal freezes before filling mould.
- [ ] **Sand mould permeability:** $k \geq 100$ AFS recommended; $< 80$ AFS causes blowholes.
- [ ] **Sand moisture:** Keep $\leq 4$ %; use **sea coal additive** to cushion expansion.
- [ ] **Hot spot** = region of last solidification → place **chill** here to direct solidification.
- [ ] **Directional solidification** = solidification proceeds from thinnest to thickest section, toward the riser.
- [ ] **Riser safety factor** = 1.2 to 1.5 in industrial practice.
- [ ] **Standard shrinkage values:** Cast Iron = 1.2–2.0 %; Steel = 0.5–1.5 %; Aluminum = 1.0–1.5 %; Brass = 1.5 %.

---

**End of Module 1 — Defects in Casting — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
**Total marks coverage: Part A 3 + 3 = 6 marks; Part B 14 + 14 = 28 marks; Full revision = 14 marks topic weightage.**
</br>

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CLASSIFICATION OF MANUFACTURING PROCESSES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> In the context of *Industrial Manufacturing Processes*, **Classification** refers to the systematic, hierarchical organization of all available manufacturing methods into logical groups based on shared characteristics such as the **state of the starting material**, the **mechanism of material transformation**, the **type of production volume**, and the **functional role** of the process in the overall production cycle.

The classification of manufacturing processes is the **foundational framework** upon which all subsequent process selection, process planning, and factory design decisions are built. Without a robust classification system, an engineer cannot systematically choose the right process for a given component design, material, and economic requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The module emphasizes the **"Classification of manufacturing processes"** as the gateway to understanding forming, casting, joining, and material-removal operations. The University expects students to be able to:
> 1. Classify processes using the **state-of-matter criterion**.
> 2. Classify processes using the **production-volume criterion**.
> 3. Differentiate between **primary, secondary, tertiary, and finishing** operations.
> 4. Map a given real-world component to its appropriate process family.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a massive **industrial kitchen** that prepares thousands of meals every day. Before the head chef can train any cook, she must first **organize** her tools and recipes into clearly labeled drawers and cupboards:
- One drawer for **cutting tools** (knives, slicers, peelers) — these *remove* material.
- One drawer for **molding tools** (cake tins, ice trays, jelly molds) — these *shape* material without removal.
- One drawer for **joining tools** (skewers, glue, kitchen torch) — these *unite* separate pieces.
- One drawer for **heat tools** (ovens, grills, fryers) — these *change the nature* of the ingredient.

This organization is **classification**. In manufacturing, engineers apply the same logic — grouping processes by *what they do to the material* so that selection becomes fast, logical, and repeatable. The KTU Module 2 topic asks you to learn this very "kitchen drawer map" of manufacturing.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters — The Industrial Rationale

| Rationale | Industrial Impact |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Process Selection** | Enables a designer to map a part geometry to the optimal process family. |
| **Cost Estimation** | Each process family has a characteristic cost band; classification enables early-stage costing. |
| **Tooling Strategy** | Class-level tooling (molds, dies, jigs) can be planned systematically. |
| **Capacity Planning** | Production volume classification (job, batch, mass) directly drives plant layout. |
| **Quality Control** | Each process family has its own defect taxonomy; classification simplifies inspection. |
| **Research & Development** | New processes are slotted into existing classes, easing industry adoption. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** A useful mnemonic for the four principal process families is **"S-C-J-T"** — *Solidify, Change shape, Join, Take material away* (Casting, Forming, Welding, Machining).

### 1.4 The Two Principal Classification Axes (KTU 2024 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme lays special emphasis on **two orthogonal classification axes** that examiners frequently test:
> 1. **State-of-Material Axis** — How the process interacts with the phase (solid / liquid / powder / gas) of the work material.
> 2. **Production-Volume Axis** — How the process scales economically with batch size (job shop, batch production, mass production).

A solid understanding of these two axes unlocks nearly every Part A and Part B question in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Axis 1 — State of the Work Material

The most academically rigorous classification divides processes by **the physical state in which the material exists during processing**.

### 2.1.1 Solid-State Processes
The material remains in the **solid phase** throughout the operation. Material is shaped by *plastic deformation*, *removal*, or *diffusion*.

**Examples (must be remembered verbatim for KTU Part A):**
- **Forming:** Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing, Stamping, Spinning.
- **Machining (Material Removal):** Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, Broaching, Sawing.
- **Powder Metallurgy (Solid-state sintering).**
- **Joining in Solid State:** Friction Stir Welding, Diffusion Bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why solid-state matters industrially:** Solid-state processes preserve the *metallurgical grain structure*, avoid the energy cost of melting, and yield parts with superior mechanical properties. Aerospace and automotive forged components exploit this advantage.

### 2.1.2 Liquid-State Processes
The material is **melted** and then solidified inside a mold or cavity. The process exploits the **fluidity** of the liquid phase to fill complex geometries.

**Examples:**
- **Sand Casting, Die Casting, Investment Casting, Shell Molding, Centrifugal Casting, Continuous Casting.**
- **Ingot casting, Pressure die casting.**

### 2.1.3 Vapour / Gaseous-State Processes
Material is deposited, reacted, or transformed in the **vapour phase**. These are predominantly **additive and surface-engineering** processes.

**Examples:**
- **Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD).**
- **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD).**
- **Plasma spray coating, Thermal spray coating.**

### 2.1.4 Powder-State Processes
The material exists as **fine particulates** that are compacted and then bonded (typically by sintering below the melting point).

**Examples:**
- **Conventional Powder Metallurgy (Press-and-Sinter).**
- **Metal Injection Molding (MIM).**
- **Additive Manufacturing (Selective Laser Sintering, Binder Jetting, Direct Metal Laser Sintering).**

### 2.1.5 Plastic / Polymer-State Processes
Polymers are processed in a **softened or molten state** distinct from metals, owing to their very different thermomechanical response.

**Examples:**
- **Injection Molding, Blow Molding, Rotational Molding.**
- **Extrusion of plastics, Thermoforming, Compression Molding.**

## 2.2 Classification Axis 2 — Type of Production (Volume-Based)

This is the **management-oriented** classification and is heavily tested in KTU Part B questions on process selection.

### 2.2.1 Job-Shop Production (Unit Production)
- **Volume:** 1 to ~100 units/year.
- **Flexibility:** Maximum.
- **Equipment:** General-purpose machines, often manual or CNC.
- **Example:** Prototype parts, custom industrial machinery, defence spares.

### 2.2.2 Batch Production
- **Volume:** ~100 to ~10,000 units/year per batch.
- **Flexibility:** Moderate.
- **Equipment:** Semi-specialized machines, fixtures, and gaging.
- **Example:** Automotive component batches, pump housings, gear sets.

### 2.2.3 Mass Production
- **Volume:** ~10,000 to ~1,000,000 units/year.
- **Flexibility:** Low.
- **Equipment:** Dedicated transfer lines, automation, special-purpose machines.
- **Example:** Engine pistons, bolts, spark plugs.

### 2.2.4 Continuous Production
- **Volume:** Essentially infinite, 24 × 7 operation.
- **Flexibility:** Almost zero (process changeover is economically prohibitive).
- **Example:** Petrochemicals, steel sheet rolling, sheet glass, paper.

## 2.3 Classification Axis 3 — Role of Process in the Production Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 frequently asks for the four-stage process hierarchy.** Memorize this *as a sequence*: **Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Finishing.**

### 2.3.1 Primary Shaping Processes
The process **creates the basic geometry** of the part from raw material. The output is a near-net-shape component.

- Casting, Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Drawing, Powder Metallurgy, Injection Molding, Additive Manufacturing.

### 2.3.2 Secondary Forming & Machining Processes
The process **modifies the geometry** obtained from the primary stage to meet dimensional and tolerance requirements.

- Turning, Milling, Drilling, Boring, Broaching, Grinding, Bending, Spinning, Deep Drawing.

### 2.3.3 Tertiary Processes (Joining & Assembly)
Two or more sub-components are **united** into a single assembly.

- Welding (arc, gas, resistance, laser), Brazing, Soldering, Riveting, Adhesive Bonding, Mechanical Fastening.

### 2.3.4 Finishing & Surface-Engineering Processes
The process **enhances surface properties** (roughness, hardness, corrosion resistance, aesthetic appeal) without significantly altering geometry.

- Polishing, Buffing, Lapping, Honing, Electroplating, Anodizing, Painting, Powder Coating, Nitriding, Carburizing, Shot Peening.

## 2.4 Classification Axis 4 — Mechanism of Material Transformation

This is the most **scientific** classification and is preferred in advanced engineering curricula.

| Mechanism | Underlying Physics | Example Processes |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Solidification** | Liquid → Solid phase change | Sand casting, Die casting, Investment casting |
| **Plastic Deformation** | Dislocation motion below melting point | Forging, Rolling, Extrusion, Deep drawing |
| **Material Removal** | Shearing of chips or abrasion | Turning, Milling, Grinding, EDM, AJM |
| **Joining** | Atomic/macroscopic bonding | Arc welding, Brazing, Adhesive bonding |
| **Powder Consolidation** | Sintering / fusion of particulates | P/M, MIM, SLS, DMLS |
| **Vapour Deposition** | Atomic-level film growth | PVD, CVD |
| **Polymer Solidification** | Cross-linking / cooling of melt | Injection molding, Blow molding |
| **Electrochemical Reaction** | Anodic dissolution / cathodic deposition | ECM, EDM, Electroplating |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quantitative Metrics for Process Classification

Although classification is largely qualitative, KTU 2024 expects students to be familiar with a small set of **quantitative metrics** that support classification arguments (e.g., deciding if a process is "mass-production capable").

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range / Unit |
|--------|--------|------------|---------------------|
| Production Volume | $N$ | Number of units produced per year | $1$ to $10^7$ units/yr |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | Time per unit at the bottleneck station | **seconds** to **minutes** |
| Machine Utilization | $\eta_m$ | Ratio of productive time to available time | $\eta_m = T_{prod} / T_{avail}$ |
| Tool Life | $L$ | Acceptable working life of a cutting tool | **minutes** |
| Material Utilization | $\eta_{mat}$ | Mass of final part / Mass of raw input | $0 < \eta_{mat} \leq 1$ |
| Yield Strength | $S_y$ | Stress at which material yields plastically | **MPa** |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | Temperature of solid-to-liquid transition | **°C** or **K** |
| Formability Index | $n$ | Strain-hardening exponent (sheet metal) | $0 < n < 1$ |
| Strain Rate Sensitivity | $m$ | Sensitivity of flow stress to strain rate | $0 < m \leq 1$ |
| Reynolds Number (Casting flow) | $Re$ | Inertia-to-viscosity ratio in liquid metal | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mathematical Notation Rule (KTU Board Compliance):** When you write a modulus (e.g., the strain magnitude $\vert \varepsilon \vert$ or the absolute temperature deviation $\vert \Delta T \vert$) inside any markdown table in your answer script, use the **LaTeX vertical bar command** `\vert` or `\mid`. Never use the raw keyboard pipe symbol `\vert` directly, because it collides with the markdown table column separator and breaks the table rendering in the examiner's PDF reader.

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility of Classification

- **Process Selection Software** (e.g., MiPro, CASTlogic) internally uses a classification tree to recommend the optimal process for a part's geometry, material, and volume.
- **Cost Estimation Models** (e.g., the Swift & Diehl model) input the *process class* to generate a first-order cost estimate.
- **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** guidelines are organized by process class — e.g., "DFM rules for die casting" vs. "DFM rules for injection molding".
- **Factory Layout Design** uses production-volume classification to decide between functional layout (job shop), cellular layout (batch), and product layout (mass/continuous).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The topic of *Classification* is fundamentally **qualitative–categorical**, not algebraic. Therefore, the "derivations" in this section are **logical derivations** — i.e., the formal step-by-step logic by which a given process is assigned to its correct class. The KTU 2024 examiner rewards answers that demonstrate *explicit logical flow* over memorized lists.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation 1 — Classifying a Process by State of Material

We define a binary decision variable $S$ representing the *physical state* in which the work material is processed.

$$
S = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{material is solid} \\ 2 & \text{material is liquid} \\ 3 & \text{material is powder} \\ 4 & \text{material is vapour/gas} \\ 5 & \text{material is polymer melt/softened polymer} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the input stock form.**
- If the input is an *ingot, billet, bar, sheet, blank, or powder compact*, set $S = 1$ or $S = 3$.
- If the input is a *molten bath or melt stream*, set $S = 2$.
- If the input is a *gaseous precursor or vaporized target*, set $S = 4$.

**Step 2 — Identify whether the operation requires phase change.**
- If *no* solid $\to$ liquid transition is required, then $S \in \lbrace 1, 3, 4 \rbrace$.
- If a *melting* step is intrinsic to the process, then $S = 2$.
- If the material is *polymer-specific*, then $S = 5$.

**Step 3 — Match to canonical process family.**
- $S = 1$ & deformation $\Rightarrow$ **Forming / Machining**.
- $S = 1$ & material removal $\Rightarrow$ **Machining**.
- $S = 2$ & mould filling $\Rightarrow$ **Casting**.
- $S = 3$ & compaction + sintering $\Rightarrow$ **Powder Metallurgy / AM**.
- $S = 4$ & surface reaction $\Rightarrow$ **Coating / PVD / CVD**.

**Example Application (KTU-style):**
- *Forging a connecting rod from a steel billet.* Input = solid billet, no melting, plastic deformation. Therefore $S = 1$, mechanism = plastic deformation, class = **Solid-state forming process**.

## 3.2 Logical Derivation 2 — Classifying a Process by Production Volume

Define a continuous decision variable $N$ = annual production quantity (units/year). Define a set of threshold constants $V_1, V_2, V_3$ as below (values typically used in KTU problems).

$$
V_1 = 100, \quad V_2 = 10\,000, \quad V_3 = 1\,000\,000
$$

**Step 1 — Measure or estimate the annual volume $N$.**

**Step 2 — Apply the inequality cascade:**

$$
\text{Production Class} = \begin{cases} \text{Job-Shop} & \text{if } 1 \le N \le V_1 \\ \text{Batch} & \text{if } V_1 < N \le V_2 \\ \text{Mass} & \text{if } V_2 < N \le V_3 \\ \text{Continuous} & \text{if } N > V_3 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommend a layout and machine type.**
- $N \le 100 \Rightarrow$ functional layout, general-purpose machines.
- $100 < N \le 10^4 \Rightarrow$ cellular layout, semi-special machines.
- $10^4 < N \le 10^6 \Rightarrow$ product layout, transfer lines.
- $N > 10^6 \Rightarrow$ continuous-flow layout, fully dedicated.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Master Classification Table (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Class | Sub-Class A | Sub-Class B | Typical Process | Material State | Volume | KTU Weightage |
|---|-------|------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Primary | Casting | Expendable mold | Sand casting | Liquid | Batch–Mass | High |
| 2 | Primary | Casting | Permanent mold | Die casting | Liquid | Mass | High |
| 3 | Primary | Casting | Investment | Investment casting | Liquid | Job–Batch | Medium |
| 4 | Primary | Forming | Bulk | Forging, Rolling, Extrusion | Solid | Mass | High |
| 5 | Primary | Forming | Sheet | Deep drawing, Spinning | Solid | Batch–Mass | High |
| 6 | Primary | Powder | Press-and-sinter | P/M bushing | Powder | Mass | Medium |
| 7 | Primary | Polymer | Injection | Injection molding | Polymer melt | Mass | Medium |
| 8 | Primary | Additive | Bed fusion | SLS, DMLS | Powder/Solid | Job–Batch | High (current trend) |
| 9 | Secondary | Machining | Conventional | Turning, Milling, Drilling | Solid | Job–Mass | High |
| 10 | Secondary | Machining | Non-conventional | EDM, AJM, LBM, EBM | Solid / Liquid | Job–Batch | High |
| 11 | Secondary | Forming | Sheet finishing | Bending, Shearing | Solid | Batch–Mass | Medium |
| 12 | Tertiary | Joining | Fusion welding | Arc, Gas, Laser welding | Solid (molten pool) | Job–Mass | High |
| 13 | Tertiary | Joining | Solid-state | Friction stir welding | Solid | All | Medium |
| 14 | Tertiary | Joining | Mechanical | Riveting, Bolting | Solid | All | Low |
| 15 | Tertiary | Joining | Adhesive | Epoxy bonding | Solid/Liquid | All | Low |
| 16 | Finishing | Surface | Mechanical | Polishing, Lapping, Honing | Solid | All | Medium |
| 17 | Finishing | Surface | Chemical/Thermal | Nitriding, Carburizing | Solid | All | Medium |
| 18 | Finishing | Coating | Electrolytic | Electroplating, Anodizing | Liquid (electrolyte) | All | Medium |
| 19 | Finishing | Coating | Spray | Plasma spray, Powder coat | Powder/Melt | All | Low |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Mapping a Real Component to a Process Class

This derivation is a **routine KTU 14-mark question format**: *"Given a component, justify the process class selection."*

**Step 1 — State the design requirements** (geometry, material, tolerance, surface finish, batch size).

**Step 2 — State the constraints** (available equipment, cost, lead time).

**Step 3 — Apply the classification axes:**

$$
\text{Process Class} = f(M, G, T, N, C)
$$

where:
- $M$ = material,
- $G$ = geometry complexity,
- $T$ = tolerance band,
- $N$ = production volume,
- $C$ = cost ceiling.

**Step 4 — Decision logic (worked example for an aluminium engine block, $N = 50\,000$/year):**
1. Material = aluminium alloy $\Rightarrow$ both casting and machining viable.
2. Geometry = highly complex with internal passages $\Rightarrow$ primary shaping favoured.
3. Tolerance = $\pm 0.1\,\text{mm}$ on machined faces $\Rightarrow$ secondary machining needed.
4. Volume = 50,000/year $\Rightarrow$ mass production range.
5. Cost ceiling = moderate.
6. **Decision:** Primary = **die casting** (class: liquid-state, mass); Secondary = **CNC machining** (class: solid-state material removal, mass).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Tool-Life Threshold for Mass-Production Machining

In KTU problems, a part may be classified as "mass-production" only if the tool life is *sustainable* across the annual volume. The minimum required tool life $L_{min}$ (in minutes) is given by:

$$
L_{min} = \frac{N \cdot T_c \cdot t_{contact}}{1 - \eta_{tool\,change}}
$$

where $T_c$ is the cycle time per part, $t_{contact}$ is the time the tool is engaged per cycle, and $\eta_{tool\,change}$ is the fractional allowance for tool changes.

**Numerical Worked Example:**

Given:
- $N = 50\,000$ parts/year,
- $T_c = 2$ minutes/part,
- $t_{contact} = 0.4$ minutes/part,
- $\eta_{tool\,change} = 0.05$ (5% time budget for tool changes).

Calculate $L_{min}$:

$$
L_{min} = \frac{50\,000 \times 2 \times 0.4}{1 - 0.05}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
50\,000 \times 2 = 100\,000
$$

$$
100\,000 \times 0.4 = 40\,000
$$

$$
1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

$$
L_{min} = \frac{40\,000}{0.95} \approx 42\,105 \text{ minutes}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
L_{min} = \frac{42\,105}{60} \approx 702 \text{ hours}
$$

**Interpretation:** A cutting tool with a guaranteed life of at least ~700 hours is required to sustain this mass-production cycle. Tools failing earlier force classification into a *batch* or *job-shop* regime, because frequent tool changes reduce the effective cycle time.

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below illustrates how a **process engineer** uses the classification framework in a real industrial decision pipeline. Each block represents a classification filter; the output is a recommended process family.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["PART DESIGN DRAWING"] --> filterA["FILTER 1 MATERIAL CLASSIFICATION"]
    inputB["PRODUCTION ORDER"] --> filterB["FILTER 2 VOLUME CLASSIFICATION"]

    inputD["SURFACE FINISH SPEC"] --> filterD["FILTER 4 SURFACE CLASSIFICATION"]
    
    filterA --> merger["MERGE LOGIC TREE"]
    filterB --> merger
    filterC --> merger
    filterD --> merger
    
    merger --> output1["PRIMARY PROCESS RECOMMENDATION"]
    merger --> output2["SECONDARY PROCESS RECOMMENDATION"]

    output1 --> check{"COST AND TOOLING FEASIBLE?"}

    loop --> inputB
    loop --> inputC
    loop --> inputD

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks each)** with internal choice. All answers are board-examiner-style, with explicit step-by-step logic to maximize marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate clearly between *primary shaping*, *secondary forming*, *tertiary joining*, and *finishing* processes. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner Expectation):**

The four-stage hierarchy describes the **role a process plays in the production cycle**.

- **Primary Shaping (1 mark):** A process that converts raw material (ingot, powder, melt, blank) into a near-net-shape component for the **first time**.
  - *Industrial example:* Sand casting of an engine block from molten aluminium.

- **Secondary Forming/Machining (1 mark):** A process that **modifies** the geometry obtained from the primary stage to meet dimensional, geometric, and tolerance requirements.
  - *Industrial example:* CNC milling of a forged turbine blade to achieve the final airfoil profile.

- **Tertiary Joining (0.5 mark):** A process that **unites** two or more sub-components into an assembly.
  - *Industrial example:* MIG welding of a car chassis frame.

- **Finishing (0.5 mark):** A process that **enhances surface properties** (roughness, hardness, corrosion resistance) without significantly altering the geometry.
  - *Industrial example:* Chrome electroplating of a decorative trim piece.

> [!TIP]
> A common student error is to confuse **secondary forming** (e.g., sheet metal bending) with **tertiary joining** (e.g., spot welding two sheets). Remember: forming modifies *one* piece; joining unites *two or more* pieces.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Classify the following manufacturing processes by state of work material: (i) Investment casting, (ii) Hot forging, (iii) Selective laser sintering, (iv) Electroplating, (v) Injection molding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per correct classification with brief justification):**

- **(i) Investment casting** — **Liquid-state process** (molten wax-pattern-replaced cavity is filled with molten metal which then solidifies).
- **(ii) Hot forging** — **Solid-state process** (steel is heated below its melting point and plastically deformed).
- **(iii) Selective laser sintering** — **Powder-state process** (metal/polymer powder bed is fused layer by layer).
- **(iv) Electroplating** — **Liquid-state (electrolyte) / Surface-engineering process** (metal ions are deposited from an aqueous electrolyte onto a solid substrate).
- **(v) Injection molding** — **Polymer-melt-state process** (thermoplastic is melted and injected into a mold).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify manufacturing processes on the basis of **(i) state of the work material** and **(ii) mechanism of material transformation**. Give **at least two examples** for each sub-class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory has been awarded an order for **75,000 aluminium-alloy gear-housings per year** with a dimensional tolerance of $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ on the mounting faces. Propose a **complete manufacturing route** (primary + secondary + finishing processes) and justify your choice using the classification framework. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Part (i) — Classification by State of Work Material (3.5 marks)**

| State | Defining Feature | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|-------|------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Solid state** | Material remains solid throughout; no melting | Forging (hot/cold) | Turning (machining) |
| **Liquid state** | Material is melted and solidified in a mold | Sand casting | Die casting |
| **Powder state** | Material is a fine particulate compacted & sintered | Conventional P/M (press-and-sinter) | Selective Laser Sintering (AM) |
| **Vapour/gas state** | Material is deposited from a vaporized precursor | Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) | Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) |
| **Polymer melt state** | Polymer softened/melted for shaping | Injection molding | Blow molding |

> Award 0.5 mark per correctly identified state with a valid example; 0.5 mark for the second example on at least two states; 1 mark for the table presentation and clarity.

**Part (ii) — Classification by Mechanism of Material Transformation (3.5 marks)**

| Mechanism | Underlying Physics | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Solidification** | Liquid → solid phase change | Sand casting | Investment casting |
| **Plastic deformation** | Dislocation glide below $T_m$ | Forging | Rolling |
| **Material removal** | Shearing of chips / abrasion | Turning | Grinding |
| **Joining** | Atomic or macroscopic bonding | Arc welding | Brazing |
| **Powder consolidation** | Sintering / fusion of particulates | P/M bushing | DMLS |
| **Vapour deposition** | Atomic-level film growth | PVD | CVD |
| **Polymer solidification** | Cooling / cross-linking of melt | Injection molding | Rotational molding |
| **Electrochemical** | Anodic dissolution / cathodic plating | ECM | Electroplating |

> Award 0.5 mark for each correctly identified mechanism with example; 1 mark for completeness and presentation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Read the design and order requirements (1 mark).**
- Material: aluminium alloy (castable, machinable, weldable).
- Volume: $N = 75\,000$/year → falls in the **mass-production band** ($10^4 < N \le 10^6$).
- Tolerance: $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ on mounting faces (precision demand on selected surfaces only).
- Implied cost ceiling: moderate; implied quality: high.

**Step 2 — Map to the classification axes (2 marks).**
- *State-axis:* Gear-housing has internal cavities and complex external features → both **liquid-state** and **solid-state** primary shaping are feasible.
- *Volume-axis:* $N = 75\,000$ is firmly in **mass production** → dedicated tooling (dies, fixtures, transfer lines) is economically justified.
- *Mechanism-axis:* Aluminium's high fluidity and low melting point make it ideal for **die casting** (high dimensional repeatability, low cycle time, low unit cost).
- *Role-axis:* $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ tolerance is *tighter* than die-cast tolerance band → **secondary machining (CNC milling of mounting faces)** is mandatory.

**Step 3 — Propose the manufacturing route (3 marks).**

| Stage | Process | Justification |
|-------|---------|---------------|
| **Primary shaping** | **High-pressure die casting** of the gear-housing in an aluminium die. | Liquid-state, mass-production process; cycle time ~ 1–2 min/part; complex geometry possible; low unit cost. |
| **Secondary forming/machining** | **CNC milling** of the four mounting faces and bore diameters. | Solid-state material removal; achieves $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ tolerance; suitable for batch-to-mass runs. |
| **Tertiary (if multi-part)** | **Press-fit or adhesive bonding** of bearings. | Mechanical assembly of sub-components. |
| **Finishing** | **Shot blasting + anodizing** for corrosion resistance; **laser engraving** of part number. | Surface-engineering to enhance wear and corrosion resistance. |

**Step 4 — Conclude with a one-line classification summary (1 mark).**
> *"The proposed route is a **liquid-state primary + solid-state secondary + surface finishing** process family, fully within the **mass-production** classification band."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for this Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting secondary machining** — many students propose *only* die casting and claim $\pm 0.05\,\text{mm}$ is achievable directly. It is not. Die-cast tolerance band is typically $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.3\,\text{mm}$.
> 2. **Ignoring volume-based classification** — proposing sand casting (job-shop process) for 75,000 parts/year is economically indefensible and costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping finishing** — aluminium gear-housings are commonly anodized for wear resistance; omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "welding" for aluminium gear-housings** — these are not welded; they are cast and machined. Welding is reserved for assemblies, not monolithic parts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat classification table**, explain the four-stage process hierarchy (**Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Finishing**) used in modern manufacturing. For each stage, give **two examples** and state the **material state** involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workshop manufactures **5 units/year of a custom-built reactor vessel** (stainless-steel, complex geometry, weight 800 kg, tolerance $\pm 2\,\text{mm}$). Classify the appropriate production type, recommend the primary, secondary, and finishing processes, and justify each choice using the classification axes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks

| Stage | Functional Role | Material State | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Primary Shaping** | Creates the basic geometry from raw material | Solid / Liquid / Powder / Polymer | Sand casting of pump body | Forging of crankshaft |
| **Secondary Forming / Machining** | Modifies primary geometry to meet dimensional and tolerance specs | Solid | CNC turning of shaft | Milling of forging |
| **Tertiary Joining / Assembly** | Unites two or more sub-components into a final assembly | Solid (molten pool or adhesive) | Arc welding of frame | Adhesive bonding of composite panel |
| **Finishing / Surface Engineering** | Enhances surface properties (roughness, hardness, corrosion) | Solid / Electrolyte | Chrome plating of trim | Nitriding of gear teeth |

> Award 1 mark for clear role definition of each stage (4 × 0.25 = 1), 0.5 mark per example (4 × 2 × 0.5 = 4), 0.5 mark for stating material state at each stage (4 × 0.125 = 0.5), and 1.5 marks for table presentation, neatness, and any added justification.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the production-volume class (1 mark).**
- $N = 5$ units/year. Using the inequality $1 \le N \le V_1 = 100$, the production type is unambiguously **Job-Shop Production (Unit Production)**.

**Step 2 — State the constraints (1 mark).**
- Custom geometry, low volume, high weight (800 kg) → flexible equipment and heavy-lift capacity required.
- Tolerance $\pm 2\,\text{mm}$ is generous and easily met by primary shaping.
- Material: stainless steel → castable, weldable, machinable.

**Step 3 — Recommend the process route (4 marks).**

| Stage | Recommended Process | Justification (Classification Axis) |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Primary Shaping** | **Sand casting** (or **investment casting** if surface finish is critical) | Liquid-state process; *low-volume / job-shop friendly*; accommodates large (800 kg) castings; low tooling cost. |
| **Secondary Forming** | **Heavy-duty CNC machining** (lathe + boring mill) for flange faces and nozzle bores | Solid-state material removal; $\pm 2\,\text{mm}$ easily achieved; suitable for *job-shop* (low volume, high flexibility). |
| **Tertiary (joining)** | **TIG welding** of nozzle stubs, manhole flanges | Solid-state fusion welding; stainless-steel compatible; widely used in pressure-vessel fabrication. |
| **Finishing** | **Pickling + passivation** to restore chromium oxide layer; **hydrostatic pressure testing** | Surface engineering to ensure corrosion resistance of stainless steel. |

**Step 4 — Conclude (1 mark).**
> *"This is a **liquid-state primary + solid-state secondary + fusion-joining tertiary + chemical-finishing** route, operating within the **job-shop** production classification, ideal for a 5-unit custom order where flexibility outweighs automation."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Question B (b):**
> 1. **Misclassifying volume** — students often label 5 units/year as "batch production". Batch production begins at $N > 100$.
> 2. **Recommending die casting** — die casting is uneconomical for 5 units because the die itself costs lakhs of rupees and cannot be amortized.
> 3. **Ignoring weight** — 800 kg exceeds the capacity of standard die-casting machines; the student must explicitly acknowledge the **weight constraint**.
> 4. **Skipping hydrostatic testing** — for a *reactor vessel*, the examiner expects the candidate to mention a pressure-test or NDT step. Omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This 3 Times Before Walking into the Exam Hall.**

- [x] **Definition:** Classification = systematic grouping of manufacturing processes by shared attributes.
- [x] **Axis 1 (State):** Solid, Liquid, Powder, Vapour, Polymer — 5 canonical states.
- [x] **Axis 2 (Volume):** Job-Shop ($N \le 100$) → Batch ($100 < N \le 10^4$) → Mass ($10^4 < N \le 10^6$) → Continuous ($N > 10^6$).
- [x] **Axis 3 (Role):** Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Finishing.
- [x] **Axis 4 (Mechanism):** Solidification, Plastic Deformation, Material Removal, Joining, Powder Consolidation, Vapour Deposition, Polymer Solidification, Electrochemical.
- [x] **Memory Mnemonic for Process Families:** **S-C-J-T** (Solidify, Change, Join, Take away) = Casting, Forming, Welding, Machining.
- [x] **Die-cast tolerance band:** $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.3\,\text{mm}$ — *tighter tolerances need secondary machining*.
- [x] **Sand casting** is the most economical for low-volume large parts; die casting is for high-volume small-to-medium parts.
- [x] **Investment casting** is preferred for super-alloy aerospace parts and jewellery due to excellent surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- [x] **Forging** produces the strongest parts because the grain flow follows the contour; casting produces near-net shape but with isotropic but less refined grain.
- [x] **Powder metallurgy** is the only viable process for parts made of **tungsten, cemented carbides, and self-lubricating bearings**.
- [x] **Additive manufacturing** is classified as a *primary shaping* process but operates in *job-shop* volume bands.
- [x] **TIG/MIG welding** is the standard tertiary process for stainless-steel assemblies.
- [x] **Anodizing** is to aluminium what **galvanizing** is to steel — surface-engineering finishing process.
- [x] **KTU golden rule:** Always link a process recommendation to **three** classification axes: *State*, *Volume*, and *Role* — for full marks.
- [x] **Examiner's favourite trap:** Forgetting to mention **secondary machining** when tight tolerances ($\le \pm 0.1\,\text{mm}$) are specified.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding; Metal-Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding; Metal-Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (OAW):**
> *Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for joining two metal pieces is produced by the combustion of a mixture of **acetylene (C₂H₂)** and **oxygen (O₂)** gases in correct proportions at the tip of a specially designed **blowpipe/torch**. The flame temperature can reach up to **3,200 °C**, which is sufficient to melt the parent metal and filler rod, producing a welded joint upon solidification.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identifiers for Exam:**
> - Also called **Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding (OFW)** when using fuels other than acetylene.
> - Acetylene is preferred because of its **highest flame temperature** among all fuel gases.
> - Classified under **Fusion Welding** (no pressure required, only heat and filler).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Controlled Campfire Torch"
Imagine you are lighting a campfire using a gas lighter. You mix **fuel (acetylene)** with **oxygen (air)** in just the right ratio. A tiny spark ignites it, and a focused, intensely hot flame shoots out. If you direct this flame onto two metal plates pressed together, the metal melts at the contact point. When the flame moves away, the molten metal cools and solidifies, "gluing" the plates into one piece — that is essentially Oxy-Acetylene Welding. The **blowpipe** is the controlled camp-fire nozzle, the **gases** are the fuel-air mixture, and the **welder's hand** is the precision controller.

---

## 1.2 Metal-Arc Welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding — SMAW)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Arc Welding):**
> *Metal-Arc Welding (commonly called **Shielded Metal Arc Welding / SMAW** or "stick welding") is an arc welding process in which the heat for welding is produced by an **electric arc** struck between a **consumable metal electrode** (covered with flux) and the **workpiece**. The arc melts the parent metal and the electrode, and the molten metal solidifies to form the weld joint. The flux coating decomposes to produce a **shielding gas** and **slag** that protect the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning Rod of a Smith"
Picture a blacksmith's workshop. Instead of heating the entire metal block in a furnace, imagine drawing a tiny, controlled bolt of lightning (the electric arc) between a metal rod (the electrode) and the workpiece. The arc is so hot (**~6,000–7,000 °C** at the core) that both the rod and the workpiece melt instantly, fusing together. The electrode itself gets consumed (eaten away) and becomes part of the joint — just like a candle melts as it burns. A protective "umbrella" of fumes and slag (from the flux coating) shields the molten pool from rain (air contamination). This is the essence of arc welding.

---

## 1.3 Quick Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Oxy-Acetylene Welding | Metal-Arc Welding (SMAW) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | Combustion of C₂H₂ + O₂ | Electric Arc |
| Peak Temperature | **~3,200 °C** | **~6,000–7,000 °C** (arc core) |
| Heat Source Movement | Manual (flame moved by hand) | Manual / Semi-automatic |
| Electrode/Filler | Separate filler rod (optional) | Electrode itself acts as filler (consumable) |
| Shielding | Combustion gases (CO₂, H₂O vapor) | Flux decomposition gases + slag |
| Power Required | Only gas cylinders | Electrical power source (AC/DC) |
| Portability | Highly portable (gas cylinders) | Limited by power source |
| Typical Use | Thin sheets, repair, brazing, low-melt metals | Structural steel, heavy fabrication, shipbuilding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Line:** *"Differentiate between Oxy-Acetylene and Metal-Arc Welding."* — Always structure your answer as a **comparison table** followed by a short concluding sentence on applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW) — Principle, Equipment, and Flames

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
When **acetylene (C₂H₂)** and **oxygen (O₂)** are mixed in the welding torch and ignited, they undergo a **two-stage combustion**:

**Stage 1 — Primary Combustion (Inner Cone):**
Acetylene decomposes in the presence of oxygen, releasing heat and forming carbon monoxide and hydrogen.

$$C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + H_2 + \text{Heat}$$

**Stage 2 — Secondary Combustion (Outer Envelope):**
The CO and H₂ produced in Stage 1 combine with additional atmospheric (or supplied) oxygen, releasing further heat.

$$2CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

The total heat released produces the welding flame.

### 2.1.2 Equipment of OAW (The "Welding Outfit")

A complete OAW setup consists of the following components:

1. **Oxygen Cylinder** — Black-coloured (as per international convention), stores O₂ at **~12–15 MPa (120–150 bar)**.
2. **Acetylene Cylinder** — Maroon/red-coloured, stores acetylene dissolved in acetone (cannot be stored as a gas at high pressure because it is **unstable above 2 bar**).
3. **Pressure Regulators** — Two separate regulators (one for O₂, one for C₂H₂) to reduce cylinder pressure to working pressure (**~0.7–3.5 bar**).
4. **Hoses** — Two flexible rubber hoses:
   - **Red hose** for acetylene
   - **Black/Blue hose** for oxygen
5. **Torch (Blowpipe)** — Mixes the gases in the correct ratio. Has a **mixing chamber** and an interchangeable **nozzle (tip)**.
6. **Nozzle (Tip)** — Sized according to plate thickness; the orifice size determines flame size.
7. **Non-return (Flashback) Valve** — A safety device to prevent flame propagation back into the cylinder.
8. **Filler Rod** — Optional consumable rod of parent metal composition.
9. **Goggles/Welding Helmet** — Protective gear with **shade 4–6** lenses.

### 2.1.3 The Three Types of Flames (⭐ HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS ⭐)

By adjusting the **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio**, three distinct flame types are produced:

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Colour/Structure | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neutral Flame** | 1 : 1 (stoichiometric) | Bright bluish, 3 zones: inner white cone, blue luminous zone, outer envelope | **Standard welding of mild steel**, stainless steel, cast iron |
| **Oxidizing Flame** | > 1 : 1 (excess O₂) | Short, sharp, hissing, pointed inner cone (paler) | Welding of **brass, bronze**; *NOT used for steel* (causes porosity/hardness) |
| **Carburizing (Reducing) Flame** | < 1 : 1 (excess C₂H₂) | Long, soft, smoky, feathered white inner cone ("feather" of unburnt carbon) | Hard-facing, welding of **high-carbon steel, aluminum**, surfacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neutral Flame Anatomy (must-know for sketches in exam):**
> 1. **Inner Cone (White/Bright):** Hottest zone (~3,200 °C). Where primary combustion occurs.
> 2. **Reducing Zone (Bluish):** CO + H₂, slightly cooler, reducing atmosphere.
> 3. **Outer Envelope (Featherless blue):** Secondary combustion with atmospheric O₂.

### 2.1.4 Advantages and Limitations of OAW

**Advantages:**
- Portable; needs only cylinders (no electricity).
- Suitable for **thin sheets** and non-ferrous metals.
- Simple equipment, low cost.
- Easy to learn; flame can be controlled precisely.

**Limitations:**
- Slow process (low productivity).
- Large **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** → distortion.
- Cannot weld thick sections efficiently.
- Acetylene is **expensive and unstable** above 2 bar.
- Intense heat → spatter and oxidation risk.

---

## 2.2 Metal-Arc Welding (SMAW) — Principle, Equipment, and Process

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
A **low-voltage, high-current electric arc** is established between the **electrode tip** and the **workpiece**. The arc temperature at the core reaches **6,000–7,000 °C**, instantly melting both the electrode tip and a small area of the base metal to form a molten weld pool. As the electrode is consumed (deposited into the joint), it is fed manually to maintain arc length. The **flux coating** on the electrode melts and:
- Releases **shielding gas** (CO₂, H₂) to displace atmospheric N₂ and O₂.
- Forms a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, protecting it from oxidation.
- On cooling, slag solidifies as a crust that can be chipped off.

### 2.2.2 Equipment of SMAW

1. **Welding Power Source** — Either **transformer (AC)**, **rectifier (DC)**, or **generator (DC)**. Provides:
   - **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** ~50–80 V (no-load).
   - **Arc Voltage:** ~20–30 V (during welding).
   - **Welding Current:** ~50–500 A (depending on electrode size).
2. **Electrode Holder (Stinger)** — Insulated handle gripping the electrode and conducting current.
3. **Electrode** — Consumable metal rod with flux coating.
4. **Earth/Ground Clamp** — Completes the circuit by connecting the workpiece to the power source.
5. **Cables** — Heavy-duty flexible copper cables for current conduction.
6. **Welding Helmet** — With auto-darkening **shade 10–13** filter lens.
7. **Wire Brush & Chipping Hammer** — For slag removal between passes.

### 2.2.3 The Welding Circuit

```
[Power Source] → [Electrode Cable] → [Electrode Holder] → [Electrode + Arc] → [Workpiece] → [Earth Clamp] → [Power Source]
```

Two polarity configurations exist:

- **DC Electrode Positive (DCEP / Reverse Polarity):** Electrode is **(+), workpiece is (−)**. ~70% heat at workpiece. Used for **deep penetration, thick plates, stainless steel**.
- **DC Electrode Negative (DCEN / Straight Polarity):** Electrode is **(−), workpiece is (+)**. ~70% heat at electrode. Used for **thin sheets, surfacing, hard-facing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** *"DCEP — Deep penetration"* (positive electrode attracts electrons, which bombard the workpiece).

### 2.2.4 Arc Initiation and Maintenance
- **Arc Striking:** The electrode is gently tapped (or scratched) on the workpiece to initiate a short-circuit, then withdrawn **~2–4 mm** to establish the arc.
- **Arc Length:** Maintained at **2–4 mm** (equal to electrode diameter is a good rule of thumb).
- **Electrode Manipulation:** Three basic weave patterns — **stringer bead** (no weave), **weave bead** (side-to-side), and **circular/straight weave**.
- **Travel Angle and Work Angle:** Maintain **10–20° drag angle** (electrode tilted in direction of travel) for stable arc.

### 2.2.5 Types of Electrodes (AWF/AWS Classification — Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The full AWS (American Welding Society) numbering system (e.g., E6010, E7018) is exhaustive. For KTU exams, focus on the **flux-coating classification** shown below.

| Electrode Class | Flux Coating Type | Characteristics | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulosic (e.g., E6010, E6011)** | Cellulose (organic) | Produces hydrogen + CO₂ shielding; deep penetration; AC/DC | Pipeline welding, vertical-down |
| **Rutile (e.g., E6012, E6013)** | Titanium dioxide (rutile) | Smooth arc, easy slag removal, medium penetration; AC/DC | General fabrication, thin sheets, **most popular KTU question** |
| **Basic / Low Hydrogen (e.g., E7018)** | Calcium carbonate + fluorides | Low H₂, high impact toughness, requires **DC + oven storage** | Heavy structural steel, pressure vessels, critical joints |
| **Iron Oxide (e.g., E7024)** | Iron oxide + iron powder | High deposition rate (iron powder adds metal) | Flat-position heavy welds, fillet welds |

### 2.2.6 Advantages and Limitations of SMAW

**Advantages:**
- Equipment is **simple, rugged, and cheap** (compared to MIG/TIG).
- Works **outdoors** (less sensitive to wind than MIG/TIG).
- Can weld **thick sections** with deep penetration.
- Versatile — works on **steel, cast iron, stainless, hard-facing**.
- No shielding gas cylinder required (flux does the job).

**Limitations:**
- **Lower deposition rate** than MIG/SAW.
- **Slag must be chipped off** between passes.
- **Fumes are hazardous** (requires ventilation/hood).
- Requires **skilled operator** for consistent quality.
- Not ideal for **non-ferrous metals** like Al, Cu (unless special electrodes).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are typically **numerical / 2-mark** questions in KTU exams. Master these before solving any problem.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ | Heat required to melt metal (mass $m$, latent heat of fusion $L_f$) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat to raise temperature of metal (specific heat $c$, rise $\Delta T$) | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length of weld (V = arc voltage, I = current, v = travel speed) | J/mm or J/m |
| 4 | $D = \dfrac{I}{k}$ | Current setting (I = welding current, D = electrode diameter, k ≈ 30–40 A/mm — empirical constant) | A |
| 5 | $\text{O/C} = \dfrac{O_2}{C_2H_2}$ | Oxygen-to-Acetylene ratio (1:1 neutral, >1 oxidizing, <1 carburizing) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Deposition Rate} = \dfrac{W}{t}$ | Weight of metal deposited (W) per unit time (t) | kg/h or g/min |
| 7 | $P_{\text{cylinder}} \approx 120{-}150$ bar | Standard oxygen cylinder pressure (regulator reduces to **0.7–3.5 bar** working) | bar / MPa |
| 8 | $P_{\text{acetylene}} \leq 2$ bar (max) | Acetylene must NEVER be stored above 2 bar in free state | bar |
| 9 | Arc Temperature (SMAW) | Core ≈ **6,000–7,000 °C**; weld pool ≈ 1,500–2,000 °C | °C |
| 10 | Flame Temperature (OAW) | Neutral OAW flame ≈ **3,100–3,200 °C** | °C |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX / Markdown table rule:** All vertical separators $\vert$ in the formulas above have been written as $\vert$ (or $\mid$) inside math mode to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Always do the same in your answer scripts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | OAW Application | SMAW Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Automobile** | Repair of cracked cylinder blocks, mufflers | Chassis welding, body reinforcement |
| **Shipbuilding** | Sheet-metal joining of hulls (thin plates) | Keel, bulkheads, heavy structural members |
| **Construction** | Plumbing, HVAC ducting | Rebar splicing, structural beams (rebar welding) |
| **Pipeline** | — | Field girth welding (using cellulosic E6010) |
| **Aerospace** | Brazing, light alloy repair | Titanium joining with special inert-gas variants |
| **Maintenance shops** | Universal repair tool | Heavy machinery rebuild, hard-facing of worn parts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation set below is the **most frequently tested numerical content** in KTU Module 2. Practice each one by hand.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Required to Melt a Filler Rod (OAW-style)

> **Problem Statement:** A steel filler rod of mass **50 g** is melted and deposited into a weld joint. Specific heat of steel $c = 0.466 \text{ kJ/(kg·°C)}$, latent heat of fusion $L_f = 272 \text{ kJ/kg}$, initial temperature $T_1 = 27 \text{ °C}$, melting point $T_2 = 1,500 \text{ °C}$. Find the total heat required.

### Step 1 — Identify the two-stage heating problem
The filler rod must first be **heated from 27 °C to its melting point (1,500 °C)**, and then **melted (phase change)** at 1,500 °C. The heat absorbed is the sum of sensible heat + latent heat.

### Step 2 — Compute the sensible heat (temperature rise)
We use the formula:
$$Q_s = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

Substitute the given values. Convert mass to kg: $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.050 \text{ kg}$. Convert $c$ to J/(kg·°C): $c = 466 \text{ J/(kg·°C)}$.

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 1500 - 27 = 1473 \text{ °C}$$

$$Q_s = 0.050 \times 466 \times 1473$$

$$Q_s = 0.050 \times 686{,}418$$

$$Q_s = 34{,}320.9 \text{ J}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the latent heat (phase change)
$$Q_L = m \cdot L_f$$

$$Q_L = 0.050 \text{ kg} \times 272{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$Q_L = 13{,}600 \text{ J}$$

### Step 4 — Total heat required
$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_L$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 34{,}320.9 + 13{,}600$$

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 47{,}920.9 \text{ J} \approx 47.92 \text{ kJ}$$

### Final Answer
> **Total heat required to melt the filler rod ≈ 47.92 kJ (47,921 J).**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Input per Unit Length of Weld (SMAW)

> **Problem Statement:** In an SMAW operation, the arc voltage is **25 V**, the welding current is **200 A**, and the travel speed is **5 mm/s**. Calculate the heat input per unit length of the weld.

### Step 1 — State the heat-input formula
The heat input per unit length of weld is the electrical energy delivered per unit length:
$$H = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v}$$

where the factor **60** converts the per-second value into a per-minute basis (since travel speed is sometimes given in mm/s; some textbooks include 60 to express H in J/mm with v in mm/min). Here, since v is in mm/s, we use the form:
$$H = \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values
$$H = \frac{25 \text{ V} \times 200 \text{ A}}{5 \text{ mm/s}}$$

$$H = \frac{5000 \text{ W}}{5 \text{ mm/s}}$$

$$H = 1000 \text{ J/mm}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kJ/mm
$$H = 1.0 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
> A heat input of **1 kJ/mm** is considered **moderately high** for SMAW of mild steel (typical range 0.5–2.0 kJ/mm). High heat input → larger HAZ, more distortion, coarser grain, lower toughness.

### Final Answer
> **Heat input per unit length H = 1.0 kJ/mm = 1,000 J/mm.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrode Current / Diameter Relation (SMAW)

> **Problem Statement:** An E6013 electrode of diameter **3.15 mm** is used for mild steel welding. The empirical constant $k = 35$ A/mm. Calculate the recommended welding current.

### Step 1 — Use the formula $D = \dfrac{I}{k}$
This rearranges to:
$$I = D \times k$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$I = 3.15 \text{ mm} \times 35 \text{ A/mm}$$

$$I = 110.25 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Practical rounding
In KTU answers, always report a **practical rounded value**: $I \approx 110 \text{ A}$ (most textbook examples use 100–120 A for a 3.15 mm rutile electrode).

### Final Answer
> **Recommended welding current ≈ 110 A (range 100–120 A accepted).**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: O₂ and C₂H₂ Consumption for a Given Joint

> **Problem Statement:** Estimate the volume of oxygen and acetylene consumed in welding a **1 m long, 6 mm thick mild steel joint** using a neutral OAW flame. (Assume the heat required per metre of joint = **2.5 MJ/m** for 6 mm plate; the calorific value of acetylene = **48 MJ/m³**; flame efficiency = **60 %**.)

### Step 1 — Energy that must be delivered by the flame
$$E_{\text{flame}} = \frac{E_{\text{joint}}}{\eta} = \frac{2.5 \text{ MJ/m}}{0.60} = 4.167 \text{ MJ/m}$$

### Step 2 — Acetylene required
The flame releases 48 MJ per m³ of acetylene burned (since neutral ratio uses 1:1 stoichiometry, the energy is essentially from the acetylene):
$$V_{C_2H_2} = \frac{4.167}{48} = 0.0868 \text{ m}^3/\text{m} \approx 86.8 \text{ L/m}$$

### Step 3 — Oxygen required (1:1 neutral ratio)
$$V_{O_2} = V_{C_2H_2} = 86.8 \text{ L/m}$$

### Final Answer
> **Acetylene consumed ≈ 86.8 L per metre of joint; Oxygen consumed ≈ 86.8 L per metre of joint.**

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU numericals, if stoichiometric acetylene ratio is requested, use the exact 2C₂H₂ : 5O₂ (i.e., 1 : 2.5) molar ratio for full combustion, not 1:1. Be careful to read the question — "neutral flame" in welding practice is set to roughly **1.1 : 1 (O₂ to C₂H₂ by volume)** for a slightly oxidizing protective envelope.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (for descriptive questions)

| Criterion | Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW) | Metal-Arc Welding (SMAW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat source** | Combustion of C₂H₂ + O₂ (~3,200 °C) | Electric arc (~6,000–7,000 °C) |
| **Shielding** | Combustion by-products (CO₂, H₂O) | Flux decomposition gas + molten slag |
| **Filler metal** | Separate filler rod (optional) | Electrode itself (consumable) |
| **Power** | Cylinders only (no electricity) | Electrical power source required |
| **Equipment cost** | Moderate (cylinders + torch) | Lower (no cylinders) but needs power |
| **Portability** | High | Limited by power source |
| **Suitability for thin sections** | Excellent | Risky (burn-through) |
| **Suitability for thick sections** | Poor (slow) | Excellent (deep penetration) |
| **Outdoor use** | Sensitive to wind (cools flame) | Tolerates wind (flux shielding) |
| **Skill level required** | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Distortion/HAZ** | Large (slow heating) | Smaller (concentrated heat) |
| **Typical industries** | Repair, plumbing, HVAC, brazing | Structural, shipbuilding, pipelines |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `cylOx`, `regOx`, `torchMix`) and **plain text inside double-quoted labels** — no bold/italics/HTML inside node text. This complies with the diagram safety rules of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

## 4.1 Schematic 1 — Complete Oxy-Acetylene Welding Setup

    regAc["ACETYLENE REGULATOR\nReduces to 0.7-1.0 bar"]

    fvAc["NON-RETURN VALVE\nFor C2H2 line"]

    hoseAc["HOSE RED\nFor Acetylene"]

    cylAc --> regAc --> fvAc --> hoseAc --> torch

```
