# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 2-mark conceptual / 3-mark direct definition.
> - **Part B:** 14-mark with internal choice (a) 7 + (b) 7 sub-parts.
> - Marks are mapped to **CO2 / CO3** of typical PBAUT404 syllabi.
> - Bloom's taxonomy levels range from **L1 (Remember)** to **L4 (Analyze)**.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define oxy-acetylene welding. With a neat sketch, describe the neutral flame and list its three zones.**

**Model Answer:**

> Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW) is a **fusion welding process** in which the joint is produced by heating the workpiece to its melting temperature using a flame obtained from the **combustion of acetylene (C₂H₂) and oxygen (O₂)** in correct proportions. No pressure is applied, and a separate filler rod may be used.
>
> A **neutral flame** is produced when the O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio is **1 : 1** (stoichiometric balance). It has **three distinct zones:**
>
> 1. **Inner Cone (White/Bright):** Hottest zone, ~3,200 °C. Primary combustion (C₂H₂ + O₂ → 2CO + H₂) occurs here. Welding is done with the inner cone tip held **2–3 mm above the workpiece**.
> 2. **Reducing Zone (Blue luminous zone):** Surrounds the inner cone; contains unburnt CO and H₂, providing a reducing atmosphere that protects the weld pool.
> 3. **Outer Envelope (Featherless blue flame):** Secondary combustion with atmospheric O₂; completes the oxidation of CO and H₂.
>
> The neutral flame is the **standard flame for welding mild steel, stainless steel, and cast iron**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the essential equipment used in shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> The essential equipment of SMAW is:
>
> 1. **Welding Power Source** (AC transformer or DC rectifier) — Supplies low-voltage, high-current electricity. Open-circuit voltage is **50–80 V**; arc voltage is **20–30 V**.
> 2. **Electrode Holder (Stinger)** — Insulated handle to grip the electrode and conduct current safely to the welder's hand.
> 3. **Welding Electrode** — Flux-coated consumable rod; provides filler metal and shielding gas/slag.
> 4. **Earth Clamp (Ground Clamp)** — Completes the electrical circuit by connecting the workpiece back to the power source.
> 5. **Cables** — Heavy-duty copper cables to carry welding current.
> 6. **Welding Helmet with Filter Lens** — Shade 10–13 lens protects eyes from UV/IR radiation and arc flash.
> 7. **Wire Brush and Chipping Hammer** — Used to remove **slag** between weld passes.
> 8. **Curettes / Fume Extractor** — For safe ventilation of harmful welding fumes.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every 14-mark question offers an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both choices are given below. The model answer for Question A is fully worked out; Question B is provided as a parallel practice set.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **principle of oxy-acetylene welding** with a neat diagram. Discuss the **three types of flames** produced and state the application of each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **principle, equipment, and working of metal-arc welding**. Differentiate between **AC and DC welding** with respect to polarity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (a) — Principle of OAW and Flame Types

**Principle (3 marks):**
Oxy-acetylene welding works on the principle of **combustion of a mixture of acetylene (C₂H₂) and oxygen (O₂)** in a specially designed blowpipe (torch). The combustion is a two-stage process:

**Stage 1 (Primary Combustion in the inner cone):**

$$C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + H_2 + \text{Heat (Primary)}$$

**Stage 2 (Secondary Combustion in the outer envelope):**

$$2CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

The total flame temperature reaches **3,100–3,200 °C**, sufficient to melt the parent metal and filler rod. The molten metal fuses and solidifies to form a metallurgical bond.

**Three Types of Flames (3 marks):**
*(See Section 2.1.3 table for complete details — reproduce it in your answer.)*

1. **Neutral Flame** — O₂ : C₂H₂ = **1 : 1**. Three zones. Used for mild steel, stainless steel, cast iron.
2. **Oxidizing Flame** — O₂ : C₂H₂ > **1 : 1**. Short, pointed, hissing. Used for **brass, bronze**.
3. **Carburizing Flame** — O₂ : C₂H₂ < **1 : 1**. Long, smoky, feathered inner cone. Used for **high-carbon steel, aluminum, hard-facing**.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Repair work, sheet-metal joining, brazing, thin-section welding, plumbing.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (b) — SMAW Principle, Equipment, and AC vs DC

**Principle (2 marks):**
In SMAW, an **electric arc** is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the **workpiece**. The arc temperature at the core is **6,000–7,000 °C**, melting both the electrode and the parent metal to form a weld pool. The flux decomposes to release shielding gas and forms a protective slag layer.

**Equipment (2 marks):** *(List from Section 2.2.2 — power source, electrode holder, electrode, earth clamp, cables, helmet, chipping hammer.)*

**Working (1 mark):**
The operator strikes an arc by touching the electrode to the workpiece and withdrawing it ~3 mm. The arc melts the electrode tip; filler metal transfers across the arc into the weld pool. The operator manually drags the electrode along the joint, maintaining a steady arc length. Slag is chipped off after each pass.

**AC vs DC Welding (2 marks):**

| Parameter | AC Welding | DC Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Alternates 50 times/sec | Fixed: DCEP or DCEN |
| Arc stability | Lower (extinguishes at zero-cross) | Higher (steady) |
| Equipment cost | Lower (transformer) | Higher (rectifier) |
| Application | General fabrication, rutile electrodes | Low-hydrogen, stainless, cellulosic, critical welds |
| Flicker/Blow effect | More pronounced | Minimized |

**Polarity Note:** DCEP = electrode positive, deep penetration. DCEN = electrode negative, faster melt rate, shallower penetration.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **equipment of oxy-acetylene welding** with a neat block diagram. List **any four safety precautions** to be observed during OAW. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Classify **welding electrodes** based on flux coating. Explain the **arc initiation procedure** and the **function of flux coating** in SMAW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer Outline — Q.B (a) — OAW Equipment & Safety

**Equipment (5 marks):** *(Reference Section 2.1.2 — draw the block diagram showing cylinders, regulators, hoses, flashback arrestor, torch, nozzle, filler rod.)*

**Four Safety Precautions (2 marks):**
1. Acetylene pressure must NEVER exceed **2 bar** in free state (unstable above this).
2. Keep cylinders **upright and chained**; away from heat sources.
3. Use **flashback arrestors** on both gas lines.
4. Use **approved welding goggles** (shade 4–6 for OAW, 10–13 for SMAW).
5. (Bonus) Ensure adequate **ventilation** to avoid CO and NOₓ buildup.

---

#### Model Answer Outline — Q.B (b) — Electrode Classification & Arc Initiation

**Electrode Classification by Flux (4 marks):** *(Reference Section 2.2.5 table — Cellulosic, Rutile, Basic/Low-Hydrogen, Iron-Oxide.)*

**Arc Initiation Procedure (2 marks):**
1. **Tapping method** — Tap electrode on workpiece, then quickly withdraw to ~3 mm.
2. **Scratching method** — Drag electrode like a match, then lift to ~3 mm.
3. Maintain **arc length = electrode diameter**.
4. Use **drag angle 10–20°** for stable arc.

**Function of Flux Coating (1 mark):**
- Releases **shielding gas** (CO₂, H₂) to displace air.
- Forms **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, blocking O₂/N₂.
- Adds **deoxidizers / alloying elements** to improve weld chemistry.
- Stabilizes the **arc** and controls **penetration profile**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make that cost marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the flame zones:** Many students write "the inner cone is the coolest zone." It is actually the **HOTTEST** zone (~3,200 °C). Wrong = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 2. **Writing the OAW chemical equation incorrectly:** The full reaction is $2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O$. Many students forget the balancing coefficients — partial credit only.
> 3. **Not labelling the SMAW diagram:** A diagram without labels for *power source, electrode holder, arc, weld pool, earth clamp* gets only 1 out of 2 diagram marks.
> 4. **Confusing DCEP and DCEN:** DCEP = electrode is **POSITIVE** (deep penetration). DCEN = electrode is **NEGATIVE** (less penetration, more melt). Reversed polarity = wrong answer.
> 5. **Skipping safety precautions in long answers:** Examiners specifically look for "acetylene pressure ≤ 2 bar" and "use of flashback arrestor" in OAW questions. Missing both = lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Using unlabelled sketches:** Always use a **pencil + ruler** for diagrams. A freehand "blob" with no labels = 0 marks.
> 7. **Mixing up OAW and SMAW applications:** OAW is **NOT** preferred for thick structural steel. SMAW is **NOT** ideal for thin sheets (burn-through). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> A high-density checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam:

- [x] **OAW = combustion of C₂H₂ + O₂**; flame temperature ~**3,200 °C**; process is **portable, no electricity**.
- [x] **OAW flame types:** Neutral (1:1), Oxidizing (>1:1), Carburizing (<1:1). **Inner cone is the hottest.**
- [x] **Neutral flame is the standard flame** for mild steel welding.
- [x] **Acetylene must NEVER exceed 2 bar** in free state — store dissolved in acetone.
- [x] **Oxygen cylinder is BLACK, Acetylene is MAROON** (international colour code).
- [x] **Hose colour code:** RED = acetylene, BLACK/BLUE = oxygen.
- [x] **SMAW = electric arc between electrode and workpiece**; arc temperature **6,000–7,000 °C** (core).
- [x] **OCV 50–80 V; Arc voltage 20–30 V** in SMAW.
- [x] **DCEP = Deep penetration** (electrode positive); **DCEN = shallow penetration**.
- [x] **Arc length ≈ 2–4 mm**; typically equal to electrode diameter.
- [x] **Flux functions:** Shielding gas + Slag formation + Arc stabilization + Deoxidation + Alloy addition.
- [x] **E6010 = cellulosic, deep penetration; E6013 = rutile, general purpose; E7018 = basic, low-hydrogen, critical joints.**
- [x] **Slag must be chipped off** between passes in SMAW.
- [x] **OAW is preferred for thin sheets and non-ferrous metals; SMAW is preferred for thick sections and structural steel.**
- [x] **Heat input formula:** $H = V \cdot I / v$ (in J/mm) — higher H = larger HAZ, more distortion.
- [x] **Electrode current sizing:** $I = D \times k$ where $k \approx 30{-}40$ A/mm.
- [x] **Master the comparison table** (Section 3.5) — it covers ~50% of 14-mark descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Resistance Welding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Resistance Welding (RW)** is a fusion welding process in which the coalescence of metals is produced by the heat obtained from the resistance of the workpieces to the flow of electric current through them, with the application of pressure and without the use of any filler metal or external heat source (such as a flame or arc). The process falls under the category of **Pressure Welding** and is governed by Joule's Law of Electric Heating. According to the **American Welding Society (AWS)**, resistance welding is defined as a group of welding processes wherein coalescence is produced by the heat obtained from the resistance of the workpieces to the flow of electric current, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Resistance welding is a high-priority topic for both Continuous Assessment (CAT) and End Semester Examinations (ESE). Students must master the **Heat Balance Equation**, the **Laws governing heat generation**, and the working principles of **Spot, Projection, Seam, and Flash-Butt welding**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to join two pieces of metal. Instead of using a flame or an electric arc (which would require a separate heat source and a filler rod), you simply pass a **massive surge of electric current directly through the two pieces** while squeezing them together. Think of it like this: - **Current (I)** is the "volume" of electricity flowing. - **Resistance (R)** is the "friction" the metal offers to that flow. - **Time (t)** is the "duration" you let the current run. When all three are combined, the friction at the **interface** between the two pieces becomes so intense that the metal locally melts. At the exact same moment, mechanical **pressure** is applied through copper electrodes, which forge the molten puddle into a solid metallic bond. As soon as the current stops, the metal solidifies — and you have a weld. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Consider a domestic electric iron pressing your clothes. The heating coil offers resistance to current, generating heat, while the iron's weight applies pressure to flatten wrinkles. In **Spot Welding** (used heavily in car body assembly at Maruti, Hyundai, and Tesla factories), the welding electrodes behave exactly like that iron — passing current, generating heat at the interface, and applying pressure simultaneously. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants The heat generated at the weld interface is governed by **Joule's First Law**, formulated by the British physicist **James Prescott Joule (1840s)**: $$H = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$ Where: - **H** = Heat generated in **Joules (J)** - **I** = Current in **Amperes (A)** (typically 2,000 – 20,000 A for spot welding) - **R** = Electrical resistance at the interface in **Ohms (Ω)** - **t** = Time of current flow in **seconds (s)** > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** The current in resistance welding is **enormous (thousands of amps)** but the voltage is extremely low (typically **2 V – 20 V**). This is why resistance welding machines are essentially **step-down transformers** with a massive turns ratio. A typical 440 V mains supply is reduced to just 4–10 V at the secondary, while the current is amplified 50–100 times. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Resistance Welding Processes Resistance welding is broadly classified into the following major types, all of which are part of the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus: | **Type** | **Joint Configuration** | **Typical Current** | **Common Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **Spot Welding** | Lap joint | 3,000 – 20,000 A | Automotive sheet metal | | **Projection Welding** | Lap joint with embossed projections | 5,000 – 25,000 A | Nuts, bolts, cross-wire welding | | **Seam Welding** | Lap joint (continuous) | 5,000 – 15,000 A | Fuel tanks, radiators, oil drums | | **Butt Welding (Upset)** | End-to-end joint | Up to 50,000 A | Rods, wires, tubes | | **Flash Butt Welding** | End-to-end joint with arcing | 25,000 – 200,000 A | Rails, heavy structural sections | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Questions in the ESE frequently test the distinction between **Spot vs. Projection Welding** and **Upset Butt vs. Flash Butt Welding**. Understand the differences in current flow, pressure application, and heat generation zone. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principle of Heat Generation The entire science of resistance welding rests on the principle of **localized Joule heating at the interface between two workpieces**. To understand *why* the interface (and not the bulk of the metal) melts, we must analyze the **resistance distribution** in a typical spot-welding circuit. ### 2.1.1 Resistance Components in a Spot-Welding Circuit The total electrical resistance encountered by the current path is composed of **five distinct resistances** in series: 1. **R₁ — Resistance of the top electrode (copper)**: Very low (≈ 10–50 μΩ) because copper is an excellent conductor. 2. **R₂ — Contact resistance between top electrode and top workpiece**: Small but significant (≈ 50–200 μΩ). 3. **R₃ — Bulk resistance of the top workpiece**: Low for thin sheets. 4. **R₄ — Contact resistance at the faying surface (weld interface)**: **HIGHEST** (≈ 200–1000 μΩ) — this is where most of the heat is generated. 5. **R₅ — Bulk resistance of the bottom workpiece**: Low for thin sheets. 6. **R₆ — Contact resistance between bottom workpiece and bottom electrode**: Small. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the interface melts first:** The **contact resistance at the faying surface (R₄)** is roughly **10–20 times greater** than the bulk resistance of the metal because: > - The actual contact between two flat metal surfaces is only at a few microscopic asperities (high spots). > - Surface oxides, films, and contaminants further increase resistance. > - As the metal softens with rising temperature, contact area increases, but the *peak* temperature always occurs at the interface during the heating cycle. ## 2.2 The Heat Balance Equation (Master Equation) Not all of the heat generated (H) is useful for welding. A significant portion is **lost** to the surrounding metal, the electrodes, and the environment via conduction, convection, and radiation. The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a thorough understanding of this **Heat Balance Equation**: $$H = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5$$ Or, expressed in a more analytically useful form: $$I^2 R t = (H_1 + H_2) + (H_3 + H_4 + H_5)$$ Where: | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **% of Total (typical)** | |---|---|---| | **H** | Total heat generated by I²R | 100% | | **H₁** | Heat used to bring the interface to melting point (sensible heat) | ~30% | | **H₂** | Heat used for actual fusion (latent heat of fusion) | ~10% | | **H₃** | Heat lost to the bulk workpieces by conduction | ~40% | | **H₄** | Heat lost to the copper electrodes by conduction | ~15% | | **H₅** | Heat lost to the surroundings by convection/radiation | ~5% | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality:** The welding efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as: > $$\eta = \frac{H_1 + H_2}{I^2 R t} \times 100\%$$ > Typically, $\eta$ lies between **60% – 80%** for well-designed spot welds. Inefficient welds occur when too much heat is lost to the electrodes (small electrode tips) or to the bulk metal (excessively thick sheets). ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Welding Process The following **five-stage sequence** describes the physical events during a single spot-weld cycle: - **Step 1 — Squeeze Time (Pre-heat / Approach):** The electrodes approach the workpiece and apply **electrode force (F)**. This compresses the surface asperities, increasing real contact area and reducing the initial contact resistance spike. - **Step 2 — Weld Time (Heat Generation Phase):** Current is passed through the electrodes and workpieces. Heat is generated at the interface according to $H = I^2 R t$. The temperature at the interface rises to the melting point of the metal (e.g., 1538 °C for steel, 660 °C for aluminum). - **Step 3 — Forge / Hold Time (Cooling under Pressure):** Current is switched off, but the electrodes continue to apply force. The molten puddle is **forged** into a solid nugget, expelling impurities and closing any shrinkage porosity. - **Step 4 — Off Time (Electrode Retraction):** The electrode force is released, and the electrodes retract. - **Step 5 — Re-approach:** For the next weld spot, the cycle repeats. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Welding Schedule:** A **Welding Schedule** is a set of prescribed values for **squeeze time, weld time, hold time, off time, electrode force, and welding current**. KTU frequently asks students to interpret a welding schedule for a given material thickness. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes **every key formula, constant, and conversion** required for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on Resistance Welding. | **#** | **Formula / Rule** | **Meaning / Variables** | **Typical Units / Values** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joule's Law of heat generation | $H$ in J, $I$ in A, $R$ in Ω, $t$ in s | | 2 | $I = \frac{V_s}{R_{total}}$ | Ohm's Law applied to the weld circuit | $V_s$ = secondary voltage (2–20 V) | | 3 | $\eta = \frac{H_1 + H_2}{I^2 R t}$ | Welding efficiency | 60% – 80% | | 4 | $H_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$ | Heat needed to melt the interface (sensible + latent) | $c$ = specific heat, $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion | | 5 | $t = \frac{H_{required}}{I^2 R \eta}$ | Required welding time for a given current | $t$ in seconds | | 6 | $F_{electrode} = k \cdot A_{nugget}$ | Electrode force proportional to nugget cross-section | $k$ = forging pressure (MPa) | | 7 | $N = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{V_p}{V_s} \right) \times \frac{I_s}{I_p}$ | Transformer turns ratio relationship | $V_p$ = primary, $V_s$ = secondary | | 8 | $d_{nugget} = 5 \sqrt{t}$ (approx.) | Empirical relation for spot weld nugget diameter in mild steel | $d$ in mm, $t$ = sheet thickness in mm | | 9 | $I_{weld} = k' \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Welding current proportional to sqrt of sheet thickness | Empirical constant $k'$ | > [!TIP] > **Avoid the pipe symbol trap:** In exam answer sheets, write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never as $|x|$, to maintain clean LaTeX rendering on digital platforms. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Resistance welding is the **backbone of mass-production manufacturing**, particularly in the following industries: - **Automotive Industry (60% of all resistance welds):** Car bodies typically have **4,000 – 5,000 spot welds**. Robotic spot-welding arms (e.g., KUKA, FANUC) perform 60–120 spots per minute. - **White Goods Manufacturing:** Washing machines, refrigerators, microwave ovens — sheet-metal cabinets are seam-welded and spot-welded. - **Aerospace (Limited Use):** Used for joining thin-gauge aluminum and titanium alloys in non-critical airframe sections. - **Electronics:** Battery tab welding, coil terminations. - **Construction / Railways:** Flash-butt welding of rails produces joints stronger than the parent rail metal. - **Wire and Cable Industry:** Cross-wire projection welding in mesh and reinforcement cages. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relating Welding Current to Sheet Thickness The most common KTU numerical problem asks the student to compute the **required welding current** for a given sheet thickness, or to determine the **weld time** for a specific current. We derive both here from first principles. ### 3.1.1 Assumption Setup Let us consider **two mild-steel sheets** of thickness $t$ (in mm) being joined by spot welding. The diameter of the weld nugget is empirically observed to be approximately: $$d_n = k_1 \sqrt{t} \quad \text{(empirical)}$$ For low-carbon steel, $k_1 \approx 5$, giving: $$d_n = 5 \sqrt{t} \quad \text{(in mm)}$$ The volume of the molten nugget is approximated as a cylinder of diameter $d_n$ and height equal to the sum of the two sheet thicknesses ($2t$): $$V_{nugget} = \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \times (2t)$$ ### 3.1.2 Mass and Heat Calculation The mass of metal to be melted is: $$m = \rho \times V_{nugget} = \rho \times \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \times 2t$$ The total heat required ($H_{req}$) consists of **sensible heat** to raise the metal to melting point plus **latent heat** for fusion: $$H_{req} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$$ Substituting $m$: $$H_{req} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \cdot 2t \cdot \left( c \cdot \Delta T + L_f \right)$$ ### 3.1.3 Equating to Joule's Heat The useful heat from Joule's effect equals: $$H_{useful} = \eta \cdot I^2 R t_{weld}$$ Setting $H_{useful} = H_{req}$: $$\eta \cdot I^2 R t_{weld} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \cdot 2t \cdot (c \cdot \Delta T + L_f)$$ ### 3.1.4 Solving for Welding Current Solving for $I$: $$I = \sqrt{ \frac{\rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2 \cdot 2t \cdot (c \cdot \Delta T + L_f)}{\eta \cdot R \cdot t_{weld}} }$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation of the result:** > - Doubling the sheet thickness **doubles** the heat required (since more metal must be melted). > - The required current scales with $\sqrt{t}$, which is why empirical rules of thumb (such as $I \propto \sqrt{t}$) work well in practice. > - Higher efficiency $\eta$ **reduces** the required current — this is why good electrode cooling (water-cooled Cu electrodes) improves weld quality. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam — May 2023 style):** > Two low-carbon steel sheets, each **2 mm thick**, are to be spot-welded. The welding current is **8000 A**, the interfacial contact resistance is **100 μΩ**, and the weld time is **0.3 s**. The welding efficiency is **75%**. Calculate: > **(a)** The total heat generated. > **(b)** The useful heat available for melting the interface. > **(c)** The heat lost to the surroundings and electrodes. ### 3.2.1 Part (a): Total Heat Generated We apply Joule's Law: $$H = I^2 R t$$ Substituting $I = 8000$ A, $R = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω, $t = 0.3$ s: $$H = (8000)^2 \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.3$$ $$H = 64{,}000{,}000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3$$ $$H = 64 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3$$ $$H = 6400 \times 0.3 = 1920 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{H = 1920 \text{ Joules}}$$ ### 3.2.2 Part (b): Useful Heat for Melting $$H_{useful} = \eta \times H = 0.75 \times 1920$$ $$\boxed{H_{useful} = 1440 \text{ Joules}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Part (c): Heat Lost By the heat balance equation: $$H_{lost} = H - H_{useful} = 1920 - 1440$$ $$\boxed{H_{lost} = 480 \text{ Joules} \quad (25\% \text{ of total})}$$ ### 3.2.4 Verification (Sanity Check) The heat loss percentage is: $$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{480}{1920} \times 100\% = 25\%$$ Since efficiency is 75%, the loss is exactly 25%. The calculation is **consistent**. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types of Resistance Welding > [!NOTE] > The following matrix is a **high-frequency KTU ESE comparison tool**. Memorize the differences — they almost always appear as 7-mark sub-questions. | **Parameter** | **Spot Welding** | **Projection Welding** | **Seam Welding** | **Upset Butt** | **Flash Butt** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Joint Type** | Lap | Lap (with embossed projection) | Lap (continuous) | End-to-end | End-to-end | | **Current** | 3,000 – 20,000 A | 5,000 – 25,000 A | 5,000 – 15,000 A | Up to 50,000 A | 25,000 – 200,000 A | | **Weld Time** | 0.1 – 0.5 s | 0.2 – 1.5 s | Continuous (roller electrodes) | 1 – 10 s | 5 – 60 s | | **Heat Generation** | I²R at faying surface | I²R at projection tip | I²R at faying surface | I²R at interface + axial upset | Arc + I²R at flashing gap | | **Pressure Application** | Electrode force | Electrode force (localized on projection) | Roller electrode force | Axial upsetting force | Axial flashing + upset force | | **Filler Metal** | None | None | None | None | None | | **Weld Appearance** | Discrete nugget | Multiple small nuggets at projections | Continuous seam | Flush butt joint | Flush butt joint with flash collar | | **Typical Materials** | Low-carbon steel, stainless steel, Al | Nuts, bolts, cross-wires | Thin sheets, tubes | Rods, wires | Rails, heavy sections | | **Strength** | Moderate (single spot) | High (multiple projections) | High (continuous) | High (full section) | Very high (forged structure) | --- ## 3.4 Extended Derivation: Electrode Force and Forge Pressure The electrode force ($F$) is not arbitrary — it must be large enough to **expel molten metal and oxides** from the nugget zone but not so large as to **crush the electrodes** or **indent the workpiece** excessively. For a circular nugget of diameter $d_n$: $$F = p_{forge} \times A_{nugget} = p_{forge} \times \frac{\pi}{4} d_n^2$$ For low-carbon steel: $p_{forge} \approx 100$ – $200$ MPa. For aluminum alloys: $p_{forge} \approx 70$ – $150$ MPa (lower because of softness). > [!NOTE] > **Why aluminum is harder to spot-weld:** Aluminum has only **one-third** the resistivity of steel but conducts heat **5 times faster**, so heat escapes to the bulk material before the interface can melt. This demands **higher current, shorter time, and more aggressive electrode cooling** when welding aluminum alloys (e.g., automotive body panels in Tesla and Audi A8). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Resistance Welding Process Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[AC Mains Supply
440 V 50 Hz] --> node2[Step Down Transformer
Turns Ratio 50 to 1] node2 --> node3[Secondary Winding
4 V to 10 V AC] node3 --> node4[Thyristor Controlled
Welding Timer] node4 --> node5[Copper Electrodes
Water Cooled] node5 --> node6[Workpiece Stack Up
Top Sheet and Bottom Sheet] node6 --> node7[Interface Faying Surface
Highest Resistance Zone] node7 --> node8[Heat Generation I squared R t
Temperature rises to Melting Point] node8 --> node9[Formation of Molten Nugget
Forge Pressure Applied] node9 --> node10[Cooling under Electrode Force
Solidification of Nugget] node10 --> node11[Completed Spot Weld
Solid State Bond] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block diagram interpretation:** The schematic traces the energy path from the 440 V mains supply to the final weld nugget. Each block represents a **physical subsystem** of a resistance welding machine. The **step-down transformer** is the heart of the system — converting high voltage/low current to low voltage/high current. The **thyristor timer** controls the precise weld time (often in milliseconds). The **water-cooled copper electrodes** carry current to the workpiece while simultaneously extracting waste heat. ## 4.2 Spot Welding — Detailed Functional Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Upper Copper Electrode] --> nodeB[Top Steel Sheet Thickness t1] nodeB --> nodeC[Faying Surface
Highest Contact Resistance] nodeC --> nodeD[Bottom Steel Sheet Thickness t2] nodeD --> nodeE[Lower Copper Electrode] nodeF[Electrode Force F
Applied Vertically] --> nodeA nodeF --> nodeE nodeG[Welding Current I
Flows through Electrodes] --> nodeA nodeG --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeH[Heat Concentrated at Interface
Forms Weld Nugget] nodeH --> nodeI[Solidified Cast Nugget Structure
Diameter d equals 5 times sqrt t] ``` ## 4.3 Heat Generation Zone Map (Resistance Distribution) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP[Copper Electrode
Very Low R about 10 microOhm] --> nodeQ[Electrode to Workpiece
Contact R about 50 microOhm] nodeQ --> nodeR[Bulk Top Sheet
Low R] nodeR --> nodeS[Faying Surface
HIGHEST R about 500 microOhm] nodeS --> nodeT[Bulk Bottom Sheet
Low R] nodeT --> nodeU[Workpiece to Electrode
Contact R about 50 microOhm] nodeU --> nodeV[Bottom Copper Electrode
Very Low R] nodeS -. Hot Spot .-> nodeW[Nugget Forms Here
Maximum Heat Concentration] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the faying surface is the hottest zone:** The Mermaid diagram above shows the resistance distribution along the current path. Notice how the **faying surface (the interface between the two sheets)** is highlighted as the **highest resistance zone**. Since heat generated is proportional to $I^2 R$, and current is uniform throughout the series circuit, the **maximum heat is deposited precisely at the interface** — which is exactly where we want the weld nugget to form. ## 4.4 Process Parameter Control Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeCtrl[Controller Unit] --> nodeI1[Current Regulator
Thyristor Phase Control] nodeCtrl --> nodeT1[Time Sequencer
Squeeze Weld Hold Off] nodeCtrl --> nodeF1[Force Regulator
Pneumatic or Hydraulic] nodeI1 --> nodeWeld[Output to Welding Head] nodeT1 --> nodeWeld nodeF1 --> nodeWeld nodeWeld --> nodeFB[Feedback Sensors
Current Voltage Force] nodeFB --> nodeCtrl ``` > [!NOTE] > **Closed-loop control in modern machines:** In contemporary resistance welding controllers (e.g., Bosch Rexroth, Medar, WTC), the three critical parameters — current, time, and force — are continuously monitored and adjusted in a **closed feedback loop**. The constant-current mode (also called CC mode) is the industry standard for automotive spot welding because it compensates for mains voltage fluctuations and electrode wear. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"State and explain Joule's Law of Heating as applied to Resistance Welding. Why is the welding current in resistance welding kept at very high values while the voltage is kept very low?"** #### Model Answer (3 marks): **Statement of Joule's Law:** The heat generated in an electrical conductor is directly proportional to the square of the current, the resistance of the conductor, and the time for which the current flows. Mathematically: $H = I^2 R t$ (1 mark). **Application to Resistance Welding:** In resistance welding, the workpieces themselves act as the conductor. When a huge current (thousands of amperes) is passed through the joint interface, the high contact resistance at the faying surface converts electrical energy into intense localized heat, raising the temperature to the melting point of the metal. The pressure applied through the electrodes then forges the molten metal into a solid joint (1 mark). **Why high current and low voltage:** Since $H \propto I^2$, even a small increase in current produces a very large increase in heat. To achieve the required melting temperature in a fraction of a second, currents of the order of 5,000 – 20,000 A are needed. The voltage is kept low (typically 2 – 20 V) for **operator safety** and to prevent arcing at the electrode-workpiece contact. This is achieved using a **step-down transformer** with a large turns ratio (1 mark). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"Differentiate between Spot Welding and Projection Welding. Mention two industrial applications of each."** #### Model Answer (3 marks): | **Basis** | **Spot Welding** | **Projection Welding** | |---|---|---| | **Surface preparation** | Flat lap joint, no special preparation required | One sheet has pre-embossed projections or bumps (1 mark) | | **Current concentration** | Current concentrated naturally at the electrode tip | Current concentrated at each projection tip — multiple nuggets form simultaneously | | **Weld time** | Shorter (0.1 – 0.5 s) | Slightly longer (0.2 – 1.5 s) | | **Applications** | Car body panels, sheet metal cabinets, refrigerator shells (1 mark) | Nut-to-plate welding, bolt-to-sheet welding, cross-wire mesh in reinforcement cages, refrigerator condenser tubes (1 mark) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** from a choice of two. Each question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below** so you can practice both choices. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** Explain the **principle, equipment, and process parameters of Spot Welding** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of **electrode force and welding time** in determining weld quality. (7 marks) **(b)** Two **mild steel sheets of 1.5 mm thickness each** are to be joined by spot welding. The **welding current is 9000 A**, the **interfacial contact resistance is 80 μΩ**, and the **weld time is 0.25 seconds**. The **welding efficiency is 70%**. Determine: - (i) The **total heat generated**. - (ii) The **useful heat** available for melting. - (iii) The **heat lost** to the surroundings and electrodes. - (iv) If the latent heat of fusion of mild steel is **272 kJ/kg** and the specific heat is **0.46 kJ/(kg·K)**, and the interface must be heated from **30 °C to 1530 °C**, estimate the **mass of metal melted** at the nugget. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks): **1. Principle of Spot Welding (2 marks):** Spot welding is a resistance welding process in which the coalescence of two overlapping sheets is achieved at a **single localized point (the spot)** by passing a large electric current through the spot for a short time, under the simultaneous application of mechanical pressure through water-cooled copper electrodes. No filler metal is used. The heat generated at the interface due to electrical resistance melts the metal, and the electrode pressure forges the molten puddle into a solid nugget. **2. Equipment Description (2 marks):** - **Step-down transformer:** Reduces mains voltage (440 V) to welding voltage (4–10 V) while stepping up the current to 5,000–20,000 A. - **Welding electrodes:** Made of water-cooled copper or copper-chromium alloy. The lower electrode is usually fixed; the upper is movable. - **Electrode force mechanism:** Pneumatic, hydraulic, or servo-motor driven. Typical force: 2,000 – 8,000 N. - **Welding timer / controller:** A thyristor-based electronic timer that controls the squeeze, weld, hold, and off times with millisecond precision. - **Cooling system:** Circulating water through the electrode holders to extract waste heat. **3. Role of Process Parameters (3 marks):** - **Welding current (I):** Directly affects the heat generated ($H = I^2 R t$). Too low → cold weld (small or no nugget). Too high → expulsion of molten metal, burn-through, electrode sticking. **Optimum current** produces a nugget diameter of approximately $5\sqrt{t}$ (in mm). - **Welding time (t):** Longer times permit more heat input and a larger nugget, but at the risk of overheating the electrodes. Typical: 0.1 – 0.5 s. - **Electrode force (F):** Provides forge pressure during solidification. Also, before current flows, it breaks down surface asperities and stabilizes the contact resistance. Insufficient force → porosity and cracks. Excessive force → thin, weak nuggets and rapid electrode wear. - The three parameters (I, t, F) are **interdependent** — KTU examiners frequently ask students to explain this coupling. #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks): **Given data:** - $I = 9000$ A - $R = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω - $t = 0.25$ s - $\eta = 0.70$ **(i) Total heat generated:** $$H = I^2 R t = (9000)^2 \times (80 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25$$ $$H = 81 \times 10^6 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.25$$ $$H = 81 \times 80 \times 0.25 = 6480 \times 0.25 = 1620 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{H = 1620 \text{ J} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$ **(ii) Useful heat:** $$H_{useful} = \eta \times H = 0.70 \times 1620$$ $$\boxed{H_{useful} = 1134 \text{ J} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}}$$ **(iii) Heat lost:** $$H_{lost} = H - H_{useful} = 1620 - 1134 = 486 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{H_{lost} = 486 \text{ J (30% of total)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}}$$ **(iv) Mass of metal melted:** Total heat required per unit mass to raise temperature from $T_1 = 30\,°C$ to $T_2 = 1530\,°C$ and then melt: $$q = c \cdot \Delta T + L_f$$ $$q = 0.46 \times 10^3 \times (1530 - 30) + 272 \times 10^3$$ $$q = 460 \times 1500 + 272{,}000$$ $$q = 690{,}000 + 272{,}000 = 962{,}000 \text{ J/kg} = 962 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Mass melted: $$m = \frac{H_{useful}}{q} = \frac{1134}{962{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{m \approx 1.179 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 1.179 \text{ g} \quad \text{(3 marks)}}$$ **Verification (sanity check):** The nugget volume would be $V = m / \rho = 1.179 \times 10^{-3} / 7850 \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³ = 0.15 cm³. For a 1.5 mm thick sheet pair (total 3 mm), the nugget diameter would be $\approx \sqrt{4V / (\pi \times 3 \text{ mm})} \approx 8$ mm, which is consistent with empirical estimates. ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Flash Butt Welding process**. Compare it with **Upset Butt Welding** in terms of current, heat generation, weld quality, and applications. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive the **Heat Balance Equation** for resistance welding. Discuss **any four** of the following process variables and explain how each affects the weld quality: (i) welding current, (ii) welding time, (iii) electrode force, (iv) electrode material and shape, (v) surface condition of the workpiece. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks): **Flash Butt Welding — Description (4 marks):** In **Flash Butt Welding**, the two workpieces are brought together with a small gap while a very high current (25,000 – 200,000 A) is passed through them. As the faces approach, they make brief contact, and a **violent arcing (flashing)** occurs across the gap. The intense arcing raises the temperature of the contacting ends to the melting point (or near-melting) and also expels contaminants, oxides, and surface films. Once a uniform layer of molten metal covers both ends, the workpieces are **forcibly pushed together (upset)**, and the current is cut off. The molten and plasticized metal is squeezed out as a **flash collar** (or burr) around the joint, leaving a sound, fine-grained weld. **Equipment (1 mark):** - Heavy-duty step-down transformer (often 200–500 kVA rating) - Clamping jaws to grip the workpieces - Hydraulic or pneumatic upsetting ram - Flashing-speed controller - Flash trimmer / deburring saw (to remove the external flash collar) **Comparison Table — Flash Butt vs. Upset Butt (2 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Flash Butt Welding** | **Upset Butt Welding** | |---|---|---| | **Current level** | 25,000 – 200,000 A (very high) | 5,000 – 50,000 A (lower) | | **Heat generation** | Mainly by **flashing arc** at the gap; some I²R at contact | Mainly by **I²R at the interface**; no arc | | **Heat-affected zone (HAZ)** | Narrow, localized | Wider, deeper | | **Surface preparation** | Less critical (flash removes oxides) | Critical (must be clean and flat) | | **Power consumption** | Higher (more heat lost in flash) | Lower | | **Weld quality** | Superior — fine-grained, no inclusions | Good but may contain oxide inclusions if not pre-cleaned | | **Applications** | Rails, large pipes, structural sections, automotive axle housings | Rods, wires, small diameter tubes, bandsaw blades | #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks): **Derivation of the Heat Balance Equation (3 marks):** The total heat generated at the weld interface by Joule's effect is: $$H = I^2 R t \quad \text{(Joules)}$$ This heat is partitioned as follows: $$H = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5$$ Where: - $H_1$ = heat to raise the interface to melting point (sensible heat) = $m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ - $H_2$ = heat for actual fusion (latent heat) = $m \cdot L_f$ - $H_3$ = heat lost to the workpieces by conduction - $H_4$ = heat lost to the electrodes by conduction - $H_5$ = heat lost by radiation and convection to the surroundings The **useful heat** is $H_1 + H_2$, and the **welding efficiency** is: $$\eta = \frac{H_1 + H_2}{I^2 R t}$$ **Discussion of Four Process Variables (4 marks — 1 mark each):** **(i) Welding current:** The most influential variable. Heat is proportional to $I^2$, so a small increase in current dramatically increases heat input. Insufficient current → cold weld, undersized nugget, low strength. Excessive current → burn-through, metal expulsion, porosity, and rapid electrode wear. **(ii) Welding time:** Controls the duration of heat input. Longer time → larger nugget, but also greater heat loss to surroundings and electrodes. Very short time with high current is preferred for thin sheets (to minimize HAZ). Long time with moderate current suits thicker sections. **(iii) Electrode force:** Must be applied before, during, and after the current pulse. It (a) reduces contact resistance, (b) provides forge pressure to consolidate the nugget, and (c) prevents porosity and shrinkage cracks during solidification. Insufficient force → porous, cracked welds. Excessive force → sheet indentation and rapid electrode mushrooming. **(iv) Surface condition:** Clean, oxide-free surfaces produce consistent contact resistance and repeatable welds. Oil, paint, rust, or galvanizing must be removed. In practice, a thin, uniform coating is acceptable, but heavy contamination causes sparking, inconsistent nuggets, and electrode pitting. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the units in numerical problems:** Always write $R$ in ohms (Ω) — if given in μΩ, convert using $1 \text{ μΩ} = 10^{-6}$ Ω. Forgetting this conversion leads to answers off by a factor of 1,000,000. > 2. **Confusing "total heat" with "useful heat":** The question may explicitly ask for *useful* heat. In that case, you must multiply by efficiency $\eta$ — do **not** simply report $I^2 R t$. > 3. **Not labeling the resistance components in a sketch:** When drawing a spot welding diagram, examiners award 1–2 marks for clearly labeling the **five resistance zones** ($R_1$ to $R_5$). Skipping this loses easy marks. > 4. **Confusing welding time with heat time:** A complete welding schedule has **four times** — squeeze, weld, hold, off. Writing "welding time" generically loses 0.5–1 mark. > 5. **Writing absolute values with vertical bars in tables:** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never $|x|$, especially in digital answer sheets. > 6. **Omitting the efficiency definition:** Even if not asked, stating $\eta = H_{useful} / H_{total}$ shows conceptual clarity and is rewarded. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-density rapid revision checklist — read this the night before the exam:** - **Joule's Law:** $H = I^2 R t$ — heat ∝ current squared × resistance × time. - **Voltage is low, current is high:** 2–20 V, 3,000–200,000 A. Step-down transformer is essential. - **Five resistance zones in spot welding:** Electrode, electrode-workpiece contact (top), bulk top sheet, **faying surface (highest R)**, bulk bottom sheet, electrode-workpiece contact (bottom). - **Heat balance equation:** $H = H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 + H_5$ — useful heat is only 60–80% of total. - **Welding efficiency:** $\eta = (H_1 + H_2) / (I^2 R t) = 60\% - 80\%$. - **Welding schedule has 4 times:** Squeeze → Weld → Hold (forge) → Off. - **Spot welding:** Discrete nugget, lap joint, used in car bodies. - **Projection welding:** Embossed projections, multiple nuggets, used for nuts/bolts. - **Seam welding:** Continuous roller electrodes, used for tanks and drums. - **Upset butt:** No flash, simple abutting of ends, lower current. - **Flash butt:** Violent arcing, very high current, fine-grained weld, used for rails. - **Nugget diameter empirical rule:** $d_n \approx 5\sqrt{t}$ for mild steel (in mm). - **Aluminum is harder to weld:** Lower resistivity, higher thermal conductivity → more heat escapes. - **Copper electrodes are water-cooled:** To extract waste heat and prevent electrode softening. - **No filler metal used:** Resistance welding is an autogenous process. - **Industrial dominance:** 4,000–5,000 spot welds in a typical passenger car; ~60% of all resistance welds go to the automotive industry. - **Master the three parameters:** Current (I), Time (t), Electrode Force (F) — these are **interdependent** and examiners love testing this coupling. ---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submerged Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** is an *arc welding process* in which the welding arc is **completely submerged** beneath a blanket (heap) of granular, fusible flux. The heat generated by the arc melts the parent metal, the continuously fed consumable electrode (wire or strip), and a portion of the flux, which forms a protective molten slag pool over the weld pool. Because the arc is invisible during operation, the process is sometimes referred to as *invisible arc welding*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** SAW is classified under **Arc Welding Processes** and is identified by the AWS designation **"SAW."** The process was patented by Robinoff (1935) and is governed by **IS 12778 / AWS A5.17** standards for low-carbon steel electrodes and fluxes.

### 1.2 Constituents of the SAW System
The SAW process consists of four key functional units working in synchrony:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Wire Feed Unit** | Delivers the consumable electrode (solid wire/strip) at a constant rate |
| **Power Source** | Provides D.C. or A.C. welding current (typically 300–1500 A) |
| **Flux Hopper & Delivery Tube** | Deposits a fresh layer of granular flux just ahead of the arc |
| **Welding Head/Torch** | Guides the electrode and flux; maintains the submerged arc |
| **Travel Mechanism** | Moves the work or the head at a controlled travel speed (mm/min) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Underground River"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a river flowing silently beneath a thick blanket of snow. You see the snow being disturbed on the surface, but the powerful, erosive flow of water is hidden underneath. Similarly, in SAW the arc is a violent, ~5000 °C "underground river" of molten metal hidden beneath a soft "blanket of snow" made of granular flux. The flux blanket shields the arc from atmospheric contamination just as snow covers and protects the river from the freezing air above.

A second analogy: **Sand-Walled Fire Pit.** If you dig a trench, fill it with fuel, light it, and pour sand over the flames, the fire still burns intensely — and the sand melts to form a glassy crust. SAW works the same way: the **arc is the fire**, the **flux is the sand**, and the **molten slag** is the glassy crust that protects the weld metal as it solidifies.

### 1.4 Why SAW Matters in Heavy Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - SAW delivers **weld deposition rates of 4–25 kg/hour** — 5 to 10× higher than Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW).
> - It produces welds of **exceptional quality** with deep penetration, minimal spatter, and no visible arc flash.
> - It is the **backbone of heavy fabrication**: shipbuilding, pressure vessels, spiral pipe manufacturing, nuclear reactor vessels, and storage tank construction.

### 1.5 Process Variants

| Variant | Description |
|---|---|
| **Single-Wire SAW** | One electrode wire; most common configuration |
| **Twin-Wire SAW (Tandem)** | Two wires in series; higher productivity |
| **Multi-Wire / Multi-Arc** | 3+ arcs; ultra-high deposition for thick plates |
| **Strip Cladding** | Wide strip electrode (e.g., 60×0.5 mm) for surfacing |
| **Narrow-Gap SAW** | For very thick plates (up to 300 mm) with minimal groove angle |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequence of Physical Events in SAW

The SAW process unfolds in a precisely ordered chain of events:

1. **Flux Deposition:** Granular flux is gravity-fed through a nozzle, forming a heap 25–50 mm deep covering the joint.
2. **Arc Ignition:** The electrode tip contacts the workpiece (or flux pile) and an arc is struck. The arc melts the surrounding flux, which becomes electrically conductive (ionized).
3. **Arc Submersion:** Within milliseconds, the molten flux forms a cavity (a *bubble*) in the flux heap, and the arc becomes **invisible** — operating inside this molten cavity.
4. **Metal Transfer:** Droplets of molten electrode wire transfer across the arc into the weld pool, while the surrounding solid flux continues to melt inward.
5. **Slag Formation:** The molten flux floats on the weld pool as a liquid slag, **shielding** it from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
6. **Solidification:** As the heat source moves forward, the slag solidifies into a glassy crust that is later **flaked off** and reclaimed (flux recovery rate ~70 %).
7. **Solid Weld Bead:** A clean, smooth, spatter-free weld bead is left behind.

## 2.2 The Multi-Functional Role of SAW Flux

> [!NOTE]
> In SAW, the flux is **not just a shield** — it is a metallurgical participant. This is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU university exams.

The flux performs **six simultaneous functions**:

| # | Function | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arc Shielding** | Forms an envelope of molten slag + gas that excludes atmospheric O₂ and N₂ |
| 2 | **Slag Formation** | Creates a protective liquid layer that floats on the weld pool |
| 3 | **Alloy Addition** | Adds Mn, Si, Cr, Mo, etc. to the weld metal via metallurgical reactions |
| 4 | **Weld Pool Shaping** | Surface tension of slag controls bead profile and penetration shape |
| 5 | **Heat Concentration** | The flux blanket traps radiant heat → higher thermal efficiency (~90 %) |
| 6 | **Deoxidization / Desulfurization** | Reacts with impurities and floats inclusions into the slag |

## 2.3 Process Parameters — The Four Levers of Control

The four interdependent parameters that govern weld quality are:

- **Arc Voltage (V):** Typically **28–38 V**. Higher voltage → wider, flatter bead with more dilution.
- **Welding Current (I):** Typically **300–1500 A** (D.C. positive electrode preferred for mild steel). Higher current → deeper penetration and higher deposition.
- **Travel Speed (S):** Typically **30–150 cm/min** (≈ 5–25 mm/s). Faster travel → narrower bead, less penetration.
- **Wire Feed Rate / Electrode Diameter (d):** Typically **1.6–4.8 mm** wire diameter; feed rate 1.5–15 m/min.

Other secondary parameters include:
- **Flux grain size** (e.g., 0.4–2.0 mm)
- **Flux depth** above the arc (25–50 mm)
- **Electrode stick-out (contact tip to work distance):** 25–40 mm
- **Joint preparation** (V, U, double-V grooves)
- **Polarity** (DCEP most common for mild steel; DCEN for stainless to reduce penetration)

## 2.4 Polarity Effects in SAW

| Polarity | Penetration | Deposition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP** (Electrode +) | **Deep** | **High** | Mild/low-alloy steel — *most common* |
| **DCEN** (Electrode −) | Shallow | Low | Stainless steel, surfacing |
| **AC** | Intermediate | Intermediate | Reduced arc blow for high-current DC applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is DCEP preferred in SAW of mild steel?"*  
> **Answer:** In DCEP, electrons flow from workpiece → electrode, generating ~2/3 of the heat at the workpiece (anode). This produces deep penetration, high deposition, and a stable arc. (~70 % heat at anode due to higher anode fall.)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency, V = voltage, I = current, S = travel speed | **kJ/mm** | For SAW, $\eta \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ (use **0.9** unless stated) |
| 2 | **Heat Input (alt)** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$ where S in mm/s | Same as above | **J/mm** | Convert: 1 kJ/mm = 1000 J/mm |
| 3 | **Deposition Rate** $D = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot v_f \cdot \rho}{4}$ | $d$ = wire dia, $v_f$ = feed rate, $\rho$ = wire density (≈ 7.85 g/cm³ for steel) | **kg/hr** | Multiply result by 0.06 to convert g/s → kg/hr |
| 4 | **Penetration (empirical)** $P \approx k \cdot \dfrac{I}{S}$ | $k$ = process constant | **mm** | For SAW, $k \approx 0.04$ to $0.10$ |
| 5 | **Area of Weld Bead** $A = \dfrac{0.785 \cdot d^2 \cdot v_f}{S}$ | $d$ = wire dia, $v_f$ = feed rate, $S$ = travel speed | **mm²** | Geometric estimate of fusion zone cross-section |
| 6 | **Current Density** $J = \dfrac{I}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}d^2}$ | $I$ = current, $d$ = wire dia | **A/mm²** | Optimal range: 50–200 A/mm² for SAW |
| 7 | **Welding Speed Relation** $S = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{H \cdot k}$ | For required heat input H | **mm/s** | Useful for procedure qualification |
| 8 | **Power Consumed** $P = V \cdot I$ | — | **Watts** or **kW** | Input electrical power |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in kJ/mm — this appears in **almost every SAW numerical in KTU exams.**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding:** SAW welds one-sided, double-sided joints on plates up to 50 mm thick.
- **Spiral Pipe Mills:** Continuous SAW produces HSAW (Helical Submerged Arc Welded) pipes for oil & gas transmission.
- **Pressure Vessel Fabrication:** ASME Section IX procedure qualification routinely uses SAW for shell courses.
- **Nuclear Industry:** SAW is used for heavy-section reactor pressure vessel welds due to its low hydrogen weld metal.
- **Wind Tower Manufacturing:** Tall wind turbine towers are welded almost entirely with tandem-arc SAW.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Heat Input Equation for SAW

The heat input (also called *arc energy*) is the **single most important quantity** in welding procedure specification. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Electrical Power Delivered to the Arc

The arc behaves as an electrical load. The instantaneous electrical power dissipated at the arc is:

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts, W}]
$$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (V) and $I$ is the welding current (A).

### Step 2 — Energy Delivered Per Unit Time

If the arc runs steadily for a time interval $t$ (seconds), the total electrical energy supplied is:

$$
E_{total} = P_{arc} \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{Joules, J}]
$$

### Step 3 — Useful Heat Available for Welding

Not all electrical energy is converted into useful heat at the weld. A fraction is lost by:
- Radiation from the arc
- Convection in the surrounding flux
- Conduction into the water-cooled copper contact tip
- Spatter and fume losses

This is captured by the **thermal efficiency**, $\eta$ (eta):

$$
E_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

For SAW, because the flux blanket traps radiant heat efficiently, $\eta$ is **very high — typically 0.85 to 0.95**. (For comparison: SMAW $\eta \approx 0.7$–0.85; GTAW $\eta \approx 0.5$–0.7.)

### Step 4 — Heat Input Per Unit Length of Weld

In a moving arc, the more meaningful quantity is the heat supplied **per millimetre of weld length**. If the travel speed is $S$ (in mm/s), then in time $t$ the arc moves a distance $L = S \cdot t$. Therefore:

$$
H = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{S \cdot t} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Unit Conversion to kJ/mm

To convert J/mm → kJ/mm, divide by 1000. For convenience, the factor 60 is also introduced so that travel speed can be expressed in mm/min (which is the industry convention). The two forms are:

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \quad \text{(with S in mm/s, gives J/mm)}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(with S in mm/min, gives kJ/mm)}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor 60 converts mm/min → mm/s, and dividing by 1000 converts J/mm → kJ/mm. These two factors together are simply $\dfrac{60}{1000} = \dfrac{3}{50}$, but they are conventionally left in the formula for clarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** KTU examinations often give travel speed in **cm/min or mm/min**. Always convert: $1 \text{ cm/min} = 10 \text{ mm/min} = 0.1667 \text{ mm/s}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Type)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]**
> In a SAW operation on a low-carbon steel plate, the following data are given:
> - Arc voltage: $V = 32$ V
> - Welding current: $I = 600$ A
> - Travel speed: $S = 30$ cm/min
> - Thermal efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Heat input in kJ/mm, (b) Heat input in J/mm, (c) Power supplied to the arc in kW.

### Solution — Part (a): Heat Input in kJ/mm

**Step 1:** Convert travel speed to mm/min.

$$
S = 30 \text{ cm/min} = 30 \times 10 = 300 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the heat input formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.90 \times 32 \times 600 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.90 \times 32 \times 36000}{300000} \\
  &= \dfrac{1036800}{300000} \\
  &= 3.456 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiply the constants $\eta, V, I$ first → 0.90 × 32 × 600 = 17,280 (Watts-equivalent). Multiply by 60 → 1,036,800 J/m of weld (when S in m/min). Divide by S (300 mm/min = 0.3 m/min) → wait, simpler to keep units: 17,280 × 60 / (300 × 1000) = 1,036,800 / 300,000 = **3.456 kJ/mm**.

### Solution — Part (b): Heat Input in J/mm

Multiply (a) by 1000:

$$
H = 3.456 \times 1000 = 3456 \text{ J/mm} \approx 3.46 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

Equivalently, using $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S}$ with $S = 5$ mm/s (since 300 mm/min ÷ 60 = 5 mm/s):

$$
H = \dfrac{0.90 \times 32 \times 600}{5} = \dfrac{17280}{5} = 3456 \text{ J/mm}
$$

### Solution — Part (c): Power Supplied to the Arc

$$
P = V \times I = 32 \times 600 = 19{,}200 \text{ W} = 19.2 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** Heat input = Power / Speed. $19.2 \text{ kW} / (5 \text{ mm/s}) = 3.84 \text{ kW·s/mm} = 3.84 \text{ kJ/mm}$. Note that this is the **total power** based heat input; the useful heat (accounting for $\eta$) is $3.84 \times 0.90 = 3.456$ kJ/mm ✓ matches Part (a).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Weld Geometry

The following matrix summarizes the **direction of influence** of each SAW parameter on bead geometry, deposition, and quality. This is a high-frequency KTU question format.

| Parameter (↑ increases) | Penetration | Bead Width | Deposition Rate | Heat Input | Spatter | Quality Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage ↑** | ↑ Slight | ↑↑ Significant | ↑ Moderate | ↑↑ | ↑ | Undercut, porosity |
| **Current ↑** | ↑↑ Strong | ↑ Slight | ↑↑ Strong | ↑↑ | — | Burn-through, HAZ coarsening |
| **Travel Speed ↑** | ↓↓ Strong | ↓↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | — | Lack of fusion, undercut |
| **Wire Feed ↑** | ↑↑ | ↑ Slight | ↑↑ Strong | ↑↑ | ↑ Slight | Higher dilution |
| **Flux Depth ↑** | ↑ Slight | ↑ Slight | — | ↑ Slight | ↓↓ | More slag entrapment |
| **DCEP vs DCEN** | Higher penetration | — | Higher | — | Lower | Stable arc, less blow |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** A common 7-mark question asks: *"Discuss the effect of varying any two SAW parameters on weld bead characteristics."* The matrix above is your ready-made answer skeleton.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: SAW vs Other Arc Welding Processes

| Feature | SAW | SMAW | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shielding | Flux + self-generated gas | Flux coating | External gas | External gas (Ar) |
| Arc visibility | Hidden (invisible) | Visible | Visible | Visible |
| Deposition rate | **Highest** | Low | Medium-high | Lowest |
| Plate thickness | 5–100+ mm | 2–30 mm | 1–25 mm | 0.5–10 mm |
| Position | Flat / horizontal only | All positions | All positions | All positions |
| Spatter | Negligible | Moderate | Moderate-High | Negligible |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate-High | High |
| Hydrogen control | Excellent | Variable | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost per kg of weld | **Lowest** | High | Medium | Highest |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SAW Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source 300-1500 A AC or DC] --> B[Welding Head Assembly]
    C[Wire Spool 1.6-4.8 mm electrode] --> D[Wire Drive Rolls]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Contact Tip and Electrode Stickout 25-40 mm]
    F[Flux Hopper] --> G[Flux Delivery Nozzle]
    G --> H[Flux Blanket Heap 25-50 mm deep]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Submerged Arc invisible]
    I --> J[Weld Pool and Fusion Zone]
    K[Travel Carriage or Manipulator] --> B
    L[Workpiece Plate] --> J
    J --> M[Solidified Weld Bead]
    H --> N[Flux Recovery Vacuum Unit]
    N --> F
    J --> O[Slag Crust flaked off]
    M --> P[Finished Welded Joint]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The power source and wire feeder act in synchrony to feed consumable electrode and current to the welding head. The flux hopper continuously replenishes the granular flux blanket that buries the arc. The travel carriage moves the assembly along the joint. Flux that doesn't melt is recovered and recycled.

---

## 4.2 SAW Arc Cavity Cross-Section — Functional Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph W[Workpiece]
        WP[Base Metal Fusion Zone HAZ]
    end
    subgraph Z[Active Weld Zone]
        S1[Solid Flux Granules outer heap]
        S2[Partially Molten Flux sintered layer]
        S3[Molten Slag Pool covers weld pool]
        S4[Molten Flux Cavity bubble holds arc]
        AR[Submerged Arc 5000 to 6000 C invisible]
        EL[Consumable Electrode Wire]
        WP2[Liquid Weld Pool]
    end
    EL --> AR
    AR --> WP2
    WP2 --> WP
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> AR
    S3 --> WP2
    S3 --> S5[Solidified Slag Crust to be removed]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Question:** *"Identify the layers from outside to inside of the SAW arc cavity."*  
> **Answer:** (1) Unmelted flux granules → (2) Sintered (partially molten) flux → (3) Molten slag blanket → (4) Molten flux cavity (where the arc burns) → (5) Weld pool below. The electrode enters from above through a small opening in the flux heap.

---

## 4.3 Process Parameter Relationship Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arc Voltage 28-38 V] --> X[Weld Pool Energy]
    B[Welding Current 300-1500 A] --> X
    C[Travel Speed 5-25 mm/s] --> X
    D[Wire Feed Rate 1.5-15 m/min] --> X
    X --> Y[Heat Input H = eta V I 60 / S 1000]
    Y --> Z1[Penetration Depth]
    Y --> Z2[Bead Width]
    Y --> Z3[Deposition Rate]
    Y --> Z4[HAZ Width]
    Y --> Z5[Distortion and Residual Stress]
    F[Polarity DCEP DCEN AC] --> X
    G[Electrode Diameter 1.6-4.8 mm] --> X
    H[Joint Design V U Groove] --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart visually emphasizes the **KTU concept of "weld pool energy"** — the central control point through which all parameters (electrical, mechanical, and geometric) ultimately govern weld quality.

---

## 4.4 Heat Flow Zone Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Source Submerged Arc] --> B[Fusion Zone FZ melted]
    A --> C[Heat Affected Zone HAZ solid but microstructure changed]
    C --> D[Subcritical HAZ below Ac1]
    C --> E[Intercritical HAZ between Ac1 and Ac3]
    C --> F[Fine Grained HAZ just above Ac3]
    C --> G[Coarse Grained HAZ peak temperature region]
    B --> H[Weld Metal W M solidifying dendrites]
    B --> I[Slag Inclusions in weld pool]
    H --> J[Unaffected Parent Metal below HAZ]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HAZ is **not melted**, but its microstructure is altered by the thermal cycle. The **coarse-grained HAZ** adjacent to the fusion line is the most crack-susceptible region, especially in low-alloy and high-strength steels.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Submerged Arc Welding. List any four advantages of SAW over Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW).**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) is an arc welding process in which the heat of an arc, established between a continuously fed consumable electrode and the workpiece, is **completely covered by a blanket of granular fusible flux**. The arc is invisible during operation, hence the term "submerged."

**Advantages over SMAW (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. **Higher deposition rate** (4–25 kg/hr vs 1–3 kg/hr in SMAW) → greater productivity.
2. **Deeper penetration** in a single pass; thicker plates (up to 25 mm) can be welded without edge preparation.
3. **Minimal spatter and fumes** because the flux suppresses metal vaporization; better working environment.
4. **No visible arc flash** → eliminates the need for heavy arc-flash eye protection (though IR/UV filtering is still recommended).
5. **Excellent weld metal quality** with consistent mechanical properties due to metallurgical control via flux chemistry.
6. **Flux is recoverable** (~70 % reusable) → lower consumable cost.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the role of flux in Submerged Arc Welding.**

**Model Answer:**

The flux in SAW performs **six key functions** (any four required for full 3 marks):
1. **Arc Shielding:** Generates a protective atmosphere by decomposing and releasing shielding gases; prevents atmospheric contamination of the weld pool.
2. **Slag Formation:** Melts to form a liquid slag blanket that floats on the weld pool, protecting it from oxidation and slowing cooling.
3. **Metallurgical Contribution:** Adds alloying elements (Mn, Si, Mo, Cr, Ni) to the weld metal through slag-metal reactions.
4. **Weld Pool Shaping:** Surface tension of the molten slag influences bead shape and undercut tendency.
5. **Heat Conservation:** The thick flux heap traps radiant and convective heat, giving SAW a high thermal efficiency (~0.9).
6. **Deoxidization:** Removes oxygen and sulfur from the weld pool as oxides/sulfides that float into the slag.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **principle and working of Submerged Arc Welding**. List the essential equipment required. (7 marks)

**(b)** A SAW operation is performed with the following data: Voltage = 35 V, Current = 750 A, Travel speed = 25 cm/min, Thermal efficiency = 0.9. Calculate the **heat input in kJ/mm** and the **deposition rate in kg/hr** for a 3.2 mm diameter steel wire fed at 4 m/min. (Take density of steel = 7.85 g/cm³.) (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle (3 marks):**
In SAW, an arc is struck between a **continuously fed consumable wire electrode** and the **workpiece**. The arc and the weld pool are **completely buried** under a heap of **granular, fusible flux** fed from a hopper. The flux melts to form:
- A **molten cavity** (bubble) around the arc → keeps the arc submerged
- A **liquid slag layer** floating on the weld pool → shields from atmosphere

The arc melts the base metal, the electrode, and a portion of the flux. The fused metal solidifies into the weld bead; the fused flux solidifies as a slag crust that is detached after solidification.

**Equipment (4 marks — list and describe briefly):**
1. **Power source** (AC or DC, constant voltage type, 300–1500 A)
2. **Wire feed unit** (constant-speed feed rolls with speed control)
3. **Welding head / torch** (guides electrode, contains contact tip)
4. **Flux hopper and delivery nozzle** (gravity-feed granular flux)
5. **Travel carriage / manipulator** (moves head or rotates work)
6. **Flux recovery system** (vacuum unit to reclaim unfused flux)
7. **Control panel** (regulates voltage, current, wire feed, travel speed)
8. **Work clamping and ground return** (cable to power source negative terminal)

**Schematic:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 — a textual schematic should show the wire spool → drive rolls → contact tip → electrode → arc → workpiece; flux hopper above; travel carriage below.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V = 35$ V, $I = 750$ A, $S = 25$ cm/min = 250 mm/min
- $\eta = 0.9$, $d = 3.2$ mm, $v_f = 4$ m/min
- $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cm}^3$

**Step 1: Heat Input (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.9 \times 35 \times 750 \times 60}{250 \times 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.9 \times 35 \times 45000}{250000} \\
  &= \dfrac{1417500}{250000} \\
  &= 5.67 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Deposition Rate (4 marks)**

Volume of wire fed per minute = cross-sectional area × length per minute.

Wire cross-section area:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3.2)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10.24}{4} = 8.0425 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Convert to cm²: $8.0425 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.080425 \text{ cm}^2$.

Wire length per minute: $v_f = 4$ m/min = 400 cm/min.

Volume per minute:

$$
V_{wire} = 0.080425 \times 400 = 32.17 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

Mass per minute (using density):

$$
m = 32.17 \times 7.85 = 252.5 \text{ g/min}
$$

Mass per hour:

$$
D = 252.5 \times 60 = 15{,}153 \text{ g/hr} = 15.15 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Faster method (verify):** $D = \dfrac{\pi d^2 \cdot v_f \cdot \rho \cdot 60}{4}$ where $d$ in cm, $v_f$ in cm/min, $\rho$ in g/cm³, gives g/hr directly.
> $D = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.32^2 \times 400 \times 7.85 \times 60}{4} = \dfrac{0.3217 \times 400 \times 7.85 \times 60}{4} = \dfrac{60,610}{4} \approx 15{,}153$ g/hr ✓

**Final Answers:**
- Heat Input: **5.67 kJ/mm**
- Deposition Rate: **15.15 kg/hr**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]*

**(a)** Compare Submerged Arc Welding with **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** and **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** on the basis of (i) shielding medium, (ii) deposition rate, (iii) weld quality, (iv) suitable plate thickness, (v) positional capability, and (vi) typical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **effect of the following SAW process parameters** on weld bead geometry (penetration, bead width) and deposition rate: (i) Arc voltage, (ii) Welding current, (iii) Travel speed. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks: 1 for intro + 6 for table)

**Introduction (1 mark):** SAW, GMAW, and GTAW are the three most widely used **arc welding processes** in modern manufacturing. While all three use an electric arc as the heat source, they differ markedly in shielding method, productivity, and application.

| Criterion | SAW | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Shielding medium** | Granular flux (self + gas) | External inert/active gas (Ar, CO₂, Ar+CO₂) | External inert gas (pure Ar or He) |
| **(ii) Deposition rate** | **Highest (4–25 kg/hr)** | Medium-high (2–8 kg/hr) | Lowest (0.5–2 kg/hr) |
| **(iii) Weld quality** | Excellent — low H, no spatter | Good — clean, but more spatter | **Highest — premium quality** |
| **(iv) Plate thickness** | 5–100+ mm (thick sections) | 1–25 mm | 0.5–10 mm (thin sections) |
| **(v) Positional capability** | **Flat & horizontal only** | All positions | All positions (best for vertical/overhead) |
| **(vi) Applications** | Pressure vessels, ships, pipes, nuclear | Auto bodies, structural steel | Stainless, aluminium, aerospace, root pass |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Effect of Parameters (7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for diagrams/examples)

**(i) Arc Voltage (2 marks):**
- **Penetration:** Slight increase with voltage (less sensitive than current).
- **Bead Width:** **Increases significantly** — higher voltage spreads the arc over a wider area.
- **Deposition Rate:** Increases moderately (wider bead = more filler per unit length).
- **Risk:** Too high voltage → flat, wide bead with **undercut** at the toes and **porosity** from gas entrapment.

**(ii) Welding Current (2 marks):**
- **Penetration:** **Increases strongly** — current is the dominant penetration parameter.
- **Bead Width:** Slight increase; bead becomes taller (more reinforcement).
- **Deposition Rate:** **Increases strongly** — proportional to wire melt rate.
- **Risk:** Excessive current → **burn-through** on thin plates, **coarse HAZ**, excessive distortion.

**(iii) Travel Speed (2 marks):**
- **Penetration:** **Decreases sharply** — less time for the arc to melt the base metal.
- **Bead Width:** Decreases — narrower bead.
- **Deposition Rate:** **Decreases** — less filler deposited per unit length.
- **Risk:** Too high travel speed → **lack of fusion**, **undercut**, and **incomplete penetration**.

**Summary relationship (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Heat Input } H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{S} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Penetration} \propto \dfrac{I}{S}, \quad \text{Bead Width} \propto V
$$

These two dimensionless ratios ($I/S$ and $V$) are the **most powerful predictors** of weld geometry and are memorized by every welding engineer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit of Travel Speed:** Converting 25 cm/min incorrectly. Use $S \text{ (mm/min)} = S \text{ (cm/min)} \times 10$. Many students forget the factor of 10 and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Thermal Efficiency:** Students often assume $\eta = 1$ for SAW. While close, KTU examiners expect $\eta = 0.85$–$0.95$ unless explicitly given. Always state the value used.
> 3. **Heat Input Units:** Confusing **J/mm** and **kJ/mm** (factor of 1000). Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Deposition Rate Units:** Forgetting to convert **g/min → kg/hr** (multiply by 60/1000 = 0.06). Always state the unit.
> 5. **No schematic in long answers:** Even a hand-drawn block diagram is worth 2–3 marks. Skipping it is a guaranteed mark loss.
> 6. **Confusing SAW with electroslag welding:** SAW uses an **arc** submerged in flux; electroslag welding (ESW) uses **molten slag resistance heating** — no arc. Examiners frequently use this as a trick question.
> 7. **Forgetting flux functions:** If a question asks "explain the role of flux," listing only 1–2 functions (typically just shielding) loses 2–3 marks. The full six functions earn full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Submerged Arc Welding**

### Core Concept
- SAW = **Arc Welding** + **Granular Flux Blanket** → arc is **invisible** during operation.
- AWS designation: **SAW**; classified as a **semi-automatic / automatic** arc welding process.

### Process Identity
- **Power Source:** Constant-voltage type, AC or DC, 300–1500 A.
- **Electrode:** Continuous solid wire or strip (1.6–4.8 mm dia) — **consumable**.
- **Polarity:** **DCEP** preferred for mild steel → deep penetration.
- **Flux depth:** 25–50 mm above the workpiece.
- **Flux recovery:** ~70 % reusable.

### Key Numerical Relationships
- **Heat Input:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm** (S in mm/min).
- **Thermal efficiency of SAW:** $\eta \approx 0.85$–$0.95$ (highest among arc processes).
- **Deposition rate:** 4–25 kg/hr (5–10× SMAW).
- **Plate thickness capability:** 5 mm to 100+ mm in single or few passes.
- **Positional limitation:** **Flat and horizontal only** — not suitable for vertical/overhead.

### Six Functions of Flux (Memorize)
1. Arc shielding 2. Slag formation 3. Alloy addition 4. Weld pool shaping 5. Heat concentration 6. Deoxidization.

### Advantages
- Highest productivity among arc welding processes
- Deep penetration, fewer passes
- Excellent weld quality (low hydrogen, low spatter)
- Inherent shielding (no external gas)
- Operator-friendly (no arc flash, no fume)
- Flux recyclable

### Limitations
- Limited to **flat/horizontal** positions
- Visible weld pool is **not possible** — operator cannot see the arc
- High initial equipment cost
- Not suitable for thin sheets (< 2 mm)
- Flux handling, storage, and disposal required
- Cannot be used for non-ferrous metals (in standard form)
