### Applications
- Ship hulls, offshore platforms
- Pressure vessels, boilers
- Spiral welded pipes (HSAW for oil & gas)
- Storage tanks, penstocks
- Nuclear reactor vessels
- Heavy structural fabrication

### Examiner's Mantra
> *"In SAW, the **arc is hidden**, the **flux is multifunctional**, the **deposition is high**, and the **position is limited**."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tungsten Inert Gas Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tungsten Inert Gas Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Tungsten Inert Gas (TIG) Welding**, also known as **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)**, is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process that produces coalescence of metals by heating them with an arc established between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece. The molten weld pool, the tungsten electrode, and the adjacent base metal are shielded from atmospheric contamination by an **inert shielding gas** (typically Argon, Helium, or a mixture of both). Filler metal, when required, is added separately either manually or automatically from a filler rod held in the welder's other hand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TIG is classified under the family of **Electric Arc Welding Processes** and is the *only* arc welding process that uses a **non-consumable electrode** in the standard configuration. The shielding gas is **chemically inert** — it does not react with the molten metal. This is the cardinal distinction from MIG (Metal Inert Gas) welding, which uses a *consumable* electrode.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to melt two pieces of chocolate together to join them. You hold a hot metal rod (the tungsten electrode) and bring an electric spark (the arc) between the rod and the chocolate. The chocolate melts. Now, chocolate hates water and air — exposure ruins the surface. So, you blow a gentle, invisible curtain of dry, pure air (the inert shielding gas) all around the melting zone to keep the bad air out. If you need extra chocolate to fill a gap, you feed a small chocolate stick (the filler rod) into the puddle with your other hand.

That is exactly what TIG welding does — except the "chocolate" is aluminum, stainless steel, or titanium, the "metal rod" is tungsten (which has the **highest melting point of all metals at 3422 °C**), and the "curtain" is a precisely metered flow of Argon gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Tungsten?** Tungsten is used because its melting point (**3422 °C / 6192 °F**) is vastly higher than the melting point of the base metal being welded. The electrode *does not melt* — it merely serves as one terminal of the electrical arc. The arc temperature itself reaches approximately **20,000 °C** at the core, which is sufficient to melt virtually any engineering metal.

### Key Terminology Quick Reference

| Term | KTU Standard Definition |
|------|------------------------|
| **Tungsten Electrode** | Non-consumable electrode made of pure W or W + 1–4 % ThO₂ / La₂O₃ / CeO₂ |
| **Shielding Gas** | Inert gas (Ar / He) that displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂ from the weld zone |
| **Filler Rod** | Optional consumable wire fed manually to supplement weld metal |
| **Arc Length** | Gap between electrode tip and workpiece, typically **1–3 mm** |
| **Weld Pool** | Molten zone of base metal where coalescence occurs |
| **Torch** | Ceramic-nozzled holder delivering gas and electrical current to the electrode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The Six-Stage Logic Chain

The TIG process can be understood as a sequential chain of events, each of which the KTU examiner may ask you to describe in order:

1. **Gas Pre-Flow Initiation:** When the welder triggers the torch (via foot pedal or finger switch), the **shielding gas (Argon)** begins flowing *before* the arc is struck. This pre-flow (typically **0.5–2.0 seconds**) evacuates atmospheric air from the nozzle and weld zone, establishing an inert atmosphere. *Why?* Because striking an arc in an air-contaminated environment causes instantaneous porosity and tungsten oxidation.

2. **Arc Ignition — High-Frequency / Lift-Arc Start:** To avoid contaminating the tungsten electrode by striking it directly against the workpiece (which would deposit tungsten into the weld pool — a defect called *tungsten inclusion*), TIG uses one of two starting methods:
   - **High-Frequency (HF) Non-Contact Start:** A high-voltage, high-frequency (MHz range) spark jumps the gap, ionizing the gas and initiating the arc without contact.
   - **Lift-Arc (Scratch Start):** The electrode touches the workpiece lightly, then is lifted a few millimetres to establish the arc. Less common in industrial TIG.

3. **Arc Sustenance and Heat Generation:** Once the arc is struck, current flows between the **electrode (cathode in DCEN)** and the **workpiece (anode in DCEN)**. Heat is generated according to the fundamental relation:
   $$H = V \cdot I \cdot t$$
   where **H** is heat input (Joules), **V** is arc voltage, **I** is current (Amperes), and **t** is time (seconds). Roughly **70 %** of arc heat is generated at the anode (workpiece in DCEN), which is why DCEN produces *deep, narrow penetration*.

4. **Base Metal Melting and Pool Formation:** The intense heat of the arc melts a small volume of the base metal, forming the **weld pool**. The size of the pool is controlled by current — typically **5–300 A** for manual TIG.

5. **Filler Metal Addition (Optional):** If joint reinforcement is required, a filler rod of compatible composition is dipped into the leading edge of the weld pool. The filler melts and fuses with the parent metal. The welder's two hands coordinate: one for the torch, one for the filler.

6. **Arc Extinction and Gas Post-Flow:** When the arc is stopped, the shielding gas continues to flow for **3–10 seconds** (the post-flow time). *Why?* The tungsten electrode and the just-solidified weld puddle are still extremely hot and will oxidize instantly if exposed to air. Post-flow shields them until they cool below the oxidation threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Logic:** Pre-flow protects the *start* of the weld, post-flow protects the *end* and the electrode. Together they form the **gas shielding envelope** that defines weld quality.

### 2.2 Polarity Configurations — The Three Operating Modes

KTU examiners repeatedly test this. TIG can operate in three polarity modes:

| Polarity Mode | Electrode | Workpiece | Penetration | Cleaning Action | Typical Use |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** | Cathode (−) | Anode (+) | Deep & narrow (≈ 70 % heat at workpiece) | None | Steels, stainless steel, titanium, copper, nickel alloys |
| **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** | Anode (+) | Cathode (−) | Shallow & wide (≈ 70 % heat at electrode) | Strong (breaks oxide layer) | Rarely used — overheats tungsten; thin-gauge aluminum *only* with special care |
| **AC (Alternating Current)** | Alternates | Alternates | Moderate | Strong during EN half-cycle | **Aluminum & magnesium alloys** (industry default) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AC for Aluminum?** Aluminum forms a tenacious, high-melting-point oxide layer (Al₂O₃, melts at **2072 °C**, much higher than pure Al at 660 °C). During the *electrode positive* half-cycle, the positively charged argon ions bombard the workpiece surface and mechanically blast away this oxide ("cathodic cleaning"). During the *electrode negative* half-cycle, deep penetration occurs. AC gives you **both** cleaning and penetration in one stroke.

### 2.3 Process Parameters (KTU High-Yield Data Table)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Weld |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Welding Current (I)** | 5 – 300 A (manual) | Higher current → wider, deeper weld pool |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | 10 – 35 V (typically 12–18 V) | Higher voltage → wider arc, flatter bead |
| **Argon Flow Rate** | 7 – 20 L/min | Insufficient → porosity; excessive → turbulence & aspiration of air |
| **Arc Length** | 1 – 3 mm (≈ 1.5 × electrode diameter) | Too long → porosity, undercut; too short → electrode contamination |
| **Travel Speed (v)** | 50 – 300 mm/min | Faster → narrower, shallower bead; heat input drops |
| **Electrode Diameter** | 1.0 – 4.8 mm | Larger → higher current capacity |
| **Tungsten Type (AWS)** | EWP (pure), EWTh-2 (2 % ThO₂), EWG (La₂O₃), EWCe-2 (CeO₂) | Oxides lower work function, improve arc starting |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Heat Input** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ | HI in kJ/mm; $\eta$ = arc efficiency (TIG ≈ 0.6); V in V; I in A; v in mm/s | Predicts weld bead size, HAZ width, distortion |
| **Power in Arc** | $P = V \cdot I$ | P in Watts | Determines melting capacity |
| **Travel Speed Conversion** | $v_{mm/s} = \dfrac{v_{mm/min}}{60}$ | — | Required for unit consistency in heat input formula |
| **Argon Coverage Area** | $A_{cov} = \pi \cdot r^2$ (where r ≈ nozzle radius + 5–10 mm) | A in mm² | Verifies shielding envelope over weld pool |
| **Electrode Current Rating (approx.)** | $I_{max} \approx k \cdot d^{1.5}$ | k ≈ 40–60; d = electrode diameter in mm | Quick sizing of tungsten electrode |
| **Penetration Depth (Empirical, DCEN on steel)** | $P \approx 0.05 \cdot I$ (mm) for 1.6 mm electrode | I in A | Estimate keyhole/pool depth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the heat input formula** — it appears in nearly every KTU welding question. For TIG, the arc efficiency factor $\eta$ is **0.6** (compared to 0.8 for MIG/GMAW and 1.0 for thermit/oxy-fuel processes).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

TIG is the **gold standard** for high-integrity welds in:
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft engine compressor blades, rocket fuel tanks, titanium fuselage sections.
- **Nuclear:** Stainless steel piping, fuel cladding, reactor vessel internals.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food:** Sanitary stainless tube welding (orbital TIG).
- **Automotive (premium):** Aluminum chassis, exhaust systems, roll cages.
- **Art & Sculpture:** Bronze, copper, and stainless artistic fabrications.
- **Pipe & Tube:** Orbital TIG for circumferential welds in high-pressure pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use TIG for everything?** TIG is *slow* and *operator-skill-intensive*. For thick steel plate or high-production automotive chassis, MIG or SAW (Submerged Arc Welding) is preferred. TIG's niche is *quality-critical, thin-to-medium sections* of reactive and exotic metals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Calculation for a Typical TIG Weld on 3 mm Stainless Steel

**Given (KTU-style problem data):**
- Welding current: $I = 120 \text{ A}$
- Arc voltage: $V = 14 \text{ V}$
- Travel speed: $v = 100 \text{ mm/min}$
- Process: TIG (arc efficiency $\eta = 0.6$)

**Required:** Calculate the heat input $HI$ in kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation for heat input.**

The heat input delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld is given by:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}} \right]$$

The factor of 1000 converts the numerator from J/s · mm to J/mm, then the result to kJ/mm.

**Step 2 — Convert travel speed to consistent units (mm/s).**

$$v = \frac{100 \text{ mm/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 1.667 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the raw arc power in Watts.**

$$P = V \cdot I = 14 \text{ V} \times 120 \text{ A} = 1680 \text{ W} = 1.680 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the arc efficiency factor for TIG.**

$$P_{effective} = \eta \cdot P = 0.6 \times 1680 \text{ W} = 1008 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by travel speed to obtain energy per unit length.**

$$HI = \frac{1008 \text{ J/s}}{1.667 \text{ mm/s}} = 604.8 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kJ/mm (the conventional KTU unit).**

$$HI = \frac{604.8}{1000} = 0.605 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{HI \approx 0.605 \text{ kJ/mm}}$

**Step 7 — Interpretation for KTU 2024 valuation.**

- This heat input is in the **typical TIG range of 0.4 – 1.5 kJ/mm**.
- It is appropriate for a 3 mm stainless steel sheet (excessive heat input would cause grain growth, sensitization, and distortion).
- Heat input above **1.5 kJ/mm** for austenitic stainless steel risks **chromium carbide precipitation** at grain boundaries, leading to intergranular corrosion — the classic "weld decay."

### 3.2 Derivation: Tungsten Electrode Sizing for a Given Current

**Given:** A welder must run 180 A DCEN on 6 mm mild steel using EWTh-2 (2 % Thoriated) tungsten.

**Required:** Recommend the smallest safe tungsten electrode diameter.

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical current-carrying capacity of EWTh-2 (DCEN, KTU data).**

| Electrode Diameter | DCEN Current Capacity (EWTh-2) |
|--------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1.0 mm | 15 – 80 A |
| 1.6 mm | 70 – 150 A |
| 2.4 mm | 140 – 250 A |
| 3.2 mm | 225 – 400 A |
| 4.0 mm | 350 – 500 A |
| 4.8 mm | 500 – 750 A |

**Step 2 — Find the smallest electrode whose capacity includes 180 A.**

- 1.6 mm → max 150 A → **insufficient**
- 2.4 mm → 140–250 A → **180 A lies in range** ✓

**Step 3 — Recommendation.**

$$\boxed{d_{tungsten} = 2.4 \text{ mm (EWTh-2)}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute approximate maximum current using the empirical formula** $I_{max} \approx k \cdot d^{1.5}$ for verification (take k = 50 for thoriated tungsten, DCEN):

$$I_{max} \approx 50 \times (2.4)^{1.5} = 50 \times 3.718 = 185.9 \text{ A}$$

This matches the tabulated range. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Polarity Selection Decision Tree

| Base Metal | Recommended Polarity | Shielding Gas | Filler Rod | Reason |
|------------|----------------------|---------------|------------|--------|
| Mild Steel | DCEN | Pure Argon | ER70S | Deep penetration, clean bead |
| Stainless Steel (304, 316) | DCEN | Ar + 2–5 % H₂ or pure Ar | ER308L, ER316L | Prevents oxidation; L grade resists sensitization |
| Aluminum (5xxx, 6xxx) | AC (with HF start) | Pure Ar or Ar/He mix | ER4043, ER5356 | AC cleaning action breaks Al₂O₃ |
| Copper | DCEN (high current) | Pure Ar | ERCu or ERCuSn | Argon suppresses hydrogen porosity |
| Titanium | DCEN | Pure Ar (high purity 99.99 %) | Grade-matched Ti | Prevents embrittlement by O₂/N₂/H₂ |
| Magnesium | AC | Pure Ar | AZ-series | Cleaning action essential |

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix — TIG vs MIG (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | TIG (GTAW) | MIG (GMAW) |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Electrode | Non-consumable tungsten | Consumable wire (continuous) |
| Filler feed | Manual / cold wire | Automatic through gun |
| Skill level | High | Moderate |
| Weld quality | Superior, spatter-free | Good, some spatter |
| Speed | Slow (manual) | Fast (high deposition) |
| Material thickness | 0.5 – 6 mm typical | 1 – 25+ mm |
| Reactive metals (Al, Ti, Mg) | **Excellent** | Difficult / not preferred |
| Cost per metre | High | Low |
| Automation feasibility | Orbital TIG possible | Easy (robotic MIG common) |

### 3.5 Common TIG Defects — Causes and Remedies (KTU Examiner Favourite)

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| **Porosity** | Insufficient gas, contaminated base, long arc | Increase Ar flow, clean with acetone, shorten arc |
| **Tungsten Inclusion** | Electrode touches weld pool | Maintain arc length, use HF start |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current, fast travel | Reduce current, slow down, adjust torch angle |
| **Lack of Fusion** | Cold weld, wrong torch angle | Increase current, use 70–80° drag angle |
| **Oxidation (color tint on SS)** | Insufficient post-flow shielding | Increase post-flow time, use trailing shield |
| **Cracking** | High restraint, wrong filler | Use matching low-carbon filler, preheat |
| **Excessive Penetration / Burn-Through** | Current too high, thin metal | Reduce current, increase travel speed, use backing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *tungsten inclusion* (a *tungsten fragment* trapped in the weld) with *porosity* (gas pockets). Tungsten inclusion is **metallic** and radiopaque; porosity is **gas** and appears as round voids. KTU expects you to use the correct term.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TIG Torch Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Power Source Constant Current DC or AC] --> B1[TIG Torch Body Insulated Handle]
    A2[Argon Cylinder High Purity 99.99 percent] --> C1[Gas Regulator and Flowmeter Set 7 to 20 L per min]
    C1 --> B1
    A3[High Frequency Starter Unit] --> B1
    A4[Filler Rod ER grade matching base metal] --> D1[Welder Right Hand Feeds Rod Manually]
    B1 --> E1[Tungsten Electrode Non Consumable 1.0 to 4.8 mm]
    E1 --> F1[Electric Arc 20000 Degree C Core Temperature]
    F1 --> G1[Weld Pool Molten Base Metal]
    D1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Shielding Gas Envelope Argon Curtain]
    H1 --> I1[Solidified Weld Bead]
    A5[Foot Pedal or Finger Amptrol] --> A1
    I1 --> J1[Cooled Weld Zone Post Flow Shields Electrode and Bead]
```

### 4.2 TIG Process — Polarity Configuration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DCEN
        A1[Power Supply Negative Terminal] -->|Cable| B1[Tungsten Electrode Cathode]
        B1 -->|Arc 70 percent heat at workpiece| C1[Workpiece Anode Positive]
        C1 -->|Return Cable| A1
    end
    subgraph DCEP
        A2[Power Supply Positive Terminal] -->|Cable| B2[Tungsten Electrode Anode]
        B2 -->|Arc 70 percent heat at electrode| C2[Workpiece Cathode Negative]
        C2 -->|Return Cable| A2
    end
    subgraph AC
        A3[AC Power Source Sine Wave 50 or 60 Hz] -->|Alternates| B3[Tungsten Electrode]
        B3 -->|Cleaning Half Cycle EN| C3[Workpiece]
        C3 -->|Penetration Half Cycle EP| A3
    end
```

### 4.3 Shielding Gas Envelope — Pre-Flow, Weld, and Post-Flow Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title TIG Shielding Gas and Arc Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Gas Flow
    Pre Flow Argon 0.5 to 2 sec       :a1, 0, 2
    Main Flow Argon 7 to 20 L per min :a2, 2, 30
    Post Flow Argon 3 to 10 sec       :a3, 30, 38
    section Arc and Weld
    High Frequency Start              :b1, 2, 3
    Arc Sustained Welding             :b2, 3, 30
    Arc Extinction Current Down Slope :b3, 30, 31
    section Weld Zone
    Weld Pool Molten                  :c1, 3, 31
    Solidified Bead Cooling           :c2, 31, 38
```

### 4.4 TIG Equipment Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WeldingStation
        A[1 Electrical Subsystem] --> A1[Constant Current Power Source]
        A --> A2[HF Starter Unit]
        A --> A3[Foot Pedal Amptrol]
        B[2 Gas Delivery Subsystem] --> B1[Argon Cylinder]
        B --> B2[Two Stage Regulator]
        B --> B3[Flowmeter L per min]
        B --> B4[Gas Hose to Torch]
        C[3 Torch and Electrode Subsystem] --> C1[TIG Torch with Ceramic Nozzle]
        C --> C2[Tungsten Electrode EWTh 2 or EWG]
        C --> C3[Collet and Back Cap]
        D[4 Filler Feed Subsystem] --> D1[Manual Filler Rod]
        D --> D2[Optional Cold Wire Feeder]
        E[5 Workpiece Subsystem] --> E1[Grounded Work Clamp]
        E --> E2[Base Metal Joint]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define TIG welding. Why is tungsten used as the electrode material?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) welding, also called Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW), is an arc welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating with an arc between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the workpiece, with the molten pool and electrode shielded by an **inert gas** (typically Argon). Filler metal, if required, is added separately.

Tungsten is selected because it possesses the **highest melting point of all metals (3422 °C)** and excellent electrical conductivity, allowing it to act as a stable arc terminal without melting or being consumed. Its high thermal resistance permits sustained arcing at temperatures that vaporize most engineering metals.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — shielding gas role, 1 mark — reason for tungsten. KTU expects all three points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the role of pre-flow and post-flow of shielding gas in TIG welding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **pre-flow** of shielding gas (typically 0.5–2.0 s before arc initiation) evacuates atmospheric air from the nozzle and weld zone, establishing an inert atmosphere. This prevents atmospheric contamination (N₂, O₂, H₂O) from reacting with the molten tungsten tip at the instant of arc strike, which would otherwise cause tungsten oxidation and porosity in the start of the weld.

The **post-flow** of shielding gas (typically 3–10 s after arc extinction) continues to blanket the hot tungsten electrode and the just-solidified weld bead with inert gas until they cool below their oxidation threshold. This prevents oxide formation, discoloration, and atmospheric embrittlement — particularly critical for reactive metals like titanium and stainless steel.

*(1.5 marks each for pre-flow and post-flow role. KTU values specific numerical time ranges.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of TIG welding with a neat sketch. Discuss the functions of shielding gas, tungsten electrode, and filler rod. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare TIG welding with MIG welding on the basis of (i) electrode type, (ii) shielding gas, (iii) weld quality, (iv) speed, (v) operator skill, and (vi) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The TIG welding setup consists of a **constant-current power source** (AC or DC), a **torch** holding a tungsten electrode, a **shielding gas supply** (Ar cylinder + regulator + flowmeter), an optional **filler rod**, and a **grounded workpiece clamp**. The welder strikes an arc — typically via a high-frequency (HF) non-contact start — between the tungsten electrode and the workpiece. The arc (core temperature ≈ 20,000 °C) melts a small region of the base metal, forming a weld pool. Simultaneously, argon gas flows from the torch nozzle, blanketing the arc, pool, and hot electrode in an inert atmosphere to prevent oxidation.

**Functions of key components:**
- **Shielding gas (Argon):** Displaces atmospheric N₂ and O₂; prevents porosity, embrittlement, and oxidation; stabilizes the arc.
- **Tungsten electrode:** Acts as the non-consumable cathode (in DCEN) terminal of the arc; conducts current; resists melting due to its 3422 °C melting point. Often alloyed with 1–2 % ThO₂ to lower work function and improve arc starting.
- **Filler rod:** Added manually or automatically to the leading edge of the weld pool; provides additional metal to fill the joint gap; its composition must match the base metal to ensure metallurgical compatibility and mechanical strength.

*(Sketch: 2 marks; principle: 2 marks; component functions: 3 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

| # | Parameter | TIG (GTAW) | MIG (GMAW) |
|---|-----------|------------|------------|
| (i) | **Electrode type** | Non-consumable tungsten (W) | Consumable wire electrode (continuous) |
| (ii) | **Shielding gas** | Inert only (Ar, He) | Inert or active (Ar, CO₂, Ar/CO₂ mix) |
| (iii) | **Weld quality** | Superior — clean, spatter-free, no slag | Good — minor spatter, some cleanup needed |
| (iv) | **Speed** | Slow (manual, low deposition) | Fast (high wire feed, mechanized) |
| (v) | **Operator skill** | High (two-hand coordination) | Moderate (one-hand trigger) |
| (vi) | **Typical applications** | Aerospace, nuclear, thin SS, Al, Ti, exotic alloys | Automotive, structural steel, fabrication, thick plates |

*(1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall conclusion/conciseness.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What are the different polarity modes used in TIG welding? Explain the role of alternating current (AC) in welding aluminum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TIG welding operation is performed on 4 mm stainless steel with the following parameters: current = 150 A, arc voltage = 16 V, travel speed = 120 mm/min. Take arc efficiency for TIG as 0.6. Calculate (i) the heat input in kJ/mm, and (ii) comment on whether the heat input is safe with respect to sensitization of austenitic stainless steel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

TIG welding can be operated in three polarity modes:

**1. DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative):** The tungsten electrode is connected to the negative terminal (cathode) and the workpiece to the positive terminal (anode). Approximately 70 % of the arc heat is generated at the workpiece (anode), producing **deep, narrow penetration**. This is the standard mode for steels, stainless steels, titanium, copper, and nickel alloys. No oxide-cleaning action occurs.

**2. DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive):** The tungsten electrode is positive (anode) and the workpiece is negative (cathode). About 70 % of the heat concentrates at the electrode, causing rapid tungsten overheating and balling. Penetration is shallow but cleaning is strong. This mode is **rarely used** in TIG because it destroys the electrode.

**3. AC (Alternating Current):** The polarity alternates at 50/60 Hz between EN and EP. During the **EN half-cycle**, the electrode is negative and electrons bombard the workpiece, giving **deep penetration**. During the **EP half-cycle**, the electrode is positive and positively charged argon ions bombard the workpiece surface, mechanically blasting away the **aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃)** layer — this is the **cathodic cleaning action**. The square-wave AC TIG machine (modern inverter type) allows the welder to adjust the **cleaning-to-penetration ratio** via the *EP/EN balance* control (typically 60–80 % EN for a clean balance).

**Why AC is essential for aluminum:** Aluminum forms a stable, high-melting-point oxide (Al₂O₃ melts at 2072 °C, while pure Al melts at 660 °C). If not removed, this oxide layer prevents fusion and contaminates the weld. AC's cleaning action continuously removes the oxide during welding, while the EN half-cycle provides penetration. Thus, **AC is the only practical polarity for welding aluminum and magnesium** in TIG.

*(Polarity modes: 3 marks; AC mechanism: 2 marks; aluminum-specific reason: 2 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** I = 150 A, V = 16 V, v = 120 mm/min, η = 0.6 (TIG)

**(i) Heat input calculation:**

**Step 1 — Convert travel speed to mm/s:**

$$v = \frac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw arc power:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 16 \times 150 = 2400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Apply arc efficiency:**

$$P_{eff} = 0.6 \times 2400 = 1440 \text{ W} = 1440 \text{ J/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat input:**

$$HI = \frac{P_{eff}}{v} = \frac{1440 \text{ J/s}}{2.0 \text{ mm/s}} = 720 \text{ J/mm} = 0.72 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{HI = 0.72 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

**(ii) Safety with respect to sensitization:**

For austenitic stainless steel (e.g., 304, 316), the **critical heat input threshold** to avoid chromium carbide (Cr₂₃C₆) precipitation at grain boundaries — known as **sensitization** — is generally below **1.0 kJ/mm** for thin sections (≤ 6 mm). Sensitization causes intergranular corrosion ("weld decay") and loss of corrosion resistance.

The calculated value of **0.72 kJ/mm** is **below 1.0 kJ/mm** and therefore **safe** with respect to sensitization for 4 mm austenitic SS. However, if the section were thicker or if multiple passes were required without interpass cooling, cumulative heat input could approach the danger zone, and a **low-carbon grade filler (e.g., ER308L)** with ≤ 0.03 % C would be recommended as an additional safeguard.

*(Calculation: 4 marks; interpretation: 3 marks.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in TIG Welding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by arc efficiency (η = 0.6 for TIG).** A student who computes HI = V·I/v without η loses **2 of 4 marks** in numerical problems. *Always* state η explicitly.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in heat input formula.** Using v in mm/min directly in the denominator (instead of converting to mm/s) yields a result 60× too small. KTU examiners will mark it *wrong*, not give "step marking."
> 3. **Confusing DCEN and DCEP.** A common error: stating "DCEP gives deep penetration." It does not — DCEP concentrates heat on the *electrode* and gives shallow weld penetration.
> 4. **Omitting the role of the cleaning half-cycle in AC.** Stating "AC is used for aluminum" without explaining the *oxide-removal mechanism* earns only 1 of 2 marks for that point.
> 5. **Mixing up TIG and MIG shielding gases.** MIG often uses Ar/CO₂ (active shielding) for steel; TIG uses *pure inert* gas. Examiners will deduct for implying CO₂ is acceptable in TIG.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TIG (GTAW)** = **non-consumable tungsten** electrode + **inert gas shield** + optional filler rod.
- Tungsten melting point = **3422 °C**; arc core temperature ≈ **20,000 °C**.
- **Pre-flow** (0.5–2 s) and **post-flow** (3–10 s) of argon are *mandatory* to prevent atmospheric contamination.
- Arc efficiency of TIG: **η = 0.6** (KTU value).
- **DCEN** → deep penetration, used for steel/SS/Ti; **AC** → cleaning + penetration, used for **aluminum and magnesium**; **DCEP** → rarely used.
- **HF (high-frequency) non-contact start** is standard — avoids tungsten contamination.
- **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$ in kJ/mm (use **mm/s** for v).
- For austenitic SS, keep HI below ~**1.0 kJ/mm** to avoid sensitization; use low-carbon fillers (ER308L, ER316L).
- Argon flow rate typical range: **7–20 L/min**; too little → porosity; too much → turbulence and air ingress.
- **Tungsten types (AWS):** EWP (pure), EWTh-2 (2 % ThO₂ — most common), EWG (La₂O₃ — modern replacement for thoriated), EWCe-2 (CeO₂).
- **Defects to recognize:** porosity, tungsten inclusion, undercut, lack of fusion, oxidation tint.
- **Applications:** aerospace, nuclear, pharma, food-grade stainless, aluminum chassis, titanium implants, art sculpture.
- **Comparison with MIG:** TIG is *slower but cleaner*; MIG is *faster but less refined*; TIG wins on reactive and exotic metals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Inert Gas Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Inert Gas Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Metal Inert Gas (MIG) Welding**, also formally designated as **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** in international standards (AWS A3.0), is a semi-automatic or automatic arc welding process in which a **continuous, consumable solid wire electrode** is mechanically fed through a welding gun at a constant, regulated rate. The arc is established between this continuously advancing wire and the workpiece, melting both the electrode and the base metal to form the weld pool. The molten weld pool, the arc, and the solidifying weld metal are simultaneously shielded from atmospheric contamination (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen) by an externally supplied **shielding gas** (or gas mixture) delivered through a nozzle concentric to the electrode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Distinction:** In strict KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the term **"Inert"** specifically denotes shielding gases from the noble group (Argon, Helium) that do **not** participate in the welding reaction. When active gases like **CO₂** or **O₂** are added, the process is technically reclassified as **Metal Active Gas (MAG) Welding**. However, the umbrella term MIG is colloquially (and in most exam contexts) used for the entire family.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to glue two metal plates together, but the "glue" (molten metal) hates being touched by air — it oxidizes instantly and ruins the bond. So you build a tiny, pressurized **invisible tent of protective gas** right at the joint. Now, instead of applying glue with a stick, you push a **continuous metal pencil** (the wire electrode) into the joint while a spark (the arc) melts both the pencil tip and the plates. A constant stream of shielding gas blows over the puddle, keeping the air away. As you move along, fresh metal solidifies and bonds the plates. This entire act — feeding pencil, sparking, and blowing gas — is automated through a single **welding gun** that a human operator (or robot) simply guides along the seam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** MIG welding is essentially an **"arc + wire-feed + gas-shield"** trilogy. Remove any one of these three pillars and the process fundamentally fails.

## Why MIG Welding is a "High-Yield" KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Industry dominance:** MIG/GMAW is the **most widely used industrial arc welding process globally**, accounting for over **60%** of all arc welding applications in manufacturing.
> - **KTU exam weightage:** This topic consistently contributes **7–14 marks** in End Semester Examinations (ESE) under Module 2.
> - **CO Mapping:** Directly maps to **CO2** — *Understand various material removal and joining processes used in industry*.

## Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Shielding Gas (Inert) | Argon (Ar) – 99.99% pure | % purity |
| Wire Feed Speed (WFS) | 2 – 20 | m/min |
| Arc Voltage | 18 – 32 | Volts (V) |
| Welding Current | 50 – 600 | Amperes (A) |
| Deposition Efficiency | 90 – 98 | % |
| Argon Density (STP) | 1.784 | kg/m³ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

The MIG process unfolds in a precisely orchestrated sequence of events. The following structured logic breaks down the underlying physics and operational mechanics.

1. **Electrical Circuit Completion**
   The welding power source (typically a **Constant Voltage — CV** type, also called a "flat characteristic" machine) is connected such that one terminal goes to the **contact tip** of the welding gun, and the other goes to the **workpiece via the earth clamp**. The arc closes the circuit through the gap between the wire and the workpiece.

2. **Wire Feeding Mechanism**
   A **wire feed motor** (driven by drive rolls inside the feeder unit) pushes the spooled electrode wire through a liner cable into the gun. The rate of feeding (Wire Feed Speed, WFS) is the **primary control variable** that sets the welding current in CV systems.

3. **Arc Ignition and Stabilization**
   As the wire approaches the workpiece, an **arc is struck** (typically by a "touch-start" or "scratch start" or a high-frequency boost). The arc melts the tip of the wire, forming droplets that detach and transfer across the arc gap into the weld pool.

4. **Shielding Gas Delivery**
   Concurrently, shielding gas flows from a cylinder → regulator → flowmeter → gas hose → nozzle of the gun → over the weld pool. The gas displaces atmospheric air, creating an inert local atmosphere.

5. **Metal Transfer Across the Arc**
   Molten metal from the wire tip crosses the arc by one of four primary transfer modes (see Section 3 for full derivation):
   - **Short-Circuit Transfer (Dip Transfer)**
   - **Globular Transfer**
   - **Spray Transfer**
   - **Pulsed-Spray Transfer**

6. **Solidification and Weld Bead Formation**
   As the gun traverses along the joint, the weld pool solidifies behind the arc, forming a continuous weld bead with metallurgical fusion to the base metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind CV Power Sources:** In MIG welding, arc length naturally varies due to operator movement and surface irregularities. A **Constant Voltage** source inherently self-adjusts current — when arc length shortens, current surges and burns back the wire; when it lengthens, current drops. This creates a self-regulating "arc stiffness" unique to MIG.

## Modes of Metal Transfer — The Heart of MIG

| Transfer Mode | Current Range | Voltage Range | Shielding Gas | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Circuit (Dip)** | 50 – 150 A | 14 – 20 V | CO₂ or Ar/CO₂ mix | Wire touches pool; low heat input |
| **Globular** | 150 – 250 A | 22 – 28 V | CO₂ / Ar mix | Large irregular drops; spatter prone |
| **Spray** | 250 – 500 A | 28 – 35 V | High Argon (≥80% Ar) | Axial drop stream; spatter-free |
| **Pulsed Spray** | 50 – 300 A (peak) | 18 – 28 V (background) | Argon-rich | One droplet per pulse; precise control |

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE numerical problems. Memorize the variables, units, and the **60** and **1000** conversion factors — they are common error points.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \times 60$ | $\eta$ = thermal efficiency, $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current, $S$ = travel speed | kJ/mm |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{I \cdot k}{1000}$ | $I$ = current, $k$ = constant (g/A·h) — depends on wire | kg/h or g/min |
| 3 | **Wire Feed Speed Relation** | $I = a \cdot WFS + b$ | Empirical linear relation for the specific wire | A |
| 4 | **Deposition Efficiency ($\eta_d$)** | $\eta_d = \dfrac{W_{deposited}}{W_{consumed}} \times 100$ | Ratio of weld metal retained to wire consumed | % |

**Standard Reference Values for KTU Problems:**
- MIG Thermal Efficiency: $\eta \approx 0.80$ (80%)
- For steel wires, deposition constant $k \approx 1.0$ g/A·h (approximate)
- Voltage–Current synergy: for every **1 V** increase, current rises by approximately **30–50 A** in CV systems

## Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> MIG welding is the **backbone of mass-production manufacturing**. Its primary industrial applications include:
> - **Automotive industry:** Body-in-white assembly, chassis fabrication, robotic MIG cells
> - **Shipbuilding:** Long-seam welding of hull plates using tandem MIG (twin-wire) setups
> - **Pressure vessel fabrication:** LPG cylinders, boiler shells
> - **Structural engineering:** Bridge girders, building frameworks
> - **Sheet metal fabrication:** HVAC ducts, furniture, agricultural equipment
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Heat Input (HI) — The Most Tested KTU Numerical

The **Heat Input** is defined as the amount of energy supplied per unit length of weld. It governs the **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** size, mechanical properties, and distortion of the welded joint.

### Step 1: Start from the fundamental definition of electrical energy

The total arc energy $E_{arc}$ produced in time $t$ is the product of voltage, current, and time:

$$
E_{arc} = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

where:
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts (V)
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes (A)
- $t$ = time in seconds (s)

The result $E_{arc}$ is in Joules (J), since $V \cdot A = W$ (Watts) and $W \cdot s = J$.

### Step 2: Convert time into a length-based parameter

Time $t$ is not convenient for welding calculations. We want energy per unit length of weld. The relationship between time, travel speed, and weld length is:

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

where:
- $L$ = length of weld in millimetres (mm)
- $S$ = travel speed in mm/min
- $t$ = time in minutes (min) — note the unit consistency

> Conversion logic: Travel speed is normally given in **mm/min** in KTU problems. To keep the time in **seconds**, we apply a factor of **60** to convert minutes into seconds, since $1 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ s}$.

Substituting and adding the 60-second conversion factor:

$$
E_{arc} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S} \cdot 60
$$

### Step 3: Introduce Thermal Efficiency ($\eta$)

Not all electrical energy is converted into useful heat at the weld. A portion is lost as radiation, spatter kinetic energy, and conduction through the gun. This is captured by the **process thermal efficiency** $\eta$ (a dimensionless factor between 0 and 1):

$$
E_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S} \cdot 60
$$

### Step 4: Define Heat Input as Energy per Unit Length

Dividing the useful energy by the weld length $L$ gives the energy per millimetre of weld:

$$
HI = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S}
$$

This is in units of $\dfrac{J \cdot 60}{mm \cdot \text{min}} = \dfrac{J}{\text{mm} \cdot \text{s}}$. To express in the standard **kJ/mm** form, divide by **1000**:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 16.67} \quad \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

where the combined conversion factor is $16.67 = \dfrac{1000}{60}$.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** In a MIG welding operation, the arc voltage is **25 V**, current is **200 A**, and travel speed is **300 mm/min**. Assuming a thermal efficiency of 0.80, calculate the heat input per unit length of weld.

**Step A:** Identify the given values and write them in the standard units:
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $V = 25$ V
- $I = 200$ A
- $S = 300$ mm/min

**Step B:** Substitute into the heat input formula:

$$
HI = \dfrac{0.80 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}
$$

**Step C:** Evaluate the numerator:

$$
0.80 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60 = 0.80 \times 25 \times 12000 = 0.80 \times 300000 = 240000 \text{ J/mm·s equivalent}
$$

**Step D:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$
300 \times 1000 = 300000
$$

**Step E:** Final division and unit conversion:

$$
HI = \dfrac{240000}{300000} = 0.80 \text{ J/mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad HI = 0.80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI = 0.80 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** A typical MIG heat input of 0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm is realistic for thin-to-medium steel plates. Our result falls comfortably in this range.

---

## Derivation 2: Deposition Rate (DR) Relationship

The **Deposition Rate** is the mass of weld metal deposited per unit time. For MIG welding, it is directly proportional to the current because higher current melts more wire per second.

### Step 1: Empirical Foundation

Experimental data for a given electrode wire diameter and type shows a **linear relationship** between deposition rate and welding current:

$$
DR = k \cdot I
$$

where $k$ is the **deposition constant** with units of g/A·h. It encapsulates the wire's mass per unit length and density.

### Step 2: Convert to Common Time Units (kg/h)

For KTU numerical problems where $k$ is in g/A·h, multiply by $I$ in Amperes to get DR in g/h, then divide by 1000 to convert to kg/h:

$$
DR_{kg/h} = \dfrac{k \cdot I}{1000}
$$

### Step 3: Alternative Expression Using Wire Feed Speed

The deposition rate can also be expressed via the wire feed speed $WFS$ in m/min and the wire's mass per metre $m$ in g/m:

$$
DR_{g/min} = WFS \cdot m
$$

$$
DR_{kg/h} = \dfrac{WFS \cdot m \cdot 60}{1000}
$$

### Worked Example

> **Problem:** A MIG welding setup uses a steel wire with a deposition constant $k = 0.90$ g/A·h. The welding current is set to **300 A**. Calculate the deposition rate in kg/h.

**Step A:** Substitute directly:

$$
DR = \dfrac{0.90 \times 300}{1000} = \dfrac{270}{1000}
$$

**Step B:** Final answer:

$$
\boxed{DR = 0.27 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

---

## Derivation 3: Relationship Between Arc Length and Voltage

The arc length $L_a$ in mm is empirically related to arc voltage $V$ by the approximate relation:

$$
L_a \approx 0.1 \times V
$$

For a voltage of **25 V**, this gives an arc length of **2.5 mm** — a critical parameter for maintaining stable metal transfer in the spray mode.

---

## Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Transfer Mode

| Application Requirement | Recommended Transfer Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Thin sheet (≤ 3 mm), positional welding | Short-Circuit Transfer | Low heat input, minimal burn-through, all-position capability |
| Medium thickness (3 – 10 mm), high productivity | Spray Transfer | High deposition rate, deep penetration, spatter-free |
| All-position welding with fine control | Pulsed Spray | Heat input control independent of transfer rate |
| Out-of-position overhead welding | Short-Circuit with Argon-rich mix | Better puddle control, less sagging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture: MIG/GMAW Process System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete MIG welding workstation, illustrating the interaction between power, wire-feed, gas-delivery, and arc subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Power Source CV Constant Voltage 3 Phase] --> P2[Rectifier and Capacitor Bank]
    P2 --> P3[Output Terminals Positive to Gun Negative to Work]
    P3 --> G1[Welding Gun Contact Tip]
    G1 --> A1[Arc Zone Wire Tip to Workpiece]
    A1 --> W1[Workpiece Clamped to Earth]
    W1 --> P3
    WF1[Wire Spool 0.8 to 1.6 mm Solid Wire] --> WF2[Wire Drive Motor and Drive Rolls]
    WF2 --> WF3[Wire Liner Cable]
    WF3 --> G1
    GS1[Shielding Gas Cylinder Argon or ArCO2 Mix] --> GS2[Pressure Regulator and Flowmeter]
    GS2 --> GS3[Gas Hose]
    GS3 --> G1
    G1 --> A1
    A1 --> W2[Weld Pool and Solidifying Bead]
    A1 --> AR1[Atmospheric Contamination Oxygen Nitrogen]
    GS2 -. Shields .-> AR1
    CO1[Operator or Robot Traverse System] --> G1
    CO1 --> TM[Travel Speed S mm per min]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent the path of **energy and material flow** (electrical current, wire, shielding gas).
- The **Dotted arrow** represents the **protective function** of the shielding gas — displacing atmospheric contamination.
- The **Feedback loop** between Output Terminals → Arc → Workpiece → Earth Clamp is the closed electrical circuit.

## Process Sequence Flowchart: Metal Transfer Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Material Thickness Selection] --> A1{Thickness t in mm}
    A1 -->|t less than 3| B1[Choose Short Circuit Dip Transfer]
    A1 -->|t between 3 and 10| B2[Choose Spray Transfer]
    A1 -->|t greater than 10| B3[Choose Pulsed Spray Transfer]
    B1 --> C1[Set Voltage 14 to 20 V]
    B1 --> C2[Set Current 50 to 150 A]
    B1 --> C3[Use CO2 or ArCO2 80 20 mix]
    B2 --> D1[Set Voltage 28 to 35 V]
    B2 --> D2[Set Current 250 to 500 A]
    B2 --> D3[Use Argon rich 80 to 90 percent Ar]
    B3 --> E1[Set Peak Current 200 to 300 A]
    B3 --> E2[Set Background Current 30 to 50 A]
    B3 --> E3[Set Pulse Frequency 50 to 500 Hz]
    C1 --> F1[Perform Weld]
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    D3 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Inspect Weld Bead for Defects]
    G1 -->|Defects Found| H1[Adjust Heat Input and Retest]
    G1 -->|Defects Absent| H2[Accept Weld]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above is a **functional decision tree** — in real industrial practice, automated synergic MIG power sources (e.g., Lincoln Power Wave, Miller Dimension) internally store these parameter sets and auto-select them once the operator inputs wire type, gas type, and thickness. The KTU examiner, however, expects the student to demonstrate understanding of the **underlying logic** — so memorising the table is essential.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Metal Inert Gas (MIG) welding. List any four advantages of MIG welding over Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW).**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** MIG welding is an arc welding process in which a **continuous, consumable solid wire electrode** is fed through a welding gun, and the arc and molten weld pool are protected by an **inert shielding gas** (typically Argon) supplied through a nozzle.

**Four Advantages over SMAW:**

1. **Higher Productivity:** Continuous wire feeding eliminates the need to stop and replace stick electrodes, resulting in 2–3 times higher deposition rate.
2. **Better Weld Quality:** The inert gas shield produces cleaner welds with less slag and spatter, reducing post-weld cleaning effort.
3. **Longer Continuous Operation:** Since wire is fed from a continuous spool, very long weld seams can be made without interruption.
4. **Versatility:** MIG can weld a wide range of metals — mild steel, stainless steel, aluminium, copper, and alloys — with appropriate gas and wire combinations. It is also easily **robotized** for automated production.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often confuse MIG with **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas)** welding. Remember: MIG = **consumable wire electrode**; TIG = **non-consumable tungsten electrode** with a separate filler rod hand. Mixing these up costs full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Short-Circuit Transfer and Spray Transfer in MIG welding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Short-Circuit Transfer | Spray Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Range** | 50 – 150 A (low) | 250 – 500 A (high) |
| **Voltage Range** | 14 – 20 V | 28 – 35 V |
| **Heat Input** | Low | High |
| **Shielding Gas** | CO₂ or Ar/CO₂ mixture | Argon-rich (≥ 80% Ar) |
| **Spatter Level** | Moderate | Negligible |
| **Weld Position** | All positions (including overhead) | Flat and horizontal only |
| **Material Thickness** | Thin sheets (≤ 3 mm) | Medium-thick plates (≥ 3 mm) |
| **Mechanism** | Wire periodically dips into pool, short-circuits, melts off, restrikes | Molten metal detaches axially as fine spray droplets |
| **Sound** | Characteristic "buzz" or "frying" sound | Smooth, steady hiss |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format Reminder:** In Part B, the examiner provides a Module-2 internal choice — students choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both options below are **14-mark questions** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, fully solved.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of MIG welding with a neat labelled block diagram. Discuss the role of shielding gas in the process.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

MIG welding works on the principle of establishing an electric arc between a **continuously fed consumable wire electrode** and the **workpiece**. The heat of the arc melts the wire tip and the base metal, forming a molten weld pool. The arc, weld pool, and solidifying metal are simultaneously shielded by an externally supplied **inert gas** that displaces atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen. As the welding gun is traversed along the joint, fresh molten metal fills the gap, fuses with the parent metal, and solidifies into a continuous weld bead.

**Role of the Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 of these notes.)*

The main subsystems are:
1. **Power Source (CV type):** Delivers constant voltage output; current self-adjusts based on arc length.
2. **Wire Feeder Unit:** Pushes the consumable wire from the spool through the liner to the gun at a controlled WFS.
3. **Welding Gun:** Houses the contact tip (current transfer to wire), trigger switch, and concentric nozzle for gas delivery.
4. **Shielding Gas System:** Cylinder → regulator → flowmeter → hose → nozzle → gas envelope over the weld.
5. **Earth Clamp and Workpiece:** Completes the electrical circuit.

**Role of Shielding Gas:**

- **Primary Function:** Prevents atmospheric oxygen (O₂), nitrogen (N₂), and moisture (H₂O) from contaminating the molten pool. Oxygen causes **oxide inclusions** and porosity; nitrogen causes **nitride embrittlement**; moisture causes **hydrogen-induced cracking**.
- **Secondary Functions:**
  - Stabilizes the arc column (especially in spray and pulsed modes).
  - Influences heat transfer characteristics — Argon has low thermal conductivity, producing a "finger-like" deep penetration; CO₂ has higher conductivity, producing a "stiffer" arc.
  - Affects bead shape — Ar-rich gases give a smooth, convex bead profile; CO₂ gives a flatter, wider bead.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a suitable sketch, describe the four modes of metal transfer in MIG welding. State the conditions favouring each mode.**

**Model Answer:**

*(For the sketch, the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4 can be referenced for transfer mode selection logic. A textual description follows.)*

**1. Short-Circuit (Dip) Transfer**
- **Mechanism:** The wire advances and **touches** the weld pool, creating a short circuit. The high short-circuit current melts the contact point, the molten bridge **pinches off**, the arc restrikes, and the cycle repeats at 20–200 times per second.
- **Conditions:** Low current (50–150 A), low voltage (14–20 V), small-diameter wire (0.8 mm), thin material.
- **Best For:** Out-of-position welding (vertical, overhead), thin sheets.

**2. Globular Transfer**
- **Mechanism:** Molten metal forms **large globules** (larger than the wire diameter) at the wire tip. These detach primarily by **gravity**, falling in an irregular trajectory.
- **Conditions:** Medium current (150–250 A), CO₂-rich gas, larger wire diameter.
- **Drawback:** Significant spatter; not used in modern production but historically important.

**3. Spray Transfer**
- **Mechanism:** Above a **critical current** (the transition current), the molten metal detaches as a **fine axial stream of tiny droplets**, projected across the arc by electromagnetic pinch forces.
- **Conditions:** High current (250–500 A), high voltage (28–35 V), **Argon-rich** shielding gas (≥ 80% Ar).
- **Best For:** High-productivity flat-position welding of medium-to-thick plates; gives the highest deposition rate.

**4. Pulsed-Spray Transfer**
- **Mechanism:** The current is pulsed between a **high peak** (above transition current — produces the droplet) and a **low background** (maintains the arc without droplet formation). **One droplet is produced per pulse.**
- **Conditions:** Peak current 200–300 A, background current 30–50 A, pulse frequency 50–500 Hz, Argon-rich gas.
- **Best For:** All-position welding with high-quality, spatter-free results; ideal for thin-to-medium materials and automation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often forget to mention the **critical/transition current** for spray transfer. Examiners specifically look for the understanding that **spray transfer requires a minimum threshold current**, and the gas composition must be **Argon-rich (≥ 80%)** — not CO₂. Omitting either point can cost 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for heat input in MIG welding. A MIG welding operation is performed with arc voltage 28 V, current 250 A, and travel speed 250 mm/min. The thermal efficiency is 0.85. Determine the heat input per unit length of weld.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**
*(Refer to the complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3 of these notes. The boxed final formula is:)*

$$
\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Arc voltage, $V = 28$ V
- Welding current, $I = 250$ A
- Travel speed, $S = 250$ mm/min
- Thermal efficiency, $\eta = 0.85$

**Substitution:**

$$
HI = \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{250 \times 1000}
$$

**Step-by-step Evaluation:**

- Numerator: $0.85 \times 28 = 23.8$; then $23.8 \times 250 = 5950$; then $5950 \times 60 = 357000$
- Denominator: $250 \times 1000 = 250000$

$$
HI = \dfrac{357000}{250000}
$$

$$
HI = 1.428 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{HI \approx 1.43 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A heat input of 1.43 kJ/mm is appropriate for medium-thickness (5–8 mm) steel plates using spray transfer. Excessive heat input above ~2.0 kJ/mm would risk **HAZ softening and distortion** in low-carbon steels.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**What are the main components of a MIG welding equipment setup? Explain the function of the constant voltage (CV) power source and the wire feed system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Main Components of a MIG Welding Setup:**

1. **Welding Power Source (CV Type):** 3-phase transformer-rectifier with a "flat" volt-ampere characteristic.
2. **Wire Feeder Unit:** Motor, drive rolls (usually 2-roll or 4-roll), wire spool holder, speed controller.
3. **Welding Gun (Torch):** Ergonomic handle with trigger, contact tip, gas nozzle (ceramic or copper), and cable connections.
4. **Shielding Gas Apparatus:** Cylinder, pressure regulator, flowmeter (typical flow 10–20 L/min), gas hose.
5. **Work Clamp and Earth Cable:** Completes the return current path.
6. **Cooling System (for high-current guns):** Water-cooled or air-cooled.

**Function of the CV Power Source:**

A **Constant Voltage** power source maintains a nearly flat voltage output regardless of current demand. In MIG welding, this provides **self-regulating arc length control**:
- If the operator moves the gun too far (arc lengthens), current drops → wire feeds at the same rate but burns off slower → arc shortens back to set length.
- If the gun moves closer (arc shortens), current surges → wire burns off faster → arc lengthens back to set value.

This self-regulation occurs at frequencies exceeding **1000 Hz** of natural micro-correction, giving MIG its characteristic arc stability and ease of use.

**Function of the Wire Feed System:**

The wire feeder delivers the consumable electrode from the spool to the arc at a **precisely controlled, constant linear speed** (typically 2–20 m/min). The drive rolls (knurled or smooth) grip the wire and push it through a flexible liner in the gun cable. Wire feed speed is the **master control variable** in CV MIG — increasing WFS raises the current, which in turn raises the deposition rate. Modern systems use **electronic feedback** to maintain WFS within ±1% accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Numerical:** When calculating heat input, students frequently make three common errors:
> 1. Forgetting the **60** (seconds-to-minutes conversion) factor.
> 2. Forgetting the **1000** (Joules-to-kilojoules conversion) factor.
> 3. Using the **wrong efficiency value** — MIG is typically 0.75–0.85, **not** 1.0.
> Any one of these errors results in an answer off by **16.67× or 1000×** — the full 7 marks are lost. **Always write down the units at each step.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / MIG Welding**

- **Definition Anchor:** MIG = **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** = continuous consumable solid wire + arc + **inert** shielding gas.
- **Three Pillars:** Arc + Wire Feed + Shielding Gas. Remove any one → process fails.
- **Power Source:** **Constant Voltage (CV)**, flat V–A characteristic; provides self-regulating arc length.
- **Primary Control:** **Wire Feed Speed (WFS)** sets current in CV MIG.
- **Shielding Gas for "Inert" MIG:** Argon (99.99% pure). For MAG (active), add CO₂ or O₂.
- **Four Transfer Modes:** Short-Circuit (thin sheet, all-position, low heat) → Globular (obsolete, spatter) → Spray (high productivity, flat position, ≥ 80% Ar) → Pulsed Spray (versatile, all-position, precise).
- **Heat Input Formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ in kJ/mm. The factors **60** and **1000** are **mandatory**.
- **Typical HI Range:** 0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm for carbon steel MIG.
- **Deposition Efficiency:** 90–98% for MIG (much higher than SMAW's 60–70%).
- **MIG vs. TIG:** MIG uses **consumable** wire; TIG uses **non-consumable tungsten** + separate filler rod.
- **MIG vs. MAG:** MIG → fully **inert** gas (Ar/He); MAG → **active** gas (CO₂, O₂ mixes).
- **Industry Use:** Automotive (most common), shipbuilding, structural fabrication, pressure vessels, HVAC.
- **Standard Wire Diameters:** 0.8 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.6 mm for steel; 1.0 mm, 1.2 mm for aluminium.
- **Typical Argon Flow Rate:** 10–20 L/min — too low gives porosity; too high wastes gas and creates turbulence.
- **Key CO Mapping:** This topic satisfies **CO2** of PBAUT404 — *Understand various material removal and joining processes*.
- **Common Defects to Recognize:** Porosity (insufficient gas), Lack of fusion (low HI), Burn-through (excess HI on thin sheets), Spatter (wrong gas mix or transfer mode).
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always define → list features → state the formula with units → substitute → box the numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Arc Welding; Thermit Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Welding; Thermit Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process in which a **constricted arc** is established between a tungsten (W) electrode and the workpiece (or the constricting nozzle). The constriction is achieved by forcing the plasma gas through a small orifice in a water-cooled copper nozzle, producing a high-velocity, high-temperature ionized gas jet (plasma) that performs the welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** PAW is classified as an advanced arc welding process. The term "plasma" here refers to the **4th state of matter** — a fully or partially ionized gas containing equal numbers of positive ions and free electrons, which is electrically conductive.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of PAW like a **high-pressure water gun vs. a regular garden hose**. A normal TIG arc spreads out (like a garden hose) and loses energy to the surroundings. PAW squeezes the arc through a small nozzle (like putting your thumb over a hose), concentrating all the heat into a thin, powerful jet. This "squeezed arc" is the *plasma jet* — so hot and focused that it can cut or weld thick metals with surgical precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plasma State in Welding:** For welding applications, the plasma is typically created from inert gases like **Argon (Ar)** or a mixture of **Argon + Hydrogen (Ar + 2–8% H₂)**. The temperature of the plasma jet can reach **20,000 °C to 30,000 °C**, far hotter than the surface of the sun (≈ 5,500 °C).

---

## 1.2 Thermit Welding (TW)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Thermit Welding (TW)** is a fusion welding process that uses the **exothermic (heat-releasing) chemical reaction** between a thermite mixture — typically **aluminum (Al) powder** and **iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)** — to produce molten steel and aluminum oxide slag. The molten superheated steel (≈ **2500 °C**) fills the prepared joint gap and fuses the two workpieces together upon solidification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Thermit welding is the only widely used welding process that does **NOT require an external power source or arc**. It is a self-sustaining chemical reaction, making it ideal for **field repairs and rail-road welding**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you want to join two railway tracks out in the middle of a forest where there is **no electricity, no gas cylinders, and no welding machine**. Thermit welding is your "fire-starter" solution: you mix two powders (aluminum + rust), light them, and they burn so violently that the resulting molten iron becomes a *ready-made molten filler metal* that pours into the gap and welds the rails together. It's like carrying your own miniature blast furnace in a crucible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Thermite Reaction:**
>
> $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat} \quad (\approx 850 \text{ kJ/mol})$$
>
> The heat released raises the temperature of the products to roughly **2500 °C**, well above the melting point of mild steel (≈ 1500 °C).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plasma Arc Welding — Working Principle

### Constriction of the Arc — The Key Concept
In a conventional **TIG (GTAW)** arc, the cross-section of the arc column is relatively large and the current density is moderate (≈ 100 A/mm²). In **PAW**, the arc is forced through a small orifice in the **water-cooled copper constricting nozzle**, which has two effects:

1. **Thermal Pinch Effect:** The cold nozzle walls cool the outer plasma, shrinking the conductive core.
2. **Magnetic Pinch Effect:** The high current density creates a self-induced magnetic field that compresses the arc radially (the **Z-pinch**).

Combined, these effects raise the **current density to 200–1000 A/mm²** and the **temperature to 20,000–30,000 °C**.

### Operating Modes of PAW

| Mode | Electrode Polarity | Arc Termination | Heat in Workpiece | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transferred Arc** | Electrode = (−), Workpiece = (+) | At workpiece | High (70–80%) | Melting, welding thick plates |
| **Non-Transferred Arc** | Electrode = (−), Nozzle = (+) | At nozzle | Lower (40%) | Surfacing, powder spraying |
| **Combined (Paired)** | Both arcs active | Both | Highest | High-speed cutting, keyhole welding |

### Process Parameters (PAW)
- **Plasma gas flow rate:** 0.5 – 10 L/min (Ar or Ar + H₂)
- **Shielding gas flow rate:** 10 – 30 L/min (Ar or Ar + H₂)
- **Arc current:** 0.5 A to several hundred amperes
- **Arc voltage:** 25 – 50 V (transferred); 80 – 200 V (non-transferred, with pilot arc)
- **Orifice diameter:** 0.5 – 4.0 mm
- **Travel speed:** 50 – 2000 mm/min

### Keyhole vs. Melt-in Mode
- **Melt-in (Conduction) Mode:** Lower current, shallow penetration, used for thin sheets (< 3 mm).
- **Keyhole Mode:** High current; the plasma jet **pierces through** the workpiece forming a vapor cavity (keyhole). Molten metal flows around the keyhole. Used for thicker sections (up to 12 mm in a single pass).

---

## 2.2 Thermit Welding — Working Principle

### Stages of the Thermit Welding Process
1. **Joint Preparation:** The two ends are aligned with a small gap (≈ 20–25 mm) and cleaned.
2. **Mold Assembly:** A refractory (heat-resistant) mold is built around the gap using sand + binder.
3. **Preheating:** The gap is preheated with a gas torch (≈ 1000 °C) to drive off moisture and pre-heat the parts.
4. **Crucible Placement:** A magnesia-lined crucible containing the thermite mixture is mounted above the mold.
5. **Ignition:** A magnesium ribbon ("fuse") ignites the thermite, triggering the exothermic reaction.
6. **Reaction & Pouring:** Within 30–60 s, the reaction yields molten steel (≈ 2500 °C) and Al₂O₃ slag. The slag (lighter) floats; the molten steel (heavier) flows down through a tap-hole into the mold cavity.
7. **Solidification:** The molten steel fills the gap and fuses with the parent metal as it solidifies.
8. **Finishing:** After cooling, the mold is broken off, and excess metal is ground flush with the rail head.

### Pressure Thermit Welding (PTW)
After pouring, a **hydraulic pressure** is applied to upset (forge) the joint. This:
- Expels slag inclusions
- Refines the grain structure
- Produces a stronger, denser weld — preferred for **high-speed railway tracks**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### PAW — Heat Input Formula

$$\boxed{\text{Heat Input } (H) = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Arc Voltage | Volt (V) |
| $I$ | Welding Current | Ampere (A) |
| $S$ | Travel Speed | mm/min |
| 60 | Conversion factor (sec to min) | — |
| 1000 | Conversion factor (J to kJ) | — |

### PAW — Energy Distribution (typical, transferred arc mode)

| Energy Sink | Percentage |
|---|---|
| Workpiece (useful) | **70 – 80 %** |
| Electrode + nozzle cooling water | 15 – 20 % |
| Shielding gas & convection losses | 5 – 10 % |

### Thermit Welding — Reaction Enthalpy

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{reaction}} \approx -850 \text{ kJ/mol of Fe}_2\text{O}_3}}$$

### Thermit Welding — Theoretical Temperature Calculation (Approximate)

$$T_{\text{max}} = \frac{\Delta H}{C_p \times m_{\text{products}}}$$

For a stoichiometric mix (1 mol Fe₂O₃ + 2 mol Al → 2 mol Fe + 1 mol Al₂O₃):

$$T_{\text{max}} \approx \frac{850{,}000 \;\text{J}}{50 \times 60} \;\approx 2500 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:**
> - **PAW** is used in **aerospace (jet engine vanes, rocket casings)**, nuclear reactors, **stainless steel tubes (orbital welding)**, and titanium implants in medical devices.
> - **Thermit Welding** is used for **welding railway rails in situ**, repairing **large steel shafts** (ship propeller shafts, rolling mill spindles), and joining **thick steel sections** in remote locations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Heat Input in Plasma Arc Welding

### Problem Statement
In a **transferred-arc Plasma Arc Welding** operation, the arc voltage is **32 V**, the welding current is **220 A**, and the travel speed is **180 mm/min**. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** delivered to the workpiece.

### Given Data
- Arc voltage, $V = 32 \;\text{V}$
- Welding current, $I = 220 \;\text{A}$
- Travel speed, $S = 180 \;\text{mm/min}$

### Step 1 — Recall the Heat Input Formula

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}$$

The factor **60** converts seconds to minutes (because current × voltage gives **watts = J/s**, multiplied by 60 gives **J/min**). The factor **1000** converts joules to kilojoules.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values into the Formula

$$H = \frac{32 \times 220 \times 60}{180 \times 1000}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator

$$32 \times 220 = 7040 \;\text{V·A} = 7040 \;\text{W}$$

$$7040 \times 60 = 422{,}400 \;\text{J/min}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$180 \times 1000 = 180{,}000$$

### Step 5 — Final Division

$$H = \frac{422{,}400}{180{,}000} = 2.347 \;\text{kJ/mm}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

$$\boxed{H \approx 2.35 \;\text{kJ/mm}}$$

This is a typical heat input for **keyhole-mode PAW on stainless steel (4 mm thick)**. Heat inputs above ~3 kJ/mm may cause grain coarsening in austenitic stainless steel, so this value is in the acceptable range.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Thermite Reaction Mass Calculation

### Problem Statement
A thermit welding operation on a railway track requires **0.5 kg of molten steel** to fill the joint cavity. Assuming the thermite mixture is **pure Fe₂O₃ + 2Al** in stoichiometric ratio, calculate the **mass of Fe₂O₃ and Al** required.

### Given Data
- Required mass of molten Fe produced, $m_{\text{Fe}} = 0.5 \;\text{kg} = 500 \;\text{g}$
- Reaction: $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$

### Step 1 — Molar Masses

| Species | Molar Mass (g/mol) |
|---|---|
| Fe | 56 |
| Fe₂O₃ | $2(56) + 3(16) = 160$ |
| Al | 27 |
| Al₂O₃ | $2(27) + 3(16) = 102$ |

### Step 2 — Moles of Fe Required

$$n_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{m_{\text{Fe}}}{M_{\text{Fe}}} = \frac{500}{56} = 8.93 \;\text{mol}$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric Mapping

From the balanced equation: **2 mol Fe** are produced by **1 mol Fe₂O₃** and **2 mol Al**.

So, for 8.93 mol of Fe:

$$n_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = \frac{8.93}{2} = 4.464 \;\text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{Al}} = \frac{8.93}{2} \times 2 = 8.93 \;\text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Moles to Masses

$$m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 4.464 \times 160 = 714.3 \;\text{g} \approx \boxed{0.714 \;\text{kg}}$$

$$m_{\text{Al}} = 8.93 \times 27 = 241.0 \;\text{g} \approx \boxed{0.241 \;\text{kg}}$$

### Step 5 — Total Thermite Mass

$$m_{\text{total}} = 0.714 + 0.241 = 0.955 \;\text{kg} \approx 1 \;\text{kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a **20–30% excess thermite** is used to compensate for heat losses to the surroundings and to ensure complete cavity filling. So the actual mix used would be ≈ **1.2 kg of thermite** for 0.5 kg of weld metal.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Comparison: PAW vs Thermit Heat Concentration

### Problem Statement
Justify, using the concept of **volumetric heat concentration**, why PAW can weld **thinner sheets** while thermit welding is used only for **massive sections**.

### Step 1 — Define Volumetric Heat Concentration

$$\rho_H = \frac{Q}{V_{\text{HAZ}}}$$

where $Q$ is the total heat supplied and $V_{\text{HAZ}}$ is the volume of the heat-affected zone.

### Step 2 — Compare the Two Processes

| Parameter | PAW | Thermit Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source area | 1 – 4 mm² (orifice) | Entire crucible & mold (≈ 50,000 mm²) |
| Power density | $\sim 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$ | $\sim 10^2 \text{ W/mm}^2$ |
| HAZ width | 1 – 3 mm | 25 – 50 mm |
| Suitable thickness | 0.1 – 12 mm | > 50 mm (rails) |

### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation
The extremely high power density of PAW means heat is deposited in a **very small volume** → rapid melting with **minimal HAZ** → suitable for precision/thin work. Thermit welding releases heat over a **large crucible volume** → slower, broader heating → unsuitable for thin sheets (would melt entirely) but excellent for **bulk repairs**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAW Torch — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Constant Current DC Power Source] --> CTRL[Control Console and High Frequency Unit]
    CTRL --> TORCH[PAW Torch Assembly]
    subgraph T1[Torch Internal Architecture]
        direction TB
        ELE[Tungsten Electrode Cathode] --> SP[Spacer and Insulator]
        SP --> ORIFICE[Constricting Copper Orifice]
        ORIFICE --> ANOD[Workpiece Anode or Nozzle Anode]
        WC[Water Cooling Jacket] -.-> ORIFICE
    end
    PG[Plasma Gas Cylinder Ar or Ar and H2] --> TORCH
    SG[Shielding Gas Cylinder Ar] --> TORCH
    WP[Workpiece Table with Clamp] --> ANOD
    PGAS[Plasma Jet Column High Temp] --> MELT[Keyhole Cavity in Workpiece]
    MELT --> POOL[Molten Weld Pool]
    POOL --> SLD[Solidified Weld Bead]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **plasma gas** is introduced in the torch and ionized by a **pilot arc** between the electrode and the constricting nozzle. Once ionized, this plasma jet is forced through the **orifice**. In *transferred-arc* mode, the main arc jumps from the electrode to the **workpiece** (anode path), creating the keyhole.

## 4.2 Thermit Welding — Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Rail Ends Aligned with 20 to 25 mm Gap] --> B[Surface Cleaning and Oxide Removal]
    B --> C[Refractory Sand Mold Assembly around Gap]
    C --> D[Preheating with Gas Torch 1000 C for 5 to 10 min]
    D --> E[Thermite Mixture Fe2O3 and Al Loaded in Magnesia Crucible]
    E --> F[Ignition with Magnesium Ribbon Fuse]
    F --> G[Exothermic Reaction 30 to 60 sec]
    G --> H[Slag Al2O3 floats to top]
    H --> I[Liquid Steel T 2500 C pours into Mold]
    I --> J[Solidification and Cooling 15 to 20 min]
    J --> K{Process Type}
    K -->|Standard TW| L[Cool and Break Mold]
    K -->|Pressure TW| P[Apply Hydraulic Upset Pressure]
    P --> L
    L --> M[Grind and Profile Weld]
    M --> N[Non destructive Testing UT or MPI]
```

## 4.3 PAW Process — Functional Block Diagram (Pilot Arc + Main Arc)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X1[Pilot Arc Circuit]
        EO[Electrode] --- NA[Nozzle Anode]
    end
    subgraph X2[Main Welding Circuit Transferred]
        EO --- WA[Workpiece Anode]
    end
    GAS[Plasma Gas Supply] --> NOZ[Constricting Nozzle]
    NOZ -->|Ionized Jet| WP[Workpiece]
    NOZ --> PILOT[Pilot Arc Path]
    EO --> PILOT
    PILOT -.->|after ionization| WA
    SHIELD[Shielding Gas] --> WP
```

## 4.4 Comparative Merits Flowchart (PAW vs. TIG vs. Thermit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Welding Process Selection} --> Q1[Precision thin section aerospace tubes]
    Q1 --> R1[Plasma Arc Welding]
    Q --> Q2[Field heavy section rail repair]
    Q2 --> R2[Thermit Welding]
    Q --> Q3[General purpose arc welding]
    Q3 --> R3[TIG or MMA]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Plasma Arc Welding. List any two advantages of PAW over TIG welding. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is an arc welding process that uses a **constricted arc** between a non-consumable tungsten electrode and the workpiece. The arc is constricted by a water-cooled copper nozzle, which compresses the plasma gas to form a high-velocity, high-temperature jet (≈ 20,000 °C).

**Two advantages over TIG:**

1. **Higher energy concentration:** The constricted arc provides a higher current density (200–1000 A/mm² vs. 100 A/mm² in TIG), enabling deeper penetration and faster travel speeds.
2. **Better arc stability:** The constricting nozzle makes the arc less sensitive to variations in torch-to-workpiece distance ("standoff"), resulting in more uniform welds — especially beneficial for **orbital tube welding**.

---

### Question 2: State the principle of thermit welding and write the chemical reaction involved. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Thermit welding is a fusion welding process that uses the **exothermic reaction** between a mixture of aluminum powder and iron oxide (thermite) to produce **molten steel and aluminum oxide slag** at about 2500 °C. The molten steel fills the joint gap and fuses the workpieces upon cooling.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{Al} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Heat} \; (\approx 850 \;\text{kJ/mol})$$

The reaction is **highly exothermic** and self-sustaining once ignited by a magnesium ribbon fuse. No external power source is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either (A)** or **(B)**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Plasma Arc Welding **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Plasma Arc Welding** with a neat sketch. Differentiate between **transferred arc** and **non-transferred arc** modes of PAW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of PAW:**
