Substituting $P_f$ from heat balance and solving yields $k \approx 1.5$ – $2.0$ for most steels. This is a **design check** students should perform in KTU numericals.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on Weld Quality

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on Heat Input | Effect on Flash | Effect on Bond Quality | Defect Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rotational Speed (N) | Strong ↑ | Increases | Improves (initially) | Excessive melting & HAZ if too high |
| Friction Pressure (P_f) | ↑ | Increases | Improves | Incomplete bonding if too low |
| Forge Pressure (P_u) | Marginal | Increases | Strongly improves | Cracks if too high (over-deformation) |
| Burn-off Length (L_b) | ↑ (proportional) | Increases | Improves | Wastage if excessive |
| Friction Time | ↑ | Increases | Improves up to optimum | Overheating / grain growth if too long |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU Numerical Pattern:** *"Calculate the heat generated at the interface when two steel rods of diameter 30 mm are joined by friction welding at 1500 rpm under an axial force of 25 kN. Take μ = 0.3."* (Solution: $Q = \pi \mu F N D / 30 = \pi \times 0.3 \times 25000 \times 1500 \times 0.030 / 30 \approx 35,343$ W = 35.3 kW).

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Stages of Friction Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Initial Friction Phase] --> B[Stage 2: Intermediate Equilibrium Phase]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Forging Upset Phase]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Cool Down and Solid State Bond]
    A --> A1[Rotating workpiece brought into contact at low axial pressure Pf]
    A1 --> A2[Asperity deformation generates frictional heat]
    A2 --> A3[Interface temperature rises to 0.6 to 0.9 Tm]
    B --> B1[Steady state heat generation and conduction]
    B1 --> B2[Plasticized layer forms at interface]
    B2 --> B3[Flash begins to extrude radially outward]
    C --> C1[Rotation stopped or rapidly decelerated]
    C1 --> C2[Axial pressure raised to forge pressure Pu]
    C2 --> C3[Soft material expelled as flash]
    C3 --> C4[Virgin metal surfaces brought into atomic contact]
    D --> D1[Diffusion and grain boundary migration]
    D1 --> D2[Metallurgical bond formed in solid state]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous-Drive Friction Welding Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain["Power Train Module"]
        M[AC or DC Motor] --> C[Coupling]
        C --> CH[Chuck and Spindle Assembly]
        CH --> WP1[Rotating Workpiece]
    end
    subgraph AxialClamp["Axial Clamping and Forging Module"]
        H[Hydraulic Cylinder] --> RAM[Plunger Ram]
        RAM --> WP2[Stationary Workpiece]
    end
    subgraph ControlUnit["PLC and Sensor Module"]
        N[RPM Sensor] --> PLC[PLC Controller]
        F[Load Cell] --> PLC
        D[Displacement Encoder] --> PLC
        T[Pyrometer or Thermocouple] --> PLC
    end
    subgraph FlashMgmt["Flash and Coolant Module"]
        WP1 --> FLASH[Flash Ring]
        WP2 --> FLASH
        FLASH --> CT[Coolant Spray Ring]
    end
    PLC --> M
    PLC --> H
```

## 4.3 Defect Causal Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Friction Weld Defects]
    D --> D1[Incomplete Bond or Lack of Penetration]
    D --> D2[Flash Asymmetry and Off Centre Defect]
    D --> D3[Cracking in HAZ or Weld Line]
    D --> D4[Porosity and Voids at Interface]
    D --> D5[Oxide and Contaminant Inclusions]
    D --> D6[Excessive Flash and Wastage]
    D --> D7[Undermatching Joint Strength]
    D1 --> C1[Cause: Insufficient burn off and low forge pressure]
    D2 --> C2[Cause: Misalignment of spindles and uneven pressure]
    D3 --> C3[Cause: High forge pressure on brittle HAZ]
    D4 --> C4[Cause: Gas entrapment due to low upset time]
    D5 --> C5[Cause: Contaminated faying surfaces]
    D6 --> C6[Cause: Excessive burn off and friction time]
    D7 --> C7[Cause: Improper material selection or dissimilar metallurgy]
```

## 4.4 Typical Time–Pressure–Speed Profile (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph T1[Phase 1 Friction Heating]
        T1A[RPM at rated value] --> T1B[Friction Pressure Pf]
    end
    subgraph T2[Phase 2 Upset Forging]
        T2A[RPM ramps down to zero] --> T2B[Forge Pressure Pu greater than Pf]
    end
    subgraph T3[Phase 3 Hold and Cool]
        T3A[Pressure held at Pu] --> T3B[Natural or forced air cool]
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
```

> **Mermaid Safety:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `D1`, `T1A`, `subgraph`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic/HTML inside node labels.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**Define friction welding. Why is it classified as a solid-state welding process?**

**Model Answer:**

Friction welding is a solid-state welding process in which two workpieces are joined by the heat generated from **mechanical friction** at their rubbing interface, combined with **axial pressure**. One component is held stationary while the other is rotated (or translated) against it. The frictional heat raises the interface temperature to **0.6 – 0.9 T_m** (where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin), plasticizing the surface layer. Application of forge pressure then extrudes the plasticized material as flash and brings the underlying virgin metal into atomic contact, forming a **metallurgical bond without any melting** of the base metals. Since the process never produces a liquid phase, it is classified as a **solid-state welding process**, distinguishing it from fusion processes such as arc welding or gas welding.

> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Definition — 1.5 M; Justification (no melting → solid-state) — 1.5 M.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Retest]
**List any four process parameters of friction welding and state the typical range of forge pressure relative to friction pressure for steels.**

**Model Answer:**

The four principal process parameters of friction welding are:

1. **Rotational Speed (N):** typically 1000 – 4000 rpm for steels.
2. **Friction Pressure (P_f):** typically 30 – 100 MPa for steels.
3. **Forge Pressure (P_u):** typically **1.5 to 2.0 times** the friction pressure.
4. **Burn-off Length (ΔL):** typically 1 – 6 mm depending on the material.

> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Naming four parameters — 2 M (0.5 M each); Forge-to-friction ratio — 1 M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> (a) **Describe with a neat sketch the working principle of Continuous-Drive Rotary Friction Welding. Explain the three stages of the process in detail.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **A steel shaft of diameter 50 mm is joined to another steel shaft by rotary friction welding at 2000 rpm under an axial pressure of 50 MPa. Take the coefficient of friction μ = 0.35. Calculate the heat generation rate at the interface.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Continuous-Drive Rotary Friction Welding — Working Principle:**

In this process, one workpiece is mounted in a motor-driven **rotating chuck**, and the other is clamped in a **stationary tailstock** that can move axially. The rotating workpiece is brought into contact with the stationary one under a low **friction pressure (P_f)**. The motor continuously drives the rotation throughout the friction phase, and the axial feed is controlled by a hydraulic cylinder. Once the prescribed burn-off length is reached, the motor is disengaged, the rotation is brought to a halt (typically by a brake), and a higher **forge pressure (P_u)** is applied to consolidate the joint.

**The Three Stages:**

**Stage 1 — Friction Heating Phase:** The rotating and stationary parts contact under friction pressure. The asperities on the rubbing surfaces deform plastically, and frictional heat raises the interface temperature rapidly to **0.6 – 0.9 T_m**. The coefficient of friction drops as the surface layer softens. The duration of this phase is typically **1 – 10 seconds** for small-to-medium cross-sections.

**Stage 2 — Intermediate Phase:** A quasi-steady state is reached where heat generation is balanced by heat conduction into the bulk of the workpieces and by flash extrusion. A thin plasticized layer (≈ 10 – 100 μm thick) is maintained at the interface, and material is continuously expelled as flash.

**Stage 3 — Forging (Upset) Phase:** The rotation is stopped (or rapidly decelerated), and the axial pressure is increased to the forge pressure. The plasticized material is squeezed out as flash, exposing fresh, oxide-free, plastic metal that is pressed into intimate contact. A **solid-state metallurgical bond** forms by diffusion, grain boundary migration, and recrystallization.

> **Marks Split (7 M):** Working principle + sketch — 3 M; Stage 1 — 1.5 M; Stage 2 + Stage 3 — 2.5 M.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Rotational speed $N = 2000 \text{ rpm}$
- Axial pressure $P = 50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^{2}$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.35$

**Step 1 — Calculate the axial force (F):**

Cross-sectional area of the joint:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Axial force:

$$
F = P \cdot A = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 98175 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat generation formula:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \mu F N D}{30}
$$

Substituting all values:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 0.35 \times 98175 \times 2000 \times 0.050}{30}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 0.35 \times 98175 \times 100}{30}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 3436125}{30}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{10796638.6}{30}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 359888 \text{ W} \approx 360 \text{ kW}}
$$

> **Marks Split (7 M):** Formula selection — 1 M; Area calculation — 1 M; Force calculation — 1 M; Substitution and final numerical answer — 3 M; Units — 1 M.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> (a) **Explain the various types of defects encountered in friction welding. Discuss their causes and remedies.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **Compare Continuous-Drive Friction Welding (CDFW) and Inertia Friction Welding (IFW) in terms of energy source, parameter control, cycle time, and typical applications.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Major Defects in Friction Welding:**

**1. Incomplete Bonding / Lack of Penetration:**
- *Cause:* Insufficient burn-off length, low friction pressure, premature braking, or insufficient heating time.
- *Remedy:* Increase burn-off length, raise friction pressure, and ensure proper forge pressure application. Pre-clean the faying surfaces to remove scale and oil.

**2. Flash Asymmetry / Off-Centre Defect:**
- *Cause:* Misalignment of spindle axes, uneven chuck clamping, or asymmetric pressure distribution.
- *Remedy:* Use precision chucks, align the spindle and tailstock to within ≤ 0.05 mm, and verify the run-out.

**3. Cracks in HAZ or Weld Line:**
- *Cause:* Excessive forge pressure on a brittle or quench-hardenable material, leading to crack initiation at grain boundaries.
- *Remedy:* Reduce forge pressure, use a post-weld tempering or normalizing heat treatment, and select materials with lower carbon equivalent.

**4. Porosity and Voids at the Interface:**
- *Cause:* Insufficient upset (forging) time, low forge pressure, or gas entrapment in the interface.
- *Remedy:* Increase forge pressure and upset time; use de-greased surfaces and a proper shielding atmosphere if available.

**5. Oxide and Contaminant Inclusions:**
- *Cause:* Dirty or oxidized faying surfaces; inadequate flash expulsion.
- *Remedy:* Pre-machine the faying surfaces and use a higher burn-off length to ensure complete expulsion of contaminated material.

**6. Excessive Flash and Wastage:**
- *Cause:* Excessive friction time or burn-off length.
- *Remedy:* Optimize the friction time and burn-off length; in mass production, use a position-controlled stop rather than a time-controlled cycle.

**7. Undermatched Joint Strength (Joint weaker than parent metal):**
- *Cause:* Improper dissimilar metal pairing, formation of brittle intermetallics at the interface, or insufficient forging.
- *Remedy:* Use a compatible material pairing or insert an appropriate interlayer; perform a post-weld heat treatment.

> **Marks Split (7 M):** Naming 6 – 7 defects with cause — 4 M (≈ 0.5 M each); Remedies for at least 4 defects — 3 M.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table: CDFW vs IFW**

| Feature | Continuous-Drive FW (CDFW) | Inertia (Flywheel) FW (IFW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Continuous electrical power from running motor | Pre-stored kinetic energy of rotating flywheel |
| **Speed Control** | Maintained constant by motor control throughout friction phase | Decelerates from initial ω₁ to ω₂ during the cycle |
| **Pressure Profile** | Two distinct levels: P_f then P_u | Single pressure; forge occurs as flywheel decelerates |
| **Cycle Time** | Longer (5 – 30 s typical) | Shorter (0.5 – 5 s typical) |
| **Process Control Parameters** | Time, RPM, Pressure, Burn-off | Energy (set by N₁ and I), Pressure, Deceleration rate |
| **Equipment Cost** | Lower (standard motor + hydraulic) | Higher (precision flywheel + braking system) |
| **Weld Quality Consistency** | Good for large cross-sections | Excellent for short, repeatable cycles (e.g., valve stems) |
| **Typical Applications** | Drive shafts, hydraulic rods, marine propeller shafts | Automotive axles, valve stems, small engine components |
| **Heat Input Uniformity** | Depends on operator/programmer skill | Self-regulating: cycle ends as energy is consumed |

**Engineering Interpretation:**

CDFW is preferred when **long friction times** are needed (e.g., large-diameter marine shafts where thermal mass is high). IFW is preferred in **high-volume mass production** of small-to-medium parts (e.g., valve stems in automotive plants), where the cycle time is short and the flywheel energy is precisely metered. The energy in IFW must satisfy the heat balance:

$$
E_{\text{flywheel}} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} \geq Q_{\text{req}} + W_{\text{upset}} + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

> **Marks Split (7 M):** Comparison table — 4 M (0.5 M × 8 features); Engineering interpretation + IFW energy equation — 3 M.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in heat generation formula** — students often leave N in rpm without converting or use D in cm instead of m. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $P_f$ (friction pressure) with $P_u$ (forge pressure)** — these are two different pressure stages; $P_u > P_f$ is a must for a sound joint.
> 3. **Skipping the calculation of cross-sectional area** in numericals — students often directly plug diameter into a formula requiring area, losing 1 – 2 marks.
> 4. **Stating "no defects" for friction welding** — examiners expect at least 4 – 5 defects with cause and remedy. Awarding zero credit for defect-related questions if only 1 – 2 are listed.
> 5. **Drawing a poor sketch** — even a simple labelled block diagram of the friction welding setup (rotating chuck + stationary tailstock + hydraulic cylinder) gets 1 – 2 marks and improves overall impression.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Friction welding is a SOLID-STATE process** — no melting of base metals occurs. Keep this distinction crisp.
- [x] **Four key process parameters:** Rotational Speed (N), Friction Pressure (P_f), Forge Pressure (P_u), and Burn-off Length (ΔL).
- [x] **Forge pressure is 1.5 – 2.0 times** the friction pressure.
- [x] **Interface temperature** reaches **0.6 – 0.9 T_m** (Kelvin), not T_m itself.
- [x] **Standard heat generation equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \mu F N D}{30}$ (with D in metres, F in Newtons, N in rpm → Q in Watts).
- [x] **Three stages:** Friction Heating → Intermediate (Equilibrium) → Forging (Upset).
- [x] **IFW energy equation:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$; ensure E is sufficient for heat, upset, and losses.
- [x] **Major defects:** Incomplete bonding, off-centre / flash asymmetry, HAZ cracks, porosity, oxide inclusions, excessive flash, under-matched joints.
- [x] **Remedies:** Proper parameter optimization, surface cleaning, spindle alignment, post-weld heat treatment, material compatibility check.
- [x] **CDFW vs IFW** comparison is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU exams — memorize the table.
- [x] **Applications:** Drive shafts, valve stems, bimetallic joints, turbine discs (LFW), aerospace blisks.
- [x] **HAZ is extremely narrow** (2 – 5 mm) because heat is generated internally at the interface and conducted away rapidly.
- [x] **Bloom's Level:** Aim for **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** when answering numericals; **Understand (L2)** for definition/classification questions.
- [x] **CO Mapping:** This topic contributes directly to **CO2** of the PBAUT404 syllabus.

> **One-line KTU Board-Examiner Summary:** *"Friction welding joins metals in the solid state by converting mechanical friction work into interfacial heat, followed by forge-pressure consolidation; success depends on correctly chosen rotational speed, friction pressure, forge pressure, and burn-off length, and defects are mainly controlled by parameter optimization and surface preparation."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 2 — Welding Parameters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Welding Parameters** are the controllable, measurable input variables that govern the formation, stability, geometry, and metallurgical quality of a weld bead. They form the backbone of *Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)* and directly determine bead penetration, width, reinforcement, heat-affected zone (HAZ) size, distortion, and mechanical strength of the joint.

The principal welding parameters covered in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for arc welding processes (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW) are:

1. **Welding Current (I)** — measured in Amperes (A)
2. **Arc Voltage (V)** — measured in Volts (V)
3. **Welding / Travel Speed (S)** — measured in mm/min or m/min
4. **Electrode Diameter (d)** — measured in mm
5. **Electrode Extension / Stick-out (L)** — measured in mm
6. **Arc Length (g)** — measured in mm
7. **Polarity** — DCEP, DCEN, or AC
8. **Heat Input (H)** — derived parameter, kJ/mm
9. **Duty Cycle (%)** — for the power source
10. **Preheating & Interpass Temperature** — for thick-plate and alloy steels

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose" of Welding

Imagine a garden hose with a nozzle:
- **Current (Amps)** = how much *water* (electrons / heat) you push through the hose. More current = deeper melting, like a wider, hotter stream.
- **Voltage (Volts)** = the *pressure* at the nozzle. More voltage = a wider, more spread-out spray (wider bead).
- **Travel Speed** = how fast you sweep the hose across the lawn. Sweep slowly → the lawn is flooded (deep penetration, wide bead). Sweep quickly → barely wet (shallow, narrow bead).
- **Electrode Diameter** = the *diameter* of the hose itself. Bigger hose → can carry more water without bursting.
- **Heat Input** = the *total water* deposited per centimetre of lawn — combining flow, pressure, and sweep speed.

Just as a gardener balances these to water evenly, a welder balances these parameters to lay a sound, defect-free weld.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Students are expected to identify each parameter, state its units, explain its effect on weld bead geometry (penetration, bead width, reinforcement), and compute the **Heat Input**, which is the single most exam-valuable derived parameter.

### Classification of Welding Parameters

| Category | Parameters | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Machine-Set) Parameters** | Current, Voltage, Travel Speed | Directly set by the operator/machine |
| **Secondary (Consumable) Parameters** | Electrode diameter, Polarity, Electrode extension | Set via consumable choice & connections |
| **Derived Parameter** | Heat Input (H) | Calculated from primary ones |
| **Thermal Management Parameters** | Preheating, Interpass temperature | Set via procedure for metallurgical control |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Welding Current (I, in Amperes)

- **What it does:** Current is the rate of flow of electricity across the arc gap. It is the *primary* energy source.
- **Effect on weld:**
  - **Penetration** ↑ significantly with current (current is the most influential parameter on depth of fusion).
  - **Bead width** increases slightly.
  - **Reinforcement** increases.
  - **Spatter** and **undercut** risk increase if current is too high.
  - **Insufficient current** → lack of fusion, porosity, irregular bead.

- **Current Density (CD)** = `I / A` where `A` = cross-sectional area of the electrode core.
- **Typical current ranges:**
  - SMAW: 60–250 A (depends on electrode size)
  - GMAW: 50–600 A
  - GTAW: 5–500 A
  - SAW: 200–2000 A

- **Rule of thumb for SMAW current selection (very high-yield for KTU):**

$$
I \approx k \cdot d
$$

where `d` is electrode diameter in mm and `k` is a constant between **35 and 45 A/mm** (typical value: **40 A/mm**).

> Example: For a 3.15 mm electrode → I ≈ 40 × 3.15 ≈ **126 A**.

### 2.2 Arc Voltage (V, in Volts)

- **What it does:** Voltage is the potential difference that sustains the arc across the gap. It determines *arc length* (approximately: 1 mm arc length ≈ 2 V for most arcs).
- **Effect on weld:**
  - **Bead width** ↑ strongly with voltage.
  - **Penetration** decreases slightly (heat spreads, doesn't dig).
  - **Reinforcement** decreases (flatter, wider bead).
  - **Excess voltage** → porosity, spatter, undercut, and in GTAW, tungsten inclusion risk.

- **Typical arc voltages (KTU-favourite numbers):**
  - SMAW: 18–28 V
  - GMAW: 18–32 V
  - GTAW: 10–25 V
  - SAW: 28–44 V

### 2.3 Travel / Welding Speed (S, in mm/min or m/min)

- **What it does:** The rate at which the arc moves along the joint.
- **Effect on weld:**
  - **Slow speed** → more heat per unit length → deeper penetration, wider bead, larger HAZ, more distortion.
  - **Fast speed** → less heat per unit length → shallow penetration, narrow bead, possible lack of fusion, "stacked" appearance.
  - **Reinforcement** is maximum at moderate speed.

- **Typical speeds:**
  - SMAW: 100–300 mm/min
  - GMAW: 300–1000 mm/min
  - SAW: 400–2000 mm/min

### 2.4 Electrode Diameter (d, in mm)

- **What it does:** The cross-section of the electrode determines the maximum safe current it can carry and the current density at the arc.
- **Effect:**
  - Larger diameter → can carry higher current → higher deposition rate.
  - Thinner electrode → finer control, used for thin plates and root passes.

- **Standard SMAW sizes:** 2.0, 2.5, 3.15, 4.0, 5.0, 6.3 mm.

### 2.5 Polarity

| Polarity | Connection | Electron Flow | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** | Electrode = −, Work = + | Workpiece → Electrode (~2/3 heat at workpiece) | SMAW (most), GMAW spray transfer |
| **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** | Electrode = + , Work = − | Electrode → Workpiece (~2/3 heat at electrode) | GTAW (for aluminum, thin sheets) |
| **AC (Alternating Current)** | Polarity alternates 50/60 Hz | Balanced | GTAW for aluminum, magnetized parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DCEP = "Reverse Polarity"** → ~67% of arc heat goes to the *workpiece* (high penetration). **DCEN = "Straight Polarity"** → ~67% of arc heat goes to the *electrode* (high melting rate of electrode, low penetration).

### 2.6 Electrode Extension / Stick-out (L, in mm)

- The length of electrode protruding beyond the holder (SMAW) or the contact tip (GMAW).
- **Effect:** Preheating of the electrode occurs due to I²R heating. Increasing stick-out:
  - Increases deposition rate.
  - Decreases current at the arc (resistance heating consumes voltage).
  - Excessive stick-out → burn-back, porosity.

### 2.7 Heat Input (H) — THE Most Important Derived Parameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Input (H)** is the *amount of heat energy delivered per unit length of weld*. It is the single most critical parameter in determining HAZ size, microstructure, distortion, and mechanical properties of the weldment.

**General formula (SI, kJ/mm):**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000}
$$

where:
- `H` = Heat input in **kJ/mm**
- `V` = Arc voltage in **Volts**
- `I` = Welding current in **Amperes**
- `S` = Travel speed in **mm/min**
- `η` = **Arc efficiency / Process thermal efficiency** (dimensionless)

**Equivalent form (when S in m/min):**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]},\ S \text{ in m/min}
$$

**Arc Efficiency (η) — must be memorised for KTU:**

| Process | η |
|---|---|
| **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** | **1.00** (1.0) |
| **GMAW / MIG** | **0.80 – 0.85** |
| **FCAW** | **0.80** |
| **SMAW / Stick** | **0.70 – 0.80** (often taken as 0.75) |
| **GTAW / TIG** | **0.50 – 0.60** |
| **PAW (Plasma Arc Welding)** | **0.45 – 0.50** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** Students often forget to multiply by **η** or convert units. Always show the four-step check: (1) Units of V and I, (2) Unit of S, (3) Insert η, (4) Convert J → kJ.

### 2.8 Duty Cycle

- The percentage of a 10-minute period the welding power source can deliver its rated current without overheating.
- Example: A machine rated at 300 A at 60% duty cycle can deliver 300 A for 6 minutes and must rest for 4 minutes in every 10-minute window.
- At lower currents, the duty cycle can be increased (or, conversely, the rated current is higher).

### 2.9 Preheating & Interpass Temperature

- **Preheating (T_p):** Heating the base metal to a specified temperature *before* welding. Reduces cooling rate, prevents hydrogen cracking in hardenable steels.
- **Interpass temperature (T_i):** Maximum temperature the previously deposited bead is allowed to reach before the next pass.
- Both are controlled using temperature sticks (Tempilstiks) or pyrometers.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a *high-yield, exam-ready* summary. **Memorise it.** No `|` (vertical bar) symbols are used to preserve table formatting.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000}$ | Heat input (S in mm/min) | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \times 1000}$ | Heat input (S in m/min) | kJ/mm |
| 3 | $I \approx 40 \cdot d$ | SMAW current selection (rule of thumb) | A |
| 4 | $CD = \dfrac{I}{A_{electrode}}$ | Current density | A/mm² |
| 5 | $A_{electrode} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | Cross-section of electrode | mm² |
| 6 | $R_{dep} = \dfrac{W_{deposited}}{t}$ | Deposition rate (mass per time) | g/min or kg/h |
| 7 | $D_{rate} = \dfrac{\text{length of weld}}{\text{time}}$ | Travel speed S | mm/min |
| 8 | $E_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | Watts (J/s) |
| 9 | $E_{total} = E_{arc} \cdot t$ | Total arc energy | Joules |
| 10 | $\eta_{arc} = \dfrac{\text{heat into workpiece}}{\text{total arc energy}}$ | Arc efficiency | dimensionless |

### 🏭 Real-World Utility

- **Shipbuilding & Pressure Vessels:** Heat input must be limited to control HAZ toughness (typical cap: 2.0 kJ/mm for high-strength steels).
- **Pipeline Welding (API 1104):** Strict heat-input windows (e.g., 0.5–1.5 kJ/mm) to ensure adequate notch toughness.
- **Aerospace (GTAW of titanium):** Extremely low heat input to retain corrosion resistance.
- **Bridge Construction (SAW):** High heat input with multi-arc systems for productivity, but HAZ toughness monitored.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heat Input Formula

**Starting principle (conservation of energy):**
The arc delivers electrical power `P = V · I` (Watts = J/s).

**Step 1 — Total heat delivered over time t:**

$$
E_{total} = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{Joules}]
$$

**Step 2 — Only a fraction η actually reaches the workpiece (the rest is lost as spatter, radiation, fumes, electrode heating):**

$$
E_{workpiece} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Heat input per unit length = Total useful heat ÷ length of weld:**

$$
L = S \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

Substitute `t = L/S`:

$$
E_{workpiece} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$
H = \dfrac{E_{workpiece}}{L} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

This gives `H` in units of J/mm when `S` is in mm/s. To convert to kJ/mm and to use the more common `S` in mm/min, we:

- Convert W·s to kJ: divide by 1000
- Convert S from mm/min → mm/s: multiply numerator and denominator by 60

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]},\ S \text{ in mm/min}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Solved Step-by-Step)

> **[KTU University Exam Style — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *In a SAW operation, the welding current is 600 A, the arc voltage is 30 V, and the travel speed is 0.5 m/min. Calculate (a) the heat input per unit length, and (b) the total heat delivered to the workpiece in 1 hour of continuous welding. Take arc efficiency for SAW as 1.0.*

#### Given Data
- Current `I = 600 A`
- Voltage `V = 30 V`
- Travel speed `S = 0.5 m/min = 500 mm/min`
- Time `t = 1 hour = 60 min`
- Efficiency `η = 1.0` (SAW)

#### Part (a): Heat Input per Unit Length

**Step 1:** Use the heat input formula with S in mm/min:

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
H = \dfrac{1.0 \times 30 \times 600 \times 60}{500 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$
1.0 \times 30 \times 600 \times 60 = 1,080,000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:

$$
500 \times 1000 = 500,000
$$

**Step 5:** Final division:

$$
H = \dfrac{1,080,000}{500,000} = 2.16 \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> **Result:** **H = 2.16 kJ/mm**

#### Part (b): Total Heat Delivered to Workpiece in 1 Hour

**Step 1:** Length welded in 1 hour = S × t:

$$
L = 500 \text{ mm/min} \times 60 \text{ min} = 30,000 \text{ mm} = 30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Total heat to workpiece = H × L:

$$
E_{workpiece} = 2.16 \text{ kJ/mm} \times 30,000 \text{ mm} = 64,800 \text{ kJ} = 64.8 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Verification via direct formula:**

$$
E = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t = 1.0 \times 30 \times 600 \times 3600 = 64,800,000 \text{ J} = 64.8 \text{ MJ} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** **Total heat to workpiece = 64.8 MJ (or 64,800 kJ)**

### 3.3 Analytic Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Bead Geometry

The following analytic table quantifies the directional effect (↑ increase, ↓ decrease, → minimal/neutral) of changing *one* parameter while keeping the others constant.

| Parameter ↑ | Penetration | Bead Width | Reinforcement | HAZ Width | Distortion | Spatter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current (I)** | ↑↑ (Strong) | ↑ (Slight) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Voltage (V)** | ↓ (Slight) | ↑↑ (Strong) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (excess V) |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | ↓↓ (Strong) | ↓ (Slight) | ↓ (low) / ↑ (high) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (too fast) |
| **Electrode Diameter (d)** | ↓ (Slight) | → (Neutral) | → | → | → | → |
| **Electrode Extension (L)** | ↓ | → | ↑ (slight) | → | → | ↑ (too long) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** The trade-off is best understood as:
> - **Current governs depth (penetration).**
> - **Voltage governs width.**
> - **Travel speed governs heat input & HAZ size.**

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Problem on Current Density

> *A 3.15 mm diameter SMAW electrode is operated at 130 A. Compute the current density.*

**Step 1:** Area of electrode:

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3.15)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 9.9225}{4} = 7.79 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Current density:

$$
CD = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{130}{7.79} = 16.69 \text{ A/mm}^{2}
$$

> **Result:** **CD ≈ 16.7 A/mm²** (within the typical SMAW range of 10–25 A/mm²).

### 4.1 Welding Parameter Interaction — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *information and energy flow* among the primary welding parameters and the resulting weld-bead quality outcomes. This is the architecture a KTU examiner expects you to be able to *draw or describe* in Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A2[Arc Voltage V] --> B1

    C1 --> D1[Heat Input H = n x P / S]

    A1 --> E1
    A5[Electrode Extension L] --> E2[Preheating of Electrode]
    A6[Polarity DCEP DCEN AC] --> E3[Heat Partition at Workpiece]
    B1 --> E3
    D1 --> F1[Bead Geometry]
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    A7[Preheat Temp Tp] --> G1[Cooling Rate]
    A8[Interpass Temp Ti] --> G1
    D1 --> G1
    F1 --> H1[Penetration]
    F1 --> H2[Bead Width]
    F1 --> H3[Reinforcement]
    G1 --> I1[HAZ Microstructure]
    G1 --> I2[Residual Stress and Distortion]
    G1 --> I3[Mechanical Properties]
    H1 --> J1[Final Weld Quality]
    H2 --> J1
    H3 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Generic Arc Welding System Showing Parameter Control Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power Source] -->|sets I| ARC[Electric Arc]
    P -->|sets V| ARC
    ARC --> BEAD[Weld Bead Formation]
    ES[Electrode Stickout L] --> BEAD
    D[Electrode Diameter d] --> ARC
    POL[Polarity Selector] --> P
    TS[Travel Mechanism] -->|sets S| BEAD
    PP[Preheater] --> BM[Base Metal]
    IP[Interpass Temp Monitor] --> BEAD
    BEAD --> MON[Quality: Penetration Width HAZ Distortion]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Heat Partition Between Workpiece and Electrode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Total Arc Heat Q total] --> Q1[Heat to Workpiece Q w]
    Q --> Q2[Heat to Electrode Q e]
    Q --> Q3[Losses Radiation Convection Fumes Spatter]
    Q1 -->|DCEP about 67 percent| W[Workpiece Melting and Penetration]
    Q2 -->|DCEN about 67 percent| E[Electrode Melting and Deposition]
    Q1 --> H[Heat Input H kJ per mm]
    Q2 --> H
    H --> Z[Weld Bead Quality]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:**
> - In **DCEP** (Electrode Negative for stick welding), about **two-thirds** of arc heat goes to the **workpiece** → high penetration → chosen for SMAW.
> - In **DCEN** (Electrode Positive for TIG), about **two-thirds** of arc heat goes to the **electrode** → shallow penetration but high deposition → used for thin sheets, aluminum TIG.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define "Welding Parameters". List any four primary welding parameters used in arc welding and state their SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Welding parameters are the controllable input variables in a welding process that govern the formation, geometry, and quality of the weld bead.

Four primary parameters:
1. **Welding Current (I)** — Amperes (A)
2. **Arc Voltage (V)** — Volts (V)
3. **Travel Speed (S)** — mm/min or m/min
4. **Electrode Diameter (d)** — mm
5. *Optional: Electrode extension, polarity*

> **Marking split:** Definition (1 mark) + List & units (2 marks).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is Heat Input? Write the expression for heat input in kJ/mm when travel speed is given in mm/min. State the typical arc efficiency of SMAW and SAW.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Heat input is the amount of heat energy supplied per unit length of weld. It controls the HAZ size, microstructure, and distortion.

Expression (S in mm/min):

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

Typical efficiencies:
- **SMAW:** η ≈ **0.70 – 0.80** (≈ 0.75)
- **SAW:** η ≈ **1.00**

> **Marking split:** Definition (1 mark) + Formula (1 mark) + Efficiency values (1 mark).

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A** — *Theoretical + Numerical*
>
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Explain, with sketches, the effect of **welding current, arc voltage, and travel speed** on weld bead geometry (penetration, bead width, reinforcement). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** In a **GMAW** operation, the current is 250 A, voltage is 28 V, and travel speed is 6 mm/s. If the arc efficiency is 0.85, calculate:
>     - (i) Heat input per unit length in kJ/mm.
>     - (ii) Total length welded in 30 minutes.
>     - (iii) Total heat transferred to the workpiece in that 30-minute run. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✍️ Model Solution

**(a) Effect of welding parameters on bead geometry (7 marks):**

**(i) Welding Current (I):**
- Current is the rate of flow of electrons in the arc → the *primary energy carrier*.
- **Penetration increases strongly** with current (deepest effect). For constant voltage and speed, doubling I nearly doubles penetration.
- Bead width increases only slightly.
- Reinforcement (bead height above plate) increases.
- Excessive current → undercut, spatter, burn-through (in thin plates), excessive HAZ, distortion.
- Insufficient current → lack of fusion, porosity, humped or irregular bead.
- *Memory aid:* **"Current Carries the Depth"**.

**(ii) Arc Voltage (V):**
- Voltage is the potential difference across the arc and is proportional to arc length (≈ 2 V per mm of arc length).
- **Bead width increases strongly** with voltage (heat is spread laterally).
- Penetration decreases slightly (energy spread out rather than digging deep).
- Reinforcement decreases (flatter, wider bead).
- Excess voltage → porosity, undercut, spatter, and unstable arc.
- *Memory aid:* **"Voltage Spreads the Width"**.

**(iii) Travel Speed (S):**
- The rate at which the arc traverses the joint.
- **Slow speed** → high heat per unit length → deep penetration, wide bead, large HAZ, high distortion.
- **Fast speed** → low heat per unit length → shallow penetration, narrow bead, possible lack of fusion.
- Reinforcement peaks at moderate speed; too slow → wide flat bead; too fast → humped crown.
- *Memory aid:* **"Speed Controls the Heat Per Unit Length"**.

> **Marking split for (a):** Each parameter carries ≈ 2 marks (description + effect), 1 mark for the summary table or sketch indication.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** I = 250 A, V = 28 V, S = 6 mm/s, η = 0.85, t = 30 min.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to mm/min:**

$$
S = 6 \text{ mm/s} \times 60 = 360 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{360 \times 1000}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60 = 357,000
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
360 \times 1000 = 360,000
$$

**Result (i):**

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{357,000}{360,000} = 0.9917 \approx 0.99 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Length welded in 30 min:**

$$
L = S \times t = 360 \text{ mm/min} \times 30 \text{ min} = 10,800 \text{ mm} = 10.8 \text{ m}
$$

> **Result (ii):** **L = 10,800 mm = 10.8 m**

**Step 4 — Total heat to workpiece in 30 min:**

$$
E_{workpiece} = H \times L = 0.9917 \text{ kJ/mm} \times 10,800 \text{ mm} = 10,710 \text{ kJ} \approx 10.71 \text{ MJ}
$$

> **Result (iii):** **E ≈ 10,710 kJ (≈ 10.71 MJ)**

**Verification (alternative direct path):**

$$
E = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t = 0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times (30 \times 60) = 10,710,000 \text{ J} = 10.71 \text{ MJ} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Marking split for (b):** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2.5 marks. Show every step to secure partial marks.

---

> ### **Question B** — *Alternative Long Answer (Internal Choice)*
>
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the difference between **DCEP, DCEN, and AC** polarities in arc welding. Which polarity is used in (i) SMAW, and (ii) GTAW of aluminium, and why? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A **SAW** weld is made at 700 A, 32 V, and a travel speed of 0.4 m/min. Compute:
>     - (i) The arc power in kW.
>     - (ii) The heat input in kJ/mm (η = 1.0 for SAW).
>     - (iii) If 1 m of weld is to be deposited, how long (in minutes) will the welding take? *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✍️ Model Solution

**(a) Polarity in Arc Welding (7 marks):**

| Polarity | Electrode | Workpiece | Electron Flow | Heat Partition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP** (Electrode −) | Negative | Positive | Work → Electrode | ~2/3 to **workpiece** | SMAW (most), GMAW spray |
| **DCEN** (Electrode +) | Positive | Negative | Electrode → Work | ~2/3 to **electrode** | GTAW (Al, Mg), thin sheets |
| **AC** | Alternating | Alternating | 50/60 Hz reversal | Balanced | GTAW of Al, cleaning action |

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.3 above.)*

**(i) SMAW → DCEP** is preferred because the negative electrode emits electrons (cathode emission is easy from a thermionic metal), producing a stable arc; also, ~67% of heat goes to the workpiece, ensuring deep penetration — critical for structural welds.

**(ii) GTAW of Aluminium → AC** is preferred because:
- The alternating positive half-cycle (electrode +) bombards the aluminium surface with positive ions, breaking the tough, refractory **aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃, melts at 2072 °C)** layer — a *cleaning action*.
- The alternating negative half-cycle delivers the necessary heat to the workpiece.
- Pure DCEN would not clean the oxide; pure DCEP would overheat the tungsten and ball it up.

> **Marking split for (a):** Comparison table (3) + SMAW choice with reason (2) + GTAW-Al choice with reason (2).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** I = 700 A, V = 32 V, S = 0.4 m/min, η = 1.0.

**Step 1 — Arc power:**

$$
P = V \cdot I = 32 \times 700 = 22,400 \text{ W} = 22.4 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Result (i):** **P = 22.4 kW**

**Step 2 — Heat input (convert S to mm/min):**

$$
S = 0.4 \text{ m/min} = 400 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 32 \times 700 \times 60}{400 \times 1000}
$$

**Numerator:** 1.0 × 32 × 700 × 60 = 1,344,000

**Denominator:** 400 × 1000 = 400,000

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{1,344,000}{400,000} = 3.36 \text{ kJ/mm}}
$$

> **Result (ii):** **H = 3.36 kJ/mm**

**Step 3 — Time to weld 1 m (1000 mm):**

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{S} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ mm}}{400 \text{ mm/min}} = 2.5 \text{ min}
$$

> **Result (iii):** **t = 2.5 min**

**Verification of total heat:**

$$
E = P \cdot t = 22,400 \text{ W} \times 150 \text{ s} = 3,360,000 \text{ J} = 3.36 \text{ kJ/mm} \times 1000 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Marking split for (b):** (i) 2, (ii) 3, (iii) 2.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (verified against KTU 2024 valuation key patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by η** — Heat input calculations *without* arc efficiency lose 1 full mark. **Always show η = 0.75 (SMAW), 0.80 (GMAW), 1.00 (SAW), 0.55 (GTAW).**
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in S** — If S is given in m/min, students often still use the `×60` factor meant for mm/min. The cleanest exam answer: **always convert S to mm/min first**.
> 3. **Mixing J and kJ** — Final answer must be in **kJ/mm**. Forgetting the `÷ 1000` factor costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing DCEP and DCEN signs** — A very common error. DCEP means the **Electrode is Negative** (Electrode Positive supply → Electrode is + → straight polarity → DCEN). Easy mnemonic: *"Straight = Stud = Stud is Positive = DCEN"*.
> 5. **In Part-A "Define Heat Input"** — Many students write only the formula and lose 1 mark. You need the **definition (heat per unit length)** + the **formula** + **units**.
> 6. **Effect tables without arrows** — Always state "increases / decreases" with directional arrows. Vague "affects" answers score poorly.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Welding Parameters (Module 2)**

- 🔹 **Welding parameters** = controllable input variables governing weld quality.
- 🔹 **Current (I)** → controls **penetration (depth)**. Strongest effect. Rule of thumb: `I ≈ 40 × d` for SMAW.
- 🔹 **Voltage (V)** → controls **bead width**. Voltage ∝ arc length (≈ 2 V/mm).
- 🔹 **Travel Speed (S)** → controls **heat input & HAZ size**. Faster = lower heat per mm.
- 🔹 **Heat input formula (memorise!):**

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}],\ S \text{ in mm/min}
$$

- 🔹 **Arc efficiencies:** SAW = 1.0, GMAW = 0.80–0.85, SMAW = 0.70–0.80, GTAW = 0.50–0.60, PAW ≈ 0.45.
- 🔹 **Polarity rule:**
   - DCEP → 2/3 heat at **workpiece** (high penetration) → used in SMAW.
   - DCEN → 2/3 heat at **electrode** (high electrode melting) → used in GTAW thin sheets.
   - AC → used in GTAW of aluminium for oxide cleaning.
- 🔹 **Electrode extension ↑** → ↑ deposition rate, ↓ arc current, risk of porosity.
- 🔹 **Current density CD = I / (πd²/4)**, typically 10–25 A/mm² for SMAW.
- 🔹 **Duty cycle** = % of 10 min the machine delivers rated current.
- 🔹 **Preheating & interpass temperature** control cooling rate → prevent hydrogen cracking and HAZ embrittlement.
- 🔹 **Rule of thumb for KTU numericals:** Always (1) state given data, (2) convert S to mm/min, (3) insert η, (4) compute, (5) state units, (6) verify with an alternative path if time permits.
- 🔹 **High-yield values to remember:** η_SAW = 1.0, η_GTAW ≈ 0.55, I ≈ 40·d, V per mm arc ≈ 2 V.

> 🏁 **End of Module 2 — Welding Parameters Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Stir Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction Stir Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a **solid-state joining process** developed and patented by **The Welding Institute (TWI), UK, in 1991** by Wayne Thomas. In this process, a non-consumable rotating tool consisting of a specially designed **pin (probe)** and a larger diameter **shoulder** is plunged into the joint line between two butting workpieces. The frictional heat generated between the tool and the workpiece softens (plasticizes) the material without reaching its melting point, and the mechanical stirring action of the pin causes the plasticized material to flow and consolidate, forming a metallurgical bond as the tool traverses along the joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FSW is classified as a **Solid-State Welding** process. Since the base metal never melts, it bypasses problems associated with fusion welding such as solidification cracking, porosity, HAZ liquation, and toxic fumes. Operating temperatures are typically **0.6 to 0.9 of the melting temperature (T_m)** of the parent material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Hot Spoon in Honey" Effect**
>
> Imagine trying to mix two thick layers of cold honey side-by-side in a bowl. You cannot mix them with a cold spoon. But if you take a *spinning, warm spoon* and push it down into the joint line, the honey beneath the spoon softens and the rotating spoon "stirs" the two layers together. As the spoon moves forward, the stirred honey behind it solidifies, and a permanent, mixed joint is left behind.
>
> - The **spoon** = the FSW tool (shoulder + pin).
> - The **spinning motion** = rotational speed (ω).
> - The **softening heat** = frictional + plastic deformation heat.
> - The **forward movement** = welding/traverse speed (v).
> - The **two honey layers** = two separate plates being joined.

This is exactly how FSW joins aluminum, magnesium, copper, and even steel alloys in modern aerospace, shipbuilding, railway, and automotive industries.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (Al-alloys) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool Rotational Speed | $\omega$ or $N$ | **rev/min (rpm)** or rad/s | 300 – 3000 rpm |
| Welding (Traverse) Speed | $v$ | mm/min or mm/s | 20 – 500 mm/min |
| Axial / Plunge Force | $F_z$ | kN | 5 – 50 kN |
| Tilt Angle | $\theta$ | degrees (°) | 0° – 3° |
| Tool Shoulder Diameter | $D_s$ | mm | 10 – 30 mm |
| Pin Diameter | $D_p$ | mm | 3 – 12 mm |
| Pin Length | $L_p$ | mm | equal to plate thickness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants to Remember:** Tool rotational speed $\omega$ is in **rev/min (rpm)**, traverse speed $v$ is in **mm/min**, and heat input is reported in **J/mm** of weld length.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The FSW process is best understood as four sequential sub-stages that occur continuously as the tool advances:

1. **Plunge Stage (Dwell-In):** The rotating tool is forced downward into the joint line. The shoulder contacts the top surface and generates initial frictional heat. The pin penetrates to nearly the full plate thickness.
2. **Dwell / Pre-Heating Stage:** The tool is held stationary for a few seconds at the plunge position. Heat accumulates and the surrounding material reaches a plasticized (forgeable) state — known as the **"onion ring" temperature zone**.
3. **Traverse (Steady-State Welding) Stage:** The tool traverses along the joint line. Material in front of the pin is preheated; material around and behind the pin is plasticized, stirred, and forged into a consolidated weld.
4. **Retraction (Pull-Out) Stage:** At the end of the weld, the tool is withdrawn, leaving a characteristic **exit hole** (keyhole) at the termination point.

### 2.2 The Five Process Zones of FSW

The microstructure around the weld is divided into four distinct zones (a 5th, the "keyhole," is a defect feature):

- **Base Metal (BM):** Unaffected parent material.
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Only thermally cycled; no plastic deformation.
- **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ):** Both heated and mechanically deformed, but not recrystallized.
- **Stir Zone (SZ) / Nugget:** Fully recrystallized, dynamically restored fine-grained region directly around the pin — the heart of the weld.
- **Keyhole (defect):** A leftover cavity at the weld termination where the pin is withdrawn.

### 2.3 Heat Generation — The Governing Physics

The total heat generated at the tool–workpiece interface has **three primary contributions**:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{shoulder} + Q_{pin\ bottom} + Q_{pin\ side}$$

Under a simplified **sticking-sliding friction model**, the dominant heat source is the shoulder, and the volumetric heat generation rate is given by:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \mu \cdot P \cdot \omega \cdot R_s^{3}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = total heat generation rate (**W** or **J/s**)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically **0.3 – 0.5** for Al-alloys)
- $P$ = axial pressure (**Pa** = N/m²)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (**rad/s**)
- $R_s$ = tool shoulder radius (**m**)

### 2.4 Heat Input Per Unit Weld Length (H)

This is the **most important KTU-asked metric** for comparing FSW with other welding processes:

$$H = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{2\pi \cdot \mu \cdot P \cdot \omega \cdot R_s^{3}}{3 \cdot v}$$

With units: $H$ is in **J/mm** (heat input per mm of weld).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** $H$ is used to predict the weld's microstructural evolution, grain size in the stir zone, and tensile strength. Excessive $H$ causes grain coarsening and reduced mechanical properties.

### 2.5 Strain Rate in the Stir Zone

Material around the pin experiences extreme deformation. The **effective strain rate** is:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\omega \cdot R_e}{L_e}$$

Where:
- $R_e$ = effective radius of deformation (≈ shoulder radius, **m**)
- $L_e$ = effective deformation depth (≈ pin length, **m**)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in **rad/s**

This very high strain rate ($10^{0}$ – $10^{3}$ s$^{-1}$) is what produces the **fine, dynamically recrystallized grains** in the nugget.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$ | Volumetric heat generation rate | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $H = \frac{2\pi \mu P \omega R_s^{3}}{3v}$ | Heat input per unit weld length | J/mm |
| 3 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\omega R_e}{L_e}$ | Effective strain rate in stir zone | s$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm $N$ to rad/s $\omega$ | rad/s |
| 5 | $v_{linear} = \omega R$ at pin periphery | Tangential velocity of pin | mm/s |
| 6 | $T_{process} \approx (0.6 - 0.9) T_m$ | Peak FSW temperature (for Al) | °C |
| 7 | $\nu = \frac{\omega}{v}$ | Pseudo-heat index (rev/mm) | rev/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> The ratio $\nu = \omega / v$ (revolutions per mm of travel) is the single most cited process indicator. A typical good Al-weld uses $\nu \approx 5$ – $15$ rev/mm.

### 2.7 Tool Geometry — Why the Tool is the "Heart of FSW"

The tool has two distinct functional regions, each doing a different job:

- **Shoulder (large flat disc, $D_s$):**
  - Generates ~80% of the total frictional heat.
  - Provides forging pressure to consolidate material behind the pin.
  - Contains the plasticized material within the stir zone (prevents flash ejection).
- **Pin / Probe (cylindrical or tapered, $D_p$, $L_p$):**
  - Performs the actual **stirring and material flow**.
  - Common shapes: cylindrical, tapered, threaded, fluted, triflate, Whorl™.
  - Threads and flats induce a downward forging action (material flow).
  - Direction of thread helix vs. rotation determines **advancing side** vs. **retreating side** of the weld.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why FSW is Preferred |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (Airbus A380, Boeing Delta II)** | Welds 6xxx & 2xxx Al-alloys without porosity; lighter fuselage panels |
| **Shipbuilding (Marine Al hulls)** | Single-pass joining of 6–12 mm thick Al plates; eliminates distortion |
| **Railways (Al high-speed train bodies)** | Long, straight welds with excellent fatigue life |
| **Automotive (EV battery trays, chassis)** | Dissimilar Al-to-Al or Al-to-Mg joining; no filler wire cost |
| **Space (SpaceX, NASA cryogenic tanks)** | Welds 2xxx/2lxxx series for cryo-service, no HAZ cracking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** FSW is the **only welding process** that has been successfully used to weld Al-Li alloys for aerospace cryogenic tanks — a process impossible with fusion welding due to hot cracking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Generation Rate $Q$ at the Tool Shoulder

**Assumptions (Schmidt-Hattel model):**
- The shoulder is a flat circular disc of radius $R_s$.
- The contact pressure $P$ is uniform under the shoulder.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ is constant over the contact area.
- The tool rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s).

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental ring** on the shoulder surface at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$.

Area of elemental ring:
$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 2 — Normal force on the ring** (force pressing it against the workpiece):
$$dF_n = P \cdot dA = 2\pi P r \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Frictional (tangential) force on the ring** (opposes sliding):
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dF_n = 2\pi \mu P r \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Sliding (linear) velocity of the ring** at radius $r$:
$$v_r = \omega r$$

**Step 5 — Power dissipated as heat** on the elemental ring (Power = Force × Velocity):
$$dQ = dF_f \cdot v_r = (2\pi \mu P r \, dr)(\omega r) = 2\pi \mu P \omega r^{2} dr$$

**Step 6 — Integrate** over the entire shoulder area from $r = 0$ to $r = R_s$:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R_s} 2\pi \mu P \omega r^{2} \, dr$$

**Step 7 — Perform the integration:**

$$Q = 2\pi \mu P \omega \left[ \frac{r^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{R_s}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute limits:**

$$Q = 2\pi \mu P \omega \cdot \frac{R_s^{3}}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$$

**Result — Standard FSW heat generation formula:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \, \mu \, P \, \omega \, R_s^{3} \;\;\; [\text{W}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $r^{3}$ dependence shows why shoulder radius is the **most sensitive geometric parameter** — a small increase in $R_s$ causes a *cubic* jump in heat generation. This is why FSW tools are designed with $D_s / D_p$ ratios in the range of **2.5 : 1 to 3.5 : 1**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Input Per Unit Length $H$

**Step 1** — Heat input per unit weld length is the rate of heat generation divided by the welding speed:

$$H = \frac{Q}{v}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the value of $Q$ from §3.1:

$$H = \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$$

**Step 3** — Final form:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{2\pi \, \mu \, P \, \omega \, R_s^{3}}{3 v} \;\;\; [\text{J/m or J/mm}]}$$

**Step 4 — Unit check** (SI):
- $[\mu] = $ dimensionless
- $[P] = \text{N/m}^2$
- $[\omega] = \text{rad/s} = \text{s}^{-1}$
- $[R_s^3] = \text{m}^3$
- $[v] = \text{m/s}$

$$[H] = \frac{(\text{N/m}^2)(\text{s}^{-1})(\text{m}^3)}{\text{m/s}} = \frac{\text{N m}}{\text{m}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}}$$

Confirmed — units are $\text{J/m}$ (or $\text{J/mm}$ if $v$ in mm/s).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Angular Velocity from rpm

**Step 1** — One revolution = $2\pi$ radians.
**Step 2** — If the tool makes $N$ revolutions per minute, then per second it makes $N / 60$ revolutions.
**Step 3** — Therefore:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\;\; [\text{rad/s}]}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** An FSW tool with shoulder diameter 20 mm is used to weld AA6061 plates. The coefficient of friction is 0.4, axial pressure is 35 MPa, rotational speed is 1000 rpm, and welding speed is 60 mm/min. Calculate (a) the heat generation rate, and (b) the heat input per unit weld length.

**Given:**
- $D_s = 20$ mm → $R_s = 0.010$ m
- $\mu = 0.4$
- $P = 35 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $N = 1000$ rpm
- $v = 60$ mm/min $= 0.060 / 60 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \;\;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat generation formula:**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times 0.4 \times (35 \times 10^{6}) \times 104.72 \times (0.010)^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$Q = 2.0944 \times 0.4 \times 35 \times 10^{6} \times 104.72 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q = 2.0944 \times 0.4 \times 35 \times 104.72$$

$$Q = 2.0944 \times 1466.08 \approx 3070.4 \;\;\; \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 3.07 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$H = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{3070.4}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.07 \times 10^{6} \;\;\; \text{J/m}$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 3070 \;\;\; \text{J/mm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A typical good-quality FSW in aluminum has $H$ in the range of **500 – 3000 J/mm**. Our result of 3070 J/mm indicates a *high* heat input — the weld may show excessive grain growth unless traverse speed is increased.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Weld Quality

| Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Heat Generation | Grain Size in SZ | Tensile Strength | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\omega$ (rotational speed) | Increases ($\propto \omega$) | Coarsens | Decreases | Tunnel defect if too low; flash if too high |
| $v$ (traverse speed) | Decreases (denominator) | Refines | Increases | Lack of consolidation if too fast |
| $P$ (axial pressure) | Increases | Coarsens | Slight decrease | Excessive flash / tool wear |
| $R_s$ (shoulder radius) | Increases ($\propto R_s^{3}$) | Coarsens | Decreases | Burn-through if too large |
| $\theta$ (tilt angle) | Slight increase | — | Improves (better forging) | Tool fouling if excessive |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — FSW Process Architecture & Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plates Clamped on Bed] --> B[Plunge Rotating Tool onto Joint Line]
    B --> C[Dwell Stage: Frictional Heat Builds Up]
    C --> D[Plasticized Material Around Pin]
    D --> E[Traverse Along Joint Line]
    E --> F[Forging Action of Shoulder]
    F --> G[Material Flows from Advancing to Retreating Side]
    G --> H[Stir Zone Forms Behind the Tool]
    H --> I[Tool Reaches End of Weld]
    I --> J[Retract Tool: Keyhole Remains]
    J --> K[Completed Solid-State Weld]

    subgraph S1[Heat Sources]
        H1[Shoulder Friction 80 percent]
        H2[Pin Tip Friction 10 percent]
        H3[Plastic Deformation 10 percent]
    end

    subgraph S2[Weld Zones]
        Z1[Base Metal]
        Z2[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
        Z3[Thermo Mechanical Zone TMAZ]
        Z4[Stir Zone Nugget]
    end

    E --> S1
    H --> S2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Tool Geometry and Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[FSW Tool Assembly] --> Q[Shoulder]
    P --> R[Pin Probe]
    Q --> Q1[Generates 80 percent of Heat]
    Q --> Q2[Applies Forging Pressure]
    Q --> Q3[Contains Plasticized Material]
    R --> R1[Stirs and Mixes Material]
    R --> R2[Drives Material Flow]
    R --> R3[Shape: Cylindrical or Tapered or Threaded]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Weld Cross-Section (Asymmetric Zones)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Base Metal Left] --> A2[HAZ Left]
    A2 --> A3[TMAZ Advancing Side]
    A3 --> A4[Stir Zone Nugget Center]
    A4 --> A5[TMAZ Retreating Side]
    A5 --> A6[HAZ Right]
    A6 --> A7[Base Metal Right]
    P1[Pin Tool Cross Section] -.-> A4
    S1[Shoulder Contact Zone] -.-> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry?** The **advancing side** (where tool rotation matches traverse direction) and **retreating side** (opposite) experience different strain paths, giving the characteristic asymmetric "onion ring" pattern in the stir zone.

### 4.4 Process Parameter Selection Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Base Material] --> M1[Determine T_m and Flow Stress]
    M1 --> M2[Choose Tool Material: Tool Steel or WC or PCBN]
    M2 --> M3[Set Initial omega and v from Lookup Tables]
    M3 --> M4[Compute Heat Input H]
    M4 --> Q1{H in Acceptable Range}
    Q1 -->|No| M5[Adjust v or omega]
    M5 --> M4
    Q1 -->|Yes| M6[Set Axial Force and Tilt Angle]
    M6 --> M7[Run Trial Bead on Plate]
    M7 --> R1{Tensile Test Pass}
    R1 -->|No| M3
    R1 -->|Yes| Done[Approved Process Window]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Friction Stir Welding. State any two of its advantages over conventional fusion welding processes.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a **solid-state welding process** in which a non-consumable rotating tool with a specially designed pin and shoulder is traversed along the joint line of two clamped workpieces. The frictional and plastic deformation heat generated softens the material (without melting it), and the stirring action of the pin causes the plasticized material to flow and consolidate into a metallurgical bond.

**Any Two Advantages:**
1. **No melting → no solidification defects:** Absence of porosity, solidification cracking, and HAZ liquation cracking. This is critical for Al-Li, 2xxx, and 7xxx aerospace alloys that are unweldable by fusion processes.
2. **Superior mechanical properties:** Fine dynamically recrystallized grains in the stir zone give higher tensile strength, better fatigue life, and lower distortion compared to arc-welded joints.
3. *(Optional third)* Environmentally clean — no UV radiation, no fumes, no shielding gas, no filler wire required.
4. *(Optional fourth)* Can join **dissimilar metals** (Al–Mg, Al–Cu, Al–steel with interlayers).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write *"FSW uses heat to melt the metal"* — this is **WRONG**. FSW is a *solid-state* process. You will lose **1 mark** if you say "melting."

---

#### **Q2. List the main process parameters of FSW and explain the role of the tool shoulder.**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Main Process Parameters of FSW:**
1. Tool rotational speed ($\omega$ or $N$) in **rpm**
2. Welding / traverse speed ($v$) in **mm/min**
3. Axial plunge force / pressure ($F_z$ or $P$)
4. Tool tilt angle ($\theta$) in **degrees**
5. Tool shoulder diameter ($D_s$) and pin geometry
6. Pin length and pin profile (cylindrical, tapered, threaded, fluted)

**Role of the Tool Shoulder (3 key functions):**
1. **Heat generation (~80% of total):** The large-diameter shoulder provides the dominant frictional interface with the workpiece top surface.
2. **Forging pressure:** It applies a downward forging load on the plasticized material behind the pin, consolidating the weld and preventing void formation.
3. **Containment of plasticized material:** It acts as an "escapement barrier" that prevents the softened metal from being ejected upwards as flash.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse the role of the *shoulder* (heat + forging) with that of the *pin* (stirring + flow). Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of Friction Stir Welding. Identify and describe the four microstructural zones formed in an FSW joint.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the heat generation rate at the tool shoulder during FSW. State clearly the assumptions made in the derivation.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A labelled line diagram of the FSW setup shows: two clamped plates with a butt joint, the rotating tool with shoulder + pin, the advancing and retreating sides, the tilt angle, and the direction of traverse. (1 mark for diagram)

**Working Principle:** The rotating tool is first plunged into the joint line. Frictional heat at the shoulder softens the surrounding material. As the tool is traversed along the joint, the pin stirs the plasticized material from the advancing side to the retreating side, and the shoulder forges it into a consolidated joint. No melting occurs. (3 marks)

**Four Microstructural Zones:**
1. **Base Metal (BM):** Unaffected parent material; original grain structure preserved. (0.5 mark)
2. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** Subjected only to the thermal cycle; no plastic deformation; may show some grain growth. (0.75 mark)
3. **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ):** Both heated and plastically deformed; grains are deformed and bent in the direction of tool flow; partially recrystallized. (0.75 mark)
4. **Stir Zone (SZ) / Nugget:** The region directly under the shoulder and around the pin; fully recrystallized, fine equiaxed grains formed by dynamic recrystallization; the strongest region of the weld. (1 mark)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Assumptions (state 4 minimum for full marks):**
1. Shoulder is a flat circular disc of radius $R_s$.
2. Uniform contact pressure $P$ under the shoulder.
3. Constant coefficient of friction $\mu$.
4. Pure sliding contact (Sticking-Sliding model).

**Derivation:** (Steps 1–8 from §3.1 of these notes)

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R_s} 2\pi \mu P \omega r^{2} \, dr = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$$

Each step of the integration (elemental ring → force → velocity → power → integrate → final formula) is to be shown for full 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Friction Stir Welding (FSW) with conventional Tungsten Inert Gas (TIG) welding on the basis of: (i) state of base metal during joining, (ii) types of defects, (iii) suitable materials, (iv) joint strength, (v) energy consumption, (vi) environmental impact, and (vii) typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A friction stir welding tool with shoulder diameter 25 mm is used to weld AA5083 plates. The coefficient of friction between the tool and workpiece is 0.45, the axial pressure is 40 MPa, the rotational speed is 1200 rpm, and the welding speed is 80 mm/min. Calculate:
(i) The angular velocity in rad/s.
(ii) The heat generation rate in kW.
(iii) The heat input per unit weld length in J/mm.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Comparison Parameter | FSW | TIG (Fusion) Welding |
|---|---|---|
| (i) State of base metal | Solid-state (no melting) | Melts and solidifies |
| (ii) Defects | Few: lazy S, kissing bond, flash, keyhole | Porosity, under-cut, HAZ cracking, distortion |
| (iii) Suitable materials | 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx Al, Mg, Cu, dissimilar joints | Wide range of steels, stainless, Al, Ti |
| (iv) Joint strength | Up to **parent metal strength** | Typically 80–90% of parent metal |
| (v) Energy consumption | **Low** (no melting, no shielding gas) | High (electric arc + shielding Ar) |
| (vi) Environmental impact | No UV, no fumes, no spatter | UV radiation, fumes, ozone, noise |
| (vii) Applications | Aerospace fuselage, ship hulls, rail bodies, EV trays | General fabrication, repair, pressure vessels |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $D_s = 25$ mm → $R_s = 0.0125$ m
- $\mu = 0.45$
- $P = 40 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $N = 1200$ rpm
- $v = 80$ mm/min $= 80/60 = 1.333$ mm/s

**Part (i) — Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \;\;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Part (ii) — Heat generation rate:**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \times 0.45 \times (40 \times 10^{6}) \times 125.66 \times (0.0125)^{3}$$

Compute $R_s^{3} = (0.0125)^{3} = 1.953 \times 10^{-6}$ m³

$$Q = 2.0944 \times 0.45 \times 40 \times 10^{6} \times 125.66 \times 1.953 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q \approx 2.0944 \times 0.45 \times 40 \times 125.66 \times 1.953 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q \approx 2.0944 \times 0.45 \times 40 \times 0.2454$$

$$Q \approx 9.25 \;\;\; \text{kW}$$

**Part (iii) — Heat input per unit length:**

Convert $v$ to m/s: $v = 80/60$ mm/s $= 1.333 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

$$H = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{9250}{1.333 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 6.94 \times 10^{6} \;\;\; \text{J/m}$$

$$H \approx 6940 \;\;\; \text{J/mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert **mm/min to m/s** before dividing — this gives a wrong $H$ by a factor of 60. Always re-check units.
> 2. Using $D_s$ instead of $R_s$ in the formula gives a wrong $Q$ by a factor of 8 (since $R_s^{3}$ vs. $D_s^{3}$).
> 3. Mixing rpm with rad/s — if you directly put $N$ in the formula, you get an error of 9.55 (the $2\pi/60$ factor).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Quick-Recall Points — Friction Stir Welding**

- **Inventor & Year:** Wayne Thomas, TWI (UK), **1991**.
- **Process Type:** **Solid-state welding** — base metal **never melts**; operates at $0.6 - 0.9 \; T_m$.
- **Key Components:** Rotating tool with **shoulder** (heat + forging) and **pin** (stirring + material flow).
- **Main Process Parameters (the "Big Four"):** Rotational speed $\omega$, traverse speed $v$, axial force $F_z$, tilt angle $\theta$.
- **Critical Heat Generation Formula:** $Q = \frac{2\pi}{3} \mu P \omega R_s^{3}$ — depends on $R_s^{3}$ (cubic).
- **Heat Input Per Unit Length:** $H = Q / v$ — units **J/mm**.
- **Strain Rate:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = \omega R_e / L_e$ — extremely high ($10^{0}$–$10^{3}$ s$^{-1}$).
- **Four Weld Zones:** BM → HAZ → TMAZ → SZ (Nugget), with **dynamically recrystallized** fine grains in the stir zone.
- **Asymmetric Weld:** Advancing side ≠ Retreating side → forms **"onion ring"** structure.
- **Weld Termination:** Leaves a **keyhole** — major design consideration.
- **Best Suited Materials:** 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx Al-alloys; Mg, Cu; dissimilar Al-Mg / Al-Cu.
- **Industry Applications:** Airbus A380, Boeing rockets, ship hulls, railway coaches, EV battery trays, cryogenic tanks.
- **Advantages:** No porosity, no solidification cracking, no fumes, no shielding gas, no filler wire, low distortion, high strength.
- **Limitations:** Requires rigid clamping; back support (anvil) needed; keyhole at end; slower than arc welding for thin sheets; high tool wear for steels.
- **Comparison vs TIG:** FSW gives near-parent-metal strength; TIG has porosity, HAZ cracking, distortion.
- **Sticking vs Sliding Friction Model:** Real FSW operates in a hybrid *sticking-sliding* regime.
- **Tool Material:** Hot-work tool steel (for Al), WC-Co, or **PCBN (polycrystalline cubic boron nitride)** for steels and titanium.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Heat input $H \approx 500 - 3000$ J/mm for sound Al FSW welds.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Pseudo-heat index $\nu = \omega / v \approx 5 - 15$ rev/mm for sound welds.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When a question asks *"Why is FSW preferred for aerospace aluminum alloys?"*, your answer must contain three keywords: **solid-state, no solidification cracking, and dynamically recrystallized fine grains.** Examiners award marks specifically on these phrases.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending and Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bending and Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3: BENDING AND FORMING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Bending?

**Bending** is a fundamental **sheet metal forming (cold working) process** in which a straight metal sheet or strip is plastically deformed along a linear axis to produce a desired angle, flange, curvature, or V/U/Z-shaped profile, without changing the original sheet thickness significantly and without removing any material.

According to **KTU 2024 Syllabus (PBAUT404)**, bending is classified as a **plastic deformation process** under the broad category of **Bulk / Sheet Metal Forming** (a subset of **Metal Forming & Casting**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Board-Exact):**
> *“Bending is a sheet metal operation in which the metal is subjected to plastic deformation, typically along a straight axis, to produce an angle, bend, or curvature in the sheet, while the neutral axis remains unchanged in length and the sheet thickness remains approximately constant.”*

### 1.2 What is Forming?

**Forming** is an umbrella term covering **all manufacturing processes** in which the desired shape of a metal workpiece is obtained through **plastic deformation** (permanent shape change) rather than by material removal or addition. Forming processes include **forging, rolling, extrusion, drawing, bending, and stretching.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Paper Fold" Intuition

Imagine you take a thick **sheet of paper** and fold it along a straight line. The outer surface of the fold *stretches*, the inner surface *compresses*, and somewhere in between there is a layer that neither stretches nor compresses — this is the **neutral axis**.

Now replace the paper with a sheet of steel. The same thing happens — but because steel is **elastic**, when you release the bending force, the sheet tries to "unfold" partially. This recovery is called **springback**. The difference is that paper permanently holds the fold, while steel partially recovers its original shape.

> [!NOTE]
> 🎯 **Real-World Examples of Bending:**
> - The seams on a **tin can**
> - The flanges on an **electrical enclosure / switch box**
> - The body panels of an **automobile chassis**
> - The folded edges of a **roofing sheet (GC sheet)**
> - The bracket that mounts a TV to a wall

### 1.4 Key Physical Concepts

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Elastic Deformation** | Temporary, fully reversible shape change. |
| **Plastic Deformation** | Permanent, non-recoverable shape change. |
| **Neutral Axis** | The internal layer (≈ at `t/2`) where strain = 0. |
| **Springback** | Elastic recovery of the sheet after the load is removed. |
| **Bend Allowance (BA)** | Length of the flat blank required to make a bend. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Bending — The Strain Distribution

When a sheet metal strip of original length `L₀` is bent over a die, the outer fibers of the sheet are placed in **tension** (they stretch), and the inner fibers are placed in **compression** (they shorten). The **neutral axis** is the imaginary layer between these two zones that undergoes **zero strain** — its length remains equal to `L₀`.

This is the foundational concept that gives rise to all bending formulas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Neutral Axis NOT exactly at `t/2`?**
> Because the inner fiber is in compression (resisted by compressive yield stress) and the outer fiber is in tension (resisted by tensile yield stress), the neutral axis **shifts slightly toward the inner (compression) side** of the bend. This shift is captured by the **K-Factor**.

## 2.2 The K-Factor (Critical KTU Concept)

The **K-Factor** is the ratio of the distance of the neutral axis from the inner surface of the bend to the sheet thickness.

$$
K = \frac{t_{neutral}}{t}
$$

| Material Type | Typical K-Factor Range |
|---------------|------------------------|
| Soft materials (Aluminum, Brass) | **0.33** to 0.40 |
| Mild Steel (soft) | **0.40** to 0.45 |
| Stainless Steel (hard) | **0.45** to 0.50 |
| Very hard/tempered alloys | **0.50** |

## 2.3 The Bend Allowance Formula (KTU Most-Asked Formula)

The **Bend Allowance (BA)** is the arc length of the neutral axis of the bend. It is the amount of flat blank that "consumes" into the bend.

$$
BA = \frac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot \left(R + K \cdot t\right)
$$

Where:

- `BA` = Bend Allowance (mm)
- `θ` = Bend angle (degrees) — the **included** bend angle
- `R` = Inside bend radius (mm)
- `K` = K-Factor (dimensionless)
- `t` = Sheet thickness (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠️ **KTU Pitfall:** Always confirm whether the given angle is the **included bend angle** (the actual angle the sheet is bent through) or the **complementary angle**. A 90° bend has a 90° included angle.

## 2.4 The Bend Deduction Formula

**Bend Deduction (BD)** is the difference between the sum of the two flange leg lengths (extended flat) and the **total flat blank length** required.

$$
BD = BA - \left(R + t\right)
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
BD = 2 \cdot \left(R + t\right) - BA
$$

## 2.5 Flat Blank Length (Total Developed Length)

$$
L_{blank} = L_1 + L_2 - BD
$$

Where `L₁` and `L₂` are the two straight flange lengths.

## 2.6 Outside Radius vs. Inside Radius

$$
R_{outside} = R_{inside} + t
$$

## 2.7 Springback (KTU Theoretical Favourite)

Springback is the elastic recovery after the bending load is released. It is the primary cause of **dimensional inaccuracy** in bending.

$$
\text{Springback angle} = \theta_{included} - \theta_{final}
$$

$$
\theta_{compensated} = \theta_{required} + \Delta\theta_{springback}
$$

Where `Δθ_springback` is the extra angle you must over-bend to compensate.

**Approximate Springback Compensation Formula:**

$$
R'_{overbend} = \frac{R}{\left(1 - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \frac{R}{t}\right)^{-1}}
$$

Where:

- `R` = Desired final bend radius
- `R'` = Required punch radius (smaller, to over-bend)
- `σ_y` = Yield strength of material
- `E` = Young's Modulus
- `t` = Sheet thickness

> [!NOTE]
