# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on **actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns. Each Part A is 3 marks, each Part B is 14 marks with internal choice. **Part B sub-parts carry 7 marks each.**

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q: Define the term "angle of bite" in flat rolling. State the relationship between angle of bite, coefficient of friction, and maximum draft.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The angle of bite ($\alpha$) is the maximum angle (measured at the roll center) subtended by the arc of contact between the roll and the stock. It is the half-angle from the entry to the exit of the contact zone.

**Relationship (2 Marks):**
- The **no-slip condition** requires that the angle of bite must not exceed the **friction angle**, i.e., $\tan(\alpha) \leq \mu$.
- The **maximum draft** (largest possible reduction per pass without slipping) is therefore:

$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R
$$

where $R$ is the roll radius. If $\Delta h$ exceeds this value, the rolls will spin on the stock surface without gripping it.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner allocates: **1 mark for definition**, **2 marks for the relationship and formula**.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**

**Q: Differentiate between hot rolling and cold rolling. List two advantages and two limitations of cold rolling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Hot Rolling** | **Cold Rolling** |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Above recrystallization ($> 0.6 T_m$) | Below recrystallization (room temp.) |
| **Surface finish** | Rough, oxidised (scale) | Smooth, bright |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Strain hardening** | None (recrystallizes) | Yes (strength increases) |
| **Reduction per pass** | Large (20–50%) | Small (5–15%) |
| **Applications** | Blooms, slabs, structural sections | Sheets, strips, auto panels |

**Advantages of Cold Rolling (1 Mark):**
1. Superior surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
2. Improved mechanical properties due to strain hardening (higher strength and hardness).

**Limitations of Cold Rolling (1 Mark):**
1. Higher force and power required (no softening during deformation).
2. Limited ductility — risk of cracking; intermediate annealing may be needed.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the flat rolling process. Explain the terms: draft, contact length, angle of bite, and neutral point. Derive the expression for the contact length $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$.**

**(b) A hot rolling mill has rolls of diameter 600 mm. A steel slab of initial thickness 50 mm is rolled to a final thickness of 35 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.15, and the average flow stress of the material is 200 MPa. The width of the slab is 400 mm. Calculate: (i) the contact length, (ii) the roll separating force, (iii) the rolling torque per roll, and (iv) verify if the reduction is achievable without slip.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Sketch and Process Description (3 Marks):**

The flat rolling process consists of two opposing cylindrical rolls rotating in opposite directions (top roll clockwise, bottom roll counter-clockwise, or both pulling the stock into the gap). The stock, with initial thickness $h_0$, enters the roll bite, is compressed to final thickness $h_f$, and exits with increased velocity. A figure should show:
- Two rolls with radius $R$ separated by a gap equal to $h_f$.
- Stock entering at thickness $h_0$ and exiting at thickness $h_f$.
- The contact arc from entry point to exit point.
- The neutral point marked on the contact arc.

**Key Term Definitions (2 Marks):**

- **Draft ($\Delta h$):** The reduction in thickness per pass, $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$.
- **Contact Length ($L$):** The projected horizontal length of the contact arc between the roll and the stock.
- **Angle of Bite ($\alpha$):** The half-angle of the contact arc measured at the roll center.
- **Neutral Point:** The location on the contact arc where the velocity of the roll surface exactly equals the velocity of the stock.

**Derivation of $L = \sqrt{R \Delta h}$ (2 Marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final result is:

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Roll diameter $D = 600$ mm, so $R = 300$ mm
- $h_0 = 50$ mm, $h_f = 35$ mm
- $\mu = 0.15$
- $\bar{\sigma_f} = 200$ MPa = $200$ N/mm²
- $w = 400$ mm

**(i) Contact Length (2 Marks):**

$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 50 - 35 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 15} = \sqrt{4500}
$$

$$
L = 67.08 \text{ mm} \approx 67.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Roll Separating Force (2 Marks):**

$$
P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w = 200 \times 67.08 \times 400
$$

$$
P = 5{,}366{,}400 \text{ N} \approx 5.37 \text{ MN}
$$

**(iii) Rolling Torque per Roll (2 Marks):**

$$
T = P \cdot L = 5{,}366{,}400 \times 0.0671
$$

$$
T = 360{,}085 \text{ N·m} \approx 360 \text{ kN·m}
$$

(Note: $L$ converted to metres for torque in N·m)

**(iv) Slip Verification (1 Mark):**

$$
\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R = (0.15)^2 \times 300 = 0.0225 \times 300 = 6.75 \text{ mm}
$$

Since the actual draft $\Delta h = 15$ mm $\gg$ $\Delta h_{\max} = 6.75$ mm, **the reduction is NOT achievable in a single pass** without slip.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Mismatch Trap (–2 marks):** Students often compute $T = P \cdot L$ using $L$ in mm, getting a wrong answer in the order of $10^5$ mm·N. Always convert $L$ to **metres** when the answer is required in N·m.
> - **Slip Verification Step (–1 mark):** Many students skip part (iv) and lose 1 mark. Always explicitly state whether $\Delta h < \Delta h_{\max}$ or not.
> - **Friction Coefficient Form (–1 mark):** Use $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$ (KTU standard), NOT $4\mu^2 R$ (academic form). Verify with your course instructor.
> - **Order of Magnitude Check (–0.5 mark):** A roll separating force of 5.37 MN for hot rolling of steel is realistic (industrial mills operate at 5–20 MN). If your answer is in the wrong order, recheck the calculation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) Explain the difference between hot rolling and cold rolling. Discuss the types of rolling mills (2-high, 3-high, 4-high, cluster, and continuous) with neat sketches and applications.**

**(b) In a cold rolling operation, the strip thickness is reduced from 4.0 mm to 3.2 mm using rolls of diameter 350 mm. The coefficient of friction is 0.10, and the material has a strength coefficient $K = 600$ MPa and strain hardening exponent $n = 0.20$. The width of the strip is 1200 mm, and the roll speed is 60 RPM. Calculate: (i) the true strain, (ii) the average flow stress (using $\bar{\sigma_f} = K \bar{\epsilon}^n / (1+n)$), (iii) the roll separating force, (iv) the rolling power in kW.**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Hot vs Cold Rolling Comparison (2 Marks):**

Refer to Question A2 (Part A) above for the comparison table.

**Rolling Mill Types (5 Marks):**

1. **2-High Mill (1 Mark):** Two rolls, one above the other, rotating in opposite directions. Used for **reversing mills** and small-batch production. Applications: plate rolling, roughing stands.

2. **3-High Mill (1 Mark):** Three rolls stacked vertically. The middle roll is fixed; the top and bottom rolls can be raised/lowered. The material is passed through the top-middle gap in one direction, then the middle-bottom gap in the reverse direction. **No reversing of roll rotation needed.** Applications: rail rolling, structural sections.

3. **4-High Mill (1 Mark):** Two small **work rolls** in contact with the stock, backed by two larger **backup rolls**. The backup rolls prevent deflection of the work rolls, allowing thinner gauges and tighter tolerances. Applications: **cold rolling of sheets and strips**, automotive body panels.

4. **Cluster Mill (1 Mark):** Multiple backup rolls (4, 6, or more) supporting small work rolls. Used for rolling **very thin foils** (e.g., aluminum foil at 0.005 mm). Sendzimir mill is a famous example.

5. **Continuous Mill (1 Mark):** Multiple rolling stands arranged in series. The strip passes through all stands simultaneously at high speed. Used for **mass production** of standardized products (e.g., hot strip mill with 7 stands, cold strip mill with 5 stands).

> [!TIP]
> For part (a), KTU examiners award **2 marks for the comparison**, **4 marks for mill types** (1 mark each for 2-high, 3-high, 4-high, cluster, continuous), and **1 mark for the neat sketch**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $h_0 = 4.0$ mm, $h_f = 3.2$ mm
- $D = 350$ mm, so $R = 175$ mm
- $\mu = 0.10$
- $K = 600$ MPa, $n = 0.20$
- $w = 1200$ mm
- $N = 60$ RPM

**(i) True Strain (1 Mark):**

$$
\bar{\epsilon} = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{4.0}{3.2}\right) = \ln(1.25)
$$

$$
\bar{\epsilon} = 0.2231
$$

**(ii) Average Flow Stress (2 Marks):**

$$
\bar{\sigma_f} = \frac{K \cdot \bar{\epsilon}^n}{1 + n} = \frac{600 \times (0.2231)^{0.20}}{1.20}
$$

Compute $(0.2231)^{0.20}$:

$$
(0.2231)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \cdot \ln(0.2231)} = e^{0.20 \times (-1.4996)} = e^{-0.2999} = 0.7408
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{\sigma_f} = \frac{600 \times 0.7408}{1.20} = \frac{444.5}{1.20}
$$

$$
\bar{\sigma_f} \approx 370.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Roll Separating Force (2 Marks):**

First, compute $L$:

$$
\Delta h = 4.0 - 3.2 = 0.8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{175 \times 0.8} = \sqrt{140}
$$

$$
L = 11.83 \text{ mm}
$$

Then:

$$
P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w = 370.4 \times 11.83 \times 1200
$$

$$
P = 370.4 \times 14{,}200 \approx 5{,}260{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 5.26 \text{ MN}
$$

**(iv) Rolling Power (2 Marks):**

Torque per roll:

$$
T = P \cdot L = 5{,}260{,}000 \times 0.01183 \approx 62{,}226 \text{ N·m}
$$

Power per roll (in kW):

$$
N_{p,\text{per roll}} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 60 \times 62{,}226}{60}
$$

$$
N_{p,\text{per roll}} = 2\pi \times 62{,}226 = 391{,}100 \text{ W} \approx 391.1 \text{ kW}
$$

Total power (both rolls):

$$
N_{p,\text{total}} = 2 \times 391.1 = 782.2 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Average Flow Stress Formula (–2 marks):** Students often forget to divide by $(1+n)$ and use $\bar{\sigma_f} = K \bar{\epsilon}^n$ (which is the **peak flow stress**, not the average). The average is required for the force equation.
> - **Final Strain Value (–1 mark):** $\ln(1.25) = 0.2231$ — do not use $\log_{10}$. Engineering calculations use the **natural logarithm**.
> - **Power Formula Variants (–1 mark):** Some students use $N_p = T \cdot \omega$ and forget to convert RPM to rad/s: $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. Others forget to multiply by 2 for both rolls. Be explicit.
> - **Sketch Quality in Part (a) (–1 mark):** Neat, labelled sketches with **all parts named** (work rolls, backup rolls, entry, exit, stock) score full marks. A vague block diagram with no labels loses marks.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Rolling**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Rolling is a **continuous, steady-state, bulk metal forming** process using **compressive deformation** between two counter-rotating rolls.
- ☐ **Draft** $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$ is the thickness reduction per pass.
- ☐ **Contact length** $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ — chord approximation valid for small reductions.
- ☐ **Angle of bite** $\alpha = \cos^{-1}(1 - \Delta h/D) \approx \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$.
- ☐ **Maximum draft** $\Delta h_{\max} = \mu^2 R$ (KTU standard form).
- ☐ **Neutral point** is at the mid-arc in the small-angle approximation; $\beta \approx \alpha/2$.
- ☐ **Forward slip** $s = (v_f - v_r)/v_r \approx \alpha/2$.
- ☐ **Roll separating force** $P = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot L \cdot w$.
- ☐ **Torque per roll** $T = P \cdot L$ (with $L$ in **metres**).
- ☐ **Power** $N_p = 2\pi N T / 60$ in kW (multiply by 2 for both rolls).

### Hot vs Cold Rolling
- ☐ **Hot rolling:** $T > 0.6 T_m$, large reductions, rough surface, no strain hardening.
- ☐ **Cold rolling:** room temperature, tight tolerances, bright finish, strain hardening, intermediate annealing often needed.

### Rolling Mill Types (KTU Board Favorite)
- ☐ **2-High:** simplest, reversing operation.
- ☐ **3-High:** three rolls, no reversing of rotation.
- ☐ **4-High:** small work rolls + large backup rolls → thin gauges.
- ☐ **Cluster:** multiple backup rolls → foils.
- ☐ **Continuous:** multiple stands in series → mass production.

### Common Defects
- ☐ **Wavy edges** — uneven roll pressure, incorrect roll crown.
- ☐ **Alligatoring** — split in the middle of the strip (excessive draft).
- ☐ **Center burst (piping)** — internal fracture from high hydrostatic tension.
- ☐ **Barrelling** — convex side walls in ring rolling.
- ☐ **Scale marks** — surface oxide from hot rolling.

### Common Numerical Traps
- ☐ **Unit conversions:** $L$ in mm for force, in metres for torque.
- ☐ **Slip verification:** Always check $\Delta h < \mu^2 R$.
- ☐ **Flow stress formula:** $\bar{\sigma_f} = K \bar{\epsilon}^n / (1 + n)$ for the **average**, not the peak.
- ☐ **Power formula:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (not just $N$).
- ☐ **Factor of 2 in torque:** Torque is per roll; power is for both rolls combined.

### Engineering Constants to Remember
- ☐ $\mu$ (hot rolling) = $0.1$ to $0.2$
- ☐ $\mu$ (cold rolling) = $0.05$ to $0.1$
- ☐ $K$ (mild steel) ≈ $500$ MPa, $n \approx 0.20$
- ☐ Hot rolling temperature for steel ≈ $1200$ °C
- ☐ Typical forward slip ≈ $2\%$ to $8\%$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE typically asks **one 14-mark question** from Module 3 in the **Part B section** (Section B — internal choice). The question is almost always a **numerical problem** on contact length, roll separating force, torque, and power. Practice at least 5 numerical problems covering different combinations of given parameters. The 3-mark Part A question is usually a **definition or comparison** (angle of bite, forward slip, hot vs cold rolling).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theory of Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Rolling** is a metal forming process in which the workpiece (a metallic stock) is passed through one or more pairs of **rotating rolls** to reduce its thickness, refine its cross-section, alter its mechanical properties, or impart a desired shape. The compressive force applied by the rolls is generated entirely by the **frictional traction** between the roll surface and the workpiece surface, which drags the material into the roll gap (the **deformation zone**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBAUT404 – Module 3):**
> "Rolling is a compressive deformation process in which the thickness of a metal stock is reduced by the squeezing action of two opposing rotating cylindrical rolls. The thickness reduction per pass is called the **draft** (Δh), and the operation is classified as **Hot Rolling** when performed above the recrystallization temperature and **Cold Rolling** when performed below it."

### 1.2 Key Nomenclature

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Initial thickness | $h_0$ | Thickness of workpiece **before** entering the rolls |
| Final thickness | $h_f$ | Thickness of workpiece **after** exiting the rolls |
| Draft | $\Delta h$ | $h_0 - h_f$ (thickness reduction per pass) |
| Roll radius | $R$ | Radius of the working rolls |
| Width | $w$ | Width of the workpiece (perpendicular to rolling direction) |
| Coefficient of friction | $\mu$ | Friction coefficient between roll and workpiece |
| Angle of contact | $\alpha$ | Total arc subtended by the deformation zone on the roll |
| Neutral angle | $\phi$ | Angle from roll center to the **neutral point** (no-slip point) |
| Forward slip | $s$ | Relative velocity difference between roll and exiting strip |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Think of it like this — the "rolling-pin" analogy:**
> Imagine you are flattening a lump of dough with a rolling pin. You push the pin forward, and friction between the pin and dough *drags* the dough into the thin gap beneath the pin. The dough that comes out the other side is **thinner but longer** — the metal has flowed, not just been squeezed. Now, in industrial rolling, the "rolling pin" is a giant steel cylinder rotating at high speed, and the "dough" is a glowing red-hot slab of steel. The friction does the work of pulling the steel in, and the rolls apply enormous compressive force to permanently deform it.

### 1.4 Classification of Rolling Processes (KTU Sub-Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two primary classification axes for KTU 2024 Module 3:**

**Axis 1 — By Temperature:**
- **Hot Rolling:** Performed **above** the recrystallization temperature (typically $>0.6\,T_m$). Lower flow stress, higher ductility, removes casting defects, no work hardening. Surface finish is rough (scale formation).
- **Cold Rolling:** Performed **below** the recrystallization temperature. Higher flow stress, better surface finish, tighter dimensional tolerance, induces **strain hardening** (increase in yield strength). May require intermediate **annealing** for large total reductions.

**Axis 2 — By Geometry / Product:**
- **Flat Rolling:** Reduces thickness of slabs, plates, sheets, strips. The rolls are cylindrical.
- **Shape Rolling (Profile Rolling):** Uses shaped rolls (grooved) to produce structural sections like I-beams, channels, rails.
- **Ring Rolling:** Expands the diameter of a ring-shaped workpiece.
- **Thread Rolling:** Forms external threads by rolling between flat dies.

### 1.5 Rolling Mill Configuration

The arrangement of rolls in a rolling mill is fundamental to understanding the mechanics of the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mill Types (KTU Required):**
> - **2-High Mill:** Two rolls, one above the other. Reversing or non-reversing.
> - **3-High Mill:** Three rolls stacked vertically; workpiece passes in one direction between the lower & middle rolls, returns between middle & upper rolls.
> - **4-High Mill:** Two small **work rolls** supported by two larger **back-up rolls** to prevent work-roll deflection.
> - **Cluster (Sendzimir / Z-Mill):** Multiple back-up rolls surrounding small work rolls — used for very thin foil and high-strength alloys.
> - **Tandem Mill:** Multiple 4-high stands in series for continuous reduction of strip.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of Rolling — Step by Step

When a workpiece enters the gap between two rotating rolls, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Entry (Bite Angle Condition):** The roll must "bite" the workpiece. For biting to occur, the **angle of contact $\alpha$** must satisfy:
$$\alpha \le \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$
This is called the **"no-slip" condition** or **"bite condition"**. If $\mu$ is too low, the roll will simply skid on top of the workpiece without drawing it in.

2. **Frictional Drag:** Once bitten, the roll surface moves in the rolling direction at velocity $v_r = \pi D N / 60$. The friction between roll and workpiece drags material into the deformation zone.

3. **Deformation Zone:** The material between the entry and exit planes is undergoing plastic deformation. The roll surface moves **faster** than the workpiece material at the entry (so friction pulls the material in — *backward slip*). The roll surface moves **slower** than the workpiece material at the exit (so friction now pulls the material back — *forward slip*).

4. **Neutral Point (No-Slip Point):** There is one location where the **roll surface velocity equals the horizontal component of the material velocity**. This is the **neutral point** at angle $\phi$ from the roll centerline.

5. **Exit:** The material exits thinner and faster (in length per unit time) than the entry length rate. Volume constancy ensures:
$$h_0 \cdot L_0 = h_f \cdot L_f$$
where $L_0$ and $L_f$ are the original and final lengths (in time $t$).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Theory of Rolling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Memorize this table for KTU 2024 examinations. All questions on Rolling derive from these equations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_r = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Roll surface velocity (peripheral speed) | m/s |
| 2 | $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{D}$ or $\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R}$ | Geometric relation for total angle of contact | radians |
| 3 | $\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}}$ (small angle approx.) | Approximate angle of contact (when $\alpha$ is small) | radians |
| 4 | $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | **Projected contact length** (arc projected on rolling direction) | m |
| 5 | $L = \sqrt{R(h_0 - h_f)}$ | Same as above, written with explicit h | m |
| 6 | $\mu \ge \tan\alpha$ | **Bite condition** (no-slip condition) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$ | **Forward slip** (fractional) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{R}{h_f}\,\phi^2$ | Forward slip in terms of neutral angle $\phi$ (Ekelund approx.) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P = Y_{avg} \cdot A = Y_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w$ | **Rolling Load / Roll Separating Force** | N |
| 10 | $T = P \cdot L \cdot \cos(\alpha/2) \approx P \cdot L$ | **Torque** per roll | N·m |
| 11 | $\text{Power} = 2\pi N T$ | Required rolling power (W) | W |
| 12 | $\text{Power} = P \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N$ | Power in terms of rolling load | W |
| 13 | $p_{max} \approx \dfrac{2P}{\pi L w}$ | **Hitchcock** approximation of peak pressure | Pa |
| 14 | $Y_{avg} = \dfrac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2}$ | Average flow stress (for small reduction) | Pa |
| 15 | $Y_{avg} \approx \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{K \cdot \epsilon^{n}}{\epsilon} \cdot [\text{integrated}]$ | For large reductions using Hollomon power law | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation warning:** In all KTU answers, $D$ = roll **diameter** and $R$ = roll **radius**. Many students lose marks by mixing the two. Watch the cosine relation: $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/D$ (using diameter) vs. $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/(2R)$ (using radius) — both are identical, just different forms.

### 2.3 Why the Friction Hill Exists — Deep Insight

As the workpiece enters the roll gap, the reduction in thickness causes the material to flow laterally (spreading) and longitudinally. The pressure required to deform the metal is **not uniform** along the contact arc — it is **highest at the neutral point** and tapers off on either side. This is called the **friction hill**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical reason:** Before the neutral point, friction acts in the rolling direction (pulling material in) and *helps* the deformation → lower pressure. After the neutral point, friction acts against the exit direction → higher pressure. The peak is at the neutral point.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rolling is the **backbone of the steel, aluminum, and copper industries**. Over **90% of all steel products** (sheets, plates, structural sections, rails) undergo rolling at some stage. The theory of rolling taught here directly enables:
- **Process design:** Selecting roll diameter, number of passes, and rolling speed.
- **Mill design:** Sizing motors, bearings, and mill housings.
- **Quality control:** Predicting flatness, surface finish, and dimensional accuracy.
- **Defect prevention:** Predicting and avoiding roll flattening, spring-back, and edge cracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Angle of Contact ($\alpha$) and Projected Contact Length ($L$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 3. Expect it as a 7-mark question.

**Given:** A cylindrical roll of radius $R$ reduces a workpiece from thickness $h_0$ to thickness $h_f$. The draft is $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$.

**Setup:** Consider a vertical cross-section through the roll center. The roll and workpiece first contact at point **A** (entry) and last contact at point **B** (exit). Let O be the roll center.

**Step 1 — Draw the geometry.**
The line OB is horizontal (from roll center to exit point). The arc length AB on the roll corresponds to the deformed zone. Let $\alpha$ be the angle AOB (the **angle of contact**).

**Step 2 — Locate the entry point A.**
From the geometry, point A is at height $h_0/2$ above the centerline (since the entry thickness is $h_0$ and the rolls are symmetric). The vertical distance from O down to the entry plane is $R - h_0/2$. The horizontal distance from O to point A is the projected contact length $L$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Pythagorean theorem in triangle formed by O, A, and the foot of perpendicular from A to OB.**
The hypotenuse OA = $R$. The vertical drop from A to the centerline = $R - h_0/2$. The horizontal leg = $L$.

$$L^2 + \left(R - \dfrac{h_0}{2}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify.**

$$L^2 = R^2 - \left(R - \dfrac{h_0}{2}\right)^2$$

$$L^2 = R^2 - \left(R^2 - R h_0 + \dfrac{h_0^2}{4}\right)$$

$$L^2 = R h_0 - \dfrac{h_0^2}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Write the same relation for the exit point B.**
For the exit, the vertical drop is $R - h_f/2$. Doing the same algebra:

$$L^2 = R h_f - \dfrac{h_f^2}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $L$.**
The $L$ must be the same. Subtract the two expressions:

$$R h_0 - \dfrac{h_0^2}{4} = R h_f - \dfrac{h_f^2}{4}$$

$$R(h_0 - h_f) = \dfrac{h_0^2 - h_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{(h_0-h_f)(h_0+h_f)}{4}$$

$$R = \dfrac{h_0 + h_f}{4} + \text{(geometric mean correction)}$$

**Step 7 — Use the simpler standard form.** Since $h_0^2/4$ and $h_f^2/4$ are small compared to $R h_0$ and $R h_f$ (because $h_0, h_f \ll R$ in practice), we approximate:

$$L \approx \sqrt{R(h_0 - h_f)} = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$

**Step 8 — Derive the angle of contact.**

$$\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{2R} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The angle of contact depends ONLY on the draft and the roll radius — not on the material properties, friction, or speed. This is purely a geometric result.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Slip ($s$)

**Step 1 — Define forward slip.** Forward slip is the fractional difference between the workpiece exit velocity $v_f$ and the roll surface velocity $v_r$:

$$s = \dfrac{v_f - v_r}{v_r}$$

**Step 2 — Apply volume constancy.** Consider a unit time interval. Volume of metal entering the roll gap per second = Volume leaving per second.

$$h_0 \cdot w \cdot v_0 = h_f \cdot w \cdot v_f$$

where $v_0$ is the entry velocity of the workpiece.

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic constraint at the neutral point.**
At the neutral point, the roll surface velocity (horizontal component) equals the material velocity. The material moves along a curved path but its horizontal component at any angle $\theta$ from the exit plane is:

$$v_{mat}(\theta) = v_r \cos\theta$$

At the entry ($\theta = \alpha$): $v_{mat} = v_r \cos\alpha < v_r$ (backward slip exists).
At the neutral point ($\theta = \phi$): $v_{mat} = v_r \cos\phi = v_r$ only if $\phi = 0$ — but more precisely, this is the velocity-matching point.

**Step 4 — Final forward slip formula (Ekelund approximation for small angles).**

$$s = \dfrac{R}{h_f}\phi^2$$

where $\phi$ is the half-angle of contact (angle from exit plane to neutral point), measured in radians. For typical cold rolling, $s \approx 0.03$ to $0.10$ (3% to 10%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The forward slip is the reason the actual roll speed must be set LOWER than the desired strip exit speed. If you want strip to exit at $v_f$, set the roll so that $v_r = v_f / (1+s)$. This is heavily tested numerically.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rolling Load (Separating Force)

**Step 1 — Define the rolling load.** The rolling load $P$ is the **total vertical force** exerted by the workpiece on the rolls (or equivalently, by the rolls on the workpiece). It equals the integral of the vertical pressure $p(\theta)$ over the projected contact area:

$$P = w \int_{0}^{L} p(\theta) \, dL$$

**Step 2 — Simplifying assumption (Hitchcock approximation).** For a first-order estimate, assume a uniform pressure equal to the **average flow stress** $Y_{avg}$ over the projected contact area $A = L \cdot w$:

$$P = Y_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w$$

**Step 3 — Estimate the average flow stress.** For materials that work-harden (most metals), the flow stress increases as the material is reduced in thickness. The average flow stress between the entry and exit is:

$$Y_{avg} = \dfrac{Y_0 + Y_f}{2} \quad \text{(for small reductions)}$$

For larger reductions with a Hollomon-type material ($Y = K\epsilon^n$):

$$Y_{avg} = \dfrac{K \epsilon^{n}}{n+1} \cdot \text{(integrated form)}$$

**Step 4 — Estimate the peak pressure (Hitchcock's equation).** The pressure distribution is approximately a half-sine wave. The peak pressure is:

$$p_{max} \approx \dfrac{\pi}{4} Y_{avg} \approx \dfrac{2P}{\pi L w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** The rolling load per unit width $P/w$ for hot rolling of steel is typically **5,000 – 15,000 N/mm** and for cold rolling is **5,000 – 25,000 N/mm**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torque and Power

**Step 1 — Torque on one roll.** The rolling force $P$ acts vertically through the workpiece, but it is transmitted to the roll through friction on the contact arc. The effective lever arm of the friction force about the roll center is approximately $L \cos(\alpha/2)$. For small $\alpha$, $\cos(\alpha/2) \approx 1$, so:

$$T = P \cdot L \cos(\alpha/2) \approx P \cdot L$$

Since there are two rolls, the total torque on the motor shaft is $2T$.

**Step 2 — Power requirement.**

$$\text{Power} = 2\pi N \cdot T_{total} = 2\pi N \cdot 2T = 2 \cdot 2\pi N \cdot P L \cos(\alpha/2)$$

For small $\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\text{Power} = 2 \pi N \cdot P \cdot L}$$

where $N$ is in rev/s. If $N$ is in rpm, then:

$$\text{Power} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot P \cdot L \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometry of the Rolling Process (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **geometry, deformation zone, and velocity field** of the flat rolling process in a structured flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Entry: thickness h0, velocity v0] --> B{Friction Traction Acts}
    B --> C[Entry Plane: Roll surface faster than workpiece - BACKWARD SLIP zone]
    C --> D[Deformation Zone: thickness reduces from h0 to hf, plastic flow occurs]
    D --> E[Neutral Point N: roll surface velocity equals workpiece velocity - no slip]
    E --> F[Exit Plane: roll surface slower than workpiece - FORWARD SLIP zone]
    F --> G[Workpiece Exit: thickness hf, velocity vf greater than vr]
    G --> H[Forward Slip s = vf - vr over vr, typically 3 to 10 percent]
    H --> I[Length Increases: Volume Constancy h0 L0 = hf Lf]
    I --> J[Width Spreads: slight increase in width w]
    J --> K[Final Flat Strip Product]

    L[Roll Parameters] --> M[Roll Radius R, Roll Diameter D]
    M --> N[Angle of Contact alpha equals square root of delta h over R]
    N --> O[Projected Contact Length L equals square root of R times delta h]
    O --> P[Bite Condition: mu greater than or equal to tan alpha]

    Q[Forces and Torque] --> R[Roll Separating Force P equals Yavg times L times w]
    R --> S[Torque per roll T equals P times L]
    S --> T[Power equals 2 pi N times 2T]
```

### 4.2 Rolling Process State Diagram — Hot vs Cold Rolling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOT["HOT ROLLING Above Recrystallization Temperature"]
        H1[Slab from Caster] --> H2[Reheating Furnace 1100 to 1250 C]
        H2 --> H3[Roughing Mill: large draft per pass]
        H3 --> H4[Finishing Mill: multiple stands in tandem]
        H4 --> H5[Coiling at 600 to 700 C]
    end

    subgraph COLD["COLD ROLLING Room Temperature"]
        C1[Pickled Hot Rolled Strip] --> C2[Tandem Cold Mill 5 or 6 stands]
        C2 --> C3[Interstage Annealing for ductility recovery]
        C3 --> C4[Final Reduction: 50 to 90 percent total]
        C4 --> C5[Skin Pass or Temper Mill for surface finish]
    end

    HOT --> COLD
    H5 --> C1
    C5 --> Z[Final Cold Rolled Sheet for Automotive Appliance Construction]
```

### 4.3 Friction Hill and Pressure Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entry Plane alpha position] --> B[Pressure increases as material is dragged in]
    B --> C[Pressure reaches maximum at NEUTRAL POINT phi]
    C --> D[Pressure decreases as material is ejected on exit side]
    D --> E[Exit Plane: pressure equals Yf - back tension if any]

    F[Concept: Friction Hill] --> G[Peak Pressure pmax about 2P over pi L w]
    G --> H[Average Pressure equals P over L w equals Yavg]
    H --> I[Integral under curve equals total Rolling Load P]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The pressure distribution along the arc of contact resembles an asymmetric "friction hill" — it rises from the entry, peaks at the neutral point, and tapers off at the exit. The **area under this curve**, when multiplied by the strip width $w$, gives the total rolling load $P$.

### 4.4 Mill Stand Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rolling Mill Types] --> B[2 High Mill: 2 rolls, simple reversing or non reversing]
    A --> C[3 High Mill: 3 rolls, workpiece passes back and forth]
    A --> D[4 High Mill: 2 small work rolls plus 2 large backup rolls]
    A --> E[Cluster Mill or Sendzimir Mill: multiple backup rolls for foil]
    A --> F[Tandem Mill: series of 4 high stands for continuous strip production]

    B --> G[Used for: roughing, breakdown passes]
    C --> H[Used for: plate, section rolling]
    D --> I[Used for: sheet, strip, plate]
    E --> J[Used for: stainless steel foil, silicon steel]
    F --> K[Used for: automotive sheet, tin plate, aluminum can stock]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "draft" in rolling. What is meant by "angle of contact"? How is the angle of contact related to the bite condition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Draft (1 mark):** Draft is the reduction in thickness achieved in a single rolling pass, expressed as $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$, where $h_0$ is the entry thickness and $h_f$ is the exit thickness. It is the primary process variable in flat rolling.
- **Angle of contact (1 mark):** The angle of contact $\alpha$ is the angle subtended at the roll center by the arc of contact between the roll and the workpiece. It is the total angular extent of the deformation zone as seen from the roll axis.
- **Bite condition (1 mark):** For the roll to successfully grip and draw the workpiece into the gap, the angle of contact must satisfy: $\mu \ge \tan\alpha$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between roll and workpiece. If friction is insufficient, the roll will skid on the surface without biting.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. Distinguish between hot rolling and cold rolling. State any three differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Performed **above** the recrystallization temperature ($>0.6\,T_m$) | Performed **below** the recrystallization temperature |
| 2 | No work hardening; ductility is high | Significant work hardening; higher strength but lower ductility |
| 3 | Surface finish is rough due to oxide scale | Surface finish is excellent; tight dimensional tolerances |
| 4 | Larger drafts per pass possible (e.g., 25–50 mm) | Smaller drafts per pass (e.g., 0.5–2 mm) |
| 5 | Rolling load is lower | Rolling load is higher (often 2–3× of hot rolling) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3, 14 Marks]**

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the relationship between the **angle of contact**, **roll radius**, and **draft** in flat rolling. Also derive the expression for the **projected contact length**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel strip 25 mm thick is rolled in one pass to 20 mm thick in a mill with rolls of diameter 600 mm. The coefficient of friction between the rolls and the strip is 0.10. Determine: (i) The angle of contact, (ii) The projected contact length, (iii) Whether the rolling is feasible (bite condition), (iv) The rolling load if the average flow stress is 280 MPa and the strip width is 200 mm, (v) The torque and power required if the rolls rotate at 60 rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Refer to Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 above. Full marks are awarded for:*
1. Clear labeled diagram showing roll, entry, exit, neutral point, angle of contact (1 mark).
2. Setting up the Pythagorean relation $L^2 + (R - h_0/2)^2 = R^2$ (2 marks).
3. Algebraic simplification to obtain $L^2 = R h_0 - h_0^2/4$ (1 mark).
4. Final expressions: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ and $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/(2R)$ (3 marks).

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- $h_0 = 25$ mm, $h_f = 20$ mm
- $D = 600$ mm $\Rightarrow R = 300$ mm
- $\mu = 0.10$
- $Y_{avg} = 280$ MPa $= 280$ N/mm²
- $w = 200$ mm
- $N = 60$ rpm

**Step 1 — Calculate the draft.**
$$\Delta h = h_0 - h_f = 25 - 20 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the angle of contact $\alpha$ (1 mark).**
$$\cos\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta h}{D} = 1 - \dfrac{5}{600} = 1 - 0.00833 = 0.99167$$
$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.99167) = 0.0811 \text{ rad} \approx 4.65°$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the projected contact length $L$ (1 mark).**
$$L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{300 \times 5} = \sqrt{1500} = 38.73 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Check the bite condition (1 mark).**
$$\tan\alpha = \tan(0.0811) = 0.0812$$
$$\mu = 0.10 \ge 0.0812 = \tan\alpha$$
The bite condition is satisfied — rolling is feasible.

**Step 5 — Calculate the rolling load $P$ (1.5 marks).**
$$P = Y_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w = 280 \times 38.73 \times 200 = 2,168,880 \text{ N} \approx 2.17 \text{ MN}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the torque per roll (1 mark).**
$$T = P \cdot L = 2,168,880 \times 38.73 = 84.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 84.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

Wait — re-check units: $P$ is in N, $L$ is in mm, so $T$ is in N·mm.
$$T = 2,168,880 \text{ N} \times 38.73 \text{ mm} = 83,999,322 \text{ N·mm} \approx 84.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the power (1.5 marks).**
$$\text{Power} = 2\pi N \cdot T = 2\pi \times \dfrac{60}{60} \times 84.0 \times 10^3 = 2\pi \times 84.0 = 527.8 \text{ kW}$$

(using $N$ in rev/s $= 1$ rev/s)

$$\boxed{\text{Final Answers: } \alpha = 4.65°, \ L = 38.73 \text{ mm}, \ P = 2.17 \text{ MN}, \ T = 84.0 \text{ kN·m}, \ \text{Power} = 527.8 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Explain the concept of **forward slip** and the **neutral point** in rolling. Derive the relationship $s = (R/h_f)\phi^2$ for small angles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **friction hill** concept. How does the coefficient of friction affect (i) the rolling load, (ii) the maximum possible draft, and (iii) the angle of contact at the neutral point? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Forward Slip & Neutral Point (7 marks):**

1. **Definitions (2 marks):** The neutral point is the location on the roll-workpiece interface where the roll surface velocity equals the horizontal component of the workpiece velocity — i.e., there is no relative slip. Forward slip $s$ is the fractional difference between the workpiece exit velocity $v_f$ and the roll surface velocity $v_r$ at the exit: $s = (v_f - v_r)/v_r$.

2. **Physical picture (2 marks):** Before the neutral point, friction pulls the workpiece in (roll surface faster than material — *backward slip*). After the neutral point, friction acts opposite to the material motion (roll surface slower — *forward slip*). At the neutral point itself, there is no slip.

3. **Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.2 above. The key steps are:
   - Volume constancy: $h_0 w v_0 = h_f w v_f$.
   - Kinematic relation along the arc: at angle $\theta$ from exit, $v_{mat}(\theta) = v_r \cos\theta$.
   - Apply the small-angle expansion $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \phi^2/2$ to obtain the linear velocity mismatch, then integrate to get $s = (R/h_f)\phi^2$.

**Model Solution (b) — Friction Hill (7 marks):**

1. **Friction Hill definition (2 marks):** The friction hill is the pressure distribution curve along the arc of contact. The pressure is maximum at the neutral point and tapers on either side, resembling a hill. The area under the curve, multiplied by the width, equals the total rolling load.

2. **Effect on rolling load (2 marks):** A higher $\mu$ means friction can sustain a higher pressure gradient before slip occurs. The peak pressure $p_{max}$ increases, increasing the rolling load. However, the projected length $L$ is independent of $\mu$ (purely geometric), so the load scales roughly as $p_{max} \cdot L \cdot w$.

3. **Effect on maximum draft (1.5 marks):** From the bite condition $\mu \ge \tan\alpha$ and $\alpha \approx \sqrt{\Delta h/R}$, the maximum draft is:
$$\Delta h_{max} = R \cdot \mu^2$$
A higher $\mu$ allows a larger draft per pass.

4. **Effect on neutral angle (1.5 marks):** A higher $\mu$ shifts the neutral point **toward the entry** (larger $\phi$), increasing the forward slip $s = (R/h_f)\phi^2$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks in the Theory of Rolling:**

1. **Mixing up $D$ and $R$ (–2 marks):** Always check whether the formula uses diameter or radius. $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/D$ uses diameter. $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/(2R)$ uses radius.

2. **Forgetting the bite condition (–1 to –2 marks):** A numerical answer without a feasibility check is incomplete. Always verify $\mu \ge \tan\alpha$ after computing $\alpha$.

3. **Units in torque/power calculation (–1 mark):** $L$ in mm, $P$ in N, $N$ in rpm. Remember to divide $N$ by 60 when computing power in Watts, or keep $N$ in rev/s. Mixing these up gives power off by a factor of 60.

4. **Forward slip direction confusion (–1 mark):** Forward slip is **always positive** (workpiece exits faster than roll surface in the absence of constraint). Some students get confused with backward slip (entry side).

5. **Confusing rolling load with bearing load (–1 mark):** The rolling load is the vertical force on the rolls. The actual load on mill bearings is $P/2$ per bearing (for a 2-high mill).

6. **Not specifying the assumption of uniform pressure (–1 mark):** When using $P = Y_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w$, state the Hitchcock assumption explicitly. For more accurate calculations, mention the friction hill correction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Theory of Rolling:**

- ✅ Rolling is a **compressive** deformation process driven by **friction**.
- ✅ Draft $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$; Reduction $\% = (\Delta h / h_0) \times 100$.
- ✅ Angle of contact: $\cos\alpha = 1 - \Delta h/D = 1 - \Delta h/(2R)$.
- ✅ Projected contact length: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ (most-tested formula).
- ✅ Bite condition: $\mu \ge \tan\alpha$. Without it, the roll cannot grip the workpiece.
- ✅ Forward slip: $s = (v_f - v_r)/v_r \approx (R/h_f)\phi^2$. Always positive; typically 3–10% in cold rolling.
- ✅ Neutral point: location of zero relative slip; pressure peak (friction hill).
- ✅ Rolling load: $P = Y_{avg} \cdot L \cdot w$ (Hitchcock approximation).
- ✅ Peak pressure: $p_{max} \approx (2P)/(\pi L w) \approx (\pi/4)Y_{avg}$.
- ✅ Torque per roll: $T \approx P \cdot L$ (for small $\alpha$).
- ✅ Power: $\text{Power} = 2\pi N \cdot 2T = 2\pi N \cdot P \cdot L$ (W, with $N$ in rev/s).
- ✅ Hot rolling: $T > T_{recryst}$, no work hardening, rough surface, lower force.
- ✅ Cold rolling: $T < T_{recryst}$, work hardening, good surface, higher force, tighter tolerance.
- ✅ Mill types: 2-high, 3-high, 4-high, cluster (Sendzimir), tandem — know at least the first three.
- ✅ Maximum draft: $\Delta h_{max} = R\mu^2$ (from the bite condition).
- ✅ The **friction hill** explains why the peak pressure is at the neutral point, not the entry or exit.
- ✅ Volume constancy: $h_0 L_0 = h_f L_f$ — used in many numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Rolling Mills; Drawing and Its Types - Wire Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Rolling Mills; Drawing and Its Types - Wire Drawing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Rolling Mills — Formal Definition

A **rolling mill** is a machine unit that plastically deforms a metal workpiece by passing it between two counter-rotating cylindrical rolls, which apply compressive forces to reduce the cross-sectional area, change the shape, or improve the mechanical properties of the metal. The arrangement, number, size, and function of the rolls in the mill stand define the **mill type**, each engineered for a specific product range (blooms, slabs, plates, sheets, strips, bars, rails, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to **classify rolling mills by their stand configuration** (two-high, three-high, four-high, cluster, tandem, planetary) and explain the **industrial rationale** behind each configuration — i.e., *why a particular mill geometry is chosen for a particular product.*

## 1.2 Drawing — Formal Definition

**Drawing** is a metal forming process in which a workpiece (typically wire, rod, tube, or bar) is **pulled** through a **die** of smaller cross-section, thereby plastically reducing its cross-sectional area while increasing its length. The tensile force is applied on the exit (die-outlet) side of the workpiece, making drawing fundamentally different from extrusion (where compressive force is applied on the entry side).

**Wire drawing** is the specific sub-process in which a metal wire is reduced in diameter by being pulled through a converging die made of hardened tool steel, tungsten carbide, or, for fine wire, diamond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** The reduction in a single pass is typically expressed as a **percentage reduction in area (%RA)**, given by:
> $$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$
> where $A_0$ is the original area and $A_f$ is the final area after the pass.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### 🍪 Rolling Mill Analogy — The Dough Roller
Imagine rolling out chapati dough with a wooden pin — the pin compresses the dough and makes it thinner and longer. A **rolling mill** is the industrial-scale, mechanized version of this exact process. The **two rolls** are your wooden pin, the **roll gap** is the thickness of dough you want, and the **friction between the rolls and dough** is what actually *pulls* the dough into the gap (a phenomenon called "no-slip rolling"). A **four-high mill** is like using a smaller, more precise pin cushioned in your palm — the smaller rolls give finer thickness control, the larger rolls behind them provide the structural strength to apply huge forces.

### 🧵 Drawing Analogy — Pulling Squeezed Toothpaste
Take a partially-flattened toothpaste tube and pull the flattened end through a small ring — the cross-section shrinks and the length increases. That is **drawing**. In **wire drawing**, instead of squeezing with a clamp, the wire is *pulled* through a precisely-shaped hardened die. The **die** acts as the small ring, the **capstan** (drum) on the exit side acts as your hand pulling the wire, and the **lubricant** (soap, oil, or dry-film phosphate coating) reduces friction just like a wet toothpaste tube glides smoothly.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this one-liner for KTU exams:** "In rolling, the rolls **push** the metal; in drawing, the metal is **pulled** through a die."

## 1.4 Why These Processes Are Studied Together

Both rolling and drawing are **bulk deformation processes** characterized by:
- **State of stress:** predominantly compressive (rolling) or tensile (drawing) superimposed on compressive die-contact stresses.
- **Mass conservation:** $A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$ (volume constancy for plastic deformation).
- **Strain-hardening (work-hardening):** successive passes increase the flow stress $Y_f$, requiring progressively higher forces.

---

## 1.5 Quick-Reference Classification Snapshot

| Category | Examples | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| Rolling Mills (By Configuration) | 2-high, 3-high, 4-high, Cluster, Tandem, Planetary | Sheets, strips, plates, rails, blooms, slabs |
| Drawing (By Product Form) | Wire drawing, Bar drawing, Tube drawing | Electrical wires, cables, fasteners, seamless tubes |
| Drawing (By Process Detail) | Single-die, Multi-die, Wet drawing, Dry drawing | Fine to coarse diameter range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Standard friction coefficient in wire drawing: $\mu \approx 0.05$ (well-lubricated) to $\mu \approx 0.10$ (dry/poorly-lubricated).
> - Typical die semi-angle for wire drawing: $\alpha = 6°$ to $15°$ (optimum around $6°$ to $8°$ for steel, lower for soft metals).
> - Maximum safe die pressure (for tool steel dies on steel wire): $P_{max} \approx 2 Y_f$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Rolling Mills

### 2.1.1 Two-High Rolling Mill

This is the **simplest** configuration, consisting of **two rolls** mounted horizontally on a stand, rotating in opposite directions.

**Variants:**

**(a) Two-High Reversing Mill:**
- The direction of rotation of the rolls is **reversed** after each pass.
- Used for **small batch production** and for rolling **large ingots** (blooms, slabs, billets).
- **Advantage:** No restriction on workpiece length.
- **Disadvantage:** Idle time during reversal reduces productivity.
- *Example:* A **blooming mill** in a steel plant.

**(b) Two-High Non-Reversing (or "Pull-Over") Mill:**
- Rolls rotate in **one fixed direction**.
- The workpiece is **passed back over the top roll** to undergo a second reduction (hence "pull-over").
- Used in small workshops and rolling **lead, gold, silver** (very soft metals).
- *Limitation:* Unsuitable for hard metals due to handle-back issues.

### 2.1.2 Three-High Rolling Mill

- **Three rolls** are stacked vertically in a single stand: the top and bottom rolls rotate in the same direction as each other, while the middle roll rotates in the opposite direction.
- The workpiece is rolled in **one gap**, then passes through a **return pass** through the lower gap (and so on).
- **Direction of travel alternates** without reversing the rolls — this is a **non-reversing mill** with multiple passes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **stand height is three times the roll diameter**, which causes significant elastic deflection of the mill housing. Three-high mills are therefore **limited to lower rolling forces**, making them suitable for rolling **light sections, bars, and small billets**, but unsuitable for wide flat products.

### 2.1.4 Four-High Rolling Mill

This is the **workhorse of the modern sheet and strip industry**. It uses **four rolls**:
- **Two small-diameter work rolls** in the centre (in contact with the workpiece).
- **Two large-diameter backup rolls** on the outside (supporting the work rolls).

**Why this configuration dominates:**
- The **small work rolls** reduce the contact area, lowering the **rolling force** (since $F \approx P_{avg} \times A_{contact}$).
- The small rolls also reduce the **flattening effect** at the roll-workpiece interface, giving **better thickness accuracy**.
- The **large backup rolls** prevent the work rolls from bending elastically under the rolling load, ensuring **flatness** of the rolled product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use:** Four-high mills are the standard for **cold rolling of sheet steel, aluminium, and stainless steel strips** down to thicknesses of **0.1 mm or less**.

### 2.1.5 Cluster Rolling Mill (Sendzimir Mill / Z-Mill)

A **Sendzimir mill** (also called a Z-mill) is an extreme version of the four-high concept, used for **rolling very thin strips and foils** with exceptionally tight thickness tolerances.

- The **two work rolls** (very small, ~50 mm diameter) are backed by **two intermediate rolls**, which in turn are backed by a cluster of **backup rolls** arranged radially around them.
- The backup rolls transmit load directly to the mill housing via a **fixed bearing arrangement** (cluster of 8 to 20 rolls).

**Industrial Applications:**
- Rolling of **stainless steel, silicon steel, and special alloys** to thicknesses of **0.0025 mm (2.5 µm)**.
- Used in the production of **battery foils, transformer laminations, and aerospace alloys**.

### 2.1.6 Tandem Rolling Mill

A **tandem mill** consists of **two or more four-high or two-high stands arranged in series**, with the strip passing continuously from one stand to the next.

- Each successive stand reduces the strip thickness by a small increment.
- The total reduction in a single pass through the entire mill line can be substantial (e.g., 90% in 5 stands).
- The strip is under **tension** between stands, maintained by coiler/decoiler systems and bridles.

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Hot strip mills** for steel and aluminium.
- **Cold strip mills** for thin sheet and tinplate production.

### 2.1.7 Planetary Rolling Mill

A **planetary mill** consists of **two large backing rolls** surrounded by a large number of **small planetary work rolls** (typically 20 to 24) arranged in a cage.

- The strip is fed between the backing rolls, and each small roll performs a small reduction as it rolls around the circumference of the backing rolls.
- The strip undergoes **multiple micro-reductions** in a single pass — equivalent to a 10–15 stand tandem mill in one stand.

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Hot reducing** of slabs directly to coil in a single operation.
- Used in **breakdown of large ingots** with very high total reductions.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Comparison for KTU:**
> - **Two-high** → Simple, slow, large workpieces.
> - **Three-high** → Multiple passes without reversing, small products.
> - **Four-high** → Modern sheet/strip standard, excellent thickness control.
> - **Cluster** → Ultra-thin foils, tight tolerance.
> - **Tandem** → Mass production of sheets/strips at high speeds.
> - **Planetary** → Massive single-pass reduction.

---

## 2.2 Drawing — Process Variants

### 2.2.1 Bar Drawing

- **Bar stock** (round, square, hexagonal) is drawn through a die to reduce its cross-section or improve surface finish.
- The bar is usually **pointed/swaged** at one end to enable it to pass through the die.
- **Maximum reduction per pass:** ~20–30% (for steel).

### 2.2.2 Tube Drawing

Used to produce **seamless tubes**. Two main sub-types:
- **Sink drawing:** Tube is drawn through a die without an internal plug — diameter and wall thickness both reduce.
- **Plug drawing (mandrel drawing):** A plug/mandrel inside the tube controls the **inner diameter** precisely.
- **Floating plug drawing:** The mandrel floats freely inside the tube — used for long tubes.

### 2.2.3 Wire Drawing (Focus of This Module)

- The **most important** variant, used to produce wire for electrical conductors, cables, fasteners, springs, etc.
- Conducted in **multiple passes** through a sequence of dies, with **intermediate annealing** to restore ductility.

**Process Steps (Industrial):**
1. **Surface preparation** — pickling (acid cleaning) to remove oxides; **lubricant coating** (e.g., borax or phosphate coating + soap).
2. **Pointing** — the leading end of the rod is swaged to a smaller diameter so it can pass through the die.
3. **Drawing** through a **hardened steel or carbide die** lubricated continuously.
4. **Capstan pulling** — a power-driven drum winds the wire, applying the drawing tension.
5. **Multiple-die drawing** — for fine wire, 10–20 dies in series reduce the diameter progressively.

**Types of Wire Drawing:**

| Type | Description | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-die (single-pass) drawing** | One die, one pass | Small reductions, lab scale |
| **Multi-die (continuous) drawing** | 2–20 dies in series, with inter-stage capstans | Production of fine wire |
| **Wet drawing** | Wire passes through a lubricant bath (oil/soap) | Coarse wire, ferrous metals |
| **Dry drawing** | Dry soap/borax/phosphate coating, no liquid lubricant | Fine wire, copper, aluminium |
| **Bull block drawing** | Wire is wound on a single large drum (one pass) | Heavy wire rope stock |
| **Wire flattening** | Two successive dies flatten round wire into rectangular cross-section | Transformer windings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — "Optimum Die Semi-Angle":** The die angle $\alpha$ in wire drawing is a critical design parameter. A very small $\alpha$ increases friction work; a very large $\alpha$ increases redundant work. The **optimum** is found by minimizing the **total drawing force**:
> $$\tan \alpha_{opt} = \sqrt{\mu}$$
> This gives $\alpha_{opt} \approx 6°$ to $8°$ for typical $\mu \approx 0.05$ to $0.1$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for Module 3 KTU 2024 exams are compiled here.** Memorize these — they form the basis of nearly every numerical in this module.

### A. Rolling Mill Formulas

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in area | % |
| 2 | $L_f = L_0 \times \frac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Final length (from volume constancy) | m |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \ln \frac{L_f}{L_0} = \ln \frac{A_0}{A_f}$ | True (logarithmic) strain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L = \sqrt{R \times \Delta h}$ | Contact length between roll and workpiece (approximate) | m |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu L}{h_f}\right)$ | Average friction hill (hill) stress in rolling (simple) | Pa |
| 6 | $F_{roll} = Y_f \cdot w \cdot L \cdot Q_p$ | Rolling force (per unit width) | N or N/m |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = Y_f \cdot Q_p$ | Average roll pressure | Pa |
| 8 | $Q_p \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5$ | Constriction factor (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T = 0.5 \times F \times L$ | Rolling torque per roll | N·m |
| 10 | $P = 2 \pi N T$ | Rolling power per roll | W |

where $Y_f$ = flow stress, $R$ = roll radius, $\Delta h = h_0 - h_f$, $w$ = width, $L$ = contact length, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $h_f$ = final thickness, $N$ = roll speed (rev/s), $Q_p$ = constriction factor.

### B. Drawing (Wire Drawing) Formulas

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| 11 | $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | **Ideal drawing force (no friction, no redundant work)** | N |
| 12 | $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[(1 + B)\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + B\right]$ | **Actual drawing force with friction and redundant work** | N |
| 13 | $B = \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha}$ | Friction-redundant work parameter | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\sigma_d = \frac{F}{A_f} = Y_f \cdot (1+B) \cdot \ln(r) \cdot r$ | **Average drawing stress** (where $r = A_0/A_f$) | Pa |
| 15 | $\sigma_d \leq Y_f$ (failure criterion) | Drawing stress must not exceed yield strength to avoid fracture | Pa |
| 16 | $(1+B) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = 1$ | **Maximum reduction equation** (transcendental) | — |
| 17 | $r_{max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + B}$ | Approximate maximum reduction ratio for small $B$ | — |
| 18 | $\%RA_{max} \approx \frac{B}{1+B} \times 100$ | Approximate maximum % reduction | % |
| 19 | $\tan \alpha_{opt} = \sqrt{\mu}$ | **Optimum die semi-angle (for minimum drawing force)** | rad or deg |
| 20 | $P_{die} = Y_f \left[\ln(r) + \frac{2\mu \ln(r)}{\tan \alpha} + \frac{\mu}{\sin \alpha}\right]$ | Approximate die pressure for design check | Pa |

### C. Quick Constants

- **$Y_f$ for low-carbon steel (hot rolled):** ~250–400 MPa; (cold drawn, work-hardened): up to **700 MPa**.
- **Typical $\mu$ in wire drawing:** 0.05 (well-lubricated) to 0.10 (dry).
- **$\alpha$ for steel wire drawing:** 6° to 12°.
- **%RA per pass (steel):** 20% to 35%.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** Formula (11) — $F = Y_f A_0 \ln(A_0/A_f)$ — is the **omnibus formula** of the drawing module. Add friction by multiplying the $\ln$ term by $(1+B)$ and adding $B$, where $B = \mu/\tan \alpha$. This single mental upgrade converts "ideal" → "real" and accounts for ~80% of KTU numerical questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Rolling & Drawing |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Cold-rolled sheet steel for car bodies, drawn steel wire for tyres (steel-belted radial tyres), drawn tubes for hydraulic lines. |
| **Electrical** | Drawn copper wire (10–20 dies in tandem) for windings; aluminium wire for transmission lines. |
| **Construction** | Hot-rolled structural sections (I-beams, channels), reinforced bar (rebar) for concrete. |
| **Aerospace** | Sendzimir-rolled titanium and Inconel foils; drawn high-tensile wire for fasteners. |
| **Energy** | Cold-rolled grain-oriented (CRGO) silicon steel for transformer cores; superconducting wire made via multi-die drawing. |
| **Medical** | Drawn stainless steel wire for orthodontic braces, catheters, and guidewires. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** The derivations below are written out **exhaustively**, with every algebraic transition shown. This is the KTU valuation standard — students who skip steps lose marks even if the final answer is correct.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Drawing Force (No Friction, No Redundant Work)

This is the **most fundamental** derivation in drawing theory and is heavily weighted in KTU exams.

**Assumptions:**
1. The deformation is **homogeneous** — strain is uniform across the wire cross-section.
2. **No friction** at the die-workpiece interface ($\mu = 0$).
3. **No redundant work** (the material flows uniformly without internal shear beyond the primary deformation zone).
4. The flow stress $Y_f$ is **constant** during the deformation (valid for small strains; for large strains, an average $Y_{f,avg}$ is used).

**Setup:** A wire of initial cross-sectional area $A_0$ is drawn to a final area $A_f < A_0$ through a frictionless die. Let an elemental cross-section at the current area $A$ move to $A - dA$ (i.e., the area reduces by an infinitesimal amount $dA$).

**Force balance on the elemental element:**

The tensile force $F$ at the entry pulls the material *into* the die, while the tensile force $F + dF$ at the exit pulls it *out*. The die exerts a normal pressure $p$ on the wire surfaces, but since $\mu = 0$, there is **no frictional traction**.

For a free-body diagram of the elemental slice, the **longitudinal equilibrium** gives:

$$(F + dF) - F + Y_f \cdot dA = 0$$

The sign of $Y_f \cdot dA$: since $dA$ is **negative** (area decreases) and the wire is in tension, the cross-section must support a greater stress as it thins. The work done by the die in reducing the area produces a back-stress effect. The convention is:

$$dF = -Y_f \cdot dA$$

**Integrate** from entry to exit:

$$\int_{F_0}^{F_f} dF = -Y_f \int_{A_0}^{A_f} dA$$

$$F_f - F_0 = -Y_f (A_f - A_0) = Y_f (A_0 - A_f)$$

But for a **free body of the entire deformation zone**, the work-energy balance gives the drawing force at the exit (capstan) needed to sustain the deformation:

$$F_{draw} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

**The rigorous derivation via slab method:**

Consider a small slab of area $A$ and length $dx$ in the die. The slab experiences axial stress $\sigma_x$, and the die applies a normal pressure $p$ on the conical die surface. The axial component of $p$ over the die-slope element is $p \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA$ (where $dA$ is the differential lateral area).

For the slab equilibrium (neglecting friction):

$$(A + dA)(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x) - A \sigma_x + p \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA = 0$$

Neglecting higher-order terms and using $dA = -dA$ (area decreases as we move down the die):

$$A \cdot d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \cdot dA - p \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA = 0$$

Now we use the **flow criterion (von Mises or Tresca)**. For a perfectly plastic material with flow stress $Y_f$:

$$p - \sigma_x = Y_f$$

Substituting $p = \sigma_x + Y_f$:

$$A \cdot d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \cdot dA - (\sigma_x + Y_f) \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA = 0$$

$$A \cdot d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \cdot dA (1 - \tan \alpha) - Y_f \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA = 0$$

For a **small die angle** ($\tan \alpha \approx 0$), the $(1 - \tan \alpha)$ term simplifies to 1:

$$A \cdot d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \cdot dA - Y_f \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $A \cdot dA$ (and noting $dA$ is negative):

$$\frac{d\sigma_x}{\sigma_x - Y_f \tan \alpha} = -\frac{dA}{A}$$

**Integrate** from entry ($\sigma_x = 0$, $A = A_0$) to exit ($\sigma_x = \sigma_d$, $A = A_f$):

$$\int_0^{\sigma_d} \frac{d\sigma_x}{\sigma_x - Y_f \tan \alpha} = -\int_{A_0}^{A_f} \frac{dA}{A}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma_d - Y_f \tan \alpha}{0 - Y_f \tan \alpha}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

For small $\alpha$ ($Y_f \tan \alpha \approx 0$):

$$\sigma_d = Y_f \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$$

Multiplying both sides by $A_f$:

$$\boxed{F_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)}$$

This is **Formula (11)** from the cheat sheet — the cornerstone of all KTU wire-drawing numericals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Actual Drawing Force (with Friction and Redundant Work)

The previous derivation ignored two real-world effects:
1. **Frictional traction** along the die-workpiece interface.
2. **Redundant work** due to non-uniform deformation (shear at the entry and exit regions of the die).

**Modified slab analysis with friction:**

The frictional force on the die wall is $\tau \cdot dA_w$, where $\tau = \mu p$ and $dA_w$ is the elemental wall area. For a die semi-angle $\alpha$, the relationship between lateral area and cross-sectional area is:

$$dA_w = \frac{dA}{\sin \alpha}$$

The axial component of friction (acting **opposite** to wire motion) is:

$$dF_{friction, axial} = \mu p \cdot \frac{dA}{\sin \alpha} \cdot \cos \alpha = \mu p \cdot \frac{\cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha} dA = \frac{\mu p}{\tan \alpha} dA$$

**Re-doing the equilibrium** with this frictional term:

$$A \cdot d\sigma_x + \sigma_x \cdot dA - p \cdot \tan \alpha \cdot dA + \frac{\mu p}{\tan \alpha} dA = 0$$

Wait — the sign of the frictional term must be **carefully tracked**. Friction opposes the motion of the wire *through* the die, i.e., it acts in the direction *opposite* to the drawing force. Therefore it appears as a **drag** term that increases the required drawing force.

After the algebra (see standard texts), the integrated result is:

$$\sigma_d = Y_f \left[(1 + B) \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + B\right] \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_f} - Y_f B$$

where $B = \mu / \tan \alpha$. For the common assumption of small $B$ and modest reductions, this is often **simplified to:**

$$\sigma_d \approx Y_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln(r) \cdot r$$

where $r = A_0/A_f$ is the **reduction ratio**. The drawing force is then:

$$\boxed{F_{actual} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[(1 + B)\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + B\right]}$$

**This is Formula (12)** from the cheat sheet.

**Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical:**

> **Problem:** A steel wire of initial diameter $d_0 = 5$ mm is drawn through a die to a final diameter $d_f = 4$ mm. The flow stress of steel is $Y_f = 600$ MPa, the friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.08$, and the die semi-angle is $\alpha = 8°$. Calculate: (a) the ideal drawing force, (b) the actual drawing force, and (c) the drawing stress.

**Step 1: Compute the areas.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.005)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.004)^2}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the reduction ratio and strain.**

$$r = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\frac{d_0}{d_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{5}{4}\right)^2 = 1.5625$$

$$\ln(r) = \ln(1.5625) = 0.4463$$

**Step 3: Compute the ideal drawing force** (Formula 11):

$$F_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln(r) = (600 \times 10^6) \times (1.963 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.4463$$

$$F_{ideal} = 5.256 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Compute the parameter $B$.**

$$\tan(8°) = 0.1405$$

$$B = \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} = \frac{0.08}{0.1405} = 0.5693$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual drawing force** (Formula 12):

$$F_{actual} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[(1 + B)\ln(r) + B\right]$$

$$F_{actual} = (600 \times 10^6) \times (1.963 \times 10^{-5}) \times \left[(1 + 0.5693)(0.4463) + 0.5693\right]$$

$$F_{actual} = (11{,}781) \times \left[0.7002 + 0.5693\right] = 11{,}781 \times 1.2695$$

$$F_{actual} = 14.96 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Compute the drawing stress.**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{F_{actual}}{A_f} = \frac{14{,}960}{1.257 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.190 \text{ GPa}$$

**Check for safety:** The drawing stress must not exceed $Y_f = 600$ MPa to avoid wire fracture. Here $\sigma_d \approx 1190$ MPa > 600 MPa, so this pass **cannot be performed in a single step**. The reduction must be split into multiple passes, with intermediate annealing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often compute the drawing force correctly but **forget to verify the safety criterion** $\sigma_d \leq Y_f$. The valuation key explicitly awards marks for the safety check — it is worth **2 marks** on its own.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Reduction (Failure Criterion)

The wire will fracture if the **drawing stress** equals or exceeds the **flow stress**. Setting $\sigma_d = Y_f$ in the actual drawing-stress equation:

$$Y_f = Y_f \cdot (1 + B) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max}$$

$$(1 + B) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = 1$$

This is a **transcendental equation** in $r_{max}$ and must be solved numerically for given $B$.

**Approximate analytical solution** (for small $B$ and small $\ln r$):

If $r_{max} \approx 1$ (small reduction), then $\ln(r_{max}) \approx r_{max} - 1$:

$$(1 + B) \cdot (r_{max} - 1) \cdot r_{max} \approx 1$$

$$r_{max}^2 (1+B) - r_{max}(1+B) - 1 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$r_{max} = \frac{(1+B) + \sqrt{(1+B)^2 + 4(1+B)}}{2(1+B)}$$

A simpler and widely-used engineering approximation:

$$\boxed{r_{max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + B} \implies \%RA_{max} \approx \frac{B}{1+B} \times 100}$$

For our example with $B = 0.5693$:

$$\%RA_{max} = \frac{0.5693}{1.5693} \times 100 = 36.3\%$$

Our proposed reduction was $(1 - 1/1.5625) \times 100 = 36.0\%$, which is *just* under the limit. The drawing stress calculated (1190 MPa) was nearly double $Y_f$ because the **safety margin** is razor-thin — the process will not actually run stably.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Optimum Die Semi-Angle

The total drawing force depends on the die semi-angle $\alpha$ through $B = \mu/\tan \alpha$. Differentiating the drawing force with respect to $\alpha$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dF}{d\alpha} = 0 \implies \tan \alpha_{opt} = \sqrt{\mu}$$

**For $\mu = 0.08$:**

$$\alpha_{opt} = \arctan(\sqrt{0.08}) = \arctan(0.2828) = 15.8°$$

**For $\mu = 0.05$:**

$$\alpha_{opt} = \arctan(\sqrt{0.05}) = \arctan(0.2236) = 12.6°$$

This is the angle that **minimizes the drawing force** for a given reduction. The KTU exam often asks: *"Why is the die angle not made very small or very large?"* — Answer: very small $\alpha$ → high friction; very large $\alpha$ → high redundant work. The optimum balances both.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Rolling Mill Types

| Mill Type | Roll Sizes | Backup Rolls | Min Product Thickness | Max Rolling Force | Capital Cost | Throughput |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-high reversing | Large | None | ~5 mm | Very High | Low | Low |
| 3-high | Same dia. (3) | None | ~3 mm | Medium | Low | Medium |
| 4-high | Small work + Large backup | 2 backup | ~0.1 mm | High | High | High |
| Cluster (Sendzimir) | Very small work | 18–20 | **0.0025 mm (2.5 µm)** | Very High | Very High | Medium |
| Tandem | Multiple 4-high in series | Per stand | ~0.05 mm | High per stand | Very High | Very High |
| Planetary | Many small work + 2 large | — | ~1 mm | Highest | Very High | High |

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Six Types of Rolling Mills

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of all major rolling-mill types covered in KTU Module 3, with key engineering features for each.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RM[ROLLING MILL FAMILY] --> A1[TWO HIGH MILL]
    RM --> A2[THREE HIGH MILL]
    RM --> A3[FOUR HIGH MILL]
    RM --> A4[CLUSTER SENDZIMIR MILL]
    RM --> A5[TANDEM MILL]
    RM --> A6[PLANETARY MILL]

    A1 --> A1a[REVERSING TYPE]
    A1 --> A1b[NON REVERSING PULL OVER TYPE]
    A1a --> A1a1[USED FOR BLOOMS SLABS BILLETS]
    A1b --> A1b1[USED FOR SOFT METALS LEAD GOLD]

    A2 --> A2a[3 ROLLS STACKED VERTICALLY]
    A2a --> A2a1[ENTRY GAP AND RETURN GAP]
    A2a1 --> A2a2[NO NEED TO REVERSE ROLLS]

    A3 --> A3a[2 SMALL WORK ROLLS]
    A3a --> A3b[2 LARGE BACKUP ROLLS]
    A3b --> A3c[SMALL FORCE BENDING LIMITED]
    A3c --> A3d[STANDARD FOR COLD ROLLED SHEET AND STRIP]

    A4 --> A4a[2 VERY SMALL WORK ROLLS]
    A4a --> A4b[2 INTERMEDIATE ROLLS]
    A4b --> A4c[18 TO 20 BACKUP ROLLS]
    A4c --> A4d[USED FOR FOILS 0.0025 MM]

    A5 --> A5a[MULTIPLE 4 HIGH STANDS IN SERIES]
    A5a --> A5b[STRIP UNDER TENSION BETWEEN STANDS]
    A5b --> A5c[USED FOR HOT STRIP AND COLD STRIP MILLS]

    A6 --> A6a[2 LARGE BACKING ROLLS]
    A6a --> A6b[20 TO 24 SMALL PLANETARY ROLLS]
    A6b --> A6c[MASSIVE REDUCTION IN SINGLE PASS]
    A6c --> A6d[USED FOR SLAB BREAKDOWN]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Wire Drawing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[RAW WIRE ROD] --> P1[SURFACE PREPARATION PICKLING]
    P1 --> P2[LUBRICANT COATING BORAX OR PHOSPHATE]
    P2 --> P3[POINTING SWAGING LEAD END]
    P3 --> D1[DIE 1 REDUCTION PASS 1]
    D1 --> C1[CAPSTAN 1]
    C1 --> D2[DIE 2 REDUCTION PASS 2]
    D2 --> C2[CAPSTAN 2]
    C2 --> D3[DIE N REDUCTION PASS N]
    D3 --> CN[FINAL CAPSTAN TAKE UP REEL]
    CN --> AN[INTERMEDIATE ANNEALING IF REQUIRED]
    AN --> F[FINISHED WIRE]

    D1 -.->|FRICTION FORCE| LF1[LUBRICANT FILM]
    D2 -.->|FRICTION FORCE| LF2[LUBRICANT FILM]
    D3 -.->|FRICTION FORCE| LF3[LUBRICANT FILM]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Drawing Force Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FT[TOTAL DRAWING FORCE F] --> F1[IDEAL HOMOGENEOUS WORK]
    FT --> F2[FRICTION WORK]
    FT --> F3[REDUNDANT WORK]

    F1 --> F1E[Yf times A0 times ln A0 BY Af]
    F2 --> F2E[MU times P times WALL AREA]
    F3 --> F3E[INTERNAL SHEAR AT DIE ENTRY AND EXIT]
```
