# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]**

### **Question A1 (3 Marks):**
**Differentiate between a two-high reversing mill and a three-high rolling mill. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Two-High Reversing Mill | Three-High Rolling Mill |
|---|---|---|
| Number of rolls | 2 | 3 (stacked vertically) |
| Pass direction | Reversed after each pass | Roll direction constant; workpiece passes through two gaps |
| Suitable for | Heavy reductions, large ingots | Light sections, small bars, multiple passes |
| Productivity | Low (idle time during reversal) | Higher (no reversal time) |
| Application | Blooming mill for steel slabs | Rolling of light bars, sections, rods |

### **Question A2 (3 Marks):**
**Define the "optimum die semi-angle" in wire drawing. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
The optimum die semi-angle $\alpha_{opt}$ is the die angle that **minimizes the total drawing force** for a given reduction and friction condition. It is given by $\tan \alpha_{opt} = \sqrt{\mu}$.

A very small die angle increases the **die-workpiece contact area**, raising friction work. A very large die angle increases **redundant work** (internal shear) and creates flow defects. The optimum balances these two effects, giving:
- Minimum drawing force.
- Best surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- Lowest power consumption.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **OPTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Question A:** (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **four-high rolling mill**. State why it is preferred over a two-high mill for cold rolling of sheets. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) A copper rod of diameter 10 mm is drawn through a die to a diameter of 7 mm. The flow stress of copper is 320 MPa, the friction coefficient is 0.06, and the die semi-angle is 10°. Calculate: (i) the ideal drawing force, (ii) the actual drawing force, (iii) the drawing stress, and (iv) the percentage reduction in area. State whether the pass is safe. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Four-High Rolling Mill (7 Marks)**

**Construction:**
A four-high rolling mill consists of:
1. **Two work rolls** of small diameter (typically 100–400 mm) — these are the rolls in direct contact with the workpiece. Small diameter reduces roll flattening and gives a smaller contact area, lowering rolling force.
2. **Two backup rolls** of large diameter (typically 500–1500 mm) — these support the work rolls against bending under the rolling load.
3. **Chocks and bearings** — the work rolls are mounted in chocks that are supported by the backup rolls through a four-high arrangement.
4. **Mill housing / frame** — a rigid stand that holds all rolls in alignment.
5. **Screws and hydraulic cylinders** — for setting and adjusting the roll gap.

**Working Principle:**
The metal strip/sheet passes between the two work rolls, which rotate in opposite directions. The compressive force is applied via the backup rolls, which transmit load from the mill housing. As the strip passes through, its thickness reduces while its length increases. The strip is coiled at the exit and may be passed through multiple stands (tandem arrangement) for further reduction.

**Why Preferred Over Two-High for Cold Rolling of Sheets:**
- **Lower rolling force:** Small work rolls reduce the contact area ($L \approx \sqrt{R \Delta h}$), hence force.
- **Better thickness control:** Small rolls deflect less; the deflection of backup rolls is small due to their large diameter.
- **Tighter tolerances:** For cold-rolled sheet (down to 0.1 mm), two-high mills cannot maintain the required flatness and gauge accuracy.
- **Avoids roll flattening:** Hertzian flattening at the roll-strip interface is much less with small work rolls.
- **Higher productivity:** Modern four-high mills operate at speeds up to 1800 m/min.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Show a cross-section with two small work rolls at the centre and two large backup rolls on the outside, indicating the strip passing through.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Wire Drawing Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 10$ mm, $d_f = 7$ mm
- $Y_f = 320$ MPa = $320 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\mu = 0.06$
- $\alpha = 10°$

**Step 1: Compute the areas.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.010)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.007)^2}{4} = 3.848 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the reduction ratio and % reduction.**

$$r = \frac{A_0}{A_f} = \left(\frac{10}{7}\right)^2 = 2.0408$$

$$\ln(r) = \ln(2.0408) = 0.7133$$

$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{2.0408}\right) \times 100 = 51.0\%$$

**Step 3: Compute the parameter $B$.**

$$\tan(10°) = 0.1763$$

$$B = \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} = \frac{0.06}{0.1763} = 0.3403$$

**Step 4: Compute the ideal drawing force.**

$$F_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln(r)$$

$$F_{ideal} = (320 \times 10^6) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.7133$$

$$F_{ideal} = 17{,}928 \text{ N} \approx 17.93 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual drawing force.**

$$F_{actual} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[(1 + B)\ln(r) + B\right]$$

$$F_{actual} = (320 \times 10^6) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-5}) \times \left[(1.3403)(0.7133) + 0.3403\right]$$

$$F_{actual} = 25{,}133 \times [0.9561 + 0.3403] = 25{,}133 \times 1.2964$$

$$F_{actual} = 32{,}581 \text{ N} \approx 32.58 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Compute the drawing stress.**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{F_{actual}}{A_f} = \frac{32{,}581}{3.848 \times 10^{-5}} = 846.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7: Safety check.**

$$\sigma_d = 846.6 \text{ MPa} > Y_f = 320 \text{ MPa}$$

**The pass is NOT SAFE.** The drawing stress is more than 2.6 times the flow stress, which will cause the wire to fracture. The reduction must be performed in **multiple passes with intermediate annealing** to bring $\sigma_d$ below $Y_f$ in each pass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> Students frequently **omit the safety verification** in the final step. Even with all numerical values correct, you will lose **2 out of 7 marks** if you fail to state explicitly that "this pass is not safe and must be split into multiple passes." Always conclude drawing-force problems with a safety statement.

---

### **OPTION B — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Question B:** (a) Explain the **types of drawing processes** with suitable examples and neat diagrams. Compare wire drawing with bar drawing. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) In a wire drawing operation, a steel wire of initial diameter 8 mm is drawn through a conical die with semi-angle 7°. The flow stress of the wire material is 700 MPa, and the friction coefficient is 0.05. Determine: (i) the maximum possible reduction in a single pass, and (ii) the corresponding final wire diameter. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to Part (a) — Types of Drawing (7 Marks)**

**Types of Drawing:**

1. **Wire Drawing:** Reduction of wire diameter by pulling it through a hardened die. The wire is usually drawn in multiple passes through a series of dies with intermediate annealing. Examples: copper wire for electrical use, steel wire for ropes, aluminium wire for cables.

2. **Bar Drawing:** Reduction of bar stock (round, square, or hexagonal cross-section) by pulling through a die. Used for producing precision bars with tight dimensional tolerances and improved surface finish. Examples: bright steel bars, hexagon bars for fasteners.

3. **Tube Drawing:** Reduction of seamless tubes by pulling through a die. Sub-types:
   - **Sink drawing:** No internal support; both OD and wall thickness reduce.
   - **Plug/mandrel drawing:** A fixed plug controls ID; gives precise ID and good surface finish inside.
   - **Floating plug drawing:** A plug floats inside the tube — used for long tubes where mandrel support is impractical.

**Comparison: Wire Drawing vs. Bar Drawing:**

| Feature | Wire Drawing | Bar Drawing |
|---|---|---|
| Starting stock | Wire rod (coiled) | Bar stock (straight) |
| Cross-section | Round (almost always) | Round, square, hexagonal |
| Typical diameter | < 25 mm (often < 5 mm) | 25–150 mm |
| Process | Continuous, multiple dies in series | Single die, single pass (or few) |
| Equipment | Bull block, multi-die block, continuous drawing machine | Draw bench, hydraulic or chain-driven |
| Surface finish | Very critical (often plated) | Important but less critical |
| Industrial use | Cables, fasteners, springs, electrical | Bright bars, shafts, machine parts |

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
- A die cross-section showing the entrance, working cone, and bearing region.
- Arrows showing wire/bar/tube being pulled by a capstan.

#### **Solution to Part (b) — Maximum Reduction Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 8$ mm, $Y_f = 700$ MPa, $\mu = 0.05$, $\alpha = 7°$

**Step 1: Compute $B$.**

$$\tan(7°) = 0.1228$$

$$B = \frac{\mu}{\tan \alpha} = \frac{0.05}{0.1228} = 0.4071$$

**Step 2: Apply the maximum reduction equation.**

$$(1 + B) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = 1$$

$$(1.4071) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = 1$$

$$\ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = \frac{1}{1.4071} = 0.7107$$

**Step 3: Solve numerically (trial and error / Newton-Raphson).**

Let $f(r) = \ln(r) \cdot r - 0.7107$

| Trial $r$ | $\ln(r) \cdot r$ | $f(r)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.5 | 0.608 | -0.103 |
| 1.6 | 0.752 | +0.041 |
| 1.55 | 0.680 | -0.031 |
| 1.57 | 0.711 | +0.0003 ✓ |

So $r_{max} \approx 1.57$.

**Step 4: Use the approximate formula to verify.**

$$r_{max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + B} = \frac{1}{1.4071} = 0.7107$$

Wait — this approximation gives the *inverse* reduction ratio. The correct interpretation: the approximate formula in section 3.3 was for the case where we approximate $\ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} \approx 1 - 1/r_{max}$, leading to:

$$r_{max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + B} \cdot (\text{larger factor})$$

Numerically, the exact solution $r_{max} = 1.57$ is more accurate. We use this value.

**Step 5: Compute the maximum percentage reduction.**

$$\%RA_{max} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{r_{max}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{1.57}\right) \times 100 = 36.3\%$$

**Step 6: Compute the corresponding final wire diameter.**

$$\frac{d_f^2}{d_0^2} = \frac{1}{r_{max}} = \frac{1}{1.57} = 0.6369$$

$$d_f = d_0 \times \sqrt{0.6369} = 8 \times 0.7981 = 6.385 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Maximum reduction per pass: $\%RA_{max} \approx 36.3\%$
- Minimum final wire diameter: $d_f \approx 6.39$ mm

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B — Numerical):**
> 1. **Do not confuse** the symbol $B = \mu/\tan \alpha$ with $B = \mu \cot \alpha$ — both appear in textbooks depending on how the die angle is defined (semi-angle vs. full angle). KTU uses **semi-angle**, so $B = \mu/\tan \alpha$.
> 2. **Transcendental equation pitfall:** The equation $\ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = \text{const}$ cannot be solved by simple algebra. The expected method is either:
>    - **Trial and error** (show at least 3 trial values) — most common in KTU.
>    - **Newton-Raphson iteration** — gives full marks if correctly executed.
>    - **Approximation** $r_{max} \approx 1/(1+B)$ — acceptable only if explicitly stated as an approximation.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always convert mm to m for area calculations in SI units.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid Revision Checklist — Rolling Mills and Drawing**

### 🔧 Rolling Mills
- [ ] **Two-high reversing** → heavy ingots, blooms, slabs.
- [ ] **Two-high non-reversing (pull-over)** → soft metals (lead, gold, silver).
- [ ] **Three-high** → light sections, multiple passes without reversing.
- [ ] **Four-high** → standard for cold-rolled sheet/strip; small work rolls, large backup rolls.
- [ ] **Cluster (Sendzimir)** → ultra-thin foils, ~20 backup rolls; minimum thickness 0.0025 mm.
- [ ] **Tandem** → multiple stands in series; high-throughput strip production.
- [ ] **Planetary** → massive single-pass reductions; many small planetary rolls between 2 backing rolls.

### 🧵 Drawing
- [ ] Drawing is a **tensile-dominated** process; rolling is **compressive**.
- [ ] Wire drawing = pulling wire through a **hardened die** to reduce diameter.
- [ ] Three types: **wire, bar, tube** drawing.
- [ ] Tube drawing sub-types: **sink, plug, floating plug**.
- [ ] **Multi-die continuous drawing** is the industrial norm for fine wire.
- [ ] **Lubrication** is critical: borax, soap, phosphate coating, oil.

### 📐 Formulas to Memorize (Bare Minimum)
- [ ] $F_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$
- [ ] $F_{actual} = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \left[(1 + B)\ln(A_0/A_f) + B\right]$ where $B = \mu/\tan \alpha$
- [ ] $\sigma_d = F/A_f$
- [ ] **Safety criterion:** $\sigma_d \leq Y_f$
- [ ] Maximum reduction equation: $(1+B) \cdot \ln(r_{max}) \cdot r_{max} = 1$
- [ ] Optimum die angle: $\tan \alpha_{opt} = \sqrt{\mu}$
- [ ] Rolling contact length: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$

### 🛑 Common KTU Mistakes
- [ ] Confusing **die semi-angle** $\alpha$ with **full die angle** $2\alpha$ in the formula for $B$.
- [ ] Forgetting to **convert mm to m** when computing area in SI units.
- [ ] Omitting the **safety verification** $\sigma_d \leq Y_f$ at the end of drawing problems.
- [ ] Using the **wrong formula** for $B$ (some texts use $\mu \cot \alpha$ when $\alpha$ is measured differently).
- [ ] Not stating the **industrial application** when describing a rolling mill — KTU values this.

### 💡 Examiner's One-Liner
> *"Every wire-drawing problem ends with: (1) computing the force, (2) computing the drawing stress, and (3) verifying the safety criterion. If any of these three is missing, expect 2-mark deductions."*

### 📊 Typical Mark Distribution in KTU ESE (Module 3)
| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Rolling mill classification/description | 3–4 |
| Sketch/diagram of a mill | 2–3 |
| Drawing force numerical | 6–7 |
| Safety check + conclusion | 1–2 |
| **Total** | **14** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: METAL FORMING PROCESSES — DEEP DRAWING, SPINNING, EXTRUSION & FORGING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Tube Drawing
**Tube Drawing** is a metal forming process in which a tubular workpiece is reduced in cross-section and wall thickness by pulling it through a die using a tensile force. The process is broadly classified into:
- **Bare Drawing (Sink Drawing)**: No internal mandrel; only outer diameter is reduced.
- **Mandrel Drawing**: A stationary or floating plug (mandrel) controls the inner diameter.
- **Floating Plug Drawing**: Self-adjusting plug for long tubes.
- **Ironing**: Reduction of wall thickness without much change in diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Tube drawing is a tension-compression process where the tube is in tension and the die-workpiece interface is under compression. Maximum reduction per pass is limited to **~20-45%** in area.

### 1.1.2 Deep Drawing
**Deep Drawing** is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat sheet (blank) is forced by a punch into a die cavity to produce a hollow, cup-shaped part without intentional change in wall thickness. It converts a 2D blank into a 3D hollow component.

**Key Components**:
- **Punch**: Male die that pushes the metal.
- **Die**: Female die cavity.
- **Blank Holder (Hold-Down)**: Prevents wrinkling of the flange.
- **Draw Die Radius (rd)**: Critical for material flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy**: Imagine pressing a piece of dough (the metal blank) into a muffin mould with your thumb (the punch) — the dough flows up the sides of the mould, taking the shape of a cup. The "blank holder" in deep drawing is like the muffin tin's top edge preventing the dough from buckling outward.

### 1.1.3 Coining
**Coining** is a cold forging (squeezing) process used to produce fine details such as engraved designs, letters, or sharp impressions on the surface of a workpiece. It is a closed-die forging operation with **flashless** dies and very high pressures.

### 1.1.4 Spinning (Hot & Cold)
**Spinning** is a chipless forming process where a sheet metal disc (blank) is rotated at high speed and gradually formed over a mandrel using rollers or tools.

- **Hot Spinning**: Performed at elevated temperatures (above recrystallization). Used for thick plates and hard materials (e.g., steel dished ends).
- **Cold Spinning**: Performed at room temperature. Used for ductile metals like aluminum, copper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition**: Spinning is a *chipless* flow-turning process with localized plastic deformation. Wall thickness can be kept constant or intentionally reduced (shear spinning).

### 1.1.5 Extrusion
**Extrusion** is a compressive forming process in which a billet of material is forced to flow through a die opening of desired cross-section to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section.

- **Direct (Forward) Extrusion**: Ram and billet move in the same direction.
- **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion**: Ram and product move in opposite directions.
- **Impact Extrusion**: Sudden impact (cold); used for collapsible tubes.
- **Hydrostatic Extrusion**: Fluid pressure replaces the ram.

### 1.1.6 Forging
**Forging** is a hot/cold/warm working process in which compressive forces are applied to a workpiece (billet) to deform it plastically between dies, producing the desired shape with improved mechanical properties due to grain flow.

- **Open-Die Forging**: Material flows freely; used for large parts.
- **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging**: Metal is confined in die cavity; flash may form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Forging**: Think of shaping a ball of clay between your two palms — the compressive force flows the clay into the shape you want, and the more you work it, the stronger and more aligned the internal grain becomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Tube Drawing — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Process Mechanics
In tube drawing, the tube is pulled through a conical die. The die cone half-angle is typically **α = 6° to 12°**. The drawing stress must be less than the yield stress of the material after drawing, otherwise the tube will fracture.

### 2.1.2 Key Parameters
- **Drawing Force (F)**
- **Reduction per Pass (r)**
- **Drawing Stress (σd)**
- **Die Angle (α)**
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)**

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Theoretical Flow
1. Tube end is reduced (pointed/swaged) to enter the die.
2. Tube passes over a mandrel (if used) and into the die cone.
3. Plastic deformation occurs in the deformation zone.
4. Wall thickness reduces, length increases.
5. Tension is applied at exit to pull the tube.

## 2.2 Deep Drawing — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Drawing Mechanics
In deep drawing, the metal blank is drawn radially into the die opening. The flange region is in **radial tension** and **circumferential compression** — this compression causes **wrinkling**, prevented by the blank holder.

### 2.2.2 Critical Phenomena
- **Wrinkling**: Buckling in flange; controlled by blank-holder force.
- **Tearing**: At punch radius; controlled by punch radius and lubrication.
- **Earing**: Uneven height of cup wall; due to anisotropy in rolled sheet.

### 2.2.3 Limiting Draw Ratio (LDR)
The maximum ratio of blank diameter to punch diameter that can be drawn in a single operation **without failure**.

## 2.3 Extrusion — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Process Mechanics
The billet is contained in a chamber. The ram applies pressure, forcing the material to flow through the die. The metal flows in a state of **triaxial compression**, which allows very large reductions (up to **95%**).

### 2.3.2 Direct vs Indirect Extrusion

| Feature | Direct Extrusion | Indirect Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Ram motion | Same as product | Opposite to product |
| Friction | High (ram-billet) | Low (no container friction on billet) |
| Maximum length | Limited by billet length | Longer products possible |
| Pressure required | Higher | Lower (~20-30% less) |

### 2.3.3 Extrusion Defects
- **Pipe (Center Burst)**: Internal chevron crack; due to high redundant work.
- **Surface Cracking**: Due to high temperature and friction.
- **Die Swell (Barreling)**: Material expands on exiting die.

## 2.4 Forging — Detailed Analysis

### 2.4.1 Process Mechanics
Forging utilizes the plastic deformation of metal under compressive loads, with the metal flowing preferentially in the direction of least resistance. The grain structure of the metal is refined and aligned along the part contour, enhancing strength and toughness.

### 2.4.2 Forging Temperature Regimes
- **Cold Forging**: T < 0.3 Tm (recrystallization does not occur).
- **Warm Forging**: 0.3 Tm < T < 0.5 Tm.
- **Hot Forging**: T > 0.5 Tm (recrystallization occurs).

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drawing Force (Tube) | $F_d = A_f \cdot \sigma_d$ | N | Tube drawing |
| 2 | Drawing Stress | $\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + Y_f$ | Pa | Ideal work |
| 3 | Maximum Reduction per Pass | $r_{max} = 1 - e^{-(1-\mu \cot\alpha)}$ | — | Die angle limit |
| 4 | Drawing Power | $P = F_d \cdot v$ | W | Motor sizing |
| 5 | Limiting Draw Ratio (LDR) | $\text{LDR} = \dfrac{D_{blank,max}}{D_{punch}}$ | — | Deep drawing design |
| 6 | Blank Holder Force | $F_{bh} = 0.015 \cdot \pi \cdot (D^2 - d^2) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_y$ | N | Prevent wrinkling |
| 7 | Extrusion Pressure (Direct) | $P = Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{\mu L/D}$ | Pa | Extrusion force |
| 8 | Extrusion Force | $F = P \cdot A_0$ | N | Ram design |
| 9 | Forging Pressure (Plane Strain) | $P = 2 Y_f \left(1 + \dfrac{\mu w}{2h}\right)$ | Pa | Forging load |
| 10 | Forging Load (Upsetting) | $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot e^{\mu \pi D/2h}$ | N | Cylinder upsetting |
| 11 | Spinning Force | $F = Y_f \cdot t \cdot L$ | N | Hot/cold spinning |
| 12 | Coining Pressure | $P = Y_f \cdot (1 + \mu \cdot \dfrac{D}{2h})$ | Pa | Closed-die forging |
| 13 | Die Swell Ratio | $B = \dfrac{A_f - A_d}{A_d} \times 100$ | % | Extrusion defect |
| 14 | Reduction in Area (Drawing) | $r = 1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_0}$ | — | Pass design |
| 15 | Strain (Drawing) | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$ | — | True strain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are essential for die design, machine capacity selection, and defect prevention in KTU board problems. Always check friction and die-angle bounds before finalizing calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Drawing Force in Tube Drawing

The drawing force equals the average drawing stress times the exit cross-sectional area:

$$F_d = \sigma_d \cdot A_f$$

The ideal drawing stress (neglecting friction and redundant work) is given by:

$$\sigma_d = \sigma_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + Y_f$$

where:
- $\sigma_f$ = average flow stress (Pa)
- $A_0$ = initial cross-sectional area (m²)
- $A_f$ = final cross-sectional area (m²)
- $Y_f$ = yield stress at exit (Pa)

### Including Friction and Die Angle:
The total drawing stress is modified to:

$$\sigma_d = Y_f \left[ \frac{1}{\eta} \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + 2\mu \cot\alpha \right]$$

where:
- $\eta$ = drawing efficiency (~0.7 to 0.9)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $\alpha$ = die semi-angle

### Worked Example (KTU Standard):

**Problem:** A tube is drawn from initial diameter 30 mm to 20 mm with initial wall thickness 3 mm to final 1.5 mm. Yield stress = 200 MPa, μ = 0.08, α = 8°, η = 0.85. Find drawing force.

**Step 1 — Initial Area:**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_0^2 - d_0^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(30^2 - 24^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(324) = 254.47 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Final Area:**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_f^2 - d_f^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(20^2 - 17^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(111) = 87.18 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Strain:**
$$\ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{254.47}{87.18}\right) = \ln(2.919) = 1.072$$

**Step 4 — Drawing Stress:**
$$\sigma_d = 200 \left[ \frac{1.072}{0.85} + 2(0.08)\cot(8°) \right]$$
$$\sigma_d = 200 \left[ 1.261 + 0.16 \times 7.115 \right] = 200 \times [1.261 + 1.138] = 200 \times 2.399$$
$$\sigma_d = 479.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Drawing Force:**
$$F_d = \sigma_d \times A_f = 479.8 \times 87.18 = 41{,}827 \text{ N} \approx 41.8 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Extrusion Pressure (Direct Extrusion)

For direct extrusion, the pressure to push a billet through a die:

$$P = Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) \cdot e^{\mu L/D}$$

This comes from:
1. **Ideal work** (no friction): $P_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \ln(A_0/A_f)$
2. **Friction work** at container wall: friction factor $e^{\mu L/D}$ (where L is billet length, D is container diameter).
3. **Redundant work**: increases pressure by ~10-30%.

### Worked Example:

**Problem:** A billet with $A_0 = 2000$ mm², $A_f = 400$ mm², $L = 300$ mm, $D = 100$ mm, $\mu = 0.1$, $Y_f = 150$ MPa. Find extrusion force.

**Step 1 — Ideal pressure:**
$$P_{ideal} = 150 \cdot \ln(2000/400) = 150 \cdot \ln(5) = 150 \times 1.609 = 241.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Friction factor:**
$$e^{\mu L/D} = e^{0.1 \times 300/100} = e^{0.3} = 1.3499$$

**Step 3 — Total pressure:**
$$P = 241.4 \times 1.3499 = 325.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Extrusion Force:**
$$F = P \times A_0 = 325.9 \times 2000 = 651{,}800 \text{ N} = 651.8 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Forging Load in Open-Die Upsetting

For a cylindrical workpiece under plane-strain compression:

$$F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot e^{\mu \pi D/(2h)}$$

### Worked Example:

**Problem:** A cylinder of steel (D = 50 mm, h = 100 mm) is upset. $Y_f = 250$ MPa, $\mu = 0.15$. Find load when h reduces to 60 mm.

**Step 1 — Volume constancy:**
$$V_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(50^2)(100) = 196{,}350 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — New diameter:**
$$D_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{4V_0}{\pi h_{new}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 196{,}350}{\pi \times 60}} = \sqrt{4166.67} = 64.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — New area:**
$$A_{new} = \frac{\pi}{4}(64.55)^2 = 3272.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Friction factor:**
$$e^{\mu \pi D/(2h)} = e^{0.15 \times \pi \times 64.55/(2 \times 60)} = e^{0.254} = 1.289$$

**Step 5 — Forging Load:**
$$F = 250 \times 3272.5 \times 1.289 = 1{,}054{,}560 \text{ N} \approx 1054.5 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Forming Processes

| Parameter | Tube Drawing | Deep Drawing | Extrusion | Forging | Spinning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stress State | Tension | Tension+Compression | Triaxial Compression | Compression | Tension+Compression |
| Tooling Cost | Medium | Medium-High | High | High | Low |
| Material Waste | Low | Low | Low (no flash) | Medium (flash) | None |
| Surface Finish | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Production Rate | High | High | Very High | Medium | Low-Medium |
| Reduction per Pass | 20-45% | LDR limited | Up to 95% | Multiple blows | 30-50% |
| Typical Products | Tubes, pipes | Cans, panels | Bars, sections | Crankshafts, gears | Dish ends, cones |
| Temperature | Cold | Cold/Hot | Cold/Hot | Hot/Cold | Cold/Hot |
| Wall Thickness Change | Yes (reduces) | Minimal | N/A (solid) | N/A | Optional (shear spinning) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagrams (Mermaid)

### 4.1.1 Tube Drawing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Tubular Billet] --> B[Pointed End Swaging]
    B --> C[Insert Through Die Cone]
    C --> D{Is Mandrel Used?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Fixed or Floating Mandrel Positioned]
    D -- No --> F[Bare Sink Drawing]
    E --> G[Plastic Deformation in Cone]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Wall Thickness Reduction]
    H --> I[Tube Length Increases]
    I --> J[Drawing Force Applied at Exit]
    J --> K[Final Drawn Tube]
    K --> L[End: Product with Reduced Diameter and Wall]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#c8e6c9
    style G fill:#fff9c4
    style J fill:#ffccbc
```

### 4.1.2 Deep Drawing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flat Sheet Blank] --> B[Place on Die Cavity]
    B --> C[Blank Holder Applies Pressure]
    C --> D[Punch Descends]
    D --> E[Blank Flange Bends Over Die Radius]
    E --> F[Radial Drawing into Die]
    F --> G{Wrinkling Risk?}
    G -- High --> H[Adjust Blank Holder Force]
    G -- Low --> I[Continue Drawing]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Material Flows Over Punch Radius]
    J --> K{Wall Tear Risk?}
    K -- High --> L[Increase Punch Radius or Lubricate]
    K -- Low --> M[Complete Cup Formation]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Stripper Removes Cup from Punch]
    N --> O[Finished Deep Drawn Component]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style O fill:#c8e6c9
    style G fill:#ffeb3b
    style K fill:#ffeb3b
```

### 4.1.3 Extrusion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heated Billet in Container] --> B{Ram Position}
    B -- Same Direction --> C[Direct Extrusion]
    B -- Opposite Direction --> D[Indirect Extrusion]
    C --> E[Ram Pushes Billet Through Die]
    D --> F[Die Moves into Container]
    E --> G[Material Flows Through Die Orifice]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Continuous Product Exits]
    H --> I{Check for Defects}
    I -- Pipe Crack --> J[Reduce Die Angle and Speed]
    I -- Surface Crack --> K[Lower Temperature and Lubricate]
    I -- OK --> L[Cut to Length]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Extruded Product]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style M fill:#c8e6c9
    style I fill:#ffeb3b
```

### 4.1.4 Forging Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Billet Material Selection] --> B[Heating to Forging Temperature]
    B --> C{Die Type}
    C -- Open Die --> D[Free Flow Between Flat Dies]
    C -- Closed Die --> E[Material Confined in Cavity]
    D --> F[Compressive Force Applied]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Plastic Deformation]
    G --> H{Grain Refinement}
    H -- Yes --> I[Enhanced Mechanical Properties]
    H -- Required --> J[Multiple Blows or Press Cycles]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Trimming Required}
    K -- Yes --> L[Remove Flash]
    K -- No --> M[Final Forged Part]
    L --> M

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style M fill:#c8e6c9
    style H fill:#fff9c4
```

### 4.1.5 Spinning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Metal Disc Blank] --> B[Mounted on Lathe Mandrel]
    B --> C[Spindle Rotates Blank]
    C --> D{Temperature}
    D -- Room Temp --> E[Cold Spinning]
    D -- Elevated --> F[Hot Spinning]
    E --> G[Roller Applies Localized Pressure]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Plastic Deformation Over Mandrel]
    H --> I[Wall Formed]
    I --> J{Shear Spinning Required}
    J -- Yes --> K[Intentional Wall Thinning]
    J -- No --> L[Constant Wall Thickness Maintained]
    K --> M[Trimming and Finishing]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Spun Component]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style N fill:#c8e6c9
    style D fill:#ffeb3b
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Coining Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece Blank] --> B[Placed Between Coining Dies]
    B --> C[Top Die with Engraved Pattern]
    B --> D[Bottom Die with Cavity]
    C --> E[Hydraulic or Mechanical Press]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Extreme Compressive Pressure]
    F --> G[Plastic Flow into Cavity]
    G --> H[Pattern Imprinted on Surface]
    H --> I[Workpiece Ejected]
    I --> J[Inspection of Detail Clarity]
    J --> K[Final Coined Product]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style K fill:#c8e6c9
    style F fill:#ffccbc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note**: The diagrams above represent *process control flow* and *material state transitions*. For physical component layouts (tooling, die geometry, etc.), refer to standard manufacturing textbooks — the block architecture captures the engineering logic of each process.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define deep drawing. List the major defects encountered during deep drawing.**

**Model Answer**:
Deep drawing is a sheet metal forming process in which a flat blank is forced by a punch into a die cavity to produce a hollow cup-shaped part. Major defects are:
1. **Wrinkling** — Buckling in the flange region due to circumferential compressive stress.
2. **Tearing** — Failure at the punch corner radius due to high tensile stress.
3. **Earing** — Uneven height of cup wall caused by directional anisotropy in the rolled sheet.
4. **Orange peel** — Coarse surface from oversized grains.
5. **Stretcher strains (Lüders bands)** — Surface markings from discontinuous yielding.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between hot and cold spinning.**

**Model Answer**:

| Feature | Hot Spinning | Cold Spinning |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallization temp | Room temperature |
| Material | Steels, titanium, hard alloys | Aluminum, copper, brass |
| Force Required | Lower | Higher |
| Surface Finish | Good (scale may form) | Excellent (bright) |
| Dimensional Accuracy | Lower (cooling) | High |
| Grain Refinement | Yes | Yes (work hardening) |
| Thickness Capability | Thick plates (up to 75 mm) | Thin sheets |
| Equipment | Heated mandrel setup | Conventional lathe |

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **tube drawing process**. Discuss the different types of tube drawing and the variables that affect the drawing force. (7 Marks)

**(b) A copper tube with initial outer diameter 40 mm and wall thickness 5 mm is drawn to outer diameter 30 mm and wall thickness 2.5 mm. Yield strength of copper is 180 MPa, average flow stress is 220 MPa, μ = 0.07, die semi-angle α = 8°, drawing efficiency = 0.85. Calculate (i) drawing stress, (ii) drawing force, (iii) power required if drawing speed is 30 m/min.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):
**Tube Drawing** uses tensile force to pull a tube through a die to reduce diameter and wall thickness.

**Setup (Sketch Components)**:
- Die: Conical die with semi-angle 6°–12°.
- Mandrel (optional): Fixed or floating plug controlling ID.
- Capstan/Block: Pulls the tube at exit.
- Pointer: Swaged end of tube to start the process.

**Types**:
1. **Bare (Sink) Drawing** — No mandrel; OD reduces, wall thins slightly.
2. **Plug (Mandrel) Drawing** — Fixed plug controls both OD and ID.
3. **Floating Plug Drawing** — Self-positioned plug for long lengths.
4. **Ironing** — Wall thickness reduces without OD change.
5. **Rod Drawing** — Solid bar through die (no tube).

**Variables Affecting Drawing Force**:
- Reduction ratio (largest effect)
- Die semi-angle (optimal 6°–12°)
- Coefficient of friction (lubrication critical)
- Drawing speed
- Material flow stress
- Back tension (if any)

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Given**:
- $D_0 = 40$ mm, $t_0 = 5$ mm
- $D_f = 30$ mm, $t_f = 2.5$ mm
- $Y = 180$ MPa, $\sigma_f = 220$ MPa
- $\mu = 0.07$, $\alpha = 8°$, $\eta = 0.85$
- $v = 30$ m/min

**Step 1: Calculate Areas**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_0^2 - (D_0 - 2t_0)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(40^2 - 30^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(700) = 549.78 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_f^2 - (D_f - 2t_f)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(30^2 - 25^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(275) = 216.0 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Drawing Stress**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\sigma_f}{\eta} \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) + 2\mu \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \cot\alpha$$

$$\ln(549.78/216.0) = \ln(2.545) = 0.934$$

$$\cot(8°) = 1/\tan(8°) = 1/0.1405 = 7.115$$

$$\sigma_d = \frac{220}{0.85}(0.934) + 2(0.07)(220)(7.115)$$

$$\sigma_d = 241.7 + 219.1 = 460.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Drawing Force**

$$F_d = \sigma_d \times A_f = 460.8 \times 216.0 = 99{,}533 \text{ N} \approx 99.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Power**

$$P = F_d \times v = 99{,}533 \times (30/60) = 99{,}533 \times 0.5 = 49{,}766 \text{ W} \approx 49.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer**: $\sigma_d = 460.8$ MPa, $F_d = 99.5$ kN, $P = 49.8$ kW.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **direct and indirect extrusion processes** with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)

**(b) A 75 mm diameter cylindrical billet is extruded through a die to produce a 25 mm diameter rod. The flow stress is 100 MPa, μ = 0.08, billet length = 250 mm, container diameter = 75 mm. Calculate (i) ideal extrusion pressure, (ii) actual extrusion pressure with friction, (iii) extrusion force.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):
**Direct Extrusion**:
- Ram pushes the billet **forward** through the die.
- Product moves in the **same direction** as the ram.
- High friction between billet and container wall.
- Limited by billet length (ram travel).

**Indirect (Backward) Extrusion**:
- Die is mounted on a hollow ram.
- Product moves **backward** through the hollow ram.
- **No relative motion** between billet and container → **no friction** at container wall.
- Lower pressure (~20-30% less) and longer products possible.

**Comparison**:

| Feature | Direct | Indirect |
|---|---|---|
| Friction | High | Low |
| Billet length | Short | Long |
| Pressure | Higher | Lower |
| Product length | Limited | Longer possible |
| Billet discard | More | Less |
| Speed | High | Lower |
| Die wear | High | Lower |

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Given**:
- $D_0 = 75$ mm, $D_f = 25$ mm
- $Y_f = 100$ MPa, $\mu = 0.08$
- $L = 250$ mm, $D_{container} = 75$ mm

**Step 1: Areas**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(75)^2 = 4417.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Ideal Extrusion Pressure**

$$P_{ideal} = Y_f \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = 100 \cdot \ln(4417.86/490.87) = 100 \cdot \ln(9) = 100 \times 2.197$$

$$P_{ideal} = 219.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Friction Factor**

$$e^{\mu L/D} = e^{0.08 \times 250/75} = e^{0.2667} = 1.3056$$

**Step 4: Actual Pressure**

$$P_{actual} = 219.7 \times 1.3056 = 286.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Extrusion Force**

$$F = P_{actual} \times A_0 = 286.8 \times 4417.86 = 1{,}266{,}748 \text{ N} \approx 1266.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer**: $P_{ideal} = 219.7$ MPa, $P_{actual} = 286.8$ MPa, $F = 1266.7$ kN.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**:
> 1. **Confusing Drawing Stress with Drawing Force** — Stress is in Pa (or MPa); Force is Stress × Area. Both have different units. (3-mark penalty)
> 2. **Using Diameter in Ln() for Extrusion** — Always use **cross-sectional area** ratios, not diameter ratios. $A_0/A_f$, not $D_0/D_f$.
> 3. **Forgetting Friction in Extrusion Calculations** — Direct extrusion must include the $e^{\mu L/D}$ term. Indirect extrusion may or may not require it.
> 4. **Mixing Up Hot vs Cold Spinning** — Students often interchange the temperatures. Remember: Hot = above recrystallization, Cold = room temperature.
> 5. **Neglecting Drawing Efficiency (η)** — The factor $\eta$ (0.7-0.9) significantly changes drawing stress. Always include it for board exams.
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency** — Areas in mm² × Stress in MPa = N. Don't mix cm and mm. Convert properly.
> 7. **Blank Holder Force in Deep Drawing** — Many students skip this in LDR calculations. Include friction at flange, not just drawing force.
> 8. **Forging Pressure Formula Selection** — Plane strain vs axisymmetric upsetting have different formulas. Read the problem carefully.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Fire Revision Checklist

#### 🔹 Tube Drawing
- ✅ Tube is pulled (in tension) through a conical die.
- ✅ Drawing stress: $\sigma_d = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\eta} \ln(A_0/A_f) + 2\mu \sigma_f \cot\alpha$
- ✅ Drawing force: $F_d = \sigma_d \times A_f$
- ✅ Maximum reduction per pass: 20–45% depending on die angle and friction.
- ✅ Types: Bare, Plug, Floating Plug, Ironing.
- ✅ Critical die angle: 6°–12° (optimum).

#### 🔹 Deep Drawing
- ✅ Converts 2D blank into 3D hollow cup.
- ✅ LDR (Limiting Draw Ratio) = max blank diameter / punch diameter.
- ✅ Wrinkling in flange, tearing at punch radius.
- ✅ Blank holder force is critical parameter.
- ✅ LDR for steel ≈ 2.0, aluminum ≈ 1.8, brass ≈ 2.2.
- ✅ Multiple draws possible with intermediate annealing.

#### 🔹 Coining
- ✅ Closed-die, flashless forging operation.
- ✅ Produces fine surface details (coins, medallions, letters).
- ✅ Very high compressive pressure required.
- ✅ No material flow beyond die cavity.

#### 🔹 Spinning
- ✅ Hot Spinning: T > 0.5 Tm, used for thick hard materials.
- ✅ Cold Spinning: T = room, for ductile materials.
- ✅ Shear Spinning: Wall thickness reduces (cone formation).
- ✅ Conventional Spinning: Wall thickness constant (cup/dome).
- ✅ Force: $F = Y_f \times t \times L$.

#### 🔹 Extrusion
- ✅ Direct: Ram and product same direction (high friction).
- ✅ Indirect: Ram and product opposite direction (low friction).
- ✅ Impact: Cold, high speed (tubes, cans).
- ✅ Hydrostatic: Fluid pressure (best quality).
- ✅ Pressure: $P = Y_f \ln(A_0/A_f) \cdot e^{\mu L/D}$.
- ✅ Defects: Pipe (center burst), surface cracks, die swell.

#### 🔹 Forging
- ✅ Open Die: Material flows freely.
- ✅ Closed Die: Material confined in die cavity; flash forms.
- ✅ Temperature regimes: Cold (< 0.3 Tm), Warm (0.3-0.5 Tm), Hot (> 0.5 Tm).
- ✅ Forging load (upsetting): $F = Y_f \cdot A_0 \cdot e^{\mu \pi D/(2h)}$.
- ✅ Grain flow follows part contour → improved strength.
- ✅ Drop forging, press forging, roll forging, upset forging.

### Memorization Mnemonics
- **"T-P-D-S-E-F"** for Module 3 forming processes: **T**ube drawing, **P**ressing (deep drawing), **D**rawing, **S**pinning, **E**xtrusion, **F**orging.
- **"LDR = D_blank / D_punch"** for deep drawing — the *Limiting* (maximum) ratio.
- **"Hot Half, Cold Low"** for forging temperatures: Hot > 0.5 Tm, Cold < 0.3 Tm.
- **"Direct Drag, Indirect Idle"** — Direct extrusion has friction (drag); Indirect has minimal friction (idle container).

### KTU Frequently Asked Topics
1. Tube drawing force formula derivation.
2. Deep drawing defects and prevention.
3. Direct vs Indirect Extrusion comparison.
4. Forging load calculation (open die).
5. Hot vs Cold Spinning differences.
6. LDR significance and values.
7. Extrusion pressure with friction term.
8. Coining process and applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Dies - Types of Forging - Open
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools and Dies - Types of Forging - Open

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Open Die Forging** (also called *Smith Forging*, *Hand Forging*, or *Free Forging*) is a hot/cold working manufacturing process in which deformation of the workpiece is carried out between **two flat or simple-shaped dies** that do not completely enclose the material. The metal is free to flow laterally (laterally unrestricted) except at the immediate contact surfaces with the dies. The required shape is obtained by manipulating and translating the workpiece between successive blows or press strokes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Open die forging is performed in a *smithy* using flat dies (or swage blocks) where the work material is never fully confined — only localized compression is applied.

### 1.2 Key Tools and Dies Used in Open Forging

| Tool / Die | Function |
|------------|----------|
| **Anvil** (flat or beak/horn) | Stationary lower die surface; provides support and shapes the workpiece |
| **Sledge / Hand Hammer** | Light tool used by the striker/holder-down |
| **Tongs** | Grip and manipulate the hot workpiece |
| **Swage Block** | Hardened steel block with shaped holes, grooves, and slots for forming curves, rounds, and hexagonal sections |
| **Fullers** (top and bottom) | Used to reduce cross-section by necking down material |
| **Flatters** | Smooth and finish flat surfaces |
| **Punches & Drifts** | Used to open up holes in the workpiece |
| **Mandrels** | Used for forging rings / hollow shafts |
| **Hardies** | Cutting tools (hot/cold sets) inserted in the anvil |
| **Hot Sets** | Cutting chisels operated at forging temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> In **hand forging**, the anvil serves as the lower die, and the hammer head serves as the upper die. The dies are therefore essentially "open" — no cavity exists to confine the metal flow.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you have a piece of **clay** in your hands. You place it on a flat wooden board (the lower die/anvil) and press it down with another flat wooden block (the upper die/hammer). The clay **spreads out sideways** because nothing is stopping it. By repeatedly pressing and moving the clay around, you can form a rough bar, a flattened disc, or taper one end. This is *exactly* how open die forging works — except with **heated metal (red-hot steel)** instead of clay, and with much greater force (hundreds of kN to MN).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** Open die = "**O**pen air" — the metal is exposed on the sides during deformation.

### 1.4 Forging vs. Casting vs. Machining — Why Forging?

> [!NOTE]
> Forging produces a **continuous grain flow** aligned with the part geometry, giving superior **toughness, fatigue strength, and impact resistance** compared to cast parts.

### 1.5 Classification of Forging Operations (Open Die)

Open die forging is broadly classified into the following operations:

1. **Upsetting** (Cross-section increases, length decreases)
2. **Drawing Out / Cogging** (Length increases, cross-section decreases)
3. **Bending**
4. **Punching / Drifting**
5. **Cutting / Hot Cutting** (using chisels)
6. **Swaging** (using swage blocks)
7. **Forge Welding** (joining by hammering)
8. **Fullering and Edging**

Standard Forging Temperature Range for Mild Steel: **900 °C to 1200 °C** (upper limit = *forging temperature*; lower limit = *finishing temperature*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — "Why" and "How"

The fundamental principle of open die forging is **plastic deformation under compressive loading** without lateral confinement. The deformation mechanics are governed by the **plastic flow of metal** when subjected to stresses exceeding the **flow stress (σf)** of the material at the working temperature.

**Step-by-step logic of the deformation process:**

- The hammer/press applies a vertical compressive force **F** on the workpiece.
- This creates a **state of triaxial compressive stress** within the deformation zone.
- Because the dies are flat, the workpiece bulges **sideways (barreling)** due to **friction at the die–workpiece interface** which restrains lateral flow.
- The **true strain (ε)** imparted per blow governs the incremental shape change.
- Multiple blows + repositioning (translation) allow the part to gradually approach the desired geometry.

### 2.2 Forces and Pressures in Upsetting

In an open die upsetting operation, the forging force **F** is given by:

$$F = \sigma_f \times A$$

Where:
- **F** = forging force (in N or kN)
- **σf** = flow stress of the material at the forging temperature (in MPa or N/mm²)
- **A** = instantaneous contact area between die and workpiece (in mm² or m²)

Due to **interface friction**, the actual force is higher than the ideal (frictionless) case by a factor called the **forging load factor (Kf)**, which depends on the **interface friction factor (m)** and the **geometry ratio (h/d)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / KTU Use |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Forging Force (Basic) | $F = \sigma_f \cdot A$ | Used for first-order estimation |
| 2 | True Strain (Upsetting) | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$ | Strain imparted in height reduction |
| 3 | Volume Constancy | $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$ | $h_0, A_0$ = initial; $h_f, A_f$ = final |
| 4 | Forging Load Factor (Slab Analysis) | $K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu \cdot d}{3 \cdot h}$ | Approximation for cylindrical upset (d = dia., h = height) |
| 5 | Max. Upset Ratio (Buckle Limit) | $\dfrac{h_0}{d_0} \leq 2.5$ | Above this, buckling occurs; not a buckle-free condition |
| 6 | Forging Pressure | $p = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Average die pressure |
| 7 | Hammer Energy (Kinetic) | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} M v^2 = W h_{\text{drop}}$ | Energy delivered per blow |
| 8 | Flow Stress (Avg.) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{\sigma_0 + \sigma_u}{2}$ | Used when only yield and ultimate known |
| 9 | Press Capacity (Tonnage) | $T = F_{\text{max}}$ (in tons) | Hydraulic/mechanical press rating |
| 10 | Strain Rate Sensitivity | $\sigma_f = K \dot{\varepsilon}^m$ | Important for hammer (high $\dot{\varepsilon}$) vs press (low $\dot{\varepsilon}$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Use $K_f = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{\mu \cdot d}{h}$ as the textbook slab-analysis approximation when friction factor $\mu$ is given.

### 2.4 Critical Rule — Bulging and Buckling Limits

A cylindrical workpiece being upset in open dies exhibits **barreling** due to frictional restraint. Buckling occurs if:

$$\frac{h_0}{d_0} > 2.5 \quad \text{(or } 2.0 \text{ in more conservative texts)}$$

To prevent buckling, a **backup plate (follower plate)** is used, or the height is reduced in steps.

### 2.5 Effect of Friction Factor on Load

| Interface Condition | Friction Factor (m or μ) |
|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Hot die, lubricated (graphite) | **0.05 – 0.10** |
| Hot die, no lubricant (dry) | **0.20 – 0.40** |
| Cold forging, lubricated | **0.05 – 0.15** |

Higher friction → Higher $K_f$ → **Larger force required** → **More barreling**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power generation**: Large rotors and shafts (up to 600 tonnes) are made by open die forging.
- **Shipbuilding**: Propeller shafts, rudder stocks, and hull forgings.
- **Aerospace**: Preforms for closed-die forging (billet preparation).
- **Defense**: Gun barrels, tank tracks, artillery components.
- **Heavy industry**: Crankshafts, rolling mill rolls, gear blanks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Forging Load Factor (Slab Method) for Cylindrical Upsetting

**Given:** A solid cylinder of initial height $h_0$ and diameter $d_0$ is upset in open flat dies under friction coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Stress state assumption (Slab analysis):**

Assume a thin horizontal slice (slab) of the cylinder of thickness $dz$ at height $z$ from the bottom die. Apply equilibrium in the vertical (axial) direction. The compressive axial stress is $\sigma_z$, and the radial (hoop) compressive stress is $\sigma_r$.

**Step 2 — Yield criterion (von Mises for upsetting):**

In plastic deformation under axial compression with radial confinement:

$$\sigma_z - \sigma_r = -Y_f = -\sigma_f$$

(Where $Y_f$ is the uniaxial flow stress.)

**Step 3 — Interface friction shear stress:**

The friction shear at the die–workpiece interface is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \sigma_r$$

**Step 4 — Force balance on the slab (considering friction acting inward at the periphery):**

A slice of radius $r$ experiences an axial force on top, axial force on bottom, and shear friction on the lateral curved surface (projected to the axial direction):

$$(\sigma_z + d\sigma_z)(\pi r^2) - \sigma_z (\pi r^2) = -\mu \sigma_r (2\pi r dz)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute from Step 2:** $\sigma_r = \sigma_z + \sigma_f$ (taking compressive signs):

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi r^2 d\sigma_z &= -\mu (\sigma_z + \sigma_f) (2\pi r dz) \\[4pt]
d\sigma_z &= -\dfrac{2\mu}{r} (\sigma_z + \sigma_f) \, dz
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate across the height (z = 0 to z = h), noting $r \approx d/2$ for thin slices:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{h} \dfrac{d\sigma_z}{\sigma_z + \sigma_f} &= -\int_{0}^{h} \dfrac{2\mu}{r} dz \\[4pt]
\ln(\sigma_z + \sigma_f) \Big|_{0}^{h} &= -\dfrac{2\mu h}{r} \\[4pt]
\sigma_{z,\text{top}} - \sigma_{z,\text{bottom}} &= \text{pressure difference}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions:**

At $z = h/2$ (mid-height), the radial stress equals half of the axial (symmetry):

$$\sigma_r (z=h/2) = -\frac{\sigma_f}{2}$$

(approx., for unconfined upset)

**Step 8 — Solve for the maximum die pressure $p$:**

$$\boxed{p = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \dfrac{2\mu r}{3h} \right) = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3h} \right)}$$

This is the **Siebel-Slaby approximation** for the average forging pressure in cylindrical open-die upsetting. The factor in parentheses is the **forging load factor $K_f$**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Upset Force Calculation Steps

| Step | Action | Equation / Logic |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Identify $h_0$, $d_0$, $h_f$ | From problem data |
| 2 | Apply volume constancy | $d_f = d_0 \sqrt{h_0 / h_f}$ |
| 3 | Calculate true strain | $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$ |
| 4 | Find flow stress | $\sigma_f$ at forging temp. & strain rate |
| 5 | Compute $K_f$ | $K_f = 1 + (\mu d_f)/(3 h_f)$ |
| 6 | Compute final area | $A_f = \pi d_f^2 / 4$ |
| 7 | Compute forging force | $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_f$ |
| 8 | Add 25–30% safety margin | $F_{\text{design}} = 1.3 F$ |

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A solid cylindrical billet of mild steel ($d_0 = 50$ mm, $h_0 = 100$ mm) is upset in open flat dies at 1100 °C to a final height $h_f = 50$ mm. Given $\sigma_f = 80$ MPa and friction coefficient $\mu = 0.3$. Calculate the (a) true strain, (b) final diameter, (c) forging load factor, and (d) total forging force.

**Solution:**

**(a) True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{100}{50} \right) = \ln 2 = 0.693$$

**(b) Final diameter (using volume constancy):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_f &= d_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}} \\[4pt]
d_f &= 50 \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{50}} = 50 \sqrt{2} \approx 70.71 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Forging Load Factor:**

$$K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu \cdot d_f}{3 \cdot h_f} = 1 + \dfrac{0.3 \times 70.71}{3 \times 50} = 1 + 0.1414 = 1.1414$$

**(d) Total Forging Force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_f &= \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (70.71)^2}{4} \approx 3927 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
F &= K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_f \\[4pt]
F &= 1.1414 \times 80 \times 3927 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
F &\approx 358{,}560 \text{ N} \approx 358.6 \text{ kN} \approx 36.6 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A hydraulic press of minimum **40-tonne capacity** is recommended (with safety factor).

### 3.4 Energy Required in Hammer Forging

$$E_{\text{req}} = F \times \delta_{\text{blow}}$$

Where $\delta_{\text{blow}}$ is the height reduction per blow (typically 2–5% of height for hammers).

The hammer must deliver kinetic energy:

$$E_k = W \times h_{\text{drop}} = M g h_{\text{drop}}$$

Where $W$ = ram weight, $h_{\text{drop}}$ = drop height. For board hammers, $h_{\text{drop}}$ ≈ 0.5–1.0 m.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open Die Forging — Setup Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Heated Workpiece at 1100 C] --> B[Placed on Anvil Lower Die]
    B --> C{Hammer Stroke}
    C --> D[Compressive Force Applied]
    D --> E[Plastic Deformation - Metal Flows Laterally]
    E --> F[Barreling Effect Due to Friction]
    F --> G[Reposition and Re-blow]
    G --> H[Translate to Next Section]
    H --> I{Desired Shape Achieved?}
    I -->|No| C
    I -->|Yes| J[Final Forged Component]
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style J fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ffd43b,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffa94d,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Tools Layout Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ForgingToolset[Open Die Forging Tools and Dies]
        T1[Anvil Flat Bottom Die]
        T2[Hammer Head Upper Die]
        T3[Swage Block with Grooves]
        T4[Fuller Top and Bottom]
        T5[Flatter]
        T6[Hot Set Chisel]
        T7[Punch and Drift]
        T8[Mandrel]
    end
    
    T1 --> Op1[Upsetting Operation]
    T2 --> Op1
    T1 --> Op2[Drawing Out Operation]
    T2 --> Op2
    T3 --> Op3[Swaging Curves]
    T4 --> Op4[Fullering Necking]
    T5 --> Op5[Surface Smoothing]
    T6 --> Op6[Hot Cutting]
    T7 --> Op7[Hole Piercing]
    T8 --> Op8[Ring Forging]
    
    style T1 fill:#74c0fc
    style T2 fill:#74c0fc
    style T3 fill:#b197fc
    style T4 fill:#b197fc
    style Op1 fill:#ffe066
    style Op2 fill:#ffe066
```

### 4.3 Forging Operation Flow Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Billet Heated to Forging Temp] --> B[Upsetting]
    B --> C[Drawing Out - Cogging]
    C --> D[Bending if Required]
    D --> E[Punching if Hollow Needed]
    E --> F[Swaging for Round Sections]
    F --> G[Trimming and Finishing]
    G --> H[Heat Treatment]
    H --> I[Inspection and Dispatch]
    
    style A fill:#ff8787
    style I fill:#69db7c
    style C fill:#ffa94d
    style E fill:#ffa94d
    style H fill:#74c0fc
```

### 4.4 Stress State During Upsetting (Mermaid Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DieInterface[Top Die Flat Surface]
        D1[Ffriction Force Acts Downward on Workpiece]
    end
    
    subgraph Workpiece[Hot Workpiece Cylinder]
        W1[Vertical Compressive Stress sigma_z]
        W2[Radial Hoop Compressive Stress sigma_r]
        W3[Axial Compressive Force F]
    end
    
    subgraph BottomDie[Bottom Die Flat Anvil]
        B1[Reaction Force Acts Upward]
    end
    
    D1 --> W1
    W1 --> W2
    W2 --> W3
    W3 --> B1
    
    style W1 fill:#ffd8a8
    style W2 fill:#ffd8a8
    style W3 fill:#ffd8a8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define open die forging. Mention any two tools used in it.*

**Model Answer:**

Open die forging (also called smith or hand forging) is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation is carried out between **two flat or simple-shaped dies** that do not completely enclose the workpiece, allowing the material to **flow laterally** during compression. It is typically performed at elevated temperatures (900–1200 °C for steel) using a hammer or press.

**Two tools used:**
1. **Anvil** — Acts as the lower die; flat or with a horn (beak).
2. **Swage Block** — A multi-holed hardened steel block used to forge round, square, and hexagonal sections.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Fuller, Flatter, Hot Set, Punch.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the maximum upset ratio for a cylindrical billet in open die forging? Why should it not be exceeded?*

**Model Answer:**

The maximum upset ratio is:

$$\dfrac{h_0}{d_0} \leq 2.5$$

If this ratio is exceeded, the cylindrical billet undergoes **buckling (Euler-type instability)** rather than uniform plastic deformation, because the slender column becomes unstable under axial compression. The result is a **"folded" or "lapped" defect** in the forging, rendering the component defective.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Open Die Forging Problems:**
> Students frequently lose marks by (1) **forgetting to apply volume constancy** to find the final diameter, (2) **omitting the forging load factor $K_f$** (friction effect), and (3) **using the initial area** instead of the **final contact area** in the force equation. Always show: $d_f = d_0\sqrt{h_0/h_f}$ → $A_f$ → $F = K_f \sigma_f A_f$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of forging operations in open die forging with neat sketches. List the various tools and dies used in hand forging. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical steel billet of diameter 60 mm and height 120 mm is upset in open flat dies at 1100 °C. The flow stress of the material at this temperature is 75 MPa. The friction coefficient at the die–workpiece interface is 0.25. The final height after upsetting is 40 mm. Calculate:
- (i) The true strain imparted.
- (ii) The final diameter of the billet.
- (iii) The forging load factor.
- (iv) The maximum forging force required. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Classification and Tools (7 Marks):**

**Classification of Open Die Forging Operations:**

| Sl. No. | Operation | Purpose |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Upsetting** | Increase cross-section; reduce height |
| 2 | **Drawing Out / Cogging** | Increase length; reduce cross-section |
| 3 | **Bending** | Form angular or curved shapes |
| 4 | **Punching / Drifting** | Pierce a hole through the workpiece |
| 5 | **Cutting / Hot Setting** | Cut the workpiece into pieces |
| 6 | **Swaging** | Form round, square, or tapered sections |
| 7 | **Fullering** | Localize material for necking down |
| 8 | **Forge Welding** | Join two pieces by hammering at welding temp. |

**Tools and Dies in Hand Forging:**

1. **Anvil** (flat top, beak, and step) — Lower die.
2. **Sledge Hammer** — Heavy hammer held by striker.
3. **Hand Hammer** — Used by the smith.
4. **Tongs** — Hold and rotate the workpiece.
5. **Swage Block** — Hardened multi-shaped block.
6. **Fullers (Top & Bottom)** — V-shaped groove to neck material.
7. **Flatters** — Finish flat surfaces.
8. **Hot Sets / Hardies** — Chisels for hot cutting.
9. **Punches & Drifts** — Open up holes.
10. **Mandrels** — For hollow forging (rings, hubs).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $d_0 = 60$ mm, $h_0 = 120$ mm, $h_f = 40$ mm, $\sigma_f = 75$ MPa, $\mu = 0.25$.

**(i) True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln \left( \dfrac{120}{40} \right) = \ln 3 = 1.0986$$

**(ii) Final Diameter (Volume Constancy):**

$$d_f = d_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{h_0}{h_f}} = 60 \sqrt{\dfrac{120}{40}} = 60 \sqrt{3} \approx 103.92 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Forging Load Factor:**

$$K_f = 1 + \dfrac{\mu d_f}{3 h_f} = 1 + \dfrac{0.25 \times 103.92}{3 \times 40} = 1 + 0.2165 = 1.2165$$

**(iv) Forging Force:**

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (103.92)^2}{4} \approx 8482 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_f = 1.2165 \times 75 \times 8482 \approx 773{,}920 \text{ N} \approx 774 \text{ kN} \approx 78.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

> With a 30% safety factor, **a 100-tonne press** should be selected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a stress element, derive the **forging pressure equation for open die cylindrical upsetting** using the slab method. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare open die forging and closed die forging on the basis of: (i) die cost, (ii) part complexity, (iii) tolerance achievable, (iv) production rate, (v) grain flow, and (vi) typical application. List the **advantages and limitations** of open die forging. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Forging Pressure (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Workpiece is a solid cylinder.
2. Material is rigid-plastic; flow stress $\sigma_f$ is constant during deformation.
3. Friction coefficient $\mu$ is constant.
4. Plane strain / axial symmetry conditions apply.
5. Dies are rigid; elastic deformation of dies is negligible.

**Stress Element Diagram (Textual):**

A cylindrical element at height $z$ with radius $r$ experiences:
- Axial stress: $\sigma_z$ (compressive)
- Radial stress: $\sigma_r$ (compressive, from lateral confinement)
- Shear at interface: $\tau = \mu \sigma_r$

**Equilibrium of the element:**

$$\dfrac{d\sigma_z}{dz} = -\dfrac{2 \mu \sigma_r}{r}$$

**Yield criterion (von Mises):**

$$\sigma_z - \sigma_r = -\sigma_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_r = \sigma_z + \sigma_f$$

**Substituting and integrating** from $z = -h/2$ to $z = +h/2$:

$$p = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \dfrac{2 \mu r}{3h} \right) = \sigma_f \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3h} \right)$$

$$\boxed{F = p \cdot A = \sigma_f A \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3h} \right)}$$

**Part (b) — Comparison and List (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Open Die Forging | Closed Die Forging |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Die Cost | **Low** (flat dies) | **High** (cavity dies) |
| Part Complexity | **Simple** shapes | **Complex** 3D shapes |
| Dimensional Tolerance | ±1.5 to ±5 mm (poor) | ±0.25 to ±0.75 mm (good) |
| Production Rate | **Low** (manual/semi-auto) | **High** (automated) |
| Grain Flow | **Approximate** (not optimal) | **Precise** (die-controlled) |
| Typical Application | Large shafts, rotors, rings | Gears, connecting rods, crankshafts |
| Skill Required | **High** (smith's skill) | **Moderate** (machine-controlled) |
| Wastage (flash) | **Minimal** | **Significant** (flash loss) |

**Advantages of Open Die Forging:**
1. Low tooling cost — accessible to small workshops.
2. Can produce very large parts (>100 tonnes).
3. Excellent for short production runs and prototypes.
4. Preserves favorable grain flow along the part axis.
5. High flexibility in geometry.

**Limitations of Open Die Forging:**
1. Poor dimensional accuracy.
2. Low production rate — labor-intensive.
3. Requires highly skilled smiths.
4. Limited to simple shapes (shafts, bars, rings).
5. Difficult to achieve thin sections and sharp corners.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Open Die Forging**

- [x] **Definition:** Open die forging = plastic deformation between flat/simple dies with **no lateral confinement**; also called **smith / hand / free forging**.
- [x] **Tools (must remember):** Anvil, Sledge, Hand Hammer, Tongs, Swage Block, Fuller, Flatter, Hot Set, Punch, Drift, Mandrel.
- [x] **Forging Temperature for Mild Steel:** **900 °C – 1200 °C**.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $F = \sigma_f A \left( 1 + \dfrac{\mu d}{3h} \right)$.
- [x] **Volume Constancy:** $A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f \Rightarrow d_f = d_0\sqrt{h_0/h_f}$.
- [x] **True Strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.
- [x] **Buckling Limit:** $h_0/d_0 \leq 2.5$ — never exceed this for a solid cylinder.
- [x] **Operations:** Upsetting, Drawing, Bending, Punching, Swaging, Fullering, Cutting, Welding.
- [x] **Hammer vs. Press:** Hammers deliver **kinetic energy** (strain rate dependent); presses deliver **static force** (lower strain rate, more uniform deformation).
- [x] **Always include $K_f$** in KTU force problems (friction is never zero in reality).
- [x] **Safety factor:** Add **25–30%** margin on calculated force for press selection.
- [x] **Common defect:** Buckling (exceed $h_0/d_0$) and laps/folds (improper translation).
- [x] **Friction Coefficient Hot, Lubricated ≈ 0.05–0.10**; **Hot, Dry ≈ 0.20–0.40**.
- [x] **High-Yield Real-World Examples:** Ship propeller shafts, power plant rotors, turbine shafts, gun barrels, armor plates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 KTU Mark Losers in This Topic:**
> 1. Using **initial area** instead of **final area** in the force equation.
> 2. Forgetting **volume constancy** step.
> 3. Ignoring the **friction load factor** $K_f$.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Tools and Dies / Types of Forging — Open (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Closed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Topic Interpretation in KTU Context

In the context of the **PBAUT404 – Industrial Manufacturing Process** syllabus (2024 Scheme) under Module 3 (Bulk Deformation & Forging Processes), the topic **"Closed"** refers to **Closed Die Forging** (also called **Impression Die Forging** or **Closed Impression Die Forging**). It is one of the most widely used forging processes in the automotive and heavy engineering industries for producing high-strength, near-net-shape components.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed Die Forging (Impression Die Forging):**
> A hot/cold bulk deformation process in which a heated metal billet (workpiece) is plastically deformed between two contoured dies (upper and lower) that contain a precisely machined cavity (the "impression"). The metal is forced to flow into the die cavity, taking the exact shape of the impression, while a small amount of excess material escapes as **flash** through a thin gutter, ensuring complete filling of the cavity.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition Pattern)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Impression** | The precisely machined cavity inside the die that defines the final shape of the forging |
| **Flash** | A thin excess layer of metal that escapes between the die halves during forging |
| **Flash Land** | A thin peripheral region of the die that controls flash thickness and resists metal flow |
| **Gutter** | A reservoir surrounding the impression that traps the escaping flash material |
| **Billet** | The initial cylindrical/square workpiece placed in the die cavity |
| **Draft Angle** | A small taper (typically **3°–7°**) on die walls to allow easy removal of the forged part |
| **Parting Line** | The line where the upper and lower die halves meet |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Waffle Iron Model"**
> Think of closed die forging exactly like a **waffle iron** or a **cookie press**:
> 1. You take soft, hot dough (the heated billet) and place it inside a closed mold (the die).
> 2. You squeeze the two halves together (the die press stroke).
> 3. The dough is forced to fill every nook and cranny of the mold's shape.
> 4. A small amount of excess dough oozes out from the edges — that is the **flash**.
> 5. After cooling, you open the mold and pop out a perfectly shaped waffle/cookie.
>
> The **flash is not waste** — it is intentionally designed to **resist the escape of metal**, thereby building up internal pressure that forces the metal to fill the deepest corners of the die cavity completely. Without flash, your forging would have **incomplete filling** (a defect).

## 1.4 Distinction: Closed Die vs Open Die Forging

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do not confuse **Closed Die Forging** with **Open Die Forging** or **Upset Forging**. KTU examiners frequently test this comparison.

| Feature | Open Die Forging | Closed Die Forging |
|---------|------------------|--------------------|
| Die Shape | Flat or simple V-shape | Contoured impression |
| Material Flow | Free flow, no confinement | Confined within die cavity |
| Flash | No flash | Flash is formed intentionally |
| Accuracy | Low (±several mm) | High (±0.25 to ±0.8 mm) |
| Production Rate | Low | High (suited for mass production) |
| Cost per Part | High (manual labor) | Low (mechanized) |
| Typical Parts | Shafts, rings, disks | Connecting rods, gears, crankshafts |

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Coefficient of friction (μ)** in hot forging: **0.2 – 0.4** (lubricated: **0.05 – 0.1**)
- **Typical flash thickness:** **1.5 mm to 3 mm** (standard KTU exam value: **2 mm**)
- **Draft angle:** **3° – 7°** (standard: **5°**)
- **Flash land width:** **3 mm to 10 mm**
- **Forging temperature for steel:** **900 °C – 1250 °C** (above recrystallization temperature)
- **Typical forging pressure:** **500 – 900 MPa** (for steel in cold forging)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of Closed Die Forging

The closed die forging process follows a precise sequence of operations that KTU examiners love to test as a short-answer question (3 marks). The complete cycle is:

1. **Billet Preparation:** A cylindrical or square billet is cut to a precise volume (slightly more than the cavity volume) and heated to the forging temperature in a furnace.
2. **Die Lubrication:** A graphite-based or water-based lubricant is sprayed onto the die cavity to reduce die wear and friction.
3. **Placement & Forging Stroke:** The hot billet is placed on the lower die. The upper die descends with high force (from a hammer or press), compressing the billet.
4. **Flash Formation:** As the dies close, excess material is squeezed out into the gutter through the flash land — this **flash acts as a flow resistor**.
5. **Cavity Filling:** The pressure build-up due to flash resistance forces the metal to completely fill the die cavity, replicating the impression geometry.
6. **Die Opening & Part Ejection:** The upper die retracts, and the part (with flash attached) is ejected using a knockout pin.
7. **Trimming:** The flash is sheared off in a separate trimming press to obtain the final forging.

## 2.2 The Critical Role of Flash

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Flash Important?**
> Flash is the **single most important design feature** of a closed die. It performs three critical functions:
> 1. **Flow Restriction:** The thin flash land creates a high resistance to material flow, which **increases die cavity pressure by 3 to 10 times**. This ensures sharp corner filling.
> 2. **Excess Material Reservoir:** It accommodates the surplus billet volume.
> 3. **Defect Indicator:** An incomplete flash indicates insufficient material, while an excessively thick flash indicates poor die design or process parameter mismatch.

## 2.3 Classification of Closed Die Forging

KTU syllabus distinguishes between:

- **Single-Impression Die Forging:** Used for simple shapes; one die pair produces the final shape in one stroke.
- **Multi-Impression (Progressive) Die Forging:** Used for complex shapes. The billet passes through several die stations (cavities), with each station performing one operation (bending, blocking, finisher).

**Standard sequence in multi-impression forging:**
> **Bender** → **Blocker (Pre-form)** → **Finisher (Final Impression)**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on Closed Die Forging.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Forging Force (Ideal, no friction)** | $F = Y_f \cdot A$ | N | Minimum force (theoretical) |
| 2 | **Forging Force (with friction & flash)** | $F = Y_f \cdot A \cdot K_f \cdot K_{flash}$ | N | **Most used in KTU** |
| 3 | **Shape Factor $K_f$** | $K_f = 1 + 0.25 \cdot \frac{D}{h}$ | — | Upsetting of cylinder |
| 4 | **Flash Correction Factor $K_{flash}$** | $K_{flash} = 1 + \frac{A_{flash}}{A_{cavity}}$ | — | Accounts for flash resistance |
| 5 | **Flash Land Area** | $A_{flash} = w_{land} \cdot L_{land}$ | mm² | Rectangular flash land |
| 6 | **Projected Area of Forging** | $A = W \cdot L$ (rect.) or $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | mm² | Used in pressure calculation |
| 7 | **Average Flow Stress $Y_f$** | $Y_f = \frac{Y_{max} + Y_{min}}{2}$ | MPa | Hot forging |
| 8 | **Die Closing Pressure** | $P = \frac{F}{A_{projected}}$ | MPa | Die design check |
| 9 | **Billet Volume (with flash allowance)** | $V_{billet} = V_{cavity} + V_{flash} + V_{burn}$ | mm³ | Process planning |
| 10 | **Flash Allowance (% of part wt.)** | $V_{flash} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20 \times V_{cavity}$ | — | Empirical rule |
| 11 | **Energy for Forging Stroke** | $W = \int_0^{h_0} F \cdot dh$ | J | Hammer/press selection |
| 12 | **Work Done in Closed Die (approx.)** | $W = Y_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | J | Energy balance |
| 13 | **Trim Force for Flash** | $F_{trim} = 0.75 \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau_{shear}$ | N | Trimming operation |
| 14 | **Die Wear Volume (Archard's Eq.)** | $V_{wear} = K \cdot \frac{P \cdot L}{H}$ | mm³ | Die life estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention Note:** The term $Y_f$ denotes **average flow stress** of the workpiece material at forging temperature. The "multiplier approach" $F = Y_f \cdot A \cdot K_f \cdot K_{flash}$ is the **gold-standard equation** KTU examiners expect in Part B (14-mark) numerical problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Closed die forging is the **backbone of the automotive forging industry**. Components produced include:
- **Connecting rods** (must withstand cyclic fatigue at 50–80 MPa for 10⁹ cycles)
- **Crankshafts** (after forging, finish-machined)
- **Gears and gear blanks** (with near-net shape to minimize subsequent machining)
- **Bevel gears, splined shafts, flanges, yokes**
- **Aircraft structural components** (Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel forgings for jet engine mounts)

The process delivers superior **grain flow continuity**, which is critical for fatigue life — a key reason forged crankshafts last longer than cast ones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forging Force in Closed Die (with Friction + Flash)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Part B questions. We derive the expression for total forging force accounting for friction and flash resistance.

### Given Data
- Workpiece: **Cylindrical billet**, initial diameter $D_0$, initial height $h_0$
- Forging flow stress: $Y_f$ (MPa)
- Coefficient of friction at die-workpiece interface: $\mu$
- Projected area of forging: $A$ (mm²)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Instantaneous Geometry During Compression**

As the die closes, the billet of height $h$ has an instantaneous diameter $D$, related by **volume constancy** (incompressibility):

$$
A_0 \cdot h_0 = A \cdot h
$$

Here, $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area. Hence:

$$
h = \frac{A_0 \cdot h_0}{A}
$$

**Step 2: Express the Forging Pressure at Any Instant**

The instantaneous forging pressure $p$ (assuming uniform pressure distribution on the workpiece) is the sum of the **flow stress term** and the **friction term**:

$$
p = Y_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot D}{3h}\right)
$$

**Why the $\frac{\mu D}{3h}$ term?**
> This is the **slab-method (upper-bound slip-line) result** for friction in upsetting. The factor of $\frac{1}{3}$ arises from integrating the linear pressure distribution along the die-workpiece contact (the friction shear $\tau = \mu p$ varies from a maximum at the center to zero at the periphery, giving an average of $\frac{1}{3} \mu p \cdot \frac{D}{2h}$, simplified to $\frac{\mu D}{3h}$).

**Step 3: Compute Instantaneous Forging Force**

$$
F = p \cdot A = Y_f \cdot A \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot D}{3h}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Apply Volume Constancy to Express $D$ and $h$ in Terms of Final Geometry**

For a cylindrical workpiece reaching a final height $h_f$ and final diameter $D_f$:

$$
D^2 \cdot h = D_0^2 \cdot h_0
$$

Hence:

$$
\frac{D}{h} = \frac{D_0^2 \cdot h_0}{D^3}
$$

For simplicity, KTU problems typically use the **final-state expression**, substituting $D = D_f$ and $h = h_f$:

$$
F = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot D_f}{3 \cdot h_f}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Introduce the Shape Factor $K_f$ and Flash Factor $K_{flash}$**

The empirical KTU formula combines everything:

$$
F = Y_f \cdot A_f \cdot K_f \cdot K_{flash}
$$

Where:

$$
K_f = 1 + 0.25 \cdot \frac{D_f}{h_f}
$$

$$
K_{flash} = 1 + \frac{A_{flash}}{A_{cavity}}
$$
