| 1. Energy source | Plasma jet | Spark/electrical discharge | Photon (light) beam |
| 2. Workpiece material | Conductive metals | Conductive only | Conductive & non-conductive |
| 3. Temperature of heat source | 11,000–28,000 °C | 8,000–12,000 °C | ~4,000 °C (surface) |
| 4. Power density (W/mm²) | 10⁶–10⁸ | 10⁵–10⁷ | 10⁶–10⁹ |
| 5. MRR | Very high (kg/hr) | Low (g/hr) | Moderate |
| 6. Surface finish (Ra) | 5–25 μm | 0.2–1.5 μm (best) | 1–10 μm |
| 7. HAZ | Large | Small | Very small |
| 8. Dielectric/medium | Plasma gas | Dielectric oil | Assist gas |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write **"temperature of LBM > PAM"** — this is WRONG. The *bulk plasma temperature* of PAM is the highest among the three; however, the *localized material heating* depends on absorption coefficient. Always state the **arc temperature** for PAM, not the workpiece surface temperature.
> 2. Don't confuse **MRR with MRV** (material removal volume). KTU expects volume (mm³/min) or mass (g/min), not just a generic "rate."
> 3. Avoid writing one-word comparisons like "**PAM: High, EDM: Low**" — you **must elaborate** with units or numeric ranges to fetch full marks.

---

#### **🔹 Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and components of a plasma torch**. List the **functions of pilot arc, shield gas, and constricting nozzle**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The **plasma arc cutting of a 10 mm thick mild steel plate** is carried out with the following data:
- Arc voltage = 120 V
- Arc current = 350 A
- Traverse speed = 6 mm/s
- Kerf width = 3.5 mm
- Process efficiency, η = 0.6

Calculate: **(i)** Material Removal Rate (MRR) in mm³/s. **(ii)** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in J/mm³.

---

##### ✏️ Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Construction of a Plasma Torch (4 marks):**

The plasma torch is the heart of the PAM system. Its main components are:

1. **Tungsten Electrode (Cathode):** Made of tungsten (sometimes thoriated tungsten), it serves as the source of electrons and forms one terminal of the arc circuit.
2. **Water-Cooled Copper Nozzle (Anode / Constricting Nozzle):** A precisely bored copper nozzle that constricts the arc and also acts as the initial anode during pilot arc operation.
3. **Cooling Jacket:** Water circulating channels around the nozzle to dissipate the intense heat (preventing nozzle melting).
4. **Gas Distribution Chamber:** Distributes the plasma gas uniformly around the electrode before exit.
5. **Shield Gas Nozzle (Outer):** Provides an annular flow of secondary gas (CO₂/air) to protect the nozzle and shape the plasma jet.
6. **Insulator Body:** Electrically isolates the cathode and anode sections of the torch.

**Functions (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
- **Pilot Arc:** A low-power, high-frequency arc used to **pre-ionize the gas** and establish a conductive path before the main transferred arc can strike between the electrode and the workpiece. It ensures **reliable arc initiation** at low voltages.
- **Shield Gas:** A secondary gas (CO₂ or compressed air) that **(i)** cools the nozzle externally, **(ii)** prevents double-arcing between nozzle and workpiece, and **(iii)** helps shape the plasma jet for cleaner cuts.
- **Constricting Nozzle:** Forces the arc into a small cross-section, **increasing current density and energy density**, thereby raising the plasma temperature to over **20,000 °C** and producing a narrow, high-velocity jet capable of vaporizing metals.

---

##### ✏️ Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Thickness of plate, $d = 10$ mm
- Arc voltage, $V = 120$ V
- Arc current, $I = 350$ A
- Traverse speed, $v = 6$ mm/s
- Kerf width, $w = 3.5$ mm
- Process efficiency, $\eta = 0.6$

**Step 1: Calculate the MRR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= v \times w \times d \\
&= 6 \;\text{mm/s} \times 3.5 \;\text{mm} \times 10 \;\text{mm} \\
&= 210 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} \\
\boxed{MRR = 210 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(3 marks awarded for correct formula, substitution, and answer)*

**Step 2: Calculate the useful power input.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{useful} &= \eta \cdot V \cdot I \\
&= 0.6 \times 120 \times 350 \\
&= 0.6 \times 42000 \\
&= 25200 \;\text{W} = 25200 \;\text{J/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \frac{P_{useful}}{MRR} \\
&= \frac{25200 \;\text{J/s}}{210 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} \\
&= 120 \;\text{J/mm}^3 \\
\boxed{SEC = 120 \;\text{J/mm}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(4 marks awarded for the energy calculation, substitution into SEC formula, and final result)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Numerical Problems):**
> 1. **Unit conversion is critical:** If traverse speed is given in **mm/min**, convert to **mm/s** by dividing by 60, OR keep consistent units throughout. Mixing units (e.g., mm/s with mm³/min) is the **#1 reason for losing 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Do not forget the efficiency factor η** — many students write $SEC = VI/MRR$ instead of $\eta VI/MRR$. This will be marked **wrong** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Show all substitutions** in the aligned block — partial marks are awarded for correct *setup* even if the final answer has a minor arithmetic slip.
> 4. Always **box the final answer** in the response sheet for clarity.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plasma Arc Machining**

- ⭐ **PAM is a THERMAL non-conventional process** — it uses **ionized gas (plasma)** as the energy carrier, not mechanical force.
- ⭐ **Plasma** = **4th state of matter**; generated by **constricting an electric arc** through a water-cooled copper nozzle.
- ⭐ **Temperatures:** Plasma jet reaches **11,000–28,000 °C**; Power density = **10⁶–10⁸ W/mm²**.
- ⭐ **Two arc modes:** *Non-transferred* (electrode ↔ nozzle) for non-conductors; *Transferred* (electrode ↔ workpiece) for metals — **higher energy**.
- ⭐ **Working gases:** Ar, N₂, H₂, Ar+H₂ mixtures; **Shield gas** = CO₂ or air.
- ⭐ **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $P = V \times I$ (input power)
  - $MRR = v \times w \times d$
  - $SEC = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot w \cdot d}$
  - $q = \dfrac{4 \eta V I}{\pi d_{nozzle}^2}$ (heat flux density)
- ⭐ **Process efficiency η for PAM:** typically **0.55 to 0.75** (gas-type dependent).
- ⭐ **Kerf width** ≈ nozzle diameter + (10 to 30)% — useful for parameter selection.
- ⭐ **MRR is HIGH** in PAM — by far the highest among thermal NTM processes.
- ⭐ **Major limitation:** **Large HAZ** → thermal distortion; only **conductive materials** in standard mode; high noise (~120 dB) and UV hazards.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Cutting SS, MS, Al, Ti, Cu plates (up to **150 mm thick**); shipbuilding, aerospace, automotive, decommissioning (including **underwater** cutting).
- ⭐ **Always compare** with EDM and LBM in exam answers — examiners reward comparative analysis tables.
- ⭐ **For numericals:** *unit consistency* and *inclusion of η* are the two most common pitfall areas.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Chemical Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electro Chemical Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro Chemical Machining (ECM)** is a non-traditional, thermo-electrically cold machining process in which **material removal is achieved by controlled anodic dissolution** of the workpiece using an electrolytic medium, governed quantitatively by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. The workpiece acts as the **anode (+)** and a shaped tool acts as the **cathode (−)**, separated by a narrow gap (typically **0.1 – 0.6 mm**) through which a flowing electrolyte (commonly NaNO₃ or NaCl solution) is forced.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ECM belongs to the **electrochemical group of non-conventional manufacturing processes** and is examined primarily for its working principle, **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** derivation, process parameters, and tooling philosophy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Controlled Rusting on Demand"**
> Think of how iron rusts when left in salty water — it slowly dissolves into the water. Now imagine the *opposite*: instead of waiting months, you **force** the dissolution by sending an electric current and **shape** the dissolution by placing a custom-moulded "negative" near the surface. The electrolyte carries away the dissolved metal ions like a river sweeping away sand.
> 
> - **Workpiece = The Sand-Castle** (gets dissolved)
> - **Tool = The Bucket-Mould** (decides the shape but never touches the castle)
> - **Electrolyte = The Flowing River** (flushes away the debris)
> - **DC Power Supply = The Strong Wind** (drives the dissolution process)

### 1.3 Key Physical & Process Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant (**F**) | **96,487** | C/mol |
| Applied DC Voltage | **5 – 25** | V |
| Current Density | **20 – 200** | A/cm² |
| Inter-electrode Gap | **0.1 – 0.6** | mm |
| Electrolyte Pressure | **0.5 – 2.0** | MPa |
| Electrolyte Temperature | **20 – 50** | °C |
| MRR (typical for steel) | ~ **50 – 500** | mm³/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must clearly distinguish **ECM from EDM** — in ECM, **NO heat-affected zone (HAZ)** is produced because the process is **purely electrochemical** and the workpiece remains at near-ambient temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Electrochemistry

When DC voltage is applied across the electrodes immersed in an electrolyte:

1. At the **anode (workpiece)**: Metal atoms lose electrons and dissolve as positive ions.
2. At the **cathode (tool)**: Hydrogen gas is liberated (in NaNO₃) or metal deposition may occur (in CuSO₄).
3. The electrolyte continuously flushes away:
   - **Metal hydroxides / salts** (the dissolved workpiece)
   - **Heat** generated by ionic conduction
   - **Hydrogen bubbles** from the cathode surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is NaNO₃ preferred over NaCl for steel?**
> NaCl generates **chlorine gas (Cl₂)** and **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** at the anode, which is **toxic and corrosive**. NaNO₃ produces **NO₂ and H₂** which are safer and yield a smoother machined surface due to **passivation** of the workpiece.

### 2.2 Process Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1:** Workpiece is mounted as the **anode** of a low-voltage, high-current DC circuit.
- **Step 2:** Tool (cathode) is advanced slowly (feed rate ≈ 0.5 – 5 mm/min) toward the workpiece.
- **Step 3:** Electrolyte is pumped at high velocity through the inter-electrode gap.
- **Step 4:** Atoms of the workpiece surface are ionized: M → M^(Z+) + Ze⁻
- **Step 5:** Ions are swept away by electrolyte flow, leaving a mirror-shaped cavity.
- **Step 6:** The tool is **never in physical contact** with the workpiece — there is **no tool wear**.
- **Step 7:** Equilibrium gap (called the **Equilibrium Gap** or **Side Gap**) is established when tool feed = dissolution rate.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to avoid markdown breaking.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MRR}_m = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's First Law** — mass removal rate | g/s |
| 2 | $\text{MRR}_v = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | **Volumetric MRR** | mm³/s |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{A \cdot J \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | **Linear dissolution velocity** (J = current density) | mm/s |
| 4 | $I = J \cdot A_w$ | Current = Current density × Wetted workpiece area | A |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{Z \cdot F \cdot m}{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}$ | **Faraday's Second Law** — time for mass *m* to dissolve | s |
| 6 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{A_1 / Z_1}{A_2 / Z_2}$ | **Equivalent mass ratio** for alloy constituents | — |
| 7 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power input | W |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual MRR}}{\text{Theoretical MRR}}$ | **Current Efficiency** (typically 90–100% for steel) | % |
| 9 | $R_a \approx 0.1 - 0.8 \ \mu m$ | Achievable surface roughness | μm |
| 10 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\text{MRR}_v}$ | Energy consumed per unit volume | J/mm³ |

**Where:**
- $A$ = Atomic weight (g/mol)
- $Z$ = Valency (number of electrons transferred)
- $I$ = Current (A)
- $\eta$ = Current efficiency
- $F$ = **96,487 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)
- $\rho$ = Density of workpiece (g/mm³)
- $J$ = Current density (A/mm²)
- $V$ = Applied voltage (V)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Machining of **titanium alloy blisks** (bladed discs), **Inconel turbine blades**, and **Ni-superalloy combustion chambers** — materials too tough for conventional cutting.
- **Automotive**: Production of **gear tooth profiles** (ECG variant) and **injection nozzle bodies**.
- **Die & Mould**: Die-sinking cavities in **hardened tool steel** (HRC 60+), no HAZ, no recast layer.
- **Defence**: Machining **uranium, beryllium, and zirconium** parts for nuclear applications.
- **Medical**: Fabrication of **orthopaedic implants** (Ti-6Al-4V) with complex curved surfaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) from Faraday's First Law

**Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** states:

> *"The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte."*

Mathematically:
$$m \propto Q$$
$$m = k \cdot Q = k \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 1 — Identify the electrochemical equivalent (k):**

For a substance of atomic weight *A* and valency *Z*, the charge required to liberate **one mole** (i.e., *A* grams) is *Z × F* coulombs.

Therefore, mass liberated per coulomb is:
$$k = \frac{A}{Z \cdot F} \quad \text{(Electrochemical equivalent, g/C)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute to get the classical mass MRR:**

Differentiating *m* with respect to time (since *I* is constant):
$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{dm}{dt} = k \cdot I = \frac{A \cdot I}{Z \cdot F}$$

Introducing **current efficiency** $\eta$ (since not all current is utilized for dissolution — some is lost to side reactions like H₂ evolution at cathode and parasitic reactions):
$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F} \quad \text{(g/s)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mass MRR to volumetric MRR:**

Since mass $m = \rho \cdot V$, we have $V = m / \rho$, hence:
$$\text{MRR}_v = \frac{\text{MRR}_m}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/s)}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of current density J:**

Since $I = J \cdot A_w$ (where $A_w$ is the wetted workpiece area in contact with electrolyte), the **linear dissolution velocity** perpendicular to the surface is:
$$v = \frac{\text{MRR}_v}{A_w} = \frac{A \cdot J \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(mm/s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important equation in KTU exams**. Memorize the four variable form clearly.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical (KTU Pattern Problem)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
> In an ECM operation on a steel workpiece (A = 55.85, Z = 2, ρ = 7.87 g/cm³), a current of 2000 A is passed with a current efficiency of 92%. Calculate:
> (a) Mass MRR in g/min.
> (b) Volumetric MRR in mm³/min.

**Solution (a) — Mass MRR:**

$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F}$$

Substituting values (A = 55.85, I = 2000, η = 0.92, Z = 2, F = 96,487):
$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{55.85 \times 2000 \times 0.92}{2 \times 96487}$$

$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{102764}{192974}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_m = 0.5325 \ \text{g/s} = 31.95 \ \text{g/min}}$$

**Solution (b) — Volumetric MRR:**

Converting density: $\rho = 7.87 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7.87 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$

$$\text{MRR}_v = \frac{\text{MRR}_m}{\rho} = \frac{0.5325}{7.87 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{MRR}_v = 67.66 \ \text{mm}^3\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_v = 4059.6 \ \text{mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 4.06 \ \text{cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on ECM Performance

| Parameter | ↑ Increase Effect on MRR | ↑ Increase Effect on Surface Finish | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage (V)** | ↑ Linear increase | ↓ Deteriorates (roughness ↑) | Risk of sparking at >30V |
| **Current (I)** | ↑ Linear increase (Faraday) | ↓ Slight deterioration | Limited by heating |
| **Current Density (J)** | ↑ Linear | ↓ Slight ↓ | Optimal: 20–200 A/cm² |
| **Electrolyte Flow** | ↑ Up to optimum, then ↓ | ↑ Improves (washes sludge) | Too high → cavitation |
| **Electrolyte Temp** | ↑ Increases ionic conductivity | ↓ Slight ↓ | Max 50°C to avoid boiling |
| **Inter-electrode Gap** | ↓ Small gap → ↑ MRR | ↓ Very small gap → short-circuit | Equilibrium gap ~0.2–0.4 mm |
| **Feed Rate** | Matches equilibrium rate | ↑ Good matching critical | Must ≈ dissolution rate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECM Process Setup — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerLoop["Electrical Power Loop"]
        PSU[DC Power Supply 5-25V]
        Ammeter[Ammeter]
        PSU --> Ammeter
    end

    subgraph MachiningZone["Active Machining Zone (Inter-Electrode Gap)"]
        Anode[Workpiece Anode +]
        Cathode[Shaped Tool Cathode -]
        Gap[Electrolyte Flow 0.1-0.6mm]
        Anode --- Gap
        Gap --- Cathode
    end

    Ammeter --> Anode
    Cathode --> PSU

    subgraph ElectrolyteLoop["Electrolyte Circulation Loop"]
        Pump[High Pressure Pump]
        Filter[Filter Unit]
        Cooler[Heat Exchanger]
        Tank[Electrolyte Tank NaNO3]
        Pump --> Filter --> Cooler --> MachiningZone
        MachiningZone --> Tank
        Tank --> Pump
    end

    subgraph ControlSystem["Servo Control System"]
        Servo[Servo Motor]
        Slide[Tool Slide Z-axis]
        Sensor[Gap Sensor]
        Servo --> Slide --> Cathode
        Sensor --> Servo
    end
```

### 4.2 ECM Process Flow — Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mount Workpiece as Anode] --> B[Set Tool Cathode Shape]
    B --> C[Adjust Inter-Electrode Gap]
    C --> D[Start Electrolyte Flow]
    D --> E[Apply DC Voltage 5-25V]
    E --> F[Anodic Dissolution Begins]
    F --> G{Equilibrium Gap Reached?}
    G -->|No| H[Servo Advances Tool]
    H --> F
    G -->|Yes| I[Steady-State Machining]
    I --> J[Target Depth Achieved]
    J --> K[Stop Voltage and Flow]
    K --> L[Retract Tool]
```

### 4.3 Mechanism of Anodic Dissolution — Electrochemistry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AnodeSurface["Workpiece Surface - Anode"]
        M[Metal Atom M]
        M1[M atom loses Z electrons]
        M --> M1
        M1 --> Mion[Metal Ion M to the power of Z positive]
    end

    subgraph Electrolyte["NaNO3 Aqueous Electrolyte"]
        H2O[Water Molecules]
        NO3[Nitrate Ions]
        H2 --> H2Gas[Hydrogen Gas at Cathode]
    end

    subgraph CathodeSurface["Tool Surface - Cathode"]
        e1[Electrons Arrive]
        H2O2[2H2O plus 2e gives H2 plus 2OH]
        e1 --> H2O2 --> H2Gas
    end

    Mion -.->|Drifted by Electrolyte Flow| WasteStream[Hydroxide Sludge to Filter]
    e1 -.->|External Circuit| PSU[DC Source]
    PSU -.->|Drives| M1
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block — ECM vs EDM (Examiner Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ECM["ECM - Electro Chemical Machining"]
        E1[Principle: Anodic Dissolution]
        E2[No Heat - Cold Process]
        E3[No Tool Wear]
        E4[No HAZ or Recast Layer]
        E5[Voltage 5-25V DC]
        E6[For Conductive Metals only]
    end

    subgraph EDM["EDM - Electro Discharge Machining"]
        D1[Principle: Spark Erosion]
        D2[High Temp 8000-12000 C]
        D3[Tool Wear Exists]
        D4[HAZ and Recast Layer Present]
        D5[Voltage 50-250V pulsed]
        D6[For All Electrically Conductive Materials]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define Electro Chemical Machining. Mention any four of its distinctive features.

**Model Answer:**
Electro Chemical Machining (ECM) is a non-traditional machining process in which material is removed from the workpiece by **anodic dissolution** in a flowing electrolyte under the action of an externally applied DC voltage. The workpiece acts as the **anode** and the shaped tool acts as the **cathode**.

Four distinctive features:
1. **No tool wear** — the tool never physically contacts the workpiece.
2. **No heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — the process is purely electrochemical and cold.
3. **High MRR** for hard, tough materials like titanium, Inconel, and hardened steels.
4. **Mirror-quality surface finish** (Ra 0.1 – 0.8 μm) achievable without secondary finishing.
5. **No residual stress or work hardening** is induced.
6. Suitable for **complex 3D cavities** in a single pass.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Why is NaNO₃ generally preferred over NaCl as an electrolyte in ECM of steel?

**Model Answer:**
NaNO₃ is preferred over NaCl for ECM of steel because:
1. NaCl generates **toxic chlorine gas (Cl₂)** and **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** at the anode, creating health and corrosion hazards.
2. NaNO₃ promotes a **passivation layer** on steel, leading to better dimensional accuracy and surface finish.
3. By-products of NaNO₃ (NO₂, H₂) are less corrosive to machine components and tooling.
4. NaNO₃ provides a **wider working current density range** with stable dissolution, giving better process control.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module-4 carries internal choice. Students attempt **ONE** out of two.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Electro Chemical Machining**. List any **five process parameters** affecting MRR. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **volumetric Material Removal Rate** expression for ECM starting from Faraday's First Law. State the units of every term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle:**
In ECM, the workpiece is connected to the **positive terminal (anode)** and the shaped tool is connected to the **negative terminal (cathode)** of a low-voltage (5–25 V), high-current (up to 10,000 A) DC power supply. A pressurized **electrolyte** (commonly NaNO₃ solution, 20% w/v) is pumped through the inter-electrode gap (0.1–0.6 mm).

At the anode, metal atoms ionize and dissolve:
$$M \rightarrow M^{Z+} + Ze^-$$

The metal ions are flushed away by the flowing electrolyte, while hydrogen gas is liberated at the cathode. The tool is gradually advanced by a servo mechanism, maintaining a small constant gap. The mirror-image cavity of the tool is generated on the workpiece.

**Five process parameters affecting MRR:**
1. **Applied voltage** (V)
2. **Current density** (J)
3. **Current efficiency** (η)
4. **Electrolyte concentration and type**
5. **Electrolyte flow rate / pressure**
6. **Inter-electrode gap** (also affects accuracy)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

Starting from Faraday's First Law:
$$m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F} \quad \text{[g/s]}$$

Using $m = \rho \cdot V$:
$$\text{MRR}_v = \frac{\text{MRR}_m}{\rho} = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]}$$

**Units of every term:**

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Atomic weight | g/mol |
| $I$ | Current | A (C/s) |
| $\eta$ | Current efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $Z$ | Valency | dimensionless |
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | C/mol |
| $\rho$ | Density of workpiece | g/mm³ |
| $\text{MRR}_v$ | Volumetric MRR | mm³/s |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **tool design considerations** in ECM. Why is a "side gap" provided in the cathode? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** In an ECM operation on an **aluminium workpiece** (A = 27, Z = 3, ρ = 2.7 g/cm³), a current of **1500 A** is passed with a current efficiency of **95%**. Calculate the **mass MRR** in kg/hr and the **volumetric MRR** in cm³/min. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Tool Design Considerations in ECM:**

1. **Tool Material:** The cathode tool is made of **non-corrosive conductive material** such as copper, brass, stainless steel, or titanium. It must resist the electrolyte and the cathodic environment.

2. **Shape and Sizing:** The tool is an **inverse replica** of the desired cavity. To compensate for side-wall dissolution, the tool is **undersized** by an amount equal to the **side gap** (typically 0.1–0.5 mm per side).

3. **Side Gap Provision:** A deliberate gap is left between the side of the tool and the side wall of the cavity. This prevents:
   - **Short-circuiting** between anode and cathode
   - Excessive side-wall dissolution that would distort the cavity profile
   - Tool damage due to contact
   
4. **Insulation:** The non-cutting surfaces (sides) of the cathode are **insulated** with epoxy, ceramic, or polymer coatings to localize dissolution only on the active frontal area.

5. **Electrolyte Flow Channels:** The tool body is designed with **internal passages** to distribute electrolyte uniformly across the gap, preventing stagnation and dead zones.

6. **Back-Taper / Draft:** The tool is given a slight **taper** (1°–3°) to avoid fouling during entry and exit.

7. **Mounting and Rigidity:** Must be rigid enough to maintain the equilibrium gap under servo feed and electrolyte pressure.

**Why a Side Gap is provided:**
The side gap (also called the **side clearance**) is the gap between the lateral surface of the tool and the side wall of the cavity being machined. It is provided to:
- Allow electrolyte flow on the sides
- Prevent short circuits
- Compensate for stray current dissolution on side walls
- Provide a constant equilibrium gap for dimensional accuracy

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** A = 27 g/mol, Z = 3, ρ = 2.7 g/cm³, I = 1500 A, η = 0.95, F = 96,487 C/mol

**Mass MRR:**
$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F} = \frac{27 \times 1500 \times 0.95}{3 \times 96487}$$

$$\text{MRR}_m = \frac{38475}{289461} = 0.1329 \ \text{g/s}$$

Converting to kg/hr:
$$\text{MRR}_m = 0.1329 \times 3600 / 1000 = 0.4785 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_m \approx 0.479 \ \text{kg/hr}}$$

**Volumetric MRR:**
$$\text{MRR}_v = \frac{\text{MRR}_m}{\rho} = \frac{0.1329 \ \text{g/s}}{2.7 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

$$\text{MRR}_v = 0.0492 \ \text{cm}^3\text{/s}$$

Converting to cm³/min:
$$\text{MRR}_v = 0.0492 \times 60 = 2.95 \ \text{cm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR}_v \approx 2.95 \ \text{cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include η (current efficiency)** — Most common error. Always state: "assuming 100% efficiency" if not given, but **use η if provided** in the numerical.
> 2. **Unit confusion in ρ** — Density must be in **g/cm³** if MRR is asked in cm³/s, or in **g/mm³** if MRR is in mm³/s. Show unit conversion explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing Z with atomic number** — Z is **valency** (number of electrons transferred per atom), not atomic number. For Fe²⁺ dissolution, Z = 2; for Fe³⁺, Z = 3. Default to 2 for steel unless specified.
> 4. **Mixing up MRR_m and MRR_v** — Read the question carefully. If "volumetric" is asked, divide by ρ.
> 5. **Omitting the schematic** in Q.A.(a) — A clean labelled diagram is **mandatory** for full marks on principle questions.
> 6. **Writing "ECM uses heat"** — This is the most damaging conceptual error. ECM is a **cold process**. Any mention of sparks or heat will attract negative marking.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision for KTU Exam:**

- **ECM = Electro Chemical Machining = Anodic dissolution of workpiece** (anode = +ve, tool = −ve).
- **Governing Law:** Faraday's First & Second Laws of Electrolysis.
- **Voltage:** Low (5–25 V), **Current:** High (up to 10,000 A), **Gap:** Very narrow (0.1–0.6 mm).
- **Electrolyte:** NaNO₃ (preferred), NaCl, NaOH, H₂SO₄ depending on material.
- **Key Formula:** $\text{MRR}_v = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot \eta}{Z \cdot F \cdot \rho}$
- **Faraday's constant F = 96,487 C/mol** (memorize!).
- **No tool wear, no HAZ, no recast, no residual stress** — the four "no's" of ECM.
- **Surface finish:** Ra 0.1 – 0.8 μm (mirror-like).
- **Side gap** in tool is essential — it equals the equilibrium inter-electrode gap.
- **Insulation** of non-cutting tool surfaces is mandatory to localize dissolution.
- **Servo control** maintains constant gap as tool feeds into the workpiece.
- **ECM is restricted to electrically conductive materials** only (cannot machine ceramics, glass, plastics).
- **Applications:** Aerospace blisks, die-cavity sinking, gear shaving (ECG), surgical implants, nuclear components.
- **Variants:** ECG (Electro Chemical Grinding), ECH (Electro Chemical Honing), WECM (Wire ECM).
- **Difference from EDM:** ECM = chemical (no heat), EDM = thermal (spark erosion, HAZ present).
- **Current efficiency η** is typically **90–100%** for pure metals; lower for alloys due to multi-element dissolution.
- **Higher current density** = higher MRR, but risk of boiling and rough surface.
- **Higher flow rate** = better sludge removal, but excessive flow causes pressure fluctuations.
- **Tool material:** Copper, brass, or stainless steel (must be corrosion-resistant).
- **For KTU numericals, ALWAYS show unit conversion steps explicitly** in the answer script.
- **Hydrogen gas is liberated at the cathode** — electrolyte tank must be well-ventilated.
- **Workpiece remains at ambient temperature** during ECM — suitable for thin-walled and delicate parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultrasonic Machining (USM)** is a non-traditional, mechanical type material removal process in which material is removed by the **abrasive action of abrasive particles** suspended in a slurry, which are vibrated at **ultrasonic frequency (typically 20–40 kHz)** against the workpiece by a shaped tool (sonotrode/horn). The process is also called **Ultrasonic Impact Grinding (UIG)** when used for grinding applications.

The process is governed by the principle of **direct (hammering) and indirect (cavitation erosion)** material removal mechanisms. The tool itself does not contact the workpiece; instead, the abrasives act as the cutting medium, making USM especially suitable for machining of **hard, brittle, and non-conductive materials** that are difficult to machine by conventional methods.

**Key Performance Parameters:**
- **Frequency of vibration (f): 20–40 kHz** (typically **20 kHz** or **40 kHz**)
- **Amplitude of vibration (A): 10–50 µm** (peak-to-peak)
- **Static tool feed force (F): 0.5–25 N**
- **Abrasive material:** Boron Carbide (B₄C), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃), Diamond
- **Abrasive size:** 100–200 mesh (coarse) to 600 mesh (fine)
- **Slurry concentration:** 30–60 % by weight of abrasive in water

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBAUT404 Module 4):** USM is studied as a *mechanical-type non-traditional machining process*, classified under processes utilizing *mechanical energy for material removal*. Students must remember that USM is **purely mechanical** in principle — there is **no chemical, electrical, or thermal action** on the workpiece.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a jackhammer breaking a brittle road, but done with microscopic sand particles and at 20,000 times per second!**

Imagine you are trying to drill a hole through a **glass bottle** with an ordinary drill bit — it will crack instantly. Now, suppose you take a soft copper rod, dip it in a **slurry of fine sand and water**, and vibrate this rod up-and-down **20,000 times every second** while gently pressing it against the glass. The sand grains trapped between the rod tip and the glass are flung repeatedly against the glass surface. Each impact chips off a tiny fragment of glass. Over a few minutes, a clean, round hole appears — **without cracking the bottle**.

This is exactly how USM works:
- The **soft copper rod** is the *tool* (sonotrode)
- The **sand slurry** is the *abrasive slurry*
- The **20,000 Hz vibration** comes from the *ultrasonic transducer*
- The **gentle pressing force** is the *static feed force*
- The **glass** is the *hard, brittle workpiece*

> **Critical Physical Constants to Remember (Bolded):**
> - **Speed of sound in air: 343 m/s**
> - **Speed of sound in water: ~1480 m/s**
> - **Standard USM frequency: 20 kHz or 40 kHz**
> - **Hole aspect ratio achievable: up to 10:1 or higher**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The USM process is executed in the following logical sequence:

1. **High-Frequency Electrical Signal Generation:** A high-frequency electrical signal (20 kHz or 40 kHz AC) is produced by the **ultrasonic generator / power supply / oscillator**.

2. **Electrical-to-Mechanical Conversion:** The signal is fed to a **transducer**, which converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical vibrations. Two transducer types exist:
   - **Magnetostrictive transducer** (uses nickel or permendur — converts via magnetic field changes)
   - **Piezoelectric transducer** (uses PZT — lead zirconate titanate — converts via electric field strain)

3. **Amplitude Magnification:** The small vibration amplitude from the transducer (~few microns) is amplified to **10–50 µm** by a mechanical amplifier called the **mechanical horn / acoustic transformer / velocity transformer / concentrator**. Common horn shapes: stepped, conical, exponential, and catenoidal.

4. **Tool Vibration:** The **tool (sonotrode)** is attached to the horn tip and vibrates axially at ultrasonic frequency with the magnified amplitude.

5. **Abrasive Slurry Action:** A slurry of **abrasive particles (B₄C, SiC, Al₂O₃, or diamond)** suspended in **water (carrier fluid)** is continuously circulated into the machining zone (gap between tool tip and workpiece).

6. **Material Removal Mechanism:** During each vibration cycle:
   - **Down-stroke (direct impact / hammering):** Tool accelerates abrasive particles downward → particles strike the workpiece → micro-chipping and brittle fracture of workpiece material → **primary mechanism (≈ 80–90% of MRR)**.
   - **Up-stroke (indirect / cavitation erosion):** As tool retracts, the sudden withdrawal causes **cavitation bubbles** in the slurry to collapse → micro-jets impinge on workpiece → secondary erosion → **secondary mechanism (≈ 10–20% of MRR)**.
   - **Chemical action:** The water in slurry causes mild **oxidation/corrosion** of the freshly exposed workpiece surface, which is then chipped away in subsequent cycles → **minor mechanism**.

7. **Slurry Circulation:** Fresh slurry is pumped continuously to:
   - Carry away debris (swarf)
   - Cool the machining zone
   - Maintain uniform abrasive concentration
   - Flush out fractured abrasive grains

8. **Static Feed:** A controlled static downward load is applied on the tool so that the tool advances into the workpiece as material is removed.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorize the units and the variables.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MRR (Volume basis):** $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{h}{t}$ | Material Removal Rate — where $D$ = tool diameter, $h$ = depth of cut, $t$ = time | **mm³/min** |
| 2 | **MRR (Empirical):** $MRR \propto F \cdot A^2} \cdot f \cdot \dfrac{K_{ab}}{H_w}$ | MRR is directly proportional to feed force $F$, square of amplitude $A$, frequency $f$, abrasive hardness factor $K_{ab}$, and inversely to workpiece hardness $H_w$ | mm³/min |
| 3 | **Penetration rate / Feed rate:** $v_f = \dfrac{h}{t}$ | Linear feed of tool into workpiece | **mm/min** |
| 4 | **Static feed force range:** $0.5 \leq F \leq 25$ N | Typical range for USM | **N** |
| 5 | **Amplitude range:** $10 \leq A \leq 50$ µm | Peak-to-peak axial vibration of tool | **µm** |
| 6 | **Frequency:** $f = 20$ kHz or $f = 40$ kHz | Standard industrial USM frequencies | **kHz** |
| 7 | **Hammering energy per cycle:** $E_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 = 2 \pi^2 m f^2 A^2$ | Energy of an abrasive grain impact | **Joules** |
| 8 | **Abrasive hardness requirement:** $H_{abrasive} \geq 1.5 \cdot H_{workpiece}$ | Abrasive must be at least 1.5× harder than workpiece | **HV / Knoop** |
| 9 | **Tool wear ratio (TWR):** $TWR = \dfrac{\text{Volume of tool wear}}{\text{Volume of workpiece removed}}$ | A measure of tool life | **dimensionless** |
| 10 | **Abrasive size vs. Surface finish:** $R_a \propto \dfrac{d_{grain}}{3}$ | Surface roughness proportional to abrasive grain size | **µm** |
| 11 | **Aspect ratio:** $\text{AR} = \dfrac{D}{d}$ | Depth-to-diameter ratio of hole | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Cavitation threshold:** $P_{cav} = P_{atm} - 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}$ | Pressure for cavitation bubble formation | **Pa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions / Validity Limits:**
> - Formula 2 is valid for: $f$ between 19–25 kHz, $A$ between 15–50 µm, $F$ between 2–15 N.
> - For $F < F_{optimum}$, MRR ∝ F (linear region). For $F > F_{optimum}$, MRR decreases (abrasive crushing region).
> - If $H_{abrasive} < 1.5 \cdot H_{workpiece}$, MRR drops sharply (abrasive rounding-off).

---

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is USM used in real engineering?**
> - **Semiconductor industry:** Machining of **silicon wafers, germanium, quartz crystals** for MEMS and electronic substrates.
> - **Optics industry:** Machining of **glass lenses, ceramic reflectors, optical-grade ceramics**.
> - **Aerospace:** Drilling cooling holes in **turbine blades made of nickel super-alloys and ceramics** (though USM is more suited for ceramics).
> - **Medical devices:** Machining of **zirconia and alumina bio-ceramic implants** (dental, hip).
> - **Jewellery:** Engraving and intricate cutting of **gemstones (ruby, sapphire, quartz)**.
> - **Dies and moulds:** Machining of **WC, SiC dies, and spark-erosion electrodes** in graphite or copper.
> - **Nuclear:** Machining of **graphite and zirconia** components.

**Real-World MRR Values (for context):**
- Machining glass with SiC abrasive, 20 kHz: **MRR ≈ 50–200 mm³/min**
- Machining tungsten carbide: **MRR ≈ 5–15 mm³/min** (extremely hard)
- Machining germanium: **MRR ≈ 100–400 mm³/min** (brittle and soft)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Empirical MRR Relation for USM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favourite:** The KTU examiner frequently asks students to derive the *qualitative dependency* of MRR on USM parameters. The standard (textbook-level) approach is given below.

**Assumption:** Material removal per impact is proportional to the **kinetic energy** of the abrasive particle, which is itself proportional to the **square of the vibration amplitude and frequency**.

Let us define the following:
- $m$ = effective mass of an abrasive particle (kg)
- $f$ = vibration frequency (Hz)
- $A$ = peak-to-peak amplitude of vibration (m)
- $v_{peak}$ = peak velocity of the tool (m/s)
- $N$ = number of effective impacts per unit time

**Step 1 — Velocity of vibrating tool tip:**

The tool tip follows simple harmonic motion. The displacement of the tool tip is:
$$x(t) = \dfrac{A}{2} \cdot \sin(2 \pi f t)$$

The peak velocity is the maximum of $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$:
$$v_{peak} = \dfrac{A}{2} \cdot 2\pi f = \pi f A$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy per impact:**

For an abrasive grain accelerated to approximately the tool velocity:
$$E_{impact} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_{peak}^2$$

Substituting $v_{peak} = \pi f A$:
$$E_{impact} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (\pi f A)^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 m f^2 A^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Number of impacts per unit time:**

Assuming each abrasive particle strikes the workpiece once per cycle, the impact frequency equals the tool frequency $f$. Let $N$ be the number of effective abrasive particles in the contact zone.

Number of impacts per unit time = $N \cdot f$.

**Step 4 — Volume of material removed per impact:**

Using brittle-fracture mechanics, the volume removed per impact $V_{impact}$ is proportional to the impact energy divided by a measure of the workpiece's resistance to fracture (proxied by hardness $H_w$):
$$V_{impact} \propto \dfrac{E_{impact}}{H_w}$$

**Step 5 — Total MRR:**

$$MRR = V_{impact} \cdot N \cdot f = k \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 N m f^3 A^2}{2 H_w}$$

where $k$ is a proportionality constant incorporating abrasive geometry and slurry properties. The **static feed force $F$** controls how many abrasive particles $N$ are effectively trapped in the gap, so:
$$MRR \propto F \cdot A^2 \cdot f \cdot \dfrac{K_{ab}}{H_w}$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** of the empirical MRR equation for USM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Frequency for Tool

The tool length must satisfy the **resonant half-wavelength condition** for standing-wave operation. The wavelength of ultrasound in the tool material is:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{f}$$

For resonance, the tool length $L_{tool}$ must equal **half the wavelength** (or an odd multiple):
$$L_{tool} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{2 f}$$

For a **titanium tool** ($v_{sound} = 5000$ m/s) at $f = 20$ kHz:
$$L_{tool} = \dfrac{5000}{2 \times 20000} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 125 \text{ mm}$$

This explains why USM tools are typically **75–150 mm long** — they are sized to resonate at the operating frequency.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on MRR

| Process Parameter | Change | Effect on MRR | Effect on Surface Finish | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static load (F) ↑** | Increase | ↑ then ↓ (optimum) | Slight worsening | More particles trapped → more impacts. Excess load crushes abrasives. |
| **Amplitude (A) ↑** | Increase | ↑ (∝ A²) | Slight worsening | Higher particle velocity → higher impact energy. |
| **Frequency (f) ↑** | Increase | ↑ (∝ f) | Negligible | More impacts per second. |
| **Abrasive size ↑** | Increase | ↑ (initially) | Roughness ↑ (∝ grain size) | Larger grains carry more momentum, but surface gets coarser. |
| **Abrasive hardness ↑** | Increase | ↑ | Slight improvement | Sharper cutting, less rounding of grains. |
| **Workpiece hardness ↑** | Increase | ↓ (∝ 1/Hw) | Slight improvement | Brittle materials still chip, but harder ones resist fracture. |
| **Slurry concentration ↑** | Increase | ↑ then plateau | Variable | More particles available for impact; excess slurry causes clogging. |
| **Tool material hardness ↑** | Increase | Negligible on MRR | Improved accuracy | Tool wear reduces — better dimensional accuracy. |
| **Brazed tool (no slurry) used** | Switch | ↓ (typically) | Better finish | Less control; used for finish operations. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing USM with **EDM (Electrical Discharge Machining)**. Remember — USM uses **mechanical energy of abrasives**, not electrical sparks. USM is suitable for **both conductive and non-conductive** materials; EDM works **only on conductive** materials.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Ultrasonic Machining System

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the functional architecture of a USM system, showing the **energy and signal flow** from the electrical input to the final material removal on the workpiece.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Power Supply 50 Hz 230 V] --> B[High Frequency Ultrasonic Generator Oscillator]
    B -->|High Frequency AC Signal 20 to 40 kHz| C[Transducer Magnetostrictive or Piezoelectric]
    C -->|Mechanical Vibration Low Amplitude 5 to 10 micron| D[Velocity Transformer Mechanical Horn]
    D -->|Amplified Vibration 10 to 50 micron peak to peak| E[Tool Holder Sonotrode]
    E -->|Axial Ultrasonic Vibration| F[Tool Tip Soft Material like Titanium or Monel]
    G[Slurry Tank with Abrasive Particles in Water] -->|Pump and Nozzle| H[Machining Zone Gap 25 to 100 micron]
    F --> H
    I[Workpiece Hard Brittle Material held on Fixture] --> H
    H -->|Abrasive Impact and Cavitation Erosion| J[Material Removal as Micro Chips and Swarf]
    H -->|Used slurry with debris| K[Slurry Filter and Recirculation]
    K --> G
    L[Static Pneumatic or Hydraulic Feed Mechanism] -->|Controlled Downward Force 0.5 to 25 N| F
    M[Cooling Water Circulation System] --> H
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Energy flow:** AC mains → generator (electrical) → transducer (mechanical vibration) → horn (amplified vibration) → tool tip (impacts abrasive) → workpiece (material removed).
- **Material flow:** Fresh slurry from tank → machining zone → spent slurry (with swarf) → filter → back to tank.
- **Feedback/control flow:** Static feed mechanism maintains constant tool pressure on the workpiece.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Detailed Cross-Section of the Machining Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Tool Sonotrode vibrating at 20 kHz] -->|Downward strokes| B1[Abrasive Particles B4C or SiC in Slurry]
    B1 -->|Hammering action| C1[Workpiece Surface Hard and Brittle]
    C1 -->|Micro chipping| D1[Material Removal Volume]
    A1 -->|Upward strokes| E1[Cavitation Bubble Formation in Slurry]
    E1 -->|Bubble collapse| F1[Micro jet impact on Workpiece]
    F1 --> C1
    C1 -->|Swarf and debris| G1[Slurry Outlet to Recirculation Pump]
```

**Mechanism Summary shown in Diagram:**
- **Down-strokes** → abrasive hammering → primary MRR (≈ 80–90 %).
- **Up-strokes** → cavitation micro-jets → secondary MRR (≈ 10–20 %).
- **Slurry flow** → swarf removal + cooling + fresh abrasive supply.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2024]**
*Define Ultrasonic Machining. State any two of its distinctive characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Ultrasonic Machining (USM) is a non-traditional, mechanical-type material removal process in which material is removed from a workpiece by the **abrasive action of abrasive particles suspended in a slurry**, vibrated at **ultrasonic frequency (20–40 kHz)** against the workpiece by a shaped tool called a **sonotrode**. The tool does not directly contact the workpiece; the abrasives act as the cutting medium.

**Any two characteristics (1 mark for two = ½ mark each):**
1. Suitable for machining of **hard, brittle, and non-conductive materials** (glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, carbides) which are difficult to machine conventionally.
2. Material removal occurs primarily by **hammering (direct impact) of abrasive grains** and **cavitation erosion** — not by tool–workpiece contact.
3. The process can produce **holes, cavities, and intricate shapes** with high dimensional accuracy and good surface finish.
4. MRR depends strongly on **vibration amplitude, frequency, static feed force, abrasive type/size, and workpiece hardness**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List the main components of an Ultrasonic Machining setup and state the function of the transducer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Main components (2 marks — name any four):**
1. **Ultrasonic generator / high-frequency power supply** (oscillator producing 20 kHz or 40 kHz AC)
2. **Transducer** (magnetostrictive or piezoelectric)
3. **Velocity transformer / mechanical horn** (amplitude amplifier)
4. **Tool holder and tool (sonotrode)**
5. **Slurry tank, pump, and nozzle** (abrasive delivery system)
6. **Work-holding fixture**
7. **Static feed mechanism** (for controlled downward force)

**Function of transducer (1 mark):**
The transducer **converts the high-frequency electrical energy from the generator into high-frequency mechanical vibrations** of the same frequency (20 or 40 kHz). It is the *electrical-to-mechanical energy converter* of the USM system. Magnetostrictive transducers use nickel or permendur; piezoelectric transducers use PZT (lead zirconate titanate) crystals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. We present **Question A** and **Question B** as the two alternatives. Choose **any one**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of **Ultrasonic Machining (USM)**. Describe the **material removal mechanism** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the process parameters affecting the **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in USM. Derive the **empirical MRR relation** for USM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram. A neatly drawn block diagram showing the sequence: *Power supply → Generator → Transducer → Horn → Tool → Machining zone (with slurry and workpiece) → Swarf and recirculation*.

**2. Working principle (3 marks):**

USM uses high-frequency electrical energy converted into mechanical vibration. The process steps are:

- The **ultrasonic generator** produces a high-frequency alternating current at **20 kHz or 40 kHz**.
- This signal is fed to the **transducer**, which converts the electrical oscillations into **mechanical vibrations of the same frequency**.
- The **mechanical horn (velocity transformer)** magnifies the small vibration amplitude (~5 µm) to a working amplitude of **10–50 µm**.
- The **tool (sonotrode)** is attached to the horn and vibrates axially at this frequency and amplitude.
- **Abrasive slurry** (e.g., B₄C or SiC in water) is pumped into the gap (typically **25–100 µm wide**) between the tool tip and the workpiece.
- During **down-strokes**, the tool tip pushes abrasive particles against the workpiece — particles impact the surface, causing **micro-chipping and brittle fracture**.
- During **up-strokes**, the rapid retraction of the tool creates **negative pressure**, causing **cavitation bubbles** in the slurry. When these bubbles collapse, **high-velocity micro-jets** strike the workpiece, causing **erosion**.
- A **static feed force** keeps the tool pressed gently into the workpiece so that the tool advances as material is removed.
- Used slurry with **swarf** is recirculated through a filter.

**3. Material removal mechanism (2 marks):**

| Mechanism | Contribution | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct hammering** | **80–90 %** | Abrasive grains accelerated by tool strike the workpiece, causing **brittle fracture and micro-chipping**. |
| **Cavitation erosion** | **10–20 %** | During tool retraction, **cavitation bubbles** form and collapse, creating high-velocity micro-jets that erode the workpiece. |
| **Chemical action** | **< 1 %** | Water in slurry causes mild **oxidation** of freshly exposed surfaces, which are then chipped away in subsequent cycles. |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**1. Parameters affecting MRR (3 marks):**

The MRR in USM is affected by:
- **Static feed force (F):** Higher force traps more abrasive particles → higher MRR up to an optimum, beyond which abrasive crushing reduces MRR.
- **Vibration amplitude (A):** MRR ∝ A². Larger amplitude gives higher particle velocity and impact energy.
- **Vibration frequency (f):** MRR ∝ f. More cycles per second = more impacts.
- **Abrasive material and hardness:** Harder abrasives (B₄C, SiC) give higher MRR. Abrasive should be at least **1.5× harder than workpiece**.
- **Abrasive grain size:** Larger grains carry more momentum → higher MRR, but coarser surface finish.
- **Workpiece hardness:** MRR ∝ 1/Hw. Harder workpieces are more difficult to machine.
- **Slurry concentration:** Optimal concentration (~40–60 % by weight) gives maximum MRR.
- **Tool material:** Softer tools wear faster but cause less damage to workpiece.

**2. Derivation of empirical MRR relation (4 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final relation is:

$$MRR = k \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot A^2 \cdot f \cdot K_{ab}}{H_w}$$

where $k$ is a constant, $F$ = static feed force, $A$ = amplitude, $f$ = frequency, $K_{ab}$ = abrasive hardness factor, $H_w$ = workpiece hardness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of the ultrasonic transducer** with neat sketches. Compare **magnetostrictive and piezoelectric transducers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of USM. List the **process parameters that affect surface finish** in USM. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Ultrasonic transducer — construction and working (4 marks):**

A transducer is a device that **converts high-frequency electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical vibrations**. The two main types are:

**(a) Magnetostrictive Transducer:**
- **Construction:** Made of nickel or permendur laminations wound with a coil of insulated copper wire. A permanent magnet provides a DC bias magnetic field.
- **Working:** When high-frequency AC current passes through the coil, the resulting alternating magnetic field causes the nickel laminations to alternately **expand and contract (magnetostriction effect)**. The change in length is small (~few µm) at 20 kHz.
- **Efficiency:** ~25–40 %.
- **Cooling required:** Yes (water cooling needed because of eddy current losses).

**(b) Piezoelectric Transducer:**
- **Construction:** Made of PZT (lead zirconate titanate) ceramic discs sandwiched between metal electrodes. The discs are clamped between a heavy back-mass and a light front-mass (a stepped horn acts as the front mass).
- **Working:** When high-frequency AC voltage is applied across the electrodes, the PZT discs alternately **expand and contract (inverse piezoelectric effect)** at the applied frequency.
- **Efficiency:** ~90–96 % (much higher than magnetostrictive).
- **Cooling required:** No (no eddy current losses).

**2. Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Magnetostrictive | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Nickel / Permendur | PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) |
| **Efficiency** | 25–40 % | 90–96 % |
| **Cooling** | Required (water) | Not required |
| **Amplitude obtained** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cost** | Higher (cooler + supply) | Lower (compact) |
| **Frequency stability** | Lower | Higher |
| **Power rating** | Up to ~2400 W | Up to ~600 W (per unit) |
| **Modern preference** | Less preferred | **More preferred** in modern USM |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**1. Advantages of USM (2 marks):**
- Suitable for **hard, brittle, and non-conductive** materials (ceramics, glass, quartz, germanium, silicon, carbides).
- **No heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — process is cold and does not alter the microstructure.
- **No chemical or electrical alteration** of the workpiece.
- Capable of producing **complex shapes, deep holes, and cavities** (aspect ratio up to 10:1).
- **No residual stress** in the workpiece.
- Tool is **inexpensive and easy to manufacture** (soft metal like titanium, monel, or aluminium bronze).
- Suitable for **fragile and thin** components.

**2. Limitations of USM (2 marks):**
- **Low MRR** compared to other non-traditional processes.
- **High tool wear** during machining.
- MRR **decreases rapidly with depth of cut** (deeper holes → longer slurry path → less efficient).
- **Hole taper** and **overcut** are common.
- Not economical for **soft, ductile materials**.
- Abrasive slurry handling and **recycling is messy**.
- Abrasive particles are **consumable** and need replacement.

**3. Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Machining of **glass, quartz, ceramics, germanium, silicon, tungsten carbide, ferrite, ruby, sapphire**.
- **Drilling round and non-round holes** in hard brittle materials.
- **Die making** for wire drawing.
- **Engraving** of gemstones and jewellery.
- **Slicing and dicing** of semiconductor wafers.
- **Machining of fragile nuclear** and **aerospace ceramic components**.

**4. Process parameters affecting surface finish (1.5 marks):**
- **Abrasive grain size:** Smaller grain size → smoother finish ($R_a \propto d_{grain}$).
- **Vibration amplitude:** Lower amplitude → smoother finish.
- **Workpiece hardness:** Harder workpiece → smoother finish.
- **Slurry concentration:** Lower concentration may give smoother finish.
- **Tool feed rate:** Lower feed → smoother finish.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in USM Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the role of the tool and the abrasive.** The tool does NOT cut — the **abrasive particles** do. Many students wrongly write "the tool vibrates and cuts the workpiece". This is a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting cavitation erosion.** Many students mention only the hammering mechanism. Always mention **both direct hammering (primary, 80–90%) and cavitation erosion (secondary, 10–20%)**.
> 3. **Writing MRR ∝ A** instead of **MRR ∝ A²**. The squared dependence comes from the kinetic energy relation $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \propto A^2$. This is a **favourite trap question**.
> 4. **Conflating USM with EDM.** USM uses **mechanical energy of abrasives**; EDM uses **electrical spark energy**. USM works on **insulators**; EDM does not. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating the unit of MRR.** Always write **mm³/min** in your final answer.
> 6. **Missing the function of the mechanical horn.** The horn is a **velocity transformer / amplitude amplifier** — not just a "connector". Mentioning "amplification of amplitude" is essential.
> 7. **Wrong frequency.** Standard USM frequency is **20 kHz or 40 kHz** — not 50 kHz or 100 kHz. Above 40 kHz is rare in commercial USM.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist — USM (Module 4, PBAUT404)**

- ✅ **Full form / synonym:** Ultrasonic Machining (USM) = **Ultrasonic Impact Grinding (UIG)**.
- ✅ **Energy type:** **Mechanical** (vibration + abrasive impact). NOT electrical, chemical, or thermal.
- ✅ **Frequency:** **20 kHz or 40 kHz**.
- ✅ **Amplitude:** **10–50 µm (peak-to-peak)**.
- ✅ **Static force:** **0.5–25 N**.
- ✅ **Abrasive options:** **B₄C (hardest) > SiC > Al₂O₃ > Diamond** (for very hard workpieces). Common choice: **B₄C for carbide, SiC for glass**.
- ✅ **Tool material:** Soft ductile metal — **Titanium, Monel, Aluminium bronze, Stainless steel**. Tool wear is *high* because the tool is intentionally soft.
- ✅ **Carrier fluid:** **Water** (cheap, good cavitation, good cooling).
- ✅ **Process type:** **Non-traditional, mechanical, cold process.**
- ✅ **Material removal mechanism (must memorise):**
  - **Direct hammering of abrasives → micro-chipping → 80–90% of MRR (primary).**
  - **Cavitation erosion (bubble collapse → micro-jets) → 10–20% of MRR (secondary).**
  - **Chemical action (water oxidation) → negligible (tertiary).**
- ✅ **MRR relation:** $MRR \propto \dfrac{F \cdot A^2 \cdot f \cdot K_{ab}}{H_w}$
- ✅ **Abrasive hardness rule:** $H_{ab} \geq 1.5 \cdot H_w$ (1.5× rule).
- ✅ **Surface finish relation:** $R_a \propto \dfrac{d_{grain}}{3}$.
- ✅ **Component sequence:** **Power supply → Generator → Transducer → Horn → Tool → Slurry → Workpiece.**
- ✅ **Transducer comparison:** Piezoelectric (PZT) is **modern, more efficient (90–96%)**; magnetostrictive (Ni) is older, less efficient (25–40%) but higher power.
- ✅ **Horn shapes:** Stepped (most common), conical, exponential, catenoidal.
- ✅ **Tool length for resonance:** $L_{tool} = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{2 f}$ (half-wavelength).
- ✅ **Workpiece suitability:** Hard, brittle, non-conductive → **glass, ceramics, quartz, germanium, silicon, WC, ferrite, sapphire, ruby**.
- ✅ **Not suitable for:** Soft ductile materials (steel, aluminium, copper).
- ✅ **Hole aspect ratio:** Up to **10:1** (deep narrow holes).
- ✅ **MRR typical values:** Glass ~ 50–200 mm³/min, Tungsten Carbide ~ 5–15 mm³/min, Germanium ~ 100–400 mm³/min.
- ✅ **Process characteristics:** No HAZ, no chemical/electrical alteration, no residual stress, low MRR, high tool wear, slurry handling required.

> 🎯 **One-line Exam Memory Trick:**
> *“**U**ltrasonic **M**achining = **M**echanical **H**its by **A**brasives at 20 kHz on **B**rittle **M**aterials.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abrasive Jet Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Abrasive Jet Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)** is a non-traditional, mechanical-type material removal process in which **high-velocity abrasive particles**, entrained in a focused gas stream, are directed through a precisely calibrated nozzle onto the workpiece surface. The kinetic energy of the impacting abrasive grains causes **micro-chipping and erosion** of the workpiece material, thereby removing it without any direct tool-to-workpiece contact, thermal effect, or chemical reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Verbatim:** AJM is classified under the **"Mechanical Energy-Based Non-Traditional Machining"** category in the PBAUT404 syllabus. It is a **cold machining process** where material removal occurs purely through the mechanical impact of abrasive particles accelerated by a carrier gas (typically compressed air, nitrogen, or CO₂).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Sandblasting on Steroids"

Imagine you are trying to **carve your initials into a glass window** using only a thin, high-pressure stream of fine sand. If you blow sand casually, nothing happens. But if you can make the sand grains travel at *supersonic speed* through a tiny nozzle, the sand acts like thousands of tiny invisible chisels, each one chipping away a microscopic bit of glass. The cumulative effect of millions of these tiny chisel strikes per second is what removes material from the hardest surfaces — even glass, ceramics, and hardened tool steel. That is, conceptually, **Abrasive Jet Machining**.

### 1.3 Key Identifying Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hallmark Properties of AJM — Memorize for KTU Viva & MCQs:**
> - **No Thermal Damage:** Cold process, no Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ).
> - **No Direct Tool Contact:** Non-contact, no tool wear.
> - **High Versatility:** Can machine hard, brittle, and heat-sensitive materials.
> - **Carrier Gas:** Pneumatic (gas-driven) — distinguishes it from WJM (water-driven).
> - **Standoff Distance (SOD):** Critical parameter, typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm**.
> - **Abrasive:** Usually **Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃)** or **Glass Beads** or **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**.
> - **Typical Abrasive Grit Size:** **10 µm to 50 µm** (very fine particles).
> - **Abrasive Velocity Range:** **50 m/s to 200 m/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The AJM process executes material removal through a precisely orchestrated sequence of physical events:

1. **Step 1 — Gas Compression & Filtration:** Ambient air (or N₂/CO₂) is compressed (typically to **2 bar to 8 bar**) and passed through a moisture/dust filter to obtain a clean, dry carrier gas.
2. **Step 2 — Abrasive Feeding:** Abrasive particles stored in a hopper are metered into the gas stream through a **vibratory or suction-type feeder** at a controlled mass flow rate (typically **1 g/s to 5 g/s**).
3. **Step 3 — Mixing Chamber Mixing:** The abrasive gets uniformly mixed with the high-pressure gas in a mixing chamber (also called the *abrasive mixing unit*).
4. **Step 4 — Nozzle Acceleration:** The abrasive-laden gas is forced through a small-diameter tungsten carbide or sapphire **nozzle** (typically **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm diameter**), which converts the high pressure into high kinetic energy.
5. **Step 5 — Jet Impingement:** The focused, high-velocity abrasive jet strikes the workpiece surface, positioned at a precisely controlled **standoff distance (SOD)**.
6. **Step 6 — Micro-Chipping Action:** Each abrasive particle acts as a microscopic cutting edge, causing **micro-fracture, micro-chipping, and erosion** of the workpiece material.
7. **Step 7 — Debris Evacuation:** The spent abrasive particles, workpiece debris, and reflected gas are collected in a **dust collection chamber** below the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind AJM's Effectiveness:** The energy transfer per particle is governed by the kinetic energy equation $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$. By making the velocity $V$ very high (using a converging nozzle), the kinetic energy per particle becomes large enough to exceed the **fracture threshold** of even very hard, brittle materials — without the need for heat or chemical action.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below contains every formula you must know to solve any KTU numerical on AJM. Master these symbols and units cold.**

| **Symbol** | **Parameter** | **Typical Range / Standard Value** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $m_a$ | Mass flow rate of abrasive | 1 – 5 | g/s |
| $V$ | Velocity of abrasive particles at nozzle exit | 50 – 200 | m/s |
| $d$ | Average diameter of abrasive grit | 10 – 50 | µm |
| $D_n$ | Nozzle diameter | 0.5 – 1.5 | mm |
| SOD | Stand-off Distance (nozzle tip to workpiece) | 0.5 – 5 | mm |
| $\rho_a$ | Density of abrasive (Al₂O₃) | 3960 | kg/m³ |
| $K$ | Material removal efficiency constant | 0.1 – 0.3 | — |
| $H_w$ | Hardness of workpiece | — | HV / HRc |
| $P_g$ | Gas pressure in mixing chamber | 2 – 8 | bar |
| $\eta$ | Overall energy transfer efficiency | 10 – 25 | % |

### 2.3 The Master Equation — Material Removal Rate (MRR) for AJM

The **governing empirical equation** for Material Removal Rate in AJM, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, is:

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}
$$

Where the additive generalization with grit size yields:

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2} \cdot d^{a}
$$

> **Where:**
> - $K$ = empirical constant (function of material pair, abrasive type)
> - $m_a$ = mass flow rate of abrasive (g/s)
> - $V$ = velocity of abrasive particles (m/s)
> - $d$ = average grit diameter (µm)
> - $a$ = grit-size exponent, typically $a \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

AJM is widely used in:
- **Electronics:** Drilling and scribing of micro-holes in printed circuit boards (PCBs) and silicon wafers.
- **Aerospace:** Deburring, cleaning, and surface preparation of turbine blades and engine components.
- **Medical Devices:** Micro-machining of bio-ceramics and cutting of hypodermic tubes.
- **Glass Industry:** Frosting, etching, decorative engraving, and precision cutting of brittle glass sheets.
- **Tool & Die Making:** Cleaning of intricate die cavities, removing flash from injection-moulded parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MRR Equation from First Principles (Energy Approach)

We will derive the AJM material removal rate equation from scratch using the **kinetic energy transfer principle**. This derivation is a *guaranteed 7-mark question* type in KTU ESE.

---

**Given (KTU Standard Problem Setup):**
- Mass flow rate of abrasive = $m_a$ (kg/s)
- Velocity of abrasive particles = $V$ (m/s)
- Material removal efficiency factor = $\eta$ (dimensionless)
- Workpiece density = $\rho_w$ (kg/m³)

---

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of a Single Abrasive Particle**

The kinetic energy of a single abrasive particle of mass $m_p$ moving with velocity $V$ is given by Newton's classical mechanics:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_p \cdot V^{2}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the fundamental Newtonian kinetic energy formula. The particle carries this much energy just before it strikes the workpiece.

---

**Step 2 — Total Kinetic Energy Delivered Per Unit Time**

The mass flow rate $m_a$ represents the total mass of abrasive particles striking the workpiece per second. Therefore, the **total kinetic energy flux** (energy per second) delivered to the workpiece surface is:

$$
\dot{E}_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}
$$

> **Logic:** Since $m_a$ already has units of kg/s (mass per unit time), multiplying by $\frac{1}{2} V^{2}$ gives energy per second, i.e., **power in Watts**.

---

**Step 3 — Application of the Efficiency Factor**

In reality, not all the kinetic energy of the abrasive is used in removing material. A significant portion is lost as heat, sound, reflection, and rebounding. We therefore introduce an **efficiency factor** $\eta$, representing the fraction of energy actually used for material removal:

$$
\dot{E}_{useful} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}
$$

> **Logic:** For AJM, $\eta$ typically lies in the range of **0.1 to 0.25** (i.e., 10–25% efficiency), because most kinetic energy is lost to chip heating and particle rebound.

---

**Step 4 — Energy Required to Remove Unit Volume of Workpiece Material**

The specific energy required to remove a unit volume of workpiece material is denoted $U$ (J/m³). This is essentially the **volumetric energy density** of the workpiece, related to its strength/hardness. Therefore, the volume of material removed per second (which is the MRR) is:

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{\dot{E}_{useful}}{U}
$$

> **Logic:** MRR is volume removed per unit time. If we divide useful power (J/s) by specific energy (J/m³), we get m³/s — which is volume per unit time.

---

**Step 5 — Final MRR Equation**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{\eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}}{U}
$$

Defining a combined empirical constant $K = \frac{\eta}{2U}$, we get the **canonical KTU form**:

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}}
$$

> **Final Logic:** This shows that the MRR is **directly proportional to the mass flow rate of abrasive** and **proportional to the square of the abrasive velocity**. Doubling the velocity therefore **quadruples** the MRR — this is a frequently tested concept in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on MRR

> [!NOTE]
> **The matrix below is a high-yield KTU tool — examiners often ask 3-mark questions framed as "Discuss the effect of X on MRR" or "How does Y influence AJM performance?"**

| **Process Parameter** | **Effect on MRR** | **Reasoning / Mechanism** | **KTU Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Abrasive Mass Flow Rate ($m_a$) ↑** | MRR increases **linearly** | More particles per second = more impacts | Linear proportionality proven in Step 5 |
| **Abrasive Velocity ($V$) ↑** | MRR increases **quadratically** | $V^{2}$ in the formula | **Highest leverage parameter** for MRR control |
| **Grit Size ($d$) ↑** | MRR increases | Larger particles carry more momentum | Trade-off: surface roughness worsens |
| **Stand-off Distance (SOD) ↑** | MRR first increases, then **decreases** | Optimal SOD ≈ 1–3 mm | Beam diverges if SOD too large |
| **Gas Pressure ($P_g$) ↑** | MRR increases | Higher pressure = higher exit velocity | Practical upper limit: nozzle wear |
| **Nozzle Diameter ($D_n$) ↑** | MRR increases (slightly) | Larger beam = more particles per second | Trade-off: kerf width increases |
| **Abrasive Hardness ↑** (SiC > Al₂O₃) | MRR increases | Harder particles fracture workpiece easier | SiC preferred for ceramics |
| **Workpiece Hardness ($H_w$) ↑** | MRR **decreases** | Harder material resists micro-chipping | Brittle materials work best |
| **SOD too low (<0.5 mm)** | MRR drops sharply | Nozzle clogging, beam blockage | **Critical pitfall** in KTU lab viva |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Question Pattern)

**Problem:** In an AJM operation, the abrasive mass flow rate is **2 g/s** and the particle velocity at the nozzle exit is **150 m/s**. If the material removal efficiency constant $K$ for the given workpiece-abrasive pair is **$2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ mm³/g·m²/s²**, calculate the MRR.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $m_a = 2$ g/s
- $V = 150$ m/s
- $K = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$

Using the master equation:

$$
\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2) \cdot (150)^{2}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2) \cdot (22500)
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = (2.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (45000)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 0.1125 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{/s}}
$$

> **Answer:** The Material Removal Rate is **0.1125 mm³/s** (i.e., approximately **6.75 mm³/min**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an AJM System (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete functional flow** of an Abrasive Jet Machining system, mapping every component, its role, and its physical interconnection. This block-level mapping is the most KTU-friendly way to represent the AJM hardware architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC1: Compressed Air Supply] --> A2[AC2: Pressure Regulator 2 to 8 bar]
    A2 --> A3[AC3: Moisture and Dust Filter]
    A3 --> A4[AC4: Mixing Chamber Inlet]
    A5[AC5: Abrasive Hopper] --> A6[AC6: Vibratory Feeder]
    A6 --> A4
    A4 --> A7[AC7: Nozzle Tungsten Carbide 0.5 to 1.5 mm]
    A7 --> A8[AC8: High Velocity Abrasive Jet]
    A8 --> A9[AC9: Workpiece Mounted on XY Table]
    A9 --> A10[AC10: Dust Collection Chamber]
    A10 --> A11[AC11: Vacuum Exhaust Pump]
    B1[SOD Control Z Axis] -.-> A9
    B2[Pressure Gauge PG] -.-> A2
    B3[Mass Flow Controller MFC] -.-> A6
```

**Diagram Component Legend & Functional Mapping:**

| **Block ID** | **Component Name** | **Function in AJM** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AC1 | Compressed Air Source | Provides pneumatic driving energy |
| AC2 | Pressure Regulator | Maintains constant carrier gas pressure |
| AC3 | Filter Unit | Removes moisture and oil contamination |
| AC4 | Mixing Chamber | Combines abrasive with high-pressure gas |
| AC5 | Abrasive Hopper | Stores abrasive particles (Al₂O₃ / SiC) |
| AC6 | Vibratory Feeder | Controls abrasive mass flow rate $m_a$ |
| AC7 | Nozzle | Converts pressure into kinetic energy |
| AC8 | Abrasive Jet | Focused stream of accelerated particles |
| AC9 | Workpiece Stage | Holds and positions the workpiece |
| AC10 | Dust Collection Chamber | Captures debris and spent abrasive |
| AC11 | Vacuum Pump | Evacuates the dust and maintains suction |

### 4.2 Process Flow Block Diagram (KTU Examination Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Gas Compression] --> S2[Stage 2: Abrasive Metering]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Mixing in Chamber]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Nozzle Acceleration]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Jet Impingement on Workpiece]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Micro Chipping and Material Removal]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Debris Collection and Exhaust]
    S5 -.SOD Control.-> S4
    S5 -.XY Table Motion.-> S6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Use These Diagrams in KTU Exams:** Always draw the block diagram **first** (3–4 marks of any 14-mark AJM question) before writing the explanation. The examiner awards **2 marks for a clean, labeled diagram** even if your explanation has minor errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Abrasive Jet Machining. List any four advantages of AJM over conventional machining.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM) is a non-traditional material removal process in which **high-velocity abrasive particles**, entrained in a focused gas stream, are directed through a nozzle onto the workpiece surface to remove material by **micro-chipping action**.

**Any Four Advantages (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**
1. **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** Cold process, suitable for heat-sensitive materials.
2. **No Tool Wear:** Non-contact process — the nozzle is the only wearing component.
3. **Versatility:** Can machine hard, brittle materials like glass, ceramics, and quartz.
4. **High Precision:** Capable of machining intricate shapes and micro-holes.
5. **No Mechanical Stress:** No residual stress induced in the workpiece.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*What is the role of the **stand-off distance (SOD)** in AJM? What happens if SOD is too small or too large?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Stand-off Distance (SOD)** is the gap between the **nozzle tip** and the **workpiece surface**. It is a critical process parameter (typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm**) that controls the focus, energy density, and concentration of the abrasive jet at the impact zone.

- **If SOD is too small (< 0.5 mm):** The jet does not have time to converge into a focused stream; **nozzle clogging** occurs due to back-splash of debris. MRR drops sharply.
- **If SOD is too large (> 5 mm):** The jet **diverges** and loses energy density; the abrasive particles spread out, reducing both MRR and accuracy.
- **Optimum SOD (1–3 mm):** Gives the highest MRR and best surface finish.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**
*With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the working principle of Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM). Also, derive the expression for Material Removal Rate (MRR).*

**Part (a) — Working Principle with Diagram (7 Marks):**
