**Schematic Diagram (3 Marks):** The block diagram (as shown in Section 4 above) must be drawn clearly, with all 6 main units labeled: (i) Gas supply, (ii) Filter, (iii) Abrasive hopper & feeder, (iv) Mixing chamber, (v) Nozzle, (vi) Workpiece and dust collector.

**Working Principle Explanation (4 Marks):**
- Compressed gas (air/N₂/CO₂) at **2–8 bar** is filtered and fed into the mixing chamber.
- Abrasive particles (Al₂O₃, 10–50 µm) are metered from a hopper via a vibratory feeder.
- The gas-abrasive mixture is forced through a **tungsten carbide nozzle** (0.5–1.5 mm), where it accelerates to **50–200 m/s**.
- The high-velocity jet impinges on the workpiece, held at a stand-off distance of **1–3 mm**.
- Material removal occurs by **micro-chipping** due to the kinetic energy of abrasive particles.
- Debris is collected in a dust chamber connected to a vacuum pump.

**Part (b) — Derivation of MRR Expression (7 Marks):**

Start with kinetic energy of one particle:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_p V^{2}
$$

For mass flow rate $m_a$ (kg/s), total KE per second is:

$$
\dot{E}_{total} = \frac{1}{2} m_a V^{2}
$$

Applying efficiency factor $\eta$:

$$
\dot{E}_{useful} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} m_a V^{2}
$$

Using specific energy $U$ of the workpiece material, MRR is:

$$
\text{MRR} = \frac{\dot{E}_{useful}}{U} = \frac{\eta \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}}{2U}
$$

Defining empirical constant $K = \frac{\eta}{2U}$:

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}}
$$

> **Conclusion (½ mark):** MRR is **linearly proportional** to the mass flow rate $m_a$ and **quadratically proportional** to the abrasive velocity $V$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**
*Discuss the process parameters of Abrasive Jet Machining in detail. How does each parameter affect the Material Removal Rate (MRR) and surface finish? List the major applications, advantages, and limitations of AJM.*

**Part (a) — Process Parameters and Their Effects (7 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Effect on MRR** | **Effect on Surface Finish** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Abrasive mass flow rate ($m_a$) | ↑ linearly with $m_a$ | Becomes rougher at high $m_a$ |
| Abrasive velocity ($V$) | ↑ as $V^{2}$ | Smoother at moderate $V$ |
| Grit size ($d$) | ↑ with $d$ | Rougher with larger grit |
| Stand-off distance (SOD) | Optimum at 1–3 mm | Best finish at optimal SOD |
| Gas pressure ($P_g$) | ↑ with $P_g$ | Controlled at moderate $P_g$ |
| Nozzle diameter ($D_n$) | ↑ slightly | Larger kerf width |
| Abrasive type (Al₂O₃ / SiC) | Higher for SiC | SiC gives finer edges |

**Part (b) — Applications, Advantages, and Limitations (7 Marks):**

**Applications (2½ Marks):**
- Drilling micro-holes in PCBs and silicon wafers.
- Engraving and decorative cutting of glass.
- Deburring and cleaning of aerospace turbine blades.
- Machining of ceramics and brittle materials.
- Surface preparation before coating.

**Advantages (2½ Marks):**
- No thermal damage (cold process).
- Can machine very hard, brittle, and heat-sensitive materials.
- No tool wear, no mechanical stress.
- High precision for micro-features.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
- Low MRR compared to conventional machining for soft metals.
- Nozzle wears out and needs frequent replacement.
- Requires dust collection and safety measures (silica dust hazard).
- Cannot produce deep cavities — limited to shallow cuts.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks in AJM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing MRR without mentioning mm³/s or cm³/min. KTU examiners deduct **½ mark** for missing units.
> 2. **Wrong Velocity Exponent:** Some students write MRR $\propto V$ instead of $V^{2}$. The exponent **2 is critical** — quadratic dependence.
> 3. **Confusing AJM with WJM:** AJM uses **gas + abrasive**; WJM uses **water + abrasive**. Mixing these up costs the entire process question.
> 4. **Missing the Diagram:** In 14-mark questions, **3–4 marks are reserved for a clean, labeled schematic**. A blank answer without a diagram is a guaranteed mark-shedder.
> 5. **Ignoring the SOD Discussion:** SOD is a *favorite* 3-mark short question. Always quote the numerical range **0.5–5 mm** and the optimum **1–3 mm**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density KTU Quick-Revision Bullets for AJM:**
> - ✅ AJM = **Mechanical-type, cold, non-contact, non-traditional** process.
> - ✅ Material removal is by **micro-chipping** — *not* by melting, not by chemical reaction.
> - ✅ Master equation: $\text{MRR} = K \cdot m_a \cdot V^{2}$ — **linear in $m_a$, quadratic in $V$**.
> - ✅ Carrier gas: **Air / N₂ / CO₂**; Abrasive: **Al₂O₃ (most common) / SiC / Glass beads**.
> - ✅ Grit size: **10–50 µm**; Abrasive velocity: **50–200 m/s**; SOD: **0.5–5 mm**.
> - ✅ Nozzle: **Tungsten carbide or sapphire**, diameter **0.5–1.5 mm**.
> - ✅ Optimum SOD ≈ **1–3 mm**; too small → clogging; too large → divergence.
> - ✅ Best for **hard, brittle, heat-sensitive** materials (glass, ceramics, quartz).
> - ✅ Major applications: **PCB drilling, glass engraving, deburring of aerospace parts**.
> - ✅ Key advantage: **No HAZ, no tool wear, no residual stress**.
> - ✅ Key limitation: **Low MRR, nozzle wear, dust hazard**.
> - ✅ Always draw the **schematic diagram first** in a 14-mark question — examiners reward it.
> - ✅ **Quadratic dependence on V** is the most tested theoretical point — memorize it.
> - ✅ Distinguish AJM (gas) from **WJM (water)** and **AWJM (abrasive water jet)** — they are *different* processes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Jet Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Jet Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Water Jet Machining (WJM)** is a non-traditional, mechanical type material removal process in which a high-velocity, coherent stream of water is used to erode material from the workpiece by impact and shear forces. It is classified under **Mechanical Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)** processes per the KTU 2024 manufacturing taxonomy, distinct from thermal (EDM, LBM) and chemical (ECM) processes.

When an **abrasive substance** (e.g., garnet, aluminium oxide) is added to the water stream, the process becomes **Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM)**, enabling cutting of hard, brittle, and tough materials like metals, ceramics, stones, and composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** WJM is **purely mechanical** — no heat, no chemical reaction, and **zero Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)**. This is its single most important distinguishing feature in board examinations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine standing at the edge of a swimming pool with a thin, high-pressure hose in your hand. The water exits as a fine, needle-like stream. Now imagine increasing that pressure by **4,000 times** what a normal garden hose delivers. The stream becomes so powerful that it can slice through steel, glass, and even granite — without generating any heat, sparks, or dust.

That is essentially what a Water Jet Machining setup does:
- A high-pressure pump **squeezes** water (just like squeezing a watermelon seed between your fingers shoots the seed out at high speed).
- The water escapes through a tiny hole (orifice), forming a thin, **supersonic jet**.
- This jet acts like a *hydraulic chisel*, cutting material along its path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** Typical operating pressures in WJM range from **100 MPa to 400 MPa** (1,000 to 4,000 bar). For comparison, a standard car tyre pressure is only ~0.3 MPa. This pressure differential is what makes the jet powerful enough for material removal.

### Working Principle — The Physics in One Line

> Water is pressurised → passed through a small orifice → exits as a high-velocity coherent jet → erodes material through **kinetic impact and hydraulic shear**.

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Standoff Distance (SOD)** | Gap between nozzle exit and workpiece | 2 mm – 25 mm |
| **Traverse Rate (v)** | Speed at which nozzle moves over workpiece | 50 mm/min – 2,000 mm/min |
| **Kerf Width** | Width of cut produced by the jet | 0.5 mm – 3 mm |
| **Orifice Diameter** | Diameter of sapphire/ruby orifice | 0.1 mm – 0.5 mm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Architecture (Components of a WJM System)

A standard WJM setup consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **High-Pressure Pump / Intensifier** — Generates the required pressure. Direct-drive pumps (up to 100 MPa) or hydraulic intensifiers (up to 400 MPa).
2. **Accumulator** — Stores high-pressure water and smooths out pressure pulsations.
3. **High-Pressure Tubing** — Conducts water to the cutting head without leaks.
4. **On/Off Valve** — Pneumatically or hydraulically actuated valve controlling jet start/stop.
5. **Nozzle Assembly** — Sapphire/ruby orifice where the jet is formed.
6. **Catcher Tank** — Collects spent water and debris.
7. **X-Y-Z CNC Traverse System** — Moves the nozzle over the workpiece (or vice versa).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

- **Why pressurise water?** Because kinetic energy (used for cutting) is proportional to velocity squared. Pressure is the *driver* of velocity.
- **Why a small orifice?** Same flow at a smaller cross-section means **higher velocity** (continuity equation).
- **Why an accumulator?** A positive-displacement pump delivers pulsating flow. The accumulator converts this into a **steady, continuous jet**.
- **Why water and not oil?** Water is incompressible, cheap, non-toxic, and environmentally safe.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho} }$ | **Jet velocity** from pressure differential (Bernoulli) | m/s |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | $F = \rho \, Q \, v_j$ | **Impact force** of the jet on workpiece | N |
| 4 | $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ | **Hydraulic power** consumed | W |
| 5 | $Q = A_{orifice} \cdot v_j$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 6 | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_j^{2}$ | **Kinetic energy** of jet per unit time | J/s (W) |
| 7 | $MRR = w \cdot d \cdot v_f$ | Material removal rate (length × width × feed) | mm³/s |
| 8 | $h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot v_j^{2} / g$ | **Pressure head** of jet (hydraulic height) | m |
| 9 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{MRR}$ | Energy per unit volume removed | J/mm³ |
| 10 | $\text{Kerf} \approx d_{or} \cdot k$ | Empirical kerf width (k is a material factor, $\mid$ value $\vert$ 1.5 – 3) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **ρ (rho)** = density of water = **1000 kg/m³** (memorise this). For seawater or fluid additives, replace as applicable.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Concept

- **Aerospace industry:** Machining of carbon-fibre-reinforced polymers (CFRP), titanium, and Inconel sheets without thermal distortion.
- **Mining & Quarrying:** Cutting marble, granite, and sandstone blocks.
- **Automotive:** Trimming of composite dashboards and pre-formed body panels.
- **Defence & Nuclear Decontamination:** Safe cutting of contaminated materials without sparks or dust ignition.
- **Food industry:** Cutting frozen foods, biscuits, and chocolate (with potable water).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Jet Velocity ($v_j$)

We apply **Bernoulli's Equation** between the high-pressure reservoir (point 1) and the free jet exit (point 2).

**Assumptions:**
- Incompressible, steady flow of water.
- No friction losses along the streamline.
- Both points are at approximately the same elevation (height terms cancel out).

Starting equation (Bernoulli between point 1 and point 2):

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2$$

Apply simplifications step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Reservoir is large**, so $v_1 \approx 0$.

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + 0 + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_j^{2}}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 2 — Heights are equal** ($z_1 = z_2$), so the $g z$ terms cancel.

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_j^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Atmospheric pressure at jet exit**: $P_2 = P_{atm}$. Take gauge pressure: $P_1 - P_{atm} = \Delta P$.

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \dfrac{v_j^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v_j$:**

$$v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho} }$$

> This is the **fundamental equation of WJM** — it directly links pump pressure to the speed at which water strikes the workpiece.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A WJM system operates at a pressure of **200 MPa**. The orifice diameter is **0.30 mm**. Calculate:
(a) The jet velocity at exit.
(b) The volumetric flow rate.
(c) The kinetic energy flux of the jet.
(d) The hydraulic power consumed by the pump.

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta P = 200 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $d_{or} = 0.30$ mm $= 0.30 \times 10^{-3}$ m

---

**Part (a) — Jet velocity:**

Apply the derived formula.

$$v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho} }$$

Substitute numerical values.

$$v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{6}}{1000} }$$

Simplify the numerator and denominator.

$$v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6}}{1000} } = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{5}}$$

Final value.

$$\boxed{v_j = 632.46 \text{ m/s}}$$

This is approximately **Mach 1.84** (supersonic range) — explaining why the jet can cut hard materials.

---

**Part (b) — Volumetric flow rate:**

Orifice area is calculated first.

$$A_{or} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{or}^{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.30 \times 10^{-3})^{2}$$

Evaluate the square.

$$A_{or} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-8} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

Apply the continuity relation $Q = A_{or} \cdot v_j$.

$$Q = 7.0686 \times 10^{-8} \times 632.46$$

$$\boxed{Q = 4.471 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 2.68 \text{ L/min}}$$

---

**Part (c) — Kinetic Energy Flux:**

The mass flow rate is required first.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1000 \times 4.471 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.04471 \text{ kg/s}$$

Apply the kinetic energy equation $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v_j^{2}$.

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 0.04471 \times (632.46)^{2}$$

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 0.04471 \times 400{,}000$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 8{,}942 \text{ W} \approx 8.94 \text{ kW}}$$

---

**Part (d) — Hydraulic Power Consumed:**

$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ (pressure × flow rate).

$$P_{hyd} = 200 \times 10^{6} \times 4.471 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 8{,}942 \text{ W} = 8.94 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The hydraulic power and kinetic energy flux are numerically equal — this confirms **100% conversion efficiency of pressure energy to kinetic energy** in the ideal Bernoulli case.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Influence on Performance

| Parameter | Increase Effect on $v_j$ | Effect on MRR | Effect on Kerf Width | Examiner's Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure ($\Delta P$) | $\uparrow$ (square root) | $\uparrow$ Significantly | $\uparrow$ Slightly | Most dominant variable |
| Orifice diameter | — (independent) | $\uparrow$ (more flow) | $\uparrow$ Directly | Affects pump load |
| Standoff distance | — (independent) | $\downarrow$ (jet disperses) | $\uparrow$ | Keep minimal for accuracy |
| Traverse rate | — | $\downarrow$ (less time contact) | $\downarrow$ Slightly | Trade-off: speed vs quality |
| Abrasive (in AWJM) | — | $\uparrow\uparrow$ (massively) | $\uparrow$ | Switches WJM → AWJM |

### 3.4 Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM) — The Extension

When the material is too hard for plain water, an **abrasive slurry** or **abrasive injection** is used. Three configurations exist:

1. **Suspension Jet** — Abrasive mixed with water before pumping (high wear).
2. **Injection Jet** — Abrasive sucked into a mixing chamber post-orifice (most common).
3. **Abrasive Suspension Jet (ASJ)** — Abrasive suspended in a polymer carrier.

The MRR in AWJM is governed by a fundamentally different mechanism — **microscopic impact erosion** — and follows the empirical relation:

$$MRR_{AWJM} = K \cdot m_a \cdot v_j^{n}$$

Where:
- $m_a$ = abrasive mass flow rate (g/s)
- $K$ = material-dependent constant
- $n \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ (typically 2 for brittle, 1.5 for ductile)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WJM System

The diagram below maps the complete flow of energy and matter through a Water Jet Machining installation. All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `pumpA`, `valveB`) to comply with Mermaid parser safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Water Inlet] --> F1[Filter Unit]
    F1 --> P1[Low Pressure Pump]
    P1 --> I1[Intensifier Stage]
    I1 --> ACC[Accumulator]
    ACC --> V1[High Pressure On-Off Valve]
    V1 --> T1[High Pressure Tubing]
    T1 --> N1[Nozzle Orifice]
    N1 --> J1[High Velocity Water Jet]
    J1 --> WP1[Workpiece]
    WP1 --> C1[Catcher Tank]
    C1 --> RC1[Recycling Unit]
    RC1 --> W1
    CNC1[CNC X-Y Traverse] -.-> N1
    ABN1[Abrasive Hopper] -.-> V1
    PWR1[Three Phase Power Supply] --> I1
    PWR1 --> CNC1
    style J1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style WP1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style I1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style ACC fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Process Parameter Interaction Schematic

The block diagram below illustrates how the principal input parameters interact to produce the final cut-quality outputs. This is the **most commonly drawn diagram** in KTU board exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pressure P] --> O1[Orifice Diameter d]
    O1 --> V1[Jet Velocity vj]
    P1 --> V1
    V1 --> F1[Impact Force F]
    SOD1[Standoff Distance SOD] --> F1
    TR1[Traverse Rate vf] --> F1
    F1 --> MRR1[Material Removal Rate]
    F1 --> KW1[Kerf Width]
    F1 --> SR1[Surface Roughness Ra]
    MRR1 --> QC1[Quality of Cut]
    KW1 --> QC1
    SR1 --> QC1
    ABR1[Abrasive Flow Rate ma] -.-> MRR1
    ABR1 -.-> KW1
    style V1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style MRR1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style QC1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix: WJM vs AWJM vs Abrasive Jet Machining (AJM)

| Property | WJM | AWJM | AJM (Dry) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Medium | Pure water | Water + abrasive | Compressed air + abrasive |
| Typical Pressure | 100–400 MPa | 100–400 MPa | 0.2–1.0 MPa |
| Can cut steel? | No | Yes | Limited (thin sheets) |
| Heat Affected Zone | None | None | None |
| Nozzle Material | Sapphire / Ruby | Sapphire + Carbide | Tungsten Carbide |
| Cost of Operation | Low (no abrasive) | High (abrasive cost) | Very Low |
| Surface Finish | Very Good | Good (slight striations) | Moderate |
| Environmental Impact | Excellent | Good (slurry disposal) | Dust generation issue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When the question says "non-thermal NTM that can cut thick steel plates," the answer is **AWJM**, not WJM. Pure WJM is restricted to soft, non-metallic materials.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Water Jet Machining. List any two advantages of WJM over conventional machining.**

**Model Answer:**

Water Jet Machining (WJM) is a non-traditional material removal process in which material is removed by the impact and erosive action of a high-velocity, coherent jet of water generated at pressures of the order of 100–400 MPa.

**Two advantages over conventional machining:**
1. **No heat-affected zone (HAZ):** Since it is a purely mechanical process, there is no thermal distortion, residual stress, or metallurgical change in the workpiece.
2. **No tool wear:** Unlike a conventional cutting tool, the water jet does not wear out; only the orifice may require periodic replacement.

**Examiner's note (1 mark):** Also acceptable — wide material applicability, environmental safety, and ability to cut complex contours.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Water Jet Machining (WJM) and Abrasive Water Jet Machining (AWJM).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | WJM | AWJM |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting medium | Pure water jet | Water + abrasive particles (garnet) |
| Pressure | 100–400 MPa | 100–400 MPa |
| Can cut metals? | No (only soft materials) | Yes (steel, Inconel, titanium) |
| Mechanism | Hydraulic shear | Erosion by abrasive impact |
| Applications | Paper, food, rubber | Aerospace, stone, metal cutting |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of Water Jet Machining. List the major process parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WJM system operates at a pressure of 250 MPa through a 0.4 mm diameter orifice. Calculate the jet velocity, volumetric flow rate, and the hydraulic power required. Take density of water = 1000 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram Description):**
- Water from a reservoir is filtered and pressurised by a high-pressure pump/intensifier.
- The pressurised water is stored in an **accumulator** to dampen pulsations.
- A pneumatically actuated **on/off valve** controls the jet.
- The water is forced through a tiny **orifice** (sapphire/ruby) in the nozzle, emerging as a high-velocity coherent jet (up to ~900 m/s).
- This jet strikes the workpiece and removes material through **impact erosion and hydraulic shear**.
- The nozzle is traversed via a **CNC X-Y table** to obtain the desired cut profile.
- Spent water and debris fall into a **catcher tank**, with water optionally recycled.

**Major Process Parameters:**
1. Water pressure ($\Delta P$)
2. Orifice / nozzle diameter
3. Standoff distance (SOD)
4. Traverse rate
5. Jet impingement angle
6. Abrasive flow rate (in AWJM)

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $\Delta P = 250 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $d_{or} = 0.4$ mm $= 0.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Jet velocity:**

$$v_j = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 250 \times 10^{6}}{1000} } = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\boxed{v_j = 707.1 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Orifice area:**

$$A_{or} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.4 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = A_{or} \cdot v_j = 1.2566 \times 10^{-7} \times 707.1$$

$$\boxed{Q = 8.886 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 5.33 \text{ L/min}}$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic power:**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 250 \times 10^{6} \times 8.886 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 22{,}215 \text{ W} \approx 22.22 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a high-pressure intensifier used in WJM. Why is it preferred over a direct-drive pump? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages, limitations, and major industrial applications of Water Jet Machining. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction:**
A hydraulic intensifier is a two-stage pressure amplifier. The **primary (low-pressure) stage** is a hydraulic ram driven by an electric motor-pump unit at $\sim$ 7–20 MPa. The **secondary (high-pressure) stage** is a smaller-diameter plunger connected to the same shaft. The pressure intensification ratio is given by:

$$P_{high} = P_{low} \times \left( \dfrac{A_{low}}{A_{high}} \right)$$

The areas are inversely proportional to the square of the diameters.

**Working:**
- Low-pressure oil drives the large piston.
- The connected small piston compresses water, multiplying the pressure by the area ratio.
- A check valve allows water in during the suction stroke, and a delivery valve releases it during the compression stroke.
- An **accumulator** smooths the resulting pulsating flow.

**Why preferred over direct-drive pump:**
1. Direct-drive pumps (e.g., triplex pumps) are limited to ~100 MPa; intensifiers easily achieve 400 MPa.
2. Intensifiers can be coupled to standard low-pressure hydraulic systems — cheaper and more reliable at extreme pressures.
3. Better pressure stability with the accumulator.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Advantages:**
1. No heat-affected zone — ideal for heat-sensitive materials.
2. Minimal scrap (narrow kerf) — material-efficient.
3. Multi-material cutting (in AWJM): metals, ceramics, composites, stone.
4. No chemical or electrical hazards — environmentally friendly.
5. Can cut very thick plates (up to 300 mm in AWJM).
6. No tool wear, no chatter, no vibration.

**Limitations:**
1. Pure WJM is restricted to soft materials.
2. High capital cost for the high-pressure system.
3. Noise level is very high (requires sound enclosures).
4. Abrasive slurry in AWJM requires disposal management.
5. Tapered cut on thick sections (divergence of jet).
6. Cannot easily machine blind pockets or 3D cavities.

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Aerospace — CFRP and titanium trimming.
2. Stone and marble — decorative cutting.
3. Automotive — composite dashboard cutting.
4. Defence — dismantling of explosive ordnance.
5. Nuclear — decontamination cutting of radioactive components.
6. Food industry — frozen food and confectionery cutting.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in WJM Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students frequently write pressure in MPa but forget to convert to Pa in the formula $v_j = \sqrt{2P/\rho}$. Always convert: **1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa**. Failure to do so will cost **2 of 7 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing WJM with AWJM:** Pure WJM cannot cut steel — the answer is *only* AWJM can. Writing "WJM cuts titanium sheets" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the accumulator:** When asked to "draw the block diagram," omitting the accumulator is a **2-mark deduction** because the examiner specifically tests whether you know its function (smoothing pressure pulsations).
> 4. **Standoff distance effect:** A common error is to write "increasing SOD increases accuracy" — this is *false*. Greater SOD *increases jet dispersion* and *decreases accuracy*.
> 5. **Bernoulli's assumptions:** When deriving $v_j$, students often forget to state assumptions (incompressible flow, frictionless, steady). The KTU answer key allocates **1 mark** for these.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Water Jet Machining**

- WJM is a **non-thermal, mechanical NTM** process using a high-velocity water jet (100–400 MPa).
- WJM alone cuts **soft, non-metallic** materials — for hard materials, **AWJM** (with garnet abrasive) is required.
- The **fundamental equation** is $v_j = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$, derived from **Bernoulli's equation**.
- Standard orifice material: **Sapphire** or **Ruby** (high wear resistance).
- **Process parameters:** Pressure, orifice diameter, standoff distance (SOD), traverse rate, abrasive flow (in AWJM).
- **Hydraulic power** = $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ = kinetic energy flux in ideal conditions.
- **MRR** for AWJM follows: $MRR \propto m_a \cdot v_j^{n}$, with $n$ between 1.5 and 2.5.
- **Kerf width** is narrow (~0.5–3 mm) → minimum material wastage.
- **Key advantage:** Zero HAZ, zero thermal distortion, environment-friendly.
- **Key limitation:** High noise, high capital cost, slurry disposal (AWJM).
- **Intensifier** is preferred over direct-drive pump for pressures above 100 MPa.
- **Applications:** Aerospace (CFRP), stone/marble, automotive composites, nuclear decontamination, food cutting.
- **ρ (water density) = 1000 kg/m³** — always state this value in numerical problems.
- Conversion factor to remember: **1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa** (commonly missed in derivations).
- **Differentiator from AJM:** WJM uses water at ultra-high pressure; AJM uses compressed air at low pressure (~0.5 MPa) with abrasive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Electron Beam Machining (EBM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Beam Machining (EBM)** is a **non-traditional, thermal-energy-based material removal process** belonging to the family of **High Energy Density (HED) beam machining** processes. In EBM, a high-velocity, focused stream of electrons is accelerated through a potential difference of **50 kV to 200 kV** and directed onto the workpiece. The **kinetic energy** of the impinging electrons is instantaneously converted into **thermal energy (heat)** upon impact, causing the workpiece material to **melt and vaporize**, thereby achieving material removal. The entire operation is conducted inside a high-vacuum chamber (typically **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr**) to prevent electron scattering and oxidation of the molten metal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cosmic Blowtorch" Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a satellite, trying to drill a hole in a metal sheet using only the Sun's energy. You use a giant magnifying glass (a **focusing coil**) to concentrate sunlight into a single, tiny, ultra-bright dot. The energy density at that dot becomes so extreme that the metal **boils away** in a flash, leaving a clean hole.

**Electron Beam Machining is the engineering version of this principle**, but instead of light photons, we use **electrons** (which are ~1800× lighter and far easier to focus with magnetic lenses) accelerated to roughly **half the speed of light**. Because electrons carry mass and charge, a magnetic field can bend them like light through a glass lens, giving us a "magnifying glass" that can focus a beam down to a spot **5 µm to 50 µm in diameter** with power densities reaching **10⁹ W/mm²** — a million times denser than a typical oxy-acetylene flame.

### 1.3 Why Vacuum? — The Engineering Necessity

| Phenomenon | Effect Without Vacuum |
|------------|----------------------|
| Air molecules | Scatter electrons, widening the beam and losing focus |
| Oxygen | Reacts with vaporized metal, contaminating the cut and forming oxides |
| Nitrogen | Absorbs electron energy, drastically reducing cutting efficiency |
| Glow discharge | Occurs at low vacuum, destroying cathode life and stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):** EBM is often confused with **Laser Beam Machining (LBM)**. The critical difference is the **energy carrier**: EBM uses a stream of charged *electrons* (requiring vacuum + electromagnetic focusing), while LBM uses a stream of neutral *photons* (no vacuum required, optical mirrors used for focusing). Both achieve comparable power densities, but EBM offers superior precision for micro-drilling.

### 1.4 Classification of EBM in the NTM Family

```
Non-Traditional Machining (NTM)
        │
        ├── Mechanical Energy Based
        │     ├── AJM, USM, WJM, AWJM
        │
        ├── Chemical / Electrochemical Based
        │     ├── ECM, EDM (partly)
        │
        └── Thermal Energy Based (HED Beam)
              ├── EBM  ← [This Module]
              ├── LBM
              ├── PAM (Plasma Arc)
              └── IBM (Ion Beam)
```

---

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight (CO1 Mapping):** Questions on EBM in KTU exams almost always begin with a "Classify EBM and justify its placement" sub-part. Remember to emphasize that it is **thermal, contact-less, and HED-based** — these three adjectives fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of EBM — Step-by-Step Working Principle

The working of EBM is best understood by tracing the journey of a single electron from generation to material removal:

### Step 1 — Electron Emission (Cathode Stage)
- A **tungsten or tantalum cathode** is heated to approximately **2500 °C** using a filament heater.
- At this temperature, electrons gain enough thermal energy to overcome the **work function** of the metal and escape the surface.
- This phenomenon is called **Thermionic Emission** (governed by **Richardson-Dushman Equation**).
- The emitted electrons form a **space charge cloud** around the cathode.

### Step 2 — Electron Acceleration (Anode Stage)
- A high **DC accelerating voltage (V)** of 50–200 kV is applied between the cathode and the anode.
- Each electron gains kinetic energy given by:
  
  $$ KE = e \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2} m_e v^2 $$

  where *e* is the electron charge and *m<sub>e</sub>* is the electron mass.
- The resulting electron velocity is roughly **0.3c to 0.7c** (where *c* is the speed of light) — i.e., relativistic.
- A **grid (Wehnelt cylinder)** controls beam current by acting as a biased electrode.

### Step 3 — Beam Focusing (Electromagnetic Lens Stage)
- A **magnetic focusing coil** acts like a convex lens for light, converging the diverging electron stream into a tight spot.
- Spot diameter achievable: **0.025 mm to 0.5 mm**.
- The beam is focused on the workpiece surface.

### Step 4 — Beam Deflection (Workpiece Movement Stage)
- **Electromagnetic deflection coils** steer the focused beam across the workpiece to cut slots, weld seams, or drill hole patterns.
- This allows complex 2D/3D contours **without physically moving the workpiece**.

### Step 5 — Material Removal (Workpiece Interaction Stage)
- Electrons striking the workpiece surface transfer all their kinetic energy in **microseconds**.
- The localized temperature rises to **10,000 °C or higher** (often exceeding the boiling point of the material).
- Material is removed primarily by **vaporization**, with secondary melting and ejection (sputtering).
- The vaporized metal is sucked out by the **vacuum pump system**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY equation you need for solving EBM numerical and analytical questions in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | $E = e \cdot V$ | Joules (J) | Energy gained per electron |
| 2 | Electron velocity | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m_e}}$ | m/s | Speed of electron after acceleration |
| 3 | Beam Power | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) | Total electrical power delivered to beam |
| 4 | Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$ | W/mm² | Energy concentration at spot |
| 5 | Energy required per unit volume of material | $E_v = \rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)$ | J/mm³ | Energy to heat + melt unit volume |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P}{\rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)}$ | mm³/s | Volume removed per second |
| 7 | Specific Energy | $SE = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Energy needed per unit volume removed |
| 8 | Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width (empirical) | $HAZ \approx 0.5 \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot t}{\alpha}}$ | mm | Extent of thermally disturbed region |
| 9 | Beam spot diameter | $D = \dfrac{d_c}{M}$ | mm | Where *M* is demagnification ratio |
| 10 | Demagnification ratio | $M = \dfrac{f_2}{f_1}$ | dimensionless | Optical analogy of magnetic lens |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $V$ = accelerating voltage, $I$ = beam current, $A$ = beam spot area, $D$ = spot diameter, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = (melting point − ambient), $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion, $\eta$ = process efficiency, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $t$ = interaction time.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

EBM is not a general-purpose shop-floor process — it occupies a **niche but critical position** in modern manufacturing:

- **Aerospace Industry:** Drilling thousands of micro-cooling holes (Ø 0.2–1.0 mm) in nickel superalloy turbine blades for jet engines. Conventional drilling is impossible at this scale.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Drilling precision fuel injector holes.
- **Electronics & Semiconductor:** Micro-via drilling in multi-layer PCBs.
- **Metallurgy Research:** Welding refractory metals (tungsten, molybdenum) and dissimilar metal combinations (steel-to-copper, ceramic-to-metal).
- **Automotive R&D:** Specialty alloy welding in prototype development.
- **Watch-making & Medical Devices:** Drilling micro-holes in surgical needles.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The largest industrial EBM chamber in commercial use is roughly 200 m³, with accelerating voltages up to 200 kV. A typical micro-hole takes **0.1 to 10 milliseconds** to drill — far faster than any conventional tool.

## 2.4 Advantages, Limitations, and Process Parameters (CO1 & CO2)

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **Power Density** | 10⁸ – 10⁹ W/mm² (highest among NTM) |
| **Beam Diameter** | 5 µm – 50 µm |
| **Cutting Width** | As small as 0.1 mm |
| **MRR** | 0.1 mm³/s to 10 mm³/s (varies with material) |
| **No Tool Wear** | "Tool" is a beam — no contact, no wear |
| **Hole Aspect Ratio** | Up to 100:1 (deep micro-holes) |
| **Major Limitation** | Vacuum + X-ray shielding → high capital cost |
| **Workpiece Size** | Limited by chamber dimensions |
| **Material Suitability** | All electrically conductive + some non-conductive (ceramics, plastics) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for EBM

This is the **most frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU exams. The derivation is shown in full, with every algebraic step explicitly explained.

### Given
- Beam power: $P$ (Watts)
- Process efficiency: $\eta$ (typically 0.6 – 0.85 for EBM)
- Workpiece density: $\rho$ (kg/m³ or g/cm³)
- Specific heat: $C_p$ (J/kg·K)
- Melting point: $T_m$, Ambient: $T_0$
- Latent heat of fusion: $L_f$ (J/kg)

### Step 1 — Energy absorbed by the workpiece per second

The beam delivers energy to the workpiece at a rate $P$. Of this, only a fraction $\eta$ is effectively used for material removal (the rest is lost as back-scattered electrons, X-rays, and re-radiation).

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P \quad \text{[Watts = J/s]}$$

### Step 2 — Energy required to process a unit volume of material

To remove 1 mm³ of material, two stages of energy input are required:

**Stage (a) — Sensible heat** to raise temperature from $T_0$ to melting point $T_m$:

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_m - T_0) = (\rho \cdot V_u) \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

where $V_u = 1 \text{ mm}^3 = 1 \text{ mm}^3$ and $\Delta T = T_m - T_0$.

**Stage (b) — Latent heat** to convert solid → liquid at $T_m$:

$$Q_{latent} = m \cdot L_f = (\rho \cdot V_u) \cdot L_f$$

**Total energy per unit volume:**

$$E_v = \rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f) \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two energies

The useful beam power must supply the volumetric energy at a rate equal to the MRR:

$$\eta \cdot P = MRR \cdot E_v$$

### Step 4 — Solve for MRR

$$\boxed{MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{\rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)} \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]}}$$

This is the **KTU gold-standard formula** for EBM MRR problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Beam Power from Voltage and Current

The relationship between the electrical input parameters and the beam output power is fundamental.

### Step 1 — Power definition
By definition, electrical power is the product of voltage and current:

$$P_{input} = V \cdot I$$

### Step 2 — Beam power (in practice, $V$ and $I$ are precisely the beam's accelerating voltage and current)

$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I$$

### Step 3 — Substituting into MRR

$$MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{\rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\eta < 1$?** Even in a high-quality EBM setup, roughly 15–40% of the beam energy is lost to: (i) back-scattered electrons from the workpiece, (ii) secondary electron emission, (iii) X-ray generation, and (iv) thermal radiation from the molten pool.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 1 mm thick mild steel plate is to be drilled using an EBM machine. Given: Accelerating voltage = 150 kV, beam current = 10 mA, process efficiency = 0.7. Properties of mild steel: $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 486 \text{ J/kg·K}$, melting point $T_m = 1500 \text{ °C}$, ambient $T_0 = 27 \text{ °C}$, $L_f = 2.72 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$. Calculate:
> (a) The beam power
> (b) The MRR
> (c) The time required to drill a through hole of diameter 0.4 mm

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Beam Power

$$P = V \cdot I = (150 \times 10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$P = 1500 \text{ W} = 1.5 \text{ kW}$$

#### Part (b) — Material Removal Rate

First, convert units carefully:

$\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cm}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/mm}^3$

$C_p \cdot \Delta T = 486 \cdot (1500 - 27) = 486 \cdot 1473 = 7.159 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$

$L_f = 2.72 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$

Total energy per kg:

$$E_{per\_kg} = C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f = 7.159 \times 10^5 + 2.72 \times 10^5 = 9.879 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$$

Energy per mm³:

$$E_v = \rho \cdot E_{per\_kg} = 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.879 \times 10^5 = 7.755 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

Now apply the MRR formula:

$$MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{E_v} = \frac{0.7 \cdot 1500}{7.755} = \frac{1050}{7.755}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 135.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

#### Part (c) — Drilling Time

Volume of the cylindrical hole:

$$V_{hole} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot t_{plate} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.4)^2 \cdot 1 = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^3$$

Time required:

$$T = \frac{V_{hole}}{MRR} = \frac{0.1257}{135.4} = 9.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 0.93 \text{ milliseconds}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This is roughly **one-thousandth of a second** — which is why EBM is genuinely described as a "flash machining" process. A human eye can barely register the event.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Power Density and its Significance

Power density is the defining metric of any HED process. The derivation links accelerating voltage, beam current, and spot diameter:

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$$

For the same power $P$, **halving** the spot diameter **quadruples** the power density. This is the geometric reason why focusing the beam is more impactful than increasing voltage.

| Process | Typical Power Density (W/mm²) |
|---------|-------------------------------|
| Oxy-fuel cutting | 10² |
| Plasma Arc | 10⁴ |
| Laser Beam | 10⁶ – 10⁸ |
| **EBM** | **10⁸ – 10⁹** |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Influence

| Parameter Increase | Effect on MRR | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Hole Quality |
|--------------------|---------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Accelerating voltage ↑ | ↑ (linear) | ↓ (deeper penetration) | Better aspect ratio |
| Beam current ↑ | ↑ (linear) | ↑ (wider) | Risk of recast layer growth |
| Pulse duration ↑ | ↑ (cumulative) | ↑↑ (heat conduction) | More spatter |
| Focusing sharpness ↑ | ↑ (concentrated) | ↓ | Narrower, cleaner hole |
| Workpiece density ↑ | ↓ (inverse) | ↓ | Less material disturbed |
| Specific heat ↑ | ↓ (inverse) | ↓ | Less efficient removal |
| Workpiece thermal conductivity ↑ | Neutral on MRR | ↑ (heat spreads) | Larger HAZ |
| Vacuum pressure ↑ (better) | ↑ | ↓ | Less oxidation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an EBM System

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Since EBM involves complex 3D vacuum chambers, electromagnetic coils, and electron paths that are difficult to render in Mermaid, the diagram below is rendered as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** showing the interconnections between the major subsystems. This is the standard approach adopted in KTU reference textbooks (Pandey & Shan, Benedict) for process schematics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU["High Voltage DC Power Supply (50-200 kV)"]
    FIL["Filament Heater (Low Voltage AC)"]
    GUN["Electron Gun Assembly (Cathode + Grid + Anode)"]
    FC["Magnetic Focusing Coil (Acts as Convex Lens)"]
    DC["Magnetic Deflection Coils (X-Y Steering)"]
    WC["Vacuum Work Chamber (10^-4 to 10^-6 torr)"]
    WP["Workpiece Table (CNC Controlled)"]
    VP["Vacuum Pumping System (Diffusion + Rotary)"]
    OBS["Optical Viewing System (Telescope + CCTV)"]
    CON["Main Control Console (PLC/CNC)"]
    XSH["X-Ray Shielding Enclosure (Lead Lined)"]
    COOL["Cooling System (Water for Coils and Walls)"]

    PSU -->|Provides accelerating V| GUN
    FIL -->|Heats cathode to 2500 C| GUN
    GUN -->|Emits focused electron beam| FC
    FC -->|Focuses beam to spot size| DC
    DC -->|Deflects beam in 2D| WC
    WC -->|Contains workpiece| WP
    VP -->|Maintains vacuum| WC
    VP -->|Maintains vacuum| GUN
    OBS -->|Real-time monitoring| WC
    CON -->|Co-ordinates all subsystems| PSU
    CON -->|Co-ordinates| VP
    CON -->|Co-ordinates| DC
    CON -->|Co-ordinates| WP
    XSH -->|Encloses entire system| WC
    XSH -->|Encloses entire system| GUN
    COOL -->|Removes heat| FC
    COOL -->|Removes heat| DC
    COOL -->|Removes heat| WC
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Material Journey in EBM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STEP 1: Thermionic Emission - Cathode heated to 2500 C"] --> B["STEP 2: Electron Acceleration - 50-200 kV potential difference"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: Beam Formation - Electrons pass through anode aperture"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: Magnetic Focusing - Beam narrowed to 5-50 um"]
    D --> E["STEP 5: Magnetic Deflection - Beam steered across workpiece"]
    E --> F["STEP 6: Kinetic to Thermal Conversion - KE converted to heat on impact"]
    F --> G["STEP 7: Material Vaporization - Local temp reaches 10,000 C"]
    G --> H["STEP 8: Vapour Extraction - Vacuum pump removes vapour"]
    H --> I["FINAL: Clean Cut Micro-Hole or Weld Formed"]
```

## 4.3 Subsystem Cross-Section Schematic (Text-Based Block Layout)

| Zone | Component | Function | Key Specification |
|------|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Filament + Cathode | Thermionic e⁻ emitter | Tungsten, 2500 °C |
| 2 | Grid (Wehnelt cylinder) | Beam current control | Biased negative w.r.t. cathode |
| 3 | Anode | Accelerates e⁻ | +50 to +200 kV |
| 4 | Focusing coil | Electromagnetic lens | 0.1–0.5 T flux density |
| 5 | Deflection coil | 2D beam steering | ±5° deflection |
| 6 | Vacuum chamber | Maintains e⁻ path | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr |
| 7 | Workpiece table | Holds and positions job | CNC 5-axis |
| 8 | Optical viewer | Process monitoring | Telescope + camera |
| 9 | X-ray shielding | Safety | 25–50 mm lead |
| 10 | Vacuum system | Sustains vacuum | Rotary + diffusion + cryo |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define EBM and classify it among non-traditional machining processes. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Electron Beam Machining (EBM) is a **non-traditional, thermal, contact-less material removal process** that uses a high-velocity, focused beam of electrons, accelerated through a potential difference of 50–200 kV, to melt and vaporize the workpiece material. It operates under high vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) to prevent electron scattering and belongs to the **High Energy Density (HED)** category of non-traditional machining processes. Classification: EBM is grouped under **thermal-energy-based NTM** (alongside LBM, PAM, IBM), distinguished by its use of charged electrons as the energy carrier and electromagnetic coils for focusing/deflection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "EBM uses light to cut metal" — this is the definition of LBM, not EBM. Always specify **electrons**, not photons. This single error can cost 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 — Why is EBM performed inside a high-vacuum chamber? State two reasons. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

EBM is performed in vacuum for two primary reasons:

1. **Prevention of electron scattering:** Air molecules, if present, would collide with the high-speed electrons in the beam, causing them to scatter and lose kinetic energy. This would widen the beam, reduce its power density, and make precise focusing impossible.

2. **Prevention of oxidation and contamination:** The vaporized metal atoms ejected from the workpiece must not react with atmospheric oxygen. Vacuum ensures the cut is clean and the molten metal does not form oxides, which would otherwise contaminate the kerf and reduce the quality of the machined surface.

(A third valid reason often expected: avoiding glow discharge that would otherwise damage the cathode.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only one reason (e.g., "to prevent oxidation") without the second one about electron scattering is considered incomplete. Always provide **at least two distinct reasons** to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

#### (a) With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the Electron Beam Machining (EBM) process. List any four process parameters that influence the MRR. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of EBM:**

The EBM process converts the **kinetic energy of accelerated electrons** into **thermal energy** at the workpiece surface, causing localized melting and vaporization. The sequence of events is:

1. **Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament cathode is heated to approximately 2500 °C by a low-voltage AC supply. At this temperature, electrons gain enough energy to escape the cathode surface via **thermionic emission**.

2. **Electron Acceleration:** A high DC voltage (50–200 kV) is applied between the cathode and anode. Each electron is accelerated, gaining kinetic energy $E = eV$. The resulting velocities are 0.3c to 0.7c.

3. **Beam Focusing:** A magnetic focusing coil concentrates the diverging electron stream into a tight spot (Ø 5–50 µm), similar to how a convex lens focuses light. This is the magnetic equivalent of a glass lens.

4. **Beam Deflection:** Electromagnetic deflection coils steer the focused beam across the workpiece in 2D, enabling cutting, drilling, or welding patterns without moving the workpiece mechanically.

5. **Material Removal:** When the high-velocity electrons strike the workpiece, their kinetic energy is converted into heat within a microsecond. Local temperatures exceed 10,000 °C, melting and vaporizing the material. Vaporized metal is removed by the vacuum system.

6. **Vacuum Environment:** The entire process occurs in a high-vacuum chamber (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr).

**Block Diagram of EBM System:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Filament Heater"] --> B["Cathode (Tungsten)"]
    B --> C["Grid (Wehnelt)"]
    C --> D["Anode (50-200 kV)"]
    D --> E["Focusing Coil"]
    E --> F["Deflection Coils"]
    F --> G["Workpiece in Vacuum"]
    G --> H["Vapour Extracted by Pump"]
```

**Four Process Parameters Influencing MRR:**

1. **Accelerating Voltage (V):** Higher voltage → higher electron velocity → higher beam power → higher MRR. Linear relationship.

2. **Beam Current (I):** Higher current → more electrons per second → more energy delivered → higher MRR. Linear relationship.

3. **Beam Spot Diameter (D):** Smaller spot → higher power density → more concentrated energy → higher MRR for the same input power. Inverse-square relationship.

4. **Pulse Duration / Dwell Time:** Longer interaction time → more energy input → higher MRR, but also increased Heat Affected Zone.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **beam current** (the current carried by the electron beam itself, in mA) with **filament current** (the current used to heat the cathode, in amperes). These are entirely different parameters. In KTU valuation, mixing them up costs full marks in numerical problems.

---

#### (b) An EBM machine operates at 100 kV accelerating voltage and 8 mA beam current to drill holes in a titanium alloy plate. The efficiency is 0.65. Given for titanium: density = 4500 kg/m³, specific heat = 520 J/kg·K, melting point = 1668 °C, ambient = 30 °C, latent heat of fusion = 4.4 × 10⁵ J/kg. Calculate: (i) Beam power, (ii) MRR, (iii) Time to drill a 0.5 mm diameter hole through a 2 mm thick plate. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 100 \text{ kV} = 100{,}000 \text{ V}$
- $I = 8 \text{ mA} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$
- $\eta = 0.65$
- $\rho = 4500 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/mm}^3$
- $C_p = 520 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- $T_m = 1668 \text{ °C}$, $T_0 = 30 \text{ °C}$
- $L_f = 4.4 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$

**Part (i) — Beam Power:**

$$P = V \cdot I = (100 \times 10^3) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{P = 800 \text{ W} = 0.8 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (ii) — Material Removal Rate:**

Step 1 — Temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = T_m - T_0 = 1668 - 30 = 1638 \text{ °C (or K)}$$

Step 2 — Sensible heat per kg:

$$Q_1 = C_p \cdot \Delta T = 520 \cdot 1638 = 8.518 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$$

Step 3 — Total energy per kg:

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + L_f = 8.518 \times 10^5 + 4.4 \times 10^5 = 1.292 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$$

Step 4 — Energy per mm³:

$$E_v = \rho \cdot Q_{total} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.292 \times 10^6 = 5.814 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

Step 5 — Apply MRR formula:

$$MRR = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{E_v} = \frac{0.65 \cdot 800}{5.814} = \frac{520}{5.814}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 89.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Part (iii) — Drilling Time:**

Volume of the through-hole:

$$V_{hole} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot t = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.5)^2 \cdot 2 = 0.3927 \text{ mm}^3$$

Time required:

$$T = \frac{V_{hole}}{MRR} = \frac{0.3927}{89.4}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 4.39 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.39 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** The most common error in this problem is forgetting to convert $\rho$ from kg/m³ to kg/mm³ (a factor of 10⁻⁹). Writing $\rho = 4500$ directly into the formula gives a wildly incorrect MRR. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly. This single oversight can cost 3–4 marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

#### (a) Compare EBM with LBM (Laser Beam Machining) on the basis of: energy source, focusing mechanism, atmosphere required, power density, and typical application. Mention two advantages and two limitations of EBM. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table: EBM vs LBM**

| Parameter | EBM | LBM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Energy Source** | Stream of accelerated electrons | Stream of photons (coherent light) |
| **Generation Mechanism** | Thermionic emission from heated cathode | Stimulated emission in optical cavity (laser) |
| **Focusing Mechanism** | Electromagnetic (magnetic) coil | Optical mirrors / lenses |
| **Atmosphere Required** | High vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) | Atmosphere, inert gas, or assist gas |
| **Power Density** | 10⁸ – 10⁹ W/mm² | 10⁶ – 10⁸ W/mm² |
| **Typical Application** | Micro-hole drilling in turbine blades, refractory welding | Sheet metal cutting, surface treatment, marking |
| **Material Restriction** | None (works on conductors and insulators) | Reflective metals (Al, Cu) are difficult |
| **Equipment Cost** | Very high (vacuum + HV) | Moderate to high |
| **X-ray Hazard** | Yes (requires lead shielding) | No |
| **Beam Deflection** | Electromagnetic (fast, precise) | Galvanometric mirrors (fast) |

**Two Advantages of EBM:**

1. **No tool wear:** Since the "tool" is an electron beam, there is zero mechanical contact with the workpiece. This eliminates tool wear, allows machining of extremely hard materials, and produces no mechanical stress or distortion.

2. **Very high precision and aspect ratio:** Beam diameters as small as 5 µm enable micro-holes with aspect ratios up to 100:1, ideal for aerospace turbine blade cooling holes — a task impossible for conventional drilling.

**Two Limitations of EBM:**

1. **High capital and operating cost:** The need for high vacuum (10⁻⁶ torr) and high-voltage (200 kV) systems, plus lead X-ray shielding, makes EBM one of the most expensive NTM processes to install and maintain.

2. **X-ray generation hazard:** When high-energy electrons strike the workpiece, **bremsstrahlung X-rays** are produced. This requires heavy lead shielding around the chamber, increases cost, and necessitates strict operator safety protocols.

> [!WARNING]
> **Comparison Tip:** In KTU, examiners expect the comparison in a **table format** with at least 5 parameters. Writing it as running paragraphs is acceptable but slower to write and easier to miss marks. Always default to a clean, aligned table.

---

#### (b) Discuss the effect of the following on EBM performance: (i) Accelerating voltage, (ii) Beam current, (iii) Vacuum level, (iv) Beam spot size. State one industrial application where EBM is the only viable machining option. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Effect of Accelerating Voltage:**

Increasing the accelerating voltage from 50 kV to 200 kV increases the kinetic energy of each electron proportionally ($E = eV$). This results in:
- Higher electron velocity (approaching relativistic speeds)
- Increased beam power ($P = VI$)
- Deeper penetration into the workpiece
- **Higher MRR** (linear relationship)
- Reduced HAZ width per unit energy
- However, **higher X-ray emission** at very high voltages (above 150 kV), increasing shielding requirements.

**(ii) Effect of Beam Current:**

Beam current determines the number of electrons striking the workpiece per second. Increasing current:
- Increases beam power linearly
- **Increases MRR linearly**
- Raises heat input → larger HAZ
- Risk of excessive melting and recast layer formation if too high
- Cathode life is reduced at very high currents

**(iii) Effect of Vacuum Level:**

Better vacuum (lower pressure) means:
- Fewer air molecules to scatter electrons
- **Sharper beam focus maintained over distance**
- Less oxidation of molten metal
- Higher achievable MRR
- Lower vacuum (< 10⁻⁵ torr) requires expensive diffusion/cryo pumps

If vacuum is compromised (< 10⁻³ torr), the beam diverges, cutting accuracy falls, and glow discharge can damage the cathode.

**(iv) Effect of Beam Spot Size:**

A smaller spot size concentrates the same power into a smaller area, dramatically increasing power density:
- $P_d = \dfrac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$
- **Halving D quadruples power density**
- Higher power density → faster vaporization, narrower kerf, smaller HAZ
- Enables micro-hole drilling (Ø < 100 µm)
- Trade-off: very small spots require precise focusing and are sensitive to vibration

**Industrial Application where EBM is the Only Viable Option:**

> **Drilling micro-cooling holes in jet engine turbine blades (Inconel or nickel superalloys):** Modern turbine blades require thousands of precisely angled micro-holes (Ø 0.2–0.5 mm) on curved, superalloy surfaces. No conventional drill bit can produce these at the required density, precision, and aspect ratio without frequent breakage. EBM drills these holes in **milliseconds** with no mechanical force on the workpiece, making it the **only economically and technically viable process** for this application.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When asked to "state one application," students often write generic answers like "cutting metals" — which is far too vague. Always specify the **industry, the material, the feature, and the reason EBM is preferred**. This level of detail is what KTU examiners reward.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This Section**

- ✅ EBM stands for **Electron Beam Machining** — a thermal, contact-less, HED-based non-traditional machining process.
- ✅ Operating voltage: **50–200 kV**, Beam current: **1–100 mA**, Vacuum: **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr**.
- ✅ Power density achievable: **10⁸ to 10⁹ W/mm²** — the highest of all NTM processes.
- ✅ Beam spot size: **5 µm to 50 µm** — finer than laser spot in most industrial setups.
- ✅ Energy conversion chain: **Electrical → Kinetic (e⁻) → Thermal → Material vaporization**.
- ✅ **Thermionic emission** at the cathode is the source of electrons.
- ✅ **Magnetic focusing coil** = electron "lens." **Deflection coil** = electron "steering wheel."
- ✅ Material removed primarily by **vaporization** (with secondary melting and sputtering).
- ✅ MRR formula: $MRR = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{\rho \cdot (C_p \cdot \Delta T + L_f)}$ [mm³/s]
- ✅ Power density: $P_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$ [W/mm²]
- ✅ Specific energy is the **inverse** of MRR per unit power — a key efficiency metric.
- ✅ **Vacuum is mandatory** to prevent electron scattering and oxidation.
- ✅ EBM produces **X-rays (bremsstrahlung)** → requires **lead shielding**.
- ✅ **No tool wear, no mechanical stress, no contact** — the three biggest practical advantages.
- ✅ **Limitation trio:** high cost, vacuum constraint, X-ray hazard.
- ✅ **Premier application:** micro-cooling holes in jet engine turbine blades (Inconel, Ni-superalloys).
- ✅ **Difference from LBM:** EBM uses electrons (charged) + magnetic focusing + vacuum; LBM uses photons (neutral) + optical focusing + any atmosphere.
- ✅ MRR is **linear in V and I** but **inversely proportional to specific energy**.
- ✅ For numericals: always convert $\rho$ to **kg/mm³** by dividing by 10⁹ (from kg/m³).
- ✅ Drilling time: $T = \dfrac{V_{hole}}{MRR}$, where $V_{hole} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot t_{plate}$.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Electric Discharge Machining (EDM)** is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which the desired shape is obtained by eroding the workpiece material through a series of controlled, rapidly recurring **electrical discharges (sparks)** between an electrode (tool) and the workpiece, both submerged in a dielectric medium. The process does not rely on mechanical contact between the tool and workpiece, making it ideal for machining materials with high hardness, complex cavities, and intricate profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (EDM):** EDM is grouped under **"Modern Machining Processes"** (also called Non-Traditional Machining). The working principle, dielectric function, process parameters (Current, Pulse-on time, Pulse-off time, Voltage, Duty cycle), MRR equation, and applications are **high-frequency board questions**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning Carving Stone"
Imagine trying to carve a deep, intricate cave into a solid block of granite using a regular metal chisel. The chisel is too soft, will break, and cannot reach the sharp internal corners. Now, instead of pushing the chisel, imagine you have a **miniature lightning bolt** that jumps from the tip of your "chisel" to the stone thousands of times per second. Each spark is so hot (around **8,000 °C – 12,000 °C**) that it instantly vaporizes a tiny speck of stone. The lightning bolt is steered along a pre-programmed path, carving the entire cave. This "lightning" is the controlled electric spark, the "chisel" is the electrode, the "stone" is the workpiece, and the "storm" is the dielectric fluid flushing away the molten debris.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Term — "Spark Erosion":** EDM is sometimes called **Spark Erosion Machining** in older texts because material is removed by localized *erosion* caused by the spark. The word "erosion" tells you the process is purely thermal — no mechanical force is applied.

---

## 1.2 Stereo-lithography (SLA) — 3D Printing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Stereo-lithography (SLA)** is a **Vat Photo-Polymerization** based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process, classified under **Material Addition Processes**, in which a three-dimensional solid object is built layer-by-layer by selectively curing a liquid photo-curable resin using a focused **Ultraviolet (UV) laser beam**. The laser traces each cross-sectional slice of the CAD model, solidifying the resin where it strikes, while un-cured resin remains liquid and is drained away post-build.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (SLA):** Stereo-lithography was the **world's first commercialized 3D printing process** (patented by Chuck Hull in 1986, founding 3D Systems). KTU examiners frequently test the *layer formation principle, UV photo-polymerization, build orientation, support structures, and STL file format*.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Magical Liquid That Hardens Under a Light Pen"
Picture a transparent tray of honey-like liquid that, on its own, stays a liquid forever. Now imagine you have a **tiny ultraviolet flashlight** that you can move around inside the tray. Wherever the UV light touches, the liquid instantly turns into solid plastic — like Superman's gaze hardening wax. To build a 3D model of, say, a chess pawn, you first slice the pawn into 100 thin horizontal "pancakes." The UV light draws the bottom pancake on the tray's surface, then the tray lowers a tiny bit, more liquid flows in, the light draws the next pancake, and so on. When all 100 pancakes are stacked and glued together, you have a complete, fully solid chess pawn. The "honey" is the photopolymer resin, the "flashlight" is the UV laser, and the "pancakes" are the cured layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Term — "Photo-polymerization":** This is the chemical reaction where liquid monomers (small molecular chains) link together into solid polymers (long chains) when exposed to UV light. *Photo* = light, *Polymerization* = making polymers. The KTU board will test this exact word.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtractive vs. Additive:** EDM is a **subtractive** process (material is *removed*). Stereo-lithography is an **additive** process (material is *added*). They sit at opposite philosophical ends of manufacturing, yet both are characterized by **no mechanical cutting force on the workpiece** — a key unifying feature of "non-traditional" and "additive" processes.

| Feature | EDM (Subtractive) | Stereo-lithography (Additive) |
|---|---|---|
| Material state at start | Solid workpiece (block) | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| Energy source | Electrical spark (thermal) | UV laser (photo-chemical) |
| Tool contact | None (gap of 0.01–0.5 mm) | None (light beam, no contact) |
| Hardness constraint | No limit (used for hardened tool steels) | Limited by resin chemistry |
| Surface finish | Very good (Ra 0.2 – 1.6 µm) | Excellent (Ra 0.01 – 0.05 µm) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) — Mechanism Breakdown

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. **Setup Stage:** The electrode (tool, often copper or graphite) and the workpiece are connected to a DC pulse generator. Both are submerged in a **dielectric fluid** (typically EDM oil, kerosene, or deionized water). A servo-controlled gap of **0.01 – 0.5 mm** is maintained between them.
2. **Ionization (Plasma Channel Formation):** When the voltage across the gap exceeds the dielectric's breakdown strength (≈ 30–200 V), the insulating fluid ionizes, forming a **plasma channel** (essentially a controlled lightning path).
3. **Discharge (Spark):** Electrons accelerate from cathode (tool, usually negative) to anode (workpiece, usually positive), creating a **spark discharge**. Temperatures in the channel reach **8,000–12,000 °C**.
4. **Material Removal:** This intense localized heat melts and vaporizes a tiny volume of the workpiece. Some molten material is also ejected by the explosive bubble-collapse of vaporized metal.
5. **Dielectric Flush:** The dielectric fluid (a) cools the gap, (b) flushes away the eroded particles ("debris" or "swarf"), and (c) restores the insulating property for the next spark.
6. **Pulse-off (De-ionization):** Current is switched off for a brief period (Pulse-off time, *t<sub>off</sub>*) to allow the dielectric to de-ionize and debris to be flushed, preventing short-circuits.
7. **Cycle Repeats:** The cycle restarts thousands of times per second, and the electrode is fed downwards (or sideways) by a servo system to maintain the gap, gradually sculpting the desired shape.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Why dielectric fluid?** It must be an *insulator* during the off-cycle (to localize the spark) and a *conductor* during the on-cycle (to allow the spark). It also cools and flushes.
- **Why pulse-off time?** Without it, the plasma channel would persist, leading to a continuous arc (a *short-circuit / arc fault*) that damages both the tool and the workpiece.
- **Why servo gap control?** As the workpiece erodes, the gap would otherwise grow, stopping the sparking. The servo continuously feeds the tool to keep the gap constant.

### Key EDM Process Parameters
- **Open Circuit Voltage (V):** 30–200 V DC.
- **Spark Current (I):** 0.5–400 A.
- **Pulse-on Time (t<sub>on</sub>):** 1–2000 µs — duration of the spark.
- **Pulse-off Time (t<sub>off</sub>):** 1–2000 µs — de-ionization interval.
- **Duty Cycle (τ):** Ratio of t<sub>on</sub> / (t<sub>on</sub> + t<sub>off</sub>).
- **Flushing Pressure / Method:** Side flushing, through-electrode flushing, or vacuum.

### Types of EDM
1. **Die-Sinking EDM (Ram EDM / Conventional EDM / Sinker EDM):** The tool is a *shaped electrode* (mirror image of the cavity). Used for dies, molds, intricate 3D cavities.
2. **Wire EDM (Wire-Cut EDM / WEDM):** A continuously moving thin wire (brass/zinc-coated brass, 0.02–0.33 mm diameter) acts as the electrode. Used for 2D profile cutting, punches, dies, very complex contours.
3. **Hole-Drilling EDM (EDM Drilling):** Uses a tubular electrode to drill very small, deep holes.
4. **Fast Hole Drilling EDM:** High-pressure dielectric flow through the electrode for fast hole drilling in turbine blades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** Die-Sinking and Wire EDM are the two most-tested types. The board often asks: *"Compare Wire EDM and Die-Sinking EDM"* or *"Explain WEDM with a neat sketch."*

### KTU Formula Sheet for EDM

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Duty Cycle (τ)** | $\tau = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | Fraction of time spark is ON. Critical parameter, must be < 1. |
| 2 | **Pulse Frequency (f)** | $f = \dfrac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | Number of spark cycles per second. |
| 3 | **MRR — Volume Basis** | $MRR = \dfrac{V_{removed}}{\Delta t}$ (mm³/min) | Direct expression; use when volume and time are given. |
| 4 | **MRR — Empirical / Energy Basis** | $MRR \propto W \cdot f$ where W = discharge energy per pulse | More energy per spark → more material removed. |
| 5 | **Discharge Energy per Pulse (W)** | $W = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ (Joules) | Energy delivered in one spark. |
| 6 | **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** | $TWR = \dfrac{V_{tool\,worn}}{V_{workpiece\,removed}} \times 100\%$ | Lower TWR is better; graphite has lower TWR than copper. |
| 7 | **Recast Layer Thickness** | Function of I and t<sub>on</sub> | Higher current / longer pulse → thicker recast (often a defect). |
| 8 | **Material Removal per Spark** | $V_{spark} \approx \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h$ | where d = crater diameter, h = crater depth. |
| 9 | **Average Current (I<sub>avg</sub>)** | $I_{avg} = I_p \cdot \tau$ | $I_p$ is peak current. |
| 10 | **Wire EDM Wire Speed (v<sub>w</sub>)** | $v_w = \dfrac{L_{wire}}{t}$ (m/min) | Controls cut quality and wear. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall (Tables):** In the markdown table above, the absolute value bars around $(t_{on} + t_{off})$ and $\frac{V_{tool\,worn}}{V_{workpiece\,removed}}$ use `\vert` (or `\dfrac{...}{...}`) to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking. **Never use `|...|` in formula tables** — the pipe is the column separator.

### Real-World Utility of EDM
- **Tool and die making:** Hardened tool-steel punches and dies.
- **Aerospace:** Machining **Inconel**, **titanium**, and **nickel super-alloys** used in turbine blades (which cannot be cut conventionally).
- **Medical implants:** Intricate micro-features in orthopedic implants.
- **Automotive:** Gear cutting dies, prototype parts.
- **Electronics:** Micro-hole drilling in printed circuit boards (PCBs), semiconductor wafer dicing.

---

## 2.2 Stereo-lithography (SLA) — Mechanism Breakdown

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. **CAD Model Creation:** A 3D model is designed in CAD software and exported as an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language / .stl) file**. The STL file approximates the surface geometry with thousands of small triangles.
2. **Slicing:** Slicing software cuts the STL model into thin horizontal layers (typical thickness: **0.05 – 0.15 mm**). Each slice becomes a 2D laser path.
3. **Build Platform Setup:** A build platform is lowered into a vat containing the liquid photopolymer resin, just below the surface. For "bat" or "top-down" configuration, the platform sits just under the resin's top surface.
4. **Layer Curing (Photo-polymerization):** A computer-controlled **UV laser** (typically a solid-state Nd:YAG at 355 nm or a He-Cd at 325 nm) scans the resin surface, tracing the cross-section. Wherever the laser hits, the monomers undergo **photo-polymerization**, turning into cross-linked solid polymer.
5. **Step Down:** Once a layer is cured, the platform moves down (or in some setups, the laser recoats and cures the next layer) by exactly one layer thickness, allowing fresh liquid resin to flow beneath.
6. **Next Layer:** The laser cures the next layer, which chemically bonds to the previous one.
7. **Repeat:** Steps 5–6 repeat for hundreds to thousands of layers.
8. **Post-Processing:** After the build, the part is lifted out, drained, washed in solvent (isopropyl alcohol) to remove uncured resin, and then **post-cured in a UV oven** for full strength. Support structures (if any) are removed.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Why a UV laser (not visible light)?** UV photons have higher energy, sufficient to break the photo-initiator's chemical bonds and trigger polymerization without generating excessive heat that could warp the part.
- **Why slice into layers?** It converts a complex 3D problem into many simple 2D drawing tasks.
- **Why support structures?** During the build, overhanging features (e.g., a human nose) have nothing beneath to support them. Supports act as temporary scaffolding, attached to the part and the build platform, and are later snapped off.
- **Why post-cure?** Initial laser exposure cures only ~60–80% of the resin. UV oven exposure completes the polymerization for full mechanical strength.

### Key SLA Process Parameters
- **Laser power (P):** 50–500 mW.
- **Scan speed (v):** 1–10 m/s.
- **Hatch spacing (h):** Distance between adjacent laser scan lines, 0.05–0.2 mm.
- **Layer thickness (t):** 0.05–0.15 mm.
- **Cure depth (C<sub>d</sub>):** Depth of resin that fully solidifies.
- **Build envelope:** Maximum build size (typical: 50–600 mm per side).
- **Resin type:** Standard, tough, flexible, castable, dental, clear, high-temperature.

### Critical SLA Physics — The "Cure Depth" Equation
The depth of polymerization follows the **Beer-Lambert Law** of light absorption:

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = cure depth (depth of solidified resin)
- $D_p$ = penetration depth of the laser into the resin (a material property)
- $E_0$ = laser energy exposure at the surface (J/cm²)
- $E_c$ = critical energy exposure needed to start polymerization (J/cm²)
- $\ln$ = natural logarithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The cure-depth equation is a *direct* consequence of the Beer-Lambert Law of light absorption in the photopolymer. The KTU board has asked this in university exams under "derive / explain the relationship between cure depth and laser exposure in SLA."

### KTU Formula Sheet for Stereo-lithography

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cure Depth (C<sub>d</sub>)** | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ | Beer-Lambert based; predicts layer cure. |
| 2 | **Critical Energy (E<sub>c</sub>)** | Material constant (J/cm²) | Minimum energy for polymerization. |
| 3 | **Penetration Depth (D<sub>p</sub>)** | Material constant (mm) | How deep UV light penetrates before fading. |
| 4 | **Working Curve / Window** | $E = E_c \cdot e^{C_d/D_p}$ | Re-arranged form; used to choose laser settings. |
| 5 | **Build Time (T<sub>build</sub>)** | $T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer}$ | $N_{layers}$ = part height / layer thickness. |
| 6 | **Number of Layers** | $N_{layers} = H / t_{layer}$ | H = total part height. |
| 7 | **Resin Volume Used (V<sub>r</sub>)** | $V_r = A \cdot H$ (plus supports) | A = part cross-section area. |
| 8 | **Theoretical Resolution (R)** | $R \approx 2 \cdot t_{layer}$ | Minimum feature size ≈ 2× layer thickness. |
| 9 | **STL File Approximation Error** | $\varepsilon \propto $ chord length of tessellation | Smaller chord → smoother surface → larger file size. |
| 10 | **Scan Velocity (v)** | $v = \dfrac{L_{scan}}{t_{scan}}$ (m/s) | L<sub>scan</sub> = total length of laser path in one layer. |

### Real-World Utility of SLA
- **Prototyping:** Concept models, visual prototypes, form-fit tests.
- **Jewelry:** Lost-wax casting patterns (resin burns out cleanly).
- **Dental:** Crowns, bridges, surgical guides, aligners.
- **Medical:** Patient-specific anatomical models, pre-surgical planning models.
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight lattice structures that are impossible to machine.
- **Optical components:** Lenses, light pipes (clear resin available).
