# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step and physical logic is shown below without abbreviation. The KTU valuation scheme penalizes skipping intermediate steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EDM — Discharge Energy per Spark and its Link to MRR

### Step 1 — State the Physical Law
A single spark acts as a transient electrical load. The energy delivered in a single spark is given by the standard electrical energy equation:

$$W = P \cdot t$$

where $P$ is the average power delivered by the spark and $t$ is the duration the spark is on. For a spark with a near-constant voltage $V$ and current $I$ during the pulse, the average power is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Power into Energy
Combining the two equations:

$$W = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$$

This is the energy (in **Joules**) delivered to the workpiece in a **single pulse**.

### Step 3 — Number of Sparks per Unit Time
If the total cycle time (pulse on + pulse off) is $T_c = t_{on} + t_{off}$, the pulse frequency (number of sparks per second) is:

$$f = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

### Step 4 — Total Energy per Unit Time (Average Power)
The average power delivered to the workpiece over many cycles is the energy per spark times the spark frequency:

$$P_{avg} = W \cdot f = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

Define the **Duty Cycle** $\tau$ as:

$$\tau = \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{avg} = V \cdot I \cdot \tau$$

### Step 5 — Empirical MRR Relationship
Empirically, MRR is directly proportional to the average power delivered (more heat → more material melted). Introducing a material-and-machine constant $K$ (units of mm³/J or mm³/min/W):

$$MRR = K \cdot P_{avg} = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \tau$$

Or, expanding the duty cycle:

$$MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation
- **Increasing I (current)** → larger, hotter spark → larger crater → more MRR, but rougher surface and more recast.
- **Increasing V (voltage)** → larger spark gap → easier flushing → more MRR, but slightly worse surface finish.
- **Increasing t<sub>on</sub>** → more energy per spark → more MRR per spark, but also more recast and heat-affected zone.
- **Increasing t<sub>off</sub>** → less frequent sparks → lower MRR but better flushing and surface finish.
- **Material constant K** depends on workpiece material (its melting point, thermal conductivity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** *"Calculate the MRR of an EDM process with V = 80 V, I = 25 A, t<sub>on</sub> = 100 µs, t<sub>off</sub> = 50 µs, and K = 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ mm³/J. Assume the constant uses the energy formulation."*
> Solution requires plugging into $MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ — a 7-mark KTU standard problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EDM — Crater Volume per Spark (Geometric)

### Step 1 — Crater Geometry Assumption
A single spark produces a small, approximately **hemispherical crater** on the workpiece. The radius of the crater is $r$ and the depth is $h$. For a hemisphere, $h \approx r$.

### Step 2 — Hemispherical Volume
The volume of a hemisphere is:

$$V_{crater} = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3$$

### Step 3 — Crater Radius and Energy Link (Empirical)
Empirical studies show the crater radius scales with the square root of the pulse energy:

$$r \propto \sqrt{W} = \sqrt{V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}}$$

Define a process constant $K_r$ (mm/J<sup>1/2</sup>) such that:

$$r = K_r \cdot \sqrt{V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into Volume
Plug $r$ into $V_{crater}$:

$$V_{crater} = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left(K_r\right)^3 \cdot \left(V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}\right)^{3/2}$$

### Step 5 — MRR from Crater Volume and Frequency
MRR = volume per spark × spark frequency:

$$MRR = V_{crater} \cdot f = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left(K_r\right)^3 \cdot \left(V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Takeaway
This derivation is **why KTU examiners ask** "How does MRR scale with current?" The answer: $MRR \propto I^{3/2}$ (for fixed V, t<sub>on</sub>), because the crater radius scales as $\sqrt{I}$ and the volume goes as the cube of the radius.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SLA — Beer-Lambert Cure Depth Relationship

### Step 1 — Start from Beer-Lambert Law
The Beer-Lambert Law for light absorption in a medium states that the **intensity of light** at a depth $z$ into the resin is:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

where $I_0$ is the intensity at the surface and $D_p$ is the **penetration depth** (the depth at which intensity drops to $1/e$ ≈ 36.8% of the surface value).

### Step 2 — Convert Intensity to Energy Exposure
Energy exposure $E$ is intensity multiplied by time. If the laser scans at velocity $v$ over a hatch spacing $h$ with power $P$, the surface exposure is:

$$E_0 = \frac{2 P}{h \cdot v} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi^{1/2}} \quad (\text{simplified; for KTU, we use } E_0 \text{ as given})$$

At depth $z$:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Curing Condition
Polymerization occurs only where the energy exposure exceeds the **critical energy** $E_c$:

$$E(z) \geq E_c$$

Substitute:

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} \geq E_c$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Maximum z (the Cure Depth)
The boundary where $E(z) = E_c$ defines the **maximum depth** of solidification, $C_d$:

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d/D_p} = E_c$$

Rearrange:

$$e^{-C_d/D_p} = \frac{E_c}{E_0}$$

Take the natural log of both sides:

$$-\frac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\frac{E_c}{E_0}\right)$$

Flip the log argument (using $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$):

$$\frac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Final Cure Depth Equation

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Physical Interpretation
- **Higher $E_0$** (more laser power or slower scan) → larger $C_d$ → thicker layer cures per pass.
- **Larger $D_p$** (clearer resin) → laser reaches deeper.
- **Higher $E_c$** (more sluggish resin) → shallower cure.
- **If $E_0 < E_c$** → no curing at all (the log is undefined for negative arguments). This is the **energy threshold** below which SLA fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 7-Mark Favored Question:** *"Derive the relationship between cure depth and laser exposure in Stereo-lithography. Explain the significance of the critical energy and penetration depth."* — The derivation above is the textbook answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: SLA — Build Time Estimation

### Step 1 — Total Layer Count
If a part has a maximum height $H$ and each layer has thickness $t_{layer}$:

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t_{layer}}$$

### Step 2 — Time per Layer
Time per layer is the total scan length divided by scan velocity, plus a small recoat time $t_{recoat}$:

$$t_{layer} = \frac{L_{scan}}{v} + t_{recoat}$$

For a part with cross-sectional area $A$ and hatch spacing $h$:

$$L_{scan} \approx \frac{A}{h}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{layer} = \frac{A}{h \cdot v} + t_{recoat}$$

### Step 3 — Total Build Time

$$T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer} = \frac{H}{t_{layer}} \cdot \left(\frac{A}{h \cdot v} + t_{recoat}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Trade-off Insight
- **Thinner layers** ($t_{layer}$ ↓) → smoother surface but **more layers** and **longer build**.
- **Faster laser scan** ($v$ ↑) → shorter time per layer but may give $E_0 < E_c$ → incomplete cure.
- **Larger hatch spacing** ($h$ ↑) → fewer scan lines → faster build but possible gaps between cures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write $N_{layers} = H \cdot t_{layer}$ (multiplication instead of division). The correct relation is division, since more layer thickness means fewer layers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Trade-off

| Aspect | EDM | Stereo-lithography (SLA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary energy** | Electrical (thermal) | Optical (photo-chemical) |
| **Material requirement** | Electrically conductive | UV-reactive photopolymer |
| **MRR / Build rate** | Moderate (mm³/min) | Slow for height, fast for thin features |
| **Surface finish** | Ra 0.2 – 1.6 µm | Ra 0.01 – 0.05 µm (best in AM) |
| **Tolerance** | ± 0.01 – 0.05 mm | ± 0.05 – 0.2 mm (depends on layer) |
| **Heat affected zone** | Yes (recast layer) | Negligible (no thermal bulk) |
| **Geometric complexity** | High (cavities, sharp corners) | Very high (internal lattices, overhangs with supports) |
| **Material waste** | Moderate (chips, sludge) | Very low (only support + waste resin) |
| **Tooling cost** | Moderate (electrode cost) | None (no physical tool) |
| **Setup cost** | Moderate | High (resin + laser machine) |
| **Best for** | Hard metals, deep cavities, dies | Prototypes, intricate polymer parts, dental/jewelry |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Constraint:** Per the protocol, Mermaid is used to build a clean, syntactically safe block-level architecture flow showing the functional interactions of the EDM and SLA systems (since their physical schematics require careful label handling).

## 4.1 EDM System — Functional Block Architecture (Die-Sinking)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Pulse Power Supply] -->|Voltage and Current pulses| B[Servo Controller]
    B -->|Maintains spark gap| C[Tool Electrode - Cathode]
    D[Workpiece - Anode] -->|Connected to power supply| A
    C -->|Spark in gap 0.01-0.5 mm| E[Plasma Channel in Dielectric]
    E -->|Localized heat 8000-12000 deg C| F[Material Removal from Workpiece]
    F -->|Eroded particles| G[Dielectric Flushing System]
    G -->|Cleaned fluid| H[Dielectric Reservoir and Filter]
    H -->|Recirculated clean dielectric| E
    D -->|Workpiece lowered or fixed| I[Servo Z-axis Slide]
    I -->|Closed loop gap control| B
    F -->|Finished cavity| J[EDMed Component]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The DC Pulse Power Supply delivers the controlled voltage and current pulses.
- The Servo Controller continuously senses the gap voltage and feeds the Tool Electrode down to keep the gap constant.
- The spark jumps from the Tool Electrode to the Workpiece through the Dielectric Fluid.
- The Dielectric Flushing System pumps clean fluid from the Reservoir, flushes out the eroded debris, filters it, and recirculates clean dielectric back to the machining zone.

## 4.2 EDM System — Wire EDM Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wire Spool - Brass 0.25 mm] -->|Continuously fed| B[Wire Guides - Diamond or Ceramic]
    B -->|Tensioned wire travels at 2-10 m/s| C[Upper and Lower Wire Guides]
    C -->|Vertical wire path| D[Machining Zone - Submerged in Dielectric Water]
    D -->|Spark between wire and workpiece| E[Workpiece - XY Table]
    E -->|CNC controlled path| F[Finished 2D Profile]
    G[Deionized Water Flushing System] -->|High pressure jets| D
    D -->|Used water| G
    H[Pulse Generator] -->|Voltage and current pulses| D
    I[Wire Cut-off and Recycle] -->|Spent wire| A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The wire is continuously unspooled, used once, and recycled (unlike die-sinking, where the same electrode is used repeatedly).
- The **XY table** moves the workpiece in 2D according to the CNC program to cut the desired 2D profile.
- Water (instead of oil) is typically used in Wire EDM to allow higher flushing pressures.

## 4.3 Stereo-lithography (SLA) — Functional Block Architecture (Top-Down / Bat Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model - 3D Geometry] -->|Exported as STL file| B[Slicing Software]
    B -->|Generates 2D layer cross sections| C[Scan Path Generator - G-code]
    C -->|Hatch spacing and laser trajectory| D[Galvanometer Mirror Scanner]
    D -->|Steered UV laser beam 355 nm| E[Resin Vat - Liquid Photopolymer]
    F[Build Platform - Initially at top of vat] -->|Stepped down by layer thickness after each cure| E
    E -->|Photo-polymerization wherever laser strikes| G[Cured Solid Layer]
    G -->|Repeated layer by layer| H[3D Solid Part - Floating in vat]
    H -->|Build complete| I[Post-Processing Station]
    I -->|Wash in IPA solvent| J[Removal of Uncured Resin]
    J -->|Final UV oven cure| K[Post-Cured Finished SLA Part]
    L[Support Structure Generator] -->|Scaffolding for overhangs| F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The CAD model is sliced into 2D cross-sections, which become laser scan paths.
- The **Galvanometer Mirrors** (very fast, accurate, rotating mirrors) steer the laser beam to trace the cross-section at high speed (m/s).
- The Build Platform steps down after each layer; in the **"bat" or top-down** configuration shown, the platform starts near the top of the vat and descends.
- Support structures are generated automatically for overhangs and are removed in post-processing.

## 4.4 SLA — Laser Curing Sequence (Per Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Layer] --> B[Build Platform Positioned at Layer Height]
    B --> C[Recoater Blade Sweeps Fresh Resin Across Surface]
    C --> D[Galvanometer Mirrors Direct UV Laser on Resin]
    D --> E[Resin Energy Exposure E0 Above Critical Energy Ec]
    E --> F[Photo-polymerization Reaction Starts]
    F --> G[Resin Cures to Depth Cd Equals Dp times ln E0 over Ec]
    G --> H{Has Entire Cross Section Been Scanned?}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I[Layer Cured and Bonded to Previous Layer]
    I --> J{All Layers Done?}
    J -->|No| K[Step Build Platform Down by One Layer Thickness]
    K --> A
    J -->|Yes| L[Build Complete - Proceed to Post Processing]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- After each layer, the recoater blade (or sometimes gravity in the "bat" config) spreads a fresh, smooth layer of resin.
- The laser scans only the cross-section; the **hatch spacing** ensures adjacent scan lines overlap slightly to ensure complete cure.
- The cure depth $C_d$ formula dictates whether the layer is properly bonded to the previous one (typically $C_d$ must be **1.5–2× the layer thickness** to ensure good inter-layer adhesion).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define EDM. State any two advantages of EDM over conventional machining.**

**Model Answer:**
Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) is a non-traditional, thermo-electric material removal process in which material is eroded from the workpiece by a series of rapidly recurring electrical discharges (sparks) between an electrode (tool) and the workpiece, both immersed in a dielectric fluid.

**Two advantages over conventional machining:**
1. **No mechanical cutting force** — there is no contact between the tool and the workpiece, making it possible to machine **very thin, delicate, and fragile** parts without distortion.
2. **No hardness limit** — EDM can machine materials irrespective of their hardness, including **hardened tool steels, tungsten carbide, Inconel, and titanium**, which are difficult or impossible to cut conventionally.
*(Other valid advantages: complex 3D cavities in one setup; very fine surface finish; low tool wear ratio with graphite electrodes.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is photo-polymerization? How is it used in Stereo-lithography?**

**Model Answer:**
**Photo-polymerization** is a chemical reaction in which liquid monomer molecules (small, reactive chains) link together to form long, cross-linked solid polymer chains when exposed to light of an appropriate wavelength (typically **UV**), in the presence of a **photo-initiator** that absorbs the light and triggers the reaction.

**Use in Stereo-lithography:**
- The vat of the SLA machine is filled with **liquid photopolymer resin** containing monomers and a photo-initiator.
- A focused **UV laser beam** is scanned across the resin surface, tracing the cross-section of the part.
- Wherever the UV laser strikes, the resin undergoes photo-polymerization and **hardens into a solid layer** bonded to the previous layer.
- By repeating this for every slice, a complete 3D solid part is built.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Electric Discharge Machining (EDM) with a neat block-level functional schematic. Discuss the role of the dielectric fluid and state the **four main functions** it performs. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the relationship between MRR and the EDM process parameters (V, I, t<sub>on</sub>, t<sub>off</sub>). A die-sinking EDM operation is performed with V = 100 V, I = 30 A, t<sub>on</sub> = 200 µs, t<sub>off</sub> = 100 µs. If the empirical constant K = 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ mm³/J, calculate the MRR. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle of EDM (7 marks)**

EDM removes material by **controlled electrical sparking** rather than mechanical cutting. The process works as follows:
1. The **tool (electrode)** and the **workpiece** are connected to a **DC pulse generator**, with the tool as the cathode and the workpiece as the anode.
2. Both are immersed in a **dielectric fluid** and separated by a small **spark gap (0.01 – 0.5 mm)**.
3. When the voltage across the gap exceeds the **breakdown voltage** of the dielectric, the fluid ionizes to form a **plasma channel**.
4. A short, high-temperature **spark discharge** (8,000 – 12,000 °C) jumps across the channel, melting and vaporizing a small volume of the workpiece.
5. The current is switched off (pulse-off time) to allow the dielectric to **de-ionize and flush** away the eroded debris.
6. The cycle repeats thousands of times per second; the tool is **servo-fed** to maintain the gap and gradually reproduce the tool's inverse shape in the workpiece.

**The four functions of the dielectric fluid:**
1. **Electrical insulation** — keeps the gap non-conductive during the pulse-off period so that sparks occur only when the voltage is applied.
2. **Coolant** — carries away the heat from the plasma channel and the workpiece surface, preventing thermal damage.
3. **Flushing medium** — sweeps the eroded metal particles (debris) out of the gap, preventing short circuits.
4. **Ionization medium** — provides ions for the plasma channel to form during the pulse-on period.

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Energy per spark:
$$W = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$$
Spark frequency:
$$f = \frac{1}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$
Average power delivered:
$$P_{avg} = V \cdot I \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$
MRR (empirical):
$$MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**
Given: V = 100 V, I = 30 A, t<sub>on</sub> = 200 µs = 200 × 10⁻⁶ s, t<sub>off</sub> = 100 µs = 100 × 10⁻⁶ s, K = 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ mm³/J.

Calculate duty cycle:
$$\tau = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{200}{300} = \frac{2}{3}$$

Energy per spark:
$$W = 100 \times 30 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 0.6 \text{ J}$$

Spark frequency:
$$f = \frac{1}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 3333.33 \text{ Hz}$$

Average power:
$$P_{avg} = 0.6 \times 3333.33 = 2000 \text{ W}$$

MRR:
$$MRR = K \cdot P_{avg} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000 = 0.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to mm³/min:
$$MRR = 0.4 \times 60 = 24 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — EDM Numerical:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert µs to seconds. Always write t<sub>on</sub> = 200 × 10⁻⁶ s, not just 200.
> 2. **Forgetting duty cycle:** Some students compute $W \cdot f$ directly without recognizing that $W \cdot f$ already includes duty cycle; do not multiply by $\tau$ again.
> 3. **Final unit:** Present MRR in both **mm³/s** and **mm³/min** — the KTU answer script format expects the final answer in mm³/min for the 7-mark problem.
> 4. **Sign of current:** Use the magnitude only; MRR is always positive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a block-level functional schematic, explain the working of **Stereo-lithography (SLA)**. List any **four advantages** and **two limitations** of the process. (7 marks)

**(b)** A photopolymer resin has a penetration depth **D<sub>p</sub> = 0.20 mm** and a critical energy **E<sub>c</sub> = 10 mJ/cm²**. The SLA laser delivers a surface exposure **E<sub>0</sub> = 80 mJ/cm²**. Calculate the **cure depth C<sub>d</sub>**. If a part has a height of 50 mm and is built with a layer thickness of 0.1 mm, determine the **total number of layers** and **the build time**, assuming each layer takes 25 seconds. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of SLA (4 marks) + Advantages/Limitations (3 marks)**

**Working Principle (Block Schematic — see Section 4.3):**
1. The **3D CAD model** is sliced into thin horizontal layers (typical layer thickness: 0.05 – 0.15 mm).
2. A vat is filled with **liquid photopolymer resin**.
3. A **UV laser** (Nd:YAG at 355 nm) is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** to trace the cross-section of each layer on the resin surface.
4. Wherever the laser hits, the resin **photo-polymerizes** and hardens.
5. The **build platform** moves down by one layer thickness after each cure, fresh resin flows in, and the next layer is cured.
6. The process repeats for all layers; the part is removed, washed, and post-cured in a UV oven.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Excellent surface finish** (Ra 0.01 – 0.05 µm) — the best among all 3D printing processes.
2. **High dimensional accuracy** and fine feature resolution.
3. **No tooling required** — direct conversion of CAD to part.
4. **Wide range of resins** — clear, tough, flexible, castable, dental-grade, high-temperature.

**Two limitations:**
1. **Limited to photopolymers** — cannot directly print metals or ceramics (only via casting routes).
2. **Post-processing is mandatory** — the part must be washed in solvent and post-cured in UV light.
*(Other valid limitations: support structures are required; resin can be toxic; build envelope is limited; slow for large parts.)*

---

**(b) Numerical — Cure Depth and Build Time (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Cure Depth (2 marks)**

Given: D<sub>p</sub> = 0.20 mm, E<sub>0</sub> = 80 mJ/cm², E<sub>c</sub> = 10 mJ/cm².

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

$$C_d = 0.20 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{80}{10}\right) = 0.20 \times \ln(8)$$

$$\ln(8) = 2.0794$$

$$C_d = 0.20 \times 2.0794 = 0.4159 \text{ mm}$$

**Result: C<sub>d</sub> ≈ 0.416 mm** (so the laser cures about 0.42 mm deep, allowing a layer thickness of 0.1 mm with good bonding margin).

---

**Step 2: Number of Layers (2 marks)**

Given: H = 50 mm, t<sub>layer</sub> = 0.1 mm.

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t_{layer}} = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500 \text{ layers}$$

---

**Step 3: Build Time (3 marks)**

Given: time per layer = 25 s.

$$T_{build} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer,time} = 500 \times 25 = 12{,}500 \text{ seconds}$$

Convert to hours:
$$T_{build} = \frac{12{,}500}{3600} \approx 3.47 \text{ hours}$$

**Result: T<sub>build</sub> ≈ 12,500 s ≈ 3.47 hours.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — SLA Numerical:**
> 1. **Forgetting the natural log:** Many students compute $C_d = D_p \times (E_0 - E_c) / E_0$ or similar wrong forms. **Always** use the Beer-Lambert-derived log formula.
> 2. **Unit check on energy:** $E_0$ and $E_c$ must be in the **same units** (both mJ/cm² or both J/cm²) before taking the ratio. The log is unitless.
> 3. **Layer count:** Some students compute $N = H \times t_{layer}$ instead of division — this is a 1-mark loss in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Time conversion:** The final answer must specify **seconds AND hours (or minutes)** — KTU 7-mark numericals require explicit time-unit conversion.
> 5. **Verifying cure depth vs. layer thickness:** Always state that $C_d \approx 0.42 \text{ mm}$ is about **4× the layer thickness**, ensuring adequate inter-layer bonding — a key conceptual point examiners look for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Cram — Both Topics:**

### EDM Rapid Recap
- ✅ EDM is a **thermo-electric** process (heat from sparks does the cutting — no mechanical force).
- ✅ Spark gap: **0.01 – 0.5 mm**; Spark temperature: **8,000 – 12,000 °C**.
- ✅ Dielectric fluid performs **four** functions: insulation, cooling, flushing, ionization.
- ✅ **Duty cycle** $\tau = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ — must be **less than 1**.
- ✅ **MRR equation:** $MRR = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \tau = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$.
- ✅ **Energy per spark:** $W = V \cdot I \cdot t_{on}$ (Joules).
- ✅ **Empirical MRR scaling:** $MRR \propto I^{3/2}$ (from crater geometry).
- ✅ **Tool Wear Ratio (TWR)** = (volume of tool worn / volume of workpiece removed) × 100%.
- ✅ **Two main types:** Die-sinking EDM (shaped electrode for cavities) and **Wire EDM** (continuous wire for 2D profiles).
- ✅ **Recast layer** = thin re-solidified metal layer on the EDM'd surface; thicker at higher I and t<sub>on</sub>.
- ✅ **EDM can machine any electrically conductive material**, regardless of hardness.
- ✅ **Graphite electrodes** have lower wear than copper but are brittle; **copper** is ductile and good for finishing.
- ✅ Wire EDM uses a **brass wire** (typically 0.25 mm) running continuously and is used in **die-making, aerospace, electronics**.

### SLA Rapid Recap
- ✅ SLA = **Vat Photo-Polymerization**, a **Material Addition (Additive Manufacturing)** process.
- ✅ Energy source = **UV laser** (e.g., Nd:YAG at 355 nm).
- ✅ Photo-polymerization = monomers → solid polymer under UV light.
- ✅ **Cure depth:** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ (Beer-Lambert Law).
- ✅ $D_p$ = penetration depth (resin property), $E_c$ = critical energy (resin property), $E_0$ = surface exposure (laser setting).
- ✅ **Cure condition:** $E_0 \geq E_c$, else no curing.
- ✅ Layer thickness is typically **0.05 – 0.15 mm**; $C_d$ should be **1.5 – 2× t<sub>layer</sub>** for good bonding.
- ✅ **Number of layers:** $N_{layers} = H / t_{layer}$ (always divide, never multiply).
- ✅ **STL file format** — triangulated surface approximation of the 3D CAD model.
- ✅ **Support structures** are required for overhangs > 45° and are removed in post-processing.
- ✅ **Post-processing:** wash in solvent (IPA) → final UV oven cure for full strength.
- ✅ **SLA is best for** smooth, intricate polymer prototypes, dental models, jewelry patterns, optical components.
- ✅ **Limitation:** only photopolymers; cannot directly print metals/ceramics.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Chuck Hull (1986), 3D Systems — first commercial 3D printing process.
- ✅ **SLA vs. SLS:** SLA uses a **laser to cure liquid resin**; SLS uses a **laser to sinter powder** — do not confuse these in the exam.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Caution:**
> 1. **EDM is SUBTRACTIVE — material is REMOVED.**
> 2. **SLA is ADDITIVE — material is ADDED layer by layer.**
> 3. EDM requires an **electrically conductive** workpiece; SLA does not.
> 4. EDM is **no contact**; SLA is also **no contact** (laser only).
> 5. **MRR in EDM is in mm³/min; build rate in SLA is in mm/hour (or mm³/hour) — units are different!**
> 6. Always end numerical answers with a **one-line interpretation sentence** (e.g., "Therefore, the cure depth is 0.416 mm, which is approximately 4× the chosen layer thickness, ensuring good inter-layer bonding.") — this often fetches the **grace mark** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Laser Sintering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Sintering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process in which a high-power laser selectively scans and sinters (heats to a temperature just below the melting point, causing particle fusion) a thin layer of powdered material—typically thermoplastic polymers, metals, or ceramics—based on a sliced 2D cross-section obtained from a 3D CAD model. After each cross-section is sintered, a fresh layer of powder is deposited, and the process is repeated layer-by-layer until the complete three-dimensional part is fabricated.

SLS belongs to the **ISO/ASTM 52900** category of **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** processes, specifically classified as a *thermal fusion* technique because it uses a directed energy source (laser) to fuse material without the need for support structures in most cases.

### Key Distinguishing Features:
- **No support structures required** (unsintered powder acts as self-support)
- **High material utilization** (unused powder can often be recycled)
- **Wide material range** (nylon, TPU, polystyrene, metals like stainless steel, titanium)
- **Operating in an inert atmosphere** (typically nitrogen for polymers, argon for metals) to prevent oxidation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Layered Sand Art of a Beach":**
> Imagine you are at the beach building a sandcastle. Instead of sculpting the entire 3D shape at once, you pour a thin, even layer of damp sand across a flat surface. Then, using a focused heat lamp (representing the laser), you selectively dry and harden only the outline of your desired shape on that layer. The untouched sand remains loose. You then pour another thin layer of damp sand on top, and the heat lamp hardens the next cross-section. Crucially, the loose sand below supports the new layer as you build upward—just like how unsintered powder in SLS naturally supports overhangs and complex geometries.

The key takeaway: SLS is essentially **3D printing with heat and powder**, where each layer is "glued" to the previous one through thermal fusion, not melting.

### Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards):
- **Typical Layer Thickness:** **0.1 mm to 0.15 mm** for polymers
- **Build Volume:** Up to **550 × 550 × 750 mm³** (industrial systems)
- **Laser Type:** **CO₂ laser (10.6 µm wavelength)** for polymers, **Fiber laser (1.06 µm)** for metals
- **Build Chamber Temperature:** Just below melting point (e.g., **170 °C** for PA12)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The SLS process follows a strict, cyclic, four-step operational sequence. Understanding each step is critical for KTU examination answers.

### Step 1: CAD Model Preparation
- A 3D CAD model is created in software (e.g., SolidWorks, CATIA).
- The model is exported as an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** file.
- The STL file is **sliced** into thin 2D cross-sections (typically 0.1 mm thick) using slicer software.

### Step 2: Build Chamber Initialization
- The **build chamber** is pre-heated to a temperature just below the material's melting point.
- This pre-heating minimizes thermal gradients and reduces the energy required from the laser.
- An **inert gas** (N₂ for polymers, Ar for metals) is purged to eliminate oxygen and prevent oxidation/degradation.

### Step 3: Powder Deposition
- A **recoater blade or roller** spreads a thin, uniform layer of powder from the feed chamber onto the build platform.
- The layer thickness is precisely controlled (typically 0.1–0.15 mm).

### Step 4: Laser Sintering (Core Step)
- The **laser beam**, directed by a **galvanometer scanner** and focused through **F-theta lenses**, selectively scans the powder bed.
- The laser raises the local temperature to the **sintering point** (not the melting point), causing the powder particles to fuse through surface diffusion and neck growth.
- After one cross-section is sintered, the build platform **descends by one layer thickness**.

### Step 5: Repetition
- Steps 3 and 4 are repeated for each layer until the full 3D part is complete.
- The part is allowed to cool gradually inside the chamber to avoid **thermal warping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is SLS called "Sintering" and not "Melting"?**
> Sintering heats the material to approximately **80–90% of its melting point**, causing particles to bond at their contact points (neck formation) without full liquefaction. This is why SLS parts retain a slightly porous microstructure and exhibit different mechanical properties compared to injection-molded parts.

## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymers) | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power** | $P$ | **5 – 50 W** | Higher power → denser parts, risk of degradation |
| **Scan Speed** | $v$ | **1000 – 5000 mm/s** | Higher speed → less energy input |
| **Hatch Spacing** | $h$ | **0.2 – 0.5 mm** | Distance between adjacent scan lines |
| **Layer Thickness** | $t$ | **0.1 – 0.15 mm** | Thinner layers → better resolution |
| **Bed Temperature** | $T_b$ | **Just below $T_m$** | Higher $T_b$ → less thermal stress |
| **Scan Pattern** | — | **Cross-hatch, spiral, contour** | Affects anisotropy and surface finish |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important equation in SLS** is the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** formula, which determines the quality, density, and mechanical strength of the sintered part.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $\text{J/mm}^3$ |
| 2 | $T_{sint} = (0.8 \text{ to } 0.9) \cdot T_m$ | Sintering temperature range | °C or K |
| 3 | $R_{build} = \dfrac{Z_{max}}{t_{layer}}$ | Total number of layers required | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_{layer} = P \cdot t_{scan}$ | Energy delivered per layer | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $t_{scan} = \dfrac{L_{scan}}{v}$ | Time to scan one hatch line | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot m \cdot c}{h_c \cdot A}$ | Approximate cooling time (Newton's law) | seconds (s) |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Laser Power (W = J/s)
- $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm)
- $T_m$ = Melting temperature
- $L_{scan}$ = Total scan length per layer (mm)
- $m$ = mass, $c$ = specific heat, $h_c$ = heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = surface area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** If **VED is too low** → part will be porous, weak, and powdery. If **VED is too high** → part will suffer from degradation, warping, and surface defects. The sweet spot is typically **0.05 – 0.20 J/mm³** for PA12.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SLS is extensively used in:
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight lattice structures and complex ducting
- **Automotive:** Rapid prototyping of interior and functional components
- **Medical:** Customized orthotics, prosthetics, and anatomical models
- **Consumer Goods:** Custom footwear, eyewear, and fashion accessories
- **Foundry Patterns:** Investment casting patterns (since SLS patterns can be investment-cast in metal)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) Formula

The VED formula is the cornerstone of SLS process control. It represents the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder.

### Step 1: Define Laser Power
The laser delivers energy at a rate defined by its power:
$$P = \frac{dE}{dt} \quad \text{[Watts = J/s]}$$

The total energy delivered over a time interval $\Delta t$ is:
$$E_{delivered} = P \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Time to Scan One Hatch Line
A single hatch line has a length $L$ and is scanned at speed $v$:
$$\Delta t = \frac{L}{v}$$

Therefore, the energy delivered to one hatch line is:
$$E_{hatch} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Volume Sintered by One Hatch Line
The volume of material sintered by one hatch line depends on the hatch spacing $h$ (width of the sintered track) and the layer thickness $t$ (depth of the sintered track):
$$V_{hatch} = L \cdot h \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Formulate the Energy Density
The energy density is the energy delivered per unit volume:
$$E = \frac{E_{hatch}}{V_{hatch}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:
$$E = \frac{P \cdot \frac{L}{v}}{L \cdot h \cdot t}$$

### Step 5: Simplification
The length $L$ cancels out from numerator and denominator:
$$\boxed{E = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

This result is dimensionally consistent:
- Numerator: $\text{W} = \text{J/s}$
- Denominator: $(\text{mm/s}) \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm} = \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$
- Result: $\dfrac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \text{J/mm}^3$ ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU Numerical Problem (Model Solution):**
> A Selective Laser Sintering machine uses a **CO₂ laser of 30 W power** to sinter **PA12 powder**. The process parameters are: **scan speed = 2500 mm/s**, **hatch spacing = 0.3 mm**, and **layer thickness = 0.12 mm**. Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density (VED).

### Given Data:
$$P = 30 \text{ W}, \quad v = 2500 \text{ mm/s}, \quad h = 0.3 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.12 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 1: Write the VED Formula
$$E = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values
$$E = \frac{30}{2500 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 0.12}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Denominator
$$2500 \cdot 0.3 = 750$$
$$750 \cdot 0.12 = 90 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 4: Final Calculation
$$E = \frac{30}{90} = 0.3333 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation
$$\boxed{E \approx 0.33 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Note:** The calculated VED of **0.33 J/mm³** is **slightly above** the recommended range for PA12 (0.05–0.20 J/mm³). This indicates the part may suffer from **thermal degradation, excessive warping, or surface roughness**. To optimize, the student should recommend either:
> - Increasing the scan speed to $v \approx 4166 \text{ mm/s}$, OR
> - Decreasing the laser power to $P \approx 18 \text{ W}$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SLS vs. Other AM Processes

| Feature | **SLS** | **SLA** | **FDM** | **DMLS/SLM** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Material State** | Powder (solid) | Liquid resin | Filament (solid) | Metal powder |
| **Energy Source** | Laser (thermal) | UV Laser (photopolymer) | Heated nozzle | Laser (full melt) |
| **Support Required** | **No** (powder self-supports) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** (often) |
| **Material Type** | Thermoplastics, metals, ceramics | Photopolymers | Thermoplastics | Metals only |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (grainy) | Excellent | Moderate (layer lines) | Good |
| **Mechanical Strength** | High (near-isotropic) | Brittle | Anisotropic | Very high (fully dense) |
| **Post-Processing** | Depowdering, bead blasting | Washing + UV cure | Support removal | HIP, machining |
| **Cost Level** | High | Moderate | **Low** | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SLS Process Flow — Block Architecture Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional architecture of an SLS system, including all critical sub-systems, their interactions, and the flow of energy and material.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model Design] --> B[STL File Export]
    B --> C[Slicer Software]
    C --> D[2D Cross Section Layers]
    D --> E[Build Chamber Pre Heating]
    E --> F[Inert Gas Purge N2 or Ar]
    F --> G[Recoater Blade Deposits Powder Layer]
    G --> H[Galvanometer Scanner Directs Laser Beam]
    H --> I[Laser Sinters Powder Cross Section]
    I --> J{Is Layer Complete?}
    J -->|No| H
    J -->|Yes| K[Build Platform Descends One Layer]
    K --> L{All Layers Complete?}
    L -->|No| G
    L -->|Yes| M[Cooling Phase Inside Chamber]
    M --> N[Depowdering and Part Extraction]
    N --> O[Post Processing Bead Blasting or Infiltration]
    O --> P[Finished 3D Part]

    subgraph EnergySystem[Energy Delivery Subsystem]
        Q[CO2 Laser Source 10.6 micrometer] --> R[Beam Expander]
        R --> S[Galvanometer XY Scanner]
        S --> T[F Theta Focusing Lens]
        T --> H
    end

    subgraph ThermalSystem[Thermal Management Subsystem]
        U[Heated Build Platform] --> E
        V[Chamber Heaters] --> E
        W[Closed Loop PID Controller] --> U
        W --> V
    end

    subgraph MaterialSystem[Material Handling Subsystem]
        X[Feed Chamber Powder Reservoir] --> G
        Y[Recoater Roller or Blade] --> G
        Z[Overflow Collection Bin] --> K
    end
```

## 4.2 Description of the Architecture

The diagram is divided into **four functional subsystems**:

### Subsystem 1: Digital Front-End
- The **CAD model** is converted to an **STL file** and sliced into 2D layers. This subsystem handles the digital input pipeline.

### Subsystem 2: Energy Delivery Subsystem
- A **CO₂ laser** (or fiber laser for metals) generates the beam.
- The **beam expander** increases the beam diameter to fill the scanner aperture.
- The **galvanometer scanner** (a pair of rapidly rotating mirrors) deflects the beam in X and Y.
- The **F-theta lens** focuses the beam onto a flat plane, ensuring uniform spot size across the build area.

### Subsystem 3: Thermal Management Subsystem
- A **closed-loop PID controller** maintains the build platform and chamber at a uniform temperature just below the material's melting point.
- This is **critical** to prevent curling, warping, and delamination between layers.

### Subsystem 4: Material Handling Subsystem
- The **feed chamber** stores fresh powder.
- The **recoater** (roller or blade) deposits a precise layer.
- The **overflow bin** collects excess powder, which is typically recycled for future builds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Selective Laser Sintering. Mention any two of its advantages over FDM. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) based additive manufacturing process in which a laser selectively sinters powdered material (polymers, metals, or ceramics) layer-by-layer, based on the sliced cross-section of a 3D CAD model, without the need for dedicated support structures.

**Two Advantages over FDM (1 Mark — ½ Mark each):**
1. **No support structures required:** The unsintered powder bed itself acts as a self-supporting medium, allowing fabrication of complex internal geometries and overhangs.
2. **Higher mechanical strength:** SLS produces near-isotropic parts with better mechanical properties compared to the highly anisotropic FDM parts, which suffer from weak inter-layer adhesion along the Z-axis.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the recoater blade in the SLS process? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **recoater blade (or roller)** plays a **critical role in powder deposition**. Its functions are:
1. **Layer Formation:** It spreads a fresh, thin, and uniform layer of powder (typically 0.1–0.15 mm thick) from the feed chamber onto the build platform. (1 Mark)
2. **Surface Flatness:** It ensures the powder surface is flat and level to receive the laser scan, which is essential for uniform energy absorption. (1 Mark)
3. **Powder Recycling:** It pushes excess powder into the overflow collection bin for reuse, improving material economy. (1 Mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse the **sintering temperature** with the **melting temperature**. Examiners award **½ mark deduction** for this error.
> - Always specify the **units** of energy density ($\text{J/mm}^3$). An answer without units loses **1 mark**.
> - When drawing the process flow, **label every block clearly**. Unlabeled blocks receive **zero credit** even if the flow is correct.
> - In numerical problems, **show the substitution step explicitly**. Skipping the substitution directly to the answer loses **1 mark** under the "Step-by-Step Marking Scheme".

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive SLS Theory and Calculation

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the working principle of the Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of pre-heating of the build chamber.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** An SLS machine uses a laser of **20 W** power to sinter **PA12 nylon powder**. The scan speed is **2000 mm/s**, the hatch spacing is **0.25 mm**, and the layer thickness is **0.1 mm**. Calculate the volumetric energy density and comment on the part quality.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle of SLS (5 Marks):**
The Selective Laser Sintering process is a powder bed fusion additive manufacturing technique that fabricates 3D parts layer-by-layer. The working principle involves the following steps:

1. **Model Preparation:** A 3D CAD model is created and converted to an STL file, which is then sliced into thin 2D cross-sections (typically 0.1 mm thick) using slicer software. (1 Mark)

2. **Chamber Preparation:** The build chamber is pre-heated to a temperature just below the melting point of the powder (e.g., 170 °C for PA12). An inert gas (N₂ for polymers) is purged to prevent oxidation. (1 Mark)

3. **Powder Deposition:** A recoater blade spreads a thin, uniform layer of powder from the feed chamber onto the build platform. (½ Mark)

4. **Laser Sintering:** A CO₂ laser, directed by a galvanometer scanner, selectively scans the powder layer based on the 2D cross-section. The laser energy sinters (fuses) the powder particles at their contact points through thermal diffusion, without fully melting them. (1 Mark)

5. **Platform Lowering:** After one layer is sintered, the build platform descends by one layer thickness, and a fresh layer of powder is deposited on top. (½ Mark)

6. **Repetition:** Steps 3 to 5 are repeated until the full 3D part is completed. (½ Mark)

7. **Cooling and Extraction:** The part is allowed to cool gradually inside the chamber, then extracted and subjected to post-processing (depowdering, bead blasting). (½ Mark)

**Role of Pre-heating (2 Marks):**
- **Reduces thermal gradients:** Pre-heating minimizes the temperature difference between the chamber and the powder, reducing internal stresses and preventing part warping. (1 Mark)
- **Lowers required laser energy:** Since the powder is already near its sintering temperature, the laser needs to supply only the additional energy required for fusion, improving energy efficiency. (1 Mark)

**Block Diagram (to be drawn by student — 0 Marks penalty if missing, but recommended):**
The student should draw a flow diagram showing: CAD → STL → Slicing → Powder deposition → Laser scan → Platform down → Repeat → Cooling → Part extraction.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
$$P = 20 \text{ W}, \quad v = 2000 \text{ mm/s}, \quad h = 0.25 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 1: Write the VED Formula (1 Mark)**
$$E = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values (1 Mark)**
$$E = \frac{20}{2000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.1}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Denominator (1 Mark)**
$$2000 \cdot 0.25 = 500 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$
$$500 \cdot 0.1 = 50 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Final Calculation (1 Mark)**
$$E = \frac{20}{50} = 0.4 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Result (½ Mark):**
$$\boxed{E = 0.4 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

**Comment on Part Quality (2.5 Marks):**
The calculated VED of **0.4 J/mm³** is **significantly higher** than the recommended range for PA12 (0.05–0.20 J/mm³). This indicates:
- The part is likely to suffer from **thermal degradation**, **warping**, and **poor dimensional accuracy**.
- **Recommendation:** To bring the VED into the optimal range, the student should either:
  - Increase the scan speed to $v = 4000 \text{ mm/s}$ (yielding $E = 0.2 \text{ J/mm}^3$), OR
  - Reduce the laser power to $P = 10 \text{ W}$ (yielding $E = 0.2 \text{ J/mm}^3$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the various process parameters affecting the quality of SLS-produced parts. Explain the concept of volumetric energy density with its mathematical formulation.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compare and contrast SLS with FDM and SLA processes based on material, energy source, support requirement, and typical applications.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Process Parameters Affecting SLS Part Quality (4 Marks):**

1. **Laser Power ($P$):** Higher laser power delivers more energy, increasing part density. However, excessive power causes material degradation. (1 Mark)

2. **Scan Speed ($v$):** Higher scan speed reduces the energy delivered per unit length, leading to weaker sintering. (½ Mark)

3. **Hatch Spacing ($h$):** Larger hatch spacing can leave unsintered gaps between adjacent scan lines, reducing inter-track bonding. (½ Mark)

4. **Layer Thickness ($t$):** Thinner layers improve resolution and surface finish but increase build time. (½ Mark)

5. **Bed Temperature ($T_b$):** Maintaining $T_b$ close to the melting point reduces thermal gradients and warping. (½ Mark)

6. **Powder Characteristics:** Particle size, shape, and flowability affect packing density and energy absorption. (½ Mark)

7. **Atmosphere:** An inert gas prevents oxidation and ensures consistent energy absorption. (½ Mark)

**Volumetric Energy Density — Concept and Formula (3 Marks):**

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is a single composite parameter that captures the combined effect of laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness. It represents the total energy delivered to the powder per unit volume.

**Mathematical Derivation (2 Marks):**
- Energy delivered to one hatch line: $E_{hatch} = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}$
- Volume sintered by one hatch line: $V_{hatch} = L \cdot h \cdot t$
- Energy density: $E = \dfrac{E_{hatch}}{V_{hatch}} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$

**Significance (1 Mark):** VED is used to optimize process parameters, predict part density, and ensure consistent mechanical properties across builds.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 Marks — 1.25 per row):**

| Criterion | **SLS** | **FDM** | **SLA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Powder (polymer, metal, ceramic) | Thermoplastic filament | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **Energy Source** | CO₂ laser (thermal) | Heated extrusion nozzle | UV laser (photo-curing) |
| **Support Required** | **No** (powder self-supports) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Typical Applications** | Functional prototypes, end-use parts, casting patterns | Concept models, low-cost prototypes, hobbyist parts | High-detail models, jewelry, dental molds |

**Summary Statement (2 Marks):**
SLS offers the unique advantage of producing **functional, end-use parts** without support structures, making it ideal for **complex geometries and load-bearing applications**. FDM is the most **cost-effective** choice for **conceptual prototypes**, while SLA delivers the **highest surface finish and dimensional accuracy**, making it preferred for **visual prototypes and intricate details**. The selection between these processes depends on the trade-off between **cost, mechanical performance, surface quality, and material requirements**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield KTU Points:**

### Core Concepts to Memorize:
- [x] **SLS = Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) process** using a laser to sinter (not melt) powder.
- [x] **Working principle:** Powder deposition → Laser scan → Platform lower → Repeat.
- [x] **No support structures needed** — unsintered powder acts as self-support.
- [x] **Sintering temperature** is **80–90% of melting temperature** ($T_{sint} = 0.8 \text{ to } 0.9 \cdot T_m$).
- [x] **Build chamber is pre-heated** to just below $T_m$ and purged with **inert gas (N₂ for polymers, Ar for metals)**.
- [x] **LASER type:** **CO₂ (10.6 µm)** for polymers; **Fiber laser (1.06 µm)** for metals.

### Key Formulas to Memorize:
- [x] **VED:** $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — units **J/mm³**
- [x] **Optimal VED for PA12:** **0.05 – 0.20 J/mm³**
- [x] **Typical layer thickness:** **0.1 – 0.15 mm** for polymers

### Process Parameters to Remember:
- [x] **Laser Power ($P$):** 5–50 W
- [x] **Scan Speed ($v$):** 1000–5000 mm/s
- [x] **Hatch Spacing ($h$):** 0.2–0.5 mm

### Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid:
- [x] ❌ Do **NOT** say "SLS melts the powder" — it **sinters** the powder.
- [x] ❌ Do **NOT** forget to mention **units** in numerical answers.
- [x] ❌ Do **NOT** confuse **SLS (powder-based, thermal fusion)** with **SLA (resin-based, photopolymerization)**.
- [x] ❌ Do **NOT** state that SLS uses **support structures** — it does **NOT**.

### Advantages vs. Limitations (Frequently Asked):
- [x] **Advantages:** No supports, high material utilization, complex geometries, isotropic strength.
- [x] **Limitations:** High equipment cost, porous microstructure, grainy surface finish, post-processing required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fused Deposition Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fused Deposition Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a material extrusion-based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process, classified under the **ASTM F2792** standard as a *Material Extrusion* process. In FDM, a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier (extruder) where it is melted and then deposited selectively layer-by-layer through a nozzle onto a build platform, where it solidifies to form a physical 3D object based on sliced CAD geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** FDM is the most widely used commercial 3D printing process (Stratasys patented, 1989). It belongs to the **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** family and is governed by ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 terminology for AM.

### Working Principle in Plain English

The process is conceptually identical to a **computerized hot glue gun** that moves in precise X-Y paths to draw each cross-sectional layer of a part, then steps up in the Z direction and repeats.

**Step-by-Step Intuition:**

1. A solid thermoplastic **filament** (typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** diameter) is fed by a drive gear (pinch roller) into the **hot end** of the printer.
2. Inside the hot end, a **heater block** melts the filament at temperatures between **180 °C and 320 °C** (depending on material).
3. The molten polymer is extruded through a brass or hardened steel **nozzle** (typical diameter: **0.2 mm – 0.8 mm**) onto the build platform.
4. The print head traverses in the X-Y plane, depositing a thin road (bead) of material that bonds to the previous layer upon cooling.
5. After completing one layer, the build platform lowers by one **layer thickness** (typically **0.05 mm – 0.3 mm**), and the next layer is deposited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine icing a cake using a piping bag, but instead of icing one full layer, you trace only the outline and infill of each cross-section, then stack these layers to build a 3D object from the bottom up.

### Standard Metrics in FDM (Industry-Recognized Defaults)

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Layer Thickness** | 0.05 mm – 0.3 mm |
| **Nozzle Diameter** | 0.2 mm – 0.8 mm |
| **Filament Diameter** | 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm |
| **Nozzle Temperature** | 180 °C – 320 °C |
| **Build Platform Temperature** | 25 °C – 110 °C |
| **Print Speed** | 30 mm/s – 150 mm/s |
| **Build Volume (Desktop)** | ~200 × 200 × 250 mm |
| **Tolerance** | ± 0.1 mm – ± 0.5 mm |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | 5 – 25 µm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FDM Process Architecture (Functional Subsystems)

An FDM machine consists of four primary functional subsystems:

1. **Material Delivery Subsystem** — Spool holder, filament guide tube, pinch-roller extruder (direct-drive or Bowden).
2. **Liquefier / Melt Subsystem** — Heater block, thermistor, thermal break, brass nozzle.
3. **Motion Control Subsystem** — Stepper motors driving lead screws (Z-axis) and timing belts/pulleys (X-Y axes).
4. **Build Environment Subsystem** — Heated bed, enclosure (optional), part-removal mechanism.

## 2.2 Why FDM Works — The Underlying Physics

- **Polymer rheology:** Thermoplastics above their glass transition temperature ($T_g$) exhibit low viscosity (typically $10^2$ – $10^4$ Pa·s), enabling flow through sub-millimeter nozzles under modest pressure.
- **Adhesion mechanisms:** Inter-layer bonding occurs via (a) **mechanical interlocking**, (b) **diffusion bonding** (polymer chain entanglement across the interface), and (c) **van der Waals** forces upon cooling.
- **Solidification:** Heat is removed primarily by **conduction** into the previously deposited layer and **convection** to ambient air, governed by Newton's law of cooling.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation the examiner can realistically test from this topic.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f$ | Volumetric material feed rate; $d_f$ = filament diameter, $v_f$ = filament feed velocity | mm³/s |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_e$ | Volumetric extrusion rate; $d_n$ = nozzle diameter, $v_e$ = extruded road velocity | mm³/s |
| 3 | $v_e = \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f$ | Linear extrusion speed derived by equating (1) and (2) | mm/s |
| 4 | $w_r \approx d_n \cdot k_w$ | Effective road width; $k_w$ = road width correction factor (1.1–1.5) | mm |
| 5 | $h_r = \dfrac{Q}{w_r \cdot v_h}$ | Road height; $v_h$ = head travel speed in X-Y plane | mm |
| 6 | $A_{\text{road}} = w_r \cdot h_r = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{\,2}}{4}$ | Idealized road cross-section (rectangle equated to circle) | mm² |
| 7 | $t_{\text{layer}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_h}$ | Time to print one layer; $L_{\text{path}}$ = total path length of the layer | s |
| 8 | $t_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}}$ | Total build time (ignoring acceleration/retraction); $n_{\text{layers}} = \dfrac{H}{t_l}$ | s |
| 9 | $H = n_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_l$ | Total part height; $t_l$ = layer thickness | mm |
| 10 | $V_{\text{part}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$ | Part volume from mass; $m$ = mass, $\rho$ = bulk density of printed part | cm³ or mm³ |
| 11 | $M = \rho \cdot Q \cdot t_{\text{total}}$ | Material consumption (mass) | g |
| 12 | $P = \rho \cdot C_p \cdot Q \cdot \Delta T$ | Power required to melt filament; $C_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | W |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 3** is the foundation of slicer firmware (Marlin, Klipper, PrusaFirmware) — it converts commanded volumetric extrusion (E-steps) into linear filament feed.
- **Formula 12** governs heater block sizing; commercial hot ends are rated at **30 W – 75 W** to maintain melt capacity up to ~15 mm³/s.
- **Formula 8** is critical for **build-time estimation** quoted to customers in service bureaus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Filament Feed Rate and Extrusion Rate

The fundamental continuity equation for an incompressible polymer melt in the liquefier states that the volumetric flow entering the heater must equal the volumetric flow leaving the nozzle (conservation of mass, steady-state, no leakage).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Volumetric flow (filament side)} \;&=\; \text{Volumetric flow (nozzle side)} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_n^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_e \\[4pt]
\text{Cancelling } \dfrac{\pi}{4} \text{ from both sides:} \quad d_f^{\,2} \, v_f \;&=\; d_n^{\,2} \, v_e \\[4pt]
\text{Solving for } v_e: \quad v_e \;&=\; \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Because the nozzle diameter is much smaller than the filament diameter (e.g., $0.4$ mm vs. $1.75$ mm), the extrusion velocity is dramatically amplified. For $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $d_n = 0.4$ mm, the velocity ratio is $\left(\dfrac{1.75}{0.4}\right)^{2} \approx 19.14$. Thus, a modest filament feed of 1 mm/s produces an extrusion velocity of ~19 mm/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Road Height from Volumetric Continuity

Consider a print head traveling at velocity $v_h$ in the X-Y plane, depositing a road of width $w_r$ and height $h_r$. In a small time interval $\Delta t$, the head advances a distance $v_h \cdot \Delta t$, and the deposited volume equals the cross-sectional area times the length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{deposited}} \;&=\; A_{\text{road}} \cdot (v_h \cdot \Delta t) \\[4pt]
\text{But also,} \quad V_{\text{deposited}} \;&=\; Q \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{(from the extruder)} \\[4pt]
\text{Equating:} \quad Q \cdot \Delta t \;&=\; w_r \cdot h_r \cdot v_h \cdot \Delta t \\[4pt]
\text{Cancelling } \Delta t: \quad Q \;&=\; w_r \, h_r \, v_h \\[4pt]
\text{Solving for road height:} \quad h_r \;&=\; \dfrac{Q}{w_r \cdot v_h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The road height equals the layer thickness. Therefore, for a fixed volumetric flow, increasing the head speed or road width decreases the layer thickness — a principle exploited by slicers to fine-tune print resolution.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A desktop FDM machine uses a **1.75 mm** diameter PLA filament and a **0.4 mm** nozzle. The filament is fed at **3 mm/s** during printing. The print head traverses a single layer at **60 mm/s** along a total path length of **1500 mm**. The layer thickness is set to **0.2 mm**, and the part has **250 layers**. Calculate: (a) the extrusion velocity at the nozzle, (b) the volumetric flow rate, (c) the effective road width assuming a circular cross-section, and (d) the total build time.

### Given Data
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $d_n = 0.4$ mm
- $v_f = 3$ mm/s
- $v_h = 60$ mm/s
- $L_{\text{path}} = 1500$ mm per layer
- $t_l = 0.2$ mm, $n_{\text{layers}} = 250$

### (a) Extrusion Velocity $v_e$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_e \;&=\; \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \left(\dfrac{1.75}{0.4}\right)^{2} \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; (4.375)^{2} \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 19.1406 \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 57.42 \;\text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Volumetric Flow Rate $Q$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.75)^{2}}{4} \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3.0625}{4} \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 2.4053 \cdot 3 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 7.2159 \;\text{mm}^{3}\!/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Effective Road Width $w_r$

The road is assumed to flatten into a rectangle of width $w_r$ and height equal to the layer thickness $h_r = t_l = 0.2$ mm. Equating the deposited cross-section to the nozzle exit area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{road}} \;&=\; w_r \cdot h_r \;\approx\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_n^{\,2}}{4} \\[4pt]
w_r \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_n^{\,2}}{4 \, h_r} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.4)^{2}}{4 \cdot 0.2} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{0.5027}{0.8} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.6283 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** In practice, plastic squishes wider than the nozzle diameter, so a correction factor $k_w \approx 1.1$–$1.4$ is applied, giving $w_r \approx 0.7$–$0.88$ mm.

### (d) Total Build Time $t_{\text{total}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{layer}} \;&=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_h} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{60} \;=\; 25 \;\text{s} \\[4pt]
t_{\text{total}} \;&=\; n_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}} \;=\; 250 \cdot 25 \;=\; 6250 \;\text{s} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{6250}{60} \;\approx\; 104.17 \;\text{min} \;\approx\; 1 \;\text{h}\;44 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Real build times are 20–40 % longer than this estimate because the formula ignores acceleration ramps, retraction events (filament pull-back to prevent stringing), and non-printing travel moves (homing, layer changes).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameters vs. Output Quality

| Process Parameter | Effect on Surface Finish | Effect on Strength | Effect on Build Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Layer thickness** | Rougher (visible steps) | Slightly stronger (fewer interfaces) | Faster |
| **↓ Layer thickness** | Smoother | Weaker (more weak inter-layer bonds) | Slower |
| **↑ Nozzle temperature** | Smoother (better fusion) | Stronger inter-layer bonds | Slight increase (melt wait) |
| **↑ Print speed** | Rougher (vibration, under-extrusion) | Weaker | Faster |
| **↑ Infill density** | Minimal effect | Stronger | Longer |
| **↑ Infill pattern (e.g., gyroid)** | Marginal | Stronger in multi-axes | Longer |
| **Heated bed ON** | Less warping | Better first-layer adhesion | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDM Process Flow (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Input STL File] --> A2[Slicing Software Cura Slic3r PrusaSlicer]
    A2 --> A3[G-code Generation Layer by Layer Toolpath]
    A3 --> A4[Microcontroller Marlin Klipper Firmware]
    A4 --> B1[Stepper Motor X Axis Drive]
    A4 --> B2[Stepper Motor Y Axis Drive]
    A4 --> B3[Stepper Motor Z Axis Drive]
    A4 --> C1[Extruder Stepper Drive]
    C1 --> C2[Heater Block Melting Zone 180 to 320 C]
    C2 --> C3[Brass Nozzle d n 0.4 mm typical]
    B1 --> D1[Print Head X Y Motion]
    B2 --> D1
    D1 --> C3
    C3 --> D2[Road Deposition on Build Platform]
    B3 --> D3[Build Platform Lowering by One Layer Thickness]
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4{All Layers Complete?}
    D4 -- No --> D1
    D4 -- Yes --> E1[Cooling and Part Removal]
    E1 --> E2[Post Processing Support Removal Sanding]
```

## 4.2 FDM Subsystem Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MS1[Material Delivery Subsystem]
        m1a[Filament Spool 1 kg] --> m1b[Guide Tube PTFE Liner]
        m1b --> m1c[Pinch Roller Extruder]
    end
    subgraph MS2[Liquefier Subsystem]
        m1c --> m2a[Heater Block Cartridge 40 W]
        m2a --> m2b[Thermistor Temperature Feedback]
        m2a --> m2c[Brass Nozzle]
    end
    subgraph MS3[Motion Control Subsystem]
        m3a[Stepper Motors NEMA 17] --> m3b[Lead Screws Z Axis]
        m3a --> m3c[Timing Belts X and Y]
    end
    subgraph MS4[Build Environment]
        m4a[Heated Bed 60 to 110 C]
        m4b[Enclosure Optional for ABS]
    end
    MS2 --> MS3
    MS3 --> MS4
    m2c --> m4a
```

## 4.3 Layer-by-Layer Build Sequence (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1 Filament Feed] --> s2[Step 2 Melting in Hot End]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Extrusion Through Nozzle]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 X Y Path Tracing of Cross Section]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Solidification by Conduction and Convection]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6 Z Platform Step Down by Layer Thickness t l]
    s6 --> s7{All Layers Deposited?}
    s7 -- No --> s1
    s7 -- Yes --> s8[Step 7 Cooling and Part Removal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guidance for the Schematic:** In a real Cartesian FDM printer (e.g., Prusa i3, Ender-3), the print head moves in X and Y while the bed moves down in Z. In a CoreXY or Delta architecture, the kinematic mapping differs, but the four-subsystem architecture above remains identical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) and list any four thermoplastic materials commonly used in the FDM process.**

**Model Answer:**

Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is heated to a semi-molten state and extruded through a nozzle, depositing material layer-by-layer to form a three-dimensional solid object from a digital CAD model.

**Four commonly used thermoplastic materials in FDM:**

1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, low warping, ideal for prototypes; print temperature 190–220 °C.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat-resistant, requires enclosed heated chamber; print temperature 220–260 °C.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — combines ease of PLA with strength of ABS; print temperature 220–250 °C.
4. **Nylon (Polyamide PA12/PA6)** — high strength and flexibility; requires high temperatures (240–280 °C) and dry filament storage.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently award partial credit for naming the materials with their print-temperature range — be precise.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List the major process parameters that affect the quality of an FDM-printed part, and explain the effect of layer thickness on surface finish.**

**Model Answer:**

**Major process parameters affecting FDM part quality:**

1. **Layer thickness**
2. **Nozzle temperature**
3. **Build platform (bed) temperature**
4. **Print speed**
5. **Infill density and pattern**
6. **Road width**
7. **Raster orientation (0°/45°/90°)**

**Effect of layer thickness on surface finish:**

Layer thickness is the vertical distance between two consecutive deposited layers. A **smaller layer thickness** (e.g., 0.05–0.1 mm) produces a **smoother surface finish** because the staircase effect between layers is reduced, particularly along curved or slanted surfaces. However, smaller layer thickness increases build time and the total number of layers. Conversely, a **larger layer thickness** (e.g., 0.25–0.3 mm) reduces build time but produces visibly stepped surfaces with higher surface roughness (Ra ≈ 15–25 µm).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process. Discuss the various process parameters that influence the quality of the FDM-built part.**

#### (a) Working Principle with Block Diagram (7 Marks)

The FDM process operates in the following sequence (see block diagram in SECTION 4.1):

1. **CAD Model Creation:** A 3D model of the desired part is created in CAD software and exported as an **STL** (Standard Tessellation Language) file.
2. **Slicing:** The STL file is imported into slicing software (Cura, Slic3r, PrusaSlicer), which slices the model into horizontal cross-sections of predefined **layer thickness $t_l$** and generates a **G-code** toolpath.
3. **Filament Feeding:** A spool of thermoplastic filament (1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter) is fed by an **extruder stepper motor** through a guide tube into the **heater block** of the print head.
4. **Melting:** Inside the heater block, the filament is heated to **180 °C – 320 °C** (depending on material) and becomes a viscous melt.
5. **Extrusion and Deposition:** The molten polymer is forced through a brass **nozzle** (diameter 0.2–0.8 mm) and deposited onto a heated **build platform** following the X-Y path of the current layer.
6. **Solidification:** The deposited material cools rapidly and bonds to the previously deposited layer through **diffusion bonding**.
7. **Layer Stacking:** After one layer is completed, the **build platform descends by one layer thickness** (or the head ascends), and the next layer is deposited. This continues until the full part height $H$ is achieved.
8. **Cooling and Post-Processing:** The completed part is allowed to cool, removed from the platform, and any **support structures** are detached. Optional post-processing includes sanding, painting, or vapour smoothing.

**Block diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 (FDM Process Flow) and SECTION 4.2 (Subsystem Block Architecture).

#### (b) Process Parameters Influencing FDM Part Quality (7 Marks)

| # | Parameter | Effect on Quality |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Layer thickness** | Smaller thickness → smoother surface, finer features, longer build time |
| 2 | **Nozzle/road width** | Affects part wall thickness and surface roughness |
| 3 | **Print (head travel) speed** | Higher speed → faster build but lower dimensional accuracy and weaker bonding |
| 4 | **Nozzle temperature** | Higher temperature → better inter-layer adhesion, smoother surface; too high → stringing and oozing |
| 5 | **Build platform temperature** | Reduces warping and improves first-layer adhesion, especially for ABS |
| 6 | **Infill density** | Higher infill → stronger but heavier and slower part |
| 7 | **Infill pattern** | Influences anisotropic strength; gyroid gives multi-axial strength, lines/rectilinear give directional strength |
| 8 | **Raster orientation (build orientation)** | Parts are stronger along the raster direction than across layers (Z-direction is weakest) |
| 9 | **Support structure placement** | Overhangs > 45° require supports; poor support design causes print failure |
| 10 | **Environmental conditions** | Drafts cause warping in ABS; enclosed chamber recommended |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only a generic list of parameters without explicitly stating the **direction of influence** (increase or decrease) and the **underlying mechanism** (e.g., diffusion bonding). This costs 2–3 marks per question. Always state: *parameter ↑ → quality ↑↓, because [reason]*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q: A FDM machine uses a 1.75 mm diameter PLA filament and a 0.4 mm diameter nozzle. The filament is fed at a rate of 4 mm/s. The print head travels at 80 mm/s while printing a single layer of total path length 2000 mm. Layer thickness is 0.15 mm and the part has 200 layers. Calculate: (a) the extrusion velocity at the nozzle, (b) the volumetric flow rate, (c) the effective road width, and (d) the total build time. Discuss the advantages and limitations of the FDM process.**

#### (a) Extrusion Velocity at the Nozzle (3 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_e \;&=\; \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \left(\dfrac{1.75}{0.4}\right)^{2} \cdot 4 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; (4.375)^{2} \cdot 4 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 19.1406 \cdot 4 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 76.56 \;\text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Volumetric Flow Rate (3 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_f^{\,2}}{4} \cdot v_f \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.75)^{2}}{4} \cdot 4 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3.0625}{4} \cdot 4 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \pi \cdot 3.0625 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 9.6211 \;\text{mm}^{3}\!/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (c) Effective Road Width (4 Marks)

The road is approximated as a rectangle of width $w_r$ and height $h_r = t_l = 0.15$ mm. Equating road cross-section to the nozzle exit area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_r \cdot h_r \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_n^{\,2}}{4} \\[4pt]
w_r \;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \, d_n^{\,2}}{4 \, h_r} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.4)^{2}}{4 \cdot 0.15} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{0.5027}{0.6} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 0.8378 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (d) Total Build Time (4 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{layer}} \;&=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_h} \;=\; \dfrac{2000}{80} \;=\; 25 \;\text{s} \\[4pt]
t_{\text{total}} \;&=\; n_{\text{layers}} \cdot t_{\text{layer}} \;=\; 200 \cdot 25 \;=\; 5000 \;\text{s} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \dfrac{5000}{60} \;\approx\; 83.33 \;\text{min} \;\approx\; 1 \;\text{h}\;23 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Advantages and Limitations of FDM (Included in the 14-Mark Framework)

**Advantages:**

- Low machine and material cost compared to other AM processes (SLA, SLS).
- Wide material selection (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, TPU, PC, PEEK).
- Simple process with minimal post-processing.
- Suitable for office and educational environments (no toxic chemicals or lasers).
- Multi-material printing possible with dual-extrusion heads.

**Limitations:**

- Anisotropic mechanical properties (Z-direction is weak due to inter-layer delamination).
- Visible **staircase effect** on curved surfaces.
- Slow build times for large parts.
- Limited resolution (typically ± 0.1 mm) compared to SLA/DLP.
- Support structures required for overhangs > 45°, leaving surface scars upon removal.
- Warping and shrinkage in engineering plastics like ABS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes** — Forgetting to convert seconds to minutes/hours costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Cross-section assumption** — Writing $w_r = d_n$ (instead of using the area-equivalence method) is conceptually wrong; the road flattens, so $w_r > d_n$. Always derive $w_r$ from the area balance.
> 3. **Forgetting $n_{\text{layers}}$** — Some students compute only one layer's time and present it as the total — this loses 3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Slicing $\pi$ early** — Keep $\pi$ symbolic until the final step to avoid intermediate rounding errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or memorize this checklist the night before the exam:**

- **Definition:** FDM = Material Extrusion AM process, ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900:2021.
- **Working Principle:** Filament → heated liquefier → extruded bead → X-Y path → Z-step → layer stack.
- **Key components:** Filament spool, extruder, heater block, nozzle, build platform, motion system, controller.
- **Standard materials:** PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, TPU, PC, PEEK; filaments of 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm.
- **Standard metrics:** Layer thickness 0.05–0.3 mm; nozzle 0.2–0.8 mm; build tolerance ± 0.1–0.5 mm; surface roughness Ra = 5–25 µm.
- **Core formula — flow continuity:** $v_e = \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_n}\right)^{2} \cdot v_f$ — *velocity amplifies dramatically at the nozzle*.
- **Core formula — road geometry:** $w_r \cdot h_r = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{\,2}}{4}$ — *road flattens wider than the nozzle*.
- **Core formula — build time:** $t_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{H \cdot L_{\text{path}}}{t_l \cdot v_h}$ — *use this for any time-estimation problem*.
- **Process parameters that matter most for examiners:** Layer thickness, print speed, nozzle temperature, bed temperature, infill density, raster orientation, build orientation.
- **Anisotropy:** Z-direction is the weakest due to inter-layer adhesion (diffusion bonding limited by short re-melt window).
- **Warping:** Most severe in ABS → solved by heated bed (60–110 °C) and enclosed chamber.
- **Staircase effect:** Caused by finite layer thickness → minimized by smaller $t_l$ and curved-layer (variable $t_l$) slicing.
- **Supports:** Required for overhangs > 45° (or > 50° for some printers); soluble supports (PVA, HIPS) used in dual-extrusion systems.
- **Common industrial applications:** Functional prototypes, jigs and fixtures, casting patterns, low-volume end-use parts, anatomical models, educational models.
- **Comparison anchor:** FDM is cheaper and simpler than SLA/SLS but lower in resolution and surface finish.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminated Object Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laminated Object Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
