> **Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)** is a **Sheet Lamination** based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process, classified under **ISO/ASTM 52900** as the *Material Extrusion / Sheet Lamination* family. In LOM, successive layers of **sheet feedstock** (paper, polymer, metal foil, or composite) are sequentially bonded together using a **thermally activated adhesive** or a **bonding agent**, and a **laser beam or mechanical cutter** traces the cross-sectional profile of each sliced layer, ultimately building a fully solid 3D object from a stack of 2D laminations.

The process was originally developed by **Helisys Inc. (USA)** in the late 1980s and is one of the seven ISO-recognized AM process categories. It belongs to the **Material Forming (Sheet Lamination)** family — distinct from powder-bed or vat photopolymerization systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of LOM as a **"3D Photocopier"** or a **"Glue-Layer Cake Maker"**. Imagine you want to build a 3D object. Instead of printing it as a continuous mass (like a 3D printer extruding plastic), you:
> 1. **Print the shape of each horizontal slice** onto a sheet of paper,
> 2. **Glue the printed sheet** onto a stack of previous sheets,
> 3. **Cut away the unwanted portions** with a sharp blade,
> 4. **Repeat** for every slice of the 3D model.

A real-world parallel: a **pancake tower** 🥞. A chef makes one flat pancake (one layer), stacks it, then makes the next one, and so on — until a 3D shape emerges. Each pancake is a *lamination layer*.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Layer Thickness (Z-resolution)** | **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** (typical ≈ **0.1 mm**) |
| **Build Volume** | Up to **800 mm × 500 mm × 500 mm** |
| **Build Speed (Volume Rate)** | ~**5 to 10 cm³/min** |
| **Laser Power (CO₂ laser)** | **25 W to 50 W** |
| **XY Resolution** | ~**0.25 mm** (governed by laser spot size) |
| **Adhesive Activation Temperature** | **120 °C – 180 °C** (paper-based systems) |

> [!NOTE]
> A key KTU takeaway: **LOM does NOT require support structures** because the uncured/unbonded sheet material around the part acts as a natural sacrificial support. This is a major distinguishing feature from SLA/SLS systems.

## 1.4 Common Feedstock Materials

- **Paper (most common)** — coated with heat-activated adhesive and silicone release layer.
- **Polymer films** — PVC, PET, polycarbonate.
- **Metal foils** — aluminum, copper, steel (for tooling inserts).
- **Ceramic green tapes** — used in advanced ceramic processing.
- **Composite prepregs** — fiber-reinforced polymer sheets for functional parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow (Layer-by-Layer Logic)

The LOM process is governed by a deterministic **slicing-and-bonding** sequence. Below is the structured procedural logic:

1. **CAD Model Acquisition** — A 3D solid model is imported into the LOM slicing software in **STL** or **3MF** format.
2. **Slicing** — The CAD model is virtually divided into **n horizontal cross-sections**, each of uniform thickness equal to the **layer thickness (Δz)**. Mathematically:
   
   $$n = \left\lceil \frac{H_{model}}{\Delta z} \right\rceil$$

   where $H_{model}$ is the total height (build direction) of the CAD model.

3. **Layer Deposition** — A fresh sheet of feedstock is **rolled or fed** onto the build platform using a heated roller.
4. **Thermal Bonding** — The **heated roller** (typically **180 °C** for paper) activates the adhesive, pressing the new sheet onto the stack below.
5. **Laser Cutting (Cross-Sectioning)** — A **CO₂ laser** (wavelength ~**10.6 μm**) traces the **contour** of the current slice and creates a **cross-hatch grid pattern** in the surplus material.
6. **Cubic Decubing** — The cross-hatched waste regions are sliced into small **cubes (decubes)** for easy removal.
7. **Indexed Stacking** — The build platform is **lowered by Δz**, and the cycle repeats until all *n* layers are processed.
8. **Post-Processing** — Excess decubed waste is manually removed, and the part is **decoated** (silicon oxide release layer is sandblasted off) and **finished**.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

- **Why laser-cut grid lines?** — Without hatching, the un-bonded perimeter sheet would be impossible to remove after build completion. The hatching converts continuous waste into manageable cubes.
- **Why paper as default feedstock?** — Paper is **cheap, dimensionally stable**, and already **pre-coated with adhesive**, making it a turnkey solution. It mimics the surface finish of **wood** after post-processing.
- **Why no support structures?** — Surrounding waste material physically anchors the part. This is a **key KTU exam point**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of layers | $n = \lceil H_{model} / \Delta z \rceil$ | dimensionless | Total laminations required |
| 2 | Build time (ideal) | $T_{build} = n \cdot t_{layer} + T_{setup}$ | minutes | Total cycle time |
| 3 | Layer time | $t_{layer} = t_{feed} + t_{bond} + t_{cut} + t_{index}$ | seconds | Per-layer cycle |
| 4 | Laser scan velocity | $v_{laser} = P_{laser} / (E_{ablation} \cdot w_{spot})$ | mm/s | Cutting speed |
| 5 | Adhesive peel strength | $\sigma_{peel} = F_{peel} / w_{bond}$ | N/m | Layer-to-layer bond |
| 6 | Build volume | $V_{build} = L \times W \times H_{max}$ | mm³ | Envelope capacity |
| 7 | Material efficiency | $\eta = V_{part} / V_{feedstock\,used}$ | % | % of feedstock in part |
| 8 | Theoretical material density | $\rho_{LOM} = \rho_{sheet} \cdot (1 - P_{void})$ | g/cm³ | Density of part |
| 9 | Surface roughness (RA) | $R_a \approx k \cdot \Delta z$ | µm | Step-staircase effect |
| 10 | Hatched cut spacing | $s_{grid} = w_{cut} \cdot f_{overlap}$ | mm | Decube grid pitch |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL for KTU**: When you see $\vert$ or absolute value expressions in tables, ALWAYS use the rendered form `abs(x)` in your written answer to avoid markdown parsing errors. Standard exam answers in KTU prefer written forms like $\lvert V_{part} \rvert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Sand casting patterns & cores** — paper-LOM patterns are burned out in furnaces (investment-casting-like workflow).
- **Foundry tooling** — large sand-mold halves.
- **Visual prototypes & architectural models** — wood-like appearance.
- **Metal tooling inserts** — laminated steel foils are diffusion-bonded to form EDM electrodes.
- **Preforms for composites** — LOM builds of carbon-fiber prepregs are consolidated in autoclaves.
- **Biomedical scaffolds** — ceramic tape LOM for bone-tissue engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Layers

**Problem Setup:** A KTU exam question states: *"A CAD model is 78 mm tall. The LOM machine is configured with a layer thickness of 0.15 mm. Calculate the number of layers required, and determine the leftover unused material at the top."*

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

$$
H_{model} = 78 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta z = 0.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the layer count formula**

$$
n = \frac{H_{model}}{\Delta z} = \frac{78}{0.15}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute the division explicitly**

$$
n = \frac{7800}{15} = \frac{1560}{3} = 520 \text{ layers (exact)}
$$

**Step 4 — Check for fractional layer (ceiling function)**

Since $n$ is a whole number, the ceiling function is redundant:

$$
n = \lceil 520.0 \rceil = 520 \text{ layers}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute leftover (in practice, the slicer fits the top layer to model height)**

$$
H_{actual} = n \cdot \Delta z = 520 \times 0.15 = 78.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The model needs **520 layers** with **zero leftover height**, since 78 is exactly divisible by 0.15.

> [!NOTE]
> If $H_{model} = 78.07$ mm, then $n = 78.07/0.15 = 520.466$, so $\lceil 520.466 \rceil = 521$ layers, and the actual built height is $521 \times 0.15 = 78.15$ mm — meaning the top 0.08 mm is *waste*. This is a **favourite KTU Part A numerical question**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Build Time Estimation

**Problem Setup:** Estimate build time for a part of height 60 mm, $\Delta z = 0.2$ mm, with each layer taking 45 s for feed+heat+cut+index, and machine setup consuming 12 minutes.

**Step 1 — Number of layers**

$$
n = \frac{60}{0.2} = 300 \text{ layers}
$$

**Step 2 — Total per-layer time**

$$
t_{layer} = 45 \text{ s} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total build time (ideal)**

$$
T_{build} = n \cdot t_{layer} + T_{setup}
$$

$$
T_{build} = 300 \times 0.75 + 12
$$

$$
T_{build} = 225 + 12 = 237 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours**

$$
T_{build} = \frac{237}{60} = 3.95 \text{ hours} \approx 3 \text{ h } 57 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Add post-processing overhead (typically +15%)**

$$
T_{total} = 237 \times 1.15 \approx 272.55 \text{ min} \approx 4.54 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU answers must show units at every step. Examiners in KTU deduct marks for missing units.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Variants of LOM

| Variant | Feedstock | Cutter | Bonding | Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Helisys LOM (Paper)** | Adhesive-coated paper | CO₂ laser (25–50 W) | Heated roller (180 °C) | Low cost, large parts | Wood-like, weak in Z |
| **Plastic LOM** | PVC / PET sheets | CO₂ laser | Adhesive film | Better Z-strength | Limited thermal range |
| **Metal LOM (Ultrasonic Additive / UAM)** | Metal foils (Al, Ti) | Sonic horn | Ultrasonic welding | **Fully dense metal** | Limited geometries, slow |
| **Ceramic LOM** | Green ceramic tape | Laser / blade | Thermoplastic binder | High-temp capable | Brittle preform |
| **CAM-LEM (Plasma)** | Steel sheets | Plasma arc | Welded | Functional tooling | Rough surfaces |
| **Composite LOM** | Carbon-fiber prepreg | Laser + compaction | Autoclave (post) | High strength-to-weight | Expensive |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Surface Roughness Estimate (Staircase Effect)

**Step 1 — Standard empirical relation**

The surface roughness of a LOM part along the build direction is approximated by:

$$
R_a \approx k \cdot \Delta z
$$

where $k$ is an empirical constant (typically **0.4 to 0.6** for laminated surfaces).

**Step 2 — Plug in typical paper-LOM values**

$$
\Delta z = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 100 \text{ µm}, \quad k = 0.5
$$

$$
R_a \approx 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to other AM processes**

$$
R_{a,\,FDM} \approx 1.5 \cdot \Delta z, \quad R_{a,\,SLA} \approx 0.2 \cdot \Delta z
$$

> **Interpretation:** LOM has **moderate** surface roughness — *better than FDM, worse than SLA* — making it well-suited for **visual prototypes** but not precision functional parts.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Bond Integrity (Lap Shear Analysis)

The bond between two adjacent laminations can be modeled as a thin adhesive layer. The shear stress is:

$$
\tau = \frac{F}{A_{bond}} = \frac{F}{L \cdot w}
$$

For a paper-LOM bond with peel strength $\sigma_{peel} = 1.5$ N/mm and bond width $w = 20$ mm over a peel length $L = 50$ mm:

$$
F_{peel} = \sigma_{peel} \cdot w = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ N}
$$

The peel energy is:

$$
G = \frac{F_{peel}}{w} \cdot \Delta z = \sigma_{peel} \cdot \Delta z
$$

$$
G = 1.5 \text{ N/mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ N} \cdot \text{mm} / \text{mm} = 0.15 \text{ J/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **common KTU Part B sub-part**, often paired with questions on *delamination failure modes*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LOM Process Flow — Functional Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **functional sequence** of the LOM process. The safeguards (alphanumeric IDs, no reserved keywords in node labels, no markdown inside quotes) have been applied.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD 3D Model] --> A2[STL or 3MF Import]
    A2 --> A3[Slicing Software]
    A3 --> A4[Layer Count Computation]
    A4 --> A5[Build Platform Initialization]
    A5 --> B1[Sheet Feed from Roll]
    B1 --> B2[Heated Roller Activation 180C]
    B2 --> B3[Thermal Bonding to Stack]
    B3 --> C1[CO2 Laser Cutting 10.6 um]
    C1 --> C2[Cross Hatch Grid Generation]
    C2 --> C3[Decube Formation]
    C3 --> D1[Platform Lower by DeltaZ]
    D1 --> E1{Layers Complete}
    E1 -->|No| B1
    E1 -->|Yes| F1[Manual Waste Decubing]
    F1 --> F2[Surface Decoating Sandblast]
    F2 --> F3[Final 3D Part]
```

## 4.2 LOM Machine — Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedstockSystem
        FS1[Sheet Roll]
        FS2[Feed Tensioner]
        FS3[Guide Roller]
    end

    subgraph BondingSystem
        BS1[Heated Roller]
        BS2[Temperature Controller]
        BS3[Pressure Roller]
    end

    subgraph CuttingSystem
        CS1[CO2 Laser Source]
        CS2[Galvanometer Scanner]
        CS3[Focusing Lens]
    end

    subgraph MotionSystem
        MS1[X Y Gantry]
        MS2[Z Elevator]
        MS3[Build Platform]
    end

    subgraph ControlSystem
        CT1[Process Computer]
        CT2[Slicer Software]
        CT3[Sensor Feedback]
    end

    FS1 --> FS2 --> FS3 --> BS1
    BS1 --> BS2
    BS1 --> BS3
    CS1 --> CS2 --> CS3
    CT1 --> CT2
    CT1 --> MS1
    CT1 --> MS2
    CT1 --> BS2
    CT3 --> CT1
    MS1 --> MS2 --> MS3
```

## 4.3 Process State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Slicing: Load STL
    Slicing --> Priming: First Layer Setup
    Priming --> LayerLoop: Begin Build
    LayerLoop --> LayerLoop: Heat Bond Cut Hatch Index
    LayerLoop --> EndBuild: Layer N Reached
    EndBuild --> Cooling: Stop Roller
    Cooling --> Decubing: Operator Intervention
    Decubing --> PostProcess: Sandblast and Finish
    PostProcess --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Legend (KTU Reference)

| Symbol in Diagrams | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Heated Roller** | ~180 °C rubber-coated cylinder | Activates adhesive and consolidates layers |
| **CO₂ Laser** | 10.6 μm wavelength | Cuts contour and gridlines |
| **Decubes** | Small waste cubes | Removable excess material |
| **Build Platform** | Z-actuated base | Lowered by Δz per layer |
| **Galvanometer** | XY beam steering | Deflects laser at high speed |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) and Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LOM | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | Pre-made sheet rolls | Thermoplastic filament |
| **Bonding** | Heated roller + adhesive | Molten extrusion |
| **Support structures** | Not required | Mandatory for overhangs |
| **Surface finish** | Wood-like, post-processed | Visible layer lines |
| **Material waste** | High (decubed waste) | Low |

**(3 marks — one mark per valid difference, max 3 differences.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why are cross-hatched grid lines cut into the waste material during LOM? Explain in 3–4 lines.*

**Model Answer:**
The cross-hatch pattern converts the continuous surplus sheet into **small detachable cubes (decubes)**. Without this grid, the un-bonded excess would remain as a single solid mass around the part, making post-build removal **mechanically impossible**. The cubes fall away easily, and the part can be **manually extracted** without damage. This is why the grid spacing is a critical LOM parameter.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for gridding concept, 1 for cube formation, 1 for easy removal rationale.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **MODULE-LEVEL INTERNAL CHOICE:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module.* Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain the working principle of Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) with a neat block diagram. List any four advantages and four limitations of the process. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. A 3D CAD model is sliced into **N horizontal layers** of uniform thickness $\Delta z$.
2. A **sheet of adhesive-coated paper (or polymer/metal foil)** is fed from a roll across the build platform.
3. A **heated roller** at ~180 °C activates the adhesive and bonds the new sheet to the top of the previously built stack.
4. A **CO₂ laser** traces the **cross-sectional contour** of the current layer and creates a **cross-hatch grid** in the surrounding waste area, cutting it into small decubes.
5. The **build platform descends by Δz**, and the cycle repeats until the entire 3D part is built.
6. After build completion, the decubed waste is **manually removed**, and the part is **decoated** (silicone layer sandblasted) for final use.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — any four):**
- No need for support structures (surrounding waste acts as support).
- Low material cost (paper-based systems).
- Can build **large parts** (build volumes up to 800 × 500 × 500 mm).
- High build speed for solid parts (no need to fill internal volume if not required).
- Good for **sand-casting patterns** (paper burns out cleanly).
- Wood-like aesthetic suitable for architectural models.

**Limitations (1.5 marks — any four):**
- Poor **Z-direction strength** due to adhesive interlayers.
- Material waste is high (cubed waste is discarded).
- Time-consuming **post-processing** (decubing and decoating).
- **Surface finish** is poor compared to SLA/SLS.
- Limited to **sheet-form materials**.
- **Dimensional accuracy** lower than vat-based AM.

**(b)** *A part of 90 mm height is to be built on a LOM machine with a layer thickness of 0.18 mm. If each layer requires 50 s for feeding, bonding, cutting, and indexing, and the machine setup takes 15 minutes, calculate (i) the number of layers, (ii) total build time in hours, and (iii) the material utilization efficiency if the part volume is 120 cm³ and total feedstock consumed is 220 cm³. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of layers (2 marks):**

$$
n = \frac{H_{model}}{\Delta z} = \frac{90}{0.18}
$$

$$
n = \frac{9000}{18} = 500 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Total build time (2.5 marks):**

Per-layer time $t_{layer} = 50$ s $= 50/60$ min $= 0.8333$ min

$$
T_{build} = n \cdot t_{layer} + T_{setup}
$$

$$
T_{build} = 500 \times 0.8333 + 15
$$

$$
T_{build} = 416.67 + 15 = 431.67 \text{ min}
$$

$$
T_{build} = \frac{431.67}{60} = 7.19 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Material utilization efficiency (2.5 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{V_{part}}{V_{feedstock}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{120}{220} \times 100\% = 54.55\%
$$

**Result Summary:** $n = 500$ layers, $T_{build} \approx 7.19$ h, $\eta \approx 54.55\%$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *With a clear schematic, describe the LOM process. Compare LOM with Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) in terms of (i) feedstock form, (ii) energy source, (iii) support requirement, and (iv) post-processing. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**LOM Schematic Description (3.5 marks):**

The LOM system consists of a **feed roll of adhesive-coated sheet**, a **heated compaction roller**, a **CO₂ laser cutting head** mounted on a **XY gantry**, and a **vertically movable build platform**. The slicer pre-computes the contour of each layer. After a fresh sheet is laid down, the heated roller bonds it, and the laser cuts the contour plus a waste-grid pattern. The platform indexes downward by Δz, and the cycle repeats. After build, **decubing** manually removes the surrounding waste, followed by **decoating** to remove the release layer.

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | LOM | SLS |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Feedstock form** | Continuous sheet/roll | Powder bed |
| **(ii) Energy source** | CO₂ laser (thermal cut) | CO₂ / fiber laser (sintering) |
| **(iii) Support requirement** | **None** (waste acts as support) | **None** (unsintered powder supports part) |
| **(iv) Post-processing** | Decubing + sandblasting | Powder removal + bead blasting |

**(b)** *Explain the role of (i) the heated roller, (ii) the CO₂ laser, (iii) the cross-hatching strategy, and (iv) the silicone release layer in the LOM process. Why is LOM particularly suited for sand-casting pattern production? (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heated Roller (1.5 marks):**
The heated roller, typically maintained at **150–180 °C**, supplies **thermal energy + pressure** to activate the **heat-sensitive adhesive** pre-coated on the paper sheet. This ensures strong **inter-layer bonding** without voids, which is critical for part integrity. The roller also **flattens the layer** and removes trapped air.

**(ii) CO₂ Laser (1.5 marks):**
The CO₂ laser emits at **10.6 μm**, which is strongly absorbed by paper, polymer, and adhesive coatings. It serves a **dual function** — (a) cutting the **contour of the cross-section** to define the part geometry, and (b) cutting the **cross-hatch grid** in the waste region. Typical power: 25–50 W.

**(iii) Cross-Hatching Strategy (1.5 marks):**
The cross-hatch grid lines convert the **continuous waste sheet** into small **cubes (decubes)**. After the build, the part is essentially a **lattice of connected decubes**. The user can then easily **snap off or sand off** the cubes to extract the part. Without hatching, the waste would be a single, inseparable membrane.

**(iv) Silicone Release Layer (1 mark):**
The bottom face of paper-LOM sheets is coated with a **silicone release agent** that prevents the sheet from **sticking permanently** to the build platform or the previous layer's waste. This is what enables **easy decubing** after the build. It is later removed via sandblasting in the decoating step.

**Why LOM suits sand-casting patterns (1.5 marks):**

- The **paper feedstock burns out cleanly** in the foundry furnace, leaving no residue.
- LOM can build **large patterns** quickly (no need to fill solid interior).
- The **dimensional accuracy** is sufficient for one-off castings.
- **Low material cost** makes it economical for sacrificial patterns.
- The wood-like cellulose paper produces **low ash content** and clean burnout.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing LOM with FDM:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if students mix up sheet-based (LOM) and filament-based (FDM) processes. Memorize: *LOM = sheet + laser cut; FDM = filament + extrusion*.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** A 14-mark question with 2–3 numerical sub-parts WILL lose 0.5–1 mark per missing unit. Always write `mm`, `min`, `cm³`, etc.
> 3. **Stating "LOM uses powder":** This is a definitive error. LOM uses **sheet feedstock**, not powder. Mixing this up with SLS is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Not mentioning decubing / cross-hatching:** If asked "explain LOM", the answer is **incomplete** without the **waste grid + decubing step**. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Skipping the slicer step:** Always start LOM explanations from **CAD → STL → Slicing → Layer n → Bond → Cut**. Skipping "slicing" is treated as a conceptual gap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **LOM = Sheet Lamination Process** (one of seven ISO 52900 AM categories).
- **Feedstock:** adhesive-coated paper rolls (default), polymer foils, metal foils, ceramic tapes.
- **Bonding:** heated roller (~180 °C) activates adhesive.
- **Cutting:** CO₂ laser (10.6 μm) cuts contour + cross-hatch grid.
- **Layer thickness Δz:** typically **0.05–0.5 mm**.
- **No support structures needed** — surrounding waste decubes provide support.
- **Post-processing:** decubing → decoating (sandblast) → optional sealing/painting.
- **Build time formula:** $T_{build} = n \cdot t_{layer} + T_{setup}$.
- **Layer count formula:** $n = \lceil H_{model} / \Delta z \rceil$.
- **Material efficiency:** typically **30–60%** (rest is decubed waste).
- **Key advantage:** large parts + low cost + no support.
- **Key limitation:** weak Z-direction strength (adhesive interlayer is the failure plane).
- **Best application:** **sand-casting patterns and cores** (paper burns out cleanly).
- **Variants:** paper-LOM (Helisys), metal-LOM (UAM), ceramic-LOM, plastic-LOM, plasma-LEM.
- **Comparison with SLS:** Both need no supports, but LOM uses sheets and SLS uses powder; LOM is cheaper for large parts, SLS gives better surface finish and Z-strength.
- **Comparison with FDM:** FDM extrudes molten plastic; LOM laminates sheets. LOM has higher Z-strength only if adhesives are strong, but is wood-like and less isotropic.
- **Z-direction weakness cause:** the adhesive layer between two sheets is the weakest plane — critical for understanding part failure.
- **Surface roughness:** $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot \Delta z$ (moderate — better than FDM, worse than SLA).
- **Waste management:** decubes are often recycled as thermal energy in paper mills — a sustainability point often asked in KTU viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Engineered Net-Shaping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laser Engineered Net-Shaping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)** is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process in which a high-power laser beam is used as the localized heat source to melt and fuse metal powders (or wires) injected through coaxial nozzles, depositing material layer-by-layer onto a substrate to build a near-net-shape three-dimensional metallic component directly from a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model.

The process was originally developed by **Sandia National Laboratories, USA (1996)** and is commercially marketed by companies such as **Optomec (LENS®)**, **DM3D Technology**, and **Trumpf**. The classification of LENS under the **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard for Additive Manufacturing falls under the **"Directed Energy Deposition (DED)"** category, where thermal energy is used to fuse materials as they are being deposited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LENS is grouped under **Powder-Based Additive Manufacturing** in Module 4 and is a high-weightage topic for Part B (14-mark) questions, especially in conjunction with **DED, SLM, EBM**, and **Laser Cladding**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **3D printing pen** that draws in mid-air with molten metal instead of plastic. Now imagine that pen is guided by a **computer** following a 3D blueprint, and instead of a single point, a **high-powered laser** is melting powdered metal exactly where the pen tip is pointing.

Picture a **chocolate fountain of metal**: tiny metal particles are sprayed through a nozzle, a laser beam acts as a localized "match" that instantly melts them on contact with a metal base plate, and the molten pool solidifies within milliseconds. As the nozzle moves (in X, Y directions), it leaves behind a solidified "track" of metal. When one layer is complete, the nozzle moves up by a small increment (Z direction) and prints the next layer on top — building a solid 3D metal object from the ground up, **without any molds, dies, or tooling**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Standard Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Net Shaping** | Fabrication of a part that requires minimal or no post-machining | Reduces material wastage |
| **DED** | Directed Energy Deposition — ISO/ASTM category for LENS | Distinguishes it from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) |
| **Coaxial Nozzle** | Powder streams converge at the same axis as the laser | Enables multi-directional deposition |
| **Substrate** | Base plate on which the build is initiated | Provides mechanical support & heat sink |
| **Build Envelope** | Maximum XYZ dimensions achievable in a single setup | LENS typically: 300 × 300 × 400 mm³ |
| **Melt Pool** | Localized molten region created by the laser | Governs microstructure & bonding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Recall:** Typical **laser power** used in industrial LENS systems ranges from **500 W to 4 kW**, with build accuracies of **± 0.13 mm** and surface roughness (Ra) between **10 – 30 µm** in as-built condition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Stepwise Logic Breakdown

The LENS process is governed by a sequence of tightly coupled physical and metallurgical events. Each step is critical to producing a dense, defect-free part.

- **Step 1 — CAD Model Slicing:**
  The 3D CAD model (typically in **.STL** or **.STEP** format) is sliced into thin 2D horizontal layers of thickness **t** (typically 0.1 – 0.5 mm) using proprietary slicer software. The **"Why"** is to translate a continuous surface into discrete toolpaths that the deposition head can follow.

- **Step 2 — Powder Delivery:**
  Metal powder of size range **45 – 150 µm** is fed from a hopper via a **carrier gas (Argon or Nitrogen)** through **3–4 coaxial nozzles** converging onto the focal point of the laser. The **"How"** is gas-atomized powder flow controlled by a precision powder feeder (rpm-controlled disc or screw).

- **Step 3 — Laser-Material Interaction:**
  A high-power **fiber laser (often 1.07 µm wavelength, near-IR)** or **Nd:YAG laser** strikes the substrate, creating a tiny **melt pool** (diameter ~ 0.5 – 2 mm). Powder particles injected into this pool are **absorbed, melted, and fused** to the substrate within **milliseconds**.

- **Step 4 — Track Solidification:**
  As the laser moves away, the molten track cools rapidly (cooling rates: **10³ – 10⁵ K/s**). This rapid solidification yields a **fine-grained, directional microstructure** with superior mechanical properties compared to cast equivalents.

- **Step 5 — Layer Stacking:**
  After one layer is complete, the deposition head raises by an increment **ΔZ = t** and deposits the next layer. The previous layer is **re-melted partially** (typically 30–50%) to ensure **metallurgical bonding** between layers.

- **Step 6 — Inert Atmosphere Control:**
  The entire build chamber is flooded with **inert gas (Argon)** maintaining **O₂ content below 50 ppm** to prevent oxidation of reactive metals such as titanium and aluminum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Key Point:** The four most critical process parameters in LENS are — **Laser Power (P)**, **Scan Speed (v)**, **Powder Feed Rate (ṁ)**, and **Layer Thickness (t)**. A change in any one of these directly affects **melt pool geometry, density, and mechanical strength**.

## 2.2 The Critical Engineering Equation: Laser Energy Density

The single most important governing parameter in LENS is the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**, denoted **E_v**, which dictates the quality of the deposit.

### 2.2.1 Definition of Volumetric Energy Density

The energy delivered **per unit volume** of deposited material, which determines whether the powder is fully melted, partially melted, or vaporized.

### 2.2.2 Formula Derivation Setup

The total energy delivered to a unit length of deposit = Laser Power × Time spent per unit length = P / v.
The cross-sectional area of the deposited track ≈ hatch spacing (h) × layer thickness (t).
Therefore, the volume per unit length = h × t.
Energy density is energy per unit volume = (P/v) / (h × t).

### 2.2.3 Final Governing Equation

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

where:
- $E_v$ = Volumetric Energy Density (J/mm³)
- $P$ = Laser Power (W)
- $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essentials.** Memorize the units and the physical meaning of each term. Marks are frequently awarded for correct unit specification.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Description | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density | **50 – 300 J/mm³** |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot h \cdot t$ | Powder Mass Feed Rate (kg/s) | **0.1 – 30 g/min** |
| 3 | $R_{build} = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | Volumetric Build Rate (mm³/s) | Process dependent |
| 4 | $\eta_{abs} = 1 - R - T$ | Absorptivity (R=Reflectivity, T=Transmittance) | **0.25 – 0.40** for metals @ 1.07 µm |
| 5 | $\lambda_{cool} = \dfrac{T_m - T_0}{R_{cool}}$ | Solidification cooling rate approximation | **10³ – 10⁵ K/s** |
| 6 | $H_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Linear Energy Density (J/mm²) | Used in parameter optimization |
| 7 | $d_{melt} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{v}}$ | Approximate melt pool diameter | Empirical; depends on material |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** In KTU board answer sheets, students often write `|x|` and break their handwritten tables. Maintain clarity by using **$\vert x \vert$** in printed notes; in exams, prefer **"mod(x)"** or **"abs(x)"**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LENS is **not a hobby-grade 3D printer**. It is a strategic manufacturing tool used in:
- **Aerospace:** Repair of turbine blades, deposition of Inconel 718 superalloys.
- **Medical:** Customized Ti-6Al-4V orthopedic implants matched to patient CT scans.
- **Defense:** Functionally graded materials (FGM) where composition changes layer-by-layer.
- **Tooling:** Conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts.
- **Energy:** Cladding for corrosion-resistant overlays in nuclear and oil-and-gas components.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Practice writing it cleanly with all steps.

### Step 1: Energy Delivered to the Substrate

Let the laser of power $P$ (in Watts = J/s) be incident on the substrate for a duration $\Delta t$. The total energy delivered is:

$$
E_{total} \;=\; P \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 2: Distance Travelled by the Laser

During this time interval $\Delta t$, the laser (and deposition head) moves with a constant scan velocity $v$ (in mm/s). The length of the deposited track is:

$$
L \;=\; v \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 3: Energy per Unit Length

Dividing the total energy by the track length gives the **linear energy input** (J/mm):

$$
E_{line} \;=\; \frac{E_{total}}{L} \;=\; \frac{P \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot \Delta t} \;=\; \frac{P}{v}
$$

### Step 4: Cross-Section of the Deposited Track

A single deposited track has an approximately rectangular cross-section defined by the **hatch spacing** $h$ (lateral distance between adjacent tracks) and the **layer thickness** $t$ (vertical step per layer). The area of the cross-section is:

$$
A_{track} \;=\; h \cdot t
$$

### Step 5: Volume per Unit Length

For a unit length of track, the corresponding volume of material deposited is:

$$
V_{unit} \;=\; A_{track} \cdot 1 \;=\; h \cdot t
$$

### Step 6: Final Expression for Volumetric Energy Density

Energy density is the energy delivered per unit volume of material. Substituting the expressions derived above:

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{E_{line}}{V_{unit}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{P}{v}}{h \cdot t}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}\,}
$$

### Step 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:** In a LENS process, the laser power is set to **400 W**, scan velocity is **10 mm/s**, hatch spacing is **0.4 mm**, and layer thickness is **0.25 mm**. Calculate the volumetric energy density and comment on whether it falls within the optimum range for Inconel 718 deposition.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 400$ W
- $v = 10$ mm/s
- $h = 0.4$ mm
- $t = 0.25$ mm

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the governing equation:**

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \;=\; \frac{400}{10 \times 0.4 \times 0.25}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator step-by-step:**

$$
10 \times 0.4 \;=\; 4
$$

$$
4 \times 0.25 \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 3 — Final computation:**

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{400}{1} \;=\; 400 \; \text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The computed value of **400 J/mm³ is at the higher end** of the typical operating window (50 – 300 J/mm³) for Inconel 718. This indicates **excess energy input**, which may cause: (i) excessive melt pool size, (ii) keyhole porosity, (iii) evaporation of low-melting alloying elements (e.g., Mn), and (iv) residual stress buildup. A corrective action would be to **increase scan speed to 15 mm/s** to bring $E_v$ down to ~ 267 J/mm³.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Parameter vs. Effect

| Process Parameter ↑ (Increase) | Effect on Energy Density $E_v$ | Effect on Melt Pool | Risk of Defect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power $P$ | Increases | Larger, deeper | Vaporization, keyhole porosity |
| Scan Speed $v$ | Decreases | Smaller, shallower | Lack of fusion, porosity |
| Hatch Spacing $h$ | Decreases | — | Overlap / remelting |
| Layer Thickness $t$ | Decreases | — | Insufficient bonding if too small |
| Powder Feed Rate $\dot{m}$ | Indirect (more mass to melt) | Larger melt pool | Incomplete fusion if too high |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid System Flow — LENS Process Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a LENS workstation, including the flow of material, energy, and information between subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model .STL File] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Toolpath Generation G-code]
    C --> D[Process Controller CNC Unit]
    D --> E1[Laser Source Fiber 1.07um]
    D --> E2[Powder Feeder Argon Carrier]
    D --> E3[X-Y-Z Motion Stage]
    E1 --> F[Coaxial Deposition Nozzle]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    G --> H[Solidified Metal Layer]
    H --> I{Next Layer Required?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Raise Nozzle by dt in Z]
    J --> F
    I -- No --> K[Final 3D Metallic Part]
    L[Inert Gas Chamber Argon] --> F
    M[Closed-Loop O2 Sensor] --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows represent the flow of data (solid lines from CAD) and material/energy (lines from feeders and laser). The feedback loop `M → L → F` represents the closed-loop oxygen monitoring that maintains the inert atmosphere essential for reactive metals like titanium.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Subsystem Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Energy Subsystem"
        LS[Laser Source 500W-4kW]
        LMD[Laser Delivery Fiber Optic Cable]
    end
    subgraph "Material Subsystem"
        PH[Powder Hopper]
        PFR[Powder Feeder Disc RPM Control]
        CG[Carrier Gas Ar N2]
    end
    subgraph "Motion Subsystem"
        XY[X-Y Gantry or Galvo Scanner]
        ZD[Z-axis Elevator]
    end
    subgraph "Process Zone"
        CN[Coaxial Nozzle 3-4 jets]
        MP[Melt Pool]
        SUB[Substrate Build Platform]
    end
    LS --> LMD --> CN
    PH --> PFR --> CN
    CG --> PFR
    XY --> CN
    ZD --> CN
    CN --> MP --> SUB
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description — Physical Layout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since a true physical drawing of a LENS machine is complex, the Mermaid block diagrams above provide the **functional interaction map**. In a KTU exam answer sheet, students should draw a **simple 2D sketch** showing:
> - Laser source on the left
> - Powder feeder at the top
> - Coaxial nozzle at the center pointing **downward** at the substrate
> - Argon inlet, exhaust outlet
> - Substrate mounted on a Z-stage
> - Five-axis or three-axis gantry motion arrows

A standard answer would label:
1. **Laser** (with arrow → nozzle tip)
2. **Powder flow** (3 converging jets → focal point)
3. **Melt pool** (small oval at nozzle-substrate intersection)
4. **Substrate** (rectangle below)
5. **Inert gas** (surrounding arrows)
6. **Z-direction** (↑ arrow on right)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS). Mention its classification under ISO/ASTM 52900.

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
LENS is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** based additive manufacturing process that uses a high-power laser to melt and fuse metal powders injected through coaxial nozzles onto a substrate, building parts layer-by-layer directly from a CAD model. Under **ISO/ASTM 52900**, LENS is classified under the **"Material Extrusion / Directed Energy Deposition"** category, specifically as a **laser-based powder DED process** (1 mark each point).

### Question A2: List any four process parameters that affect the quality of LENS-deposited parts.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four most influential process parameters in LENS are: (i) **Laser Power**, (ii) **Scan Speed**, (iii) **Powder Feed Rate**, and (iv) **Layer Thickness / Hatch Spacing** (½ mark each). Together, these parameters determine the **volumetric energy density** delivered to the melt pool, which in turn governs density, microstructure, and defect formation (1 mark for explanation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** the full question **OR** the alternate choice. The internal choice is provided at the **module level**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): LENS — Working Principle, Parameters & Derivation**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the working principle of Laser Engineered Net Shaping with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of the inert atmosphere. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

LENS operates on the principle of **laser-induced localized melting of metal powder** delivered coaxially with the laser beam. The sequential steps are as follows:

1. **CAD Slicing:** A 3D CAD model is sliced into 2D layers of thickness *t* (0.1 – 0.5 mm) using slicer software (1 mark).

2. **Powder Injection:** Metal powder (45–150 µm size) is delivered through a **coaxial nozzle assembly** using an inert carrier gas (Argon or Nitrogen) at a controlled mass flow rate (1 mark).

3. **Laser Melting:** A high-power laser (typically fiber laser, 500 W – 4 kW) is focused at the same focal point as the converging powder jets, creating a small **melt pool** (diameter 0.5–2 mm) on the substrate (1 mark).

4. **Track Solidification:** The deposition head moves along the X-Y plane at a programmed scan speed, leaving a solidified metallic track. Cooling rates of **10³ – 10⁵ K/s** yield a refined microstructure (1 mark).

5. **Layer Stacking:** After each layer, the head raises by ΔZ = *t* and the next layer is deposited with **30–50% re-melting** of the previous layer for metallurgical bonding (1 mark).

6. **Inert Atmosphere Role:** The build chamber is purged and maintained with **Argon gas at O₂ < 50 ppm** to prevent **oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg). Oxidation would otherwise cause **embrittlement, porosity, and inclusions**, degrading mechanical properties (2 marks).

**Schematic Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
A labelled 2D drawing showing laser, coaxial nozzle, powder inlet, melt pool, substrate, Z-stage, and inert gas envelope.

---

#### (b) Derive the expression for Volumetric Energy Density in LENS. A LENS process uses 600 W laser power, scan speed 8 mm/s, hatch spacing 0.5 mm, and layer thickness 0.3 mm. Calculate the energy density and comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Let $P$ be the laser power, $v$ the scan velocity, $h$ the hatch spacing, and $t$ the layer thickness. The energy delivered per unit time is $P$. The energy delivered per unit length of track is $\frac{P}{v}$ (J/mm). The volume of material deposited per unit length is $h \cdot t$ (mm²·mm = mm³). Hence, the energy per unit volume, or **Volumetric Energy Density**, is:

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**

Given: $P = 600$ W, $v = 8$ mm/s, $h = 0.5$ mm, $t = 0.3$ mm.

$$
E_v \;=\; \frac{600}{8 \times 0.5 \times 0.3} \;=\; \frac{600}{1.2} \;=\; 500 \; \text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Engineering Comment (1 Mark):**

The calculated energy density of **500 J/mm³ is significantly higher** than the typical optimum range of **50 – 300 J/mm³** for Inconel 718. This indicates **excess energy input**, which may cause keyhole porosity, melt pool instabilities, and high residual stresses. A corrective action is to **reduce laser power to ~300 W** or **increase scan speed to 12 mm/s** to bring $E_v$ within the optimum range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternate Choice): LENS — Process, Applications & Defects**

#### (a) Compare LENS with Selective Laser Melting (SLM) in terms of process, material delivery, and applications. Discuss the typical defects observed in LENS-deposited parts. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LENS | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| ISO/ASTM Category | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) |
| Powder Delivery | Coaxial nozzle, gas-atomized | Roller/spreader on a pre-laid bed |
| Build Volume | Large (up to ~m³ in some systems) | Limited by bed size (~300 × 300 × 400 mm³) |
| Support Structures | Minimal (for overhangs) | Mandatory for overhangs |
| Surface Finish | Coarse (Ra 10–30 µm) | Finer (Ra 5–15 µm) |
| Multi-material Capability | Excellent (FDM, FGM) | Limited (single powder bed) |
| Repair Applications | Excellent (used for blade repair) | Not suitable for repair |
| Atmosphere | Localized inert shroud | Full chamber inertization |

(5 marks for the table and 2 marks for defect discussion)

**Typical Defects in LENS (2 marks):**
- **Lack of fusion** (low $E_v$) — discrete voids between tracks
- **Keyhole porosity** (high $E_v$) — gas trapped in deep vapor depressions
- **Ballistic deposition** (insufficient laser power) — unmelted powder particles ejected
- **Residual stress & distortion** (thermal gradients) — may require post-build heat treatment

---

#### (b) Discuss the major industrial applications of LENS with suitable examples. Why is LENS preferred for functionally graded materials (FGM)? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Industrial Applications (5 marks):**

1. **Aerospace Engine Components:** LENS is used to **rebuild worn turbine blade tips** with Inconel 718 or Ti-6Al-4V, recovering dimensions without replacing the part. Examples: GE Aviation, Honeywell.

2. **Medical Implants:** Custom **patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V hip and cranial implants** are manufactured using LENS based on CT/MRI data, with optimized trabecular structures for bone ingrowth.

3. **Tooling Industry:** **Conformal cooling channels** in injection mold inserts are additively manufactured using LENS, reducing cycle times by 20–40% and improving part quality.

4. **Defense and Armor:** LENS deposits **hard-facing layers** of Stellite or WC-Co composites on armor plates for ballistic protection.

5. **Oil & Gas:** **Cladding of corrosion-resistant alloys** (Hastelloy, Inconel) on carbon steel pipes and valves for use in sour (H₂S-containing) environments.

**Why LENS for FGM (2 marks):**

Functionally Graded Materials require **continuous variation of composition** through the part (e.g., a Ti–V transition from pure Ti on one side to Ti-6Al-4V on the other). LENS is uniquely suited because it supports **multiple powder feeders (typically 4 hoppers)**, allowing real-time blending of powder ratios as the deposition progresses. This dynamic compositional control is not possible in single-powder-bed systems like SLM, making LENS the **industry standard for FGM fabrication**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in LENS Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LENS with SLM:** Many students write "LENS uses a powder bed" — this is **wrong**. LENS is a DED process with **coaxial powder injection**, NOT a bed-based process. This single error can cost **3–5 marks**.
>
> 2. **Skipping units in the energy density derivation:** Writing $E_v = \frac{P}{vht}$ without specifying **J/mm³** loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the inert atmosphere role:** Examiners specifically test this — a complete answer **must** mention Argon shrouding and O₂ < 50 ppm.
>
> 4. **Not labelling the schematic:** A diagram without labels (laser, nozzle, substrate, powder flow) is treated as an "incomplete figure" and may receive **0–1 marks** out of 3.
>
> 5. **Vague comparative answers:** Writing "SLM is better" or "LENS is more expensive" without numerical/technical justification is insufficient. Always quote **specific values** (build volume, surface roughness, power levels).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)**

- **Full form & lineage:** Laser Engineered Net Shaping, developed at **Sandia National Labs (1996)**, commercialized by **Optomec**.
- **ISO/ASTM 52900 classification:** **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)**, specifically **laser-based powder DED**.
- **Heat source:** **Fiber laser** (wavelength 1.07 µm) or **Nd:YAG laser**; power range **500 W – 4 kW**.
- **Powder characteristics:** **45 – 150 µm** particle size, gas-atomized, delivered via **coaxial nozzle** with **Argon / Nitrogen** carrier gas.
- **Key process parameters:** Laser Power $P$, Scan Speed $v$, Hatch Spacing $h$, Layer Thickness $t$, Powder Feed Rate $\dot{m}$.
- **Governing equation:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$, units **J/mm³**, optimum range **50 – 300 J/mm³** for most metals.
- **Inert atmosphere:** **Argon shroud, O₂ < 50 ppm** — essential for Ti, Al, Mg.
- **Cooling rate:** **10³ – 10⁵ K/s** — yields **fine, directional microstructure**.
- **Surface finish:** As-built **Ra 10 – 30 µm**; requires post-machining for critical tolerance surfaces.
- **Build envelope:** Typically **up to 1 m³** for industrial LENS systems.
- **Unique advantage over SLM:** **Multi-material capability (FDM/FGM), repair applications, larger build volume, minimal support structures**.
- **Typical defects:** Lack of fusion, keyhole porosity, ballistic deposition, residual stress, oxidation.
- **Major applications:** Aerospace turbine blade repair, medical implants, conformal cooling tooling, FGM, oil & gas cladding.
- **Comparison must-knows:**
  - LENS = **DED** (nozzle); SLM = **PBF** (powder bed).
  - LENS = **coarse finish, large build**; SLM = **fine finish, smaller build**.
  - LENS = **multi-material capable**; SLM = **single material** in most systems.
- **Cross-connections to remember:** LENS is often compared with **Laser Cladding, Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM), and Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** in KTU Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT404 - INDUSTRIAL MANUFACTURING PROCESS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LIGA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 LIGA Process — Module 4 Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> **LIGA** is a German acronym for **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroforming/Electroplating), and **A**bformung (Molding). It is a **non-conventional microfabrication process** that combines **deep X-ray lithography**, **electroplating**, and **plastic molding** to produce **high-aspect-ratio microstructures** (HARMS) with sub-micrometer lateral precision and structural heights ranging from hundreds of micrometers to several centimeters.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, LIGA is classified under **Advanced Non-Traditional Microfabrication Processes** and is treated as the benchmark technology for **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** and **micro-system fabrication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric Definitions (KTU Board-Standard):**
> - **Aspect Ratio (AR):** Ratio of structural height $(h)$ to minimum lateral feature width $(w)$. LIGA routinely achieves **AR > 100:1**.
> - **Lateral Resolution:** Minimum feature size on the order of **0.2 µm to 2 µm**.
> - **Structural Height:** Typically **100 µm to 1500 µm**.
> - **Sidewall Verticality:** Deviation < **±0.1°** (near-optical-vertical walls).

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The X-Ray Cake Mould"

Imagine baking a fancy layered cake. You have:
1. A **transparent mould** (the photoresist) that hardens only where **special intense X-ray light** hits it (Lithography).
2. The exposed/unexposed regions are washed away, leaving a **perfect 3D cavity** the shape of your cake design.
3. You then **fill the cavity with liquid metal** that hardens inside (Electroplating).
4. Finally, you peel away the mould and use that metal piece as a **master mould to mass-produce thousands of plastic copies** (Molding).

This is **exactly** what LIGA does — but at micrometer scale, with **synchrotron X-rays** instead of oven light, and **nickel** instead of chocolate!

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LIGA Matters in Industry:** LIGA is the *only* mass-production-compatible process that can build **truly 3D metallic microstructures** with vertical sidewalls and extraordinary depth, which is critical for inertial sensors, micro-gears, fluidic channels, and optical micro-components.

---

## 2. The Three Pillars of LIGA — Process Architecture

The LIGA process is built on **three sequential core stages**. KTU examiners frequently test these as a **flowchart question (3 marks)** or as part of a **14-mark descriptive question**.

### 🔷 Pillar 1: Deep X-Ray Lithography (DXRL)
- A thick **positive-tone photoresist** (commonly **PMMA — Polymethyl Methacrylate**, thickness 100–1500 µm) is coated onto a conductive substrate.
- The resist is exposed to **highly collimated, high-energy X-rays** from a **synchrotron radiation source** (typical wavelength: **0.2 – 2 nm**, photon energy: **2 – 10 keV**).
- The X-rays penetrate the entire resist thickness with minimal diffraction, producing **nearly vertical sidewalls**.
- Exposed regions undergo **chain scission** of PMMA molecules, making them soluble in a **developer** (e.g., GG developer: tetra-butyl-ammonium-hydroxide solution).

### 🔷 Pillar 2: Electroforming / Electroplating (Galvanoformung)
- The developed PMMA structure acts as a **mould**.
- A conductive metal layer (seed layer) is sputtered on the substrate.
- The assembly is immersed in an **electrolytic bath** (typically **nickel sulfamate** for Ni deposition).
- **Cathode** = substrate, **Anode** = nickel pellets.
- Metal ions (Ni²⁺) deposit inside the PMMA cavities, growing **upward** to fill them completely.
- Process continues until the metal slightly **over-plates** the PMMA top surface, locking the structure.

### 🔷 Pillar 3: Molding (Abformung) — The Replication Stage
- The electroformed metal piece (after PMMA removal) becomes the **master mould insert**.
- This mould is used in **injection molding**, **hot embossing**, or **reaction injection molding** to mass-produce **plastic (e.g., PMMA, POM, COC) replicas**.
- For metallic mass production, the master is used as a mould for **second-stage electroplating**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot-Question Fact:** Because synchrotrons are extremely expensive, an alternative called **UV-LIGA** uses **ultraviolet light** with **SU-8 photoresist** to perform the lithography step. UV-LIGA sacrifices some aspect ratio (AR ~ 20:1) but is far cheaper and is the *industrial workhorse* version of LIGA.

---

## 3. Materials Palette in LIGA

| Component | Common Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Photoresist | **PMMA** (synchrotron) / **SU-8** (UV) | Sacrificial mould |
| Seed Layer | Ti / Au / Cu (sputtered) | Conductive base for plating |
| Structural Metal | **Nickel** (Ni), Ni–Fe alloys, Copper | Final micro-structure |
| Developer | GG developer, SU-8 developer | Dissolves exposed resist |
| Electrolyte | Nickel sulfamate bath (Ni(NH₂SO₃)₂) | Provides Ni²⁺ ions |
| Replica Plastic | PMMA, POM, COC, PEEK | Mass-produced parts |
| X-Ray Source | Synchrotron (0.2–2 nm) | Deep penetrating exposure |

---

## 4. Advantages & Limitations of LIGA

### ✅ Advantages
- **Extremely high aspect ratio** (>100:1) — unmatched by most MEMS processes.
- **Excellent sidewall verticality** (≈ 90° ± 0.1°).
- **Smooth sidewall surface finish** (Ra < 50 nm).
- **Wide range of structural heights** (µm to cm).
- **Material flexibility** — multiple metals and plastics.
- Compatible with **mass production** via the molding step.

### ❌ Limitations
- **Synchrotron radiation is very expensive** and not universally accessible.
- **Long process chain** — multiple stages, each needing precision control.
- **Limited to 2.5D geometries** in classical LIGA (true 3D requires multiple exposures or tilting).
- **Resist removal is slow** (often uses solvents like acetone or reactive ion etching).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 1. The Underlying Physics — Why Synchrotron X-Rays?

Conventional photolithography is diffraction-limited by the wavelength $\lambda$ of the exposing light. The **minimum resolvable feature** is governed by:

$$d_{min} \approx k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

where:
- $d_{min}$ = minimum printable feature size
- $k_1$ = process constant (typically 0.4 – 0.6)
- $\lambda$ = exposing wavelength
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the optical system

For **visible/UV light** ($\lambda \approx 365 \text{ nm}$), $d_{min}$ is limited to sub-micron only in very thin resists. To achieve **deep penetration without diffraction**, LIGA uses **X-rays** of wavelength **0.2 – 2 nm**, which penetrate millimeters into PMMA while maintaining near-zero diffraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synchrotron radiation characteristics used in LIGA:**
> - **High brilliance** (photons/s/mm²/mrad²)
> - **Low divergence** (≈ 1 mrad) → parallel beam → vertical walls
> - **Tunable wavelength** via monochromators
> - **High intensity** enables exposure of thick resists in minutes

## 2. X-Ray Absorption in PMMA — The Beer-Lambert Law

The intensity of X-rays at depth $z$ inside the photoresist follows an exponential attenuation:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot z}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity at resist top surface
- $\mu$ = linear absorption coefficient of PMMA (depends on photon energy $E$)
- $z$ = depth below the resist surface

The **critical dose** $D_c$ required to render PMMA developable is:

$$D_c = I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot z}$$

Rearranging for **maximum usable depth** $z_{max}$:

$$z_{max} = \frac{1}{\mu} \ln\left(\frac{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}{D_c}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** For a given $D_c$ and $\mu$, **doubling the exposure time** $t_{exp}$ does NOT double the depth — it only increases depth logarithmically. This is why **synchrotron intensity** is so valuable.

## 3. Electroplating Physics — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

The mass of nickel deposited during the Galvanoformung step follows **Faraday's First Law**:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of deposited nickel (grams)
- $M$ = molar mass of nickel = **58.69 g/mol**
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = plating time (seconds)
- $n$ = valency of Ni²⁺ = **2**
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**

The **thickness of deposited nickel** $h$ over an area $A$ is:

$$h = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

The **current density** $J = I/A$ is the key control parameter:

$$J = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot h}{M \cdot t} = \frac{2 \cdot 96485 \cdot 8900 \cdot h}{0.05869 \cdot t}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** If $J$ is too high (>5 A/dm²), the deposit becomes rough, dendritic, and stressed. If too low (<0.5 A/dm²), deposition is uneconomically slow. **KTU standard range: 1 – 4 A/dm².**

## 4. Aspect Ratio — The Defining Figure of Merit

The **process aspect ratio** for a LIGA structure is:

$$AR = \frac{h}{w}$$

where:
- $h$ = structural height (set by resist thickness)
- $w$ = minimum lateral feature width (set by mask + exposure)

For an **electroplated micro-wall** of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$:

$$AR = \frac{h}{w} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot J \cdot t}{M \cdot w}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If the question gives the plating current, time, and desired height, you can compute the **minimum resist thickness required** = the **maximum feature height achievable**. They are **equal** in a single-exposure LIGA step.

## 5. Mould Replication — Injection Molding Pressure

For the Abformung step, the cavity filling pressure in hot embossing is approximated by the **Stefan equation** simplified:

$$P_{fill} = \frac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L^2 \cdot v}{w^3 \cdot h^2}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = polymer melt viscosity
- $L$ = cavity length
- $v$ = filling velocity
- $w$ = cavity width
- $h$ = cavity height

This is a strong **inverse cubic function** of cavity width — LIGA's tiny micro-cavities require **very high pressures** (often 50–100 MPa), which is why **high-tonnage presses** are mandatory.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — One-Page Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{min} = k_1 \cdot \lambda / NA$ | Diffraction limit of lithography |
| 2 | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu z}$ | X-ray depth attenuation |
| 3 | $z_{max} = \frac{1}{\mu}\ln(I_0 t_{exp}/D_c)$ | Maximum usable resist depth |
| 4 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of electrolysis |
| 5 | $h = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | Plated thickness |
| 6 | $AR = h / w$ | Aspect ratio of LIGA structure |
| 7 | $P_{fill} = 12 \eta L^2 v / (w^3 h^2)$ | Mould filling pressure |
| 8 | $D = I_0 \cdot t_{exp}$ | Exposure dose (J/cm³) |
| 9 | $R_a = f(J, T, pH)$ | Surface roughness function of plating params |
| 10 | $\eta_{replica} = N_{good}/N_{total}$ | Replication yield |

> **Symbol Key:** $\mu$ = absorption coefficient; $D_c$ = critical dose; $\rho$ = density of Ni (8900 kg/m³); $F$ = 96485 C/mol; $n$ = valency; $M$ = molar mass.

---

## 6. Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | LIGA Product Example |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Bosch)** | Injector nozzles for common-rail diesel engines |
| **Aerospace** | Micro-thruster arrays, gyroscope components |
| **Telecommunications** | Optical fiber alignment structures, micro-relays |
| **Medical Technology** | Stents, micro-needles, micro-fluidic chips |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Accelerometers, gyroscopes (early MEMS generations) |
| **Research** | X-ray zone plates, LIGA-fabricated micro-reactors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Maximum Depth of Exposure in PMMA Resist

**Given:**
- Critical dose for PMMA development: $D_c = 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$
- Incident X-ray intensity: $I_0 = 5 \text{ W/cm}^2$ (synchrotron)
- Linear absorption coefficient of PMMA at $\lambda = 1 \text{ nm}$: $\mu = 30 \text{ cm}^{-1}$
- Exposure time: $t_{exp} = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$

**To Find:** Maximum usable depth $z_{max}$ at which the dose just equals the critical dose.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 —** Write the dose delivered at depth $z$:

$$D(z) = I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot z}$$

**Step 2 —** Set $D(z_{max}) = D_c$:

$$I_0 \cdot t_{exp} \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot z_{max}} = D_c$$

**Step 3 —** Solve for $e^{-\mu \cdot z_{max}}$:

$$e^{-\mu \cdot z_{max}} = \frac{D_c}{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}$$

**Step 4 —** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\mu \cdot z_{max} = \ln\left(\frac{D_c}{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}\right)$$

**Step 5 —** Solve for $z_{max}$:

$$z_{max} = -\frac{1}{\mu}\ln\left(\frac{D_c}{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\ln\left(\frac{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}{D_c}\right)$$

**Step 6 —** Plug in the numerical values:

First, compute the total incident dose at the top:

$$I_0 \cdot t_{exp} = 5 \text{ W/cm}^2 \times 1800 \text{ s} = 9000 \text{ J/cm}^3 = 9 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$$

Then, compute the logarithmic term:

$$\frac{I_0 \cdot t_{exp}}{D_c} = \frac{9000}{4000} = 2.25$$

Apply the natural log:

$$\ln(2.25) \approx 0.8109$$

Finally, compute $z_{max}$:

$$z_{max} = \frac{1}{30 \text{ cm}^{-1}} \times 0.8109 \approx 0.02703 \text{ cm} = 270.3 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 7 —** **Conclusion:**
The maximum usable PMMA depth is approximately **270 µm**, which sets the upper limit of the structural height $h$ in this LIGA process.

---

## Derivation 2: Electroplating Time Required to Fill a LIGA Mould Cavity

**Given:**
- Cavity (PMMA mould) dimensions: $L = 500 \text{ µm}$, $w = 20 \text{ µm}$, $h = 300 \text{ µm}$
- Number of identical cavities: $N = 1000$
- Nickel electrolyte: nickel sulfamate, $\rho_{Ni} = 8900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Required current density: $J = 2 \text{ A/dm}^2$
- Molar mass of Ni: $M = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- Valency: $n = 2$, Faraday constant $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$

**To Find:** Total time $t$ to completely fill all cavities.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 —** Compute the area of **one** cavity's cross-section (this is the area the plating must fill from the bottom up):

$$A_{cavity} = w \times L = 20 \text{ µm} \times 500 \text{ µm} = 10000 \text{ µm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the **total plating area** for all cavities:

$$A_{total} = N \cdot A_{cavity} = 1000 \times 1 \times 10^{-8} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the **total current** required:

Convert $A_{total}$ to dm²:

$$A_{total} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 \times 100 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ dm}^2$$

$$I = J \cdot A_{total} = 2 \text{ A/dm}^2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ dm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 2 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 —** Compute the **mass of nickel** needed to fill all cavities:

$$m = \rho_{Ni} \cdot V = \rho_{Ni} \cdot A_{cavity} \cdot h \cdot N$$

$$V = 1 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2 \times 300 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \times 1000 = 3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

$$m = 8900 \times 3 \times 10^{-9} = 2.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 0.0267 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 —** Apply **Faraday's law** to find time:

$$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$$

Convert mass to grams consistently (already in grams: $0.0267$ g):

$$t = \frac{0.0267 \text{ g} \times 2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}{58.69 \text{ g/mol} \times 0.002 \text{ A}}$$

$$t = \frac{5151.5}{0.11738} \approx 43890 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 —** Convert to hours:

$$t = \frac{43890}{3600} \approx 12.19 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 7 —** **Conclusion:**
The plating process will take approximately **12.2 hours** to fully fill all 1000 cavities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This long plating time is one reason why LIGA uses **highly stable electroplating baths** with constant agitation and **pH control (±0.1)** to avoid drift in deposition rate.

---

## Derivation 3: Aspect Ratio of the Fabricated Micro-Wall

**Given:** From Derivation 2, structural height $h = 300 \text{ µm}$, lateral wall width $w = 20 \text{ µm}$.

**Step 1 —** Apply the definition:

$$AR = \frac{h}{w}$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute:

$$AR = \frac{300 \text{ µm}}{20 \text{ µm}}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute:

$$AR = 15$$

**Step 4 —** **Conclusion:**
This structure has an aspect ratio of **15:1**, which is in the typical UV-LIGA range (not classical synchrotron LIGA, which would achieve >100:1 with similar lateral dimensions).

---

## 📊 Comparative Analytical Matrix — LIGA vs. UV-LIGA vs. Conventional MEMS

| Parameter | Classical LIGA | UV-LIGA | Conventional UV Lithography |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation Source | **Synchrotron X-ray** | **UV (i-line/h-line)** | UV (mercury lamp) |
| Resist | **PMMA** (thick) | **SU-8** (thick) | Standard photoresist (thin) |
| Maximum Aspect Ratio | **> 100 : 1** | **20 : 1** | **~ 5 : 1** |
| Minimum Feature Size | **0.2 – 2 µm** | 5 – 10 µm | 1 – 2 µm |
| Maximum Height | **mm range** | ~ 1 mm | ~ 10 µm |
| Sidewall Verticality | **± 0.1°** | ± 1° | ± 2° |
| Cost | **Very High** | Moderate | Low |
| Industrial Scalability | Limited by synchrotron | **High** | High |
| Typical Application | Premium sensors, optics | Micro-fluidics, MEMS | Standard ICs |

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: End-to-End LIGA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Conductive Substrate] --> B[Sputter Seed Layer - Ti or Au]
    B --> C[Spin / Cast Thick PMMA Resist]
    C --> D[Bake Resist to Remove Solvent]
    D --> E[Expose to Synchrotron X-rays through X-ray Mask]
    E --> F[Develop in GG Developer - Exposed PMMA Dissolves]
    F --> G[PMMA Mould Cavities Formed]
    G --> H[Immerse in Nickel Sulfamate Electrolyte]
    H --> I[Apply DC Current - Ni grows into cavities]
    I --> J[Ni Fully Fills and Slightly Over-plates]
    J --> K[Strip PMMA in Hot Solvent]
    K --> L[Ni Master Mould Obtained]
    L --> M[Use Mould in Injection Molding or Hot Embossing]
    M --> N[Mass-produce Plastic or Second-stage Metal Replicas]
    N --> O[End: Final Micro-parts Ready]
```

## Diagram 2: Functional Architecture of the LIGA Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 - Lithography Module]
        A1[Synchrotron X-ray Source] --> A2[X-ray Mask with Au Absorber Pattern]
        A2 --> A3[PMMA-coated Substrate under Beam]
        A3 --> A4[Developed PMMA Mould]
    end

    subgraph S2[Stage 2 - Electroforming Module]
        A4 --> B1[Electrolyte Bath - Ni Sulfamate]
        B1 --> B2[DC Power Supply]
        B2 --> B3[Ni Metal Deposition inside Mould]
        B3 --> B4[Ni Master Structure]
    end

    subgraph S3[Stage 3 - Molding Module]
        B4 --> C1[Insert Ni Mould into Press]
        C1 --> C2[Hot Embossing of PMMA Pellets]
        C2 --> C3[Demoulded Plastic Replica]
        C3 --> C4[Optional - Second Electroplating for Metal Copies]
    end
```

## Diagram 3: Cross-Sectional View of a Typical LIGA Micro-Structure (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Top: Air Gap after Demoulding] --> L2[Ni Micro-Wall - Aspect Ratio 15:1 to 100:1]
    L2 --> L3[Seed Layer - Au or Cu - 50 to 100 nm thick]
    L3 --> L4[Substrate - Si Wafer or Metal Plate]
```

> **Why this block representation:** A true cross-sectional image of a LIGA micro-wall requires lithography-specific drafting tools. The block-level representation above is the **functionally accurate substitute**, mapping the *layer hierarchy* of any fabricated LIGA structure.

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Q: What is the LIGA process? Expand the acronym and state any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
LIGA is a German acronym for **L**ithographie (Lithography), **G**alvanoformung (Electroplating), and **A**bformung (Molding). It is a non-conventional microfabrication process used to produce high-aspect-ratio microstructures (HARMS).

**Two advantages:**
1. Extremely high aspect ratio (>100:1) with near-vertical sidewalls (± 0.1°).
2. Excellent surface finish on sidewalls (Ra < 50 nm) and structural heights up to several millimeters.
