## ✏️ PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the LIGA process in detail with emphasis on the deep X-ray lithography step. Explain why synchrotron radiation is preferred over conventional UV light for this step.

**Model Solution:**

The LIGA process consists of three sequential stages: Deep X-ray Lithography, Electroforming, and Molding.

**Deep X-ray Lithography (DXRL) — Detailed Description:**

1. **Substrate Preparation:** A clean, conductive substrate (silicon wafer with sputtered metal seed layer, or a polished metal plate) is used as the base.

2. **Resist Coating:** A thick layer of **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** — a positive-tone photoresist — is applied. Thickness typically ranges from **100 µm to 1500 µm**. This is achieved by:
   - Spin coating (for thin layers, up to ~100 µm), or
   - Sheet-casting/bonding pre-cast PMMA sheets (for thick layers, up to mm scale).

3. **Pre-Bake:** The coated substrate is baked (typically at **90–110°C** for 1–2 hours) to remove residual solvent and improve adhesion.

4. **Mask Preparation:** An **X-ray mask** is used, consisting of:
   - A thin **membrane** (e.g., 2–5 µm thick silicon carbide, titanium, or polyimide) transparent to X-rays.
   - A **patterned absorber** (gold, ~10–25 µm thick) that blocks X-rays in unwanted areas.

5. **X-Ray Exposure:** The assembly (mask above, resist below) is exposed to **synchrotron radiation**. Typical exposure parameters:
   - **Wavelength:** 0.2 – 2 nm
   - **Photon energy:** 2 – 10 keV
   - **Dose:** 1 – 10 kJ/cm³
   - **Exposure time:** minutes to ~1 hour, depending on resist thickness and beam intensity.

6. **Development:** The exposed PMMA is dissolved in a **GG developer** (a mixture of tetrahydro-1,3-dioxolan-2-one-based solvents and amino-alcohol). This creates a **high-aspect-ratio mould** with nearly vertical sidewalls.

**Why Synchrotron Radiation is Preferred over UV Light:**

- **Penetration Depth:** X-rays penetrate deeply (mm range) into PMMA without significant attenuation, while UV is absorbed within microns.
- **Wavelength & Diffraction:** $\lambda_{X-ray} \ll \lambda_{UV}$, so diffraction is negligible — enabling sub-micron lateral resolution even in very thick resists.
- **Parallel Beam:** Synchrotron radiation has very low divergence (~1 mrad), producing **vertical sidewalls** over millimeter heights.
- **High Intensity:** Required dose is delivered in minutes, which is impractical with laboratory X-ray sources.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of Faraday's law, calculate the time required to electrodeposit a nickel layer of thickness 250 µm over a substrate of area 10 cm² using a current density of 2 A/dm². Given: $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $\rho_{Ni} = 8900$ kg/m³.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Identify the plating area:

$$A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.001 \text{ m}^2 = 0.1 \text{ dm}^2$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the current:

$$I = J \cdot A = 2 \text{ A/dm}^2 \times 0.1 \text{ dm}^2 = 0.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the volume of Ni to be deposited:

$$V = A \times h = 0.001 \text{ m}^2 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 —** Compute the mass:

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 8900 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 2.225 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 2.225 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 —** Apply Faraday's first law:

$$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}$$

$$t = \frac{2.225 \times 2 \times 96485}{58.69 \times 0.2}$$

$$t = \frac{429358.25}{11.738}$$

$$t \approx 36580 \text{ s} \approx 10.16 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 —** **Conclusion:** The plating time is approximately **10.16 hours**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4 — Internal Choice Alternative]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Electroforming and Molding stages of the LIGA process. Discuss the role of the seed layer and the choice of electrolyte in achieving high-quality micro-structures.

**Model Solution:**

**Electroforming Stage (Galvanoformung):**

After lithography, the developed PMMA structure is a mould with cavities of the desired geometry. The electroforming process converts this mould into a **metal master**:

1. **Seed Layer Deposition:** A thin conductive layer (typically **Ti/Au, Ti/Cu, or Cr/Au**, 50–200 nm thick) is deposited on the substrate before PMMA coating. This seed layer acts as the **cathode** during electroplating and ensures uniform current distribution across all cavities.

2. **Electrolyte Selection:** The most common electrolyte is the **nickel sulfamate bath** $\left[Ni(NH_2SO_3)_2\right]$, chosen because:
   - It produces **low internal stress** in the deposit.
   - It enables **high deposition rates** (up to 25 µm/hour).
   - It yields deposits with **fine grain structure** and smooth surfaces.
   - pH is maintained at **3.5 – 4.5**; temperature at **45 – 55°C**.

3. **Plating Setup:**
   - **Cathode:** The seeded substrate.
   - **Anode:** Pure nickel pellets in a titanium basket.
   - **Current density:** Typically 1 – 4 A/dm².
   - **Agitation:** Air or mechanical stirring to prevent concentration gradients.

4. **Deposition Kinetics:** Ni²⁺ ions migrate to the cathode, accept two electrons, and deposit as Ni metal:

$$Ni^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Ni_{(s)}$$

5. **Process Termination:** Plating continues until the cavities are **completely filled** and the deposit slightly **over-plates** above the PMMA surface. This locks the structure for easy PMMA removal.

6. **PMMA Removal:** The PMMA is stripped using a solvent (acetone, methylene chloride) or reactive ion etching, leaving the **free-standing Ni micro-structure**.

**Molding Stage (Abformung):**

The Ni master is used to mass-produce replicas via:
- **Hot Embossing** (most common for polymer replicas)
- **Injection Molding** (high-volume production)
- **Reaction Injection Molding** (for thermosets)
- **Second-stage electroplating** (for metallic mass production)

**Role of Seed Layer:** Without a uniform seed layer, plating current would not distribute evenly → **uneven plating heights** → some cavities over-filled while others under-filled → poor yield.

**Role of Electrolyte Choice:** A poorly chosen electrolyte leads to **pitting, dendrite formation, hydrogen embrittlement, or high internal stress** — all fatal to LIGA micro-structures.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List any four industrial applications of LIGA-fabricated micro-structures and explain the role of LIGA in MEMS fabrication.

**Model Solution:**

**Four Industrial Applications:**

1. **Automotive Fuel Injectors** — Bosch uses LIGA-fabricated micro-nozzle plates for diesel common-rail injectors, enabling finer fuel atomization and lower emissions.
2. **Inertial Sensors (Accelerometers, Gyroscopes)** — High-aspect-ratio LIGA structures form proof masses and flexures with excellent sensitivity.
3. **Optical Fiber Alignment Structures** — LIGA-fabricated V-grooves align optical fibers with sub-micron precision in telecom modules.
4. **Medical Micro-Implants** — LIGA enables stents, micro-needles, and micro-fluidic channels for drug delivery systems.

**Role of LIGA in MEMS Fabrication:**

LIGA is foundational to MEMS because:
- It provides **3D micro-mechanical structures** with high aspect ratio — not achievable by surface micromachining.
- It allows **batch fabrication** through the molding step, making MEMS commercially viable.
- It supports **multiple materials** (Ni, Ni-Fe alloys, plastics, ceramics), enabling diverse MEMS device families.
- It bridges the gap between IC fabrication and **true 3D micro-mechanical engineering**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Confusing LIGA with UV-LIGA** in a question that specifies "synchrotron" — always read the radiation source carefully.
> - **Forgetting to convert cm² ↔ m² ↔ dm²** units in electroplating problems — KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency in Faraday's law problems.
> - **Stating "LIGA is used in IC fabrication"** — LIGA is for **MEMS and micro-systems**, NOT standard ICs (which use optical lithography on thin resists).
> - **Ignoring the role of the seed layer** in electroplating answers — a common 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
> - **Not mentioning the molding step** in process flowcharts — students often describe only lithography + plating, forgetting Abformung.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LIGA = Lithography + Galvanoformung + Abformung** (German acronym).
- **Three stages:** Deep X-ray Lithography (DXRL) → Electroplating (Galvanoformung) → Molding (Abformung).
- **Photoresist:** PMMA (positive tone, 100–1500 µm thick).
- **X-ray source:** Synchrotron radiation, λ = 0.2 – 2 nm, energy 2 – 10 keV.
- **Maximum aspect ratio:** Classical LIGA > **100:1**; UV-LIGA ~ **20:1**.
- **Sidewall verticality:** ± 0.1° (best in class).
- **Structural metal:** Nickel (most common), from nickel sulfamate electrolyte.
- **Seed layer:** Sputtered Au/Cu/Ti — mandatory for uniform plating.
- **Faraday's law:** $m = M \cdot I \cdot t / (n \cdot F)$ — controls plating thickness.
- **Critical process parameters:** Synchrotron dose, current density (1–4 A/dm²), pH (3.5–4.5), temperature (45–55°C).
- **X-ray attenuation:** $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu z}$ — governs maximum usable depth.
- **UV-LIGA** = cheaper alternative using SU-8 + UV light; sacrifices some AR but is industry-friendly.
- **Industrial use cases:** Fuel injectors, MEMS sensors, optical components, medical implants.
- **Mastering limitations:** Limited to 2.5D geometries, expensive synchrotron access, slow PMMA removal.
- **Key distinction:** LIGA → **MEMS & micro-systems**, NOT integrated circuits.
